topics.title: Narrow and Wide Band FM and Their Spectra
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT502 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Narrow and Wide Band FM and Their Spectra

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Frequency Modulation (FM)?

**Frequency Modulation (FM)** is an angle modulation technique in which the **instantaneous frequency** of the carrier wave is varied in proportion to the amplitude of the modulating (message) signal, while the carrier amplitude remains constant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> In FM, the frequency of the carrier $f_c$ is changed according to the message signal $m(t)$, producing an instantaneous frequency $f_i(t) = f_c + k_f \, m(t)$, where $k_f$ is the frequency sensitivity of the modulator (measured in **Hz/volt**).

The general FM waveform is mathematically expressed as:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos\!\left[2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{0}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau\right]$$

The term inside the cosine is called the **total phase** $\theta_i(t)$, and the rate of change of phase gives the **instantaneous frequency**:

$$f_i(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\frac{d\theta_i(t)}{dt} = f_c + k_f\, m(t)$$

## 1.2 Modulation Index — The Deciding Parameter

The **modulation index** $\beta$ is the single most important parameter that classifies FM into Narrowband or Wideband:

$$\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \frac{k_f \, A_m}{f_m}$$

where:
- $\Delta f = k_f A_m$ = **frequency deviation** (maximum deviation from carrier)
- $A_m$ = peak amplitude of the message signal
- $f_m$ = message frequency

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification Rule Used in KTU Examinations:**
> - **Narrowband FM (NBFM)** $\rightarrow$ $\beta \ll 1$ (typically $\beta < 0.5$ or considered $\beta < 0.3$)
> - **Wideband FM (WBFM)** $\rightarrow$ $\beta \gg 1$ (typically $\beta > 1$)
> - The boundary condition for analysis is **$\beta = 1$**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Pitch" of a Whistle

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Train Whistle Approaching You**
> Imagine a train blowing a whistle. As the train approaches, the pitch (frequency) of the whistle seems higher; as it moves away, the pitch drops. This is because the *frequency* changes due to motion. Now imagine the train whistle's pitch is being controlled by a *microphone* picking up someone's voice — when the voice is loud, the pitch swings widely; when the voice is soft, the pitch barely changes.
> - **Narrowband FM** = *Whistle with a soft voice (small pitch swing)* → the frequency barely wiggles around the carrier.
> - **Wideband FM** = *Whistle with a loud voice (large pitch swing)* → the frequency swings far from the carrier, producing many sidebands.

The **spectrum** of FM (which frequencies are present and how strong they are) is heavily dependent on whether $\beta$ is small (NBFM) or large (WBFM). This is the central focus of this topic.

## 1.4 Key Distinction Between NBFM and WBFM (At a Glance)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** The question "Compare NBFM and WBFM" appears almost every semester. Memorize the following table format for guaranteed full marks.

| Aspect | NBFM | WBFM |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Modulation index $\beta$ | $\beta \ll 1$ | $\beta \gg 1$ |
| Frequency deviation $\Delta f$ | Small | Large |
| Bandwidth | $\approx 2 f_m$ (same as AM) | $2(\beta + 1) f_m$ (Carson's Rule) |
| Sidebands | Carrier + 1 pair (2 sidebands) | Carrier + infinite sideband pairs |
| Mathematical tools | Trigonometric approximation | Bessel functions $J_n(\beta)$ |
| Noise immunity | Poor (similar to AM) | Excellent |
| Application | Mobile comms, 2-way radio | FM broadcasting (88–108 MHz) |
| Spectrum envelope | Two delta-like impulses | Spreading Bessel-shaped lobes |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 General FM Expression for Single-Tone Modulation

Let the message signal be a single-tone sinusoid:

$$m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$$

Substituting into the general FM equation:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos\!\left[2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{0}^{t} A_m \cos(2\pi f_m \tau)\, d\tau\right]$$

Integrating the message:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos\!\left[2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)\right]$$

where $\beta = \dfrac{k_f A_m}{f_m}$ is the modulation index.

This is the **master equation** that we will specialize for NBFM and WBFM.

## 2.2 Narrowband FM (NBFM) — Derivation Logic

**Why NBFM exists:** When $\beta$ is small, the term $\beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)$ is a small angle. Using the small-angle approximation:

$$\cos(\theta + \phi) \approx \cos\theta - \phi\sin\theta \quad \text{when } \phi \ll 1$$

Applying this with $\theta = 2\pi f_c t$ and $\phi = \beta\sin(2\pi f_m t)$:

$$s_{NBFM}(t) \approx A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) - \beta A_c \sin(2\pi f_c t)\sin(2\pi f_m t)$$

Using the trigonometric identity $2\sin A \sin B = \cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B)$:

$$s_{NBFM}(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) - \frac{\beta A_c}{2}\cos\!\big[2\pi(f_c - f_m)t\big] + \frac{\beta A_c}{2}\cos\!\big[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t\big]$$

### Key Observations of NBFM Spectrum

- **Carrier component**: $A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ — same as unmodulated carrier.
- **Lower Sideband (LSB)**: at frequency $f_c - f_m$ with amplitude $\beta A_c / 2$.
- **Upper Sideband (USB)**: at frequency $f_c + f_m$ with amplitude $\beta A_c / 2$.
- **Phase reversal** of LSB (negative sign) — this is the *signature* that distinguishes FM from AM.
- **Bandwidth**: $BW_{NBFM} = 2 f_m$ (same as conventional AM).

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Insight for KTU:** NBFM has the same bandwidth as AM, but the lower sideband is *phase-inverted*. This makes FM detection by a simple envelope detector impossible — a discriminator or phase-locked loop is needed.

## 2.3 Wideband FM (WBFM) — Derivation Logic

**Why Bessel functions appear:** For $\beta$ not small, the term $\beta\sin(2\pi f_m t)$ is large, and the small-angle approximation breaks down. We must use the **exact trigonometric identity**:

$$\cos(A + B) = \cos A \cos B - \sin A \sin B$$

Expanding $\cos[\beta\sin(2\pi f_m t)]$ and $\sin[\beta\sin(2\pi f_m t)]$ into Fourier series yields **Bessel functions of the first kind**, $J_n(\beta)$.

The WBFM signal becomes:

$$s_{WBFM}(t) = A_c \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta)\, \cos\!\big[2\pi(f_c + n f_m) t\big]$$

Using the symmetry property $J_{-n}(\beta) = (-1)^n J_n(\beta)$, this can be written as a real signal:

$$s_{WBFM}(t) = A_c \left\{ J_0(\beta)\cos(2\pi f_c t) + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} J_n(\beta)\Big[\cos 2\pi(f_c + n f_m)t + (-1)^n \cos 2\pi(f_c - n f_m)t\Big]\right\}$$

### WBFM Spectrum Properties

- **Carrier amplitude** $= A_c J_0(\beta)$ — *not* always equal to $A_c$ (unlike AM).
- **Sidebands** at frequencies $f_c \pm n f_m$ with amplitudes $A_c J_n(\beta)$ for $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$
- **Infinite number** of sidebands theoretically, but practically we ignore those with $J_n(\beta) < 0.01$ (i.e., $n > \beta + 1$).
- **Power conservation**: The total power $\frac{A_c^2}{2}$ is constant regardless of $\beta$.

## 2.4 Carson's Rule for Bandwidth

The bandwidth that contains approximately **98% of the total signal power** is given by:

$$BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2(\beta + 1)f_m$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carson's Rule — KTU Favourite Formula:**
> $BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$ is used in EVERY numerical problem. The 98% power criterion comes from neglecting all Bessel components with $|J_n(\beta)| < 0.04$.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a comprehensive, exam-ready reference. **Memorize every entry** before the test.

| Formula | Expression | Physical Meaning | Units / Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Modulation Index | $\beta = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \dfrac{k_f A_m}{f_m}$ | Strength of modulation | Dimensionless |
| Frequency Deviation | $\Delta f = k_f A_m$ | Max swing of instantaneous freq | **Hz** |
| NBFM Approximation | $s(t) \approx A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) - \beta A_c \sin(2\pi f_c t)\sin(2\pi f_m t)$ | Small-angle expansion | Valid when $\beta \ll 1$ |
| NBFM Bandwidth | $BW = 2 f_m$ | Same as conventional AM | Hz |
| WBFM (Bessel form) | $s(t) = A_c \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta) \cos[2\pi(f_c + n f_m)t]$ | Exact WBFM expression | Valid for all $\beta$ |
| Carrier Component | $J_0(\beta)$ | Amplitude scaling of carrier | Dimensionless |
| Sideband Amplitude | $J_n(\beta)$ | Amplitude of nth-order sideband | Dimensionless |
| Carson's Rule BW | $BW = 2(\beta + 1) f_m$ | 98% power bandwidth | Hz |
| Total FM Power | $P_T = \dfrac{A_c^2}{2} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n^2(\beta) = \dfrac{A_c^2}{2}$ | Constant (power conservation) | Watts |
| Carrier Vanishing | $\beta = 2.405, 5.52, 8.65, \ldots$ | Roots of $J_0(\beta)$ | Dimensionless |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of NBFM vs WBFM

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Applications at a Glance:**
> - **NBFM** is used in **two-way mobile radio communication**, where bandwidth conservation is critical. The audio quality is acceptable but noise immunity is not the best.
> - **WBFM** is used in **FM radio broadcasting (88–108 MHz)** because it offers excellent noise immunity and high-fidelity audio, at the cost of larger bandwidth.
> - **TV audio** uses WBFM (4.5 MHz deviation, 25 kHz message, so $\beta \approx 180$).
> - The fact that $\beta$ can be *engineered to control bandwidth vs noise tradeoff* is why FM replaced AM in broadcasting.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of NBFM Signal

We start from the single-tone FM expression:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos\!\big[2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)\big]$$

**Step 1:** Recognize that for NBFM, $\beta \ll 1$, so the angle $\beta\sin(2\pi f_m t)$ is always small.

**Step 2:** Apply the small-angle trigonometric identity:

$$\cos(A + \phi) = \cos A \cos\phi - \sin A \sin\phi$$

Setting $A = 2\pi f_c t$ and $\phi = \beta\sin(2\pi f_m t)$:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)\cos\!\big[\beta\sin(2\pi f_m t)\big] - A_c \sin(2\pi f_c t)\sin\!\big[\beta\sin(2\pi f_m t)\big]$$

**Step 3:** Use small-angle approximations $\cos x \approx 1$ and $\sin x \approx x$ for $x \ll 1$:

$$\cos\!\big[\beta\sin(2\pi f_m t)\big] \approx 1$$

$$\sin\!\big[\beta\sin(2\pi f_m t)\big] \approx \beta\sin(2\pi f_m t)$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the approximations back:

\begin{aligned}
s_{NBFM}(t) &\approx A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)\cdot 1 - A_c \sin(2\pi f_c t)\cdot \beta\sin(2\pi f_m t) \\
&= A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) - \beta A_c \sin(2\pi f_c t)\sin(2\pi f_m t)
\end{aligned}

**Step 5:** Apply the product-to-sum identity $2\sin X \sin Y = \cos(X-Y) - \cos(X+Y)$:

With $X = 2\pi f_c t$ and $Y = 2\pi f_m t$:

\begin{aligned}
s_{NBFM}(t) &= A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) - \frac{\beta A_c}{2}\Big[\cos\!\big(2\pi f_c t - 2\pi f_m t\big) - \cos\!\big(2\pi f_c t + 2\pi f_m t\big)\Big] \\
&= A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) - \frac{\beta A_c}{2}\cos\!\big[2\pi(f_c - f_m)t\big] + \frac{\beta A_c}{2}\cos\!\big[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t\big]
\end{aligned}

**Step 6 — Interpretation:** This shows NBFM contains exactly three spectral components:
- Carrier at $f_c$ with amplitude $A_c$
- LSB at $f_c - f_m$ with amplitude $\beta A_c / 2$ and a **negative sign (phase inversion)**
- USB at $f_c + f_m$ with amplitude $\beta A_c / 2$

**Step 7 — Bandwidth:** The highest frequency is $f_c + f_m$ and lowest is $f_c - f_m$, so:

$$BW_{NBFM} = (f_c + f_m) - (f_c - f_m) = 2 f_m$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of WBFM Spectrum (Bessel Function Form)

We start again from the general FM expression:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos\!\big[2\pi f_c t + \beta\sin(2\pi f_m t)\big]$$

**Step 1:** Use the identity $\cos(A+B) = \cos A \cos B - \sin A \sin B$:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)\cos\!\big[\beta\sin(2\pi f_m t)\big] - A_c \sin(2\pi f_c t)\sin\!\big[\beta\sin(2\pi f_m t)\big]$$

**Step 2:** The two functions $\cos[\beta\sin(2\pi f_m t)]$ and $\sin[\beta\sin(2\pi f_m t)]$ are **periodic** in $t$ with period $T = 1/f_m$. Hence they admit Fourier series expansions.

**Step 3:** By standard Fourier–Bessel theory, we can write:

$$\cos\!\big[\beta\sin(2\pi f_m t)\big] = J_0(\beta) + 2\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} J_{2n}(\beta)\cos\!\big[2\pi(2n f_m) t\big]$$

$$\sin\!\big[\beta\sin(2\pi f_m t)\big] = 2\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} J_{2n+1}(\beta)\sin\!\big[2\pi(2n+1) f_m t\big]$$

**Step 4:** Substituting into $s(t)$ and using trigonometric identities, after simplification (which is standard textbook work) we obtain the **Bessel function expansion**:

$$s_{WBFM}(t) = A_c \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} J_n(\beta)\, \cos\!\big[2\pi(f_c + n f_m) t\big]$$

**Step 5:** Separate the $n=0$ term (carrier) and the positive/negative $n$ terms (sidebands). Using $J_{-n}(\beta) = (-1)^n J_n(\beta)$:

$$s_{WBFM}(t) = A_c J_0(\beta)\cos(2\pi f_c t) + A_c \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} J_n(\beta)\cos\!\big[2\pi(f_c + n f_m)t\big] + A_c \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} J_{-n}(\beta)\cos\!\big[2\pi(f_c - n f_m)t\big]$$

\begin{aligned}
s_{WBFM}(t) = A_c J_0(\beta)\cos(2\pi f_c t) &+ A_c \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} J_n(\beta)\cos\!\big[2\pi(f_c + n f_m)t\big] \\
&+ A_c \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} (-1)^n J_n(\beta)\cos\!\big[2\pi(f_c - n f_m)t\big]
\end{aligned}

## 3.3 Derivation of Carson's Rule (98% Power Bandwidth)

The total power in the FM signal is:

$$P_T = \frac{A_c^2}{2} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n^2(\beta) = \frac{A_c^2}{2}$$

(The Bessel function identity $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n^2(\beta) = 1$ ensures power conservation.)

**Step 1:** The fraction of power contained in frequencies $|f - f_c| \leq N f_m$ is:

$$\eta_N = \frac{\sum_{n=-N}^{N} J_n^2(\beta)}{\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n^2(\beta)} = \sum_{n=-N}^{N} J_n^2(\beta)$$

**Step 2:** For large $\beta$, Bessel functions $J_n(\beta)$ become negligible when $n > \beta + 1$. Empirical observation shows that including all sidebands up to $n = \beta + 1$ captures approximately 98% of the total power.

**Step 3:** The number of significant sideband pairs is $(\beta + 1)$ on each side of the carrier.

**Step 4:** The highest significant frequency is $f_c + (\beta + 1)f_m$ and the lowest is $f_c - (\beta + 1)f_m$.

**Step 5:** Therefore, the bandwidth:

$$BW = 2(\beta + 1) f_m = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$$

This is **Carson's Rule**.

## 3.4 Numerical Problem Walkthrough — KTU Standard Pattern

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Pattern (KTU Frequently Repeated):**
> An FM signal has $A_c = 10$ V, $f_c = 100$ MHz, message amplitude $A_m = 4$ V, message frequency $f_m = 500$ Hz, and $k_f = 25$ kHz/V. Determine (i) $\beta$, (ii) $\Delta f$, (iii) Carson's BW, and (iv) significant sidebands.

### Solution

**Given:**
- $A_c = 10$ V, $f_c = 100$ MHz, $A_m = 4$ V, $f_m = 500$ Hz, $k_f = 25{,}000$ Hz/V

**(i) Frequency Deviation:**

$$\Delta f = k_f \cdot A_m = 25{,}000 \times 4 = 100{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 100 \text{ kHz}$$

**(ii) Modulation Index:**

$$\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \frac{100{,}000}{500} = 200$$

Since $\beta = 200 \gg 1$, this is **WBFM**.

**(iii) Carson's Rule Bandwidth:**

$$BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2(100{,}000 + 500) = 201{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 201 \text{ kHz}$$

**(iv) Significant Sidebands:** Significant sidebands exist for $n = 1, 2, \ldots, (\beta + 1) = 201$. So there are **201 pairs** of significant sidebands.

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | NBFM | WBFM |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Condition on $\beta$ | $\beta < 0.5$ (typically $\ll 1$) | $\beta > 1$ (typically $\gg 1$) |
| Frequency spectrum | 1 carrier + 1 sideband pair | 1 carrier + many sideband pairs |
| Mathematical form | Linear (small-angle approx.) | Nonlinear (Bessel functions) |
| Bandwidth formula | $2 f_m$ | $2(\beta + 1) f_m$ |
| Sideband amplitude | $\beta A_c / 2$ (linearly growing) | $A_c J_n(\beta)$ (oscillatory Bessel) |
| Carrier amplitude | Constant $A_c$ | $A_c J_0(\beta)$ — varies with $\beta$ |
| Noise immunity | Same as AM | Excellent |
| Detection method | FM discriminator / PLL | FM discriminator / PLL |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FM Signal Generation and Spectrum Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MSG["Message Signal m(t) = Am cos 2πfmt"] --> INT["Integrator 1 over s m s ds"]
    INT --> KFM["Multiplier kf - Frequency Sensitivity"]
    KFM --> VCO["VCO - Voltage Controlled Oscillator"]
    VCO --> FMOP["FM Output s t = Ac cos 2πfct + β sin 2πfmt"]
    CAR["Carrier Oscillator fc"] --> VCO
```

**Description:** The message signal is integrated (since FM is integration of message), scaled by frequency sensitivity $k_f$, and used to vary the VCO frequency around $f_c$, producing the FM output.

## 4.2 NBFM vs WBFM Spectrum Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["FM Signal s t = Ac cos 2πfct + β sin 2πfmt"]
    START --> CLASSIFY{"Compare β to 1"}
    CLASSIFY -- "β is much less than 1" --> NBFM["Narrowband FM"]
    CLASSIFY -- "β is much greater than 1" --> WBFM["Wideband FM"]
    NBFM --> NBSPECTRUM["3 spectral lines - Carrier fc, LSB fc minus fm, USB fc plus fm - Bandwidth 2fm"]
    WBFM --> WBSPECTRUM["Infinite Bessel-weighted sidebands - Bandwidth 2 β plus 1 fm - via Carson Rule"]
```

## 4.3 Bessel Function Spectrum Anatomy (WBFM)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "WBFM Spectrum for given β"
        CAR["Carrier at fc - Amplitude J0 β × Ac"]
        SB1["Sidebands n=1 at fc ± fm - Amplitude J1 β × Ac"]
        SB2["Sidebands n=2 at fc ± 2fm - Amplitude J2 β × Ac"]
        SB3["Sidebands n=3 at fc ± 3fm - Amplitude J3 β × Ac"]
        SBN["Sidebands n=N at fc ± Nfm - Amplitude JN β × Ac - negligible when N greater than β + 1"]
    end
    CAR --> SB1 --> SB2 --> SB3 --> SBN
```

**Description:** The WBFM spectrum consists of a carrier scaled by $J_0(\beta)$ and an infinite series of sideband pairs. Only sidebands up to $n \approx \beta + 1$ are significant.

## 4.4 Power Distribution and Carson's Rule Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TOTAL["Total FM Power = Ac squared over 2 - Constant for all β"]
    CARRIER["Carrier Power = Ac squared over 2 times J0 squared β"]
    SB["Sideband Power = Ac squared over 2 times sum of all Jn squared β - n from 1 to infinity"]
    CONS["Power Conservation - Carrier Power + Sideband Power = Total Power - Always true"]
    CARSON["Carsons Rule BW = 2 β + 1 fm contains approx 98 percent of total power"]
    TOTAL --> CARRIER
    TOTAL --> SB
    CARRIER --> CONS
    SB --> CONS
    CONS --> CARSON
```

## 4.5 NBFM Spectrum Diagram (Conceptual Visualization)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Frequency Axis"
        LSB["Frequency fc minus fm - Amplitude β Ac over 2 - PHASE INVERTED"]
        CARR["Frequency fc - Amplitude Ac"]
        USB["Frequency fc plus fm - Amplitude β Ac over 2 - in phase"]
    end
    LSB --> CARR --> USB
    SBW["Total NBFM Bandwidth = 2 fm - SAME AS AM"]
    USB -.-> SBW
```

> [!NOTE]
> **The phase inversion of the LSB** is the spectral fingerprint that distinguishes NBFM from conventional AM. This is a critical visual difference that KTU examiners often test.

---
