<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define pre-emphasis and de-emphasis in FM systems. Why is the time constant chosen as 75 µs?*

**Model Answer:**

**Pre-emphasis** is a signal processing technique applied at the FM transmitter that **artificially boosts the high-frequency components** of the message signal (above a corner frequency $f_1$) before modulation. It is implemented as a high-pass filter with transfer function:

$$H_{pe}(f) = \frac{j(f/f_1)}{1 + j(f/f_1)}, \quad f_1 = \frac{1}{2\pi \tau}$$

**De-emphasis** is the complementary low-pass filter applied at the receiver **after FM demodulation**, with transfer function:

$$H_{de}(f) = \frac{1}{1 + j(f/f_1)}$$

It **attenuates the high-frequency components** that were boosted by pre-emphasis, restoring the original message spectrum while simultaneously suppressing the high-frequency noise that is dominant in FM systems.

**Why $\tau = 75\ \mu s$?**
The 75 µs time constant yields a corner frequency $f_1 = \frac{1}{2\pi(75 \times 10^{-6})} \approx 2122$ Hz. This value is **standardized internationally** for commercial FM broadcasting because it optimally balances two competing requirements:
1. It places the corner above the dominant energy range of typical audio program material (which lies below 1 kHz) so that the message is not distorted.
2. It is low enough that most of the audio spectrum benefits from noise reduction.

The CCIR (now ITU-R) standardized 75 µs for the Americas and 50 µs for Europe — KTU problems use 75 µs unless specified.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain the noise performance improvement achieved by pre-emphasis and de-emphasis filtering in FM systems.*

**Model Answer:**

The **output noise PSD** at an FM discriminator is parabolic: $S_{n_o}(f) \propto f^2$. This means the high-frequency portion of the message band suffers from disproportionately high noise. Pre-emphasis boosts these high frequencies at the transmitter, while the de-emphasis at the receiver attenuates them back, but in doing so **also attenuates the high-frequency noise** that was added during transmission.

The cascaded effect suppresses the noise spectrum according to:

$$S_{n_o}'(f) = S_{n_o}(f) \cdot \vert H_{de}(f) \vert^2 = \frac{8\pi^2 k_f^2 N_0}{A_c^2} \cdot \frac{f^2}{1 + (f/f_1)^2}$$

The result is that the integrated noise power is reduced. For typical broadcast audio material, the **SNR improvement is approximately 13 dB** (or about 22 times in linear power). This improvement is achieved **without any increase in transmitted bandwidth** — a remarkable engineering trade-off unique to FM.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Path 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the transfer function of the pre-emphasis network used in FM broadcast systems. Show that it is a high-pass filter with a corner frequency $f_1 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$. State the standard value of $RC$ used in commercial FM and justify the choice. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the transfer function of the corresponding de-emphasis network and show that the cascaded response of pre-emphasis and de-emphasis networks is approximately unity at high frequencies. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Pre-emphasis Network Topology:** A series capacitor $C$ followed by a shunt resistor $R$ to ground. The input voltage $V_{in}$ is applied across the series $C$–$R$ chain, and the output $V_{out}$ is taken across $R$.

**Step 1 — Apply the voltage divider rule:**

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R}{R + \frac{1}{sC}}$$

**Step 2 — Algebraic simplification:**

$$H_{pe}(s) = \frac{sRC}{1 + sRC} = \frac{s\tau}{1 + s\tau}, \quad \tau = RC$$

**Step 3 — Frequency domain substitution $s = j2\pi f$:**

$$H_{pe}(f) = \frac{j2\pi f RC}{1 + j2\pi f RC}$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude and corner frequency identification:**

$$\vert H_{pe}(f) \vert = \frac{2\pi f RC}{\sqrt{1 + (2\pi f RC)^2}}$$

Setting $\vert H_{pe}(f_1) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ gives $2\pi f_1 RC = 1$, so:

$$f_1 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

**Step 5 — Normalized form:**

$$H_{pe}(f) = \frac{j(f/f_1)}{1 + j(f/f_1)}$$

This is a **high-pass response**: zero gain at DC, asymptotic gain of 1 for $f \gg f_1$, and $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ at the corner $f_1$.

**Standard Value:** $RC = \tau = 75\ \mu s$, giving $f_1 \approx 2122$ Hz.

**Justification:** This value is internationally standardized (CCIR/FCC) for FM broadcasting. It optimally balances:
- Avoiding distortion of the message (corner placed above dominant speech/music energy).
- Maximizing noise suppression in the HF region where FM noise is worst.
- Practical component values that are manufacturable and stable with temperature.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**De-emphasis Network Topology:** A series resistor $R$ followed by a shunt capacitor $C$ to ground. Output is taken across $C$.

**Step 1 — Voltage divider:**

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{\frac{1}{sC}}{R + \frac{1}{sC}} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + sRC}$$

**Step 2 — Transfer function:**

$$H_{de}(s) = \frac{1}{1 + s\tau}, \quad H_{de}(f) = \frac{1}{1 + j(f/f_1)}$$

This is a **first-order low-pass filter** with DC gain of 1 and asymptotic roll-off of $-20$ dB/decade for $f \gg f_1$.

**Step 3 — Cascade of pre-emphasis and de-emphasis:**

$$H_{pe}(f) \cdot H_{de}(f) = \frac{j(f/f_1)}{1 + j(f/f_1)} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + j(f/f_1)} = \frac{j(f/f_1)}{[1 + j(f/f_1)]^2}$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude of the cascade:**

$$\vert H_{pe}(f) \cdot H_{de}(f) \vert = \frac{f/f_1}{1 + (f/f_1)^2}$$

**Step 5 — Asymptotic behavior at high frequencies** ($f \gg f_1$):

$$\lim_{f \to \infty} \frac{f/f_1}{1 + (f/f_1)^2} = \lim_{f \to \infty} \frac{f/f_1}{(f/f_1)^2} = \lim_{f \to \infty} \frac{1}{f/f_1} = 0$$

Wait — this shows the cascade actually **rolls off at high frequencies**, not unity. To obtain unity at high frequencies, the *true* de-emphasis must include a zero at the origin to invert the pre-emphasis zero exactly. In practical implementations, the pre-emphasis is approximated as $H_{pe} \approx 1 + j(f/f_1)$ (with DC gain of 1) and the de-emphasis is $H_{de} = \frac{1}{1 + j(f/f_1)}$. The cascade is then:

$$H_{pe}(f) \cdot H_{de}(f) = [1 + j(f/f_1)] \cdot \frac{1}{1 + j(f/f_1)} = 1 \quad \text{(exactly)}$$

This is the **idealized** model. In this case the cascade is exactly unity for all frequencies. (The actual physical network has a slight deviation, but for engineering purposes the cascade is considered flat.)

> **Reconciliation:** The two formulations correspond to two different physical networks. The "ideal lead-lag" form yields exact unity cascade. The "RC integrator" form (covered in Section 3.1) yields a non-flat cascade with peak loss at $f_1$. The exact form depends on the textbook convention used in your reference. For KTU, the **idealized unity cascade** is the expected answer.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Path 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain why the output noise power spectral density of an FM discriminator is triangular (i.e., proportional to $f^2$). Why does this necessitate pre-emphasis and de-emphasis? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an FM system with $\tau = 75\ \mu s$ and message bandwidth $W = 15$ kHz, calculate the corner frequency and explain qualitatively how the SNR is improved. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Why FM Noise PSD is Triangular:**

The FM demodulator (frequency discriminator) operates by computing the **derivative** of the input signal's phase to extract instantaneous frequency. Mathematically, the discriminator's transfer function is proportional to $f$:

$$H_{disc}(f) = j2\pi f \cdot k_d$$

where $k_d$ is the discriminator constant. When input AWGN with constant two-sided PSD $N_0/2$ passes through this differentiator, the output noise PSD is:

$$S_{n_o}(f) = \frac{N_0}{2} \cdot \vert j2\pi f k_d \vert^2 = 2\pi^2 k_d^2 N_0 f^2$$

This **parabolic growth with $f^2$** is the origin of the "triangular noise spectrum" — the noise power density doubles for each octave of frequency.

**Consequence:** The high-frequency components of the demodulated message are corrupted by disproportionately large noise, while the low-frequency components experience less noise. The **SNR is therefore non-uniform across the message band**, with HF regions being noisy.

**Why Pre-emphasis / De-emphasis?**

To equalize the SNR across the message band:
- **Pre-emphasis** at the TX boosts HF message components *before* modulation, ensuring that the message has more energy where the noise will be severe.
- **De-emphasis** at the RX attenuates these same HF components back to their original level after demodulation, *simultaneously suppressing the HF noise* that entered during transmission.

The net effect: the **message is faithfully restored**, but the integrated noise power is significantly reduced (by approximately 13 dB for typical audio).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Step 1 — Compute the corner frequency:**

$$f_1 = \frac{1}{2\pi \tau} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 75 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{10^6}{2\pi \times 75}$$

$$f_1 = \frac{10^6}{471.24} \approx 2122\ \text{Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the ratio $W / f_1$:**

$$\frac{W}{f_1} = \frac{15000}{2122} \approx 7.07$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the de-weighted noise integral:**

The de-emphasis reduces the noise PSD from $\propto f^2$ to $\propto \frac{f^2}{1 + (f/f_1)^2}$. At low frequencies ($f \ll f_1$), the response is unchanged (still grows as $f^2$). At high frequencies ($f \gg f_1$), the response rolls off as a constant (the $f^2$ in numerator cancels the $f^2$ in denominator), giving a **bounded** noise PSD.

**Step 4 — Compute the noise power reduction qualitatively:**

The integrated noise power with de-emphasis:

$$N_o' = \frac{16\pi^2 k_f^2 N_0}{A_c^2} \left[ f_1 W - f_1^2 \tan^{-1}(W/f_1) \right]$$

$$N_o' \propto 2122 \times 15000 - (2122)^2 \times \tan^{-1}(7.07)$$

$$N_o' \propto 3.183 \times 10^7 - 4.503 \times 10^6 \times 1.430 \propto 2.54 \times 10^7$$

(All in arbitrary units scaled by the constant $\frac{16\pi^2 k_f^2 N_0}{A_c^2}$.)

**Step 5 — Compute the SNR improvement:**

Without pre-emphasis, $N_o \propto \frac{16\pi^2 k_f^2 N_0}{A_c^2} \cdot \frac{2W^3}{3}$. For $W = 15$ kHz:

$$N_o \propto \frac{2 \times (15000)^3}{3} = 2.25 \times 10^{12}$$

The improvement factor:

$$I = \frac{N_o}{N_o'} = \frac{2.25 \times 10^{12}}{2.54 \times 10^7} \times \text{(common scale)} \approx \text{much greater than 1}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Mistaking the topology** — A common error is drawing the pre-emphasis network as a low-pass filter (series R, shunt C). The correct topology for *boosting HF* is **series C, shunt R** for the lead network, or equivalently the **idealized form** $H_{pe} = 1 + j(f/f_1)$. Conflating the two is a guaranteed 2–3 mark deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting the squared magnitude** — When computing noise power from PSD, students often integrate $\frac{f^2}{1+(f/f_1)^2}$ without squaring $\vert H_{de} \vert$. The correct factor is $\vert H_{de} \vert^2 = \frac{1}{1+(f/f_1)^2}$.
> 3. **Mixing up 75 µs vs 50 µs** — KTU default is 75 µs. If a problem states "European standard" then use 50 µs. Always read the problem statement carefully.
> 4. **Cascade mistake** — Students frequently claim the cascade is *exactly* unity. In the practical RC realization, the cascade has a $-6$ dB peak loss at $f_1$. In the idealized lead-lag form, the cascade is exactly unity. **Know which form your reference uses.**
> 5. **Missing the noise PSD derivation** — "Triangular noise" is a direct consequence of differentiation; students often skip this justification and lose 2 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- [x] **Core Reason:** FM discriminator output noise PSD grows as $f^2$ (parabolic/triangular). HF components of the message are masked by noise.
- [x] **Pre-emphasis** is a **high-pass filter** placed *before* FM modulation at the TX. It boosts high frequencies of the message.
- [x] **De-emphasis** is a **low-pass filter** placed *after* FM demodulation at the RX. It restores the message spectrum while suppressing HF noise.
- [x] **Standard time constant:** $\tau = RC = 75\ \mu s$ (international FM broadcast standard).
- [x] **Corner frequency:** $f_1 = \frac{1}{2\pi \tau} \approx 2122$ Hz.
- [x] **Pre-emphasis TF:** $H_{pe}(f) = \frac{j(f/f_1)}{1 + j(f/f_1)}$ (RC realization) OR $H_{pe}(f) = 1 + j(f/f_1)$ (idealized lead).
- [x] **De-emphasis TF:** $H_{de}(f) = \frac{1}{1 + j(f/f_1)}$.
- [x] **Cascade** (idealized form): $H_{pe}(f) \cdot H_{de}(f) = 1$ — message is faithfully restored.
- [x] **SNR Improvement:** Approximately **13 dB** (≈22× linear) for typical audio program material.
- [x] **Realized without bandwidth increase** — pure SNR trade-off.
- [x] **Noise integration trick:** $\int \frac{u^2}{1+u^2} du = u - \tan^{-1}(u) + C$.
- [x] **50 µs alternative** for European/Indian CCIR systems (verify with problem statement).
- [x] **KTU Bloom's Level:** Application (derivation + design calculation) and Analysis (noise PSD justification) — practice numerical problems from Module 1 to gain full marks.

> **Final Note:** Pre-emphasis and de-emphasis are the *unsung heroes* of FM broadcasting. They are a brilliant example of how understanding the noise structure of a modulation scheme allows engineers to engineer *the signal itself* to fight noise — a technique with no analog in AM systems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison of AM and FM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT502 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Comparison of AM and FM**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Amplitude Modulation (AM)** is a continuous-wave modulation technique in which the **amplitude** of a high-frequency sinusoidal carrier signal is varied in direct proportion to the instantaneous amplitude of the low-frequency message (baseband) signal, while the **frequency** and **phase** of the carrier remain constant.

$$s_{AM}(t) = A_c\left[1 + k_a\, m(t)\right]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Frequency Modulation (FM)** is a continuous-wave modulation technique in which the **instantaneous frequency** of the high-frequency sinusoidal carrier signal is varied in direct proportion to the instantaneous amplitude of the low-frequency message signal, while the **amplitude** of the carrier remains constant.

$$s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** AM and FM are classified as **Angle Modulation Techniques** under the parent category of *Continuous-Wave (CW) Modulation*. AM is a **linear** modulation scheme, whereas FM is a **non-linear** modulation scheme. This classification is heavily tested in Part A (3-mark) questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ocean Waves" Analogy

Imagine you are standing on a beach watching ocean waves roll in:

- **Amplitude Modulation (AM)** is like a surfer **changing the HEIGHT of each wave** as it passes by — short waves, tall waves, short waves — but the waves still arrive at the same regular time interval. Here, the *height* carries your information, and the *timing* (frequency) stays fixed.
- **Frequency Modulation (FM)** is like the surfer **changing how QUICKLY the waves arrive** — bunched-up waves, spread-out waves, bunched-up waves — but every wave is exactly the same height. Here, the *spacing* (frequency) carries your information, and the *height* (amplitude) stays rock-steady.

In radio broadcasting, **AM radio** (Medium Wave 540–1700 kHz) varies the wave-height to encode voice/music, while **FM radio** (VHF 88–108 MHz) varies the wave-spacing — and the constant-amplitude nature of FM is precisely why FM is so much better at rejecting lightning static and electrical noise (which are mostly amplitude disturbances!).

> [!NOTE]
> Standard Metric: AM broadcast operates at carrier frequencies of **540 kHz to 1700 kHz**; FM broadcast operates at **88 MHz to 108 MHz** with a channel spacing of **200 kHz** and a maximum frequency deviation of **±75 kHz**.

## 1.3 The Modulation Index — The "Sensitivity Knob"

The **modulation index** quantifies "how much" the parameter is varied relative to its unmodulated value:

- For **AM**: $\mu = \dfrac{A_m}{A_c} = k_a A_m$  (also called *modulation depth*, expressed as a percentage)
- For **FM**: $\beta = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \dfrac{k_f A_m}{f_m}$  (dimensionless)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In AM, $\mu$ is **constrained to $\leq 1$** to avoid envelope distortion (overmodulation). In FM, $\beta$ can be **any non-negative value** — there is **no overmodulation** because only frequency (not amplitude) varies.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

### AM Generation Logic
1. Start with carrier $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and message $m(t)$ (band-limited to $f_m$ Hz).
2. Add a **DC offset** of 1 to the normalized message: $[1 + k_a m(t)]$ ensures the envelope is always positive.
3. Multiply by the carrier → output is a high-frequency sinusoid whose **envelope** (outline) traces the message.
4. Detection: A simple **envelope detector** (diode + RC) recovers the message — extremely cheap receiver.

### FM Generation Logic
1. Start with carrier $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$.
2. Differentiate the message: $m'(t) = \dfrac{dm(t)}{dt}$.
3. The instantaneous frequency deviation is $\Delta f(t) = k_f\, m(t)$.
4. Integrate $k_f m(t)$ to obtain the **phase deviation**: $\phi(t) = 2\pi k_f \int m(\tau)\, d\tau$.
5. Frequency is varied; **amplitude remains constant at $A_c$** — noise spikes that change amplitude are rejected.
6. Detection: Requires a **frequency discriminator** or **Phase-Locked Loop (PLL)** — more complex and expensive receiver.

## 2.2 The Core "Why" — Noise Immunity of FM

Electrical noise and atmospheric disturbances are predominantly **amplitude perturbations**. Because an FM receiver contains a **limiter** that strips off all amplitude variations *before* demodulation, these disturbances are discarded automatically. AM has no such limiter — any amplitude noise rides straight through to the loudspeaker.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most important reason** FM dominates high-fidelity broadcasting (VHF) and two-way radio, while AM dominates cheap long-range talk radio (MW/SW).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | AM Expression | FM Expression |
|---|-----------|---------------|---------------|
| 1 | Carrier equation | $A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | $A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ |
| 2 | Modulated signal | $A_c[1+\mu m_n(t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | $A_c\cos\!\big(2\pi f_c t + \beta\sin(2\pi f_m t)\big)$ |
| 3 | Parameter varied | **Amplitude** $A_c$ | **Instantaneous frequency** $f_i$ |
| 4 | Modulation index | $\mu = A_m/A_c$ | $\beta = \Delta f / f_m$ |
| 5 | Frequency deviation | None | $\Delta f = k_f A_m$ |
| 6 | **Bandwidth (ideal)** | $BW = 2 f_m$ | $BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2 f_m(\beta + 1)$ |
| 7 | **Carson's Rule** | Not applicable | $BW \approx 2(\Delta f + f_m)$ |
| 8 | Total transmitted power | $P_t = P_c\!\left(1 + \dfrac{\mu^2}{2}\right)$ | $P_t = \dfrac{A_c^2}{2R} = P_c$ (constant!) |
| 9 | Carrier power $P_c$ | $\dfrac{A_c^2}{2R}$ | $\dfrac{A_c^2}{2R}$ |
| 10 | Sideband power $P_{SB}$ | $\dfrac{A_c^2 \mu^2}{4R} = P_c \dfrac{\mu^2}{2}$ | Distributed in infinite Bessel sidebands |
| 11 | Sidebands | 1 USB + 1 LSB (2 total) | Infinite (theoretical); ~98% in $(\beta+1)$ pairs |
| 12 | Noise performance (SNR) | SNR_out $\propto \mu^2$ (poor) | SNR_out $\propto 3\beta^2(\beta+1)$ (excellent) |
| 13 | Capture effect | Absent | **Present** (stronger station dominates) |
| 14 | Receiver complexity | Very low (envelope detector) | Moderate–High (discriminator/PLL) |
| 15 | Channel spacing (broadcast) | 10 kHz | 200 kHz |
| 16 | Frequency band (broadcast) | 540–1700 kHz (MW) | 88–108 MHz (VHF) |
| 17 | Modulation type | **Linear** | **Non-linear** |
| 18 | Information in envelope? | **Yes** | No (envelope is constant) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical exam tip:** AM bandwidth is **independent** of modulation depth. FM bandwidth **grows** with modulation index $\beta$. This is the most common conceptual trap in KTU questions.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **AM** is used in citizen-band (CB) walkie-talkies, aviation VHF communication (118–137 MHz, AM), and long-range maritime/shortwave broadcasting because of its **narrow bandwidth** and **simple receivers**.
- **FM** is used in VHF radio broadcasting, analog TV audio, two-way police/fire/ambulance radios, and the analog audio subcarrier of TV broadcast because of its **superior noise immunity** and **capture effect**.
- **Single-Sideband (SSB-AM)** is a bandwidth-efficient derivative of AM used in HF military and amateur radio.
- **Pre-emphasis and De-emphasis** filters in FM (75 µs in North America, 50 µs in Europe) further boost high-frequency SNR by ~13 dB.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bandwidth of AM (Double-Sideband Full Carrier)

**Given:** Message $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$ modulating carrier $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$.

**Step 1** — Write the AM equation:

$$s(t) = A_c[1 + \mu \cos(2\pi f_m t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 2** — Expand the product (multiply through):

$$s(t) = A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t) + \mu A_c\cos(2\pi f_m t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 3** — Apply the trigonometric product-to-sum identity
$\cos A \cos B = \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$:

$$s(t) = A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t) + \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos\big[2\pi(f_c - f_m)t\big] + \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos\big[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t\big]$$

**Step 4** — Identify the three spectral components:

| Component | Frequency | Amplitude | Interpretation |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|----------------|
| Carrier | $f_c$ | $A_c$ | Unmodulated carrier |
| Lower Sideband (LSB) | $f_c - f_m$ | $\mu A_c/2$ | Lower mirror of message |
| Upper Sideband (USB) | $f_c + f_m$ | $\mu A_c/2$ | Upper mirror of message |

**Step 5** — Compute the bandwidth (highest frequency − lowest frequency):

$$BW_{AM} = (f_c + f_m) - (f_c - f_m) = 2 f_m$$

> [!NOTE]
> Result is **independent of $\mu$** and depends only on the **highest baseband frequency $f_m$**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Carson's Rule for FM Bandwidth

**Given:** Single-tone FM with message $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$ and frequency sensitivity $k_f$ (Hz/V).

**Step 1** — Instantaneous frequency deviation:

$$f_i(t) - f_c = k_f m(t) = k_f A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$$

**Step 2** — Maximum (peak) frequency deviation:

$$\Delta f = k_f A_m \quad \text{(Hz)}$$

**Step 3** — Modulation index for FM:

$$\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \frac{k_f A_m}{f_m}$$

**Step 4** — Using the Bessel-function expansion of $s_{FM}(t)$ with single-tone message:

$$s_{FM}(t) = A_c \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta)\cos\!\big[2\pi(f_c + n f_m)t\big]$$

**Step 5** — Bessel-function identity for power conservation:

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n^2(\beta) = 1$$

**Step 6** — Define Carson's rule: the bandwidth is the range that contains all sidebands whose amplitude is $\geq 1\%$ of the unmodulated carrier (i.e., $J_n(\beta) \geq 0.01$).

**Step 7** — For all valid $\beta$, this cutoff occurs at approximately $n = \beta + 1$ pairs of sidebands:

$$BW_{FM} = 2(\beta + 1)f_m = 2 f_m + 2 \Delta f$$

**Step 8** — Substituting $\Delta f = k_f A_m$:

$$\boxed{BW_{FM} = 2\left(f_m + k_f A_m\right) = 2 f_m(\beta + 1)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For $\beta \ll 1$ (narrowband FM, NBFM): $BW \to 2 f_m$ — *same as AM*. For $\beta \gg 1$ (wideband FM, WBFM): $BW \to 2 \Delta f$ — *much wider than AM*.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Power Distribution in AM

**Step 1** — Total instantaneous power into load $R$:

$$P_t = \frac{1}{R}\langle s^2(t)\rangle$$

**Step 2** — Use the expanded AM signal from §3.1. Cross-terms average to zero (orthogonal cosines at different frequencies):

$$\langle s^2(t)\rangle = \frac{A_c^2}{2} + 2 \cdot \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{4}\cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{A_c^2}{2} + \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{4}$$

**Step 3** — Express in terms of carrier power $P_c = A_c^2/2R$:

$$P_t = P_c\!\left(1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right) = P_c + 2 P_{SB}$$

**Step 4** — Carrier power: $P_c = \dfrac{A_c^2}{2R}$. Sideband power (total USB+LSB): $P_{SB} = \dfrac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{4R} = P_c \dfrac{\mu^2}{2}$.

> [!WARNING]
> For $\mu = 1$ (100% modulation), only **one-third** of total power is in the sidebands carrying information; the remaining **two-thirds** is wasted in the carrier. This is the **fundamental inefficiency of AM** and the reason SSB-AM exists.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Quick-Revision)

| Performance Criterion | Winner | Justification |
|------------------------|--------|----------------|
| Bandwidth efficiency | **AM** | $2f_m$ vs $2f_m(\beta+1)$ |
| Power efficiency | **FM** | All power carries information (constant envelope) |
| Noise immunity | **FM** | Limiter strips amplitude noise; $SNR \propto \beta^2(\beta+1)$ |
| Receiver simplicity | **AM** | Envelope detector (one diode + RC) |
| System complexity | **AM** (simpler) | FM needs discriminator/PLL |
| Cost of transmitter | **AM** (cheaper) | Class-C amplifier OK for AM |
| Cost of receiver | **AM** (cheaper) | FM needs IF stages, discriminator, de-emphasis |
| Susceptibility to fading | **AM** (worse) | No limiter; envelope is corrupted |
| Capture effect | **FM** (only) | Stronger co-channel station captures the receiver |
| Multipath performance | **FM** (better) | Envelope is unaffected by selective fading |
| Audio fidelity (broadcast) | **FM** (15 kHz, hi-fi) | AM audio is limited to ~4.5 kHz (telephony-quality) |
| Long-range propagation | **AM** (better) | MW/SW ground-wave and sky-wave propagation |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define modulation index for AM and FM. State the range of valid values in each case. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **For AM:** The modulation index $\mu$ (also called *modulation depth*) is defined as the ratio of peak amplitude of the message signal to the peak amplitude of the carrier:

$$\mu = \frac{A_m}{A_c} = k_a A_m$$

- **For FM:** The modulation index $\beta$ is the ratio of peak frequency deviation $\Delta f$ to the message frequency $f_m$:

$$\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \frac{k_f A_m}{f_m}$$

- **Valid ranges:** For AM, $0 \leq \mu \leq 1$ (must not exceed 1 to avoid envelope distortion). For FM, $\beta \geq 0$ — there is **no upper limit** and hence no "over-modulation" effect.

*(Full marks: 1 mark for AM definition, 1 mark for FM definition, 1 mark for valid ranges.)*

---

### Question 2: List any three advantages of FM over AM. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Better noise immunity** — FM uses a limiter to strip amplitude noise before demodulation, giving a post-detection SNR improvement of $3\beta^2(\beta+1)$ over AM.
2. **Higher power efficiency** — All transmitted power in FM lies within the constant-amplitude carrier; in AM, up to two-thirds of total power may be wasted in the unmodulated carrier.
3. **Capture effect** — An FM receiver locks onto the stronger of two co-channel stations, eliminating interference; AM suffers from co-channel beat notes.
4. *(Optional bonus 4th point)* Better audio fidelity: FM broadcast supports 15 kHz audio bandwidth versus ~4.5 kHz for AM broadcast.

---

## PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU End-Semester Examination (ESE) Part B questions in Module 1 typically carry **14 marks** with an **internal choice**. The most common 14-mark structure is: **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the AM signal when the message is a single-tone sinusoid $A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$ modulating a carrier $A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$. Sketch the spectrum and determine the transmission bandwidth. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive Carson's rule for the bandwidth of an FM signal. Given $f_m = 5\text{ kHz}$ and $\Delta f = 15\text{ kHz}$, compute the modulation index $\beta$ and the FM bandwidth. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1** — Write the AM time-domain equation with the single-tone message:

$$s_{AM}(t) = A_c[1 + \mu \cos(2\pi f_m t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

where $\mu = A_m / A_c$.

**Step 2** — Expand the product:

$$s_{AM}(t) = A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t) + \mu A_c \cos(2\pi f_m t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 3** — Apply the identity $\cos A \cos B = \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$:

$$s_{AM}(t) = A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t) + \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos\big[2\pi(f_c - f_m)t\big] + \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos\big[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t\big]$$

**Step 4** — Identify the spectrum: three discrete spectral lines at $f_c - f_m$, $f_c$, and $f_c + f_m$ with amplitudes $\mu A_c/2$, $A_c$, and $\mu A_c/2$ respectively.

**Step 5** — Compute the bandwidth:

$$BW_{AM} = (f_c + f_m) - (f_c - f_m) = 2 f_m$$

**Step 6** — Sketch (description for 1 mark): Carrier spike of height $A_c$ at $f_c$, two sideband spikes of height $\mu A_c/2$ symmetrically placed at $f_c \pm f_m$.

*(Mark split: 2 for expansion, 2 for trig identity application, 1 for spectrum identification, 1 for bandwidth, 1 for sketch description.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1** — Express the single-tone FM signal with message $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$:

$$s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + \frac{k_f A_m}{f_m}\sin(2\pi f_m t)\right) = A_c \cos\!\big[2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)\big]$$

**Step 2** — Define the modulation index and peak deviation:

$$\beta = \frac{k_f A_m}{f_m} = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m}$$

**Step 3** — Expand using the Bessel-function identity $\cos(\beta \sin\theta) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta)\cos(n\theta)$:

$$s_{FM}(t) = A_c \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta)\cos\!\big[2\pi(f_c + n f_m)t\big]$$

**Step 4** — State the Bessel power-conservation identity:

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n^2(\beta) = 1$$

**Step 5** — State Carson's rule: bandwidth encompasses all sidebands with amplitude $\geq 1\%$ of unmodulated carrier, which empirically occurs at $n = \beta + 1$:

$$BW_{FM} = 2(\beta + 1) f_m = 2 f_m + 2 \Delta f$$

**Step 6** — Numerical calculation:

Given $f_m = 5\text{ kHz}$ and $\Delta f = 15\text{ kHz}$:

$$\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \frac{15}{5} = 3$$

$$BW_{FM} = 2(\beta + 1)f_m = 2(3+1)(5\text{ kHz}) = 40\text{ kHz}$$

*(Mark split: 2 for FM equation, 2 for Bessel expansion, 1 for Carson's rule statement, 1 for $\beta$ calculation, 1 for BW calculation.)*

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative): `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Compare AM and FM systems on the basis of (i) parameter varied, (ii) bandwidth, (iii) noise immunity, and (iv) transmission power. Derive the AM power expression. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the capture effect in FM? Explain why FM is said to have superior noise performance to AM. Derive the output SNR improvement factor of FM over AM in terms of $\beta$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Criterion | AM | FM |
|-----------|----|----|
| (i) Parameter varied | Amplitude | Instantaneous frequency |
| (ii) Bandwidth | $2 f_m$ (independent of modulation index) | $2 f_m(\beta+1)$ (depends on $\beta$) |
| (iii) Noise immunity | Poor — no limiter | Excellent — limiter strips amplitude noise |
| (iv) Transmission power | $P_t = P_c(1 + \mu^2/2)$ varies with $\mu$ | $P_t = P_c$ (constant, all useful) |

**Derivation of AM power (3 marks):**

Instantaneous power $P(t) = s^2(t)/R$. With $s(t)$ expanded as in §3.1:

$$P_t = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T \frac{s^2(t)}{R}dt = \frac{A_c^2}{2R} + \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{4R}$$

Let $P_c = A_c^2/2R$:

$$\boxed{P_t = P_c\left(1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right)}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Capture Effect (2 marks):**
The capture effect is a phenomenon unique to FM whereby a receiver that is tuned to a particular station will **lock onto and demodulate only the stronger** of two co-channel signals, completely suppressing the weaker one provided the stronger signal is at least the **capture ratio** (typically 1–6 dB) above the weaker. AM receivers, lacking this effect, suffer from audible co-channel interference (beat notes and whistles).

**Why FM has superior noise performance (2 marks):**
- Noise disturbances (lightning, ignition, thermal) are predominantly **amplitude** perturbations.
- The FM receiver contains a **limiter** that clips off all amplitude variations, converting them to zero.
- The remaining noise affects the **phase/frequency** of the carrier, but the discriminator output is proportional to the **rate of phase change** — and noise-induced phase deviations are small.
- The discriminator's output SNR grows with $\beta$, allowing trade-off of bandwidth for noise performance.

**Derivation of SNR improvement (3 marks):**

For an input SNR $(S/N)_{in}$ and assuming $S = A_c^2/2$ and noise PSD $\eta/2$ in bandwidth $BW$:

**For AM (envelope detection):**

$$\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{out,AM} = \mu^2 \cdot \left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{in}$$

**For FM (discriminator detection):**

$$\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{out,FM} = 3\beta^2(\beta+1) \cdot \left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{in}$$

**Improvement factor:**

$$\eta_{FM/AM} = \frac{(S/N)_{out,FM}}{(S/N)_{out,AM}} = \frac{3\beta^2(\beta+1)}{\mu^2}$$

For $\beta = 5$ and $\mu = 1$: $\eta = 3(25)(6) = 450$, i.e., a **26.5 dB** advantage.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\mu$ and $\beta$** — They are *not* analogous. $\mu$ is bounded by 1; $\beta$ is unbounded. *(−2 marks if swapped)*
> 2. **Forgetting the factor-of-2** in Carson's rule — $BW = 2(\beta+1)f_m$, not $(\beta+1)f_m$. *(−1 mark)*
> 3. **Assuming FM bandwidth is constant** — Many students write $BW = 2f_m$ for FM. This is true **only for NBFM** ($\beta \ll 1$). *(−2 marks)*
> 4. **Drawing the AM spectrum with no carrier spike** — The carrier is present at $f_c$ with amplitude $A_c$. The sidebands each have amplitude $\mu A_c/2$. *(−1 mark)*
> 5. **Writing the FM equation without the integral-of-message** — Phase deviation is the integral of message in FM, NOT the message itself. *(−2 marks)*
> 6. **Stating "AM is more power-efficient than FM"** — This is the *exact opposite* of the truth. AM wastes up to 67% of power in the carrier. *(−2 marks)*

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **AM** = vary **Amplitude**; **FM** = vary **Frequency** (Phase is constant in ideal AM, Amplitude is constant in ideal FM).
- ⭐ **AM modulation index** $\mu = A_m/A_c$, **constrained $0 \leq \mu \leq 1$**.
- ⭐ **FM modulation index** $\beta = \Delta f / f_m$, **unbounded ($\beta \geq 0$)**.
- ⭐ **AM bandwidth** $BW = 2 f_m$ — **independent** of $\mu$.
- ⭐ **FM bandwidth (Carson's rule)** $BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2 f_m(\beta + 1)$.
- ⭐ **NBFM** ($\beta \ll 1$): $BW \approx 2 f_m$ (same as AM).
- ⭐ **WBFM** ($\beta \gg 1$): $BW \approx 2 \Delta f$ (much wider).
- ⭐ **AM total power** $P_t = P_c(1 + \mu^2/2)$ — **depends** on $\mu$.
- ⭐ **FM total power** $P_t = P_c$ — **constant**, regardless of $\beta$.
- ⭐ **AM efficiency** (fraction of power carrying information) $\eta = \mu^2/(2+\mu^2)$. Max = **33.3%** at $\mu=1$.
- ⭐ **FM efficiency** = **100%** — all power lies in the information-bearing signal (constant envelope carries the modulation).
- ⭐ **AM is linear** (superposition holds); **FM is non-linear** (superposition fails).
- ⭐ **FM noise advantage** factor = $3\beta^2(\beta+1)$ — at $\beta = 5$ this is ~26.5 dB.
- ⭐ **FM broadcast** standards: $f_c \in 88$–$108$ MHz, $\Delta f_{max} = 75$ kHz, channel spacing 200 kHz, $\beta_{max} = 5$.
- ⭐ **AM broadcast** standards: $f_c \in 540$–$1700$ kHz, channel spacing 10 kHz, audio BW 4.5 kHz.
- ⭐ **Limiter** in FM receiver is what gives FM its noise immunity.
- ⭐ **Pre-emphasis / De-emphasis** (75 µs in US, 50 µs in Europe) further improves FM SNR by ~13 dB.
- ⭐ **Capture effect** exists **only in FM**, not in AM.
- ⭐ **Envelope detector** is the AM demodulator; **Frequency discriminator or PLL** is the FM demodulator.
- ⭐ **AM is used for** aviation VHF, CB radio, MW/SW broadcast, maritime. **FM is used for** VHF broadcast, TV audio, public safety radios, two-way mobile.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Block Diagram of FM Receiver
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT502 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Block Diagram of FM Receiver

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **FM Receiver** (Frequency Modulation Receiver) is a superheterodyne radio receiver specifically engineered to detect, amplify, and demodulate frequency-modulated carrier waves within the commercial broadcast band (typically **88 MHz to 108 MHz** for mono/stereo FM broadcasting). The architecture follows the *superheterodyne principle*, translating the incoming RF signal to a fixed **Intermediate Frequency (IF) of 10.7 MHz** before performing coherent detection via a frequency discriminator.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCECT502, Module 1), the FM receiver must perform three cardinal operations: **selectivity** (filtering the desired channel), **sensitivity** (amplifying weak signals), and **fidelity** (faithful demodulation of the modulating waveform).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The standard IF for commercial FM broadcast is **10.7 MHz** (as opposed to 455 kHz for AM broadcast). This higher IF is mandatory because FM signals occupy wider bandwidths (200 kHz channel spacing, ±75 kHz peak deviation), and a higher IF allows better image rejection and accommodates the broader passband without adjacent channel interference.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Translator Booth" Intuition

Imagine a crowded international conference where **100 people** are speaking different languages (carriers) at the same time. Your ear cannot pick out a single voice from this chaos. The FM receiver acts like a **personal translator booth**:

1. **RF Amplifier & Antenna** = The **microphone array** that captures the weak voice from the speaker far away.
2. **Mixer + Local Oscillator** = The **language translator** who shifts the foreign speaker's voice into a *standard, fixed pitch* (the IF) that your ear can recognize.
3. **IF Amplifier** = The **sound system amplifier** that boosts the standardized voice to a comfortable level.
4. **Limiter** = The **noise-cancellation curtain** that chops off sudden loud bursts (amplitude spikes from noise/interference).
5. **FM Discriminator (Detector)** = The **interpreter** who converts the standardized voice back into the original message.
6. **Audio Amplifier + Speaker** = The **loudspeaker** that delivers the message clearly to your ears.

The genius of this design is that the receiver always listens at a *fixed internal pitch* (10.7 MHz), regardless of which station is tuned in — the translator's ear is always calibrated to the same internal frequency.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Standard Parameters (FCC / KTU Reference)**
> - **RF Carrier Band (FM Broadcast):** 88 MHz to 108 MHz
> - **Intermediate Frequency (IF):** **10.7 MHz**
> - **Maximum Frequency Deviation ($\Delta f$):** **±75 kHz**
> - **Channel Spacing / Bandwidth:** **200 kHz**
> - **Pre-emphasis Time Constant:** **75 µs**
> - **Receiver Sensitivity Threshold:** typically **–60 dBm** for home receivers
> - **Image Frequency Rejection Ratio:** > 60 dB (commercial grade)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Block-by-Block Operational Analysis

The FM superheterodyne receiver chain is engineered to provide **high gain, narrow selectivity, and amplitude-noise rejection**. Below is the logical breakdown of each block:

**Stage 1 — RF Amplifier (RF Amp)**
- **Function:** Selects the desired station using a tunable bandpass filter and amplifies the weak RF signal captured by the antenna.
- **Why it matters:** Improves the **signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)** before mixing. The noise figure (NF) of this stage dominates the receiver's overall noise performance (Friis' formula).
- **Typical Gain:** 10–25 dB.

**Stage 2 — Mixer (Frequency Converter)**
- **Function:** Multiplies the incoming RF signal ($f_{RF}$) with a locally generated sinusoidal signal from the Local Oscillator ($f_{LO}$) to produce sum and difference frequencies.
- **Key Equation:** $f_{IF} = f_{LO} - f_{RF}$ (using the upper side heterodyning convention for FM).
- **Output Components:** $f_{LO} + f_{RF}$ (sum) and $f_{LO} - f_{RF} = f_{IF}$ (difference). The sum is filtered out.

**Stage 3 — Local Oscillator (LO)**
- **Function:** Generates a stable, tunable sinusoidal waveform whose frequency tracks $f_{RF} + 10.7$ MHz.
- **Stability Requirement:** Must have low phase noise; typically a **Colpitts or Clapp oscillator** with automatic frequency control (AFC).

**Stage 4 — IF Amplifier (Multi-stage, Fixed-Tuned)**
- **Function:** Provides the bulk of the receiver's gain and selectivity at the fixed IF of 10.7 MHz.
- **Selectivity Realization:** Uses ceramic or crystal filters with a **bandwidth of ~200 kHz** (the FM channel width) to reject adjacent channels.
- **Typical Total IF Gain:** 60–90 dB across multiple cascaded stages.

**Stage 5 — Limiter**
- **Function:** Removes residual amplitude modulation (AM noise) by hard-clipping the IF signal to a constant envelope.
- **Critical Role:** FM carries information in *frequency variation*, not amplitude. Hence, any amplitude fluctuations (from noise, fading, or interference) can be erased without losing the message.
- **Threshold:** Begins limiting when input exceeds ~ 1 µV.

**Stage 6 — FM Discriminator (Demodulator)**
- **Function:** Converts frequency variations of the IF signal back into a proportional baseband voltage.
- **Common Implementations:**
   - **Slope Detector** (educational, simple)
   - **Foster-Seeley Discriminator** (balanced, classic)
   - **Ratio Detector** (good AM rejection without separate limiter)
   - **Phase-Locked Loop (PLL)** (modern, robust)
   - **Quadrature Detector** (integrated circuit friendly)

**Stage 7 — De-emphasis Network**
- **Function:** A simple RC low-pass filter with time constant $\tau = 75\ \mu s$ that attenuates high-frequency noise boosted by the transmitter's pre-emphasis.
- **Why:** FM systems use 75 µs pre-emphasis to improve high-frequency SNR; the receiver must undo this.

**Stage 8 — Audio Amplifier**
- **Function:** Boosts the recovered audio signal to a level sufficient to drive a loudspeaker (typically 1–10 W into 8 Ω).

**Stage 9 — Loudspeaker**
- **Function:** Transduces the electrical audio signal into acoustic pressure waves.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Intermediate Frequency | $f_{IF} = f_{LO} - f_{RF}$ | Heterodyne translation | Hz |
| Image Frequency | $f_{image} = f_{RF} + 2f_{IF}$ | Spurious response frequency | Hz |
| FM Modulation Index | $\beta = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_m}$ | Ratio of deviation to modulating freq | dimensionless |
| Carson's Rule Bandwidth | $BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2(\beta + 1) f_m$ | Total FM signal bandwidth | Hz |
| Output SNR (FM) | $\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)_o = \dfrac{3}{2}\beta^2 \left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)_c$ | Trade-off for noise performance | dimensionless |
| Threshold Effect | $\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)_{c,th} \approx 20(\beta + 1)$ | Carrier SNR for threshold | dimensionless |
| Pre-emphasis Corner | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\tau}$ with $\tau = 75\ \mu s$ | 3-dB break frequency ≈ 2.122 kHz | Hz |
| Voltage Gain of Cascade | $A_{total} = A_1 \cdot A_2 \cdot A_3 \cdots$ | Multiplication in linear scale | V/V |
| Noise Figure (Cascade) | $F_{total} = F_1 + \dfrac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \dfrac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2} + \cdots$ | Friis' formula for receiver noise | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The superheterodyne FM receiver architecture is the universal backbone of **every modern radio** — from car stereos to two-way police radios, aircraft VHF systems (118–137 MHz), marine VHF (156–162 MHz), and even the downconverter stage in software-defined radios (SDRs). The 10.7 MHz IF standard ensures that any commercial FM receiver can be mass-produced with standardized ceramic filters, drastically reducing manufacturing cost.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The superheterodyne FM receiver concept revolutionized wireless communication in the 1920s (invented by Edwin Armstrong, 1918) because it solved a critical problem: building high-gain, stable amplifiers at *variable* RF frequencies (88–108 MHz) was nearly impossible with the vacuum tube technology of that era. By shifting every incoming signal to a *fixed* IF, engineers could use multiple cascaded narrowband IF amplifiers to achieve 100+ dB total gain **without oscillation**. This same principle, with minor modifications, is still used in 5G cell phones, satellite transponders, GPS receivers, and Wi-Fi chipsets — a testament to the timeless genius of Armstrong's design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Signal at the Output of the Mixer (Stage 2)

Let the incoming FM signal at the antenna be:

$$
s_{RF}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left( 2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \!\int_{0}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau \right)
$$

where $A_c$ is the carrier amplitude, $f_c$ is the carrier frequency, $k_f$ is the frequency sensitivity (Hz/V), and $m(t)$ is the message signal.

Let the local oscillator produce:

$$
s_{LO}(t) = A_{LO} \cos(2\pi f_{LO} t)
$$

The mixer is a nonlinear device modeled as a multiplier. Its output is:

$$
s_{mix}(t) = s_{RF}(t) \cdot s_{LO}(t) = A_c A_{LO} \cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + \phi(t)\right) \cos(2\pi f_{LO} t)
$$

where $\phi(t) = 2\pi k_f \int_{0}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau$.

Applying the trigonometric product-to-sum identity:

$$
\cos(X)\cos(Y) = \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[\cos(X - Y) + \cos(X + Y)\bigr]
$$

We obtain:

$$
s_{mix}(t) = \frac{A_c A_{LO}}{2}\cos\!\left(2\pi(f_c - f_{LO})t - \phi(t)\right) + \frac{A_c A_{LO}}{2}\cos\!\left(2\pi(f_c + f_{LO})t + \phi(t)\right)
$$

The IF bandpass filter rejects the high-frequency sum term $(f_c + f_{LO})$. The surviving difference term is:

$$
s_{IF}(t) = \frac{A_c A_{LO}}{2}\cos\!\left(2\pi f_{IF}\, t + \phi(t)\right)
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The mixer is essentially a frequency *subtractor* — it preserves the instantaneous frequency deviation (and hence the modulation) but translates the carrier to a fixed IF. Note that $\phi(t)$ remains unchanged, which is the key reason the message information is preserved through the heterodyning process.

### 3.2 Derivation: Image Frequency Calculation

The image frequency is the *unwanted* RF frequency that also produces $f_{IF}$ after mixing. By definition:

$$
f_{LO} - f_{image} = f_{IF} \quad \text{with} \quad f_{LO} = f_{RF} + f_{IF}
$$

Substituting:

$$
(f_{RF} + f_{IF}) - f_{image} = f_{IF}
$$

Solving for $f_{image}$:

$$
f_{image} = f_{RF} + 2 f_{IF}
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):** Suppose you are tuned to $f_{RF} = 100$ MHz. The LO operates at $f_{LO} = 100 + 10.7 = 110.7$ MHz. The image frequency is then:

$$
f_{image} = 100 + 2(10.7) = 121.4\ \text{MHz}
$$

If the RF amplifier's preselector is not sharp enough, a strong station at 121.4 MHz will also produce a 10.7 MHz IF output and create interference. This is why the RF stage must have a **preselector filter** with adequate selectivity.

### 3.3 Derivation: Output SNR of an FM Receiver

For a single-tone message $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$, the modulation index is $\beta = \Delta f / f_m$. The FM signal bandwidth (Carson's rule) is:

$$
BW = 2(\beta + 1) f_m
$$

The input SNR at the discriminator input is:

$$
\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_c = \frac{A_c^2 / 2}{N_0\, BW}
$$

After passing through the **limiter** (which removes AM noise) and the **discriminator** (which acts as a differentiator, converting frequency noise to voltage noise), the output SNR becomes:

$$
\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_o = \frac{3}{2} \beta^2 \left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_c
$$

**Derivation Logic:** The factor of $3$ arises from averaging $\cos^2$ over a cycle, and the $\beta^2$ factor reflects the fact that larger frequency deviations produce stronger output signals relative to the triangular noise spectral density at the discriminator output. This is the famous **FM noise trade-off**: increasing $\beta$ (deviation) improves output SNR at the cost of bandwidth, exactly as dictated by the **bandwidth–SNR exchange principle**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Threshold Effect:** Equation $(S/N)_o = (3/2)\beta^2 (S/N)_c$ holds only when $(S/N)_c$ is above the threshold value of approximately $20(\beta + 1)$. Below this threshold, the noise exhibits *impulsive spikes* (clicks) that catastrophically degrade reception — a phenomenon first analyzed by S.O. Rice in 1948.

### 3.4 Complete Analytical Matrix — Signal Flow at Each Stage

| Stage | Input Signal | Output Signal | Key Transformation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Antenna | EM wave $E(t)$ | $s_{RF}(t) = A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi(t))$ | EM → electrical |
| 2. RF Amp | $s_{RF}(t)$ (weak) | $G_{RF}\cdot s_{RF}(t)$ | Amplification + preselection |
| 3. Mixer + LO | $G_{RF}\cdot s_{RF}(t)$ and $A_{LO}\cos(2\pi f_{LO} t)$ | $\frac{G_{RF}A_c A_{LO}}{2}\cos(2\pi f_{IF} t + \phi(t))$ | Frequency translation |
| 4. IF Amp | IF signal (small) | $G_{IF}\cdot s_{IF}(t)$ | Bulk amplification (60–90 dB) |
| 5. Limiter | IF with AM noise | Constant-envelope IF | AM noise removal |
| 6. Discriminator | Constant-envelope IF | $k_d \cdot k_f \cdot m(t)$ | Frequency → voltage |
| 7. De-emphasis | $k_d k_f m(t)$ (HF-boosted) | $k_d k_f m(t)$ (flat) | RC low-pass filter |
| 8. Audio Amp | Audio (mW) | Audio (1–10 W) | Power amplification |
| 9. Speaker | Audio voltage | Acoustic sound | Electroacoustic transduce |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Standard Superheterodyne FM Receiver

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Antenna] --> B1[RF Amplifier with Preselector Filter]
    B1 --> C1[Mixer]
    D1[Local Oscillator 10.7 MHz above RF] --> C1
    C1 --> E1[IF Amplifier Chain 10.7 MHz Center]
    E1 --> F1[Limiter]
    F1 --> G1[FM Discriminator Foster Seeley or PLL]
    G1 --> H1[De emphasis Network 75 microsecond]
    H1 --> I1[Audio Amplifier]
    I1 --> J1[Loudspeaker]

    style A1 fill:#e1f5ff
    style B1 fill:#fff4e1
    style C1 fill:#ffe1f5
    style D1 fill:#ffe1f5
    style E1 fill:#fff4e1
    style F1 fill:#e1ffe1
    style G1 fill:#f5e1ff
    style H1 fill:#e1ffe1
    style I1 fill:#fff4e1
    style J1 fill:#e1f5ff
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Signal Spectrum Transformation at Each Stage

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Spectrum Stage 1: Antenna Output, Center fc 100 MHz, BW 200 kHz, Very Low Amplitude] --> S2[Spectrum Stage 2: After RF Amp, Center fc 100 MHz, BW 200 kHz, Amplified by 20 dB]
    S2 --> S3[Spectrum Stage 3: After Mixer, Center fIF 10.7 MHz, BW 200 kHz, Lower Amplitude, Sidebands Preserved]
    S3 --> S4[Spectrum Stage 4: After IF Amp, Center fIF 10.7 MHz, BW 200 kHz, Amplified by 80 dB]
    S4 --> S5[Spectrum Stage 5: After Limiter, Constant Envelope, AM Noise Removed]
    S5 --> S6[Spectrum Stage 6: After Discriminator, Baseband Audio 30 Hz to 15 kHz, Plus De emphasis Curve]
    S6 --> S7[Spectrum Stage 7: After Audio Amp, 1 to 10 W Audio Power, Drives Speaker]

    style S1 fill:#e3f2fd
    style S2 fill:#fff3e0
    style S3 fill:#fce4ec
    style S4 fill:#fff3e0
    style S5 fill:#e8f5e9
    style S6 fill:#f3e5f5
    style S7 fill:#e3f2fd
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow — Discriminator Internal Logic (PLL-based)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[IF Input 10.7 MHz] --> P2[Phase Detector]
    P3[Voltage Controlled Oscillator VCO] --> P2
    P2 --> P4[Loop Filter LPF]
    P4 --> P5[Demodulated Audio Output]
    P4 --> P6[Feedback to VCO Control Input]
    P6 --> P3

    style P1 fill:#e1f5ff
    style P2 fill:#fff4e1
    style P3 fill:#ffe1f5
    style P4 fill:#e1ffe1
    style P5 fill:#f5e1ff
    style P6 fill:#f5e1ff
```

**Explanation of PLL Discriminator Operation:**
- The **Phase Detector** compares the phase of the incoming IF signal with the VCO's output.
- When the input frequency deviates, a phase error develops.
- The phase detector outputs an error voltage proportional to the frequency deviation.
- The **Loop Filter** smooths this error voltage (low-pass filter) and produces the demodulated audio.
- A portion of the filtered voltage is fed back to control the VCO, forcing it to track the input frequency — this is the **closed-loop feedback mechanism** that gives PLLs their excellent linearity and noise rejection.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**List the functional blocks of a superheterodyne FM receiver and state the standard intermediate frequency used for commercial FM broadcast.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The functional blocks of a superheterodyne FM receiver are:

1. **RF Amplifier** with tunable preselector filter
2. **Mixer** (frequency converter)
3. **Local Oscillator** (tunable)
4. **IF Amplifier** (fixed-tuned, multi-stage at 10.7 MHz)
5. **Limiter** (removes AM noise)
6. **FM Discriminator / Demodulator**
7. **De-emphasis Network** (75 µs)
8. **Audio Amplifier**
9. **Loudspeaker**

The standard intermediate frequency for commercial FM broadcast is **10.7 MHz**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for listing 6–7 blocks, 1 mark for the remaining blocks, 1 mark for stating the IF correctly.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Why is a Limiter used before the FM discriminator? What would happen if it were omitted?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Limiter** is used before the FM discriminator to remove any residual **amplitude modulation (AM) components** in the IF signal, including those introduced by noise, fading, or interference. Since FM information is carried entirely by **frequency variations** of the carrier, amplitude variations are unwanted and would manifest as output noise if fed directly to the discriminator.

If the limiter were **omitted**, amplitude variations would pass through the discriminator (which is sensitive to envelope changes) and appear as **distortion and noise** at the audio output, significantly degrading the **signal-to-noise ratio** and **fidelity** of the recovered message. The limiter ensures a **constant-envelope** signal, isolating the message from AM noise.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for the function, 1 mark for the principle (FM insensitive to AM), 1 mark for the consequence of omission.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Draw the block diagram of a superheterodyne FM receiver and explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An FM receiver is tuned to receive a station at 98 MHz. The IF is 10.7 MHz. Calculate: (i) the local oscillator frequency, (ii) the image frequency, and (iii) comment on the role of the RF amplifier preselector in rejecting the image. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Block Diagram and Functions (7 marks):**

The block diagram is shown in Section 4.1 above. The function of each block is:

- **RF Amplifier:** Selects the desired station using a tunable bandpass filter and amplifies the weak RF signal; improves SNR before mixing.
- **Mixer:** Combines the RF signal with the LO output to translate the carrier frequency to the fixed IF.
- **Local Oscillator:** Generates a stable, tunable frequency $f_{LO} = f_{RF} + f_{IF}$.
- **IF Amplifier:** Provides the bulk of receiver gain (60–90 dB) and adjacent-channel selectivity at 10.7 MHz.
- **Limiter:** Hard-clamps the IF signal to remove AM noise and provide a constant envelope.
- **FM Discriminator:** Converts frequency deviations of the IF signal back into a proportional audio voltage.
- **De-emphasis Network:** RC low-pass filter ($\tau = 75\ \mu s$) that undoes the transmitter's pre-emphasis.
- **Audio Amplifier:** Boosts the recovered audio to speaker-driving power levels.
- **Loudspeaker:** Transduces the audio signal into sound.

**(b) Numerical Calculations (7 marks):**

**Given:** $f_{RF} = 98$ MHz, $f_{IF} = 10.7$ MHz.

**(i) Local Oscillator Frequency:**

$$
f_{LO} = f_{RF} + f_{IF} = 98 + 10.7 = 108.7\ \text{MHz}
$$

**(ii) Image Frequency:**

$$
f_{image} = f_{RF} + 2 f_{IF} = 98 + 2(10.7) = 119.4\ \text{MHz}
$$

**(iii) Role of RF Preselector in Image Rejection:**

The RF amplifier's preselector is a tunable bandpass filter centered at $f_{RF}$ (98 MHz) with a bandwidth of approximately 200 kHz. It strongly attenuates signals at the image frequency (119.4 MHz) before they reach the mixer. Without adequate preselection, a strong signal at 119.4 MHz would also mix with the LO (108.7 MHz) to produce:

$$
108.7 - 119.4 = -10.7\ \text{MHz} \rightarrow |{-10.7}| = 10.7\ \text{MHz} = f_{IF}
$$

This spurious 10.7 MHz output would pass through the IF chain and cause **image interference** (a phantom station appearing at the tuned frequency). The preselector's role is therefore to **reject the image frequency** by typically 40–60 dB before the mixing process occurs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Split:** 1 mark for block diagram, 3 marks for function explanation, 1 mark for $f_{LO}$, 1 mark for $f_{image}$, 2 marks for preselector discussion.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a superheterodyne FM receiver. Discuss why the IF is chosen to be 10.7 MHz and not a lower value. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the performance of an FM receiver with that of an AM receiver in terms of (i) bandwidth, (ii) SNR, and (iii) immunity to noise. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Superheterodyne Working and IF Choice (7 marks):**

The block diagram is as shown in Section 4.1. The receiver operates on the **heterodyne principle**: the incoming RF signal is mixed with a local oscillator signal to produce a fixed intermediate frequency (10.7 MHz), where the bulk of amplification and filtering takes place.

**Why IF = 10.7 MHz (and not lower)?**

1. **Image Rejection:** Image frequency $f_{image} = f_{RF} + 2 f_{IF}$. A higher IF means the image is farther from the desired signal, making it easier to filter out using the RF preselector. For $f_{RF} = 100$ MHz, $f_{image} = 121.4$ MHz — well separated from 100 MHz.
2. **Adjacent Channel Selectivity:** FM channels are 200 kHz wide. A 10.7 MHz IF allows the use of practical ceramic/crystal filters with adequate Q-factor to achieve 200 kHz bandwidth. A lower IF (e.g., 455 kHz as in AM) would require impractically high-Q filters.
3. **IF Amplifier Stability:** At 10.7 MHz, neutralization and shielding are easier than at VHF (88–108 MHz), and parasitic oscillations are avoided with multi-stage cascaded gain of 60–90 dB.
4. **Bandwidth Accommodation:** The wider FM signal (±75 kHz deviation, 200 kHz channel) cannot be efficiently processed at a low IF (like 455 kHz) without image and adjacent-channel interference issues.

**(b) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | AM Receiver | FM Receiver |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Bandwidth | $2 f_m$ (e.g., 10 kHz) | $2(\Delta f + f_m)$ via Carson's rule (e.g., 200 kHz) |
| (ii) SNR Performance | $(S/N)_o = (S/N)_c$ (linear) | $(S/N)_o = \frac{3}{2}\beta^2 (S/N)_c$ (improved with $\beta$) |
| (iii) Noise Immunity | Poor — noise directly added to envelope | Excellent — limiter removes AM noise; FM noise is triangular |
| Channel Spacing | 10 kHz | 200 kHz |
| IF | 455 kHz | 10.7 MHz |
| Modulation Index | $m_a \leq 1$ (to avoid distortion) | $\beta$ can be >>1 |
| Power Efficiency | Low (carrier carries no info) | High (constant envelope) |
| Capture Effect | Absent | Strong — weaker co-channel signals are suppressed |
| Pre/De-emphasis | Not used | 75 µs standard |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Split:** 3 marks for block diagram and working, 2 marks for IF justification, 2 marks for the comparison table, 2 marks for the noise immunity discussion.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing IF values:** Writing 455 kHz (which is the **AM** IF) instead of **10.7 MHz** for FM receivers. This is the most common single error and loses 1–2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting the Limiter:** Many students draw the block diagram but omit the Limiter block, which is a hallmark of an FM receiver. An FM receiver without a limiter is graded as incomplete.
> 3. **Image Frequency Formula Sign Error:** Some students write $f_{image} = f_{RF} - 2 f_{IF}$, which is incorrect. The correct formula is $f_{image} = f_{RF} + 2 f_{IF}$ for high-side LO injection.
> 4. **No De-emphasis block:** Pre-emphasis/de-emphasis is a defining feature of FM and earns easy marks if mentioned.
> 5. **Confusing the discriminator with an envelope detector:** AM uses an envelope detector; FM uses a discriminator (Foster-Seeley, ratio detector, or PLL). Using the wrong name loses marks.
> 6. **Carson's Rule misapplication:** Some students apply AM bandwidth ($2 f_m$) to FM. Always use $BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Block Diagram of FM Receiver**

- The FM receiver is a **superheterodyne** receiver, first invented by Edwin H. Armstrong.
- Standard **Intermediate Frequency (IF) = 10.7 MHz** for commercial FM broadcast.
- Block chain: **Antenna → RF Amp → Mixer + LO → IF Amp → Limiter → Discriminator → De-emphasis → Audio Amp → Speaker**.
- **Local Oscillator Frequency:** $f_{LO} = f_{RF} + f_{IF}$ (high-side injection).
- **Image Frequency:** $f_{image} = f_{RF} + 2 f_{IF}$.
- The **Limiter** is essential to remove AM noise and create a constant-envelope IF signal.
- The **Discriminator** (Foster-Seeley, ratio detector, or PLL) converts frequency variation to voltage.
- **De-emphasis** uses an RC filter with time constant $\tau = 75\ \mu s$ to undo transmitter pre-emphasis.
- **Carson's Rule:** $BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2(\beta + 1) f_m$.
- **FM Output SNR:** $(S/N)_o = \frac{3}{2}\beta^2 (S/N)_c$ — the famous **bandwidth–SNR exchange**.
- **Threshold effect** occurs at $(S/N)_c \approx 20(\beta + 1)$ — below this, impulsive noise spikes dominate.
- **RF Preselector** rejects the image frequency; its selectivity is critical to receiver performance.
- **Friis' Noise Formula:** $F_{total} = F_1 + (F_2 - 1)/G_1 + \cdots$ — RF amp noise figure dominates.
- **Capture effect** is unique to FM: the stronger of two co-channel signals captures the receiver.
- Always distinguish **AM receiver IF (455 kHz)** from **FM receiver IF (10.7 MHz)** in numerical problems.
- A block diagram drawn without the **Limiter** is marked as incomplete in KTU valuation.
- The superheterodyne architecture is used in **every modern radio**: FM broadcast, aviation, marine, cellular, Wi-Fi, GPS, and SDRs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT502 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Superheterodyne Receivers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Superheterodyne Receiver** (often abbreviated as **superhet**) is a sophisticated radio receiver architecture that employs **frequency mixing (heterodyning)** to convert the incoming Radio Frequency (RF) signal into a fixed, lower **Intermediate Frequency (IF)** before the final stages of amplification and demodulation are carried out. It was invented by **Edwin Armstrong in 1918** and remains the dominant architecture in virtually all commercial AM, FM, TV, radar, and communication receivers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "A superheterodyne receiver is a receiver in which the incoming RF signal is mixed with a locally generated oscillator signal to produce a fixed intermediate frequency (IF) signal, which is then amplified and demodulated."

### 1.2 The Core Problem It Solves

In a **tuned radio frequency (TRF) receiver**, all the RF amplification must occur *at the original carrier frequency*. This means that as the user tunes across the AM broadcast band (540 kHz – 1640 kHz) or FM band (88 MHz – 108 MHz), **every tuned circuit in every amplifier stage must be re-tuned simultaneously** to the new frequency. Achieving high gain, narrow bandwidth, and good selectivity *simultaneously* at a variable frequency is practically impossible with passive components.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Superheterodyne's Brilliance:** Instead of trying to do everything at a *changing* frequency, the superhet converts the incoming RF to a **fixed Intermediate Frequency (IF)**, where all the heavy-duty amplification, filtering, and most of the selectivity work is performed. This is why AM radios always have an IF of **455 kHz** and FM/TV receivers use **10.7 MHz**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Currency Exchange" Counter

Imagine you are a money-changer at an international airport:

- **Customers arrive** with different currencies (US Dollars, Euros, Yen, Pounds). These are like the **incoming RF signals** — each at a different frequency.
- You cannot memorize, count, and verify *every* type of currency at full speed. It is overwhelming and error-prone.
- So, you **convert all currencies to a single, familiar currency** (say, your local currency) at a fixed exchange rate. This single currency is the **Intermediate Frequency (IF)**.
- A single, well-trained accountant then carefully verifies, counts, and validates **only this one currency** with high precision. This is the **IF amplifier and detector**.

The exchange counter is the **Mixer**, the exchange-rate board you adjust is the **Local Oscillator**, and the IF is the standardized "currency" in which all subsequent processing happens.

## 1.4 Block-Level Functional Description

A typical **AM superheterodyne receiver** consists of the following functional blocks in cascade:

1. **Antenna** — Captures the modulated RF signal from free space.
2. **RF Amplifier (LNA)** — Provides initial low-noise amplification and preliminary preselection (image rejection).
3. **Local Oscillator (LO)** — Generates a tunable sinusoidal signal whose frequency is mechanically (or electronically) ganged to the RF tuning.
4. **Mixer (Frequency Converter)** — A non-linear device that multiplies the RF and LO signals, producing sum and difference frequencies.
5. **IF Amplifier** — A high-gain, fixed-tuned, highly selective amplifier centered at **f_IF**.
6. **Detector / Demodulator** — Extracts the original baseband (audio) information from the IF.
7. **Audio Amplifier** — Boosts the recovered audio to drive a loudspeaker.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard IF Frequencies (must be memorized for KTU exams):**
> - **AM Broadcast Receivers:** $f_{IF} = 455 \text{ kHz}$
> - **FM Broadcast Receivers:** $f_{IF} = 10.7 \text{ MHz}$
> - **TV Receivers:** $f_{IF} = 38.9 \text{ MHz}$ (video) and $33.4 \text{ MHz}$ (audio)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Heterodyning (Mixing) Process — Mathematical Foundation

The mixer is a **non-linear device** (or a linear multiplier fed by two sinusoids). When the incoming RF signal $s_{RF}(t) = A \cos(2\pi f_{RF} t)$ and the local oscillator signal $s_{LO}(t) = B \cos(2\pi f_{LO} t)$ are applied to a multiplier, the output is:

$$
s_{mix}(t) = A \cos(2\pi f_{RF} t) \cdot B \cos(2\pi f_{LO} t)
$$

Applying the trigonometric identity $\cos(\alpha)\cos(\beta) = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(\alpha-\beta) + \cos(\alpha+\beta)]$:

$$
s_{mix}(t) = \frac{AB}{2}\Big[\cos\big(2\pi(f_{RF} - f_{LO})t\big) + \cos\big(2\pi(f_{RF} + f_{LO})t\big)\Big]
$$

The IF amplifier (with its fixed bandpass filter) selects either the **difference frequency** or the **sum frequency** as the IF:

$$
f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_{RF} \vert \quad \text{or} \quad f_{IF} = f_{LO} + f_{RF}
$$

In standard broadcast receivers, the **difference frequency** is selected.

## 2.2 Local Oscillator Tuning — High-Side vs. Low-Side Injection

To keep $f_{IF}$ constant as the user tunes $f_{RF}$, the local oscillator must track with an offset:

- **High-Side Injection (Standard for AM/FM):** $\quad f_{LO} = f_{RF} + f_{IF}$
- **Low-Side Injection:** $\quad f_{LO} = f_{RF} - f_{IF}$

> [!TIP]
> **Why High-Side?** Tracking the LO *above* the RF avoids the LO signal falling within the received band (which would cause self-radiation and pulling) and yields simpler tracking capacitor design.

## 2.3 The Image Frequency Problem (Critical KTU Topic)

A devastating side-effect of mixing is the **Image Frequency** — a completely unwanted signal that, when mixed with the LO, *also* produces the IF.

With high-side injection $f_{LO} = f_{RF} + f_{IF}$, suppose an interfering signal exists at frequency $f_{im}$ such that:

$$
f_{LO} - f_{im} = f_{IF} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_{im} = f_{LO} - f_{IF}
$$

Substituting $f_{LO} = f_{RF} + f_{IF}$:

$$
f_{im} = (f_{RF} + f_{IF}) - f_{IF} = f_{RF} + 2f_{IF}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> The image frequency is spaced **$2f_{IF}$ away from the desired RF signal** and is symmetrically placed on the *opposite* side of the LO. If it is not suppressed *before* the mixer, it will create an identical IF signal and cause severe interference.

## 2.4 Image Rejection Ratio (IRR)

Since the image falls outside the IF passband, it must be rejected by the **RF amplifier's preselector filter**. The Image Rejection Ratio is defined as:

$$
\text{IRR} = \frac{\text{Desired RF signal amplitude at the RF amplifier output}}{\text{Image signal amplitude at the RF amplifier output}}
$$

In decibels:

$$
\text{IRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\alpha_s}{\alpha_i}\right)
$$

where $\alpha_s$ and $\alpha_i$ are the voltage gains of the RF stage at $f_{RF}$ and $f_{im}$ respectively.

## 2.5 Tracking and Alignment

The RF preselector and the LO must be tuned *together* (ganged) so that their difference remains exactly $f_{IF}$ across the entire band. Perfect tracking is impossible with a single-section variable capacitor, so the practical solution is **Three-Point Tracking** using two padding capacitors (a **padder** in series and a **trimmer** in parallel).

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Engineering Meaning |
|---|-----------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Mixer Output | $f_{out} = \vert f_{LO} \pm f_{RF} \vert$ | Sum and difference products at mixer output |
| 2 | High-Side LO | $f_{LO} = f_{RF} + f_{IF}$ | Standard AM/FM injection scheme |
| 3 | Low-Side LO | $f_{LO} = f_{RF} - f_{IF}$ | Used in some specialized receivers |
| 4 | Intermediate Frequency | $f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_{RF} \vert$ | Fixed by design (455 kHz, 10.7 MHz) |
| 5 | **Image Frequency (high-side)** | $f_{im} = f_{RF} + 2f_{IF}$ | Critical interference frequency |
| 6 | **Image Frequency (low-side)** | $f_{im} = f_{RF} - 2f_{IF}$ | Critical interference frequency |
| 7 | Image Offset | $\Delta f_{im} = 2f_{IF}$ | Always twice the IF |
| 8 | Image Rejection Ratio (linear) | $\text{IRR} = \alpha_s / \alpha_i$ | Ratio of desired to image gain |
| 9 | Image Rejection Ratio (dB) | $\text{IRR}_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(\alpha_s / \alpha_i)$ | Expressed in decibels |
| 10 | Adjacent Channel Rejection | Depends on IF filter $Q$ | Selectivity of IF stage |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Use:** The superhet principle is used in **every commercial radio**, **cell phones**, **satellite transponders**, **radar systems**, **spectrum analyzers**, and **software-defined radios (SDR)**. The fixed IF allows cheap, high-$Q$ ceramic or SAW filters to deliver exceptional selectivity.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Image Frequency from First Principles

**Given:**
- Receiver is tuned to a desired RF signal of frequency $f_{RF}$.
- The local oscillator is set to $f_{LO} = f_{RF} + f_{IF}$ (high-side injection).
- An unwanted, interfering signal exists in the ether at frequency $f_{im}$.

**To Prove:** $f_{im} = f_{RF} + 2f_{IF}$.

**Step 1 — Express the desired mixing product.**

The mixer multiplies the incoming RF signal with the LO. Using the trigonometric identity:

$$
s_{mix}(t) = A\cos(2\pi f_{RF} t) \cdot B\cos(2\pi f_{LO} t)
$$

$$
s_{mix}(t) = \frac{AB}{2}\Big[\cos\big(2\pi(f_{RF} - f_{LO})t\big) + \cos\big(2\pi(f_{RF} + f_{LO})t\big)\Big]
$$

**Step 2 — The IF filter selects only the difference frequency.**

The IF bandpass filter, centered at $f_{IF}$, passes only the term with frequency $f_{LO} - f_{RF}$ (since $f_{LO} > f_{RF}$ in high-side injection):

$$
f_{IF} = f_{LO} - f_{RF} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_{LO} = f_{RF} + f_{IF}
$$

This is the *intended* behavior.

**Step 3 — Now introduce the image signal.**

Suppose an interfering signal $C\cos(2\pi f_{im} t)$ enters the mixer along with the LO. The new mixer output is:

$$
s'_{mix}(t) = C\cos(2\pi f_{im} t) \cdot B\cos(2\pi f_{LO} t)
$$

$$
s'_{mix}(t) = \frac{CB}{2}\Big[\cos\big(2\pi(f_{im} - f_{LO})t\big) + \cos\big(2\pi(f_{im} + f_{LO})t\big)\Big]
$$

**Step 4 — Find the value of $f_{im}$ that also produces $f_{IF}$ at the output.**

For the image signal to masquerade as the desired signal, its difference-frequency product must equal $f_{IF}$:

$$
\vert f_{im} - f_{LO} \vert = f_{IF}
$$

Since the image lies *above* the LO (because it is symmetric to the RF signal about the LO), $f_{im} > f_{LO}$:

$$
f_{im} - f_{LO} = f_{IF} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_{im} = f_{LO} + f_{IF}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $f_{LO} = f_{RF} + f_{IF}$:**

$$
f_{im} = (f_{RF} + f_{IF}) + f_{IF}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_{im} = f_{RF} + 2f_{IF}}
$$

**Step 6 — Geometric Interpretation.**

The image frequency is located on the opposite side of the LO from the desired RF, at a distance $f_{IF}$:

- Desired RF: $f_{LO} - f_{IF}$
- Local Oscillator: $f_{LO}$
- Image: $f_{LO} + f_{IF}$
- Total separation RF ↔ Image: $2f_{IF}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Doubling the IF doubles the image offset, which makes the image *easier* to filter out — this is the principal reason modern FM/TV receivers chose a high IF of 10.7 MHz.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** An AM superheterodyne receiver is tuned to receive a station at **f_RF = 1000 kHz**. The AM IF is **455 kHz**, and high-side injection is used. Determine:

(a) The local oscillator frequency.
(b) The image frequency.
(c) If the RF preselector has a gain of **20 dB** at $f_{RF}$ and **−10 dB** at $f_{im}$, calculate the **Image Rejection Ratio in dB**.

### Solution

**Part (a):** Using the high-side injection formula:

$$
f_{LO} = f_{RF} + f_{IF} = 1000 \text{ kHz} + 455 \text{ kHz} = 1455 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Part (b):** Using the image frequency formula:

$$
f_{im} = f_{RF} + 2f_{IF} = 1000 \text{ kHz} + 2(455 \text{ kHz}) = 1000 + 910 = 1910 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Part (c):** Converting dB gains to linear scale:

$$
\alpha_s = 10^{20/20} = 10
$$

$$
\alpha_i = 10^{-10/20} = 10^{-0.5} \approx 0.3162
$$

Image Rejection Ratio (linear):

$$
\text{IRR} = \frac{\alpha_s}{\alpha_i} = \frac{10}{0.3162} \approx 31.62
$$

In decibels:

$$
\text{IRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(31.62) \approx 30 \text{ dB}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Short-cut for dB problems:** Since both gains are in dB, the IRR in dB is simply the **arithmetic difference** of the two gains:
> $$\text{IRR}_{dB} = 20 - (-10) = 30 \text{ dB}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Functional Block Properties

| Block | Frequency of Operation | Bandwidth | Key Function |
|-------|------------------------|-----------|--------------|
| Antenna | Variable (full band) | Wide | Energy capture |
| RF Amplifier | Variable (tuned) | Moderate | LNA + preselection |
| Mixer | RF + LO → IF | Wide at input | Frequency translation |
| Local Oscillator | $f_{LO} = f_{RF} + f_{IF}$ | Narrow (sinusoidal) | Generates beating signal |
| IF Amplifier | **Fixed** at $f_{IF}$ | Very narrow | High gain + sharp selectivity |
| Detector | Fixed at $f_{IF}$ | Input bandwidth | Demodulation |
| Audio Amplifier | Baseband (audio) | 20 Hz – 20 kHz | Power amplification |
