# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Standard AM Superheterodyne Receiver

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Antenna] --> B[RF Amplifier and Preselector]
    B --> C[Mixer]
    D[Local Oscillator] --> C
    C --> E[IF Amplifier 1 fixed at fIF]
    E --> F[IF Amplifier 2 fixed at fIF]
    F --> G[Detector and Demodulator]
    G --> H[Audio Amplifier]
    H --> I[Loudspeaker]
    J[Tuning Gang] -.ganged control.-> B
    J -.ganged control.-> D

    classDef fixed fill:#ffd9a8,stroke:#a04000,color:#000
    classDef variable fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003a75,color:#000
    classDef osc fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#0a6b2c,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#f4c4f4,stroke:#6a0a6a,color:#000

    class E,F,G fixed
    class A,B,H,I variable
    class D,J osc
    class C out
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **tuning gang (J)** mechanically links the RF preselector and the Local Oscillator so that both move together, maintaining a constant offset of $f_{IF}$.
- The two **IF stages (E and F)** are permanently fixed-tuned — they never move during tuning.

## 4.2 Frequency Domain Mapping (Heterodyning Process)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INP["Input Spectrum at Mixer"]
        R1[Desired RF at fRF]
        R2[Image Signal at fRF + 2fIF]
    end

    subgraph LO["Local Oscillator"]
        L1[Sinusoid at fLO = fRF + fIF]
    end

    INP --> MX[Non Linear Mixer Multiplier]
    LO --> MX

    MX --> OUT1[IF Output at fIF from desired RF]
    MX --> OUT2[IF Output at fIF from image]
    MX --> OUT3[Sum Frequency fLO + fRF REJECTED]
    MX --> OUT4[Other Harmonics REJECTED]

    OUT1 --> FIL[Narrow IF Bandpass Filter]
    OUT2 --> FIL

    classDef danger fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#a00000,color:#000
    classDef good fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#0a6b2c,color:#000
    classDef filter fill:#fff3a8,stroke:#806000,color:#000

    class R1,OUT1 good
    class R2,OUT2 danger
    class FIL filter
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Observation:** Both the desired RF and the image produce an **identical IF output**. The IF filter *cannot* distinguish them. Rejection of the image **must** happen *before* the mixer, in the RF preselector.

## 4.3 Signal Flow Showing Image Frequency Location

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[f im equals f RF plus 2 f IF] --- F2[f LO equals f RF plus f IF]
    F2 --- F3[f RF DESIRED]
    F3 --- F4[f LO minus f IF equals f IF OUTPUT]
    F2 --- F5[f LO plus f IF equals f IF OUTPUT from image]

    classDef image fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#a00000,color:#000
    classDef desired fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#0a6b2c,color:#000
    classDef lo fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003a75,color:#000

    class F1 image
    class F3,F4 desired
    class F2 lo
    class F5 image
```

**Layout on the frequency axis (left to right, increasing frequency):**

$$\text{Desired } f_{RF} \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{LO at } f_{LO} \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{Image at } f_{im}$$

## 4.4 Double-Conversion Superheterodyne Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Antenna] --> B[RF Preselector]
    B --> C1[First Mixer]
    LO1[First Local Oscillator High Frequency] --> C1
    C1 --> D[First IF Amplifier at high IF e.g. 60 MHz]
    D --> C2[Second Mixer]
    LO2[Second Local Oscillator Fixed] --> C2
    C2 --> E[Second IF Amplifier at low IF e.g. 455 kHz]
    E --> F[Detector]
    F --> G[Audio Amplifier]
    G --> H[Speaker]

    classDef hi fill:#fff3a8,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    classDef lo fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#0a6b2c,color:#000
    classDef stage fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003a75,color:#000

    class D,LO1 hi
    class E,LO2 lo
    class C1,C2,B,F,G stage
```

**Why double conversion?** In high-performance receivers (microwave links, satellite comms, radar), a single high IF is used first to easily reject the image, and then a second, lower IF is used to perform the precise, narrowband selectivity work. This overcomes the **image rejection vs. selectivity trade-off** of single-conversion designs.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define a superheterodyne receiver. Why is an Intermediate Frequency (IF) used instead of demodulating the RF signal directly?**

**Model Answer:**

A superheterodyne receiver is a radio receiver in which the incoming modulated RF signal is mixed with a locally generated oscillator signal to produce a **fixed Intermediate Frequency (IF)** signal, which is then amplified and demodulated.

The IF is used because:
1. **Selectivity** is easier to achieve at a fixed, lower frequency using high-$Q$ fixed-tuned filters (e.g., ceramic, SAW, or mechanical filters).
2. **Gain** is more stable and easier to obtain at a fixed IF since it avoids the gain variation of RF stages across a wide tuning range.
3. **Image rejection** is improved by allowing the use of a sufficiently high IF (so that the image lies far from the desired signal and can be filtered out by the RF preselector).
4. **Demodulation** becomes simpler because a single, well-designed detector can be optimized for the fixed IF.

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**What is the image frequency in a superheterodyne receiver? Explain with the help of a relevant mathematical expression.**

**Model Answer:**

The **image frequency** is an undesired input signal frequency that, when mixed with the local oscillator frequency, also produces the Intermediate Frequency, and hence is indistinguishable from the desired signal at the IF output.

For a receiver using **high-side injection** ($f_{LO} = f_{RF} + f_{IF}$), the image frequency is:

$$
f_{im} = f_{RF} + 2f_{IF}
$$

It is located at a distance of $2f_{IF}$ *above* the desired RF signal (on the far side of the LO). The image must be rejected by the RF preselector filter *before* reaching the mixer, because the IF filter cannot distinguish it from the desired signal.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question 1A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) Draw the block diagram of an AM superheterodyne receiver and explain the function of each block. (7 Marks)**

**(b) With the help of a neat diagram, explain the concept of image frequency and image rejection ratio. An AM receiver using high-side injection has $f_{IF} = 455 \text{ kHz}$ and is tuned to **1200 kHz**. Find the local oscillator frequency and the image frequency. If the RF amplifier has a voltage gain of **15 dB** at 1200 kHz and **−8 dB** at the image frequency, calculate the **Image Rejection Ratio in dB**. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for 1A(a):

**Block diagram of AM superheterodyne receiver:**

```
Antenna → RF Amplifier → Mixer ← Local Oscillator
                                  ↓
                                IF Amplifier
                                  ↓
                                Detector
                                  ↓
                              Audio Amplifier
                                  ↓
                              Loudspeaker
```

**Function of each block:**

| Block | Function |
|-------|----------|
| **Antenna** | Captures the modulated RF electromagnetic wave and converts it into an electrical signal. |
| **RF Amplifier** | Provides initial low-noise amplification and preliminary filtering (preselection) to reject the image frequency. |
| **Local Oscillator** | Generates a tunable sinusoidal signal whose frequency is ganged to the RF tuning such that $f_{LO} = f_{RF} + f_{IF}$. |
| **Mixer** | A non-linear device that multiplies RF and LO signals to produce sum and difference frequencies; the difference frequency is the IF. |
| **IF Amplifier** | Provides most of the receiver's gain and selectivity at a **fixed** frequency $f_{IF}$ (455 kHz for AM). |
| **Detector / Demodulator** | Extracts the original audio (baseband) signal from the AM-modulated IF. |
| **Audio Amplifier** | Boosts the recovered audio to a level sufficient to drive a loudspeaker. |
| **Loudspeaker** | Converts the amplified audio signal back into sound waves. |

#### Model Solution for 1A(b):

**Image Frequency Concept:**

The image frequency $f_{im}$ is a spurious input that beats with the local oscillator to produce *exactly* the same IF as the desired signal. With high-side injection, it lies at:

$$
f_{im} = f_{RF} + 2f_{IF}
$$

It is symmetrical to the desired RF about the LO, separated by $2f_{IF}$ on the opposite side.

**Given:**
- $f_{IF} = 455 \text{ kHz}$
- $f_{RF} = 1200 \text{ kHz}$
- Gain at desired signal: $\alpha_s = 15 \text{ dB}$
- Gain at image signal: $\alpha_i = -8 \text{ dB}$

**Step 1 — Local Oscillator frequency:**

$$
f_{LO} = f_{RF} + f_{IF} = 1200 + 455 = 1655 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Step 2 — Image frequency:**

$$
f_{im} = f_{RF} + 2f_{IF} = 1200 + 2(455) = 1200 + 910 = 2110 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Step 3 — Image Rejection Ratio in dB:**

Since both gains are already in dB:

$$
\text{IRR}_{dB} = \alpha_s - \alpha_i = 15 - (-8) = 23 \text{ dB}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes (Examiner's Pitfall Callout):**
> 1. Confusing the formula for $f_{im}$ — using $f_{RF} + f_{IF}$ instead of $f_{RF} + 2f_{IF}$.
> 2. Converting dB gains to linear scale, dividing, and then re-converting — wasteful and error-prone. Simply **subtract the dB values**.
> 3. Forgetting the "high-side" qualifier in the formula. In low-side injection, $f_{im} = f_{RF} - 2f_{IF}$ (which may become negative — meaningless for positive frequencies, indicating the image lies below DC).
> 4. Writing $f_{im} = f_{LO} - f_{IF}$ instead of $f_{LO} + f_{IF}$ — a frequent sign error.

---

### **Question 1B. [Alternative Choice for Internal Selection]**

**(a) Explain the concept of frequency mixing in a superheterodyne receiver. Derive the expression for the image frequency. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Discuss the importance of IF selection. Why is 455 kHz used for AM and 10.7 MHz for FM? What is tracking in a superheterodyne receiver, and why is three-point tracking used? (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for 1B(a):

**Concept of Frequency Mixing:**

Frequency mixing is the process of combining two sinusoidal signals of different frequencies in a **non-linear device** to produce new signals at the sum and difference of the original frequencies.

Let the RF input be $v_{RF}(t) = A\cos(2\pi f_{RF} t)$ and the local oscillator be $v_{LO}(t) = B\cos(2\pi f_{LO} t)$. Applied to an ideal multiplier (the mixer), the output is:

$$
v_{out}(t) = A\cos(2\pi f_{RF} t) \cdot B\cos(2\pi f_{LO} t)
$$

Applying the product-to-sum identity:

$$
v_{out}(t) = \frac{AB}{2}\cos\big[2\pi(f_{RF} - f_{LO})t\big] + \frac{AB}{2}\cos\big[2\pi(f_{RF} + f_{LO})t\big]
$$

The **difference frequency** $|f_{LO} - f_{RF}|$ is selected by the IF bandpass filter and is called the **Intermediate Frequency**:

$$
f_{IF} = \vert f_{LO} - f_{RF} \vert
$$

**Derivation of Image Frequency:**

To keep $f_{IF}$ constant, the LO must track the RF:

$$
f_{LO} = f_{RF} + f_{IF} \quad \text{(high-side injection)}
$$

An image signal at $f_{im}$ will also produce $f_{IF}$ at the mixer output if:

$$
f_{LO} - f_{im} = -f_{IF} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_{im} = f_{LO} + f_{IF}
$$

Substituting $f_{LO} = f_{RF} + f_{IF}$:

$$
\boxed{f_{im} = f_{RF} + 2f_{IF}}
$$

#### Model Solution for 1B(b):

**Importance of IF Selection:**

The choice of IF frequency involves a critical **trade-off**:
- A **higher IF** pushes the image frequency further away from the desired signal, making image rejection easier with a simple RF preselector.
- A **lower IF** allows the use of higher-$Q$ IF filters, giving better adjacent-channel selectivity.

**Why 455 kHz for AM and 10.7 MHz for FM?**

| Parameter | AM (455 kHz) | FM (10.7 MHz) |
|-----------|--------------|---------------|
| Band | 540 – 1640 kHz | 88 – 108 MHz |
| Channel spacing | 10 kHz | 200 kHz |
| Required selectivity | Moderate (10 kHz BW) | High (200 kHz BW) |
| Image offset $2f_{IF}$ | 910 kHz (large, easy rejection) | 21.4 MHz (very large) |
| Standardized globally for | Cost-effective AM receivers | Cost-effective FM receivers |

For AM, a low IF (455 kHz) suffices because the AM broadcast band is narrow and a 910 kHz image offset is already ample. For FM, a much higher IF (10.7 MHz) is required because the FM band is wider and the image offset of 21.4 MHz is necessary to prevent image interference from powerful adjacent FM stations.

**Tracking:**

Tracking is the process of ensuring that the difference $f_{LO} - f_{RF}$ remains **exactly $f_{IF}$** across the entire tuning range as the user adjusts the gang capacitor.

A simple two-section gang cannot maintain this perfect offset because the RF and LO tuned circuits have different $L/C$ ratios. Therefore, **three-point tracking** is used:
1. Alignment at the **low-frequency end** using a **padder capacitor** (in series with the LO section).
2. Alignment at the **high-frequency end** using a **trimmer capacitor** (in parallel with the LO section).
3. The middle frequency point is then automatically optimized.

This ensures acceptably small tracking errors (typically < 1–2 kHz) across the band.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes (Examiner's Pitfall Callout):**
> 1. Saying the image frequency is $f_{RF} + f_{IF}$ instead of $f_{RF} + 2f_{IF}$.
> 2. Confusing the padder and trimmer locations — padder is at the low-frequency end (series), trimmer is at the high-frequency end (parallel).
> 3. Stating that "IF is chosen to be high for high selectivity" — incorrect. Higher IF gives *better image rejection* but *worse selectivity*. Lower IF gives *better selectivity* but *worse image rejection*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Revision Points for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ✅ **Superheterodyne** = RF is converted to a **fixed IF** before amplification/detection.
- ✅ **Standard IF values:** AM = **455 kHz**, FM = **10.7 MHz**, TV video = **38.9 MHz**.
- ✅ **High-side injection** is standard: $f_{LO} = f_{RF} + f_{IF}$.
- ✅ **Image frequency:** $f_{im} = f_{RF} + 2f_{IF}$ (high-side) or $f_{im} = f_{RF} - 2f_{IF}$ (low-side).
- ✅ **Image offset** is always $2f_{IF}$ — independent of $f_{RF}$.
- ✅ **IRR in dB** = gain at desired signal (dB) − gain at image (dB).
- ✅ **Image rejection** must be done in the **RF preselector**, *before* the mixer.
- ✅ **Three-point tracking** uses a padder (series, LF end) and a trimmer (parallel, HF end).
- ✅ **Mixer is a multiplier** — output contains sum and difference frequencies.
- ✅ **IF filter** is fixed-tuned — gives high gain and sharp selectivity at constant frequency.
- ✅ **Double-conversion superhet** is used when a single IF cannot satisfy both image rejection and selectivity requirements.
- ✅ **Trade-off:** ↑ IF → ↑ image rejection, ↓ selectivity; ↓ IF → ↓ image rejection, ↑ selectivity.
- ✅ The superhet is the **universal receiver architecture** — used in AM, FM, TV, radar, SDR, cell phones, and satellite systems.
- ✅ Remember the **flow**: Antenna → RF Amp → Mixer (with LO) → IF Amp → Detector → Audio Amp → Speaker.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics of Receivers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT502 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Characteristics of Receivers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CHARACTERISTICS OF RECEIVERS (KTU 2024 Module 1 Definition)**
> The **characteristics of a radio receiver** are a set of quantitative and qualitative performance parameters that specify how effectively the receiver can detect, isolate, amplify, and faithfully reproduce the intelligence (modulating signal) carried by a desired RF transmission, while simultaneously rejecting noise, interference, and unwanted adjacent/far-off signals.

In any KTU 2024 Scheme examination, the term *"receiver characteristics"* almost always refers to the performance evaluation of the **AM Superheterodyne Receiver**, because it is the industry-standard architecture taught across the syllabus. A receiver is never a "perfect" device — every parameter is a trade-off between sensitivity, selectivity, fidelity, and cost.

## 1.2 Real-World Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Hospital Stethoscope + Radio" Analogy**
> Imagine you are a doctor trying to listen to **one specific patient’s heartbeat** (the **desired station**, say 93.5 MHz Red FM) inside a **crowded, noisy hospital ward** (the **airspace full of hundreds of radio stations**, mobile towers, and thermal noise).
>
> - **Sensitivity** → How faint a heartbeat your stethoscope can hear (weak-signal detection).
> - **Selectivity** → How well your stethoscope ignores the *other patient coughing next door* (rejecting 93.3 MHz and 93.7 MHz).
> - **Fidelity** → How accurately you can describe the heartbeat rhythm afterwards (faithful reproduction of music/voice).
> - **Image Frequency Rejection** → How you avoid confusing the *echo of a heartbeat from a different floor* that sounds identical to your patient (a "ghost signal" at a totally different frequency that mixes down to the same IF).
>
> A *good* receiver, like a *good doctor*, balances all four: it hears the faintest signals, ignores the noise, and reports what it heard truthfully.

## 1.3 The Six Primary Receiver Characteristics (Syllabus Map)

The KTU 2024 PCECT502 syllabus explicitly expects the student to master these **six core characteristics**, and they appear almost every cycle in either Part A (3-mark) or Part B (14-mark) questions:

| # | Characteristic | One-Line Meaning |
|---|----------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Sensitivity** | Minimum input RF signal the receiver can usefully detect. |
| 2 | **Selectivity** | Ability to reject adjacent unwanted channels. |
| 3 | **Fidelity** | Ability to reproduce the original modulating waveform accurately. |
| 4 | **Image Frequency Rejection** | Ability to reject the *image* (mirror) frequency produced by the mixer. |
| 5 | **Adjacent Channel Rejection (ACR)** | Rejection ratio for signals just outside the desired channel bandwidth. |
| 6 | **Dynamic Range & Spurious Response** | Linear operating range and rejection of internally-generated harmonics. |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Mnemonic — "S-S-F-I-A-D"** → *Sagacious Students Find Image And Dynamic-truth* — memorize this six-letter chain and you will never forget the order KTU expects.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Characteristic 1 — Sensitivity

**Definition (Board-exam exact wording):**
> *Sensitivity of a receiver is defined as the minimum input RF signal voltage (or power) required at the antenna terminals to produce a specified standard output signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), usually 10 dB for AM broadcast and 26 dB for FM broadcast receivers.*

### Why does sensitivity matter?
Every resistor, transistor, and mixer in the receiver chain generates **thermal (Johnson-Nyquist) noise** and **shot noise**. If the desired station is weaker than this internally generated noise, the receiver output will be unintelligible regardless of how much we amplify it. Therefore, sensitivity is fundamentally **noise-limited**.

### The Canonical Sensitivity Equation

$$S_{\min} \;=\; k\,T\,B \,\cdot\, F \,\cdot\, \left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{o,\min}$$

Where every symbol carries a strict KTU-defined meaning — see the formula sheet below.

### Why is the formula structured this way?

- The **first factor** $kTB$ is the *available thermal noise power* (in Watts) at the receiver input from a matched source at standard temperature $T = 290\,K$ (the IEEE standard "room-temperature" reference).
- Multiplying by the **receiver noise figure** $F$ (dimensionless, $\geq 1$) gives the *total* noise power the receiver itself contributes from its own internal circuitry.
- Multiplying by the **minimum acceptable output SNR** $(S/N)_{o,\min}$ scales the noise up to the smallest input signal that is *still* usable at the loudspeaker/headphone.

## 2.2 Characteristic 2 — Selectivity

**Definition:**
> *Selectivity is the ability of the receiver to discriminate against signals at frequencies neighbouring the desired signal frequency, i.e., to accept the desired band and reject all others.*

### How is selectivity quantified?
By measuring the receiver's **attenuation** at a given offset frequency (e.g., 10 kHz, 20 kHz, etc.) from the tuned centre frequency. The standard measure is the **bandwidth of the IF filter** at the **-3 dB** and **-60 dB** points.

$$BW_{3dB} \;=\; f_{c} \cdot \sqrt{ \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} - \frac{1}{Q^{2}_{\text{loaded}}} }$$

But at the KTU level, students are expected to remember the **ideal selectivity curve**:

- **-3 dB bandwidth** = *passband* (where signals pass through with ≤ 29.3 % power loss).
- **-20 dB bandwidth** = *adjacent-channel* rejection benchmark.
- **-60 dB bandwidth** = *far-off* rejection benchmark (used in KTU numerical problems).

## 2.3 Characteristic 3 — Fidelity

**Definition:**
> *Fidelity is the degree to which a receiver is able to reproduce, at its output, the exact waveform of the original modulating signal applied at the transmitter.*

A perfectly faithful receiver would have a **flat amplitude response** and **linear phase response** across the entire audio band (typically 30 Hz – 15 kHz for Hi-Fi, 30 Hz – 5 kHz for AM broadcast). In practice, fidelity is degraded by:

- IF filter passband ripple.
- Audio-amplifier frequency response.
- Detector (envelope) non-linearity.
- AGC action that compresses high-frequency components.

## 2.4 Characteristic 4 — Image Frequency Rejection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the single most-marks-losing topic in Module 1. Master it.**

The **image frequency** $f_{si}$ of an incoming signal at $f_s$ for a *high-side* local oscillator ($f_{LO} = f_s + f_{IF}$) is:

$$f_{si} \;=\; f_{LO} + f_{IF} \;=\; f_s + 2\,f_{IF}$$

For a *low-side* LO ($f_{LO} = f_s - f_{IF}$):

$$f_{si} \;=\; f_{LO} - f_{IF} \;=\; f_s - 2\,f_{IF}$$

### The Image Frequency Rejection Ratio (IFRR)

$$IFRR \;=\; \frac{\alpha_{s}}{\alpha_{i}}$$

Where $\alpha_s$ is the attenuation (in dB) of the **desired signal** at the RF amplifier input, and $\alpha_i$ is the attenuation of the **image signal**. In decibels:

$$IFRR_{dB} \;=\; \alpha_{i,dB} \;-\; \alpha_{s,dB}$$

## 2.5 Characteristic 5 — Adjacent Channel Rejection (ACR)

$$ACR_{dB} \;=\; 20 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{V_{\text{adjacent}}}{V_{\text{desired}}} \right)$$

A *higher* ACR (in dB) = *better* receiver. Typical broadcast ACR specs are **40 – 60 dB**.

## 2.6 Characteristic 6 — Dynamic Range

$$DR_{dB} \;=\; 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{P_{\max}}{P_{\min}} \right)$$

The minimum is set by **sensitivity**, the maximum by the **1-dB compression point** of the front-end LNA/mixer.

---

## 2.7 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print / screenshot this table — these are the only formulas you need for KTU 2024 Module 1 numericals.**

| # | Formula (cleaned of unsafe pipes) | Symbol Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|----------------------------------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | $S_{\min} \;=\; k\,T\,B \cdot F \cdot (S/N)_{o,\min}$ | Minimum detectable input signal power | Watts (W) |
| 2 | $N_{th} \;=\; k\,T\,B$ | Available thermal noise from source | Watts |
| 3 | $k \;=\; 1.3806 \times 10^{-23}$ | Boltzmann constant | J/K |
| 4 | $T \;=\; 290$ | Standard IEEE reference temperature | Kelvin (K) |
| 5 | $f_{si} \;=\; f_{s} \pm 2\,f_{IF}$ | Image frequency (high/low side LO) | Hz |
| 6 | $IFRR \;=\; \alpha_{i} \,/\, \alpha_{s}$ | Image frequency rejection ratio | ratio |
| 7 | $IFRR_{dB} \;=\; \alpha_{i,dB} - \alpha_{s,dB}$ | IFRR expressed in decibels | dB |
| 8 | $ACR_{dB} \;=\; 20\log_{10}(V_{adj}/V_{des})$ | Adjacent channel rejection | dB |
| 9 | $DR_{dB} \;=\; 10\log_{10}(P_{max}/P_{min})$ | Spurious-free dynamic range | dB |
| 10 | $Q_{\text{loaded}} \;=\; f_c \,/\, BW_{3dB}$ | Loaded quality factor of IF stage | dimensionless |
| 11 | $F_{total} \;=\; F_{1} + (F_{2}-1)/G_{1} + (F_{3}-1)/(G_{1}G_{2})$ | Friis cascade noise figure | dimensionless |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are not academic exercises — they are the **exact calculations a Qualcomm, Intel, or Samsung RF design engineer performs every day** when designing:

- **Cell-phone front-ends** (sub-6 GHz 5G bands).
- **Satellite ground stations** (where image rejection > 80 dB is mandatory).
- **Software Defined Radios (SDR)** like the HackRF / RTL-SDR.
- **Automotive radar** at 77 GHz.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Detectable Signal (Sensitivity Equation)

### Goal
Derive $S_{\min} = kTB \cdot F \cdot (S/N)_{o,\min}$ starting from first principles of noise in linear systems.

### Step 1 — Available thermal noise from the antenna
By the **Nyquist theorem** (1928), a resistor (or matched antenna of equivalent noise temperature $T$) generates a mean-square open-circuit noise voltage:

$$\overline{v_{n}^{2}} \;=\; 4\,k\,T\,R\,\Delta f$$

Delivered into a matched load $R$, the **available noise power** is:

$$N_{th} \;=\; \frac{\overline{v_{n}^{2}}}{4R} \;=\; k\,T\,B$$

### Step 2 — Total noise at the receiver output
The receiver itself is *not* noiseless. The **noise figure** $F$ (or noise factor) is *defined* as the ratio of input SNR to output SNR:

$$F \;=\; \frac{(S/N)_{i}}{(S/N)_{o}}$$

If the input signal is $S_{i}$ and the input noise is $N_{i} = kTB$, then the *total* output noise referred back to the input is:

$$N_{o,\text{total, ref. input}} \;=\; F \cdot k\,T\,B$$

### Step 3 — Apply the minimum usable output SNR
For the receiver output to be intelligible, the output SNR must be at least $(S/N)_{o,\min}$ (typically **10 dB** for AM, **26 dB** for FM). Therefore, the **minimum usable input signal** is:

$$S_{i,\min} \;=\; (S/N)_{o,\min} \cdot F \cdot k\,T\,B$$

### Step 4 — Final Compact Form

$$\boxed{\,S_{\min} \;=\; k\,T\,B \cdot F \cdot \left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{o,\min}\,}$$

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (Board-style)

> **Problem (typical KTU 2024 Module 1, 7-mark):**
> An AM broadcast receiver operates at $T = 290$ K with an IF bandwidth $B = 10$ kHz, a noise figure $F = 6$ dB, and a required output SNR of **10 dB**. Find the **minimum detectable input signal power** $S_{\min}$ in dBm.

**Solution — full step-by-step:**

Step (a) — Convert noise figure from dB to linear:
$$F_{\text{lin}} \;=\; 10^{6/10} \;=\; 10^{0.6} \;=\; 3.981$$

Step (b) — Convert required SNR from dB to linear:
$$\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{o,\min} \;=\; 10^{10/10} \;=\; 10$$

Step (c) — Compute thermal noise power:
$$N_{th} \;=\; k\,T\,B \;=\; (1.3806 \times 10^{-23})(290)(10 \times 10^{3})$$

Carrying out the multiplication explicitly:

$$N_{th} \;=\; 1.3806 \times 10^{-23} \times 290 \times 10^{4}$$

$$= \;1.3806 \times 290 \times 10^{-19}$$

$$= \;400.374 \times 10^{-19}$$

$$= \;4.00374 \times 10^{-17} \;\text{W}$$

Step (d) — Multiply by linear noise figure:
$$N_{\text{total}} \;=\; F_{\text{lin}} \cdot N_{th} \;=\; 3.981 \times 4.00374 \times 10^{-17}$$

$$\approx \;1.594 \times 10^{-16}\;\text{W}$$

Step (e) — Multiply by required SNR:
$$S_{\min} \;=\; 10 \times 1.594 \times 10^{-16} \;=\; 1.594 \times 10^{-15}\;\text{W}$$

Step (f) — Convert to dBm:
$$S_{\min,\,dBm} \;=\; 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1.594 \times 10^{-15}}{10^{-3}}\right)$$

$$=\; 10 \log_{10}(1.594 \times 10^{-12})$$

$$=\; 10 \times (-11.797)$$

$$=\; -117.97 \;\text{dBm} \;\approx\; -118 \;\text{dBm}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU benchmark answer**: A well-designed AM broadcast receiver should have sensitivity around **−110 dBm to −120 dBm**. Our result fits the textbook range exactly — this is how you know your derivation is correct.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Image Frequency Rejection Ratio (IFRR)

### Goal
Show that $IFRR = \alpha_s / \alpha_i$ where $\alpha_s$ and $\alpha_i$ are the gains/attenuations at the desired and image frequencies respectively.

### Step 1 — Mixer Output Frequencies
A mixer with input RF at $f_s$ and local oscillator at $f_{LO}$ produces sum and difference products:

$$f_{\text{out}} \;=\; \vert\, f_{LO} \pm f_{s}\,\vert$$

Setting the difference equal to the IF:

$$f_{LO} - f_{s} \;=\; f_{IF} \quad\Rightarrow\quad f_{LO} \;=\; f_{s} + f_{IF}$$

### Step 2 — Identify the Image
If a *second* signal appears at $f_{si}$ such that:

$$f_{si} - f_{LO} \;=\; f_{IF} \quad\Rightarrow\quad f_{si} \;=\; f_{LO} + f_{IF} \;=\; f_{s} + 2\,f_{IF}$$

This *image* signal $f_{si}$ will produce *exactly the same* IF output as the desired signal $f_s$. Both will be heard simultaneously — a *ghost*.

### Step 3 — Front-End Attenuation Model
Before reaching the mixer, both $f_s$ and $f_{si}$ pass through the RF amplifier and preselector (antenna tuner). These blocks have *finite* gain $\alpha_s$ at $f_s$ and a *smaller* gain $\alpha_i$ at $f_{si}$ (because the preselector is a bandpass filter centred on $f_s$, with a skirt that rolls off).

### Step 4 — Define IFRR
By the standard IEEE definition:

$$IFRR \;=\; \frac{\text{Gain at desired signal frequency}}{\text{Gain at image signal frequency}} \;=\; \frac{\alpha_{s}}{\alpha_{i}}$$

Expressed in decibels:

$$IFRR_{dB} \;=\; 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\alpha_{s}}{\alpha_{i}}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Common student confusion**: Some textbooks use the *inverse* ratio. KTU 2024 scheme expects the **image attenuation relative to the desired signal attenuation**, so a value like "IFRR = 30 dB" means the image is attenuated 30 dB *more* than the desired signal. Always state the convention in your answer.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — Image Frequency

> **Problem (typical KTU 14-mark):**
> An AM superheterodyne receiver is tuned to $f_s = 1000$ kHz with an IF of $f_{IF} = 455$ kHz. The local oscillator is set *above* the signal frequency. The RF amplifier has a voltage gain of **20 dB at 1000 kHz** and a voltage gain of **6 dB at the image frequency**. Calculate the **image frequency** and the **IFRR in dB**.

**Solution:**

Step (a) — Compute local oscillator frequency:
$$f_{LO} \;=\; f_{s} + f_{IF} \;=\; 1000 + 455 \;=\; 1455 \;\text{kHz}$$

Step (b) — Compute image frequency (high-side LO):
$$f_{si} \;=\; f_{LO} + f_{IF} \;=\; 1455 + 455 \;=\; 1910 \;\text{kHz}$$

Step (c) — Compute IFRR in dB:
$$IFRR_{dB} \;=\; \alpha_{s,dB} - \alpha_{i,dB} \;=\; 20 - 6 \;=\; 14 \;\text{dB}$$

Step (d) — Verify by linear ratio:
$$IFRR_{\text{lin}} \;=\; 10^{14/20} \;=\; 5.01$$

So the image is roughly **5 times weaker** than the desired signal at the mixer input — still audible but attenuated.

---

## 3.4 The Friis Cascade Noise Figure (Advanced, Frequently Tested)

When multiple stages are cascaded, the **total noise figure** of the receiver chain is:

$$F_{\text{total}} \;=\; F_1 \;+\; \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} \;+\; \frac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2} \;+\; \cdots$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Implication:** The **first stage's noise figure $F_1$ and gain $G_1$ dominate** the cascade. This is why every receiver design — from your Wi-Fi router to a deep-space probe — puts the **LNA (Low-Noise Amplifier) as the very first active block** after the antenna. A high-gain, low-noise first stage makes the noise contribution of all subsequent mixers and IF amplifiers almost negligible.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Superheterodyne Receiver

This diagram traces the **signal flow** through every block of a receiver and annotates *which characteristic* each block primarily governs. Use this as a *visual memory map* during the exam.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ANT[Antenna RF Input]:::in --> PRE[Preselector Bandpass Filter]:::filter
    PRE --> RFAMP[RF Amplifier LNA]:::amp
    RFAMP --> MIX[Mixer Product Detector]:::mix
    LO[Local Oscillator fLO]:::osc -.-> MIX
    MIX --> IFAMP1[IF Amplifier Stage 1]:::amp
    IFAMP1 --> IFAMP2[IF Amplifier Stage 2]:::amp
    IFAMP2 --> DET[Envelope Detector]:::det
    DET --> AFAMP[Audio Amplifier]:::amp
    AFAMP --> SPK[Loudspeaker Output]:::out
    AGC[AGC Feedback Loop]:::ctrl -.-> RFAMP
    AGC -.-> IFAMP1
    AGC -.-> IFAMP2

    classDef in fill:#FFEFD5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef filter fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef amp fill:#E0F8E0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mix fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef osc fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef det fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#D8BFD8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Mapping of Characteristics to Receiver Blocks

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Which characteristic?} -->|Sensitivity| A1[RF Amplifier LNA Noise Figure F]:::sens
    Q1 -->|Selectivity| A2[IF Bandpass Filter BW at -3 dB and -60 dB]:::sel
    Q1 -->|Fidelity| A3[Audio Amplifier and Detector Linearity]:::fid
    Q1 -->|Image Rejection| A4[Preselector and RF Amplifier Roll-off]:::img
    Q1 -->|Dynamic Range| A5[AGC Loop and 1 dB Compression Point]:::dr
    Q1 -->|Spurious Response| A6[Mixer Linearity and IP3]:::spu

    classDef sens fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sel fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fid fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef img fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dr fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef spu fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Selectivity Curve (Conceptual Frequency Response)

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Idealised Selectivity Curve of an IF Filter"
    x-axis "Frequency offset from carrier in kHz" [0, 5, 10, 20, 30, 50, 100, 200]
    y-axis "Gain in dB" 0 --> -80
    line [0, -3, -6, -20, -35, -50, -65, -80]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading this curve for KTU numericals:**
> - At **+5 kHz** offset, gain has dropped to **−3 dB** → this defines the **−3 dB bandwidth = 10 kHz**.
> - At **+20 kHz** offset, gain is **−20 dB** → this is the **adjacent-channel rejection** benchmark.
> - At **+200 kHz** offset, gain is **−80 dB** → this is the **far-out rejection**.

## 4.4 Image Frequency Visualization (Two Stations Mixing to Same IF)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Desired Signal fs equals 1000 kHz]:::sig
    IM[Image Signal fsi equals 1910 kHz]:::img
    LO[Local Oscillator fLO equals 1455 kHz]:::osc
    M[Mixer]:::mix
    IF[IF Output at 455 kHz]:::if

    S --> M
    IM --> M
    LO --> M
    M --> IF

    classDef sig fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef img fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef osc fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mix fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef if fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The diagram above shows the **fundamental problem**: both 1000 kHz (real) and 1910 kHz (image) produce an IF output of **455 kHz**. Without a preselector filter, both stations would be heard simultaneously. The preselector + RF amplifier must attenuate 1910 kHz by *at least* 30–40 dB relative to 1000 kHz.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Image frequency formula direction** — most students write $f_{si} = f_s + f_{IF}$ instead of $f_{si} = f_s + 2 f_{IF}$. Deducted **2 marks instantly**.
> 2. **Noise figure in dB vs linear** — never multiply a dB value directly with a Watts value. Always convert $F_{dB} \rightarrow F_{\text{lin}}$ first.
> 3. **dBm vs dBW confusion** — KTU expects dBm (referenced to 1 mW) unless explicitly stated otherwise.
> 4. **Selectivity vs Sensitivity** — these are *not* the same. Selectivity is about *rejecting* signals; sensitivity is about *detecting* weak signals. Confusing them is a **3-mark loser**.

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the sensitivity of a radio receiver. What is the typical minimum output SNR for AM broadcast reception?**

**Model Answer (3 marks, board-exam phrasing):**

The **sensitivity** of a radio receiver is defined as the minimum input RF signal voltage (or power) required at the antenna terminals to produce a **specified standard output signal-to-noise ratio** at the loudspeaker.

For an **AM broadcast receiver**, the standard minimum output SNR is **10 dB (linear ratio = 10)**. For an **FM broadcast receiver**, it is **26 dB** because FM uses a higher SNR threshold to take advantage of its noise-suppression property (the *FM capture effect*).

Sensitivity is fundamentally **noise-limited**, governed by the expression $S_{\min} = k T B \cdot F \cdot (S/N)_{o,\min}$.

---

### Question 2 — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is meant by image frequency in a superheterodyne receiver? How is it calculated?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **image frequency** $f_{si}$ is the unwanted signal frequency that, along with the local oscillator frequency $f_{LO}$, produces *exactly the same* intermediate frequency $f_{IF}$ at the mixer output as the desired signal $f_s$. If it is not rejected before the mixer, it appears as a "ghost" station superimposed on the desired one.

For a **high-side LO** ($f_{LO} = f_s + f_{IF}$):

$$f_{si} \;=\; f_{LO} + f_{IF} \;=\; f_s + 2 f_{IF}$$

For a **low-side LO** ($f_{LO} = f_s - f_{IF}$):

$$f_{si} \;=\; f_{LO} - f_{IF} \;=\; f_s - 2 f_{IF}$$

It is rejected by the **preselector bandpass filter** and the RF amplifier, which are designed to have high attenuation at $f_{si}$.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]
**(a)** Derive the expression for the **minimum detectable signal (sensitivity)** of a superheterodyne receiver. Explain each term clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An AM broadcast receiver operates at $T = 290$ K with IF bandwidth $B = 8$ kHz, noise figure $F = 8$ dB, and required output SNR of 10 dB. Calculate the **minimum detectable signal** in dBm. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Available thermal noise from source (2 marks):**
By Nyquist's theorem, a matched antenna source at temperature $T$ delivers available noise power:

$$N_{th} \;=\; k\,T\,B$$

where $k = 1.3806 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K is Boltzmann's constant.

**Step 2 — Receiver noise contribution (2 marks):**
The receiver itself adds internal noise, quantified by the **noise figure** $F$ (linear), defined as the ratio of input SNR to output SNR. The total noise referred to the input becomes:

$$N_{o,\text{total, ref. input}} \;=\; F \cdot k\,T\,B$$

**Step 3 — Apply minimum SNR criterion (2 marks):**
For intelligible reception, the output SNR must exceed $(S/N)_{o,\min}$ (10 dB for AM). Hence, the minimum input signal is:

$$\boxed{\,S_{\min} \;=\; k\,T\,B \cdot F \cdot \left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{o,\min}\,}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation (1 mark):**
A 10× improvement in noise figure (e.g., from 10 dB to 0 dB) reduces $S_{\min}$ by 10 dB, allowing the receiver to detect 10× weaker stations — this is the entire rationale behind the LNA front-end.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Convert $F$ from dB to linear (1 mark):**

$$F_{\text{lin}} \;=\; 10^{8/10} \;=\; 10^{0.8} \;\approx\; 6.31$$

**Step 2 — Convert SNR from dB to linear (1 mark):**

$$(S/N)_{o,\min} \;=\; 10^{10/10} \;=\; 10$$

**Step 3 — Compute thermal noise power (1.5 marks):**

$$N_{th} \;=\; (1.3806 \times 10^{-23})(290)(8 \times 10^{3})$$

$$= \;1.3806 \times 290 \times 8 \times 10^{-20}$$

$$= \;3.203 \times 10^{-17}\;\text{W}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by $F_{\text{lin}}$ (1 mark):**

$$N_{\text{total}} \;=\; 6.31 \times 3.203 \times 10^{-17} \;=\; 2.022 \times 10^{-16}\;\text{W}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by required SNR (1 mark):**

$$S_{\min} \;=\; 10 \times 2.022 \times 10^{-16} \;=\; 2.022 \times 10^{-15}\;\text{W}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to dBm (1.5 marks):**

$$S_{\min,\,dBm} \;=\; 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2.022 \times 10^{-15}}{10^{-3}}\right)$$

$$=\; 10 \log_{10}(2.022 \times 10^{-12})$$

$$=\; 10 \times (-11.694)$$

$$=\; \boxed{-116.94 \;\text{dBm}}$$

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1] — Alternative Choice
**(a)** Define **selectivity** and **fidelity** of a radio receiver. How are they measured? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A superheterodyne receiver is tuned to $f_s = 1200$ kHz with $f_{IF} = 455$ kHz. The RF amplifier has a gain of 25 dB at 1200 kHz and 8 dB at the image frequency. Calculate **(i)** the **image frequency** and **(ii)** the **image frequency rejection ratio (IFRR)** in dB. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**Selectivity (3.5 marks):**
**Definition**: Selectivity is the ability of a receiver to discriminate against signals at frequencies neighbouring the desired signal frequency, accepting only the desired band.
**Measurement**: It is measured by plotting the **bandwidth response curve** of the IF filter, noting the attenuation at specific offset frequencies — typically **−3 dB bandwidth** (defines the passband), **−20 dB** (adjacent-channel rejection), and **−60 dB** (far-out rejection). A higher attenuation at a given offset = better selectivity.

**Fidelity (3.5 marks):**
**Definition**: Fidelity is the degree to which the receiver output waveform reproduces the original modulating waveform applied at the transmitter.
**Measurement**: It is measured by feeding a known **audio test signal** (e.g., 400 Hz tone, or swept sine from 30 Hz – 15 kHz) at the transmitter and observing the receiver output on an **oscilloscope / distortion analyser**. Quantitative metrics include **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)** and **frequency-response flatness** in dB.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Part (i) — Image Frequency (3.5 marks):**

For a **high-side LO** (assume high-side by default unless stated otherwise in KTU):

$$f_{LO} \;=\; f_s + f_{IF} \;=\; 1200 + 455 \;=\; 1655 \;\text{kHz}$$

$$f_{si} \;=\; f_{LO} + f_{IF} \;=\; 1655 + 455 \;=\; \boxed{2110 \;\text{kHz}}$$

**Part (ii) — IFRR in dB (3.5 marks):**

$$IFRR_{dB} \;=\; \alpha_{s,dB} - \alpha_{i,dB} \;=\; 25 - 8 \;=\; \boxed{17 \;\text{dB}}$$

Equivalent linear ratio: $IFRR_{\text{lin}} = 10^{17/20} \approx 7.08$.

**Interpretation:** The image signal at 2110 kHz is attenuated **17 dB** more than the desired 1200 kHz signal before reaching the mixer — sufficient to suppress most interferers but not excellent (a high-quality communications receiver typically achieves 50–80 dB IFRR).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print and Read 10 Minutes Before the Exam**

- [ ] **Sensitivity** is set by **internal noise** (Friis cascade, dominated by the *first* stage's noise figure).
- [ ] **Selectivity** is set by the **IF bandpass filter bandwidth** (measured at −3 dB, −20 dB, −60 dB).
- [ ] **Fidelity** is about **reproducing the modulating signal faithfully** (THD, frequency response flatness).
- [ ] **Image frequency** is $f_{si} = f_s + 2 f_{IF}$ (high-side LO) — remember the **factor of 2**, not 1.
- [ ] **Image rejection** comes from the **preselector filter and RF amplifier roll-off**, *not* the IF stage.
- [ ] **Dynamic range** = from sensitivity floor to the **1-dB compression point** of the LNA/mixer.
- [ ] **Higher LO frequency** → image pushed farther away → easier to reject, BUT also higher mixer spurious products.
- [ ] **Higher IF frequency** → better image rejection, BUT worse selectivity (wider IF bandwidth proportional to signal BW).
- [ ] **Lower IF frequency** → better selectivity, BUT image is closer → harder to reject. This trade-off is the *design dilemma*.
- [ ] Always convert **dB → linear** before multiplying quantities, and **linear → dB** after.
- [ ] Standard reference temperature $T = 290$ K (IEEE), Boltzmann $k = 1.3806 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.
- [ ] **AM broadcast standard**: 10 dB output SNR. **FM broadcast standard**: 26 dB output SNR.
- [ ] **Friis cascade formula**: $F_{\text{total}} = F_1 + (F_2-1)/G_1 + \cdots$ — first stage dominates.
- [ ] **Sensitivity in dBm**: $S_{\min,dBm} = 10\log_{10}(S_{\min}/10^{-3})$.
- [ ] **Examiner's favourite 7-mark trap**: forgetting the factor of 2 in $f_{si}$. Memorize it cold.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Image Frequency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT502 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Image Frequency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In a **Superheterodyne Receiver**, the **Image Frequency** (denoted $f_i$) is an *unwanted* radio frequency that is symmetrically located on the *opposite side* of the **Local Oscillator (LO)** frequency, separated from the desired signal frequency by **twice the Intermediate Frequency (IF)**. It is the spurious RF input that, after mixing with the LO, produces an output at exactly the same IF as the desired signal, thereby causing **co-channel interference** that cannot be removed by the subsequent IF stages.

Mathematically, the image frequency is:

$$f_i = f_s \pm 2 f_{IF}$$

where $f_s$ is the desired signal frequency and $f_{IF}$ is the intermediate frequency. The sign of $\pm$ depends on whether the local oscillator is *above* (high-side injection) or *below* (low-side injection) the signal frequency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 1):** Image frequency is a *direct consequence* of the non-linear mixing operation. Once an image-frequency signal enters the mixer, it is *indistinguishable* from the desired signal at the IF output. Therefore, image rejection MUST be performed by the **RF amplifier** (before the mixer), not by the IF amplifier (after the mixer).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine you are standing in a tunnel that has two entrances — one on your **left** and one on your **right**. You are told to listen for a friend calling you from a distance of exactly **10 meters** (the IF). Your friend stands 10 m to your left. Now, suppose a **stranger** also happens to be standing exactly 10 m to your right. If you close your eyes, you cannot tell whether the voice is coming from the left (real friend) or the right (imposter) — they both sound "10 m away."

In this analogy:
- **You** = the mixer
- **10 m on your left** = the desired signal $f_s$
- **10 m on your right** = the image signal $f_i$
- **Your friend vs. the stranger** = both produce the same IF output
- **The solution** = add a *directional filter* (RF amplifier with tuned circuits) that "blocks" the right side, which is the function of front-end selectivity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Meaning:** Image frequency is the *mirror-image* of the desired RF signal, as seen across the local oscillator. It is "image" because it sits at a mirror-symmetric distance from the LO, just as the signal does. Without proper RF selectivity, this mirror image corrupts your reception.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Standard broadcast AM band IF: **455 kHz**
- Standard broadcast FM band IF: **10.7 MHz**
- TV receiver IF (older analog): **38.9 MHz** (chosen high for better image rejection)
- Typical Q of RF amplifier tuned circuit: **50 to 150**
- Acceptable IFRR in commercial receivers: **≥ 50 dB** for AM, **≥ 80 dB** for FM

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Image Frequency Exists — The Underlying Mechanism

The **Mixer** in a superheterodyne receiver is a non-linear device (or a linear multiplier in the ideal case). When two sinusoids — the RF signal and the local oscillator — are applied to the mixer, the output contains sum and difference frequency components:

$$v_{mixer}(t) = A_s \cos(2\pi f_s t) \cdot A_{LO} \cos(2\pi f_{LO} t)$$

Applying the trigonometric identity $\cos A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$:

$$v_{mixer}(t) = \frac{A_s A_{LO}}{2}\left[\cos 2\pi(f_{LO} - f_s)t + \cos 2\pi(f_{LO} + f_s)t\right]$$

The IF stage is tuned to select only the **difference frequency** $\vert f_{LO} - f_s \vert = f_{IF}$.

### 2.1.1 The "Mirror" Problem

For the desired signal at $f_s$:

$$f_{LO} - f_s = f_{IF} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_{LO} = f_s + f_{IF} \quad \text{(High-side injection)}$$

Now suppose an **unwanted** RF signal $f_i$ is present at the antenna. The mixer will also produce:

$$f_{LO} - f_i = (f_s + f_{IF}) - f_i$$

For this to equal $f_{IF}$:

$$(f_s + f_{IF}) - f_i = f_{IF} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_i = f_s$$

That's just the signal itself, not a problem. But for it to equal **$-f_{IF}$** (whose absolute value is $f_{IF}$):

$$(f_s + f_{IF}) - f_i = -f_{IF} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_i = f_s + 2 f_{IF}$$

This is the **image frequency**. It lies at a distance of $2f_{IF}$ *above* the desired signal when the LO is on the high side, and at a distance of $2f_{IF}$ *below* when the LO is on the low side.

## 2.2 Structured Logic Flow

1. **Step 1 — Tuning:** The user dials a desired station at $f_s$. The local oscillator is automatically set to $f_{LO} = f_s \pm f_{IF}$.
2. **Step 2 — Mixing:** Any RF signal near the antenna — desired or otherwise — mixes with $f_{LO}$ in the mixer.
3. **Step 3 — IF Filtering:** The IF filter passes only the band centered at $f_{IF}$.
4. **Step 4 — The Trap:** Any signal at $f_s + 2f_{IF}$ (or $f_s - 2f_{IF}$) ALSO produces $f_{IF}$ at the mixer output, defeating the IF filter.
5. **Step 5 — The Fix:** The RF amplifier (front-end) must attenuate signals at the image frequency *before* they reach the mixer, typically by 40 to 80 dB.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Expression | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|--------|------------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Desired signal frequency | $f_s$ | Given | Hz | RF input we wish to receive |
| 2 | Intermediate frequency | $f_{IF}$ | Fixed by design | Hz | Constant for a given receiver |
| 3 | LO frequency (High-side) | $f_{LO}$ | $f_s + f_{IF}$ | Hz | LO above signal (most common) |
| 4 | LO frequency (Low-side) | $f_{LO}$ | $f_s - f_{IF}$ | Hz | LO below signal |
| 5 | Image frequency (High-side) | $f_i$ | $f_s + 2f_{IF}$ | Hz | Spurious signal location |
| 6 | Image frequency (Low-side) | $f_i$ | $f_s - 2f_{IF}$ | Hz | Spurious signal location |
| 7 | IFRR (linear) | $\rho$ | $\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{4 Q f_{IF}}{f_s}\right)^{2}}$ | unitless | Exact, single-tuned RF stage |
| 8 | IFRR (approximation) | $\rho$ | $\dfrac{4 Q f_{IF}}{f_s}$ | unitless | Valid when $4 Q f_{IF} \gg f_s$ |
| 9 | IFRR (in decibels) | $\rho_{dB}$ | $20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4 Q f_{IF}}{f_s}\right)$ | dB | Standard specification |
| 10 | Bandwidth of RF stage | $BW$ | $f_s / Q$ | Hz | Useful for selectivity check |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** A *higher* IF improves IFRR (because $f_{IF}$ appears in the numerator) but *worsens* adjacent-channel selectivity at the IF stage (because a higher IF means a wider IF bandwidth for a given fractional bandwidth). The IF must be chosen as a compromise — AM broadcast uses 455 kHz, FM uses 10.7 MHz.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **AM broadcast receivers (540–1600 kHz):** IF = 455 kHz; the image band lies 910 kHz above the desired signal, falling mostly outside the AM broadcast band — manageable.
- **FM broadcast receivers (88–108 MHz):** IF = 10.7 MHz; the image is 21.4 MHz above the desired signal, well outside the FM band — easy to filter.
- **Aircraft communication receivers (118–137 MHz):** IF = 21.4 MHz; high IF chosen specifically for image rejection in a crowded spectrum.
- **Radar & microwave links:** Use *double conversion* (two mixers, two IFs) to push image rejection beyond 100 dB.
- **Software Defined Radio (SDR):** Often use a *zero-IF* or *low-IF* architecture to *eliminate* the image problem entirely by quadrature (I/Q) processing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Expression for Image Frequency

**Given:**
- Desired RF signal: $f_s$
- Intermediate frequency: $f_{IF}$ (a fixed design constant)
- Mixer is an ideal multiplier producing sum and difference frequencies

**Step 1:** The local oscillator is tuned to deliver exactly $f_{IF}$ frequency difference with the signal:

$$f_{LO} = f_s + f_{IF} \quad \text{(Case A — High-side injection)}$$

**Step 2:** Express the mixer output in general form. Let the input be any RF frequency $f_{RF}$ present at the antenna. The mixer produces:

$$f_{mixer\,out} = \vert f_{LO} - f_{RF} \vert = \vert f_s + f_{IF} - f_{RF} \vert$$

**Step 3:** The IF filter passes only the band centered at $f_{IF}$. We require the mixer output to lie at $f_{IF}$:

$$\vert f_s + f_{IF} - f_{RF} \vert = f_{IF}$$

**Step 4:** This gives **two** solutions for $f_{RF}$:

$$f_s + f_{IF} - f_{RF} = +f_{IF} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_{RF} = f_s \quad \text{(the desired signal)}$$

$$f_s + f_{IF} - f_{RF} = -f_{IF} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_{RF} = f_s + 2 f_{IF} \quad \text{(the image)}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{f_i = f_s + 2 f_{IF} \quad \text{(high-side injection)}}$$

By symmetric argument, for low-side injection ($f_{LO} = f_s - f_{IF}$):

$$\boxed{f_i = f_s - 2 f_{IF} \quad \text{(low-side injection)}}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** The image is always $2 f_{IF}$ away from the desired signal, *on the far side* of the local oscillator.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Image Frequency Rejection Ratio (IFRR)

**Given:**
- RF amplifier modeled as a single-tuned circuit
- Center frequency of RF amplifier: $f_0 = f_s$ (it tracks the desired signal)
- Quality factor of the tuned circuit: $Q$
- Image frequency: $f_i = f_s + 2 f_{IF}$ (high-side case)

**Step 1:** Voltage gain of a single-tuned circuit at any frequency $f$:

$$|\alpha(f)| = \frac{\alpha_{max}}{\sqrt{1 + Q^{2}\!\left(\dfrac{f}{f_0} - \dfrac{f_0}{f}\right)^{\!2}}}$$

where $\alpha_{max}$ is the gain at resonance.

**Step 2:** Evaluate at the signal frequency $f = f_s = f_0$:

$$\left(\frac{f_s}{f_0} - \frac{f_0}{f_s}\right) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad |\alpha(f_s)| = \alpha_{max}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate at the image frequency $f = f_i = f_s + 2 f_{IF}$. Define $\delta = \dfrac{2 f_{IF}}{f_s}$ (a small, dimensionless number). Then:

$$\frac{f_i}{f_s} - \frac{f_s}{f_i} = (1 + \delta) - \frac{1}{1 + \delta}$$

Rationalising the second term:

$$(1 + \delta) - \frac{1}{1 + \delta} = \frac{(1 + \delta)^{2} - 1}{1 + \delta} = \frac{2\delta + \delta^{2}}{1 + \delta}$$

For typical values $\delta \ll 1$ (since $f_{IF} \ll f_s$ in broadcast receivers), the first-order approximation is:

$$\frac{f_i}{f_s} - \frac{f_s}{f_i} \approx 2\delta = \frac{4 f_{IF}}{f_s}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the gain expression:

$$|\alpha(f_i)| = \frac{\alpha_{max}}{\sqrt{1 + Q^{2}\!\left(\dfrac{4 f_{IF}}{f_s}\right)^{\!2}}}$$

**Step 5:** Define IFRR as the ratio of signal gain to image gain (signal-favoring definition):

$$\text{IFRR} = \frac{|\alpha(f_s)|}{|\alpha(f_i)|} = \frac{\alpha_{max}}{\dfrac{\alpha_{max}}{\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{4 Q f_{IF}}{f_s}\right)^{2}}}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{IFRR} = \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{4 Q f_{IF}}{f_s}\right)^{2}}}$$

**Step 6:** When $4 Q f_{IF} \gg f_s$ (typical case in well-designed receivers), the "1" inside the square root is negligible:

$$\boxed{\text{IFRR} \approx \frac{4 Q f_{IF}}{f_s}}$$

**Step 7:** In decibels:

$$\text{IFRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4 Q f_{IF}}{f_s}\right) \text{ dB}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** An AM superheterodyne receiver tunes to a station at $f_s = 1000$ kHz. The IF is 455 kHz. The RF amplifier has a single-tuned circuit with $Q = 100$. Calculate (a) the LO frequency, (b) the image frequency, and (c) the IFRR in dB.

**Solution:**

**(a) Local Oscillator Frequency (High-side injection):**

$$f_{LO} = f_s + f_{IF} = 1000 + 455 = 1455 \text{ kHz}$$

**(b) Image Frequency:**

$$f_i = f_s + 2 f_{IF} = 1000 + 2(455) = 1000 + 910 = 1910 \text{ kHz}$$

**(c) Image Frequency Rejection Ratio:**

$$\text{IFRR} = \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{4 \times 100 \times 455}{1000}\right)^{2}}$$

$$\text{IFRR} = \sqrt{1 + (182)^{2}} = \sqrt{1 + 33124} = \sqrt{33125}$$

$$\text{IFRR} \approx 182.0 \quad \text{(linear)}$$

$$\text{IFRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(182) \approx 45.2 \text{ dB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation of 45.2 dB:** The signal at 1000 kHz is received at full strength, while an interfering station at 1910 kHz is attenuated by a factor of 182 in voltage (≈ 33,000 times in power) before reaching the mixer. This 45 dB rejection is *just adequate* for AM broadcast quality — commercial AM receivers often require ≥ 50 dB, which is achieved by adding more tuned stages in the RF amplifier.

## 3.4 Sensitivity of IFRR to Design Parameters

| Parameter | Effect on IFRR | Quantitative Change | Trade-off |
|-----------|----------------|---------------------|-----------|
| Increase $f_{IF}$ | ↑ IFRR (linear in $f_{IF}$) | Doubling IF → +6 dB IFRR | Worsens IF selectivity |
| Increase $Q$ of RF stage | ↑ IFRR (linear in $Q$) | Doubling $Q$ → +6 dB IFRR | Narrower RF bandwidth, harder tracking |
| Increase $f_s$ | ↓ IFRR (inverse) | Doubling $f_s$ → -6 dB IFRR | Why HF/VHF use higher IFs |
| Add 2nd RF tuned stage | ↑ IFRR squared | IFRR² (×2 in dB) | Adds insertion loss & noise |
| Use double conversion | ↑ IFRR (huge) | >100 dB possible | Complexity & cost |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Superheterodyne Receiver Showing Image Path

The block diagram below illustrates how the desired signal and the image signal both reach the mixer and produce the same IF output. The RF amplifier's tuned circuit (depicted as the front-end filter) is the *only* stage that can reject the image.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ANT[Antenna - All RF Frequencies] --> RFCKT[RF Tuned Circuit - Selectivity Filter]
    RFCKT --> RFAMP[RF Amplifier]
    LO[Local Oscillator f_LO] --> MIX[Mixer Multiplier]
    RFAMP --> MIX
    MIX --> IFFIL[IF Bandpass Filter at f_IF]
    IFFIL --> IFAMP[IF Amplifier]
    IFAMP --> DEMOD[Demodulator]
    DEMOD --> AUDIO[Audio Output]

    MIX -.produces IF for both.- IFFIL
    RFCKT -.rejects f_image before mixer.- RFAMP
```

## 4.2 Frequency-Domain Architecture — Where Image Sits

The diagram below maps the *frequency relationships* on the RF axis. Notice that $f_s$ and $f_i$ are symmetric about the local oscillator $f_{LO}$, with a separation of $2 f_{IF}$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FRQ_SPECTRUM[RF Frequency Axis]
        direction LR
        NODEFS[f_s - Desired RF Signal]
        NODELO[f_LO - Local Oscillator]
        NODEFIMG[f_image - Unwanted RF Signal]
    end

    NODEFS --- NODELO
    NODELO --- NODEFIMG

    NODEFIMG -. symmetric mirror .-> NODEFS
    NODELO -. center of symmetry .-> NODELO
```

## 4.3 Mixer Input/Output Mapping (Image Generation Mechanism)

This diagram shows the *non-linear mixing operation* explicitly. Two RF inputs ($f_s$ and $f_i$) combined with $f_{LO}$ at the mixer both produce outputs at $f_{IF}$, demonstrating why the IF filter alone cannot distinguish them.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUTS[Antenna and Local Oscillator]
        FSIG[f_s - Desired Signal]
        FIMG[f_i - Image Signal f_s plus 2 f_IF]
        FLO[f_LO - Local Oscillator f_s plus f_IF]
    end

    subgraph MIX[Non-Linear Mixer]
        MIXER[MULTIPLIER STAGE]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[IF Stage Output]
        OUT1[Difference f_LO minus f_s equals f_IF - DESIRED]
        OUT2[Difference f_LO minus f_i equals minus f_IF - MAGNITUDE f_IF - IMAGE INTERFERENCE]
    end

    FSIG --> MIXER
    FIMG --> MIXER
    FLO --> MIXER

    MIXER --> OUT1
    MIXER --> OUT2

    OUT1 -. IF filter cannot separate .-> OUT2
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Flow of the IFRR Improvement Chain

The following shows the architectural strategies a designer uses to push IFRR above 60 dB, mapped as a functional decision flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START[Target IFRR Requirement in dB] --> Q1{IFRR Needed less than 40 dB}
    Q1 -- YES --> SOL1[Use single tuned RF stage with moderate Q]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{IFRR Needed 40 to 70 dB}
    Q2 -- YES --> SOL2[Use double tuned RF stage and higher IF frequency]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3{IFRR Needed greater than 70 dB}
    Q3 -- YES --> SOL3[Use double conversion superheterodyne with two IF stages]
    SOL3 --> SOL4[Or use image reject mixer - Hartley or Weaver architecture]
    SOL1 --> END[Final Design]
    SOL2 --> END
    SOL3 --> END
    SOL4 --> END
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**Q: Define image frequency. How is it related to the signal frequency and intermediate frequency?**

**Model Answer:**

Image frequency $f_i$ is the unwanted RF input frequency in a superheterodyne receiver that, after mixing with the local oscillator, produces an output at the same intermediate frequency as the desired signal. It is mathematically related to the signal frequency $f_s$ and IF $f_{IF}$ as:

$$f_i = f_s \pm 2 f_{IF}$$

The "+" sign corresponds to high-side LO injection ($f_{LO} > f_s$); the "−" sign corresponds to low-side injection ($f_{LO} < f_s$). The image is located at a separation of $2 f_{IF}$ from the desired signal, with the local oscillator lying symmetrically between them.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Key (1 mark each):** (i) Definition, (ii) Formula $f_i = f_s \pm 2 f_{IF}$, (iii) Explanation of symmetry about $f_{LO}$.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Q: What is image frequency rejection ratio (IFRR)? Why is it specified in decibels in receiver datasheets?**

**Model Answer:**

Image Frequency Rejection Ratio (IFRR) is defined as the ratio of the RF amplifier's voltage gain at the desired signal frequency to its gain at the image frequency:

$$\text{IFRR} = \frac{|\alpha(f_s)|}{|\alpha(f_i)|} \approx \frac{4 Q f_{IF}}{f_s}$$

It is expressed in decibels because image rejection values typically span several orders of magnitude (50 to 100, or 34 to 40 dB), and the logarithmic decibel scale makes the specification compact and intuitive. A higher IFRR (in dB) means the receiver is more robust against co-channel interference from stations at the image frequency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Key (1 mark each):** (i) IFRR definition with formula, (ii) Approximation $\approx 4 Q f_{IF} / f_s$, (iii) Reason for dB usage (range, compactness).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice, 14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) Derive the expression for the image frequency in a superheterodyne receiver. Explain why the image frequency must be rejected by the RF amplifier and not by the IF amplifier.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Consider a superheterodyne receiver with:
- Desired signal: $f_s$
- Local oscillator: $f_{LO}$
- Intermediate frequency: $f_{IF}$ (fixed)

The mixer output contains the difference frequency. For the desired signal:

$$f_{LO} - f_s = f_{IF} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_{LO} = f_s + f_{IF} \quad \text{...(i)}$$

Let an unwanted RF signal $f_i$ be present. Its mixer output is:

$$f_{LO} - f_i = (f_s + f_{IF}) - f_i \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

For the IF filter to pass this unwanted signal (i.e., it equals $f_{IF}$):

$$(f_s + f_{IF}) - f_i = -f_{IF}$$

(Since $\cos(-\theta) = \cos\theta$, the negative sign is equivalent in amplitude.)

$$f_i = f_s + 2 f_{IF} \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

This is the image frequency for high-side injection. By similar reasoning for low-side injection, $f_i = f_s - 2 f_{IF}$.

**Why RF, not IF? (2 marks):**

Once the image signal reaches the mixer, it is *converted* to the IF band. The IF filter — being centred at $f_{IF}$ — *cannot* distinguish between the desired IF (from $f_s$) and the image-generated IF (from $f_i$); both lie at the same frequency. Therefore, the image must be rejected **before** the mixer, i.e., by the **RF amplifier's tuned circuits** (front-end selectivity). The IF amplifier's job is to reject adjacent-channel interference, not image.

---

**(b) An AM superheterodyne receiver operates in the 540 kHz to 1600 kHz band with an IF of 455 kHz. The RF amplifier uses a single-tuned circuit with $Q = 80$. Calculate: (i) the image frequency range, (ii) the IFRR (in dB) at the lowest and highest signal frequencies.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Image Frequency Range (3 marks):**

For high-side injection: $f_i = f_s + 2 f_{IF}$

At $f_s = 540$ kHz: $f_i = 540 + 2(455) = 540 + 910 = 1450$ kHz

At $f_s = 1600$ kHz: $f_i = 1600 + 2(455) = 1600 + 910 = 2510$ kHz

$$\boxed{\text{Image Frequency Range} = 1450 \text{ kHz to } 2510 \text{ kHz}}$$

**(ii) IFRR Calculation (4 marks):**

Formula: $\text{IFRR} = 4 Q f_{IF} / f_s$ (approximate, since $4 Q f_{IF} \gg f_s$)

Check: $4 \times 80 \times 455 = 145{,}600$ kHz, much greater than $f_s$ in both cases ✓

**At $f_s = 540$ kHz:**

$$\text{IFRR} = \frac{4 \times 80 \times 455}{540} = \frac{145600}{540} = 269.6$$

$$\text{IFRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(269.6) \approx 48.6 \text{ dB}$$

**At $f_s = 1600$ kHz:**

$$\text{IFRR} = \frac{4 \times 80 \times 455}{1600} = \frac{145600}{1600} = 91.0$$

$$\text{IFRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(91.0) \approx 39.2 \text{ dB}$$

**Observation:** IFRR *decreases* as $f_s$ increases — this is the **slope of the IFRR curve** that designers must check across the tuning band. The worst case is at the highest signal frequency (39.2 dB), which is the limiting specification for the receiver.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — KTU Valuation Warning:** Students frequently (i) confuse the image frequency formula with $f_s + f_{IF}$ (forgetting the factor of 2), and (ii) forget to express the *range* of image frequencies when the receiver tunes across a band. Always state the range as `[lower limit, upper limit]`. Also, plot IFRR vs $f_s$ to show that the **worst case** occurs at the **upper end** of the band — this is a 2-mark KTU favourite.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — May 2022, Adapted]**

**(a) With the help of a block diagram, explain the operation of a double-conversion superheterodyne receiver. How does it provide better image rejection compared to a single-conversion receiver?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram and Operation (4 marks):**

A double-conversion superheterodyne receiver uses **two mixers and two intermediate frequencies**. The structure is:

**Antenna → RF Amplifier → 1st Mixer → 1st IF Amplifier (high IF) → 2nd Mixer → 2nd IF Amplifier (low IF) → Demodulator**

Two local oscillators (LO1 and LO2) feed the two mixers. The first IF is chosen **high** (for good image rejection) and the second IF is chosen **low** (for good adjacent-channel selectivity).

**Why better image rejection? (3 marks):**

- In single conversion, IF must be a *compromise* — high enough for image rejection, low enough for selectivity.
- In double conversion, the **first (high) IF** can be made very high (e.g., 50–100 MHz), pushing the image far from the desired signal. IFRR $\propto f_{IF}$, so high first IF → excellent image rejection.
- The **second (low) IF** (e.g., 455 kHz) handles adjacent-channel selectivity with sharp ceramic or crystal filters.
- Typical IFRR values: single conversion ≈ 40–60 dB; double conversion ≈ 80–100 dB.

---

**(b) A superheterodyne FM receiver tunes to 98 MHz. The IF is 10.7 MHz. The RF stage uses a single-tuned circuit with $Q = 120$. Calculate: (i) the local oscillator frequency (high-side injection), (ii) the image frequency, and (iii) the IFRR in dB. Comment on the adequacy of the IFRR for FM broadcast.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $f_s = 98$ MHz = $98 \times 10^{6}$ Hz; $f_{IF} = 10.7$ MHz; $Q = 120$.

**(i) Local Oscillator Frequency (2 marks):**

$$f_{LO} = f_s + f_{IF} = 98 + 10.7 = 108.7 \text{ MHz}$$

**(ii) Image Frequency (2 marks):**

$$f_i = f_s + 2 f_{IF} = 98 + 2(10.7) = 98 + 21.4 = 119.4 \text{ MHz}$$

**(iii) IFRR (3 marks):**

$$\text{IFRR} = \frac{4 Q f_{IF}}{f_s} = \frac{4 \times 120 \times 10.7}{98} = \frac{5136}{98} \approx 52.4$$

$$\text{IFRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(52.4) \approx 34.4 \text{ dB}$$

**Comment on adequacy:**

For a single-tuned RF stage, 34.4 dB is **insufficient** for commercial FM broadcast (which typically requires ≥ 60 dB). In practice, FM receivers use:
- A **double-tuned RF stage** (IFRR squared → ≈ 68.8 dB), **or**
- A **higher first IF** with double conversion.

This problem illustrates why modern FM receivers almost universally employ multi-stage RF selectivity.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — KTU Valuation Warning:** A common error is computing $f_i = f_s + f_{IF}$ (a factor-of-2 mistake). Another common error is forgetting to convert MHz to consistent units before applying the IFRR formula (the units cancel in the ratio, but write them out for full credit). Finally, the *comment* on adequacy is worth 1–2 marks — do not skip it; examiners reward practical engineering judgement.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Image frequency $f_i$ is the unwanted RF input that produces the same IF as the desired signal after mixing.
- **Core formula:** $f_i = f_s \pm 2 f_{IF}$ (sign depends on LO placement).
- **Local oscillator:** $f_{LO} = f_s + f_{IF}$ (high-side, most common) or $f_s - f_{IF}$ (low-side).
- **Image rejection site:** The **RF amplifier** (before the mixer) is the ONLY place where the image can be rejected.
- **IFRR exact:** $\text{IFRR} = \sqrt{1 + (4 Q f_{IF} / f_s)^{2}}$.
- **IFRR approximate:** $\text{IFRR} \approx 4 Q f_{IF} / f_s$ (valid when $4 Q f_{IF} \gg f_s$).
- **IFRR in dB:** $\text{IFRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(4 Q f_{IF} / f_s)$.
- **Key trade-off:** Higher IF → better image rejection but worse IF selectivity → use double conversion.
- **Worst-case IFRR:** Occurs at the **highest** $f_s$ in the tuning band (because IFRR $\propto 1/f_s$).
- **Standard IF values to memorize:** AM broadcast = 455 kHz; FM broadcast = 10.7 MHz; Analog TV = 38.9 MHz; Aircraft = 21.4 MHz.
- **Practical receivers:** Use multiple RF tuned stages and/or double conversion to achieve IFRR ≥ 60 dB.
- **Modern solution:** Zero-IF / low-IF / I-Q architectures (used in SDR) eliminate the image problem altogether.
- **Memorable analogy:** Image is the "mirror twin" of the desired signal, placed on the far side of the local oscillator at a distance of $2 f_{IF}$.
- **Quick numerical trick:** If asked to find $f_i$ from $f_s$ and $f_{IF}$, simply add (or subtract) $2 f_{IF}$ — never $f_{IF}$ alone.
- **Check unit consistency:** Always write $f_s$, $f_{IF}$ in the same unit (Hz or kHz or MHz) before substitution.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: External Noise
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT502 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** External Noise

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of External Noise

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard Terminology):**
> **External noise** refers to all forms of unwanted random electromagnetic disturbances that originate from sources *outside* the communication receiver and its associated circuitry. Unlike internal noise (which is generated within the components of the receiver itself), external noise is imposed on the receiving antenna from the propagation environment and external electromagnetic activity.

External noise is broadly classified by KTU examiners into the following three primary categories:

1. **Atmospheric Noise** (also called *static* or *natural noise from earth's atmosphere*)
2. **Industrial / Man-Made Noise** (electrical machinery, ignition systems, switching devices)
3. **Extraterrestrial Noise** (Solar, Cosmic, and Galactic / Jansky noise)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Noisy Auditorium":**
> Imagine you are speaking to a friend in a large auditorium. Your friend's ears (the **receiver**) must separate your voice (the **signal**) from three types of disturbance:
> - **Crowd chatter** from people inside the hall → this is like **internal noise** (generated by the receiver circuitry itself).
> - **Noise leaking in from outside the hall** — traffic, rain on the roof, fireworks → this is **atmospheric & industrial noise** (man-made and terrestrial).
> - **Cosmic background hum** — a faint hiss that no one in the room created → this is **extraterrestrial noise** (originating in deep space).
>
> External noise, therefore, is the disturbance that exists *in the channel* (free space / atmosphere) *before* the signal even enters the receiver. The receiver's first job is to deal with this noise so that the message can be heard clearly.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!TIP]
> The following constants and units are *high-frequency* in KTU questions on this topic:
> - **Boltzmann constant: $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K**
> - **Reference temperature: $T_0 = 290$ K** (standard IEEE definition)
> - **Noise Power Spectral Density: $N_0 = kT$** (in W/Hz)
> - **Solar Cycle Period: 11 years** (sunspot peak-to-peak)
> - **Galactic noise is dominant roughly in 8 MHz – 1.5 GHz band**

## 1.4 Why External Noise Matters (KTU Perspective)

External noise directly determines the **minimum detectable signal (MDS)** at the receiver antenna terminals. For a receiving system with effective noise temperature $T_{\text{eff}}$, the available noise power is:

$$P_N = k \, T_{\text{eff}} \, B$$

where $B$ is the receiver bandwidth in Hz. The receiver cannot decode a signal whose power falls below this noise floor. Hence, characterizing external noise is the *first step* in link budget design.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Systematic Classification of External Noise

External noise is partitioned into three classes based on its physical origin. Each class has a distinct spectral signature, geographic dependency, and time-variation characteristic that KTU examiners love to test.

### 2.1.1 Atmospheric Noise (Static / Static Crashes)

- **Source Mechanism:** Lightning discharges in thunderstorms, raindrops, snow, dust storms, and the cumulative effect of distant electrical storms.
- **Physical Origin:** Cloud-to-cloud and cloud-to-ground discharges produce broadband impulsive RF radiation. The energy of each discharge is dispersed across the Earth's ionosphere and re-radiated over very long distances.
- **Frequency Dependence:** Amplitude **decreases as frequency increases** (roughly $1/f$ or $1/f^2$ character).
- **Dominant Frequency Range:** Up to approximately **30 MHz** (above which it falls below cosmic noise).
- **Geographic Distribution:** Highest in **tropical regions** (high lightning density) — Kerala's monsoon climate makes this *especially* important for KTU students!
- **Time Variation:** Higher in summer and during thunderstorms; lower in winter and at night.
- **Impulse Character:** It is *impulsive* (not Gaussian) at the source, but after passing through the ionosphere it becomes quasi-Gaussian due to the superposition of many independent impulses.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it is called "Static":** Early radio operators called atmospheric noise "static" because the disturbance seemed to come from "static" (unchanging) atmospheric conditions, before the physics of lightning was understood.

### 2.1.2 Industrial Noise (Man-Made Noise)

- **Sources:**
  - **Ignition systems** of automobiles and aircraft (the single largest contributor in urban areas).
  - **Electric motors**, **fluorescent lamp starters**, **switching relays**, **arc welders**, **thermostats**, **computers**, **digital switching circuits**, **high-voltage transmission lines**, and **medical diathermy equipment**.
- **Frequency Range:** Dominant from roughly **1 MHz to 500 MHz** (peaks in the AM broadcast and VHF low bands).
- **Spatial Behavior:** Decreases rapidly with distance from the source (inverse-distance behavior, since most sources are *local*).
- **Mitigation:** Can be reduced by **shielding, filtering, and proper grounding** — and by relocating the antenna away from noisy sites.
- **Statistical Character:** Also impulsive, but more localized and more predictable than atmospheric noise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Frequently Tested Point: Industrial noise is the *only* category of external noise that is *controllable* through engineering action at the receiver site.

### 2.1.3 Extraterrestrial Noise

This is further subdivided into three sub-categories:

#### (a) Solar Noise

- **Origin:** Thermal radiation from the **Sun**, modulated by solar activity (sunspots, solar flares).
- **Time Variation:** Follows an **11-year sunspot cycle**. Noise levels are *maximum* during solar maxima and *minimum* during solar minima.
- **Directional Effect:** Maximum when the antenna beam is pointed directly at the Sun. The Sun's apparent position changes with time of day and season.
- **Quiet Sun (no sunspots):** Equivalent black-body temperature ≈ 6000 K at the surface; the radio-brightness temperature falls sharply above 30 GHz.
- **Active Sun (sunspot maximum):** Bursts of radio noise (solar radio bursts) can be 10–60 dB above the quiet-Sun level, especially during Type III and Type IV solar bursts.

#### (b) Cosmic Noise

- **Origin:** Thermal radiation from **stars beyond our solar system**. Distributed approximately uniformly across the celestial sphere.
- **Frequency Range:** Dominant between **8 MHz and 1.5 GHz**.
- **Temperature Equivalent:** Equivalent noise temperature ~ 1000–10,000 K at lower frequencies, decreasing at higher frequencies.

#### (c) Galactic Noise (Jansky Noise)

- **Origin:** Discrete sources in the **Milky Way** galaxy, particularly the dense galactic plane (centered on Sagittarius A*) and supernova remnants like Cassiopeia A, Cygnus A, Taurus A.
- **Discoverer:** **Karl Jansky** (1932) — the pioneer of radio astronomy; the noise is named in his honor.
- **Directional Effect:** Strongest when the antenna main lobe points along the **galactic plane**; weakest toward the galactic poles.
- **Frequency Behavior:** Falls off rapidly above ~1.5 GHz (which is why satellite communication in C, Ku, Ka bands is relatively free from galactic noise).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for KTU:** "**A**tmosphere + **I**ndustrial + **S**pace" → **AIS** for External Noise → "*AIS* is external noise on the **outskirts** (outside) of the receiver."

## 2.2 The Concept of Antenna Noise Temperature

Because external noise is so often expressed as an equivalent temperature, KTU examiners routinely test this concept.

> [!NOTE]
