> **Definition:** The **antenna noise temperature** $T_a$ is defined as the temperature of an equivalent hypothetical resistor at the antenna terminals that would deliver the same noise power (in a given bandwidth) as the actual external noise received by the antenna.

For an antenna pointed at a source of equivalent black-body temperature $T_s$, with directive gain $G(\theta, \phi)$ and thermal loss $L$:

$$T_a = \frac{1}{4\pi} \iint_{4\pi} T_s(\theta, \phi) \, G(\theta, \phi) \, d\Omega$$

where $d\Omega$ is the element of solid angle and $G(\theta,\phi)$ is normalized such that $\iint G \, d\Omega = 4\pi$.

## 2.3 Total Effective Noise Temperature

The **total effective system noise temperature** at the receiver input is:

$$T_{\text{eff}} = T_a + T_{\text{eq}}$$

where:
- $T_a$ — antenna noise temperature (external + sky)
- $T_{\text{eq}}$ — equivalent noise temperature of the receiver's internal stages (referred to the antenna terminals)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $P_N = k \, T \, B$ | Thermal noise power available from a resistor at temperature $T$ in bandwidth $B$ | W |
| 2 | $N_0 = kT$ | Single-sided noise power spectral density | W/Hz |
| 3 | $T_{\text{eff}} = T_a + T_{\text{eq}}$ | Total effective system noise temperature | K |
| 4 | $\text{NF (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}(F)$ | Noise figure in dB; $F = T_{\text{eq}} / T_0 + 1$ | dB |
| 5 | $\lambda = c / f$ | Wavelength–frequency relation; $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s | m |
| 6 | Solar cycle period | 11 years (peak-to-peak of sunspot activity) | years |
| 7 | Atmospheric noise dominant up to | 30 MHz | Hz |
| 8 | Industrial noise dominant range | 1 MHz – 500 MHz | Hz |
| 9 | Galactic/cosmic noise dominant range | 8 MHz – 1.5 GHz | Hz |
| 10 | $T_0$ (IEEE reference) | 290 K | K |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often write $T_{\text{eff}} = T_a \times T_{\text{eq}}$. It is a **sum**, not a product! The *powers* add, not the temperatures directly, but since $P \propto T$ for equal bandwidth, the temperatures add.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Satellite Link Design:** Galactic noise temperature $T_a$ enters directly into the satellite uplink/downlink $G/T$ figure of merit. At 4 GHz, $T_a \approx 5$–10 K; at 1 GHz, $T_a$ can be 30–100 K depending on antenna pointing.
- **HF Radio Communication:** Atmospheric noise dominates the HF band (3–30 MHz), which is why HF receivers require very high $G/T$ and large bandwidths are avoided.
- **Radio Astronomy:** Galactic noise is the *signal* of interest for radio telescopes — the very same noise that troubles a communication engineer enables the science of radio astronomy.
- **EMC Compliance:** Industrial noise limits force governments to regulate emission levels of consumer electronics, leading to standards like FCC Part 15 in the USA and CISPR 22 in the EU.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Available Noise Power from an Antenna of Temperature $T_a$

**Given:**
- Antenna delivers noise to receiver input in a bandwidth $B$.
- Antenna noise temperature is $T_a$ (an equivalent temperature by definition).

**To Derive:** The expression for noise power available at the antenna terminals.

**Step 1:** By the **Rayleigh-Jeans approximation** of Planck's law (valid when $hf \ll kT$, true for $f < 100$ GHz at room temperature), the spectral energy density of black-body radiation is:

$$u(f) = \frac{8\pi h f^3}{c^3} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{hf/kT} - 1}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the low-frequency approximation $hf \ll kT$, so that $e^{hf/kT} - 1 \approx hf/kT$:

$$u(f) \approx \frac{8\pi h f^3}{c^3} \cdot \frac{kT}{hf} = \frac{8\pi f^2 kT}{c^3}$$

**Step 3:** The spectral energy density per unit bandwidth is $u(f)/df = 8\pi f^2 kT / c^3$. The noise power flowing through a unit area (Poynting vector magnitude) is $\tfrac{1}{2}$ of the energy density (because half travels each way through the surface), giving spectral power flux density:

$$S(f) = \frac{f^2 kT}{c^2} \quad \text{[W/m}^2\text{/Hz]}$$

**Step 4:** The power captured by an antenna of effective aperture $A_e$ is $P = S \cdot A_e$, and the noise power spectral density delivered is:

$$N_0 = k T$$

**Step 5:** Integrate over a rectangular receiver bandwidth of width $B$ (assumed flat across the band):

$$P_N = \int_0^{B} kT \, df = kT \cdot B$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{P_N = k \, T_a \, B}$$

This is the universal **Nyquist / Johnson noise formula** (1928), applied to the antenna — historically the most important equation in radio engineering, and guaranteed to appear on a KTU exam.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Derivation of Noise Figure in Terms of $T_{\text{eq}}$

**Given:** A two-port network with input temperature $T_0 = 290$ K, equivalent input noise temperature $T_{\text{eq}}$, and gain $G$. The output noise power in bandwidth $B$ is the sum of amplified input noise and internally added noise.

**Step 1:** Input available noise power (from source at $T_0$):

$$N_{\text{in}} = k T_0 B$$

**Step 2:** Output noise power = Amplified input noise + internally added noise:

$$N_{\text{out}} = G \cdot k T_0 B + N_{\text{added}}$$

**Step 3:** Define the **noise factor** $F$ as the ratio of actual output noise to that which *would* be present if the network were noise-free:

$$F = \frac{N_{\text{out}}}{G \cdot k T_0 B} = \frac{G \, k T_0 B + N_{\text{added}}}{G \, k T_0 B}$$

**Step 4:** Define $T_{\text{eq}}$ such that the internally added noise equals $G k T_{\text{eq}} B$:

$$N_{\text{added}} = G k T_{\text{eq}} B$$

**Step 5:** Substitute into the $F$ expression:

$$F = \frac{G k T_0 B + G k T_{\text{eq}} B}{G k T_0 B} = \frac{T_0 + T_{\text{eq}}}{T_0}$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $T_{\text{eq}}$:

$$\boxed{T_{\text{eq}} = T_0 (F - 1)}$$

In dB, $\text{NF (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}(F)$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cascaded Noise Figure (Friis's Formula)

For three cascaded stages with gains $G_1, G_2, G_3$ and noise figures $F_1, F_2, F_3$:

$$F_{\text{total}} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \frac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2}$$

**Step-by-step derivation:**

**Step 1:** Total output noise of stage 1 alone: $G_1 k T_0 B F_1$

**Step 2:** Stage 2 sees this as input. Its internally added noise contributes an extra factor $(F_2 - 1)$ divided by $G_1$ (because stage 2's added noise appears at the output *after* the gain $G_1$ has already been applied). At the final output:

$$N_2^{\text{added, at output}} = G_2 \cdot (F_2 - 1) \cdot k T_0 B$$

But relative to the final gain, this is divided by $G_1$:

$$\text{Equivalent extra noise at input} = \frac{(F_2 - 1) \, k T_0 B}{G_1}$$

**Step 3:** Similarly for stage 3, the input-referred contribution is $(F_3 - 1) / (G_1 G_2) \cdot k T_0 B$.

**Step 4:** Total input-referred noise power = $kT_0 B \left[ F_1 + \dfrac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \dfrac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2} \right]$

**Step 5:** Divide by $kT_0 B$ to obtain $F_{\text{total}}$:

$$\boxed{F_{\text{total}} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \frac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** This is why the **first stage** of a receiver (typically the LNA in a satellite receiver) must have both **low noise figure** AND **high gain** — every dB of gain at the front end suppresses the noise contribution of all subsequent stages.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Frequency-Band vs Dominant External Noise

The following matrix is a high-yield KTU exam table. Students who memorize this matrix typically score full marks on questions that ask *"Which noise dominates at frequency $f$?"*

| Frequency Band | Frequency Range | Dominant External Noise | Engineering Implication |
|----------------|-----------------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| VLF (Very Low Frequency) | 3 – 30 kHz | Atmospheric (thunderstorm static) | Submarine communication |
| LF (Low Frequency) | 30 – 300 kHz | Atmospheric (dominates) | Navigation, AM long-wave |
| MF (Medium Frequency) | 300 kHz – 3 MHz | Atmospheric + Industrial | AM broadcast |
| HF (High Frequency) | 3 – 30 MHz | Atmospheric, fading + Industrial (urban) | Short-wave radio |
| VHF (Very High Frequency) | 30 – 300 MHz | Industrial + Galactic (8–300 MHz) | FM broadcast, TV |
| UHF (Ultra High Frequency) | 300 MHz – 3 GHz | Galactic/cosmic (8–1500 MHz), Thermal | TV, mobile, GPS, satellite |
| SHF (Super High Frequency) | 3 – 30 GHz | Mainly thermal (external very low) | Satellite, radar |
| EHF (Extremely High Frequency) | 30 – 300 GHz | Atmospheric absorption (gases) | mm-wave, 5G |

## 3.5 Numerical-Style Worked Example (Solved)

**Problem:** A satellite receiver operating at $f = 4$ GHz has an antenna noise temperature $T_a = 35$ K (a typical value at 4 GHz when pointed at the sky) and a receiver with equivalent noise temperature $T_{\text{eq}} = 70$ K. The receiver bandwidth is $B = 36$ MHz. Calculate:

(a) Effective system noise temperature
(b) Available noise power at the receiver input
(c) Noise figure of the receiver in dB

**Solution:**

**(a)** Effective noise temperature:

$$T_{\text{eff}} = T_a + T_{\text{eq}} = 35 + 70 = 105 \text{ K}$$

**(b)** Available noise power:

$$P_N = k \, T_{\text{eff}} \, B$$

$$P_N = (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 105 \times (36 \times 10^6)$$

$$P_N = 1.38 \times 105 \times 36 \times 10^{-17}$$

$$P_N = 5216.4 \times 10^{-17} = 5.22 \times 10^{-14} \text{ W}$$

Converting to dBm:

$$P_N \text{ (dBm)} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{5.22 \times 10^{-14}}{10^{-3}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(5.22 \times 10^{-11})$$

$$P_N \text{ (dBm)} = 10 \times (-10.282) = -102.82 \text{ dBm}$$

**(c)** Noise figure:

$$F = 1 + \frac{T_{\text{eq}}}{T_0} = 1 + \frac{70}{290} = 1 + 0.2414 = 1.2414$$

$$\text{NF (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}(1.2414) = 0.94 \text{ dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** A satellite receiver LNA with NF ≈ 0.94 dB is a *realistic, high-performance* figure — premium GaAs LNAs in the 1980s reached exactly this regime. KTU examiners often set up numbers so that the answer is around 1 dB, reinforcing physical intuition.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flow of External Noise

The following block-level architecture diagram maps the complete taxonomy of external noise as it appears in the KTU syllabus.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[EXTERNAL NOUE ORIGINATES OUTSIDE THE RECEIVER] --> B[ATMOSPHERIC NOUE]
    A --> C[INDUSTRIAL MAN MADE NOUE]
    A --> D[EXTRATERRESTRIAL NOUE]

    B --> B1[SOURCE LIGHTNING DISCHARGES]
    B --> B2[FREQUENCY UP TO 30 MHZ]
    B --> B3[CHARACTER IMPULSIVE]
    B --> B4[REGION HIGHEST IN TROPICS]

    C --> C1[SOURCE IGNITION MOTORS RELAYS]
    C --> C2[FREQUENCY 1 MHZ TO 500 MHZ]
    C --> C3[CHARACTER IMPULSIVE LOCAL]
    C --> C4[MITIGATION SHIELDING FILTERING]

    D --> D1[SOLAR NOUE]
    D --> D2[COSMIC NOUE]
    D --> D3[GALACTIC JANSKY NOUE]

    D1 --> D11[CYCLE 11 YEAR SUNSPOT]
    D1 --> D12[PEAK TYPE III IV BURSTS]

    D2 --> D21[SOURCE STARS BEYOND SOLAR SYSTEM]
    D2 --> D22[FREQUENCY 8 MHZ TO 1.5 GHZ]

    D3 --> D31[ORIGIN MILKY WAY DISCRETE SOURCES]
    D3 --> D32[DISCOVERER KARL JANSKY 1932]
```

## 4.2 Frequency-Band Allocation Map of External Noise

The next schematic shows the *spectral dominance regions* of each type of external noise, with frequency on a logarithmic scale.

```mermaid
graph LR
    F1[BAND VLF 3 TO 30 KHZ] --> F2[BAND LF 30 TO 300 KHZ]
    F2 --> F3[BAND MF 300 KHZ TO 3 MHZ]
    F3 --> F4[BAND HF 3 TO 30 MHZ]
    F4 --> F5[BAND VHF 30 TO 300 MHZ]
    F5 --> F6[BAND UHF 300 MHZ TO 3 GHZ]
    F6 --> F7[BAND SHF 3 TO 30 GHZ]
    F7 --> F8[BAND EHF 30 TO 300 GHZ]

    F1 --- N1[DOMINANT ATMOSPHERIC]
    F2 --- N2[DOMINANT ATMOSPHERIC]
    F3 --- N3[ATMOSPHERIC PLUS INDUSTRIAL]
    F4 --- N4[ATMOSPHERIC PLUS INDUSTRIAL]
    F5 --- N5[INDUSTRIAL PLUS GALACTIC]
    F6 --- N6[GALACTIC PLUS COSMIC PLUS THERMAL]
    F7 --- N7[MAINLY THERMAL EXTERNAL LOW]
    F8 --- N8[ATMOSPHERIC GAS ABSORPTION]
```

## 4.3 Receiver Noise Model — Block Diagram

The block diagram below shows how external noise is added to the signal path at the *antenna*, while internal noise is added at every stage of the receiver. This is the model used in Friis's formula.

```mermaid
graph LR
    ANT[ANTENNA TA] --> LNA[STAGE 1 LNA GAIN G1 NF1]
    LNA --> ST2[STAGE 2 MIXER GAIN G2 NF2]
    ST2 --> ST3[STAGE 3 IF AMP GAIN G3 NF3]
    ST3 --> DET[DETECTOR OUTPUT]

    EXT[EXTERNAL NOUE POWER KTA B] -.ADDED.-> ANT
    INT1[INTERNAL NOUE STAGE 1] -.ADDED.-> LNA
    INT2[INTERNAL NOUE STAGE 2] -.ADDED.-> ST2
    INT3[INTERNAL NOUE STAGE 3] -.ADDED.-> ST3

    ANT --- S0[SIGNAL S]
```

## 4.4 Sunspot Cycle and Solar Noise Variation

The 11-year sunspot cycle modulates solar noise output. The diagram below conceptualises the temporal pattern.

```mermaid
graph TD
    SC[SOLAR CYCLE 11 YEARS] --> SMIN[MINIMUM QUIET SUN]
    SC --> SMAX[MAXIMUM ACTIVE SUN]

    SMIN --> SMIN1[TEMPERATURE 6000 K EQUIVALENT]
    SMIN --> SMIN2[NOUE LEVEL LOW]

    SMAX --> SMAX1[SUNSPOTS NUMEROUS]
    SMAX --> SMAX2[SOLAR BURSTS TYPE III IV]
    SMAX --> SMAX3[NOUE LEVEL HIGH PLUS 10 TO 60 DB ABOVE QUIET]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The 11-year cycle is actually the *magnetic* polarity cycle of the Sun. The full sunspot activity cycle (minimum → maximum → minimum) is approximately 11 years. Two such cycles (~22 years) constitute the complete Hale cycle.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]

**Q: Differentiate between internal noise and external noise. List the main types of external noise.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Internal Noise | External Noise |
|--------|----------------|----------------|
| Origin | Generated *within* receiver components (resistors, transistors) | Originates *outside* the receiver (atmosphere, space, man-made) |
| Examples | Thermal, shot, flicker, transit-time noise | Atmospheric, industrial, extraterrestrial |
| Control | Can be reduced by component selection, cooling, design | Can be reduced by antenna siting, filtering, shielding |
| When it acts | After signal enters receiver | Before signal enters receiver (at the antenna) |

**Three types of external noise:** (i) Atmospheric noise, (ii) Industrial noise, (iii) Extraterrestrial noise (which is sub-divided into solar, cosmic, and galactic noise). *(1 mark for distinction, 2 marks for the list with examples.)*

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Modified]

**Q: What is antenna noise temperature? Why is it important in satellite communication?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Antenna noise temperature $T_a$ is defined as the temperature of an equivalent hypothetical resistor connected to the antenna terminals that would deliver the same available noise power (within the receiver bandwidth) as the actual external noise collected by the antenna.

**Importance in satellite comms (1.5 marks):**
- It directly enters the $G/T$ (figure of merit) of the ground station and satellite transponder.
- For a given antenna gain, **lower $T_a$ improves the link $C/N_0$** and hence the bit error rate.
- At 4 GHz, $T_a \approx 5$–35 K; at 1 GHz, $T_a$ can be 30–100 K due to galactic noise; this sets a *floor* on achievable system noise.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions in Module 1 are typically framed as a single 14-mark question with two 7-mark sub-parts, OR two 7-mark sub-parts under a common stem. Internal choice is offered between two full 14-mark questions. Below is the **standard KTU pattern** with two alternatives.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Question]

**Q: (a)** Classify external noise in detail. Discuss the sources, frequency range, characteristics, and methods to mitigate each type. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A receiver has an antenna noise temperature of $T_a = 50$ K and an equivalent noise temperature of $T_{\text{eq}} = 145$ K. The receiver bandwidth is $B = 200$ kHz.
  - (i) Calculate the total effective noise temperature. *(2 marks)*
  - (ii) Calculate the available noise power at the receiver input. *(3 marks)*
  - (iii) Calculate the noise figure in dB. *(2 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of External Noise — 7 marks:**

External noise is classified into three major categories:

**(1) Atmospheric Noise (2.5 marks):**
- **Source:** Lightning discharges, thunderstorms, precipitation static. *(0.5)*
- **Frequency Range:** Dominant up to ~30 MHz. *(0.5)*
- **Characteristics:** Impulsive, falls with frequency, tropical regions suffer most, diurnal and seasonal variation. *(1.0)*
- **Mitigation:** Directional antennas, narrow-band receivers, error-correction coding, frequency hopping. *(0.5)*

**(2) Industrial / Man-Made Noise (2.5 marks):**
- **Source:** Ignition systems, electric motors, fluorescent lights, switching devices. *(0.5)*
- **Frequency Range:** Dominant 1 MHz – 500 MHz. *(0.5)*
- **Characteristics:** Local, impulsive, predictable, can be measured statistically. *(1.0)*
- **Mitigation:** Shielding, filtering, proper grounding, antenna relocation, regulatory emission standards. *(0.5)*

**(3) Extraterrestrial Noise (2 marks):**
- **Solar Noise:** From the Sun, follows 11-year cycle, bursts of high intensity. *(0.7)*
- **Cosmic Noise:** From stars beyond solar system, uniform spatial distribution. *(0.6)*
- **Galactic (Jansky) Noise:** From Milky Way plane, strong along galactic equator, dominant 8 MHz–1.5 GHz. *(0.7)*

**(b) Numerical Problem — 7 marks:**

**(i) Total effective noise temperature (2 marks):**

$$T_{\text{eff}} = T_a + T_{\text{eq}} = 50 + 145 = 195 \text{ K}$$

**(ii) Available noise power (3 marks):**

$$P_N = k \, T_{\text{eff}} \, B$$

$$P_N = (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 195 \times (200 \times 10^3)$$

$$P_N = 1.38 \times 195 \times 200 \times 10^{-20}$$

$$P_N = 53820 \times 10^{-20} = 5.382 \times 10^{-16} \text{ W}$$

Converting to dBm:

$$P_N \text{ (dBm)} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{5.382 \times 10^{-16}}{10^{-3}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(5.382 \times 10^{-13})$$

$$P_N \text{ (dBm)} = 10 \times (-12.269) = -122.69 \text{ dBm}$$

**(iii) Noise figure in dB (2 marks):**

$$F = 1 + \frac{T_{\text{eq}}}{T_0} = 1 + \frac{145}{290} = 1 + 0.5 = 1.5$$

$$\text{NF (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) = 1.76 \text{ dB}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [Internal Choice Alternative]

**Q: (a)** With the aid of a block diagram, derive Friis's formula for the total noise figure of a three-stage cascaded receiver. Explain why the first stage must have low noise figure and high gain. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A satellite earth station operating at 6 GHz uses an antenna with $T_a = 25$ K. The receiver has a noise figure of 1.8 dB. The receiver bandwidth is 30 MHz.
  - (i) Convert the noise figure to equivalent noise temperature. *(2 marks)*
  - (ii) Compute the total effective noise temperature. *(1 mark)*
  - (iii) Calculate the noise power in dBm at the receiver input. *(3 marks)*
  - (iv) If the antenna is repointed toward the galactic centre, $T_a$ rises to 120 K. By how many dB does the noise power increase? *(1 mark)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Friis's Formula Derivation — 7 marks:**

Refer to the block diagram below:

```
ANTENNA --> STAGE 1 (G1, F1) --> STAGE 2 (G2, F2) --> STAGE 3 (G3, F3) --> DETECTOR
```

- The input-referred available noise power at the antenna is $kT_0 B$ (at reference temperature $T_0 = 290$ K). *(0.5)*
- The output noise power of stage 1 (referred to its own input) is $kT_0 B \, F_1$. *(0.5)*
- At the input of stage 2, this is divided by the gain $G_1$, so the *equivalent input-referred noise contribution* of stage 2 is $(F_2 - 1) k T_0 B / G_1$. *(1.5)*
- Similarly, the contribution of stage 3 (referred to the system input) is $(F_3 - 1) k T_0 B / (G_1 G_2)$. *(1.5)*
- Total input-referred noise = $kT_0 B \left[ F_1 + \dfrac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \dfrac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2} \right]$. *(1.0)*
- Dividing by the noise-free case $kT_0 B$ gives: *(1.0)*

$$\boxed{F_{\text{total}} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \frac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2}}$$

**Why first stage must have low NF and high gain (1.0 mark):** The first stage's $F_1$ enters the formula directly, while subsequent stages' noise figures are *divided* by the gain $G_1$ of the first stage. A high $G_1$ attenuates the relative contribution of downstream noise, and a low $F_1$ keeps the dominant term small. This is why the front-end LNA is the most expensive and carefully designed component in a satellite receiver.

**(b) Numerical Problem — 7 marks:**

**(i) Convert NF = 1.8 dB to $T_{\text{eq}}$ (2 marks):**

$$F = 10^{1.8/10} = 10^{0.18} = 1.514$$

$$T_{\text{eq}} = T_0 (F - 1) = 290 \times (1.514 - 1) = 290 \times 0.514 = 149.06 \text{ K}$$

**(ii) Total effective noise temperature (1 mark):**

$$T_{\text{eff}} = T_a + T_{\text{eq}} = 25 + 149.06 = 174.06 \text{ K}$$

**(iii) Noise power in dBm (3 marks):**

$$P_N = k \, T_{\text{eff}} \, B = (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 174.06 \times (30 \times 10^6)$$

$$P_N = 1.38 \times 174.06 \times 30 \times 10^{-17}$$

$$P_N = 7206.08 \times 10^{-17} = 7.206 \times 10^{-14} \text{ W}$$

Converting to dBm:

$$P_N \text{ (dBm)} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{7.206 \times 10^{-14}}{10^{-3}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(7.206 \times 10^{-11})$$

$$P_N \text{ (dBm)} = 10 \times (-10.142) = -101.42 \text{ dBm}$$

**(iv) Increase due to antenna repointing (1 mark):**

New $T_{\text{eff}} = 120 + 149.06 = 269.06$ K.

Ratio of new to old noise power = $269.06 / 174.06 = 1.5458$.

$$\Delta P_N \text{ (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5458) = 1.89 \text{ dB}$$

The noise power increases by **1.89 dB** when the antenna is repointed toward the galactic centre — this is a textbook case of why satellite earth-station antennas avoid pointing close to the galactic plane.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in External Noise Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing atmospheric and industrial noise frequency ranges.** Atmospheric dominates *up to 30 MHz*; industrial is dominant from *1 MHz to 500 MHz*. The two regions overlap, but the *primary* dominant type differs. Many students lose a mark by writing one of them incorrectly.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the *sum* in $T_{\text{eff}} = T_a + T_{\text{eq}}$.** It is *additive*, not multiplicative. Writing $T_{\text{eff}} = T_a \times T_{\text{eq}}$ is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
>
> 3. **Using the wrong temperature in NF conversion.** $F = 1 + T_{\text{eq}} / T_0$ uses $T_0 = 290$ K, not the actual ambient or antenna temperature. Examiners specifically test this.
>
> 4. **Writing noise power in dBm incorrectly.** Remember: $\text{dBm} = 10 \log_{10}(P / 1 \text{ mW})$. A common error is using dBW; both are acceptable but must be stated.
>
> 5. **Omitting the 11-year solar cycle period in solar noise descriptions.** It is a *high-yield* one-line fact that examiners love to test.
>
> 6. **Misidentifying Jansky noise.** Galactic noise was discovered by **Karl Jansky** in **1932**, not by Hertz or Marconi. Examiners have asked this as a 1-mark MCQ in recent papers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] External noise = noise originating **outside** the receiver.
- [x] Three main types: **Atmospheric, Industrial, Extraterrestrial** (Solar, Cosmic, Galactic).
- [x] Atmospheric noise → lightning → dominant up to **30 MHz** → impulsive → tropical regions worst affected.
- [x] Industrial noise → ignition systems, motors, switches → dominant **1 MHz – 500 MHz** → can be *controlled* by shielding/filtering/grounding.
- [x] Solar noise → 11-year sunspot cycle → bursts at solar max.
- [x] Cosmic noise → from distant stars → 8 MHz – 1.5 GHz.
- [x] Galactic / Jansky noise → from Milky Way plane → discovered by **Karl Jansky (1932)** → strong along galactic equator.
- [x] **Antenna noise temperature** $T_a$: equivalent resistor temperature giving same noise power.
- [x] **Effective system noise temperature**: $T_{\text{eff}} = T_a + T_{\text{eq}}$ (sum, not product).
- [x] **Nyquist formula**: $P_N = kTB$ (W) — *the* most important equation in noise analysis.
- [x] **Noise factor**: $F = 1 + T_{\text{eq}}/T_0$, $T_0 = 290$ K.
- [x] **Noise figure** in dB: $\text{NF} = 10 \log_{10}(F)$.
- [x] **Friis's formula**: $F_{\text{total}} = F_1 + (F_2-1)/G_1 + (F_3-1)/(G_1 G_2)$ — first stage dominates.
- [x] **Boltzmann constant**: $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K — *must* be remembered numerically.
- [x] **Reference temperature** $T_0 = 290$ K (IEEE) — *not* 273 K, *not* 300 K.
- [x] Galactic noise falls off above ~1.5 GHz — satellite links at C-band (4 GHz), Ku (12 GHz), Ka (30 GHz) are largely free from galactic noise.
- [x] Atmospheric noise is also called **static** or **static crashes** in older textbooks.
- [x] dBm conversion: $\text{dBm} = 10 \log_{10}(P/10^{-3})$ — practice two or three conversions.
- [x] For 14-mark questions, the KTU pattern is *always* (7 + 7) sub-parts with full derivation/numerical shown step by step.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** "**A**tmospheric up to **3**0 MHz, **I**ndustrial **1**–**5**00 MHz, **G**alactic **8**–1500 MHz" — note the sequence **3, 1, 8** and **30, 500, 1500** that is hard to forget once seen.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Internal Noise
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT502 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Internal Noise

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1: Internal Noise — Section 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Internal Noise

> [!NOTE]
> **Internal Noise** refers to the unwanted, random electrical signals that are *generated internally* within an electronic device or communication system due to the discrete nature of charge carriers (electrons and holes) and the thermal agitation of these carriers. It is present even in the absence of any external signal or interference and represents the **fundamental physical lower limit** on the sensitivity of any receiver.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, internal noise is treated as the primary bottleneck limiting the **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** at the receiver's output, which in turn governs the **Bit Error Rate (BER)** in digital links and the **minimum detectable signal** in analog links.

## 1.2 Classification of Internal Noise

Internal noise is broadly classified into the following categories — each arising from a distinct physical mechanism:

| # | Noise Type | Origin (Physical Cause) |
|---|------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Thermal Noise (Johnson / Nyquist Noise)** | Random thermal agitation of electrons in conductors/resistors |
| 2 | **Shot Noise (Schottky Noise)** | Discrete nature of charge carriers crossing a PN junction |
| 3 | **Flicker Noise (1/f Noise)** | Traps, impurities, and surface defects in semiconductor devices |
| 4 | **Transit-Time Noise** | Finite time taken by carriers to cross the device junction |
| 5 | **Partition Noise** | Random division of current between two electrodes (e.g., tetrode) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Humming Auditorium" Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a quiet lecture hall where a single professor is whispering a lecture. Now imagine 100 students whispering *among themselves* before the lecture starts. The professor's signal is the "message"; the students' whispers are the "noise." The whispers (noise) exist **even when the professor is silent** (no input signal), they **add to the professor's voice** (additive noise), and they come from **inside the hall itself** (internal noise).

This analogy explains the three defining traits of internal noise:
- **Always present** (even with no signal)
- **Additive** to the desired signal
- **Generated inside the receiver** (not from outside)

## 1.4 Why is Internal Noise Critical in Communication?

In any receiver, the weakest detectable signal is governed by:

$$
S_{min} \;\propto\; k\,T\,B \times (SNR)_{min}
$$

This shows that:
- **Higher bandwidth $B$** → more noise collected
- **Higher temperature $T$** → more thermal agitation
- **Required SNR** → depends on modulation/BER specification

A real-world example: **Deep-space communication (e.g., Voyager 1)** uses cryogenically cooled (≈ 20 K) amplifiers to drop thermal noise by a factor of ~70 compared to room temperature (290 K).

## 1.5 Physical Constants Used in Noise Calculations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following constants appear in **every** KTU noise problem:
> - Boltzmann's constant: $\;k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$
> - Charge of an electron: $\;q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$
> - Standard reference temperature: $\;T_0 = 290\ \text{K}$ (often used in noise figure calculations)

These three constants are *non-negotiable* — committing them to memory saves precious minutes during the 3-hour KTU End Semester Exam (ESE).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermal Noise (Johnson / Nyquist Noise) — Deep Dive

### 2.1.1 Physical Origin
Every conductor contains free electrons that move randomly due to thermal energy. Although the *average* current is zero (since motion is random), the *instantaneous* current fluctuates, producing a random noise voltage across the terminals.

### 2.1.2 Derivation Logic (Step-by-Step Bulleted Reasoning)

- **Step 1:** Each electron has kinetic energy of order $\frac{1}{2}mv^2$ from equipartition theorem → $\frac{1}{2}mv^2 = \frac{1}{2}kT$
- **Step 2:** This random motion is equivalent to a noise current source $i_n(t)$ with zero mean: $\overline{i_n(t)} = 0$
- **Step 3:** Compute the **mean-square noise current** $\overline{i_n^2}$ over a small bandwidth $\Delta f$:
  - Spectral density is **flat** (white) up to very high frequencies ($f \approx 10^{13}$ Hz at room T)
- **Step 4:** Using Nyquist's theorem (matched load assumption):
  $$\overline{v_n^2} = 4kTBR \quad \text{(for a resistor R over bandwidth B)}$$
- **Step 5:** The **maximum noise power** transferred to a matched load is obtained when load resistance equals source resistance:
  $$P_n = kTB \quad \text{(the famous "kTB" formula)}$$

### 2.1.3 Key Properties of Thermal Noise
- **Gaussian** amplitude distribution
- **White** spectral density (up to ~THz)
- **Additive** and present at **all frequencies**
- Independent of current flow — exists even in an *open* circuit (only as a voltage)

## 2.2 Shot Noise — Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 Physical Origin
In a PN junction (diode, BJT, FET), charge carriers are *discrete particles*. The current is the sum of randomly arriving electrons. Fluctuations from the average constitute shot noise.

### 2.2.2 Derivation Logic
- **Step 1:** Average current $I_{dc}$ is steady.
- **Step 2:** Random arrival times of charge carriers give variance in the current.
- **Step 3:** Schottky's theorem gives the **mean-square shot noise current**:
  $$\overline{i_n^2} = 2 q I_{dc} B$$
- **Step 4:** This is also a **white** noise (constant spectral density) up to frequencies where transit-time effects become significant.

## 2.3 Flicker Noise (1/f Noise) — Deep Dive

### 2.3.1 Physical Origin
Caused by **traps and imperfections** at the surface of semiconductor devices and by contact noise at imperfect ohmic contacts.

### 2.3.2 Spectral Characteristic
- Spectral density is **inversely proportional to frequency**:
  $$\overline{i_n^2} \propto \frac{1}{f^n}, \quad n \approx 1$$
- Dominates at **low frequencies** (typically below 1 kHz)
- Negligible in **RF and microwave** systems (which is why KTU Module 1 emphasizes thermal + shot noise)

## 2.4 Transit-Time Noise

Occurs when the **transit time** of carriers across a junction becomes comparable to the **period of the signal**. It sets the **upper frequency limit** of transistors and diodes. Significant above ~GHz, hence usually neglected in KTU-level problems.

## 2.5 Partition Noise

Arises when current is divided between two electrodes (e.g., from plate to screen grid in a tetrode). Since the division is random, fluctuations in the current at one electrode produce noise. Important in vacuum tubes; negligible in BJTs/FETs.

---

## 2.6 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Print This!)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every formula you need to solve Module 1 noise problems in the KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize it, but more importantly, understand *when to use which one*.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | When to Use |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Thermal noise power (matched) | $P_n = kTB$ | Watts | Power available at matched load |
| 2 | Thermal noise voltage (across R) | $v_n = \sqrt{4kTBR}$ | Volts (rms) | Resistor noise source |
| 3 | Thermal noise current (through R) | $i_n = \sqrt{4kTB/R}$ | Amps (rms) | Norton equivalent of noisy R |
| 4 | Shot noise current | $i_n = \sqrt{2qI_{dc}B}$ | Amps (rms) | PN junction / diode / BJT |
| 5 | Noise Figure (linear) | $F = \dfrac{S_i/N_i}{S_o/N_o}$ | Dimensionless | Receiver characterization |
| 6 | Noise Figure (dB) | $NF = 10\log_{10}(F)$ | dB | Decibel form for plot/spec sheet |
| 7 | Effective input noise temperature | $T_e = T_0(F - 1)$ | Kelvin | Low-noise systems (satcom) |
| 8 | Total system noise temperature | $T_{sys} = T_e + T_{ant}$ | Kelvin | Link budget calculations |
| 9 | Friis cascade formula | $F_{total} = F_1 + \dfrac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \dfrac{F_3 - 1}{G_1G_2} + \dots$ | Dimensionless | Cascaded amplifiers |
| 10 | Output SNR | $\dfrac{S_o}{N_o} = \dfrac{S_i/N_i}{F}$ | Dimensionless | Final SNR after receiver |
| 11 | Output noise power | $N_o = F\,k\,T_0\,B\,G$ | Watts | Receiver output noise |
| 12 | Flicker noise PSD | $S_i(f) = \dfrac{K_f I_{dc}^2}{f}$ | A²/Hz | Low-frequency (<1 kHz) |
| 13 | Noise bandwidth (rectangular equiv.) | $B_n = \dfrac{1}{\vert H(f_{max}) \vert^2}\int_0^\infty \vert H(f) \vert^2 df$ | Hz | For non-flat filters |
| 14 | Signal + noise voltage (rms sum) | $V_{rms} = \sqrt{V_s^2 + V_n^2}$ | Volts | Independent noise sources |

> [!WARNING]
> **Units Pitfall:** When using $kTB$, $T$ **must be in Kelvin** and $B$ **in Hz**. A common KTU error is using $T = 27^\circ$C directly — this is wrong! Convert: $T(K) = 27 + 273 = 300$ K.

## 2.7 Noise Figure — Conceptual Clarity

The **Noise Figure (F)** tells us *by how much* the receiver degrades the input SNR.

$$
F = \frac{\text{SNR at input}}{\text{SNR at output}}
$$

- **Ideal receiver:** $F = 1$ (0 dB) → no degradation
- **Real receiver:** $F > 1$ → always degrades SNR
- **Why?** Because the receiver adds its own internal noise on top of amplifying the input noise

A **low-noise amplifier (LNA)** placed at the very front-end of a receiver is critical because the Friis formula shows that the **first stage's noise figure dominates** the overall system noise figure.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Why Internal Noise Matters |
|--------|----------------------------|
| **Satellite communication** | Receivers at the ground station pick up faint signals; thermal noise in LNA sets minimum receivable signal |
| **MRI scanners** | Body-tissue noise + preamp noise determines image resolution |
| **Radio astronomy** | Cosmic signals are 10⁻²⁶ W; need cryogenic amplifiers (T ≈ 20 K) |
| **Mobile phones** | Internal noise in RF front-end limits cell-edge sensitivity |
| **Fiber optic comms** | Shot noise in photodiodes + thermal noise in TIA limits BER |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mean-Square Thermal Noise Voltage Across a Resistor

**Given:** A resistor $R$ at absolute temperature $T$ Kelvin, with a noise measurement bandwidth $B$ Hz.

**To Derive:** $\overline{v_n^2} = 4kTBR$

### Step-by-Step Derivation (Exhaustive — No Steps Skipped)

**Step 1 — Equipartition Principle:**
For a system in thermal equilibrium, the average energy per degree of freedom is $\frac{1}{2}kT$. For a one-dimensional harmonic system (like the random motion of electrons in a conductor modeled as a damped oscillator), the total energy is:

$$
E = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 + \frac{1}{2}kx^2 = 2 \times \frac{1}{2}kT = kT
$$

where $k$ here is the spring constant (we'll use $k_B$ for Boltzmann to avoid confusion).

$$
E = k_B T
$$

**Step 2 — Spectral Density of Available Noise Power:**
According to Nyquist (1928), a resistor can be modeled as a Thevenin source with mean-square noise voltage in a small bandwidth $df$:

$$
d\overline{v_n^2} = S_v(f)\, df
$$

where $S_v(f)$ is the *voltage spectral density*. From the equipartition result and Planck's law (with classical limit $hf \ll k_BT$):

$$
S_v(f) = 4 k_B T R
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate Over the Measurement Bandwidth:**
For a system with equivalent noise bandwidth $B$ Hz:

$$
\overline{v_n^2} = \int_0^B S_v(f)\, df = \int_0^B 4 k_B T R\, df
$$

**Step 4 — Perform the Integration:**

$$
\overline{v_n^2} = 4 k_B T R \int_0^B df
$$

$$
\overline{v_n^2} = 4 k_B T R \cdot (B - 0)
$$

$$
\boxed{\overline{v_n^2} = 4 k_B T R B}
$$

**Step 5 — Corresponding Noise Current (Norton Equivalent):**
Using Ohm's law $\overline{v_n^2} = \overline{i_n^2} R^2$:

$$
\overline{i_n^2} = \frac{\overline{v_n^2}}{R^2} = \frac{4 k_B T R B}{R^2} = \frac{4 k_B T B}{R}
$$

**Step 6 — Maximum Power Transfer (Matched Load Case):**
For a Thevenin source $v_n$ with series resistance $R$ driving a matched load $R_L = R$, the noise power delivered to the load is:

$$
P_n = \frac{\overline{v_n^2}}{(2R)^2} \cdot R = \frac{\overline{v_n^2}}{4R}
$$

Substituting Step 4:

$$
P_n = \frac{4 k_B T R B}{4R} = k_B T B
$$

$$
\boxed{P_n = k_B T B}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The $kTB$ result is one of the most beautiful and frequently tested formulas in KTU's Analog & Digital Communication paper. It is **independent of resistance** — a counter-intuitive but rigorously proven result.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shot Noise Variance (Schottky's Theorem)

**Given:** A PN junction carrying DC current $I_{dc}$ with electron charge $q$.

**To Derive:** $\overline{i_n^2} = 2 q I_{dc} B$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Model the Current as a Poisson Process:**
The number of charge carriers $N$ crossing the junction in time $t$ follows a Poisson distribution:

$$
P(N, t) = \frac{(I_{dc} t / q)^N e^{-I_{dc} t / q}}{N!}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Mean and Variance of N:**
For a Poisson process:

$$
\langle N \rangle = \text{Var}(N) = \frac{I_{dc} t}{q}
$$

**Step 3 — Express Current Fluctuation:**
Total current $i(t) = \frac{q \cdot N(t)}{t}$, so fluctuation $\Delta i = \frac{q \cdot \Delta N}{t}$.

Mean-square current fluctuation:

$$
\overline{(\Delta i)^2} = \frac{q^2 \cdot \text{Var}(N)}{t^2} = \frac{q^2 \cdot (I_{dc} t / q)}{t^2} = \frac{q I_{dc}}{t}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Power Spectral Density:**
For a stationary random process, the mean-square fluctuation in a bandwidth $B$ is the PSD integrated over $B$. For white shot noise (constant PSD), we get:

$$
\overline{i_n^2} = S_i(f) \cdot B = 2 q I_{dc} B
$$

(The factor 2 arises from the two-sided PSD convention; in one-sided PSD, $S_i(f) = qI_{dc}$.)

$$
\boxed{\overline{i_n^2} = 2 q I_{dc} B}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Friis' Formula for Noise Figure of Cascaded Stages

**Given:** A cascade of $n$ stages, each with noise figure $F_i$ and power gain $G_i$.

**To Derive:** 
$$
F_{total} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \frac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2} + \dots
$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define Output Noise Power from Each Stage:**
For stage 1 with input noise $N_i = kT_0 B$:

$$
N_{o,1} = F_1\, G_1\, kT_0 B
$$

**Step 2 — Decompose Output Noise into Two Parts:**
- Amplified input noise: $G_1 kT_0 B$
- Noise added by stage 1 itself: $(F_1 - 1) G_1 kT_0 B$

**Step 3 — Propagate Through Stage 2:**
At stage 2's input:

$$
N_{i,2} = N_{o,1} = F_1 G_1 kT_0 B
$$

At stage 2's output:

$$
N_{o,2} = F_2 G_2 \cdot N_{i,2} + (\text{stage 2's own noise added})
$$

Wait — the correct recursive approach uses the **effective input noise power** definition.

**Step 4 — Equivalent Noise Power Referenced to Stage 1 Input:**
The total output noise referred back to stage 1 input is:

$$
N_{i,total}^{eq} = N_i + (N_1)_{added}/G_1 + (N_2)_{added}/(G_1 G_2) + \dots
$$

Substituting $N_i = kT_0 B$, $(N_1)_{added} = (F_1 - 1) kT_0 B$, etc.:

$$
N_{i,total}^{eq} = kT_0 B \left[ 1 + (F_1 - 1) + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \frac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2} + \dots \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Define Total Noise Figure:**
By definition, $F_{total} = N_{i,total}^{eq} / (kT_0 B)$:

$$
\boxed{F_{total} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \frac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2} + \dots + \frac{F_n - 1}{G_1 G_2 \cdots G_{n-1}}}
$$

**Step 6 — Key Insight:**
The first stage's noise figure $F_1$ appears directly, while subsequent stages' contributions are **divided by the gains of all previous stages**. This is why a **high-gain, low-noise front-end** is critical in any receiver design.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Noise Temperature from Noise Figure

**Given:** A receiver with noise figure $F$ at standard temperature $T_0 = 290$ K.

**To Derive:** $T_e = T_0 (F - 1)$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Definition of Effective Input Noise Temperature:**
$T_e$ is the temperature at which a *hypothetical resistor* at the receiver's input would produce the same noise power as the receiver's internal noise sources.

**Step 2 — Noise Power from Equivalent Resistor at $T_e$:**

$$
N_{eq} = k_B T_e B
$$

**Step 3 — Excess Noise Added by the Receiver:**
The receiver adds $(F - 1) k_B T_0 B$ of noise (above and beyond amplifying the source noise):

$$
N_{added} = (F - 1) k_B T_0 B
$$

**Step 4 — Equate the Two Expressions:**

$$
k_B T_e B = (F - 1) k_B T_0 B
$$

Cancel $k_B B$ from both sides:

$$
\boxed{T_e = (F - 1)\, T_0}
$$

**Step 5 — Total System Noise Temperature:**
When the antenna noise temperature is $T_{ant}$ (due to sky noise, ground noise, etc.):

$$
T_{sys} = T_{ant} + T_e
$$

And the total noise power at the receiver input is:

$$
N_{total} = k_B (T_{ant} + T_e) B
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Problem Types & Their Solutions

| Problem Type | Given | Find | Key Formula |
|--------------|-------|------|-------------|
| Resistor noise voltage | $R$, $T$, $B$ | $v_n$ (rms) | $v_n = \sqrt{4k_B T R B}$ |
| Resistor noise current | $R$, $T$, $B$ | $i_n$ (rms) | $i_n = \sqrt{4k_B T B / R}$ |
| Available noise power | $T$, $B$ | $P_n$ (matched) | $P_n = k_B T B$ |
| Shot noise | $I_{dc}$, $B$ | $i_n$ (rms) | $i_n = \sqrt{2 q I_{dc} B}$ |
| NF ↔ F conversion | F (linear) | NF (dB) | $NF = 10 \log_{10} F$ |
| Cascade noise figure | $F_1, F_2, G_1$ | $F_{total}$ | Friis formula |
| Noise temperature | F (linear) | $T_e$ | $T_e = 290(F-1)$ |
| Output noise power | $F$, $G$, $B$ | $N_o$ | $N_o = F k T_0 B G$ |
| Output SNR | SNR$_i$, F | SNR$_o$ | $SNR_o = SNR_i / F$ |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define thermal noise. Derive an expression for the thermal noise voltage generated by a resistor.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Thermal noise is the random noise generated in a resistor due to the thermal agitation of charge carriers (electrons) at temperatures above absolute zero.
- **Expression (2 marks):** The mean-square thermal noise voltage across a resistor $R$ at temperature $T$ K over bandwidth $B$ Hz is:
  $$\overline{v_n^2} = 4 k_B T R B$$
  $$\Rightarrow v_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R B} \;\;\text{(rms)}$$

---

### **Question A2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is shot noise? Write the expression for shot noise current.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Shot noise arises in PN junction devices due to the discrete nature of charge carriers crossing the junction. The random arrival of electrons at the collector/drain produces fluctuations in the DC current.
- **Expression (1.5 marks):** 
  $$\overline{i_n^2} = 2 q I_{dc} B$$
  where $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C is the electron charge, $I_{dc}$ is the DC current, and $B$ is the measurement bandwidth.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]**

**A receiver has the following cascade:**
- **Stage 1 (LNA):** Noise figure $F_1 = 2$ dB, Gain $G_1 = 20$ dB
- **Stage 2 (Mixer):** Noise figure $F_2 = 6$ dB, Gain $G_2 = -8$ dB (conversion loss)
- **Stage 3 (IF Amplifier):** Noise figure $F_3 = 10$ dB, Gain $G_3 = 30$ dB

The input signal power is $S_i = -90$ dBm, and the antenna delivers $T_{ant} = 150$ K to a receiver with effective noise temperature $T_e$. The measurement bandwidth is $B = 10$ MHz. Compute:
**(a)** The total noise figure (in dB) of the cascade using Friis' formula.
**(b)** The output signal-to-noise ratio (SNR_o) in dB at the IF output.

---

#### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Convert dB Values to Linear:**

$$
F_1 = 10^{2/10} = 1.585
$$

$$
G_1 = 10^{20/10} = 100
$$

$$
F_2 = 10^{6/10} = 3.981
$$

$$
G_2 = 10^{-8/10} = 0.1585
$$

$$
F_3 = 10^{10/10} = 10
$$

$$
G_3 = 10^{30/10} = 1000
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Friis' Formula:**

$$
F_{total} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \frac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2}
$$

$$
F_{total} = 1.585 + \frac{3.981 - 1}{100} + \frac{10 - 1}{(100)(0.1585)}
$$

$$
F_{total} = 1.585 + \frac{2.981}{100} + \frac{9}{15.85}
$$

$$
F_{total} = 1.585 + 0.02981 + 0.5678
$$

$$
F_{total} \approx 2.183
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to dB:**

$$
NF_{total} = 10 \log_{10}(2.183) \approx 3.39\ \text{dB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice how the LNA's noise figure (1.585) dominates the cascade, and the mixer's high noise figure (3.981) is *attenuated by the LNA's gain* (factor of 100), making it contribute only 0.0298.

---

#### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Compute Total Gain:**

$$
G_{total} = G_1 \cdot G_2 \cdot G_3 = 100 \times 0.1585 \times 1000 = 15{,}850
$$

In dB:

$$
G_{total,\,dB} = 20 - 8 + 30 = 42\ \text{dB}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Output Signal Power:**

$$
S_o = S_i + G_{total} = -90\ \text{dBm} + 42\ \text{dB} = -48\ \text{dBm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Input Noise Power (Antenna + Receiver):**

$$
T_{sys} = T_{ant} + T_e = T_{ant} + T_0(F_{total} - 1) = 150 + 290(2.183 - 1)
$$

$$
T_{sys} = 150 + 290(1.183) = 150 + 343.1 = 493.1\ \text{K}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Input Noise Power:**

$$
N_i = k_B T_{sys} B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 493.1 \times 10^7
$$

$$
N_i = 6.804 \times 10^{-14}\ \text{W} = -71.67\ \text{dBm}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Output Noise Power:**

$$
N_o = N_i + G_{total} = -71.67 + 42 = -29.67\ \text{dBm}
$$

**Step 6 — Output SNR:**

$$
\text{SNR}_o = S_o - N_o = -48 - (-29.67) = -18.33\ \text{dB}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{SNR}_o \approx -18.33\ \text{dB}}
$$

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]**

**A resistor of $R = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ is at temperature $T = 300$ K. The measurement is done over a bandwidth $B = 100$ kHz. Compute:**
**(a)** The RMS thermal noise voltage across the resistor.
**(b)** If two such resistors are connected in series (each at 300 K) and the same bandwidth is used, find the new RMS noise voltage. Justify whether the noise powers add or whether the noise voltages add.

---

#### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Identify Given Values:**
- $R = 10{,}000\ \Omega$
- $T = 300\ \text{K}$
- $B = 100{,}000\ \text{Hz} = 10^5\ \text{Hz}$
- $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$

**Step 2 — Apply the Thermal Noise Voltage Formula:**

$$
\overline{v_n^2} = 4 k_B T R B
$$

$$
\overline{v_n^2} = 4 \times (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 300 \times 10{,}000 \times 10^5
$$

$$
\overline{v_n^2} = 4 \times 1.38 \times 3 \times 10^{4} \times 10^{-23+5}
$$

Let me recompute carefully:

$$
\overline{v_n^2} = 4 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300 \times 10^4 \times 10^5
$$

$$
= 4 \times 1.38 \times 300 \times 10^{-23+4+5}
$$

$$
= 4 \times 1.38 \times 300 \times 10^{-14}
$$

$$
= 1656 \times 10^{-14} = 1.656 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{V}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Take Square Root for RMS Value:**

$$
v_n = \sqrt{1.656 \times 10^{-11}} = \sqrt{16.56 \times 10^{-12}}
$$

$$
v_n = 4.07 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{V} = 4.07\ \mu\text{V}
$$

$$
\boxed{v_n \approx 4.07\ \mu\text{V (rms)}}
$$

---

#### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Compute Series Equivalent Resistance:**

$$
R_{series} = R_1 + R_2 = 10{,}000 + 10{,}000 = 20{,}000\ \Omega
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Formula for Combined Resistor:**

$$
\overline{v_{n,total}^2} = 4 k_B T R_{series} B = 4 k_B T (2R) B = 2 \times (4 k_B T R B)
$$

So the noise power **doubles** when resistance doubles. The noise voltage becomes:

$$
v_{n,total} = \sqrt{2 \times 1.656 \times 10^{-11}} = \sqrt{3.312 \times 10^{-11}}
$$

$$
v_{n,total} = 5.755 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{V} \approx 5.76\ \mu\text{V (rms)}
$$

**Step 3 — Justification (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> The **noise powers add** (not the noise voltages directly). This is because thermal noise from two independent sources is **uncorrelated**. The mean-square voltages add, and only the *total mean-square voltage* is then square-rooted to obtain the combined RMS noise voltage.
>
> Mathematically:
> $$\overline{v_{total}^2} = \overline{(v_{n1} + v_{n2})^2} = \overline{v_{n1}^2} + \overline{v_{n2}^2} + 2\overline{v_{n1} v_{n2}}$$
> Since the two noise sources are **uncorrelated**, $\overline{v_{n1} v_{n2}} = 0$. Hence:
> $$\overline{v_{total}^2} = \overline{v_{n1}^2} + \overline{v_{n2}^2}$$
>
> **Important pitfall:** $\;v_{total} \ne v_{n1} + v_{n2}$. The correct form is $v_{total} = \sqrt{v_{n1}^2 + v_{n2}^2}$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Easy Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Temperature unit error (–2 to –3 marks):** Using $T$ in Celsius instead of Kelvin. Always convert: $T(K) = T(°C) + 273$.
> 
> 2. **Confusing noise power addition with noise voltage addition (–2 marks):** When two independent noise sources are present, **powers add** (or equivalently, mean-square voltages add). Direct voltage addition is wrong.
> 
> 3. **Friis formula in dB (–3 marks):** Friis formula works in **linear scale only**. Converting each $F$ and $G$ to dB and adding them is a *fatal* error. Always: convert dB → linear → apply Friis → convert back to dB.
> 
> 4. **Mixing $k_B$ with $k$ from spring constant (–1 mark):** Use $k_B$ or $k$ (with subscript) consistently for Boltzmann's constant.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting the factor of 4 in noise voltage (–2 marks):** $4k_BTRB$ is for a *single resistor's* Thevenin model. Some students mistakenly use $2k_BTRB$ (the factor-of-2 comes from a different derivation path).
> 
> 6. **Bandwidth units (–1 mark):** $B$ must be in Hz, not kHz or MHz. If the problem gives $B = 100$ kHz, write $B = 10^5$ Hz in the formula.
> 
> 7. **Shot noise formula missing factor of 2 (–2 marks):** The one-sided PSD has a factor 2; the two-sided does not. Be consistent.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist. Revise it 30 minutes before entering the KTU exam hall.**

### **Core Formulas (Must Memorize):**
- ☐ Thermal noise power: $P_n = k_B T B$
- ☐ Thermal noise voltage: $v_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R B}$
- ☐ Thermal noise current: $i_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T B / R}$
- ☐ Shot noise current: $i_n = \sqrt{2 q I_{dc} B}$
- ☐ Noise figure: $F = (S_i/N_i) / (S_o/N_o)$
- ☐ Effective noise temperature: $T_e = T_0(F - 1)$
- ☐ Friis formula: $F_{total} = F_1 + (F_2-1)/G_1 + (F_3-1)/(G_1 G_2) + \dots$

### **Physical Constants (Must Memorize):**
- ☐ $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
- ☐ $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C
- ☐ $T_0 = 290$ K (standard noise reference temperature)

### **Conceptual Points (Must Understand):**
- ☐ Thermal noise is **white** and **Gaussian**; exists at all temperatures > 0 K.
- ☐ Shot noise is due to **discrete carrier** arrival; requires current flow.
- ☐ Flicker noise dominates **below 1 kHz**; negligible in RF systems.
- ☐ Transit-time noise matters only at **microwave frequencies**.
- ☐ **First stage of a receiver** dominates the cascade noise performance.
- ☐ Higher gain in stage 1 → reduces the impact of subsequent stage noise.
- ☐ **Noise figure in dB ≠ NF linear** when applying Friis — always work linearly.
- ☐ Uncorrelated noise sources add in **power** (mean-square), not in voltage.

### **Quick Trick for KTU Numerical Problems:**
- ☐ To convert dB ↔ linear: $F_{lin} = 10^{NF/10}$, $G_{lin} = 10^{G/10}$
- ☐ Always carry 4 significant figures in intermediate steps; round only at the end.
- ☐ If $S_i$ is in dBm, output SNR in dB: $SNR_o = S_i - (-174 + 10\log B + NF) + G_{total}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Note for KTU 2024 ESE Aspirants:** Module 1 (Internal Noise) is highly **scorable** because the questions are formulaic. If you memorize the formulas in this recap and practice 5–10 numerical problems, you should secure **12–14 out of 14** marks in any Part B question on this topic. Focus on the Friis formula and effective noise temperature — they are the **most repeated** concepts in KTU's ADC paper.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: White Noise
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT502 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** White Noise

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**White Noise** is formally defined as a zero-mean **Wide-Sense Stationary (WSS)** random process $n(t)$ whose power spectral density (PSD) is **constant and uniform across the entire frequency domain** (i.e., from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$ Hz). In signal-processing literature, this property gives it the name "white," drawing a direct analogy to *white light*, which contains equal intensities of all visible wavelengths.

Mathematically, the two-sided Power Spectral Density of an ideal white noise process is expressed as:

$$S_N(f) = \frac{N_0}{2} \quad \text{for all } f \in (-\infty, +\infty)$$

where $\frac{N_0}{2}$ is the constant noise power spectral density measured in **Watts per Hertz (W/Hz)**. The total average power is theoretically infinite because the area under a constant curve over an infinite bandwidth is unbounded.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** *A random process $n(t)$ is called white noise if its power spectral density $S_N(f)$ is a constant for all frequencies. Its mean value is zero and its autocorrelation function is a delta function at the origin.*

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "White Light" of Signals

Imagine you are standing in a room flooded with pure white light. This light is special because it contains **every color of the visible spectrum in equal proportion** — red, blue, green, yellow — all blended together. If you were to filter it through a prism, you would see a perfectly flat, uniform rainbow.

White noise is the **acoustic / electrical equivalent** of this white light:
- Instead of *colors* (frequencies of light), white noise contains **every possible frequency of electrical signal in equal measure**.
- Just as white light is the sum of all colors, white noise is the sum of all frequency components, each carrying an equal share of the total noise power.
- If you connected a white noise source to a spectrum analyzer, you would see a **flat horizontal line** stretching across the entire screen — meaning the power does not favor any frequency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Reality Check:** True mathematical white noise has infinite bandwidth and infinite power, which is physically unrealizable. In practice, we work with **Bandlimited White Noise (BWN)**, which is flat over a finite bandwidth of interest (e.g., the bandwidth of a communication channel) and zero outside it.

### 1.3 Core Physical and Statistical Properties

| Property | Mathematical Formulation | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Mean | $E[n(t)] = 0$ | DC component is zero — no bias |
| Autocorrelation | $R_N(\tau) = \frac{N_0}{2}\delta(\tau)$ | Samples are uncorrelated |
| Power Spectral Density | $S_N(f) = \frac{N_0}{2}$ (two-sided) | Flat across all frequencies |
| Variance | $\sigma^2 = R_N(0) = \infty$ | Infinite power (ideal case) |
| Stationarity | Wide-Sense Stationary (WSS) | Statistics time-invariant |

### 1.4 Why White Noise is the Bedrock of Communication Theory

In KTU's Analog and Digital Communication syllabus, white noise serves as the **canonical worst-case interference model** because:
- It is mathematically tractable (simple, clean equations).
- It represents the **thermal (Johnson-Nyquist) noise** generated by random electron motion in conductors at temperature $T$, characterized by $N_0 = kT$, where $k$ is **Boltzmann's constant** $= 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.
- Any practical communication system's noise performance is benchmarked against AWGN (Additive White Gaussian Noise) channels.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposing White Noise — A Layered Analytical View

White noise is best understood by dissecting it across **three orthogonal dimensions** of signal analysis: the **time domain**, the **frequency domain**, and the **statistical domain**. All three viewpoints are mathematically linked by the **Wiener-Khinchin theorem**.

#### Layer 1: Time-Domain Behavior
- A white noise sample function $n(t)$ is a **random, jittery waveform** with no periodicity, no discernible pattern, and abrupt, uncorrelated fluctuations.
- Any two samples taken at *different* instants are **statistically independent**, regardless of how infinitesimally close they are in time.
- This statistical independence is the defining hallmark of white noise and is the reason the autocorrelation function collapses to a Dirac delta.

#### Layer 2: Frequency-Domain Behavior
- A flat PSD means **every frequency component from DC to infinite frequency** carries the same infinitesimal power.
- Since the bandwidth is infinite, the total integrated power $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S_N(f) \, df$ diverges to infinity — hence ideal white noise is a **mathematical idealization**.
- In engineering practice, the PSD is constant only over the **system's operational bandwidth** $B$ (Hz), giving finite power: $P_N = N_0 B$.

#### Layer 3: Statistical Behavior
- For a *Gaussian* white noise process (AWGN), the amplitude at any instant follows a **zero-mean Gaussian probability distribution**: $n(t) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$.
- The Gaussian assumption is critical: it is justified by the **Central Limit Theorem** because thermal noise arises from the superposition of countless independent microscopic electron motions.
- Higher-order moments beyond the second vanish or are fully determined by the mean and variance, simplifying all subsequent analysis.

### 2.2 The Wiener-Khinchin Bridge

The most powerful tool in noise analysis is the **Wiener-Khinchin theorem**, which states that the **autocorrelation function** and the **power spectral density** form a **Fourier Transform pair**:

$$S_N(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_N(\tau)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} R_N(\tau) e^{-j2\pi f \tau} \, d\tau$$

$$R_N(\tau) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{S_N(f)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S_N(f) e^{j2\pi f \tau} \, df$$

For white noise, $S_N(f) = \frac{N_0}{2}$ is a constant. Substituting this into the inverse transform:

$$R_N(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{N_0}{2} e^{j2\pi f \tau} \, df = \frac{N_0}{2} \delta(\tau)$$

This is the **fundamental duality**: a flat spectrum in frequency ↔ a Dirac delta in the time-lag domain.

### 2.3 Bandlimited White Noise — The Engineer's Workhorse

Because infinite-bandwidth white noise is a physical impossibility, every real-world system operates on **bandlimited white noise**, defined over $[-B, +B]$ Hz:

$$S_N(f) = \begin{cases} \frac{N_0}{2}, & \vert f \vert \leq B \\ 0, & \vert f \vert > B \end{cases}$$

The corresponding autocorrelation function is no longer a delta but a **sinc pulse**:

$$R_N(\tau) = N_0 B \cdot \text{sinc}(2B\tau) = N_0 B \cdot \frac{\sin(2\pi B \tau)}{2\pi B \tau}$$

The variance (DC power) is finite: $\sigma^2 = R_N(0) = N_0 B$.

### 2.4 Effect of White Noise on a Linear System (Output Noise Power)

When white noise with PSD $S_N(f) = \frac{N_0}{2}$ is fed into a **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) system** with transfer function $H(f)$, the output noise PSD becomes:

$$S_{N,\text{out}}(f) = S_N(f) \cdot \vert H(f) \vert^2 = \frac{N_0}{2} \vert H(f) \vert^2$$

The total **average output noise power** is:

$$P_{N,\text{out}} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{N_0}{2} \vert H(f) \vert^2 \, df$$

For a low-pass system with rectangular transfer function of bandwidth $B$, this elegantly reduces to $P_{N,\text{out}} = N_0 B$.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S_N(f) = \frac{N_0}{2}$ | Two-sided PSD of white noise | W/Hz |
| 2 | $R_N(\tau) = \frac{N_0}{2}\delta(\tau)$ | Autocorrelation of ideal white noise | W |
| 3 | $E[n(t)] = 0$ | Zero mean | V or A |
| 4 | $\sigma^2 = N_0 B$ | Variance (bandlimited) | W |
| 5 | $R_N(\tau) = N_0 B \cdot \text{sinc}(2B\tau)$ | Autocorrelation of bandlimited WN | W |
| 6 | $S_{N,\text{out}}(f) = \frac{N_0}{2}\vert H(f)\vert^2$ | Output noise PSD through LTI | W/Hz |
| 7 | $P_{N,\text{out}} = N_0 \int_0^{\infty} \vert H(f)\vert^2 df$ | Output noise power (one-sided) | W |
| 8 | $N_0 = kT$ | Thermal noise PSD (one-sided) | W/Hz |
| 9 | $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ | Boltzmann's constant | J/K |
| 10 | $P_n = kTB$ | Thermal noise power (one-sided) | W |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always remember:** KTU problems typically use the **two-sided PSD** convention $\frac{N_0}{2}$ when integrating from $-\infty$ to $\infty$, and the **one-sided PSD** $N_0$ when integrating from $0$ to $\infty$. Failing to use the correct convention is a guaranteed mark-deduction trap.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Receiver Sensitivity Calculations:** Determines the **minimum detectable signal** at the input of a radio receiver.
- **Bit Error Rate (BER) Analysis:** AWGN is the noise model for deriving BER of BPSK, QPSK, QAM in Module 4.
- **Radar Range Equation:** The $kT_0 B F$ term that bounds radar receiver performance.
- **Optical Communication:** Shot noise and thermal noise in photodetectors are white over many decades of frequency.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Autocorrelation of Ideal White Noise from its PSD

**Goal:** Starting from the constant PSD, derive the impulse-like autocorrelation.

**Step 1 — Apply the Wiener-Khinchin Theorem.**
The autocorrelation and PSD are a Fourier Transform pair. Therefore:

$$R_N(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S_N(f) \, e^{+j2\pi f \tau} \, df$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the white noise PSD.**
For ideal white noise, $S_N(f) = \frac{N_0}{2}$ for all $f$:

$$R_N(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{N_0}{2} \, e^{+j2\pi f \tau} \, df$$

**Step 3 — Factor out the constant $\frac{N_0}{2}$.**
Since $\frac{N_0}{2}$ does not depend on $f$, it can be pulled out of the integral:

$$R_N(\tau) = \frac{N_0}{2} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{+j2\pi f \tau} \, df$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the inverse Fourier Transform of unity.**
The integral $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{+j2\pi f \tau} \, df$ is the standard representation of the **Dirac delta function** $\delta(\tau)$:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{+j2\pi f \tau} \, df = \delta(\tau)$$

**Step 5 — Final Result.**
Substituting back:

$$\boxed{R_N(\tau) = \frac{N_0}{2} \, \delta(\tau)}$$

> **Interpretation:** The autocorrelation is non-zero *only* at $\tau = 0$, meaning noise samples at any two distinct time instants are uncorrelated. The impulse strength is $\frac{N_0}{2}$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Average Power of Bandlimited White Noise

**Goal:** Compute the total average power when white noise is observed through an ideal brick-wall filter of bandwidth $B$ Hz.

**Step 1 — Set up the PSD expression.**
For bandlimited white noise, the two-sided PSD is:

$$S_N(f) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{N_0}{2}, & \vert f \vert \leq B \\ 0, & \vert f \vert > B \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Compute total power by integrating PSD.**
Average power of a WSS process equals the integral of its PSD over all frequencies (Parseval's theorem for power):

$$P_N = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S_N(f) \, df$$

**Step 3 — Apply the piecewise definition.**
The integrand is non-zero only over $[-B, B]$:

$$P_N = \int_{-B}^{B} \frac{N_0}{2} \, df$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$P_N = \frac{N_0}{2} \Big[ f \Big]_{-B}^{B} = \frac{N_0}{2} (B - (-B)) = \frac{N_0}{2} \cdot 2B$$

**Step 5 — Final Result.**

$$\boxed{P_N = N_0 B}$$

> **Interpretation:** The average noise power is directly proportional to both the system bandwidth $B$ (Hz) and the noise spectral density $N_0$ (W/Hz). This is the famous **$kTB$ formula** in disguise, since $N_0 = kT$ for thermal noise.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Output Noise Power Across an LTI Filter

**Goal:** Show that when white noise passes through a filter $H(f)$, the output PSD is shaped by $\vert H(f) \vert^2$.

**Step 1 — Express the input noise as a Fourier sum.**
Decompose input white noise into infinitesimal sinusoids of amplitude $dN(f)$ at each frequency $f$:

$$S_N(f) \, df = \text{power in } df \text{ around } f$$

**Step 2 — Pass each spectral component through the filter.**
An LTI system with transfer function $H(f)$ attenuates/amplifies each frequency component by $\vert H(f) \vert$. Hence the output PSD is the input PSD multiplied by the power transfer function $\vert H(f) \vert^2$:

$$S_{N,\text{out}}(f) = S_N(f) \cdot \vert H(f) \vert^2 = \frac{N_0}{2} \vert H(f) \vert^2$$

**Step 3 — Integrate to get total output noise power.**

$$P_{N,\text{out}} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{N_0}{2} \vert H(f) \vert^2 \, df$$

**Step 4 — Exploit even symmetry.**
Since $\vert H(f) \vert^2$ is an even function of $f$ for real systems:

$$P_{N,\text{out}} = N_0 \int_{0}^{\infty} \vert H(f) \vert^2 \, df$$

**Step 5 — Specialize to an ideal low-pass filter of bandwidth $B$.**

$$P_{N,\text{out}} = N_0 \int_{0}^{B} (1)^2 \, df = N_0 B$$

This confirms the result of Derivation 2 — the filter's role is simply to define the bandwidth of integration.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A communication receiver operates at a temperature of $T = 290$ K with an equivalent noise bandwidth of $B = 10$ kHz. Compute the thermal noise power at the receiver input.

**Solution:**

Given:
- $T = 290$ K
- $B = 10 \times 10^3$ Hz
- $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K

**Step 1 — Compute one-sided noise PSD.**

$$N_0 = kT = (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 290 = 4.002 \times 10^{-21} \text{ W/Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Compute total noise power.**

$$P_N = N_0 B = 4.002 \times 10^{-21} \times 10 \times 10^3$$

$$P_N = 4.002 \times 10^{-17} \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to dBm for engineering readability.**

$$P_N \text{ (dBm)} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4.002 \times 10^{-17}}{10^{-3}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(4.002 \times 10^{-14})$$

$$P_N \text{ (dBm)} \approx -133.98 \text{ dBm} \approx -134 \text{ dBm}$$

> **Insight:** The receiver's *noise floor* is approximately $-134$ dBm. Any received signal weaker than this cannot be reliably detected.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: White Noise vs. Colored Noise

| Feature | White Noise | Colored Noise |
|---|---|---|
| PSD shape | Flat (constant) | Non-flat (e.g., $1/f^2$, pink) |
| Autocorrelation | $\delta(\tau)$ | Non-zero for $\tau \neq 0$ |
| Bandwidth | Infinite (ideal) | Finite or shaped |
| Total power | Infinite (ideal) | Finite |
| Example | Thermal noise in resistors at high $f$ | Flicker noise ($1/f$), shot noise at low $f$ |
| Realizability | Mathematical ideal | Physically realizable |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Model — White Noise Across Time, Frequency, and Statistics

The following block diagram captures the **three mathematical views** of white noise, which are the only lenses needed to solve any KTU noise problem.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["WHITE NOISE PROCESS n of t"] --> B["TIME DOMAIN VIEW"]
    A --> C["FREQUENCY DOMAIN VIEW"]
    A --> D["STATISTICAL DOMAIN VIEW"]
    B --> B1["Random Jittery Waveform"]
    B --> B2["Uncorrelated Samples"]
    B --> B3["Delta Autocorrelation R_N of tau = N0 by 2 times delta tau"]
    C --> C1["Flat PSD S_N of f = N0 by 2"]
    C --> C2["Infinite Bandwidth"]
    C --> C3["Equal Power at All Frequencies"]
    D --> D1["Zero Mean"]
    D --> D2["Gaussian Amplitude PDF"]
    D --> D3["Variance sigma squared = N0 B"]
    C1 -.Fourier Pair.-> B3
    B3 -.Inverse Fourier.-> C1
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The three domains are connected by the **Wiener-Khinchin theorem** (Fourier duality, shown by the dotted arrows). Solving a noise problem typically involves switching between these three representations.

---

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — White Noise Through an LTI Communication Channel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC["Signal Source s of t"] --> SUM(("+"))
    WNG["White Noise Generator n of t with PSD N0 by 2"] --> SUM
    SUM --> CHN["LTI Channel Transfer Function H of f Bandwidth B"]
    CHN --> RCVR["Receiver Output y of t = s of t star h of t + n out of t"]
    RCVR --> DEC["Decision / Demodulator"]
    DEC --> OUT["Detected Information"]
    style SUM fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style WNG fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style CHN fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style RCVR fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

**Interpretation of each block:**

| Block | Function | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Signal Source** | Generates the message-bearing waveform $s(t)$ | Modulated signal |
| **White Noise Generator** | Models thermal/shot noise injected by the channel | $n(t)$, PSD $= N_0/2$ |
| **Summer (Summing Junction)** | Models **Additive** noise corruption: $s(t) + n(t)$ | Composite noisy signal |
| **LTI Channel** | Bandlimits the white noise to bandwidth $B$ | Noise PSD becomes $\frac{N_0}{2}\vert H(f)\vert^2$ |
| **Receiver** | Filters, amplifies, and prepares for detection | $y(t)$ |
| **Decision Block** | Compares received symbol to threshold | Bit decisions |

---

### 4.3 Spectral Visualization (Mermaid-Based Plot Emulation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FREQ_PLOT["Power Spectral Density S_N of f vs Frequency f"]
        direction LR
        AXIS["f axis from minus infinity to plus infinity"] --- FLAT_LINE["Constant Flat Line at height N0 by 2 across ALL frequencies"]
    end
    subgraph AUTOCORR_PLOT["Autocorrelation R_N of tau vs Lag tau"]
        direction LR
        AXIS2["tau axis"] --- IMPULSE["Vertical Impulse at tau = 0 with strength N0 by 2"]
    end
    FREQ_PLOT --- AUTOCORR_PLOT
    style FLAT_LINE fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style IMPULSE fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Duality note:** The flat line in the frequency plot ↔ the isolated impulse in the autocorrelation plot. This visual duality is the geometric face of the Wiener-Khinchin theorem.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

Define white noise. State any two of its important properties.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
White noise is a wide-sense stationary random process $n(t)$ whose power spectral density $S_N(f)$ is **constant for all frequencies**. Mathematically, $S_N(f) = \frac{N_0}{2}$ for all $f \in (-\infty, \infty)$, where $\frac{N_0}{2}$ is the noise power spectral density in W/Hz.

**Two important properties (1.5 marks):**
1. **Zero mean:** $E[n(t)] = 0$ for all $t$.
2. **Autocorrelation is a Dirac delta:** $R_N(\tau) = \frac{N_0}{2}\delta(\tau)$, implying that any two distinct noise samples are uncorrelated, no matter how close in time they are.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write "white noise has constant power at all frequencies." This is imprecise. The PSD is constant, not the *power*. Power is the integral of PSD, and that integral diverges. Always use the term *power spectral density* in your KTU answer.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional Exam]**

What is the autocorrelation function of an ideal white noise process? What does it imply about the statistical relationship between noise samples at different time instants?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Autocorrelation expression (1.5 marks):**
The autocorrelation function of an ideal white noise process with two-sided PSD $\frac{N_0}{2}$ is:

$$R_N(\tau) = \frac{N_0}{2} \delta(\tau)$$

where $\delta(\tau)$ is the Dirac delta function.

**Statistical implication (1.5 marks):**
Because $R_N(\tau) = 0$ for all $\tau \neq 0$, the noise samples at any two distinct time instants are **statistically uncorrelated**. For a Gaussian process, uncorrelatedness further implies **statistical independence**, meaning knowledge of $n(t_1)$ provides no information about $n(t_2)$ when $t_1 \neq t_2$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Adapted]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the autocorrelation function of an ideal white noise process from its power spectral density using the Wiener-Khinchin theorem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A low-pass communication channel has a rectangular transfer function of bandwidth $B = 4$ kHz. White noise with one-sided PSD $N_0 = 4 \times 10^{-21}$ W/Hz is applied at the input. Calculate: (i) the total average noise power at the output, and (ii) the output noise power spectral density. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of $R_N(\tau)$ for white noise (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — State the Wiener-Khinchin theorem (1 mark).**
The autocorrelation function $R_N(\tau)$ and the power spectral density $S_N(f)$ are related by Fourier transform:

$$R_N(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S_N(f) \, e^{j2\pi f \tau} \, df$$

**Step 2 — Write the PSD of white noise (1 mark).**
For ideal white noise:

$$S_N(f) = \frac{N_0}{2}, \quad \forall f \in (-\infty, \infty)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the integral (1 mark).**

$$R_N(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{N_0}{2} e^{j2\pi f \tau} \, df$$

**Step 4 — Pull constant out of integral (1 mark).**

$$R_N(\tau) = \frac{N_0}{2} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j2\pi f \tau} \, df$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the delta function representation (2 marks).**
By the standard Fourier identity:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j2\pi f \tau} \, df = \delta(\tau)$$

**Step 6 — Final expression (1 mark).**

$$\boxed{R_N(\tau) = \frac{N_0}{2} \delta(\tau)}$$

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Bandwidth $B = 4 \text{ kHz} = 4 \times 10^3$ Hz
- One-sided PSD $N_0 = 4 \times 10^{-21}$ W/Hz
- Two-sided PSD: $\frac{N_0}{2} = 2 \times 10^{-21}$ W/Hz

**(i) Total average noise power at output (4 marks):**

For an ideal rectangular low-pass filter of bandwidth $B$:

$$P_{N,\text{out}} = N_0 \cdot B$$

$$P_{N,\text{out}} = (4 \times 10^{-21}) \times (4 \times 10^3)$$

$$P_{N,\text{out}} = 16 \times 10^{-18} = 1.6 \times 10^{-17} \text{ W}$$

**Converting to dBm:**

$$P_{N,\text{out}} \text{ (dBm)} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1.6 \times 10^{-17}}{10^{-3}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(1.6 \times 10^{-14})$$

$$P_{N,\text{out}} \text{ (dBm)} \approx -127.96 \text{ dBm} \approx -128 \text{ dBm}$$

**(ii) Output noise power spectral density (3 marks):**

For an ideal low-pass channel with $\vert H(f) \vert = 1$ within the passband:

$$S_{N,\text{out}}(f) = \frac{N_0}{2} \cdot \vert H(f) \vert^2 = \begin{cases} 2 \times 10^{-21} \text{ W/Hz}, & \vert f \vert \leq 4 \text{ kHz} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (Part B):** Students often forget to **convert kHz to Hz** before multiplying, leading to an off-by-1000-factor error worth 2 marks. Also, marking schemes penalize mixing one-sided and two-sided PSD conventions in the same problem — pick one and stick to it.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the meaning of bandlimited white noise. Derive the expression for its autocorrelation function and total average power. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A receiver operates at room temperature $T = 300$ K with an effective noise bandwidth $B = 100$ kHz. Compute the available thermal noise power at the receiver input in (i) Watts, and (ii) dBm. Take Boltzmann's constant $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Bandlimited White Noise (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):**
Bandlimited white noise is a noise process whose power spectral density is **constant over a finite frequency interval** $[-B, +B]$ and **zero elsewhere**. It is a practical, physically realizable approximation of ideal white noise, with finite total power.

$$S_N(f) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{N_0}{2}, & \vert f \vert \leq B \\ 0, & \vert f \vert > B \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Derivation of autocorrelation (3 marks):**
Apply the inverse Fourier transform:

$$R_N(\tau) = \int_{-B}^{B} \frac{N_0}{2} e^{j2\pi f \tau} \, df = \frac{N_0}{2} \left[ \frac{e^{j2\pi f \tau}}{j2\pi \tau} \right]_{-B}^{B}$$

$$R_N(\tau) = \frac{N_0}{2} \cdot \frac{e^{j2\pi B \tau} - e^{-j2\pi B \tau}}{j2\pi \tau} = \frac{N_0}{2} \cdot \frac{\sin(2\pi B \tau)}{\pi \tau}$$

Recognizing the **normalized sinc function** $\text{sinc}(x) = \frac{\sin(\pi x)}{\pi x}$:

$$R_N(\tau) = N_0 B \cdot \text{sinc}(2B\tau)$$

**Step 3 — Total average power (2 marks):**
Average power is $R_N(0)$. As $\tau \to 0$, $\text{sinc}(2B\tau) \to 1$:

$$P_N = R_N(0) = N_0 B$$

---

**(b) Thermal Noise Power Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $T = 300$ K, $B = 100 \text{ kHz} = 10^5$ Hz, $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.

**Step 1 — Compute one-sided noise PSD (2 marks):**

$$N_0 = kT = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300 = 4.14 \times 10^{-21} \text{ W/Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Compute power in Watts (3 marks):**

$$P_N = N_0 B = 4.14 \times 10^{-21} \times 10^5 = 4.14 \times 10^{-16} \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to dBm (2 marks):**

$$P_N \text{ (dBm)} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4.14 \times 10^{-16}}{10^{-3}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(4.14 \times 10^{-13})$$

$$P_N \text{ (dBm)} \approx 10 \times (-12.383) \approx -123.83 \text{ dBm} \approx -124 \text{ dBm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Many students write $P = kT$ instead of $P = kTB$ — losing 3 marks instantly. Always remember: noise **power** requires the **bandwidth** dimension; noise **spectral density** $N_0 = kT$ alone is in W/Hz, not Watts.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** White noise is a WSS process with a **flat (constant) PSD** across all frequencies: $S_N(f) = \frac{N_0}{2}$.
- **Mean:** Always zero: $E[n(t)] = 0$.
- **Autocorrelation:** $R_N(\tau) = \frac{N_0}{2}\delta(\tau)$ — uncorrelated samples.
- **Variance/Power (Bandlimited):** $\sigma^2 = N_0 B$.
- **Thermal Noise Relation:** $N_0 = kT$, with $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.
