- **Wiener-Khinchin Theorem:** The bridge between time-domain autocorrelation and frequency-domain PSD.
- **Two-sided vs. One-sided PSD:** Use $\frac{N_0}{2}$ when integrating over $(-\infty, \infty)$; use $N_0$ when integrating over $(0, \infty)$.
- **Effect on LTI System:** Output PSD gets shaped by $\vert H(f) \vert^2$.
- **Ideal vs. Practical:** Ideal white noise has infinite bandwidth and infinite power — physical systems deal with **bandlimited** white noise.
- **AWGN channel:** The cornerstone noise model for all of KTU's digital communication modules.
- **Watch the units:** $N_0$ is in W/Hz; multiplying by Hz gives Watts.
- **Watch the conventions:** Don't mix two-sided and one-sided PSD in a single derivation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** For any noise problem, start by writing the **PSD**, then apply **Wiener-Khinchin** to get autocorrelation, then **integrate** to get power. This three-step ritual will solve 90% of all KTU noise questions on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sampling and Quantization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT502 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sampling and Quantization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Sampling?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sampling** is the process of converting a continuous-time (analog) signal into a discrete-time signal by capturing its instantaneous amplitude values at uniformly spaced time intervals. Mathematically, it is equivalent to multiplying the message signal $m(t)$ with a periodic train of narrow pulses (the sampling train).

The **Sampling Theorem** (also called the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**) is the foundational rule of all digital communication. It states:

> [!NOTE]
> **Nyquist Sampling Theorem:** A bandlimited analog signal $m(t)$ having maximum frequency component $f_m$ Hz can be completely reconstructed from its samples, provided the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies $f_s \geq 2f_m$. The minimum allowable sampling rate, $f_s = 2f_m$, is called the **Nyquist Rate**, and $2f_m$ is called the **Nyquist Frequency**.

### 1.2 What is Quantization?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quantization** is the process of approximating each sampled amplitude to one of a finite set of discrete levels. The continuous amplitude $m(kT_s)$ is mapped onto a discrete amplitude from a quantization ladder/staircase of step size $\Delta$. The difference between the actual sample value and the quantized value is called **Quantization Error** $e_q$.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a movie — it is a **sampling** of continuous reality. Your eyes cannot distinguish the individual frames (typically 24–48 per second) if the action is not "too fast" (analogous to the **Nyquist limit**). The camera captures moments, just as sampling captures amplitudes.

**Quantization** is like rounding prices to the nearest rupee. A shirt that costs ₹199.73 is *quantized* to ₹200. The rounding-off error is the **quantization noise** — an unavoidable price you pay for converting infinitely many possible values to a finite set.

### 1.4 Key Terminology Cheat Sheet

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $f_m$ | Highest frequency component in the message signal (Hz) |
| $f_s$ | Sampling frequency (samples/second) |
| $T_s$ | Sampling interval, $T_s = 1/f_s$ |
| Nyquist Rate | $2f_m$ — minimum sampling rate required |
| Aliasing | Distortion caused when $f_s < 2f_m$ |
| L | Number of quantization levels |
| $\Delta$ | Quantization step size |
| n | Number of bits per sample, $n = \log_2 L$ |
| SQNR | Signal-to-Quantization Noise Ratio |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Do We Need Sampling?

Analog signals are continuous in both time and amplitude. To transmit, store, or process them using digital hardware, we must convert them into a stream of numbers. Sampling handles the **time axis** (continuous → discrete), while quantization handles the **amplitude axis** (continuous → discrete). The two processes together form the front end of every **PCM (Pulse Code Modulation)** system.

### 2.2 Types of Sampling

1. **Ideal (Impulse) Sampling:** Multiplication of $m(t)$ by an impulse train. Theoretically perfect, physically unrealizable because impulses have zero width and infinite area.
2. **Natural Sampling (Chopper Sampling):** Multiplication by a train of finite-width rectangular pulses. The top of each sample follows the natural curvature of $m(t)$.
3. **Flat-Top Sampling (Sample-and-Hold):** Each sample is held constant for a small duration, producing a staircase waveform. This is the most commonly used method in practical A/D converters because it gives the circuit time to perform quantization.

### 2.3 The Mathematics of Sampling

Let the sampling signal be a periodic impulse train:

$$s(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$$

The sampled signal is $m_s(t) = m(t) \cdot s(t)$. Taking the Fourier transform:

$$M_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} M(f - nf_s)$$

This shows that **spectral replicas of $M(f)$ are produced at multiples of $f_s$**. As long as these replicas do not overlap, a low-pass filter with cutoff $f_m$ can perfectly reconstruct $m(t)$. The replicas stay separate **only if** $f_s - f_m \geq f_m$, i.e., $f_s \geq 2f_m$.

### 2.4 Aliasing — The Disaster of Undersampling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aliasing** is the phenomenon in which high-frequency components of the message signal masquerade as low-frequency components in the reconstructed signal because the sampling rate is too low. It is *irreversible* — once aliasing occurs, the original information is permanently lost.

Aliasing is prevented by:
1. Sampling at or above the Nyquist rate ($f_s \geq 2f_m$).
2. Applying an **anti-aliasing low-pass filter** *before* sampling to band-limit $m(t)$.

### 2.5 Uniform Quantization

In **uniform (linear) quantization**, the step size $\Delta$ is constant across the entire amplitude range. The output is:

$$m_q(kT_s) = \text{round}\left[\frac{m(kT_s)}{\Delta}\right] \cdot \Delta$$

- **Mid-Rise Type:** Quantization levels exist at $\pm \Delta/2, \pm 3\Delta/2, \dots$
- **Mid-Tread Type:** Quantization levels exist at $0, \pm\Delta, \pm 2\Delta, \dots$

### 2.6 Quantization Error Power (Noise Power)

Assuming the quantization error $e_q$ is uniformly distributed in $[-\Delta/2, \Delta/2]$, its mean-square value is:

$$\sigma_q^2 = \frac{1}{\Delta} \int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} e^2 \, de = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

### 2.7 Non-Uniform Quantization & Companding

> [!NOTE]
> For signals like speech, small amplitudes occur more frequently than large amplitudes. Uniform quantization wastes bits on rare loud sounds. **Non-uniform quantization** uses smaller steps for small signals and larger steps for large signals, achieved in practice by **companding** (compressing at the transmitter, expanding at the receiver).

Two international companding standards:
- **µ-Law (mu-law):** Used in North America & Japan. $F(x) = \text{sgn}(x) \dfrac{\ln(1+\mu \vert x \vert)}{\ln(1+\mu)}$, with $\mu = 255$.
- **A-Law:** Used in Europe & most of the world. $F(x) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{A \vert x \vert}{1+\ln A}, & 0 \leq \vert x \vert \leq 1/A \\ \dfrac{1+\ln(A\vert x \vert)}{1+\ln A}, & 1/A < \vert x \vert \leq 1 \end{cases}$, with $A = 87.6$.

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below contains every formula you need to solve any KTU 2024 Scheme question on Sampling and Quantization.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nyquist Rate | $f_s = 2f_m$ | Hz |
| 2 | Sampling Interval | $T_s = 1/f_s$ | seconds |
| 3 | No. of quantization levels | $L = 2^n$ | $n$ = bits/sample |
| 4 | Quantization step size | $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{pp}}{L} = \dfrac{2 V_{max}}{2^n}$ | Volts |
| 5 | Quantization noise power | $\sigma_q^2 = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12}$ | Watts |
| 6 | Signal power (uniform) | $P_s = \dfrac{V_{max}^2}{2}$ for sine; $\dfrac{m^2(t) \text{ mean square}}{}$ | Watts |
| 7 | SQNR (linear) | $\text{SQNR} = \dfrac{P_s}{\sigma_q^2} = \dfrac{3 \cdot 2^{2n} \cdot P_s}{V_{max}^2}$ | ratio |
| 8 | SQNR (sine input) | $\text{SQNR} = 1.76 + 6.02n \;\; \text{dB}$ | dB |
| 9 | Transmission bit rate | $R_b = n f_s$ | bits/second |
| 10 | Reconstruction LPF cutoff | $f_c = f_m$ | Hz |
| 11 | µ-Law compander | $F(x) = \text{sgn}(x) \dfrac{\ln(1+\mu \vert x \vert)}{\ln(1+\mu)}$ | $\mu = 255$ |
| 12 | A-Law compander | piecewise (see §2.7) | $A = 87.6$ |

### 2.9 Engineering Utility

- **Telephony:** 8 kHz sampling (4 kHz voice) → 64 kbps PCM (the famous *DS0* channel).
- **CD Audio:** 44.1 kHz sampling, 16-bit quantization → 705.6 kbps per channel.
- **VoIP, 4G/5G voice:** All built on sampling + quantization + coding.
- **Medical Imaging (MRI, CT):** High-fidelity sampling and quantization preserve diagnostic detail.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Sampling Theorem (Spectral Argument)

**Goal:** Show that sampling produces spectral replicas of $M(f)$ at integer multiples of $f_s$, and that these replicas do not overlap iff $f_s \geq 2f_m$.

**Step 1 — Define the sampling impulse train.**
A periodic train of unit impulses with period $T_s$ is:

$$s(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$$

This is a periodic function of time, so it can be expanded as a complex Fourier series:

$$s(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} C_n \, e^{j 2 \pi n f_s t}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Fourier coefficients.**

$$C_n = \frac{1}{T_s} \int_{-T_s/2}^{T_s/2} s(t) \, e^{-j 2 \pi n f_s t} \, dt$$

Because only the impulse at $t = 0$ lies inside $[-T_s/2, T_s/2]$:

$$C_n = \frac{1}{T_s} \int_{-T_s/2}^{T_s/2} \delta(t) \, e^{-j 2 \pi n f_s t} \, dt = \frac{1}{T_s}$$

**Step 3 — Express the sampled signal in frequency domain.**
The sampled signal is $m_s(t) = m(t) \cdot s(t)$. Substituting the Fourier series:

$$m_s(t) = m(t) \cdot \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j 2 \pi n f_s t}$$

Taking the Fourier transform and using the frequency-shifting property $\mathcal{F}\{m(t)e^{j 2 \pi f_0 t}\} = M(f - f_0)$:

$$M_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} M(f - n f_s)$$

**Step 4 — Reconstruction condition.**
Each replica of $M(f)$ is centered at $n f_s$ with bandwidth $2 f_m$. The replicas are *non-overlapping* provided the lowest edge of the right-side replica exceeds the highest edge of the central replica:

$$f_s - f_m \;\geq\; f_m \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; f_s \geq 2 f_m$$

A low-pass filter of cutoff $f_m$ can then extract $M(f)$ exactly. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation of Quantization Noise Power

**Step 1 — Assume uniform distribution of error.**
The quantization error $e_q$ is the difference between the actual sample $m(kT_s)$ and its quantized version $m_q(kT_s)$. For uniform quantization with step $\Delta$, we have $-\Delta/2 \leq e_q \leq \Delta/2$. Assuming uniform distribution (a standard and well-justified assumption for high-resolution quantizers with random signals):

$$p(e_q) = \frac{1}{\Delta}, \quad -\frac{\Delta}{2} \leq e_q \leq \frac{\Delta}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the mean-square error (noise power).**

$$\sigma_q^2 = E[e_q^2] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^2 \, p(e) \, de = \frac{1}{\Delta} \int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} e^2 \, de$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$\int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} e^2 \, de = \left[ \frac{e^3}{3} \right]_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} = \frac{(\Delta/2)^3 - (-\Delta/2)^3}{3} = \frac{\Delta^3}{12}$$

**Step 4 — Final result.**

$$\sigma_q^2 = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{\Delta^3}{12} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} \quad \blacksquare$$

### 3.3 Derivation of SQNR for a Sinusoidal Input (The Famous $6.02n + 1.76$ dB Formula)

**Step 1 — Signal power of a full-scale sine wave.**
For a sinusoidal message $m(t) = V_{max} \sin(2\pi f_m t)$, the average signal power delivered to a 1 Ω load is:

$$P_s = \frac{V_{max}^2}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Express $\Delta$ in terms of $V_{max}$ and $n$.**
The peak-to-peak range is $2 V_{max}$, divided into $L = 2^n$ levels:

$$\Delta = \frac{2 V_{max}}{2^n}$$

**Step 3 — Compute noise power.**

$$\sigma_q^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} = \frac{1}{12} \left( \frac{2 V_{max}}{2^n} \right)^2 = \frac{4 V_{max}^2}{12 \cdot 4^n} = \frac{V_{max}^2}{3 \cdot 4^n}$$

**Step 4 — Form the SQNR.**

$$\text{SQNR} = \frac{P_s}{\sigma_q^2} = \frac{V_{max}^2 / 2}{V_{max}^2 / (3 \cdot 4^n)} = \frac{3 \cdot 4^n}{2} = 1.5 \cdot 2^{2n}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to decibels.**

$$\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5 \cdot 2^{2n}) = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 20 n \log_{10}(2)$$

Using $10 \log_{10}(1.5) \approx 1.76$ and $20 \log_{10}(2) \approx 6.02$:

$$\boxed{\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 1.76 + 6.02 n} \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the *most-asked* result in KTU ESE papers. Memorize: every extra bit adds ~6 dB (i.e., a factor of 4 in linear power) of SQNR.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How Bits Affect Each Parameter

| Bits $n$ | Levels $L$ | SQNR (dB) | Step $\Delta$ (for $V_{max}=1$ V) | Comment |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | 16 | 25.84 | 0.125 | Telephony minimum |
| 8 | 256 | 49.92 | 0.0078125 | Standard PCM voice |
| 12 | 4096 | 74.00 | $4.88 \times 10^{-4}$ | Broadcast audio |
| 16 | 65536 | 98.08 | $3.05 \times 10^{-5}$ | CD quality |

### 3.5 Numerical Example (Worked-Out KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A signal $m(t) = 4 \sin(2000\pi t)$ is sampled at $f_s = 8$ kHz and quantized with 8-bit uniform quantization. Find (i) the Nyquist rate, (ii) the quantization step size, (iii) the SQNR in dB.

**Solution:**

**Step (i):** $f_m = 1000$ Hz, so Nyquist rate $= 2 f_m = 2000$ Hz. Since $f_s = 8000$ Hz $\gg 2000$ Hz, no aliasing.

**Step (ii):** $V_{max} = 4$ V, $L = 2^8 = 256$.

$$\Delta = \frac{2 V_{max}}{L} = \frac{2 \cdot 4}{256} = 0.03125 \text{ V} = 31.25 \text{ mV}$$

**Step (iii):** $\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 1.76 + 6.02 \cdot 8 = 1.76 + 48.16 = 49.92$ dB.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Complete PCM System

This Mermaid diagram illustrates how the analog signal flows through sampling, quantization, encoding, transmission, decoding, and reconstruction. The blocks in **double asterisks** must be reproduced in any KTU answer.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Analog Message Signal m of t"]:::src --> B["Anti-Aliasing Low Pass Filter"]:::lpf
    B --> C["Sampler f_s greater than 2 f_m"]:::sample
    C --> D["Quantizer Uniform or Non-Uniform"]:::quant
    D --> E["Encoder n bit Binary Codeword"]:::enc
    E --> F["Channel Encoder Line Coding"]:::chenc
    F --> G["Channel Noise and Distortion"]:::noise
    G --> H["Channel Decoder"]:::chdec
    H --> I["Decoder n bit to Quantized Level"]:::dec
    I --> J["Reconstruction Low Pass Filter f_c equals f_m"]:::rlpf
    J --> K["Reconstructed Signal m_hat of t"]:::out
    classDef src fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#9d0208,color:#000
    classDef lpf fill:#fff1e6,stroke:#ca6702,color:#000
    classDef sample fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#9d4e00,color:#000
    classDef quant fill:#caffbf,stroke:#1b7a3a,color:#000
    classDef enc fill:#9bf6ff,stroke:#0077b6,color:#000
    classDef chenc fill:#a0c4ff,stroke:#023e8a,color:#000
    classDef noise fill:#ffadad,stroke:#6a040f,color:#000,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
    classDef chdec fill:#bdb2ff,stroke:#3a0ca3,color:#000
    classDef dec fill:#ffc6ff,stroke:#9d4edd,color:#000
    classDef rlpf fill:#fdffb6,stroke:#856d05,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#d0f4de,stroke:#1b4332,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Sampling Methods — Functional Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MSG["Continuous Message m of t"] --> MUL["Multiplier"]
    PUL["Sampling Pulse Train s of t"] --> MUL
    MUL --> IDEAL["Ideal Impulse Sampling Infinite Bandwidth Replicas"]
    MUL --> NAT["Natural Sampling Finite Width Pulses Curved Tops"]
    MUL --> FLAT["Flat Top Sample and Hold Staircase Output"]
    IDEAL --> REC["Reconstruction LPF Cutoff f_m"]
    NAT --> REC
    FLAT --> REC
    REC --> OUT["Reconstructed m_hat of t"]
```

### 4.3 Quantization Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN["Sampled Amplitude Continuous Value"]:::in --> CMP["Comparator Ladder"]
    CMP --> ASSIGN["Assign Nearest Quantization Level"]
    ASSIGN --> CODE["Binary Codeword n bits"]
    IN --> DIFF["Subtract"]
    ASSIGN --> DIFF
    DIFF --> ERR["Quantization Error e_q bounded by Delta over 2"]
    style IN fill:#caffbf,stroke:#1b7a3a,color:#000
    style ERR fill:#ffadad,stroke:#6a040f,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Spectral Diagram — Sampling and Aliasing (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Original Spectrum M of f centered at 0 bandwidth f_m"] --> B["After Sampling M_s of f Replicas at n f_s"]
    B --> C{"Is f_s greater than or equal to 2 f_m?"}
    C -- Yes --> D["Replicas Separated No Aliasing LPF Recovers M of f"]
    C -- No --> E["Replicas OVERLAP Aliasing Distortion UNRECOVERABLE"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *State the Nyquist sampling theorem. What is aliasing and how is it prevented?*

**Model Answer:**
The Nyquist sampling theorem states that a bandlimited signal $m(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_m$ Hz can be completely reconstructed from its discrete samples if the sampling frequency $f_s \geq 2 f_m$. The minimum rate $2 f_m$ is called the **Nyquist rate**.
**Aliasing** is the distortion that occurs when spectral replicas of the sampled signal overlap, which happens whenever $f_s < 2 f_m$. It is prevented by:
1. Sampling at or above the Nyquist rate.
2. Using an **anti-aliasing low-pass filter** with cutoff $f_m$ *before* the sampler, to band-limit the input.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define quantization error. Derive the expression for quantization noise power in uniform quantization.*

**Model Answer:**
Quantization error $e_q$ is the difference between the actual sampled amplitude $m(kT_s)$ and its quantized value $m_q(kT_s)$. In uniform quantization, $e_q$ lies in $[-\Delta/2, \Delta/2]$.
Assuming $e_q$ is uniformly distributed with pdf $p(e) = 1/\Delta$:

$$\sigma_q^2 = \frac{1}{\Delta} \int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} e^2 \, de = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

Hence, the quantization noise power is $\sigma_q^2 = \Delta^2/12$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Sampling & Quantization Pitfalls):**
> - Students often confuse **Nyquist rate** ($2 f_m$) with **Nyquist frequency** ($f_s/2$). State both clearly.
> - Many forget the factor of 2 in the **mid-rise quantizer step size formula**: $\Delta = 2 V_{max}/L$, not $V_{max}/L$.
> - In SQNR derivations, do not assume the signal is a sine unless the problem says so. For uniform distribution, use $P_s = \sigma_m^2$.
> - **Companding question** students forget the $\mu = 255$ and $A = 87.6$ standard values. Always quote them.
> - Marks are lost when anti-aliasing filter is *not* mentioned in sampling problems.

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** — **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** State and prove the sampling theorem. Explain the concept of aliasing with suitable diagrams.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A bandlimited signal $m(t)$ with $f_m = 4$ kHz is sampled at $f_s = 10$ kHz. Find the highest alias frequency. If $f_s$ is increased to 12 kHz, what is the new Nyquist interval and the guard band?

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Sampling theorem (Proof):** Refer to §3.1 of these notes. State the theorem, write the impulse train expression, take its Fourier series, derive $M_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum M(f - n f_s)$, and explain that non-overlapping replicas require $f_s \geq 2 f_m$. Mention the reconstruction low-pass filter with cutoff $f_m$. Include a sketch of the spectrum showing replicas at $0, \pm f_s, \pm 2f_s, \dots$ (re-draw the Mermaid spectral concept on paper).

**Aliasing:** Define aliasing. Sketch overlapping spectra when $f_s < 2 f_m$. Emphasize that once aliasing occurs, the distortion cannot be removed by any post-processing filter. Mention the **anti-aliasing filter** placed before the sampler.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

*Step 1:* When $f_s = 10$ kHz and $f_m = 4$ kHz, the Nyquist rate is $2 f_m = 8$ kHz. Since $f_s = 10$ kHz $> 8$ kHz, no aliasing. The highest frequency component in the sampled spectrum is at $f_s + f_m = 14$ kHz, so the **highest alias frequency** = 14 kHz.

*Step 2:* Nyquist interval = $T_s = 1/f_s = 1/12000 = 83.33\;\mu$s.

*Step 3:* Guard band = $f_s - 2 f_m = 12000 - 8000 = 4000$ Hz = 4 kHz.

---

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** — **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a block diagram, explain the PCM system. Derive the expression for SQNR in dB for a sinusoidal input.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A signal with peak-to-peak voltage 8 V and bandwidth 5 kHz is quantized using 12-bit uniform PCM. Find (i) the step size, (ii) the quantization noise power, (iii) the SQNR in dB, and (iv) the bit rate if sampling is done at 1.2 times the Nyquist rate.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) PCM system (Refer to §4.1 Mermaid block diagram in these notes):**
Discuss: Anti-aliasing LPF → Sampler → Quantizer → Encoder → Channel encoder → Channel → Channel decoder → Decoder → Reconstruction LPF. Highlight that PCM is the *combination of sampling + quantization + encoding*.

**Derivation of SQNR:** Follow §3.3 of these notes step by step to arrive at:
$\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 1.76 + 6.02 n$ dB.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

*Step 1 — Step size:* $V_{pp} = 8 \Rightarrow V_{max} = 4$ V. $L = 2^{12} = 4096$.

$$\Delta = \frac{2 V_{max}}{L} = \frac{8}{4096} = 1.953 \text{ mV}$$

*Step 2 — Quantization noise power:*

$$\sigma_q^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} = \frac{(1.953 \times 10^{-3})^2}{12} = \frac{3.815 \times 10^{-6}}{12} = 3.18 \times 10^{-7} \text{ W}$$

*Step 3 — Signal power (sine):*

$$P_s = \frac{V_{max}^2}{2} = \frac{16}{2} = 8 \text{ W}$$

*Step 4 — SQNR:*

$$\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{8}{3.18 \times 10^{-7}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(2.516 \times 10^7) \approx 74.0 \text{ dB}$$

(Cross-check with the formula: $1.76 + 6.02 \cdot 12 = 74.0$ dB. ✓)

*Step 5 — Bit rate:* Nyquist rate = $2 f_m = 10$ kHz. Sampling at $1.2 \times 10$ kHz = 12 kHz.

$$R_b = n f_s = 12 \times 12000 = 144 \text{ kbps}$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE**

- **Sampling Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ is the absolute condition for faithful reconstruction; Nyquist rate = $2 f_m$, Nyquist frequency = $f_s/2$.
- **Anti-aliasing filter** is *mandatory* before the sampler in any real system.
- **Three sampling types:** Ideal (impulse), Natural (curved top), Flat-top (sample-and-hold). PCM uses flat-top sampling.
- **Quantization error:** bounded by $\pm \Delta/2$, treated as a uniformly distributed random variable.
- **Noise power:** $\sigma_q^2 = \Delta^2/12$ — this is the single most-tested formula in this module.
- **Step size:** $\Delta = 2 V_{max}/L$ for **mid-rise** type (no level at zero); $\Delta = 2 V_{max}/(L-1)$ for **mid-tread** type (level at zero).
- **SQNR for sine:** $\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 1.76 + 6.02n$. Every additional bit adds ~6 dB.
- **Companding:** µ-law ($\mu = 255$, US/Japan) and A-law ($A = 87.6$, Europe). Companding is what allows telephone voice to be quantized with only **8 bits** while maintaining acceptable quality.
- **Bit rate:** $R_b = n f_s$ bits/second — required to determine channel bandwidth and storage.
- **PCM system chain:** Anti-alias LPF → Sampler → Quantizer → Encoder → Channel encoder → Channel → Reverse chain → Reconstruction LPF.
- **Aliasing** is *irreversible*; the only fix is prevention via proper $f_s$ and pre-filtering.
- **Reconstruction:** low-pass filter of cutoff $f_m$ recovers the original message *exactly* (in the absence of quantization and channel noise) when the sampling theorem is satisfied.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SQNR for Uniform Quantization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT502 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** SQNR for Uniform Quantization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — SQNR for Uniform Quantization

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Signal-to-Quantization Noise Ratio (SQNR)** is the engineering metric that quantifies the quality of a digitized (PCM) signal by comparing the power of the original input signal to the power of the **quantization error** introduced by the analog-to-digital converter.

For a **uniform quantizer** — one in which the step size $\Delta$ (also called the resolution or quantum) is constant across the entire input dynamic range — the SQNR is given by the well-established KTU / Proakis formula:

$$
\text{SQNR} = \frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{quantization noise}}} = 1.5 \times 2^{2n}
$$

Expressed in decibels (the form demanded in all KTU numerical problems):

$$
\boxed{\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 1.76 + 6.02\,n \;\;\text{dB}}
$$

where $n$ = number of bits per sample, and the input is assumed to be a **full-amplitude sinusoid** spanning the quantizer's complete dynamic range.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> The 2024 scheme places heavy emphasis on the **derivation of SQNR for uniform quantization**. You are expected to know the assumptions, the integral that yields $\Delta^2/12$, and the final $6.02n + 1.76$ dB result by heart. Any KTU question on PCM quality, dynamic range, or bit-rate trade-offs pivots on this expression.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Metre Stick" View

Imagine you are an engineer asked to measure the heights of 1,000 people using only a **wooden metre stick with no fine markings**. You can only assign whole-centimetre values. A person whose true height is 172.4 cm gets recorded as 172 cm. That 0.4 cm error is your **quantization noise**.

- If you replace the stick with a stick marked every **millimetre**, the error drops to 0.4 mm. The finer your "ruler" (i.e., the more **bits** you have), the smaller the error, and the better the "signal quality" — that's exactly what SQNR measures.
- A **uniform** stick means the spacing is the same at the top of the scale and the bottom — unlike a tailor's tape that has coarser markings near the ends. Uniform quantization is "democratic": it treats small and large signals with the same precision.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Assumed quantizer loading factor (sinusoid)**: $\sigma_s^2 = A^2/2$, giving a peak-to-average power ratio of **2 (3 dB)**.
- **Step size**: $\Delta = 2A_{\max} / L$, where $L = 2^n$ is the number of reconstruction levels.
- **Standard bit-efficiency rule of thumb**: every additional bit improves SQNR by **6.02 dB** (the famous *"6 dB per bit"* rule).

> [!NOTE]
> **$1.76$ dB constant** — this is the contribution of the sinusoidal waveform shape and the $\Delta^2/12$ noise variance. It is not universal; for a uniform-distribution input the constant becomes $\approx 0$ dB. KTU problems almost always use the sinusoidal (1.76 dB) form.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block-Level Logic of the Derivation

The derivation is logically a four-step pipeline. Every KTU answer that asks *"derive the SQNR of a uniform quantizer"* must reproduce these four stages in order.

**Step 1 — Quantization error model.** When an analog sample $x$ is mapped to its nearest level $x_q$, the error is:
$$
e = x_q - x, \quad e \in [-\Delta/2,\;+\Delta/2]
$$

**Step 2 — Statistical assumption.** For a fine quantizer driven by a smooth, high-entropy signal, $e$ is modelled as a **zero-mean uniformly distributed random variable**. Hence its probability density function is:
$$
p(e) = \frac{1}{\Delta}, \quad -\Delta/2 \le e \le \Delta/2
$$

**Step 3 — Noise power.** Compute the mean square error (which equals the variance because the mean is zero):
$$
\sigma_q^2 \;=\; E[e^2] \;=\; \int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} e^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta}\, de \;=\; \frac{\Delta^2}{12}
$$

**Step 4 — Signal power & ratio.** For a full-range sinusoid of amplitude $A$, $P_s = A^2/2$. Combine with the step size relation $\Delta = 2A/2^n$ to get the closed-form SQNR.

## 2.2 Why Uniform Quantization Is the KTU Default

- **Simplicity of hardware**: a uniform quantizer is just a bank of comparators with equally spaced thresholds — exactly what a flash ADC implements.
- **Mathematically tractable**: the noise PDF is constant, which is what makes the $\Delta^2/12$ integral possible.
- **Worst-case reference**: any non-uniform quantizer (e.g., $\mu$-law, A-law) is benchmarked against the uniform case.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility.** SQNR is the single number that tells a design engineer how many bits are needed for a given application: telephony needs $\sim 8$ bits (≈ 50 dB), CD audio uses $16$ bits (≈ 98 dB), high-fidelity studio recording uses $20$–$24$ bits. The $6.02n + 1.76$ dB formula is the design cornerstone of every ADC specification sheet.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Number of quantization levels | $L = 2^n$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Step size (full range $2A$) | $\Delta = 2A / L = 2A / 2^n$ | Volts (or signal units) |
| 3 | Quantization error range | $e \in [-\Delta/2,\;+\Delta/2]$ | bounded, deterministic amplitude |
| 4 | Error PDF | $p(e) = 1/\Delta$ for $\vert e \vert \le \Delta/2$ | uniform distribution |
| 5 | Quantization noise power (variance) | $\sigma_q^2 = \Delta^2 / 12$ | W (signal-power units) |
| 6 | Sinusoidal signal power | $P_s = A^2 / 2$ | full-amplitude sinusoid |
| 7 | SQNR (linear) | $\text{SQNR} = 1.5 \times 2^{2n}$ | ratio, dimensionless |
| 8 | SQNR (dB) — sinusoid | $\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 1.76 + 6.02\,n$ | **KTU gold formula** |
| 9 | SQNR (dB) — uniform-distribution input | $\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02\,n$ | $1.76$ dB is replaced by $0$ |
| 10 | Output bit-rate (PCM) | $R_b = n \cdot f_s$ | bits/sec |
| 11 | Channel bandwidth (Nyquist) | $BW = R_b / 2$ | Hz |
| 12 | SNR improvement per extra bit | $\Delta\text{SNR} = 6.02$ dB | linear in $n$ |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of $\sigma_q^2 = \Delta^2/12$

Start from the definition of the mean square of the uniform quantization error $e$.

$$
\sigma_q^2 \;=\; E\!\left[e^2\right] \;=\; \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e^{2}\, p(e)\, de
$$

Step a — substitute the uniform PDF $p(e) = 1/\Delta$ (a constant over the integration interval):

$$
\sigma_q^2 \;=\; \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e^{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta}\, de
$$

Step b — pull the constant $1/\Delta$ outside the integral:

$$
\sigma_q^2 \;=\; \frac{1}{\Delta}\int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e^{2}\, de
$$

Step c — integrate $e^2$ analytically. The antiderivative is $e^3/3$:

$$
\sigma_q^2 \;=\; \frac{1}{\Delta}\left[\frac{e^{3}}{3}\right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2}
$$

Step d — evaluate at the two limits. The cubed limits are:

$$
\left(\frac{\Delta}{2}\right)^{3} = \frac{\Delta^{3}}{8}, \qquad \left(-\frac{\Delta}{2}\right)^{3} = -\frac{\Delta^{3}}{8}
$$

Step e — subtract the lower limit from the upper limit:

$$
\sigma_q^2 \;=\; \frac{1}{\Delta}\cdot\frac{1}{3}\left[\frac{\Delta^{3}}{8} - \left(-\frac{\Delta^{3}}{8}\right)\right] = \frac{1}{\Delta}\cdot\frac{1}{3}\cdot\frac{\Delta^{3}}{4}
$$

Step f — simplify by cancelling one $\Delta$ from the numerator with the $\Delta$ in the denominator:

$$
\sigma_q^2 \;=\; \frac{\Delta^{2}}{12} \quad \checkmark
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Signal Power $P_s$ for a Sinusoid

A full-range sinusoid is written as:

$$
x(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t)
$$

The average power of a periodic signal is the mean square of its instantaneous amplitude:

$$
P_s = \langle x^2(t) \rangle = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^{T} A^{2}\sin^{2}(2\pi f t)\, dt
$$

Step a — use the trigonometric identity $\sin^2\theta = (1 - \cos 2\theta)/2$:

$$
P_s = \frac{A^{2}}{T}\int_0^{T}\frac{1 - \cos(4\pi f t)}{2}\, dt
$$

Step b — the cosine term averages to zero over an integer number of periods, so:

$$
P_s = \frac{A^{2}}{2} \quad \checkmark
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the SQNR Formula (The KTU Showcase Derivation)

Step 1 — definition of SQNR as the ratio of signal power to quantization-noise power:

$$
\text{SQNR} \;=\; \frac{P_s}{\sigma_q^2} \;=\; \frac{A^{2}/2}{\Delta^{2}/12} \;=\; \frac{12\,A^{2}}{2\,\Delta^{2}} \;=\; \frac{6\,A^{2}}{\Delta^{2}}
$$

Step 2 — express $\Delta$ in terms of the signal amplitude $A$ and the number of bits $n$. The full dynamic range is $2A$ and there are $2^n$ levels:

$$
\Delta \;=\; \frac{2A}{2^{n}} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \Delta^{2} \;=\; \frac{4A^{2}}{2^{2n}}
$$

Step 3 — substitute $\Delta^2$ back into the SQNR expression:

$$
\text{SQNR} \;=\; \frac{6\,A^{2}}{4A^{2}/2^{2n}} \;=\; \frac{6\,A^{2}\cdot 2^{2n}}{4\,A^{2}}
$$

Step 4 — cancel $A^2$ and reduce the numeric fraction $6/4 = 3/2 = 1.5$:

$$
\text{SQNR} \;=\; 1.5 \times 2^{2n} \quad \checkmark
$$

Step 5 — convert to decibels using $10\log_{10}(\cdot)$:

$$
\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(1.5 \times 2^{2n}\right) = 10\log_{10}(1.5) + 10\log_{10}\!\left(2^{2n}\right)
$$

Step 6 — evaluate the two log terms. Using $\log_{10}(1.5) \approx 0.1761$ and $\log_{10}(2) \approx 0.3010$:

$$
\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 1.761 + 2n \times 3.010 = 1.76 + 6.02\,n \;\;\text{dB} \quad \checkmark
$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU Style)

**Example A:** Find the SQNR for $n = 8$ bits, sinusoidal input.

$$
\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 1.76 + 6.02 \times 8 = 1.76 + 48.16 = 49.92 \;\text{dB} \approx 49.9 \;\text{dB}
$$

**Example B:** How many bits are needed to achieve SQNR $\geq 60$ dB for a speech-like signal?

$$
n \ge \frac{60 - 1.76}{6.02} = \frac{58.24}{6.02} \approx 9.67 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; n = 10 \;\text{bits}
$$

**Example C:** An audio system uses 16-bit uniform PCM. Determine (i) SQNR, (ii) the equivalent noise amplitude for a 2 V peak signal.

(i) $\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 1.76 + 6.02 \times 16 = 1.76 + 96.32 = 98.08$ dB.

(ii) Linear SQNR $\approx 10^{9.808} \approx 6.43 \times 10^{9}$. With $P_s = (2)^2/2 = 2$ W, the noise power is $2 / 6.43 \times 10^{9} \approx 3.11 \times 10^{-10}$ W, giving a noise RMS amplitude of $\sqrt{3.11 \times 10^{-10}} \approx 1.76 \times 10^{-5}$ V $\approx 17.6 \,\mu\text{V}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Uniform Quantizer Inside a PCM Transmitter

The Mermaid diagram below maps every functional block of a uniform-quantization PCM system, showing where the quantizer sits and how its step size $\Delta$ controls the SQNR computed in Section 3.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA["Analog Source x(t)"] --> lpfA["Anti-Alias LPF"]
    lpfA --> sampA["Sampler f_s"]
    sampA --> quantA["Uniform Quantizer Step size Delta"]
    quantA["Uniform Quantizer Step size Delta"] --> encA["Binary Encoder n bits per sample"]
    encA --> chA["Channel / Line Coder"]
    quantA -.-> errA["Quantization Error e(n)"]
    errA --> noiseA["Noise Power Delta squared over 12"]
    quantA --> stepA["Step Size Delta equals 2A over 2 to the n"]
    stepA --> ratioA["SQNR equals P_s divided by Noise Power"]
    noiseA --> ratioA
    ratioA --> outA["SQNR in dB equals 1.76 plus 6.02 n"]
```

## 4.2 Decision Thresholds and Reconstruction Levels — A Level-Crossing View

The diagram below shows how a uniform quantizer partitions the input voltage axis into $2^n$ equal-width intervals. Each input sample falls into one interval and is mapped to its midpoint reconstruction level. The maximum possible error in any interval is $\pm \Delta/2$.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    axisA["Input Voltage Axis"]
    axisA --> lvl0["Level 0 : -A to -A plus Delta"]
    axisA --> lvl1["Level 1 : -A plus Delta to -A plus 2 Delta"]
    axisA --> lvl2["Level 2 : ... continues uniformly ..."]
    axisA --> lvlN["Level 2 to the n minus 1 : top of range"]
    lvl0 --> mid0["Midpoint x_q0 : reconstruction level"]
    lvl1 --> mid1["Midpoint x_q1 : reconstruction level"]
    lvl2 --> mid2["Midpoint x_q2 : reconstruction level"]
    lvlN --> midN["Midpoint x_qN-1 : reconstruction level"]
    mid0 --> err0["Max error equals plus or minus Delta over 2"]
    mid1 --> err1["Max error equals plus or minus Delta over 2"]
    mid2 --> err2["Max error equals plus or minus Delta over 2"]
    midN --> errN["Max error equals plus or minus Delta over 2"]
```

## 4.3 Quantization-Error Probability Model

This diagram captures the **statistical abstraction** that turns the deterministic bounded error $e \in [-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$ into a uniform random variable — the step that unlocks the $\Delta^2/12$ integral.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inpA["Input Sample x"] --> subA["Subtractor: e equals x_q minus x"]
    outA["Quantized Output x_q"] --> subA
    subA --> errB["Quantization Error e"]
    errB --> pdfA["Uniform PDF p of e equals 1 over Delta"]
    pdfA --> msA["Mean Square: integrate e squared p of e de from -Delta/2 to Delta/2"]
    msA --> resA["Result: Delta squared divided by 12"]
    resA --> sqnrA["Feeds into SQNR computation"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Signal-to-Quantization Noise Ratio (SQNR) for a uniform quantizer. State the standard SQNR expression in dB for a full-range sinusoidal input.**

**Model Answer:**

> **SQNR** is defined as the ratio of the average power of the input signal to the average power of the quantization error introduced by the quantizer.
>
> $$\text{SQNR} = \frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}} = \frac{\sigma_s^{2}}{\sigma_q^{2}}$$
>
> For a full-range sinusoidal input and an $n$-bit uniform quantizer, the standard expression is:
>
> $$\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 1.76 + 6.02\,n \;\text{dB}$$
>
> This result assumes the quantization error is uniformly distributed in $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$, giving a noise variance $\sigma_q^{2} = \Delta^{2}/12$.

**[3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the formula in linear form, 1 mark for the dB expression]**

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is quantization noise? Why is it modelled as a uniform random variable in the analysis of uniform quantizers?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Quantization noise** $e(n) = x_q(n) - x(n)$ is the difference between the quantized sample and the original analog sample at the quantizer output. For a fine-resolution uniform quantizer, $e$ is bounded between $\pm \Delta/2$ and exhibits a rapidly varying, signal-independent behaviour that resembles a zero-mean random process.
>
> It is modelled as a **uniform random variable** because:
> 1. The error can take any value in $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$ with equal likelihood when the input is rich and the quantizer is fine.
> 2. This makes the PDF a simple rectangle: $p(e) = 1/\Delta$, which yields the closed-form variance $\sigma_q^{2} = \Delta^{2}/12$.
> 3. The uniform assumption decouples signal and noise, allowing clean power-ratio analysis.

**[3 marks: 1 mark for defining quantization noise, 2 marks for the uniform-model justification]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) Derive the expression for the Signal-to-Quantization Noise Ratio (SQNR) of a uniform quantizer for a sinusoidal input signal. Show all steps clearly. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Number of levels and step size.** For an $n$-bit uniform quantizer:

$$L = 2^{n}, \qquad \Delta = \frac{\text{Full Range}}{L} = \frac{2A}{2^{n}}$$

**Step 2 — Quantization error model.** The quantization error is:

$$e = x_q - x, \quad -\frac{\Delta}{2} \le e \le \frac{\Delta}{2}$$

For a fine quantizer, $e$ is a zero-mean uniformly distributed random variable with PDF:

$$p(e) = \frac{1}{\Delta}, \quad \text{for } \vert e \vert \le \frac{\Delta}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Quantization noise power.** Compute the variance:

$$
\sigma_q^{2} = E[e^{2}] = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e^{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta}\, de
$$

$$
\sigma_q^{2} = \frac{1}{\Delta}\left[\frac{e^{3}}{3}\right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} = \frac{1}{\Delta}\cdot\frac{1}{3}\cdot\frac{\Delta^{3}}{4} = \frac{\Delta^{2}}{12}
$$

**Step 4 — Signal power for a sinusoid.** For $x(t) = A\sin(2\pi f t)$:

$$P_s = \langle x^{2}(t) \rangle = \frac{A^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Form the SQNR ratio.**

$$
\text{SQNR} = \frac{P_s}{\sigma_q^{2}} = \frac{A^{2}/2}{\Delta^{2}/12} = \frac{6A^{2}}{\Delta^{2}}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $\Delta = 2A/2^{n}$.**

$$
\text{SQNR} = \frac{6A^{2}}{4A^{2}/2^{2n}} = \frac{6 \times 2^{2n}}{4} = 1.5 \times 2^{2n}
$$

**Step 7 — Convert to dB.**

$$
\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10\log_{10}(1.5 \times 2^{2n}) = 1.76 + 6.02\,n \;\text{dB} \quad \blacksquare
$$

**(b) An audio signal is sampled at 44.1 kHz and quantized using a 16-bit uniform quantizer. Calculate (i) the SQNR in dB, (ii) the bit rate of the PCM signal, and (iii) the minimum transmission bandwidth assuming Nyquist signalling. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) SQNR in dB:**

$$
\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 1.76 + 6.02 \times 16 = 1.76 + 96.32 = 98.08 \;\text{dB}
$$

**(ii) Bit rate of the PCM signal:**

$$
R_b = n \cdot f_s = 16 \times 44.1 \times 10^{3} = 705.6 \;\text{kbps}
$$

**(iii) Minimum transmission bandwidth (Nyquist criterion):**

$$
BW_{\min} = \frac{R_b}{2} = \frac{705.6 \times 10^{3}}{2} = 352.8 \;\text{kHz}
$$

**[7 marks: 2 marks for (i), 2 marks for (ii), 3 marks for (iii)]**

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a PCM system. Show how quantization noise arises in the quantizer block. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block diagram description (text equivalent for writing into the answer book):**

The PCM transmitter consists of three cascaded blocks:
1. **Anti-aliasing low-pass filter** — limits the input bandwidth to $f_m$ so that the sampling theorem $f_s \ge 2f_m$ is sufficient.
2. **Sampler** — produces discrete-time samples $x(nT_s)$ at rate $f_s$.
3. **Uniform quantizer** — maps each sample to the nearest of $2^n$ reconstruction levels; introduces quantization error $e(n) = x_q(n) - x(n)$.
4. **Binary encoder** — assigns an $n$-bit codeword to each quantized level.

The PCM receiver performs the inverse operations: decoder → reconstruction (low-pass) filter → analog output $\hat{x}(t)$.

**Where quantization noise arises:**

Quantization noise is the **deterministic but signal-dependent error** $e(n)$ created at the quantizer output. For a uniform quantizer with step size $\Delta$:

$$
e(n) = x_q(n) - x(n), \quad \vert e(n) \vert \le \frac{\Delta}{2}
$$

This error is treated as additive noise $e(n)$ added to the input. Its average power is $\sigma_q^{2} = \Delta^{2}/12$. It is the **fundamental limiting factor** of any PCM system; it cannot be removed by filtering because its spectrum overlaps the signal spectrum.

**(b) A sinusoidal signal with peak amplitude 2 V is uniformly quantized using an 8-bit quantizer with full-scale range $\pm 2$ V. Compute (i) the step size, (ii) the quantization noise power, (iii) the signal power, and (iv) the SQNR in dB. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $A = 2$ V, $n = 8$ bits, full-scale range $\pm 2$ V (so total range $= 4$ V).

**(i) Step size:**

$$
\Delta = \frac{2A}{2^{n}} = \frac{4}{256} = 0.015625 \;\text{V} = 15.625 \;\text{mV}
$$

**(ii) Quantization noise power:**

$$
\sigma_q^{2} = \frac{\Delta^{2}}{12} = \frac{(0.015625)^{2}}{12} = \frac{2.4414 \times 10^{-4}}{12} = 2.0345 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{V}^{2}
$$

**(iii) Signal power:**

$$
P_s = \frac{A^{2}}{2} = \frac{2^{2}}{2} = 2 \;\text{W}
$$

**(iv) SQNR in dB:**

$$
\text{SQNR} = \frac{P_s}{\sigma_q^{2}} = \frac{2}{2.0345 \times 10^{-5}} = 98\,304
$$

$$
\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10\log_{10}(98\,304) = 49.92 \;\text{dB}
$$

**Cross-check using the KTU formula:**

$$
\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 1.76 + 6.02 \times 8 = 49.92 \;\text{dB} \quad \checkmark
$$

**[7 marks: 1 mark for (i), 2 marks for (ii), 1 mark for (iii), 3 marks for (iv)]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the 1.76 dB constant.** Many students write $\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02n$, losing **1 mark** per occurrence. The $1.76$ dB comes from the $1.5$ factor in $1.5 \times 2^{2n}$ and is **mandatory** for sinusoidal inputs.
> 2. **Using the wrong signal-power formula.** For uniform-distribution input, $P_s = A^{2}/3$, giving $\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02n + 0$ dB. KTU's default is the **sinusoidal** case; always state the assumption.
> 3. **Confusing $\Delta = 2A/2^n$ with $\Delta = A/2^n$.** The "2" in the numerator (the total dynamic range, not half) is a frequent slip. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 4. **Forgetting units.** When asked for noise power in V², never give it in dB directly — convert only at the SQNR stage.
> 5. **Inverting the ratio.** SQNR is **signal/noise**, not noise/signal. Markers deduct heavily for this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **SQNR definition:** ratio of signal power to quantization-noise power; always positive in dB.
- **Core assumptions:** (i) uniform step size $\Delta$, (ii) error uniformly distributed in $[-\Delta/2, \Delta/2]$, (iii) error independent of the signal, (iv) input is a full-range sinusoid.
- **The KTU gold formula:** $\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 1.76 + 6.02\,n$.
- **6 dB-per-bit rule:** every extra bit adds **6.02 dB** of SQNR — used as a quick design sanity check.
- **Step size:** $\Delta = 2A/2^n = 2A/\,L$, where $L$ is the number of levels.
- **Noise power:** $\sigma_q^{2} = \Delta^{2}/12$ — derived from the uniform-PDF integral.
- **Signal power (sinusoid):** $P_s = A^{2}/2$.
- **Linear SQNR:** $1.5 \times 2^{2n}$. Useful for engineering back-of-envelope calculations.
- **Bit-rate link:** $R_b = n \cdot f_s$ — connects SQNR design to channel bandwidth.
- **Dynamic range:** increasing $n$ by 1 doubles the number of levels and quadruples the linear SQNR.
- **Comparison anchor:** $\mu$-law / A-law companding beats uniform quantization for speech by trading SQNR for **dynamic range** — a separate Module 2 sub-topic.
- **Practical bit counts:** telephony $\approx 8$ bits, CD audio $= 16$ bits, studio audio $\ge 20$ bits.
- **Common KTU traps:** drop the $1.76$ dB, flip signal/noise, mix up step size, ignore units — all lead to zero on that sub-part.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Companding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT502 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Companding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Companding** is a non-linear signal processing technique used in Pulse Code Modulation (PCM) systems to improve the **Signal-to-Quantization Noise Ratio (SQNR)** by compressing the dynamic range of the input signal at the transmitter and expanding it back to its original form at the receiver.

The term **COMPANDING** is a portmanteau derived from the two opposing processes:
- **COMPRESSING** (at the Transmitter/Encoder)
- **EXPANDING** (at the Receiver/Decoder)

Mathematically, companding implements **non-uniform quantization** by passing the input message signal $m(t)$ through a logarithmic compressor before uniform quantization is applied.

## 1.2 The Problem: Why Do We Need Companding?

In standard PCM, **uniform quantization** is used. The major drawback is that the quantization step size $\Delta$ is constant across the entire amplitude range of the input signal.

$$SQNR = 2^{2n} \cdot \frac{S}{N_q}$$

Where $n$ is the number of bits per sample, $S$ is signal power, and $N_q$ is quantization noise power.

For **speech signals**, this is highly inefficient because:
1. **Speech is a non-stationary signal** with a high peak-to-average ratio.
2. **Weak signals (low amplitude, like whispers) are sampled more frequently** in real conversations.
3. With uniform quantization, weak signals suffer a **poor SQNR** because the fixed step size $\Delta$ is too large relative to their amplitude, leading to granular noise.
4. Strong signals (loud speech) have an unnecessarily high SQNR, wasting bits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Companding is the standard solution to the poor dynamic range of uniform quantization in speech coding. It must be applied **before** sampling and quantization in the PCM transmitter.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Library Shelving System:**
> Imagine you are a librarian storing books of vastly different sizes — some encyclopedias, some thin pamphlets. If you use **uniform shelves** (uniform quantization), the pamphlet either gets lost in the back of a deep shelf (low SQNR) or the encyclopedia gets crushed trying to fit.
>
> Now, imagine you use **adjustable shelves**: tall shelves for big books, shallow shelves for small books. You physically **compress** the big books to fit medium shelves, then store them. When a reader takes a book, they have an **expander** (a magnifying glass/uncompressor) that restores the original size.
>
> In this analogy:
> - **Uniform shelves** = Uniform Quantization (wastes space, poor resolution for small signals)
> - **Adjustable shelves + Magnifier** = Companding (high resolution for both large and small signals)
> - **Librarian's compression desk** = Compressor at TX
> - **Reader's magnifier** = Expander at RX

## 1.4 The Companding Process Block-Level View

The overall system consists of two complementary operations:

| Stage | Operation | Function | Implementation |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| **Transmitter** | Compressor | Reduces dynamic range logarithmically | Non-linear amplifier (diode network) |
| **Channel** | Uniform Quantizer & Encoder | Standard PCM coding | ADC with fixed step size $\Delta$ |
| **Receiver** | Expander | Restores original dynamic range | Non-linear amplifier (inverse function) |

> [!NOTE]
> The end-to-end compander must produce a **net linear transfer characteristic** so that the output signal at the receiver is a faithful reproduction (amplified) version of the original input, devoid of any non-linear distortion.
</content>
</invoke>

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The companding operation is logically divided into **three sequential stages** at the transmitter and **two inverse stages** at the receiver.

### Stage 1: Normalization of Input Signal
- The input message signal $m(t)$ is first normalized to its peak amplitude $V_{peak}$.
- The normalized signal is denoted as $x = \dfrac{m(t)}{V_{peak}}$, where $x \in [-1, +1]$.

### Stage 2: Logarithmic Compression
- The normalized signal $x$ is passed through a non-linear logarithmic compressor.
- The compressor output $y$ is given by the **compression characteristic**: $y = C(x)$.
- The compressor's slope $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ is **large for small $\vert x \vert$** and **small for large $\vert x \vert$**.
- This means small signals get **amplified more** (better resolution) and large signals get **amplified less** (preventing overload).

### Stage 3: Uniform Quantization and Encoding
- The compressed signal $y$ is fed into a **uniform PCM encoder** (linear ADC).
- Because $y$ has a compressed dynamic range, the uniform step size $\Delta$ is now appropriate for the entire signal.
- The binary codeword is transmitted over the channel.

### Stage 4: Decoding and Expansion (Receiver)
- The received codeword is converted back to the quantized compressed value $\hat{y}$.
- The expander applies the inverse characteristic: $\hat{x} = C^{-1}(\hat{y})$.
- The output $\hat{m}(t) = V_{peak} \cdot \hat{x}$ is recovered.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Point:** Companding is applied to the **message signal** (typically speech), not to the noise. The compressor does not eliminate quantization noise, but rather **equalizes the SQNR** across the entire signal amplitude range.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units / Notation | Remarks |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Normalized Input | $x = \dfrac{m(t)}{V_{peak}}$ | Dimensionless | $x \in [-1, +1]$ |
| 2 | Compressor Output | $y = C(x)$ | Dimensionless | Logarithmic curve |
| 3 | Uniform Quantization Step | $\Delta = \dfrac{2V_{peak}}{L} = \dfrac{2V_{peak}}{2^n}$ | Volts | $L$ = no. of levels, $n$ = bits |
| 4 | Quantization Noise Power | $N_q = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12}$ | Watts | For uniform quantizer |
| 5 | Output SQNR (no companding) | $SQNR = 2^{2n} \cdot \dfrac{\overline{x^2(t)}}{\overline{x^2_{max}}}$ | dB | Depends on signal power |
| 6 | μ-Law Compression | $y = \dfrac{\ln(1 + \mu \vert x \vert)}{\ln(1+\mu)} \cdot \text{sgn}(x)$ | — | US/Japan Standard |
| 7 | A-Law Compression | $y = \dfrac{A \vert x \vert}{1 + \ln A} \cdot \text{sgn}(x), \quad \vert x \vert \le \dfrac{1}{A}$ | — | European/Intl. Standard |
| 8 | A-Law (for large $\vert x \vert$) | $y = \dfrac{1 + \ln(A \vert x \vert)}{1 + \ln A} \cdot \text{sgn}(x), \quad \dfrac{1}{A} < \vert x \vert \le 1$ | — | Logarithmic region |
| 9 | Standard μ value | $\mu = 255$ | Dimensionless | North America, Japan |
| 10 | Standard A value | $A = 87.6$ | Dimensionless | Europe, India |
| 11 | Companding Gain (approx.) | $G_{comp} \approx 24 \text{ dB}$ to $30 \text{ dB}$ | dB | For typical speech signals |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Note:** In KTU exams, do not confuse the **μ-law** (logarithmic for entire range) with the **A-law** (linear for small signals, logarithmic for large signals). The parameter $A = 87.6$ is a fixed international standard value.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of Companding

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Applications:**
> 1. **Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN):** All landline voice calls in India and Europe use **A-law (A = 87.6)** companding.
> 2. **North American Digital Telephony:** Uses **μ-law (μ = 255)** companding (T1 carrier systems).
> 3. **Mobile Communications:** GSM employs **13-bit A-law** or **16-bit linear** PCM internally; modern codecs like AMR use sophisticated quantization.
> 4. **Audio CD Mastering:** Companding principles appear in perceptual coders like MP3 and AAC.
> 5. **Digital Voice Recorders:** Use companding to store long-duration voice memos efficiently.

## 2.4 The Two International Companding Standards

### μ-Law Companding (North America & Japan)
- **Standard Value:** $\mu = 255$
- **Continuous logarithmic curve** across the entire range $\vert x \vert \in [0, 1]$.
- **Disadvantage:** Has a discontinuity in slope at $x = 0$ (derivative is infinite at origin), making it difficult to implement with continuous analog circuits.
- **Approximation:** Implemented in practice using **15 linear segments** (μ-255 law) to approximate the smooth log curve.

### A-Law Companding (Europe, India, KTU Exam Focus)
- **Standard Value:** $A = 87.6$
- **Piecewise function:** Linear segment for $\vert x \vert \le 1/A$, logarithmic for $\vert x \vert > 1/A$.
- **Advantage:** The slope is **continuous and finite at the origin**, making it easier to implement with 13 linear segments in hardware.
- **This is the KTU-preferred standard for India.**

</content>
</invoke>

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Mathematical Analysis

## 3.1 Derivation of the A-Law Compression Characteristic

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Derive the A-law compression equation by imposing two physical constraints on the compressor transfer function $y = C(x)$.

### Step 1: Apply the Boundary Conditions

The compression characteristic $y = C(x)$ must satisfy two boundary conditions for a symmetric input $x \in [-1, +1]$:

**Condition 1 — Zero Input:** $C(0) = 0$
**Condition 2 — Peak Input:** $C(1) = 1$

These conditions ensure the compressor does not introduce a DC offset and the peak signal is not clipped.

### Step 2: Hypothesize a Logarithmic Form

For the region $\vert x \vert > 1/A$, we assume the compression follows a natural logarithm:

$$y = C(x) = \frac{1 + \ln(A \vert x \vert)}{1 + \ln(A)} \cdot \text{sgn}(x)$$

### Step 3: Verify the Peak Condition

Substitute $x = 1$ into the hypothesized form:

$$y(1) = \frac{1 + \ln(A \cdot 1)}{1 + \ln(A)} = \frac{1 + \ln A}{1 + \ln A} = 1$$

This satisfies Condition 2. ✓

### Step 4: Impose Slope Continuity at the Breakpoint

For the linear region ($0 \le \vert x \vert \le 1/A$), we propose:

$$y = \frac{A \vert x \vert}{1 + \ln A} \cdot \text{sgn}(x)$$

At the breakpoint $\vert x \vert = 1/A$, the linear and logarithmic expressions must give the **same value AND the same slope** (slope continuity).

**Continuity of Value at $\vert x \vert = 1/A$:**

$$\text{Linear}: \quad y_{lin} = \frac{A \cdot (1/A)}{1 + \ln A} = \frac{1}{1 + \ln A}$$

$$\text{Log}: \quad y_{log} = \frac{1 + \ln(A \cdot 1/A)}{1 + \ln A} = \frac{1 + \ln(1)}{1 + \ln A} = \frac{1}{1 + \ln A}$$

The values are equal. ✓

**Slope of the Linear Region:**

$$\frac{dy_{lin}}{dx} = \frac{A}{1 + \ln A}$$

**Slope of the Log Region at $\vert x \vert = 1/A$:**

$$\frac{dy_{log}}{dx} = \frac{d}{dx} \left[ \frac{1 + \ln(A \vert x \vert)}{1 + \ln A} \right] = \frac{1}{(1 + \ln A) \vert x \vert}$$

At $\vert x \vert = 1/A$:

$$\frac{dy_{log}}{dx}\bigg|_{\vert x \vert = 1/A} = \frac{1}{(1 + \ln A) \cdot (1/A)} = \frac{A}{1 + \ln A}$$

The slopes are equal. ✓ Hence, the A-law characteristic is **smooth and continuous** at the breakpoint.

### Step 5: Final A-Law Characteristic

$$\boxed{
y = \begin{cases}
\dfrac{A \vert x \vert}{1 + \ln A} \cdot \text{sgn}(x), & 0 \le \vert x \vert \le \dfrac{1}{A} \\[10pt]
\dfrac{1 + \ln(A \vert x \vert)}{1 + \ln A} \cdot \text{sgn}(x), & \dfrac{1}{A} < \vert x \vert \le 1
\end{cases}
}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the μ-Law Compression Characteristic

### Step 1: Apply Boundary Conditions
Same as A-law: $C(0) = 0$ and $C(1) = 1$.

### Step 2: Hypothesize the Logarithmic Form

The μ-law applies the log function across the **entire** range $0 \le \vert x \vert \le 1$:

$$y = C(x) = \frac{\ln(1 + \mu \vert x \vert)}{\ln(1 + \mu)} \cdot \text{sgn}(x)$$

### Step 3: Verify Peak Condition

At $x = 1$:

$$y(1) = \frac{\ln(1 + \mu)}{\ln(1 + \mu)} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

### Step 4: Slope at the Origin

The slope of μ-law at $x = 0$ is:

$$\frac{dy}{dx}\bigg|_{x=0} = \frac{\mu}{\ln(1+\mu)}$$

For $\mu = 255$:

$$\frac{dy}{dx}\bigg|_{x=0} = \frac{255}{\ln(256)} = \frac{255}{5.545} \approx 45.98$$

This **very high slope at the origin** means small signals are amplified by a factor of ~46, dramatically improving SQNR for weak signals.

### Step 5: Final μ-Law Characteristic

$$\boxed{y = \frac{\ln(1 + \mu \vert x \vert)}{\ln(1 + \mu)} \cdot \text{sgn}(x)}$$

## 3.3 SQNR Improvement Due to Companding

The **signal-to-quantization-noise ratio with companding** can be derived by treating the compressed signal as having a "compressed" peak value.

**For Uniform Quantization (no companding):**

$$SQNR_{uniform} = 2^{2n} \cdot \frac{\overline{x^2(t)}}{\overline{x^2_{max}}}$$

In dB:

$$SQNR_{uniform} (\text{dB}) = 10 \log_{10}(2^{2n}) + 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\overline{x^2}}{\overline{x^2_{max}}}\right)$$

$$SQNR_{uniform} (\text{dB}) = 6.02n + 4.77 - 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{x_{rms}}{x_{max}}\right)$$

**With μ-law Companding:**

For a sinusoidal input, the output SQNR becomes:

$$SQNR_{\mu} \approx 6.02n + 4.77 - 20 \log_{10}\left[\ln(1+\mu)\right] + 20 \log_{10}\left[\mu \cdot \frac{x_{max}}{x_{rms}}\right]$$

**With A-law Companding:**

$$SQNR_{A} \approx 6.02n + 4.77 - 20 \log_{10}\left[1 + \ln A\right] + 20 \log_{10}\left[A + \ln(A \cdot x_{max}/x_{rms})\right]$$

**Net Companding Advantage (for μ-law, μ = 255, sinusoid):**

$$\Delta SQNR = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\mu}{\ln(1+\mu)}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(45.98) \approx 33.25 \text{ dB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Conclusion:** Companding provides a **dynamic SNR improvement** — the weaker the signal (smaller $x_{rms}/x_{max}$), the **larger the improvement in SQNR**. For typical speech signals with $x_{rms}/x_{max} \approx 0.2$, the improvement is approximately **24–30 dB**.

## 3.4 Worked Example — A-Law Compression Calculation

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** An input signal has a normalized amplitude $x = 0.05$. Compute the A-law compressed output $y$ using $A = 87.6$.

**Step 1 — Determine the Region:**

$$\frac{1}{A} = \frac{1}{87.6} \approx 0.0114$$

Since $\vert x \vert = 0.05 > 0.0114$, the input lies in the **logarithmic region**.

**Step 2 — Apply the A-law Log Formula:**

$$y = \frac{1 + \ln(A \vert x \vert)}{1 + \ln A}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Numerator:**

$$A \cdot \vert x \vert = 87.6 \times 0.05 = 4.38$$

$$\ln(4.38) = 1.477$$

$$1 + \ln(4.38) = 1 + 1.477 = 2.477$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Denominator:**

$$1 + \ln(87.6) = 1 + 4.473 = 5.473$$

**Step 5 — Final Result:**

$$y = \frac{2.477}{5.473} \approx 0.4525$$

**Interpretation:** A relatively small input of 0.05 is mapped to a compressed output of 0.4525 — a **9× amplification**. This is precisely how companding gives high resolution to weak signals.
</content>
</invoke>

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Companding PCM System Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Analog Input m(t)"] --> nodeB["Compressor C(x)"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Sampler"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Uniform Quantizer Q(y)"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Binary Encoder"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Channel"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Binary Decoder"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Reconstruction Lattice"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Expander C-inverse x hat"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Reconstructed Output m hat t"]
    
    classDef txBlock fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef chBlock fill:#FF7F7F,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef rxBlock fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    
    class nodeA,nodeB,nodeC,nodeD,nodeE txBlock
    class nodeF chBlock
    class nodeG,nodeH,nodeI,nodeJ rxBlock
```

## 4.2 Compression Characteristic Curve (A-law vs μ-law)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SG1["Compression Characteristics Comparison"]
        direction TB
        nodeInfo["Input x on horizontal axis 0 to 1"]
        nodeInfo --> nodeMU["MU Law mu equals 255 smooth log curve entire range high slope near origin"]
        nodeInfo --> nodeAL["A Law A equals 87.6 piecewise linear near origin then logarithmic after 1 over A"]
    end
    
    nodeMU -.-> nodeMU2["Discontinuous infinite slope at origin hard to implement analog"]
    nodeAL -.-> nodeAL2["Continuous finite slope easier hardware friendly 13 segment approximation"]
    
    classDef infoBox fill:#E0E0FF,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef muBox fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef aBox fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    
    class nodeInfo infoBox
    class nodeMU,nodeMU2 muBox
    class nodeAL,nodeAL2 aBox
```

## 4.3 Companding Process: Compressor + Expander Pair

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Original Signal m(t)"] --> node2["Normalization: x = m(t) / Vpeak"]
    node2 --> node3["Compressor: y = C(x) Logarithmic"]
    node3 --> node4["Uniform PCM Encoder L levels"]
    node4 --> node5["Channel Binary Transmission"]
    node5 --> node6["Uniform PCM Decoder"]
    node6 --> node7["Expander: x hat = C-inverse(y hat)"]
    node7 --> node8["Reconstruction: m hat t = Vpeak * x hat"]
    
    classDef norm fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#8B8B00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef comp fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef chan fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef exp fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    
    class node1,node2,node8 norm
    class node3,node7 comp
    class node4,node5,node6 chan
    class node7 exp
```

## 4.4 Piecewise A-law Characteristic — Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start["Normalized Input x"] --> checkA{"Is |x| less than or equal to 1/A = 0.0114"}
    
    checkA -- Yes --> linRegion["Linear Region: y = A|x|/(1+ln A)"]
    checkA -- No --> logRegion["Logarithmic Region: y = 1 + ln(A|x|)/(1+ln A)"]
    
    linRegion --> linProp["Slope = A/(1+ln A) = 45.7"]
    logRegion --> logProp["Variable slope, decreases as |x| increases"]
    
    linProp --> merge["Both regions meet smoothly at |x| = 1/A"]
    logProp --> merge
    
    merge --> out["Compressed Output y in 0 to 1"]
    
    classDef decision fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef region fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    
    class checkA decision
    class linRegion,logRegion,linProp,logProp region
    class merge,out result
```

## 4.5 SQNR Comparison: With vs Without Companding

```mermaid
flowchart LR

        u2 --> u3["SQNR uniform proportional to x_rms squared"]

        c1["Input x"] --> c2["Compressor C(x)"]
        c2 --> c3["Uniform ADC"]
        c3 --> c4["Expander C inverse"]
        c4 --> c5["SQNR companded approximately constant 25 to 33 dB gain"]
    end
    
    SG_Uniform --> SG_Comp
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
>
> **Q1.** Define companding. Why is it required in PCM systems? **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Companding is a non-linear signal processing technique that combines **compressing** the dynamic range of the input signal at the transmitter and **expanding** it back to the original range at the receiver. It is implemented by using a non-uniform quantizer (effectively).

It is required in PCM systems because:
1. Speech signals have a **large dynamic range** (~40 dB), and uniform quantization provides poor SQNR for weak signals.
2. Companding **equalizes the SQNR** across the entire amplitude range, providing higher resolution for low-amplitude signals and preventing overload for high-amplitude signals.
3. It results in a net **SQNR improvement of ~24–30 dB** for typical speech, without increasing the bit rate $n$.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]**
>
> **Q2.** Compare μ-law and A-law companding standards. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | μ-Law | A-Law |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Standard Value** | $\mu = 255$ | $A = 87.6$ |
| **Origin Behavior** | Slope is infinite at $x = 0$ (discontinuous) | Slope is finite and continuous at $x = 0$ |
| **Region** | Logarithmic for entire range $0 \le \vert x \vert \le 1$ | Linear for $0 \le \vert x \vert \le 1/A$, logarithmic for $1/A < \vert x \vert \le 1$ |
| **Used In** | North America, Japan (T1) | Europe, India, KTU syllabus |
| **Hardware Implementation** | 15 linear segments | 13 linear segments |
| **Continuity at Origin** | Not smooth | Smooth (continuous slope) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – End Semester Model Question Paper]**

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

> **(a)** Derive the A-law compression characteristic equation. Explain why A-law companding is preferred over μ-law for practical digital telephony systems. **(7 Marks)**

> **(b)** A PCM system uses A-law companding with $A = 87.6$. An input sample has a normalized value of $x = 0.02$. Determine the compressed output value $y$. Also, comment on the SQNR improvement obtained by using companding for this signal. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Define the compressor as a non-linear mapping:**

The compressor is a non-linear device characterized by $y = C(x)$, where $x$ is the normalized input and $y$ is the compressed output, both bounded in $[-1, +1]$.

**Step 2 — Boundary Conditions:**

Two conditions must be satisfied:
- $C(0) = 0$ (zero input gives zero output)
- $C(1) = 1$ (peak input gives peak output)

**Step 3 — Hypothesize the Functional Form:**

For the linear region, we assume a straight line through the origin:
$$y = \frac{A \vert x \vert}{1 + \ln A} \cdot \text{sgn}(x), \quad 0 \le \vert x \vert \le \frac{1}{A}$$

For the logarithmic region, we use:
$$y = \frac{1 + \ln(A \vert x \vert)}{1 + \ln A} \cdot \text{sgn}(x), \quad \frac{1}{A} < \vert x \vert \le 1$$

**Step 4 — Verify Peak Condition at $x = 1$:**

Substituting $x = 1$ into the log expression:
$$y(1) = \frac{1 + \ln A}{1 + \ln A} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Verify Slope Continuity at $\vert x \vert = 1/A$:**

Slope of linear region:
$$\frac{dy_{lin}}{dx} = \frac{A}{1 + \ln A}$$

Slope of log region at the breakpoint:
$$\frac{dy_{log}}{dx}\bigg|_{\vert x \vert = 1/A} = \frac{1}{(1 + \ln A) \cdot (1/A)} = \frac{A}{1 + \ln A}$$

Both slopes are equal — the function is **continuous and smooth** at the breakpoint.

**Why A-law is preferred over μ-law for telephony:**

1. **Smooth origin behavior:** A-law has a finite, continuous slope at $x = 0$, making it easier to implement with analog circuits (diode networks) and 13-segment piecewise linear approximations. μ-law has an infinite slope at the origin (discontinuous derivative), requiring complex anti-log circuits.
2. **Better low-level performance:** The A-law's piecewise linear section provides more stable behavior for very small inputs.
3. **International standard:** A-law is used in the **E-carrier digital hierarchy (E1, E2...)** which is the European/Indian standard — making it directly relevant to KTU's Indian engineering context.
4. **Less sensitive to DC offset:** A-law is more tolerant of small DC drifts in the input signal.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify the Region:**

The breakpoint is at:
$$\frac{1}{A} = \frac{1}{87.6} \approx 0.0114$$

Since $\vert x \vert = 0.02 > 0.0114$, the input lies in the **logarithmic region**.

**Step 2 — Apply the A-law Log Equation:**

$$y = \frac{1 + \ln(A \vert x \vert)}{1 + \ln A}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Term $A \vert x \vert$:**

$$A \vert x \vert = 87.6 \times 0.02 = 1.752$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Numerator:**

$$\ln(1.752) = 0.5602$$

$$1 + \ln(1.752) = 1 + 0.5602 = 1.5602$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Denominator:**

$$\ln(87.6) = 4.4728$$

$$1 + \ln(87.6) = 1 + 4.4728 = 5.4728$$

**Step 6 — Final Compressed Value:**

$$y = \frac{1.5602}{5.4728} = 0.2851$$

**Step 7 — Compression Ratio Analysis:**

A normalized input of $0.02$ is compressed to $0.2851$ — an amplification factor of $14.25$. This means the corresponding quantization step $\Delta$ now resolves this small signal into $\approx 0.2851 \cdot 2^n$ effective levels, dramatically improving SQNR for weak signals.

**Step 8 — SQNR Comment:**

For uniform quantization, this signal would have a poor SQNR of:
$$SQNR_{uniform} \approx 6.02n - 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{x_{max}}{x_{rms}}\right) \approx 6.02n - 34 \text{ dB}$$

With A-law companding, the SQNR becomes approximately:
$$SQNR_{A} \approx 6.02n - 20\log_{10}(1 + \ln A) + 20\log_{10}(A) \approx 6.02n - 14.77 + 38.84 \approx 6.02n + 24 \text{ dB}$$

This represents an **improvement of approximately 34 dB** for this weak signal.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

> **(a)** Explain the μ-law compression and expansion process in detail. Derive the μ-law characteristic equation. **(7 Marks)**

> **(b)** A 7-bit PCM system uses μ-law companding with $\mu = 255$. Compute the SQNR for an input sinusoid of normalized amplitude $x = 0.5$. Compare with the SQNR obtained using uniform quantization. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — μ-law Compression Concept:**

μ-law companding applies a logarithmic compression function across the **entire range** $0 \le \vert x \vert \le 1$. Unlike A-law, there is no linear region.

**Step 2 — Functional Form:**

The compression equation is:
$$y = \frac{\ln(1 + \mu \vert x \vert)}{\ln(1 + \mu)} \cdot \text{sgn}(x)$$

**Step 3 — Boundary Condition Verification:**

At $x = 1$:
$$y(1) = \frac{\ln(1 + \mu)}{\ln(1 + \mu)} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

At $x = 0$:
$$y(0) = \frac{\ln(1 + 0)}{\ln(1 + \mu)} = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Slope at the Origin:**

$$\frac{dy}{dx}\bigg|_{x=0} = \frac{1}{\ln(1+\mu)} \cdot \frac{\mu}{1 + \mu \cdot 0} = \frac{\mu}{\ln(1+\mu)}$$

For $\mu = 255$:
$$\frac{dy}{dx}\bigg|_{x=0} = \frac{255}{\ln(256)} = \frac{255}{5.545} = 45.98$$

This **large slope at the origin** amplifies weak signals by a factor of ~46, providing excellent SQNR for low-amplitude inputs.

**Step 5 — Expansion Process (Inverse Function):**

The expander applies the inverse function:
$$x = C^{-1}(y) = \frac{(1+\mu)^{\vert y \vert} - 1}{\mu} \cdot \text{sgn}(y)$$

This is implemented using an **anti-log amplifier** at the receiver.

**Step 6 — Why 15-Segment Approximation:**

The smooth μ-law curve is approximated by **15 linear segments** in practice:
- 8 positive segments and 7 negative segments (or 8 positive + 8 negative for symmetry).
- Each segment has a different slope — segments near the origin are steep (high gain for small signals) and segments near the peak are shallow (low gain for large signals).

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Uniform PCM SQNR Formula:**

For a uniform PCM system with $n = 7$ bits and a sinusoidal input with $x_{rms}/x_{max} = 1/\sqrt{2}$:

$$SQNR_{uniform} = 6.02n + 1.76 = 6.02(7) + 1.76 = 42.14 + 1.76 = 43.9 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2 — Effect of Signal Amplitude:**

For an input with normalized peak $x = 0.5$, the actual $x_{rms}/x_{max}$ for a sinusoid of amplitude 0.5 is $0.5/\sqrt{2} = 0.3536$.

Without companding, the SQNR is:
$$SQNR_{uniform}(0.5) = 6.02(7) + 1.76 - 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{0.5}\right)$$
$$= 43.9 - 6.02 = 37.88 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 — With μ-law Companding:**

The compressed output for $x = 0.5$ with $\mu = 255$ is:
$$y = \frac{\ln(1 + 255 \times 0.5)}{\ln(256)} = \frac{\ln(128.5)}{\ln(256)} = \frac{4.857}{5.545} = 0.8759$$

Since the compressed signal has a much higher "effective" amplitude, the quantization noise relative to the signal is reduced.

The μ-law companding gain is:
$$G_{\mu} = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{\mu}{\ln(1+\mu)}\right) = 20\log_{10}(45.98) = 33.25 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — Companded SQNR:**

$$SQNR_{\mu}(x=0.5) \approx 6.02n + 1.76 - 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{\ln(1+\mu)}{x_{max}/x_{rms}}\right)$$

For a sinusoid:
$$SQNR_{\mu} \approx 6.02(7) + 1.76 + 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{255 \times 0.5}{\sqrt{2} \cdot \ln(256)}\right)$$

$$= 43.9 + 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{127.5}{1.414 \times 5.545}\right) = 43.9 + 20\log_{10}(16.26)$$

$$= 43.9 + 24.22 = 68.12 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison and Conclusion:**

| Quantization Type | SQNR (dB) | SQNR Improvement |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Uniform (no companding, $x = 0.5$) | $37.88$ dB | — |
| μ-law Companded ($x = 0.5$) | $68.12$ dB | $+30.24$ dB |
| Uniform (sinusoid full scale) | $43.9$ dB | — |

**Conclusion:** μ-law companding provides a **substantial improvement of ~30 dB** in SQNR for moderate-amplitude signals, and even more for low-amplitude signals. This is achieved **without increasing the bit rate** $n$ — a critical advantage in bandwidth-limited communication systems.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\mu$ and $A$ values:** Always write the standard values explicitly — $\mu = 255$ and $A = 87.6$. Examiners deduct marks for vague statements like "$\mu$-law is logarithmic."
>
> 2. **Forgetting the $\text{sgn}(x)$ function:** The compression equation is defined for the **normalized** signal $x \in [-1, +1]$. Skipping the sign function in A-law or μ-law expressions will lose at least 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Not verifying both boundary conditions:** In derivation questions (7 marks), students must show **both** $C(0) = 0$ and $C(1) = 1$ to get full marks.
>
> 4. **Confusing compressor with quantizer:** The compressor is a **non-linear amplifier** placed **before** the uniform quantizer. It does not quantize — quantization happens after compression.
>
> 5. **Wrong region for A-law:** When computing compressed values, **always** check whether $\vert x \vert$ is in the linear region ($\le 1/A$) or logarithmic region ($> 1/A$). Applying the wrong formula costs the entire 7 marks of the numerical question.
>
> 6. **Missing units in formula sheets:** The "KTU Formula Cheat Sheet" type questions require units (dB, volts, watts) wherever applicable. Always include them.
>
> 7. **Sketching the curves:** In 7-mark descriptive questions, a clear sketch of the compression characteristic with labeled axes ($x$ vs $y$) and the breakpoint at $1/A$ is **mandatory**. Examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for the figure.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- [x] **Companding = Compression + Expanding**, applied to overcome the poor dynamic range of uniform quantization.
- [x] **Compressor** is at the transmitter; **Expander** is at the receiver.
- [x] **μ-law (μ = 255):** Continuous log curve, used in **North America & Japan**, infinite slope at origin, 15-segment hardware approximation.
- [x] **A-law (A = 87.6):** Piecewise (linear for small, log for large signals), used in **Europe & India**, finite slope at origin, 13-segment hardware approximation.
- [x] **A-law Linear Region:** $\vert x \vert \le 1/A = 1/87.6 \approx 0.0114$, formula $y = A \vert x \vert / (1 + \ln A)$.
- [x] **A-law Log Region:** $1/A < \vert x \vert \le 1$, formula $y = (1 + \ln(A \vert x \vert)) / (1 + \ln A)$.
- [x] **μ-law Formula:** $y = \ln(1 + \mu \vert x \vert) / \ln(1 + \mu) \cdot \text{sgn}(x)$.
- [x] **Boundary Conditions:** $C(0) = 0$ and $C(1) = 1$ for both laws.
- [x] **Slope Continuity:** A-law ensures smooth transition at $\vert x \vert = 1/A$; μ-law has a discontinuity in slope at $x = 0$.
- [x] **SQNR Improvement:** Approximately **24–30 dB** for typical speech signals; more for weak signals.
- [x] **Net Effect:** Compander should ideally be **transparent** — end-to-end transfer is linear, just with reduced quantization noise.
- [x] **Application:** Used in **PSTN, GSM codecs, digital voice recorders, audio compression**.
- [x] **Key Trade-off:** No increase in bit rate $n$, but slight increase in circuit complexity (analog compressor/expander or digital lookup tables).
- [x] **Standard SQNR formula:** $SQNR = 6.02n + 1.76$ dB (sinusoid, full-scale, uniform quantizer, no companding).
- [x] **In KTU exams, always** specify whether the question uses A-law or μ-law; never assume.

> **Final KTU Tip:** Practice drawing both compression characteristics by hand. Examiners in KTU frequently award 1–2 marks in 7-mark questions specifically for a well-labeled figure showing the breakpoint and the curves' shapes.
</content>
</invoke>

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pulse Code Modulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT502 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
