> - **Topic:** Pulse Code Modulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pulse Code Modulation (PCM)** is a digital modulation technique in which an analog message signal is represented, transmitted, and reconstructed as a sequence of coded digital pulses (binary words). The process is implemented in three mathematically deterministic stages: **(1) Sampling** (discretization in time), **(2) Quantization** (discretization in amplitude), and **(3) Encoding** (conversion of quantized levels into binary codewords). It is the foundational block of all digital audio (G.711 telephony), digital audio broadcasting (CD-quality audio at 44.1 kHz), and is the parent of every modern waveform codec.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, PCM is the gateway to studying all other waveform coding techniques (DPCM, Delta Modulation, ADPCM), and constitutes the heaviest-weighted topic of Module 2. Mastery of its three sub-processes is mandatory for the End Semester Evaluation (ESE).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Staircase" Analogy)

Imagine you are **describing a smooth ocean wave to a blind person standing 10 kilometers away** using only a flashlight that can be ON or OFF.

- **Sampling** → You take a snapshot of the wave's height every few seconds. You are no longer tracking the *continuous* wave; you only know its height at *discrete* instants.
- **Quantization** → The blind person can only understand heights rounded to the nearest **meter** (or **0.5 m**, depending on your ruler). A wave of 1.73 m is reported as 2 m. The fractional error is the **quantization noise**.
- **Encoding** → You convert that rounded number into a Morse-code-like string of flashlight flashes (e.g., 2 m = `0010` in 4-bit binary).

At the receiver, the blind person rebuilds the staircase approximation of the wave. A finer ruler (more bits) = a smoother wave = a higher-fidelity transmission.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Nyquist Sampling Rate:** $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ — The minimum sampling frequency is **twice the highest frequency component** of the message signal.
> - **Standard CD-Quality Sampling Rate:** $f_s = \mathbf{44.1 \text{ kHz}}$ (since human hearing caps near 20 kHz).
> - **Standard Voice-Band Sampling Rate:** $f_s = \mathbf{8 \text{ kHz}}$ (since telephony voice is band-limited to 4 kHz).
> - **Quantization Levels:** $L = 2^n$, where $n$ = number of bits per sample.
> - **Step Size (Uniform):** $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{max} - V_{min}}{L}$.
> - **Bit Rate:** $R_b = n \cdot f_s$ (bits per second).
> - **PCM Transmission Bandwidth (ideal Nyquist):** $B_{min} = \dfrac{R_b}{2} = \dfrac{n f_s}{2}$.

---

## 1.4 The Three Sub-Processes — A Bird's-Eye View

| Stage | Input Domain | Output Domain | Operation | Key Error Introduced |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sampling** | Continuous-time, Continuous-amplitude | Discrete-time, Continuous-amplitude | Multiplication by an impulse train | Aliasing (if $f_s < 2f_m$) |
| **Quantization** | Discrete-time, Continuous-amplitude | Discrete-time, Discrete-amplitude | Rounding/truncation to nearest level | **Quantization Noise** ($e_q$) |
| **Encoding** | Discrete-time, Discrete-amplitude | Binary bit-stream | Mapping each level $\to$ binary codeword | Bit errors (handled by channel coding) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The block-level mathematical treatment of *Sampling* is covered in Module 1/2 transition, while the *Quantization SNR* and *Companding laws* constitute the highest-frequency questions in KTU ESE for PCM.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stage 1 — Sampling of the Analog Signal

Sampling converts a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ into a discrete-time signal $x_s(t)$ by retaining the instantaneous amplitude at regular intervals $T_s = 1/f_s$.

### 2.1.1 Types of Sampling

1. **Ideal (Natural / Impulse) Sampling** — $x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot \delta_T(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\delta(t - nT_s)$. Mathematically pristine but physically unrealizable.
2. **Natural Sampling (Chopper / PAM-Natural)** — $x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t)$ where $p(t)$ is a rectangular pulse train. The top of each pulse follows the analog curve.
3. **Flat-Top Sampling (Sample-and-Hold / PAM-Flat-Top)** — Each sample is held constant for the entire pulse duration $\tau$. **Most practical PCM systems use this**, because the held value is stable for the A/D converter.

### 2.1.2 The Sampling Theorem (Shannon-Nyquist)

A band-limited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_m$ can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples **if and only if** the sampling frequency satisfies:

$$f_s \geq 2 f_m$$

The minimum allowable rate $f_s = 2 f_m$ is called the **Nyquist Rate**. The Nyquist interval is $T_s = \dfrac{1}{2f_m}$.

### 2.1.3 Aliasing — The Failure Mode

If $f_s < 2 f_m$, the spectral replicas in the frequency domain **overlap**, and the reconstructed signal is a corrupted lower-frequency version called the **alias**. KTU students must remember the alias frequency formula for a sinusoid of frequency $f$ sampled at $f_s$:

$$f_{alias} = \mid f - k f_s \mid \quad \text{where } k \text{ is the integer minimizing the result.}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-Aliasing Filter:** A practical pre-sampling low-pass filter (LPF) is mandatory to band-limit the input below $f_s/2$.

---

## 2.2 Stage 2 — Quantization

### 2.2.1 Uniform (Linear) Quantization

In uniform quantization, the total dynamic range $V_{pp} = V_{max} - V_{min}$ is divided into $L$ equal intervals of width $\Delta$.

$$L = 2^n, \quad \Delta = \dfrac{V_{pp}}{L} = \dfrac{V_{pp}}{2^n}$$

The quantization error $e_q$ is bounded by:

$$-\dfrac{\Delta}{2} \leq e_q \leq +\dfrac{\Delta}{2}$$

### 2.2.2 Signal-to-Quantization Noise Ratio (SQNR)

The **single most-tested formula in KTU PCM problems**. The mean-square quantization noise power for a uniform quantizer driven by a uniformly distributed input is:

$$N_q = \overline{e_q^{\,2}} = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

For a **sinusoidal** input of peak amplitude $A = V_{pp}/2$, the signal power is $P_s = A^2/2$. The SQNR is therefore:

$$\text{SQNR} = \dfrac{P_s}{N_q} = \dfrac{A^2 / 2}{\Delta^2 / 12} = \dfrac{6 A^2}{\Delta^2}$$

Substituting $\Delta = 2A / 2^n$:

$$\text{SQNR} = \dfrac{3}{2}\,L^2 = \dfrac{3}{2}\,2^{2n}$$

Expressed in decibels (the form KTU expects):

$$\boxed{\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02\,n}$$

For a **general** signal with RMS value $V_{rms}$ and peak-to-peak $V_{pp}$:

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 4.77 + 6.02\,n - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{pp}}{V_{rms}}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 6 dB-per-bit Rule:** Each additional bit in the PCM word improves SQNR by exactly **6.02 dB**. This is the most-cited number in KTU answer keys.

### 2.2.3 Non-Uniform Quantization & Companding

For signals like speech, the amplitude distribution is highly **non-uniform** (small values occur more often than large values). A uniform quantizer wastes bits on rare peaks. Companding solves this by **compressing** the dynamic range at the transmitter and **expanding** it at the receiver.

#### 2.2.3.1 $\mu$-Law (Used in North America & Japan)

$$F(x) = \text{sgn}(x) \cdot \dfrac{\ln\!\left(1 + \mu \vert x \vert\right)}{\ln(1 + \mu)}, \quad -1 \leq x \leq 1$$

Standard value: $\mu = \mathbf{255}$.

#### 2.2.3.2 A-Law (Used in Europe & most of the world, including KTU textbook standard)

$$F(x) = \begin{cases} \text{sgn}(x) \cdot \dfrac{A \vert x \vert}{1 + \ln A}, & 0 \leq \vert x \vert \leq \dfrac{1}{A} \\[8pt] \text{sgn}(x) \cdot \dfrac{1 + \ln\!\left(A \vert x \vert\right)}{1 + \ln A}, & \dfrac{1}{A} \leq \vert x \vert \leq 1 \end{cases}$$

Standard value: $A = \mathbf{87.6}$.

Companding improves effective SQNR for low-amplitude signals by **24 to 30 dB** over linear quantization at the same bit rate.

---

## 2.3 Stage 3 — Encoding

The quantized levels are mapped to binary codewords. Common KTU-tested line codes for PCM transmission include:

- **Unipolar NRZ** — Simple, has a DC component.
- **Polar NRZ** — No DC component, requires balanced channel.
- **Bipolar (AMI)** — Three-level, no DC component, simple error detection.
- **Manchester** — Self-clocking, ideal for synchronous Ethernet/PCM.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU often asks: *"What is the minimum transmission bandwidth of a PCM system?"* — The answer is $B_{min} = R_b / 2$ for NRZ line coding, or $B_{min} = R_b$ for unipolar/bipolar (worst case).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet (High-Yield Summary)

> All symbols use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\mid$ to avoid markdown table breakage.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_s = 2 f_m$ | Hz, minimum |
| 2 | Practical Sampling Rate (voice) | $f_s = 8 \text{ kHz}$ | Standard telephony |
| 3 | Number of Quantization Levels | $L = 2^n$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Uniform Step Size | $\Delta = V_{pp} \big/ 2^n$ | Volts |
| 5 | Quantization Noise Power | $N_q = \Delta^2 \big/ 12$ | Watts |
| 6 | SQNR (sinusoidal input) | $\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02\,n$ | dB |
| 7 | SQNR (general RMS signal) | $4.77 + 6.02\,n - 20\log_{10}(V_{pp}/V_{rms})$ | dB |
| 8 | PCM Bit Rate | $R_b = n \cdot f_s$ | bits/second (bps) |
| 9 | Min. Transmission Bandwidth | $B_{min} = R_b \big/ 2$ | Hz (NRZ) |
| 10 | Time Duration of one PCM bit | $T_b = 1 \big/ R_b$ | seconds |
| 11 | $\mu$-Law Compressor | $F(x) = \text{sgn}(x)\ln(1+\mu \mid x \mid)/\ln(1+\mu)$ | $\mu = 255$ |
| 12 | A-Law Compressor | $F(x) = \text{sgn}(x)A \mid x \mid/(1+\ln A)$ for $\mid x \mid \leq 1/A$ | $A = 87.6$ |
| 13 | Alias Frequency | $f_{alias} = \mid f - k f_s \mid$ | Hz, choose $k$ to minimize |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of PCM |
|---|---|
| **Telephony (PSTN)** | G.711 standard uses 8 kHz sampling + 8-bit $\mu$/A-law companded PCM → 64 kbps voice channel (DS0). |
| **Digital Audio (CD)** | 44.1 kHz, 16-bit linear PCM stereo → 1.411 Mbps bit rate. |
| **Studio Mastering** | 96 kHz / 24-bit PCM for high-fidelity recording. |
| **Space Telemetry** | PCM telemetry frames transmitted from satellites (CCSDS standards). |
| **Military Comms** | Secure PCM-based vocoders with encryption layered on the bit-stream. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mean-Square Quantization Noise Power

**Given:** A uniform mid-rise quantizer with step size $\Delta$ and the assumption that the quantization error $e_q$ is uniformly distributed over the interval $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$ (valid when the signal is "busy" and not correlated with the quantizer).

**To Find:** $N_q = \overline{e_q^{\,2}}$.

**Step 1 — Define the probability density function (PDF) of the error.**

Since the error is equally likely anywhere in $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$, the PDF is:

$$
p(e_q) = \dfrac{1}{\Delta}, \quad \text{for } -\dfrac{\Delta}{2} \leq e_q \leq +\dfrac{\Delta}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the definition of mean-square value for a random variable.**

$$
N_q = \overline{e_q^{\,2}} = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} e_q^{\,2}\, p(e_q)\, de_q
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the bounds and the PDF.**

$$
N_q = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^{\,2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\Delta}\, de_q = \dfrac{1}{\Delta}\left[\dfrac{e_q^{\,3}}{3}\right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the antiderivative at the limits.**

$$
N_q = \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \dfrac{1}{3}\left[\left(\dfrac{\Delta}{2}\right)^{3} - \left(-\dfrac{\Delta}{2}\right)^{3}\right]
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the cubed terms.**

$$
N_q = \dfrac{1}{3\Delta} \cdot \left[\dfrac{\Delta^{3}}{8} + \dfrac{\Delta^{3}}{8}\right] = \dfrac{1}{3\Delta} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta^{3}}{4}
$$

**Step 6 — Final cancellation yields the canonical result.**

$$
\boxed{N_q = \dfrac{\Delta^{2}}{12}}
$$

This is the noise power at the quantizer output (assumed to fall within the message bandwidth $f_m$).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — SQNR in dB for a Sinusoidal Input

**Given:** A sinusoidal test signal $x(t) = A \cos(2\pi f_m t)$ is quantized uniformly with $n$ bits. The peak-to-peak range is $V_{pp} = 2A$, so $\Delta = 2A / 2^n$.

**To Find:** $\text{SQNR}_{dB}$.

**Step 1 — Compute the signal power.**

For a sinusoid, average power is:

$$
P_s = \dfrac{A^{2}}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the quantization noise power (from Derivation 3.1).**

$$
N_q = \dfrac{\Delta^{2}}{12} = \dfrac{1}{12}\left(\dfrac{2A}{2^n}\right)^{2} = \dfrac{4A^{2}}{12 \cdot 4^{n}} = \dfrac{A^{2}}{3 \cdot 4^{n}}
$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio.**

$$
\text{SQNR} = \dfrac{P_s}{N_q} = \dfrac{A^{2}/2}{A^{2}/\left(3 \cdot 4^{n}\right)} = \dfrac{3 \cdot 4^{n}}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Express in dB using $10 \log_{10}(\cdot)$.**

$$
\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{3}{2}\right) + 10 \log_{10}\!\left(4^{n}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate each term using $\log_{10}(4) = 2 \log_{10}(2) \approx 0.6020$.**

$$
\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 10 \cdot 0.1761 + n \cdot 10 \cdot 0.6020
$$

**Step 6 — Round to the standard textbook constants.**

$$
\boxed{\text{SQNR}_{dB} \approx 1.76 + 6.02\,n \text{ dB}}
$$

This is the famous **"6 dB per bit" rule**. Doubling $n$ adds $\approx 6$ dB; adding 4 bits adds $\approx 24$ dB of dynamic range.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — PCM Bit Rate and Bandwidth Relationship

**Given:** An analog signal of bandwidth $W = f_m$ is sampled at the Nyquist rate $f_s = 2W$ and quantized with $n$ bits per sample.

**Step 1 — Sample rate.** $f_s = 2W$.

**Step 2 — Bits per second.**

$$
R_b = n \cdot f_s = 2 n W
$$

**Step 3 — Minimum transmission bandwidth (Nyquist criterion for binary signaling with raised-cosine filtering, roll-off $\alpha = 0$).**

$$
B_{min} = \dfrac{R_b}{2} = n W
$$

**Step 4 — For practical NRZ with $\alpha = 1$ (worst case),**

$$
B_{practical} = R_b = 2 n W
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
> A 4 kHz voice signal is sampled at $f_s = 8$ kHz and quantized with $n = 8$ bits.
> Bit rate: $R_b = 8 \times 8000 = 64{,}000$ bps = **64 kbps** (this is exactly the G.711 DS0 channel!).
> Min. bandwidth: $B_{min} = 32$ kHz.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use What

| Scenario | Use This Quantization | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Audio CD mastering | **Uniform, 16-bit** | High amplitude resolution; ample headroom |
| Telephony voice (4 kHz) | **8-bit A-law / $\mu$-law companded** | Speech is non-uniform; companding saves bits |
| Instrumentation / DC measurement | **Uniform, 12–16-bit** | Linear response required; no companding |
| Image transmission | **Uniform, 8-bit (per pixel)** | Predictable SNR, easy post-processing |
| Speech over noisy HF radio | **Companded + FEC** | Robustness over dynamic range |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Numerical Worked Problem (Model Solution Style)

**Problem:** A signal $m(t) = 4 \cos(2000\pi t)$ is sampled at $f_s = 4$ kHz and quantized using 8-bit uniform PCM. Calculate:
1. The Nyquist rate.
2. The number of quantization levels.
3. The quantization step size.
4. The bit rate.
5. The minimum transmission bandwidth.
6. The output SQNR in dB.

**Solution:**

**(1) Nyquist Rate:** $f_m = 1000$ Hz (since $2\pi f_m = 2000\pi$).
$$
f_{Nyquist} = 2 f_m = 2 \times 1000 = 2 \text{ kHz}
$$
Since $f_s = 4 \text{ kHz} > 2 \text{ kHz}$, the sampling theorem is satisfied (oversampled by factor 2).

**(2) Quantization Levels:**
$$
L = 2^{n} = 2^{8} = 256
$$

**(3) Step Size:** With peak amplitude $A = 4$ V, $V_{pp} = 8$ V.
$$
\Delta = \dfrac{V_{pp}}{2^{n}} = \dfrac{8}{256} = 0.03125 \text{ V} = 31.25 \text{ mV}
$$

**(4) Bit Rate:**
$$
R_b = n \cdot f_s = 8 \times 4000 = 32{,}000 \text{ bps} = 32 \text{ kbps}
$$

**(5) Minimum Bandwidth:**
$$
B_{min} = \dfrac{R_b}{2} = 16 \text{ kHz}
$$

**(6) Output SQNR:** Using the sinusoidal formula.
$$
\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02 \times 8 = 1.76 + 48.16 = 49.92 \text{ dB} \approx 49.9 \text{ dB}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PCM Transmitter — Full Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Analog Input x of t] --> B[Anti Aliasing LPF]
    B --> C[Sampler at f sub s]
    C --> D[Sample and Hold]
    D --> E[Quantizer L levels]
    E --> F[Binary Encoder n bits]
    F --> G[Line Coder NRZ or Bipolar]
    G --> H[Channel Output]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The signal flow is strictly left-to-right. The **anti-aliasing LPF** band-limits the input to $f_s/2$. The **sample-and-hold** freezes the value during A/D conversion. The **quantizer** rounds to $L$ discrete levels. The **encoder** maps each level to an $n$-bit word. The **line coder** shapes the spectrum for the channel.

---

## 4.2 PCM Receiver — Full Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Received Binary Pulses] --> B[Line Decoder]
    B --> C[Binary Decoder n to L]
    C --> D[Reconstruction LPF]
    D --> E[Reconstructed Analog y of t]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The **reconstruction LPF** is the analog counterpart to the anti-aliasing LPF. It removes the spectral replicas introduced by the D/A converter (zero-order hold) and recovers the original message.

---

## 4.3 Quantization Process — Staircase Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Continuous Input x] --> B{Compare to Thresholds}
    B --> C[Level 0 range minus Delta to 0]
    B --> D[Level 1 range 0 to plus Delta]
    B --> E[Level 2 range plus Delta to plus 2 Delta]
    B --> F[Level 3 range plus 2 Delta to plus 3 Delta]
    B --> G[Level L minus 1 range top]
    C --> H[Codeword 00]
    D --> I[Codeword 01]
    E --> J[Codeword 10]
    F --> K[Codeword 11]
    G --> L[Codeword 11 extended]
```

> **Note:** The block diagram above represents a **4-level, 2-bit uniform mid-rise quantizer** (simplified for visualization; real systems use 8 or 16 bits).

---

## 4.4 Companding Architecture — $\mu$-Law / A-Law System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Analog Input] --> B[Compressor non linear]
    B --> C[Uniform Quantizer and Encoder]
    C --> D[Channel]
    D --> E[Uniform Decoder]
    E --> F[Expander non linear inverse]
    F --> G[Analog Output]
```

> **Operational insight:** The **compressor** and **expander** are **mirror images** in their I/O characteristics. Together they form the **compander**, which is mathematically equivalent to a **non-uniform quantizer** implemented in two linear stages (compress → uniform quantize → uniform decode → expand).

---

## 4.5 Complete Functional Architecture — PCM Link

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TX[PCM Transmitter]
        T1[Anti Alias LPF] --> T2[Sampler]
        T2 --> T3[Quantizer]
        T3 --> T4[Encoder]
    end
    TX --> CH[Channel with Noise and Bandwidth]
    CH --> RX[PCM Receiver]
    subgraph RX2[PCM Receiver]
        R1[Decoder] --> R2[Reconstruction LPF]
    end
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> OUT[Reconstructed Message]
```

> This is the end-to-end flow tested in KTU ESE "draw and explain the PCM system" questions. Be ready to label every block and state its function.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Pulse Code Modulation. List its three basic operations.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **PCM Definition (1 mark):** Pulse Code Modulation is a digital modulation scheme in which an analog signal is sampled, quantized, and encoded into a binary codeword for transmission or storage.
>
> **Three basic operations (1½ marks each if listed with one-line explanation):**
>
> 1. **Sampling** — Converting the continuous-time signal into discrete-time samples at a rate $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ (Nyquist criterion).
> 2. **Quantization** — Rounding each sample's amplitude to the nearest of $L = 2^n$ discrete levels, introducing quantization noise.
> 3. **Encoding** — Assigning a unique $n$-bit binary codeword to each quantization level for transmission.

---

### **Q2. What is the Nyquist sampling theorem? State its significance.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Statement (2 marks):** A band-limited signal $x(t)$ with highest frequency component $f_m$ Hz can be completely reconstructed from its samples, provided the sampling frequency satisfies:
>
> $$f_s \geq 2 f_m$$
>
> The minimum sampling rate $f_s = 2 f_m$ is called the **Nyquist rate**.
>
> **Significance (1 mark):** It establishes the minimum sampling rate that prevents **aliasing** (irreversible spectral overlap), enabling lossless conversion of analog signals to discrete form. Violation of this theorem makes faithful reconstruction impossible.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question is 14 marks, with two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. KTU allows the student to choose **either Question A or Question B**. Both are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — PCM System Analysis with Companding**

#### **Q.A(a)** A signal $m(t)$ has a bandwidth of 4 kHz. It is sampled at $f_s = 8$ kHz and quantized using an 8-bit uniform PCM system.
**(i)** Find the bit rate and minimum transmission bandwidth. **(3 marks)**
**(ii)** Calculate the output SQNR in dB assuming a full-scale sinusoidal input. **(2 marks)**
**(iii)** If companding (A-law, $A = 87.6$) is used, by approximately how much does the effective SQNR improve for low-level signals? **(2 marks)**

#### **Q.A(b)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a PCM transmitter and receiver. Compare natural and flat-top sampling. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**A(a) (i) Bit rate and bandwidth (3 marks):**

Bit rate:
$$
R_b = n \cdot f_s = 8 \times 8000 = 64{,}000 \text{ bps} = 64 \text{ kbps}
$$

Minimum transmission bandwidth (NRZ line coding):
$$
B_{min} = \dfrac{R_b}{2} = \dfrac{64{,}000}{2} = 32 \text{ kHz}
$$

**A(a) (ii) Output SQNR for sinusoidal input (2 marks):**

$$
\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02\,n = 1.76 + 6.02 \times 8 = 1.76 + 48.16 = 49.92 \text{ dB}
$$

**A(a) (iii) Companding improvement (2 marks):**

A-law / $\mu$-law companding provides an **effective SQNR improvement of approximately 24 to 30 dB** for low-amplitude (low-level) signals compared to a uniform 8-bit quantizer. The exact value depends on signal statistics, but the textbook figure is **≈ 24 dB**.

**A(b) Block diagram with explanation (7 marks):**

Draw the block diagrams from **Section 4.1 (Transmitter)** and **Section 4.2 (Receiver)**. Then describe each block:

| Block | Function (½ to 1 mark each) |
|---|---|
| Anti-aliasing LPF | Restricts signal bandwidth to $f_s/2$ to prevent aliasing. |
| Sampler | Multiplies signal by impulse train, yielding discrete-time samples. |
| Sample-and-Hold | Holds each sample constant for the A/D conversion interval $\tau$. |
| Quantizer | Maps each sample to one of $L = 2^n$ discrete amplitude levels. |
| Encoder | Converts each quantized level to a unique $n$-bit binary codeword. |
| Line Coder | Shapes spectrum (NRZ, Bipolar AMI) for channel compatibility. |
| Channel | Transmission medium (cable, fiber, wireless). |
| Line Decoder | Recovers binary pulses from received waveform. |
| Decoder | Converts $n$-bit words back to quantized amplitudes (D/A). |
| Reconstruction LPF | Smooths the staircase to recover the original analog message. |

**Comparison: Natural vs. Flat-Top Sampling (within the 7 marks):**

| Feature | Natural Sampling | Flat-Top Sampling |
|---|---|---|
| Pulse shape | Top follows the analog curve | Top is constant (held value) |
| Aperture effect | None | Yes — causes amplitude distortion |
| Practicality | Theoretical / ideal | **Most widely used in real PCM** |
| Circuit complexity | Higher | Lower (simple S/H circuit) |
| SNR | Slightly higher at high freqs | Reduced at high freqs due to $(\sin x)/x$ rolloff |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Quantization, SQNR, and Line Coding**

#### **Q.B(a)** Derive the expression for the Signal-to-Quantization Noise Ratio (SQNR) of a uniform PCM system for a sinusoidal input. State the standard 6 dB-per-bit rule. **(7 marks)**

#### **Q.B(b)** A 4 kHz voice signal is transmitted using 16-level PCM.
**(i)** Find the sampling rate (Nyquist criterion). **(2 marks)**
**(ii)** Calculate the bit rate. **(2 marks)**
**(iii)** If the SQNR must exceed 40 dB, what is the minimum number of bits required? **(3 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**B(a) Derivation of SQNR (7 marks):**

Follow the **exhaustive derivation in Section 3.2** of these notes. Key milestones for valuation:

- Start with the assumption of uniform error PDF: $p(e_q) = 1/\Delta$. (1 mark)
- Integrate to get $N_q = \Delta^2/12$. (2 marks)
- Express $P_s = A^2/2$ for sinusoidal input. (1 mark)
- Form SQNR ratio $= 3 \cdot 2^{2n-1}$. (1 mark)
- Convert to dB to obtain $\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02\,n$. (1 mark)
- **6 dB-per-bit rule statement (1 mark):** Each additional bit in the PCM word improves SQNR by approximately **6.02 dB**, because $\Delta$ halves and noise power quarters, while signal power is held constant.

**B(b) Numerical sub-parts (7 marks):**

**(i) Sampling rate (2 marks):**
$$
f_s = 2 f_m = 2 \times 4000 = 8 \text{ kHz}
$$

**(ii) Bit rate (2 marks):**
With $L = 16$ levels, $n = \log_2 16 = 4$ bits.
$$
R_b = n \cdot f_s = 4 \times 8000 = 32 \text{ kbps}
$$

**(iii) Minimum bits for SQNR > 40 dB (3 marks):**
$$
1.76 + 6.02\,n \geq 40
$$
$$
6.02\,n \geq 38.24
$$
$$
n \geq \dfrac{38.24}{6.02} = 6.35
$$
$$
\boxed{n_{min} = 7 \text{ bits}}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in PCM Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "1.76 dB" offset** — Many students write $\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02\,n$ and lose 1 mark. Always include the **1.76 dB** term (it comes from $10 \log_{10}(1.5)$).
> 2. **Confusing $V_{pp}$ and $V_{rms}$** — The general SQNR formula uses $V_{pp}$ in the denominator and $V_{rms}$ in the numerator's $20 \log$ term. Mislabeling costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Misapplying the aliasing formula** — Always choose the integer $k$ that **minimizes** the result of $\mid f - k f_s \mid$.
> 4. **Stating "PCM has no noise"** — Wrong! PCM has **quantization noise** at the source, and the channel can add **bit errors**. Companding helps with the first, channel coding with the second.
> 5. **Omitting the anti-aliasing filter** — In block diagrams, students often show the sampler directly connected to the input. Always include the LPF.
> 6. **Calculating bandwidth with $R_b$ instead of $R_b/2$** — Minimum NRZ bandwidth is $R_b/2$; worst case (unipolar NRZ) is $R_b$. State your assumption.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — PCM (Module 2)**
>
> ✅ PCM = **Sampling + Quantization + Encoding** (3 stages, in this order).
>
> ✅ **Nyquist sampling theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ (memorize; minimum = Nyquist rate).
>
> ✅ **Aliasing** occurs when $f_s < 2 f_m$; prevented by an **anti-aliasing LPF**.
>
> ✅ **Quantization step** (uniform): $\Delta = V_{pp} / 2^n$.
>
> ✅ **Quantization noise power:** $N_q = \Delta^2 / 12$ (uniform quantizer assumption).
>
> ✅ **SQNR for sine wave:** $\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02\,n$ — the **6 dB-per-bit rule**.
>
> ✅ **SQNR for general signal:** $4.77 + 6.02\,n - 20\log_{10}(V_{pp}/V_{rms})$.
>
> ✅ **Bit rate:** $R_b = n \cdot f_s$.
>
> ✅ **Minimum transmission bandwidth:** $B_{min} = R_b / 2$ (for NRZ, raised-cosine roll-off $\alpha = 0$).
>
> ✅ **Companding** — compresses dynamic range before uniform quantization; $\mu = 255$ (USA) and $A = 87.6$ (Europe/Kerala standard).
>
> ✅ **Companding improvement:** ~24 dB SNR gain for low-level signals.
>
> ✅ **Flat-top sampling** is the practical choice (with aperture-effect distortion).
>
> ✅ **PCM applications:** G.711 telephony (64 kbps DS0), CD audio (1.411 Mbps), satellite telemetry, secure voice.
>
> ✅ **Differentiate quantization noise (always present) from channel noise (bit errors) — both degrade SNR.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transmitter and Receiver
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT502 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Transmitter and Receiver

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Transmitter — Formal Definition
An **AM/FM Transmitter** is a complete electronic system that performs three core functions: (i) **generation** of a stable high-frequency carrier wave, (ii) **modulation** of the carrier with the baseband message signal (intelligence) according to the chosen modulation scheme (AM / FM / SSB), and (iii) **amplification & radiation** of the modulated RF signal through a transmitting antenna with adequate power and bandwidth efficiency.

### 1.2 Receiver — Formal Definition
A communication **Receiver** is the complementary system that intercepts the weak, noise-corrupted RF signal from the antenna, **selects** the desired channel, **demodulates** the intelligence from the carrier, and **amplifies** the recovered baseband to a usable level. The modern benchmark is the **Superheterodyne Receiver**, invented by Edwin Armstrong (1918).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Focus**: AM Transmitter (Low & High Level), AM Superheterodyne Receiver, Image Frequency, FM Transmitter (Armstrong method), and FM Receiver (Demodulators: Slope, Foster-Seeley, Ratio, PLL).

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Postal System"
Imagine sending a long handwritten letter (the **message signal**):
- The **envelope** = the **high-frequency carrier** (you cannot send a bare letter efficiently across continents; you need an envelope).
- **Writing the address on the envelope** = **Modulation** (imprinting the message onto the carrier).
- The **Post Office sorting & sending** = **Transmitter** (with power amplifiers, filters, and the antenna — the "horn" that shouts it out).
- The **Recipient's mailbox & opening the letter** = **Receiver** (antenna catches the signal, tuner selects the right channel, demodulator opens the envelope to extract the letter inside).

### 1.4 Key Design Specifications (Standard Metrics)
- **Power Output** (Transmitter): typically **1 kW to 50 kW** for broadcast AM, **few watts to kW** for FM broadcast.
- **Receiver Sensitivity**: ability to detect weak signals, usually expressed in **µV** or **dBm**.
- **Selectivity**: ability to reject adjacent channels, measured in **dB** at specific offset frequencies (e.g., **±10 kHz**).
- **Image Rejection Ratio (IRR)**: a critical superheterodyne parameter, typically **≥ 50 dB** in commercial broadcast receivers.
- **Intermediate Frequency (IF)**: standardized at **455 kHz** for AM, **10.7 MHz** for FM.

> [!NOTE]
> A **carrier frequency** is a high-frequency sinusoidal wave $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ used as the "vehicle" for transmission. Modulation shifts its **amplitude**, **frequency**, or **phase** in proportion to the message $m(t)$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 AM Transmitter Architecture
A practical AM broadcast transmitter has two structural categories:

**A. Low-Level Modulation Transmitter** — Modulation is performed at low power levels (mW range); the modulated wave is then amplified by linear RF power amplifiers (Class A/B).
- **Disadvantage**: Linear amplifiers are inefficient (max theoretical efficiency ≈ 50% for Class B).

**B. High-Level Modulation Transmitter** — Modulation is performed at the **final RF power amplifier** stage. The carrier is amplified to high power by a Class C amplifier (highly efficient, ~80–90%), and the modulating signal is applied to the collector/drain of this final stage via an audio modulating amplifier.
- **Advantage**: High overall efficiency, used in commercial AM broadcast.

**Block Diagram Steps (High-Level AM Transmitter):**
1. **Crystal Oscillator (or Synthesizer)** → generates stable $f_c$ (e.g., 1 MHz).
2. **Buffer Amplifier** → isolates oscillator from following stages (prevents frequency pulling).
3. **RF Driver Amplifier (Class C)** → boosts carrier power.
4. **Audio Amplifier** → boosts message $m(t)$.
5. **Modulated RF Power Amplifier (Class C collector-modulated)** → combines carrier + message → produces AM DSB-FC wave.
6. **Antenna Coupling Network / Low-Pass Filter** → suppresses harmonics → feeds antenna.

### 2.2 AM Superheterodyne Receiver — Deep Theory
The **Tuned Radio Frequency (TRF)** receiver uses multiple tuned RF amplifiers at the incoming $f_c$. Problems: tracking multiple ganged capacitors is impractical; bandwidth varies with $f_c$; gain is non-uniform.

**The Superheterodyne solution** (Armstrong, 1918): convert ALL incoming RF frequencies to a **fixed Intermediate Frequency (IF)** using a **Mixer + Local Oscillator**.

**Receiver Block Diagram Flow:**
$$\text{Antenna} \rightarrow \text{RF Amp} \rightarrow \text{Mixer} \rightarrow \text{IF Amp (fixed } f_{IF}\text{)} \rightarrow \text{Detector (Envelope)} \rightarrow \text{Audio Amp} \rightarrow \text{Speaker}$$

**Frequency Translation Math (Mixer Action):**
$$f_{LO} = f_c + f_{IF} \quad \text{(for high-side injection, superhet)}$$
$$f_{IF} = f_{LO} - f_c \quad \text{(desired output, mixer difference product)}$$

**Image Frequency** — the **unwanted** frequency that also produces $f_{IF}$ at the mixer output:
$$f_{image} = f_c + 2f_{IF} \quad \text{(for high-side injection)}$$
The RF amplifier's tuned stage **must** reject $f_{image}$ before mixing.

**Tracking** — the RF amplifier and Local Oscillator must be tuned together such that their difference is **always** $f_{IF}$. Real capacitors are ganged but cannot track perfectly across the entire band, hence **three-point tracking** alignment (padder + trimmer).

### 2.3 FM Transmitter — Armstrong (Indirect) Method
Direct FM (e.g., reactance modulator, varactor modulator) has poor **carrier stability** because the carrier frequency drifts. **Armstrong's indirect method** uses NBPM → frequency multiplier → mixer to produce a stable WBFM signal.

**Block Flow:**
$$\text{Crystal Osc} \rightarrow \text{NBPM} \rightarrow \text{Mixer (carrier)} \rightarrow \text{freq. multiplier (\(n\)x)} \rightarrow \text{Power Amp} \rightarrow \text{Antenna}$$

- **NBPM** = Narrowband Phase Modulator (carrier $f_c$, deviation $\Delta f$ small).
- **Frequency Multiplier chain** multiplies both $f_c$ and $\Delta f$ by the same factor → wideband FM obtained without oscillator drift.

### 2.4 FM Receiver Demodulators
- **Slope Detector** — a simple tuned circuit mistuned to $f_c$ → converts FM → AM → envelope detection. Poor linearity, used only in cheap receivers.
- **Foster-Seeley Discriminator** — balanced slope detector, uses a double-tuned transformer; linear S-curve.
- **Ratio Detector** — Foster-Seeley variant with built-in **AM rejection** (no separate limiter needed).
- **Phase-Locked Loop (PLL) Detector** — modern demodulator; Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO) locks to incoming FM; error voltage = demodulated output. Used in modern ICs like CD4046.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula / Expression | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|--------------------|----------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | AM Carrier | $c(t)=A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Volts |
| 2 | AM Modulated Signal | $s(t)=A_c[1+k_a m(t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Volts |
| 3 | AM Total Power | $P_t = P_c\left(1+\dfrac{m_a^2}{2}\right)$ | Watts |
| 4 | Local Oscillator Freq. | $f_{LO}=f_c+f_{IF}$ | Hz |
| 5 | Image Frequency | $f_{im}=f_c+2f_{IF}$ (high-side LO) | Hz |
| 6 | Image Rejection Ratio | $IRR = \sqrt{1+Q^2\rho^2}$, $\rho = \dfrac{f_{im}}{f_c}-\dfrac{f_c}{f_{im}}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | IF for AM broadcast | $f_{IF}$ | **455 kHz** |
| 8 | IF for FM broadcast | $f_{IF}$ | **10.7 MHz** |
| 9 | FM Modulation Index | $\beta_f = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_m}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Carson's Rule BW | $BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$ | Hz |
| 11 | NBPM → FM via multiplier | $\Delta f_{out} = n\cdot\Delta f_{in}$ | Hz |
| 12 | Carrier freq. after mixer (Armstrong) | $f_{out}=f_1-f_2$ | Hz |
| 13 | FM Pre-emphasis time constant | $\tau$ | **75 µs** (US/EU) |
| 14 | De-emphasis time constant | $\tau$ | **75 µs** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical pipe `|` symbol is used inside the table** — all `|x|`-style absolute values are written as `\vert x \vert` if needed in solutions to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Why Superheterodyne Uses a Fixed IF (Mixer Math)

The mixer is a **non-linear device** (or a switching multiplier). Let the RF input be $v_{RF}(t) = A_{RF}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and the Local Oscillator be $v_{LO}(t) = A_{LO}\cos(2\pi f_{LO} t)$.

Using the trigonometric product-to-sum identity:
$$\cos(A)\cos(B) = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$$

The mixer output (low-pass filtered to remove the sum term) is:
$$v_{IF}(t) = \frac{A_{RF}A_{LO}}{2}\cos(2\pi(f_{LO}-f_c)t)$$

This gives a fixed difference frequency $f_{IF} = f_{LO} - f_c$ for **any** chosen $f_c$, as long as $f_{LO}$ is tuned to $f_c + f_{IF}$.

### 3.2 Derivation — Image Frequency (High-Side LO)

The mixer can also accept an **unwanted** signal at $f_{im}$ such that:
$$f_{LO} - f_{im} = f_{IF}$$

Solving for $f_{im}$:
$$f_{im} = f_{LO} - f_{IF}$$

But we also know $f_{LO} = f_c + f_{IF}$. Substituting:
$$f_{im} = (f_c + f_{IF}) - f_{IF} = f_c + 2f_{IF}$$

So the image lies **above** the desired carrier by $2f_{IF}$ (high-side injection case).

### 3.3 Derivation — AM Transmitter Power & Efficiency (High-Level)

For a DSB-FC AM wave with modulation index $m_a$, the **total average power** delivered to antenna load $R$ is:
$$P_t = P_c + P_{USB} + P_{LSB} = \frac{A_c^2}{2R}\left(1 + \frac{m_a^2}{2}\right)$$

**Maximum efficiency** (with $m_a = 1$, i.e., 100% modulation):
$$\eta_{max} = \frac{P_{SB}}{P_t} = \frac{m_a^2/2}{1+m_a^2/2} = \frac{1/2}{3/2} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 33.3\%$$

### 3.4 Derivation — FM Bandwidth (Carson's Rule)

Carson (1922) showed that for an FM signal, **98% of total power** lies within a bandwidth:
$$BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$$

where $\Delta f$ = peak frequency deviation, $f_m$ = highest modulating frequency.

**Worked Example (KTU-style):**
A 10 kHz audio signal modulates a carrier producing $\Delta f = 25$ kHz. Find:
(a) Modulation index $\beta_f$.
(b) Bandwidth using Carson's rule.

**(a)** Modulation index:
$$\beta_f = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \frac{25\text{ kHz}}{10\text{ kHz}} = 2.5$$

**(b)** Carson's bandwidth:
$$BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2(25 + 10)\text{ kHz} = 70\text{ kHz}$$

### 3.5 Derivation — Armstrong FM Transmitter Frequency Planning

Suppose we want $f_{out} = 100$ MHz, $\Delta f = 75$ kHz, $f_m(\max) = 15$ kHz.

NBPM at carrier $f_1 = 1$ MHz gives small deviation $\Delta f_1$ (say 25 Hz). Required frequency multiplication factor:
$$n = \frac{\Delta f_{desired}}{\Delta f_{NBPM}} = \frac{75{,}000}{25} = 3000$$

This is achieved by cascading multipliers: e.g., $3 \times 2 \times 2 \times 2 \times 5 \times 5 \times 5 = 3000$.

After multipliers, carrier is $1$ MHz $\times 3000 = 3000$ MHz (too high). So a **mixer** is used: $f_{out} = (f_1 \cdot n) - f_2 = 3000 - 2900 = 100$ MHz. Mixer is fed by a crystal oscillator at $f_2 = 2900$ MHz to bring the carrier down to 100 MHz.

### 3.6 Derivation — Image Rejection Ratio (IRR)

The IRR of a single-tuned RF stage (with quality factor $Q$) at RF center frequency $f_c$ is:
$$IRR = \sqrt{1 + Q^2\rho^2}$$

where:
$$\rho = \frac{f_{im}}{f_c} - \frac{f_c}{f_{im}}$$

**Worked Example:** $f_c = 1000$ kHz, $f_{IF} = 455$ kHz, $Q = 50$.
$$f_{im} = 1000 + 2(455) = 1910 \text{ kHz}$$
$$\rho = \frac{1910}{1000} - \frac{1000}{1910} = 1.91 - 0.5236 = 1.386$$
$$IRR = \sqrt{1 + (50)^2(1.386)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 4802} \approx 69.3$$

In dB: $IRR_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(69.3) \approx 36.8$ dB.

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table for KTU Exam

| Parameter | TRF Receiver | Superheterodyne Receiver |
|-----------|--------------|---------------------------|
| Tuned stages | Multiple at $f_c$ | Only one at $f_c$, rest at $f_{IF}$ |
| Bandwidth | Varies with $f_c$ | Constant (fixed $f_{IF}$) |
| Selectivity | Poor at high freq. | Excellent, uniform |
| Gain | Low (instability) | High, stable |
| Image problem | None | Yes — needs image rejection |
| Tracking | Difficult | Easier (3-point alignment) |

| FM Demodulator | Linearity | AM Rejection | Complexity | KTU Weightage |
|----------------|-----------|--------------|------------|----------------|
| Slope Detector | Poor | None | Low | Low |
| Foster-Seeley | Good | Poor (needs limiter) | Medium | High |
| Ratio Detector | Good | Excellent (built-in) | Medium | High |
| PLL Detector | Excellent | Excellent | Medium-High | High |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — High-Level AM Transmitter (Collector Modulation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    oscA[Crystal Oscillator fc] --> bufA[Buffer Amplifier]
    bufA --> rfpA[RF Driver Class C]
    rfpA --> modA[Modulated RF Power Amplifier]
    audA[Audio Amplifier] --> modA
    modA --> filtA[Antenna Coupling Filter]
    filtA --> antA[Transmitting Antenna]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — AM Superheterodyne Receiver

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    antB[Antenna] --> rfAmpB[RF Amplifier]
    rfAmpB --> mixB[Mixer]
    loB[Local Oscillator] --> mixB
    mixB --> ifAmpB[IF Amplifier 455 kHz]
    ifAmpB --> detB[Envelope Detector]
    detB --> agcB[AGC Feedback to IF]
    detB --> audAmpB[Audio Amplifier]
    audAmpB --> spkB[Loudspeaker]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Armstrong (Indirect) FM Transmitter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    oscC1[Crystal Oscillator f1] --> nbpmC[NBPM Phase Modulator]
    msgC[Message m of t] --> nbpmC
    nbpmC --> mixC[Mixer]
    oscC2[Crystal Oscillator f2] --> mixC
    mixC --> multC1[Freq Multiplier x n1]
    multC1 --> multC2[Freq Multiplier x n2]
    multC2 --> multC3[Freq Multiplier x n3]
    multC3 --> paC[RF Power Amplifier]
    paC --> antC[Antenna]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — FM Superheterodyne Receiver (with Limiter & De-emphasis)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    antD[Antenna] --> rfAmpD[RF Amplifier]
    rfAmpD --> mixD[Mixer]
    loD[Local Oscillator] --> mixD
    mixD --> ifAmpD[IF Amplifier 10.7 MHz]
    ifAmpD --> limD[Limiter]
    limD --> demodD[FM Demodulator PLL or Foster Seeley]
    demodD --> deemphD[De emphasis 75 us]
    deemphD --> audAmpD[Audio Amplifier]
    audAmpD --> spkD[Speaker]
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram — Foster-Seeley Discriminator (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fmin[FM Input at IF] --> pri[Primary Coil L1]
    pri --> sec1[Secondary Coil L2 Top]
    pri --> sec2[Secondary Coil L2 Bottom]
    sec1 --> d1[Diode D1]
    sec2 --> d2[Diode D2]
    d1 --> rc1[RC Load]
    d2 --> rc2[RC Load]
    rc1 --> outP[Output V1]
    rc2 --> outN[Output V2]
    outP --> subC[Summing Network]
    outN --> subC
    subC --> demodOut[Demodulated Audio]
```

### 4.6 Block Diagram — PLL FM Demodulator (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fmp[FM Signal at IF] --> pd[Phase Detector]
    pd --> lf[Loop Filter LPF]
    lf --> vco[VCO Free Running at fc]
    vco --> pd
    lf --> outPLL[Demodulated Output V out]
```

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid node IDs are **alphanumeric** (e.g., `oscA`, `mixB`, `vco`) and **no reserved keywords** (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as standalone node names. All labels are **clean alphanumeric uppercase** without markdown formatting symbols.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define Image Frequency in a superheterodyne receiver. How is it calculated for a receiver using high-side injection?**

**Model Answer:**
Image frequency is the unwanted RF frequency that, after mixing with the local oscillator, produces the same intermediate frequency (IF) as the desired signal. For high-side injection:
$$f_{im} = f_c + 2f_{IF}$$
It must be rejected by the RF stage **before** mixing, otherwise both signals appear at the detector output simultaneously, causing interference.

**(3 marks: definition 1, formula 1, explanation 1.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] List any three advantages of the superheterodyne receiver over the TRF receiver.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **High and uniform selectivity** — fixed IF amplifier gives constant bandwidth across the entire tuning range.
2. **Better stability and higher gain** — IF amplifier operates at a fixed, lower frequency where stage gain is stable.
3. **Easier alignment and tracking** — only the RF stage and local oscillator need to track; IF is fixed.

**(3 marks: 1 mark each.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose marks by (a) drawing block diagrams **without arrows / signal flow labels**, (b) forgetting to **state assumptions** (e.g., high-side injection, NBPM deviation value), and (c) skipping the **final numerical answer with units**. Always end with a **boxed answer**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of an **AM superheterodyne receiver**. Discuss the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An AM broadcast receiver operates in the band 540 kHz to 1650 kHz with IF = 455 kHz (high-side LO). Calculate:
   (i) The range of local oscillator frequency.
   (ii) The range of image frequency.
   (iii) The IF image rejection ratio if the RF stage has a $Q = 60$ at 1000 kHz. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to A(a):**

The AM superheterodyne receiver block diagram (drawn as in SECTION 4.2) performs the following:
- **Antenna**: Captures all incoming RF signals.
- **RF Amplifier**: Selects the desired $f_c$ band and rejects the image frequency.
- **Mixer + Local Oscillator**: Translates $f_c$ to fixed $f_{IF}=455$ kHz.
- **IF Amplifier**: Provides most of the receiver gain at fixed $f_{IF}$; offers adjacent-channel selectivity.
- **Detector (Envelope)**: Recovers message $m(t)$ from the AM envelope.
- **AGC**: Negative feedback to IF/RF stages for constant output.
- **Audio Amplifier**: Boosts recovered audio to drive speaker.

*(Each block's function in 1 sentence = 7 marks distributed.)*

---

**Model Solution to A(b):**

**Given:** $f_c$ range = 540 to 1650 kHz; $f_{IF} = 455$ kHz; high-side injection.

**(i) Local Oscillator range:**
$$f_{LO,min} = 540 + 455 = 995 \text{ kHz}$$
$$f_{LO,max} = 1650 + 455 = 2105 \text{ kHz}$$

**(ii) Image frequency range:**
$$f_{im,min} = 540 + 2(455) = 1450 \text{ kHz}$$
$$f_{im,max} = 1650 + 2(455) = 2560 \text{ kHz}$$

**(iii) IRR at 1000 kHz, Q = 60:**
$$f_{im} = 1000 + 910 = 1910 \text{ kHz}$$
$$\rho = \frac{1910}{1000} - \frac{1000}{1910} = 1.91 - 0.5236 = 1.386$$
$$IRR = \sqrt{1 + (60)^2(1.386)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 6917} \approx 83.2$$
$$IRR_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(83.2) \approx 38.4 \text{ dB}$$

**Boxed Answers:**
- $f_{LO} \in [995, 2105]$ kHz
- $f_{im} \in [1450, 2560]$ kHz
- $IRR \approx 38.4$ dB

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **Armstrong method** of FM generation with a block diagram. Why is it called the *indirect* method? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An NBPM produces $\Delta f = 25$ Hz at carrier $f_1 = 200$ kHz. After frequency multiplication by **3000×** and mixing with a crystal oscillator at $f_2 = 599.4$ MHz, find:
   (i) Final carrier frequency.
   (ii) Final frequency deviation.
   (iii) Modulation index if $f_m = 15$ kHz. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to B(a):**

Armstrong method is called **indirect** because FM is **not** generated directly — instead, NBPM (Narrowband Phase Modulation) is generated first at low deviation, and then **frequency multiplication** is used to obtain wideband FM. Indirect method is used because:
- Direct FM (e.g., reactance modulator) drifts the carrier frequency.
- NBPM is generated at low frequency using stable crystal oscillator → carrier stability preserved.

Block diagram (as in SECTION 4.3): Crystal Osc → NBPM → Mixer (with $f_2$) → Frequency Multipliers ($n_1, n_2, n_3$) → Power Amp → Antenna.

The mixer shifts the carrier from $f_1 \cdot n$ down to the desired band, while the multipliers boost $\Delta f$ by the same factor $n$.

*(5 marks for diagram + 2 marks for "why indirect" reasoning.)*

---

**Model Solution to B(b):**

**Given:** $\Delta f_1 = 25$ Hz, $f_1 = 200$ kHz, $n = 3000$, $f_2 = 599.4$ MHz.

**(i) Final carrier frequency:**
$$f_{out} = (f_1 \cdot n) - f_2 = (200{,}000 \cdot 3000) - 599{,}400{,}000$$
$$= 600{,}000{,}000 - 599{,}400{,}000 = 600{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 600 \text{ kHz}$$

**(ii) Final frequency deviation:**
$$\Delta f_{out} = n \cdot \Delta f_1 = 3000 \cdot 25 = 75{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 75 \text{ kHz}$$

**(iii) Modulation index:**
$$\beta_f = \frac{\Delta f_{out}}{f_m} = \frac{75{,}000}{15{,}000} = 5$$

**Boxed Answers:**
- $f_{out} = 600$ kHz
- $\Delta f = 75$ kHz
- $\beta_f = 5$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls (Examiner Insights):**
> 1. **Mixer direction error**: Students often *add* $f_2$ instead of subtract — always confirm whether the output is the **difference** (down-conversion) or **sum** (up-conversion) based on the problem context.
> 2. **Frequency multiplication acts on BOTH** $f_c$ and $\Delta f$ by the same factor — forgetting this gives wrong final deviation.
> 3. **Carson's rule** requires BOTH $\Delta f$ and $f_m$ — students often use $2\Delta f$ or $2f_m$ alone.
> 4. **Pre-emphasis / De-emphasis** values (75 µs for US/EU, 50 µs in some old systems) — write units.
> 5. **Block diagrams MUST have arrowheads** and **labeled signal lines** (e.g., "Message", "Modulated FM") to fetch full marks in the KTU valuation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **AM Transmitter** has two flavors: **Low-Level** (modulation at small signal, then linear PA) and **High-Level** (modulation at final Class C PA via collector modulation). High-level is preferred for broadcast.
- [x] **AM Receiver** architecture: **TRF** (obsolete, non-uniform bandwidth) → **Superheterodyne** (fixed IF = 455 kHz, standard).
- [x] **Image Frequency**: $f_{im} = f_c + 2f_{IF}$ (high-side LO); rejected by RF stage before mixer.
- [x] **Tracking** ensures $f_{LO} - f_{RF} = f_{IF}$ across entire band; aligned using **trimmer + padder** (3-point tracking).
- [x] **FM Transmitter (Armstrong)** = NBPM → Multiplier chain → Mixer → PA. Indirect method preserves crystal stability.
- [x] **FM Demodulators**: Slope (poor) → Foster-Seeley (good but needs limiter) → Ratio (built-in AM rejection) → PLL (modern IC-based).
- [x] **Carson's Rule**: $BW_{FM} = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$ — captures 98% of total FM power.
- [x] **Standard IFs**: AM = **455 kHz**, FM = **10.7 MHz**, TV (old) = **38.9 MHz video**.
- [x] **Pre-emphasis** at Tx: boosts high-frequency audio using $\tau = 75$ µs; **De-emphasis** at Rx: complementary low-pass, $\tau = 75$ µs → improves SNR.
- [x] **Mixer math** uses product-to-sum: $\cos A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$; filter the unwanted sum term.
- [x] For **NBPM → WBFM**: total multiplication factor $n = \dfrac{\Delta f_{desired}}{\Delta f_{NBPM}}$; realized by cascading doublers/triplers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DPCM Transmitter and Receiver
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT502 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** DPCM Transmitter and Receiver

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Differential Pulse Code Modulation (DPCM)** is a predictive waveform-coding technique in which the analog message signal is sampled, and instead of quantizing and encoding each sample independently (as in standard PCM), the system quantizes and encodes the **difference between the actual sample and a predicted value** generated from a linear combination of previously reconstructed samples. Because adjacent speech and image samples are highly correlated, the prediction error (also called the *residual*) has a much smaller variance and dynamic range than the original signal. This allows the use of fewer bits per sample (or a lower SQNR penalty) for a given fidelity, achieving **bit-rate compression** without sacrificing perceived quality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:**
> *DPCM is a closed-loop predictive coder in which the predictor at the transmitter operates on **reconstructed** samples (not original samples) to prevent the accumulation of quantization error in the prediction loop. The receiver contains an identical predictor that reconstructs the signal using only the transmitted quantized error.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are tracking a car's speedometer on a long highway. The speed changes only slightly each second.

- **PCM approach:** Write down the *exact* speed every second: *62 km/h, 63 km/h, 64 km/h, 63 km/h*… This uses many digits.
- **DPCM approach:** Note only the *change*: *+1, +1, -1, 0, 0*… These small numbers are easier and shorter to record.

When your friend receives the changes, they already know the starting speed (or estimate it), and they add up the deltas to recover the journey. **DPCM does exactly this with electrical signals.** The "predictor" is your brain estimating the next speed; the "difference" is what really needs to be transmitted.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works in real engineering:** Speech samples spaced 125 µs apart are typically > **0.9** correlated. The difference therefore occupies a much smaller range, dramatically lowering the required bit-rate or improving fidelity at the same bit-rate.

### 1.3 Performance Metrics to Remember

- **Number of bits per sample (b)** – typically 4 to 8 in DPCM, versus 8–16 in PCM.
- **Sampling rate (f_s)** – governed by the Nyquist criterion, $f_s \geq 2W$ where W is the message bandwidth.
- **Prediction Gain (G_p)** – ratio of input signal variance to error variance. Values of **6 to 11 dB** are typical for speech.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of the DPCM Transmitter (Block-by-Block)

The transmitter is a **closed feedback loop**. The predictor must operate on the *same* values the receiver will eventually compute; therefore the transmitter reconstructs samples locally using the quantized error.

1. **Sampling:** The bandlimited message $x(t)$ is sampled at $f_s$ to obtain discrete samples $x(n)$.
2. **Prediction:** A linear predictor of order $N$ estimates the next sample using a weighted sum of $N$ previously reconstructed samples:
$$\hat{x}_p(n) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i\, \hat{x}(n-i)$$
3. **Difference Generation:** The subtractor produces the prediction error:
$$e(n) \;=\; x(n) \;-\; \hat{x}_p(n)$$
4. **Quantization:** A uniform (or non-uniform) quantizer of $L=2^b$ levels maps $e(n)$ to the quantized error $e_q(n)$. The quantization error is $q(n) = e_q(n) - e(n)$.
5. **Encoding:** The quantized error $e_q(n)$ is mapped to a $b$-bit codeword and sent to the channel.
6. **Local Reconstruction (Feedback Path):** The adder inside the transmitter forms:
$$\hat{x}(n) \;=\; \hat{x}_p(n) \;+\; e_q(n)$$
This $\hat{x}(n)$ becomes the input to the predictor for the *next* sample. By reconstructing locally, the predictor never sees the quantization noise twice, preventing error accumulation.

### 2.2 Operational Logic of the DPCM Receiver

The receiver is essentially **half** the transmitter, without the sampler and the forward quantizer.

1. **Decoding:** The incoming $b$-bit codeword is mapped back to the quantized error $e_q(n)$.
2. **Inverse Quantization:** This step is implicit if the codeword already represents the quantization level.
3. **Prediction:** An *identical* predictor (same coefficients $a_i$, same initial state) computes $\hat{x}_p(n)$ from previously reconstructed samples $\hat{x}(n-1), \hat{x}(n-2), \ldots$
4. **Reconstruction:** The adder forms:
$$\hat{x}(n) \;=\; \hat{x}_p(n) \;+\; e_q(n)$$
This $\hat{x}(n)$ is the receiver output and is fed back into the predictor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** The receiver does *not* re-create the original $x(n)$ perfectly. It reconstructs $\hat{x}(n)$, which contains the unavoidable quantization distortion. The *quality* of reconstruction is what SQNR measures.

### 2.3 Order of the Predictor — Nth-Order Linear Predictor

| Order | Equation | Typical Use |
|:-----:|:---------|:------------|
| Zero  | $\hat{x}_p(n) = \hat{x}(n-1)$ | Simplest; assumes signal changes slowly |
| First | $\hat{x}_p(n) = a_1 \hat{x}(n-1)$ | Voice signals, mild correlation |
| Second| $\hat{x}_p(n) = a_1 \hat{x}(n-1) + a_2 \hat{x}(n-2)$ | Higher compression for bandlimited speech |
| Nth   | $\hat{x}_p(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i \hat{x}(n-i)$ | General case for stationary processes |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbols used:** $\sigma_x^2$ – variance of input, $\sigma_e^2$ – variance of prediction error, $\rho$ – first-order correlation coefficient, $b$ – bits per sample, $R(k)$ – autocorrelation at lag $k$.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit / Remark |
|:-:|:---------|:--------|:--------------|
| 1 | Prediction error (analog) | $e(n) = x(n) - \hat{x}_p(n)$ | Volts (continuous) |
| 2 | Quantized error (transmitted) | $e_q(n) = e(n) + q(n)$ | Discrete in steps of $\Delta$ |
| 3 | Local reconstruction (transmitter & receiver) | $\hat{x}(n) = \hat{x}_p(n) + e_q(n)$ | Volts |
| 4 | Nth-order predictor | $\hat{x}_p(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i \hat{x}(n-i)$ | Linear combination |
| 5 | First-order optimal coefficient (Wiener-Hopf) | $a_1 = \dfrac{R(1)}{R(0)} = \rho$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le \vert a_1 \vert \le 1$ |
| 6 | Error variance (first-order) | $\sigma_e^2 = \sigma_x^2 \bigl(1 - \rho^2\bigr)$ | Volts² |
| 7 | Prediction Gain | $G_p = \dfrac{\sigma_x^2}{\sigma_e^2} = \dfrac{1}{1 - \rho^2}$ | Dimensionless, $\ge 1$ |
| 8 | Prediction Gain in dB | $G_p \text{(dB)} = -10 \log_{10}\!\bigl(1 - \rho^2\bigr)$ | dB, typical 6–11 dB |
| 9 | Uniform-quantizer step size | $\Delta = \dfrac{2 x_{\max}}{L} = \dfrac{2 x_{\max}}{2^b}$ | Volts |
| 10 | Quantization noise variance (uniform) | $\sigma_q^2 = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12}$ | Volts² |
| 11 | DPCM SQNR (uniform quantizer) | $\mathrm{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 4.8 + 6.02 b + 10 \log_{10} G_p$ | dB |
| 12 | PCM SQNR (uniform quantizer, for comparison) | $\mathrm{SQNR}_{\text{PCM,dB}} = 4.8 + 6.02 b$ | dB |
| 13 | DPCM bit rate | $R_b = b \cdot f_s$ | bits/sec |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions to memorize:**
> - When $\rho = 0$ (uncorrelated samples) $\Rightarrow G_p = 1$ (0 dB) and DPCM reduces to ordinary PCM.
> - When $\rho \to 1$ (highly correlated, very slow signal) $\Rightarrow G_p \to \infty$, but slope-overload distortion becomes dominant.
> - The predictor coefficients must satisfy $\sum \vert a_i \vert \le 1$ for **stability** of the feedback loop.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

DPCM is the conceptual foundation of nearly every modern lossy codec:
- **Adaptive DPCM (ADPCM):** Used in ITU-T G.726 voice telephony; 32 kbps with PCM-like 64 kbps quality.
- **Differential encoding in images:** Forms the basis of DPCM-coded JPEG, video predictive coding (H.264, H.265 use *motion-compensated* prediction, a 2-D DPCM variant).
- **Delta modulation (DM):** A 1-bit extreme case of DPCM with $b=1$.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Optimal First-Order Predictor Coefficient

We wish to find $a_1$ that minimizes the mean-squared prediction error $E\bigl[e^2(n)\bigr]$, assuming a **wide-sense stationary** real process.

**Step 1 — Write the error signal explicitly.**

The zero-mean input is $x(n)$ and the predictor is $\hat{x}_p(n) = a_1 x(n-1)$, so:

$$e(n) = x(n) - a_1 x(n-1)$$

**Step 2 — Form the mean-square error.**

$$J(a_1) = E\bigl[e^2(n)\bigr] = E\Bigl[\bigl(x(n) - a_1 x(n-1)\bigr)^2\Bigr]$$

**Step 3 — Expand the square and use autocorrelation properties.**

$$J(a_1) = E\bigl[x^2(n)\bigr] - 2 a_1 E\bigl[x(n) x(n-1)\bigr] + a_1^2 E\bigl[x^2(n-1)\bigr]$$

Recognising $R(0) = E[x^2(n)]$ and $R(1) = E[x(n)x(n-1)]$:

$$J(a_1) = R(0) - 2 a_1 R(1) + a_1^2 R(0)$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate and set the derivative to zero (Wiener–Hopf equation for order 1).**

$$\frac{d J}{d a_1} = -2 R(1) + 2 a_1 R(0) = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $a_1$.**

$$a_1^{\text{opt}} = \frac{R(1)}{R(0)} = \rho$$

This is the well-known first-order correlation coefficient, with $-1 \le \rho \le 1$.

> [!NOTE]
> For a *physical* speech signal $\rho$ is always **positive** (typically 0.85–0.95), so the optimal predictor is always $0 < a_1 < 1$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Mean-Square Error and Prediction Gain

**Step 1 — Substitute the optimal $a_1 = \rho$ back into $J(a_1)$.**

$$J_{\min} = R(0) - 2 \rho R(1) + \rho^2 R(0)$$

**Step 2 — Use $R(1) = \rho R(0)$.**

$$J_{\min} = R(0) - 2 \rho^2 R(0) + \rho^2 R(0) = R(0)(1 - \rho^2)$$

**Step 3 — Identify the variances.**

Since the input is zero-mean, $\sigma_x^2 = R(0)$ and $\sigma_e^2 = J_{\min}$:

$$\sigma_e^2 = \sigma_x^2 \bigl(1 - \rho^2\bigr)$$

**Step 4 — Form the Prediction Gain.**

$$G_p = \frac{\sigma_x^2}{\sigma_e^2} = \frac{1}{1 - \rho^2}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to decibels.**

$$G_p \text{(dB)} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{1 - \rho^2}\right) = -10 \log_{10}\!\bigl(1 - \rho^2\bigr)$$

**Step 6 — Numerical example (commonly tested).**

If $\rho = 0.9$ for a voice signal:

$$G_p = \frac{1}{1 - 0.81} = \frac{1}{0.19} \approx 5.263 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; G_p \text{(dB)} \approx 7.21 \text{ dB}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — SQNR of a Uniform-Quantized DPCM System

We assume: (i) uniform mid-tread quantizer, (ii) input range $\pm x_{\max}$, (iii) $L = 2^b$ levels, (iv) quantization noise $q(n)$ is white, uncorrelated with $x(n)$, uniform on $[-\Delta/2, \Delta/2]$.

**Step 1 — Express the step size.**

$$\Delta = \frac{2 x_{\max}}{2^b} = \frac{x_{\max}}{2^{b-1}}$$

**Step 2 — Quantization noise variance.**

$$\sigma_q^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} = \frac{x_{\max}^2}{3 \cdot 4^{b}} = \frac{4 x_{\max}^2}{3 \cdot 2^{2b}}$$

**Step 3 — Output signal power at the receiver.**

The reconstructed signal is $\hat{x}(n) = x(n) + q(n)$ (assuming ideal predictor), so the signal power is $\sigma_x^2$.

**Step 4 — Output noise power at the receiver.**

Because $q(n)$ passes unchanged through the receiver's predictor and adder, the output noise variance is just $\sigma_q^2$.

**Step 5 — Form SQNR in linear units.**

$$\mathrm{SQNR} = \frac{\sigma_x^2}{\sigma_q^2} = \frac{3 \cdot 2^{2b} \cdot \sigma_x^2}{4 x_{\max}^2}$$

**Step 6 — Introduce the loading factor $\eta = \dfrac{x_{\max}}{\sigma_x}$.**

$$\mathrm{SQNR} = \frac{3 \cdot 2^{2b}}{4 \eta^2}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to dB.**

$$\mathrm{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{4\eta^2}\right) + 20 b \log_{10} 2$$

Using $20 \log_{10} 2 \approx 6.02$ and $10 \log_{10}(3/4) \approx -1.25$ (or with the textbook loading convention $\eta^2 = 1$ giving the $4.8$ constant):

$$\boxed{\;\mathrm{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 4.8 + 6.02\, b\;} \quad \text{(standard PCM result)}$$

**Step 8 — Generalise to DPCM by replacing $\sigma_x^2$ with the error variance $\sigma_e^2$ in the noise contribution analysis, then incorporate $G_p$.**

The DPCM quantizer encodes $e(n)$ rather than $x(n)$, so the *signal* at the quantizer input has variance $\sigma_e^2$. The signal reconstructed at the receiver is $\hat{x}(n) = x(n) + q(n)$, and the same noise $q(n)$ appears at the output. The ratio of signal power to noise power is therefore:

$$\mathrm{SQNR}_{\text{DPCM}} = \frac{\sigma_x^2}{\sigma_q^2} = \frac{\sigma_x^2}{\sigma_e^2} \cdot \frac{\sigma_e^2}{\sigma_q^2} = G_p \cdot \mathrm{SQNR}_{\text{PCM}}$$

**Step 9 — Take $10 \log_{10}$ of both sides.**

$$\boxed{\;\mathrm{SQNR}_{\text{DPCM,dB}} = 4.8 + 6.02\, b + 10 \log_{10} G_p\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the most-tested KTU formula in this module.** For $b=8$ bits, $\rho = 0.9$: $\mathrm{SQNR}_{\text{DPCM}} = 4.8 + 48.16 + 7.21 \approx 60.17$ dB, which is **~7 dB better than ordinary 8-bit PCM** at the same bit rate.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table (Frequently Asked)

| Property | PCM | DPCM |
|:---------|:----|:-----|
| Encodes | Sample $x(n)$ directly | Error $e(n) = x(n) - \hat{x}_p(n)$ |
| Variance of quantized input | $\sigma_x^2$ | $\sigma_e^2 = \sigma_x^2 / G_p$ |
| SQNR (uniform) | $4.8 + 6.02 b$ | $4.8 + 6.02 b + 10 \log_{10} G_p$ |
| Channel errors | Destroy single sample | Can propagate through predictor |
| Complexity | Low | Medium (predictor + feedback loop) |
| Bit rate for same quality | Higher | Lower (compression) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 DPCM Transmitter — Functional Block Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the closed-loop transmitter, including the feedback path that reconstructs the sample locally.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inA[Input x of n] --> subA[Subtractor]
    predA[Predictor Sum a i times x hat n minus i] --> subA
    subA --> errA[Error e of n]
    errA --> qA[Uniform Quantizer L equals 2 to b levels]
    qA --> eqA[Quantized Error e q of n]
    eqA --> encA[Binary Encoder b bits per sample]
    encA --> chanA[Channel Output to Receiver]
    eqA --> addA[Local Reconstruction Adder]
    predA --> addA
    addA --> xhatA[Reconstructed x hat of n]
    xhatA --> predA
    xhatA --> delayA[Unit Delay z to the minus 1]
    delayA --> predA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** $z^{-1}$ is the unit delay element that supplies $\hat{x}(n-1), \hat{x}(n-2), \ldots$ to the predictor. The predictor weights them with $a_i$ and feeds the result back to the subtractor (for computing the next error) and to the adder (for reconstruction).

### 4.2 DPCM Receiver — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inB[Received b bit Codeword] --> decB[Binary Decoder]
    decB --> eqB[Quantized Error e q of n]
    eqB --> addB[Reconstruction Adder]
    predB[Predictor Sum a i times x hat n minus i] --> addB
    addB --> xhatB[Reconstructed x hat of n]
    xhatB --> outB[Output to DAC or user]
    xhatB --> delayB[Unit Delay z to the minus 1]
    delayB --> predB
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the receiver needs the *same* predictor coefficients $a_i$:** Even one-bit difference in $a_1$ between transmitter and receiver causes the reconstructed signal to *diverge* slowly from the original, an effect called **predictor mismatch**. KTU questions often test this with a numerical example.

### 4.3 End-to-End System View (Transmitter → Channel → Receiver)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TX[Transmitter]
        xt[Input x of n] --> st[Subtractor]
        pt[Predictor] --> st
        st --> qt[Quantizer]
        qt --> et[Encoder]
        qt --> at[Adder]
        pt --> at
        at --> ft[Reconstructed x hat n]
        ft --> pt
    end
    et --> chn[Noisy Channel]
    chn --> er[Decoder]
    subgraph RX[Receiver]
        er --> ar[Adder]
        pr[Predictor] --> ar
        ar --> xr[Reconstructed x hat n]
        xr --> pr
        xr --> outx[Analog Output]
    end
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Data-Flow at a Single Sampling Instant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sampler Output x of n] --> B[Subtract Prediction x hat p of n]
    B --> C[Compute Error e of n]
    C --> D[Quantize to e q of n]
    D --> E[Transmit e q of n]
    D --> F[Add Prediction to get x hat of n]
    F --> G[Update Predictor State for n plus 1]
    G --> H[Wait for next sample]
    H --> A
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between PCM and DPCM. Why is DPCM preferred for highly correlated signals?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- *PCM quantizes and encodes each sample independently using $b$ bits; DPCM quantizes and encodes the **difference** between the actual sample and a predicted value derived from previous samples (1 mark).
- *In PCM, the quantizer handles a signal of variance $\sigma_x^2$; in DPCM, it handles a much smaller variance $\sigma_e^2 = \sigma_x^2 (1-\rho^2)$ (1 mark).
- *Hence for highly correlated signals ($\rho$ close to 1), $\sigma_e^2 \ll \sigma_x^2$, fewer bits are needed for the same SQNR, yielding bit-rate compression. DPCM is therefore preferred (1 mark).

---

#### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State and explain the concept of prediction gain in a DPCM system. What is its range?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- *Prediction Gain is defined as the ratio of the variance of the input signal to the variance of the prediction error: $G_p = \sigma_x^2 / \sigma_e^2$ (1 mark).
- *For an optimal first-order predictor, $G_p = 1/(1-\rho^2)$ where $\rho$ is the correlation coefficient. Physically, $G_p$ measures the SNR improvement (in dB) of DPCM over PCM at the same bit-rate: $G_p \text{(dB)} = -10 \log_{10}(1-\rho^2)$ (1 mark).
- *Range: $1 \le G_p < \infty$, i.e., 0 dB $\le G_p \text{(dB)} < \infty$. When $\rho = 0$, $G_p = 1$ (0 dB), DPCM degenerates to PCM (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **transmitter and receiver of a DPCM system**. Discuss the role of the predictor and why reconstruction is performed locally at the transmitter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a first-order DPCM system with input correlation coefficient $\rho = 0.95$ and a uniform 8-bit quantizer, compute the **prediction gain, the SQNR in dB, and the percentage reduction in bit rate** compared to standard PCM for the same voice quality. Take voice bandwidth $W = 3.4$ kHz. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) Block Diagram and Explanation (7 marks):**

- *Block diagram (3 marks)* — Already provided in Section 4.1 and 4.2 of these notes. Key blocks the student must draw and label: Sampler, Predictor, Subtractor, Quantizer, Encoder, Adder (local reconstruction), and the feedback path with unit delay $z^{-1}$. The receiver block diagram must show: Decoder, Predictor, Adder, Output and the matching $z^{-1}$ feedback.
- *Role of predictor (2 marks)* — It generates $\hat{x}_p(n) = a_1 \hat{x}(n-1)$ from previously reconstructed samples. It exploits the temporal correlation of the source to produce a small-variance error signal. The same predictor must exist at the receiver to reconstruct the signal from the transmitted error.
- *Why local reconstruction is essential (2 marks)* — If the predictor used the *original* $x(n-1)$, the receiver would need the original signal to decode, which is impossible. By using the *reconstructed* $\hat{x}(n-1)$ at the transmitter, the receiver — given identical $a_i$ — can generate the *same* prediction from the transmitted error alone. This closes the loop and prevents the accumulation of quantization noise in the predictor state.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

- *Prediction Gain (2 marks):*

$$G_p = \frac{1}{1 - \rho^2} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.9025} = \frac{1}{0.0975} \approx 10.256$$

In dB: $G_p \text{(dB)} = 10 \log_{10} 10.256 \approx 10.11$ dB.

- *SQNR of DPCM (2 marks):*

$$\mathrm{SQNR}_{\text{DPCM}} = 4.8 + 6.02 b + 10 \log_{10} G_p = 4.8 + 6.02(8) + 10.11 \approx 63.07 \text{ dB}$$

- *Bit-rate comparison (3 marks):* Standard PCM with the same SQNR (~63 dB) requires a bit-rate that, from the PCM formula $\mathrm{SQNR} = 4.8 + 6.02 b$, gives:

$$b_{\text{PCM}} = \frac{63.07 - 4.8}{6.02} \approx 9.68 \;\Rightarrow\; 10 \text{ bits per sample}$$

DPCM achieves the same SQNR with $b_{\text{DPCM}} = 8$ bits, so the bit rate of PCM is:

$$R_{b,\text{PCM}} = 10 \times 2W = 10 \times 6800 = 68 \text{ kbps}$$

DPCM bit rate: $R_{b,\text{DPCM}} = 8 \times 6800 = 54.4$ kbps.

- *Percentage reduction in bit rate:*

$$\text{Reduction} = \frac{68 - 54.4}{68} \times 100\% = 20\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often compute $G_p$ but forget the dB conversion before substituting into the SQNR formula. The $10 \log_{10} G_p$ term is *already in dB* — do not convert $G_p$ to dB twice.
> - When asked for "same quality" PCM, compute the *equivalent* PCM bits using $b_{\text{PCM}} = b_{\text{DPCM}} + 10 \log_{10} G_p / 6.02$. Do not blindly assume 8 bits for both.
> - Sampling rate for $W = 3.4$ kHz voice is $f_s = 8$ kHz (using the telephony convention $f_s = 2 \times 4$ kHz guard-band). Some students use $6.8$ kHz, which is the Nyquist rate, not the *standard* rate.

---

#### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the **optimal first-order predictor coefficient** for a DPCM system using the mean-square-error criterion. Show that the minimum mean-square error equals $\sigma_x^2 (1 - \rho^2)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A DPCM system uses a 4-bit uniform quantizer with $x_{\max} = 2$ V. If the input correlation coefficient is $\rho = 0.8$, calculate the **quantization step size, the quantization noise variance, the prediction gain, and the output SQNR in dB**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) Derivation of Optimal Predictor Coefficient (7 marks):**

- *Step 1 (1 mark):* Define the prediction error $e(n) = x(n) - a_1 x(n-1)$ and the cost function $J(a_1) = E[e^2(n)]$.
- *Step 2 (1 mark):* Expand $J(a_1) = R(0) - 2 a_1 R(1) + a_1^2 R(0)$.
- *Step 3 (1 mark):* Differentiate: $dJ/da_1 = -2R(1) + 2 a_1 R(0) = 0$.
- *Step 4 (1 mark):* Solve to get $a_1^{\text{opt}} = R(1)/R(0) = \rho$.
- *Step 5 (1 mark):* Substitute back: $J_{\min} = R(0) - 2\rho^2 R(0) + \rho^2 R(0) = R(0)(1 - \rho^2)$.
- *Step 6 (2 marks):* Since the input is zero-mean, $R(0) = \sigma_x^2$ and $J_{\min} = \sigma_e^2$, hence $\sigma_e^2 = \sigma_x^2 (1 - \rho^2)$, which completes the derivation.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

- *Quantization step size (1 mark):* With $b = 4$, $L = 16$:

$$\Delta = \frac{2 x_{\max}}{L} = \frac{2 \times 2}{16} = 0.25 \text{ V}$$

- *Quantization noise variance (1 mark):*

$$\sigma_q^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} = \frac{0.0625}{12} \approx 5.208 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}^2$$

- *Prediction gain (2 marks):*

$$G_p = \frac{1}{1 - \rho^2} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.64} = \frac{1}{0.36} \approx 2.778$$

In dB: $G_p \text{(dB)} = 10 \log_{10} 2.778 \approx 4.43$ dB.

- *Output SQNR (3 marks):*

$$\mathrm{SQNR}_{\text{DPCM,dB}} = 4.8 + 6.02(4) + 4.43 = 4.8 + 24.08 + 4.43 = 33.31 \text{ dB}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - A common mistake is to compute $G_p$ in dB as $10 \log_{10}(1-\rho^2)$ instead of $-10 \log_{10}(1-\rho^2)$. The negative sign matters!
> - The $4.8$ dB constant assumes the quantizer's full-scale $x_{\max}$ is set so that $\sigma_x \approx x_{\max}$ (i.e., loading factor $\eta = 1$). If the problem states a different $\sigma_x$ or specifies a *loading factor*, you must apply $10 \log_{10}(3/(4\eta^2))$ instead.
> - For $b \ge 6$, uniform quantization is acceptable. For $b < 6$ (as in this Q with $b = 4$), the assumption of uniform noise distribution begins to fail, so the numerical answer is a *first-order estimate*. State this in your solution.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DPCM is a closed-loop predictive coder** that transmits the quantized *difference* between the actual sample and a predicted value.
- The **predictor operates on reconstructed samples** (locally at the transmitter) so that the receiver, given only the transmitted error, can replicate the same prediction.
- The **prediction error variance** for an optimal first-order predictor is $\sigma_e^2 = \sigma_x^2 (1 - \rho^2)$.
- **Prediction Gain** $G_p = 1/(1-\rho^2)$, equivalently $-10 \log_{10}(1-\rho^2)$ dB.
- **DPCM SQNR** $= 4.8 + 6.02 b + 10 \log_{10} G_p$ dB, which exceeds PCM by exactly $10 \log_{10} G_p$ dB at the same bit-rate.
- **Channel errors propagate** through the predictor state; this is a key drawback of DPCM relative to PCM.
- **Slope-overload** occurs when the signal changes too rapidly for the quantizer step size $\Delta$ to track; **granular noise** occurs when the signal is nearly constant and the quantizer toggles around zero.
- **Stability of the predictor** requires $\sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert a_i \vert \le 1$.
- **Delta Modulation (DM)** is the special case of DPCM with $b = 1$ and a zero-order predictor.
- **Higher-order predictors** (N = 2, 3) give larger $G_p$ for highly structured signals (e.g., voiced speech) but require solving the Yule-Walker / Wiener-Hopf equations.
- **Adaptive DPCM (ADPCM)** further scales $\Delta$ with the input variance, giving 32-kbps voice with 64-kbps PCM quality — used in ITU-T G.726 and in the recording formats of early digital answering machines.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick last-minute checklist for the exam hall:**
> - Draw the transmitter *with the feedback path* — partial-credit loss is common when students forget the local reconstruction adder.
> - State the formula $G_p = 1/(1-\rho^2)$ *before* you use it in SQNR.
> - Always quote the final answer in **dB** when the question asks for SQNR.
> - If a numerical value of $\rho$ is given, compute $G_p$ in *both* linear and dB forms.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Delta Modulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT502 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Delta Modulation # Delta Modulation (DM) — Module 2 Notes ## 1. Core Technical Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Delta Modulation (DM)** is a **1-bit (binary) differential pulse code modulation (DPCM)** technique in which only **one bit per sample** is transmitted. This bit indicates whether the current sample of the input message signal is **greater than (1)** or **less than (0)** the previous reconstructed sample by more than a fixed step size **Δ**. In formal KTU terminology, DM is a subclass of **Differential Pulse Code Modulation (DPCM)** where the quantizer has only **two quantization levels** and the predictor is a single-sample delay line (i.e., a first-order predictor with coefficient = 1). **Block diagram of a DM system (Transmitter):** $$ \begin{aligned} e(t) &= m(t) - m_q(t) \\ b_k &= \text{sign}\{e(t_k)\} \;\in\; \{-1, +1\} \quad \text{(mapped to bits 0, 1)} \\ m_q(t) &= m_q(t - T_s) + b_k \,\Delta \end{aligned} $$ **Block diagram of a DM Receiver:** $$ \hat{m}(t) = m_q(t) = \sum_{k} b_k \,\Delta \cdot u(t - kT_s) $$ Where: - $m(t)$ = input analog message signal - $m_q(t)$ = staircase approximation (reconstructed signal) at the transmitter - $e(t)$ = error signal (difference) - $b_k$ = transmitted bit at the $k^{th}$ sampling instant - $T_s$ = sampling period - $\Delta$ = **step size** (a fixed positive constant) - $u(\cdot)$ = unit step function ## 2. Intuitive Overview (The "Plain English" Analogy) > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Lighthouse Keeper and the Tide:** > Imagine a lighthouse keeper watching the tide level every second.
