Instead of writing down the exact tide height (a large number), he simply shouts **"UP"** if the tide rose compared to his last memory, or **"DOWN"** if it fell. Over time, the receiver at another lighthouse applies those UP/DOWN commands using a **fixed step** (say, 1 inch) and reconstructs a reasonable approximation of the tide. > - **The step size $\Delta$** is the 1-inch lever. > - **The transmitted bit** is just the direction. > - **The receiver** just adds or subtracts $\Delta$ to its current estimate. This 1-bit-per-sample approach is what makes DM the **simplest and most bandwidth-efficient** of the pulse-code family — at the cost of accuracy. ## 3. Why Delta Modulation? — Motivation - **PCM** transmits the full quantized amplitude → needs $n$ bits/sample (e.g., 8 bits). - **DPCM** transmits the quantized *difference* → typically 4–6 bits/sample. - **DM** is DPCM pushed to the **extreme**: 1 bit per sample. Hence the bit-rate = $f_s$ bits/sec. For a signal of bandwidth $W$, taking $f_s = 2W$ (Nyquist), the **DM bit rate** is: $$ R_b = 2W \text{ bits/second} $$ > [!TIP] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** Because DM transmits only one bit per sample, it is the most bandwidth-efficient waveform coding scheme — but it suffers from two critical distortions: **Slope Overload** and **Granular Noise**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic The DM system works on a **closed feedback loop**. The transmitter actually contains a local decoder identical to the receiver so that the comparison is always made against the *staircase* approximation, never the original signal. **Step 1 — Sampling:** The input $m(t)$ is sampled at $t = kT_s$ to get $m(kT_s)$. **Step 2 — Comparator:** Compute the error: $$ e(kT_s) = m(kT_s) - m_q((k-1)T_s) $$ **Step 3 — 1-bit Quantizer (Signum function):** $$ b_k = \text{sgn}\big[e(kT_s)\big] = \begin{cases} +1 & \text{if } e(kT_s) \geq 0 \\ -1 & \text{if } e(kT_s) < 0 \end{cases} $$ **Step 4 — Local Decoder (Accumulator):** Update the staircase: $$ m_q(kT_s) = m_q((k-1)T_s) + b_k \cdot \Delta $$ This is a digital integrator implemented as an accumulator. The output is held (zero-order hold) for one sample period to form the staircase $m_q(t)$. **Step 5 — Transmission:** Send $b_k$ over the channel. **Step 6 — Receiver Reconstruction:** The receiver performs the *same* accumulator operation on the received bit stream, producing $\hat{m}(t)$ — a delayed version of $m_q(t)$. ## 2.2 The Two Inherent Distortions in Delta Modulation > [!IMPORTANT] > These two errors are the **single most tested DM concept** in KTU exams. Master the difference. ### A. Slope Overload Distortion This occurs when the **rate of change of the message signal exceeds the maximum slew rate of the staircase**. The staircase simply **cannot keep up** with the signal. **Condition to avoid slope overload:** $$ \left|\frac{dm(t)}{dt}\right|_{\max} \leq \frac{\Delta}{T_s} = \Delta f_s $$ > Equivalently: > $$ > \Delta \geq T_s \cdot \left|\frac{dm(t)}{dt}\right|_{\max} > $$ **Visual cue:** The staircase has a slope $\Delta / T_s$. If the signal's slope is steeper, the staircase "drifts behind" the signal — visible as a persistent gap. ### B. Granular (Idle-Channel) Noise This occurs when the input signal is **relatively flat or slowly varying**, but the staircase still jumps by $\pm \Delta$ around the signal — like a noisy, jittery fit. **Condition to bound granular noise:** For a randomly varying message, the granular noise power (assuming uniform distribution of error in $[-\Delta, +\Delta]$) is: $$ N_{\text{granular}} = \frac{\Delta^2}{3} $$ ## 2.3 Granularity vs Slope Overload — Trade-off | Distortion Type | Caused by | Direction of fix | Effect on $\Delta$ | |---|---|---|---| | Slope Overload | $\Delta$ **too small** for fast signal | **Increase** $\Delta$ | Larger $\Delta$ reduces slope overload | | Granular Noise | $\Delta$ **too large** for slow signal | **Decrease** $\Delta$ | Smaller $\Delta$ reduces granular noise | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Trap Question:** "Increasing step size eliminates both distortions." — **FALSE.** Increasing $\Delta$ cures slope overload but *worsens* granular noise. This is the **fundamental DM trade-off**, and is exactly why **Adaptive Delta Modulation (ADM)** was invented. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All symbols are KTU-standard. Memorize this table — it covers > 80% of numerical problems in the university exam. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Bit rate of DM | $R_b = f_s = \dfrac{1}{T_s}$ | bits/sec | | 2 | Nyquist sampling rate | $f_s \geq 2W$ | Hz, $W$ = signal bandwidth | | 3 | Max signal slope (no slope overload) | $\left|\dot{m}(t)\right|_{\max} \leq \dfrac{\Delta}{T_s}$ | V/s | | 4 | Minimum step size (no slope overload) | $\Delta_{\min} = T_s \cdot \left|\dot{m}(t)\right|_{\max}$ | Volts | | 5 | Granular noise power | $N_g = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{3}$ | Watts | | 6 | Granular noise power (with smoothing) | $N_g = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{f_s}{2W}$ | Watts (after low-pass filter) | | 7 | Signal power (sine wave $A\sin\omega t$) | $P_s = \dfrac{A^2}{2}$ | Watts | | 8 | Output SNR (sine input, no slope overload) | $\text{SNR} = \dfrac{3}{8\pi^2} \cdot \dfrac{8W^3}{\Delta^2 f_s^2 \cdot (\text{slope factor})}$ | Linear ratio | | 9 | Sine wave SNR (no slope overload) | $\text{SNR} = \dfrac{3}{8\pi^2} \cdot \dfrac{f_s^3}{W^3}$ | Linear (for $A = \Delta$ critical case) | | 10 | Adaptive step size growth rule | $\Delta_k = \Delta_{k-1} \cdot G$ if slope overload | $G > 1$, growth constant | | 11 | Adaptive step size decay rule | $\Delta_k = \Delta_{k-1} / P$ if granular | $P > 1$, decay constant | > **Notation convention used:** $\left|\dot{m}(t)\right|_{\max}$ means $\left|\dfrac{dm(t)}{dt}\right|_{\max}$. In the formula sheet, vertical bars are written as `\vert` for absolute value to preserve table rendering. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Speech coding in low-bandwidth tactical/military radios** (e.g., older voice scramblers). - **Telemetry systems** in spacecraft and satellites where power and bandwidth are at a premium. - **Submarine cable** early-generation digital speech. - **Building block of DPCM codecs** for video (e.g., ADPCM in modems). - **First step** in understanding more advanced schemes like **CVSDM** (Continuously Variable Slope Delta Modulation) used in Bluetooth early versions and military COMSEC. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Amplitude of a Sine Wave without Slope Overload This is the **most repeated numerical problem** in KTU exams. We derive the largest peak amplitude $A_{\max}$ of a sinusoidal input $m(t) = A\sin(2\pi f_m t)$ that the DM staircase can track without slope overload. **Step 1 — Write the signal and its derivative:** $$ m(t) = A \sin(2\pi f_m t) $$ $$ \frac{dm(t)}{dt} = 2\pi f_m A \cos(2\pi f_m t) $$ **Step 2 — Identify the maximum absolute slope:** $$ \left|\frac{dm(t)}{dt}\right|_{\max} = 2\pi f_m A_{\max} $$ (achieved at $t=0$ where $\cos = 1$). **Step 3 — Apply the slope overload condition:** $$ \left|\frac{dm(t)}{dt}\right|_{\max} \leq \frac{\Delta}{T_s} = \Delta f_s $$ **Step 4 — Substitute:** $$ 2\pi f_m A_{\max} \leq \Delta f_s $$ **Step 5 — Solve for the maximum allowable amplitude:** $$ A_{\max} = \frac{\Delta f_s}{2\pi f_m} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Result:** $A_{\max} = \dfrac{\Delta f_s}{2\pi f_m} = \dfrac{\Delta}{2\pi f_m T_s}$ This is the boundary amplitude. Any signal whose peak exceeds $A_{\max}$ will produce slope overload distortion for the given $\Delta$ and $f_s$. **Step 6 — Worked KTU Example:** > Given: $f_m = 4$ kHz, $f_s = 32$ kHz, $\Delta = 50$ mV. Find $A_{\max}$. > > $$ > A_{\max} = \frac{0.050 \times 32\,000}{2\pi \times 4\,000} = \frac{1600}{25\,132.7} \approx 0.0637 \text{ V} = 63.7 \text{ mV} > $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Granular Noise Power Granular noise arises because the staircase is forced to jump by $\pm\Delta$ even when the signal is nearly flat, so the error $e(t) = m(t) - m_q(t)$ is a random variable approximately **uniformly distributed in $[-\Delta, +\Delta]$**. **Step 1 — Assumption:** The granular error $e_g$ is uniformly distributed: $$ f(e_g) = \frac{1}{2\Delta}, \quad -\Delta \leq e_g \leq +\Delta $$ **Step 2 — Compute the mean square error (power):** $$ N_g = E[e_g^2] = \int_{-\Delta}^{+\Delta} e_g^2 \cdot \frac{1}{2\Delta} \, de_g $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate the integral:** $$ N_g = \frac{1}{2\Delta} \left[ \frac{e_g^3}{3} \right]_{-\Delta}^{+\Delta} = \frac{1}{2\Delta} \cdot \frac{2\Delta^3}{3} = \frac{\Delta^2}{3} $$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Standard Answer:** Granular noise power = $\Delta^2/3$ Watts. This is exact and is the form examiners expect you to write directly. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Output Signal-to-Granular-Noise Ratio for a Sinusoidal Input **Step 1 — Signal power (sine wave of amplitude $A$):** $$ P_s = \frac{A^2}{2} $$ **Step 2 — Granular noise power AFTER the receiver's low-pass filter of bandwidth $W$:** Since the granular noise is roughly white up to $f_s/2$, the fraction that falls inside the message bandwidth $W$ is $2W/f_s$. Therefore: $$ N_g = \frac{\Delta^2}{3} \cdot \frac{2W}{f_s} $$ **Step 3 — SNR:** $$ \left(\frac{S}{N_g}\right) = \frac{A^2/2}{(\Delta^2/3)(2W/f_s)} = \frac{3 A^2 f_s}{4 \Delta^2 W} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute the critical-case amplitude** $A = A_{\max} = \dfrac{\Delta f_s}{2\pi f_m}$ (with $f_m = W$): $$ A^2 = \frac{\Delta^2 f_s^2}{4\pi^2 W^2} $$ **Step 5 — Plug back into SNR:** $$ \left(\frac{S}{N_g}\right) = \frac{3}{4 \Delta^2 W} \cdot \frac{\Delta^2 f_s^2}{4\pi^2 W^2} \cdot f_s = \frac{3 f_s^3}{16 \pi^2 W^3} $$ > **Result:** > $$ > \boxed{\left(\frac{S}{N_g}\right)_{\max} = \frac{3}{16\pi^2} \cdot \left(\frac{f_s}{W}\right)^3 \approx 0.019 \left(\frac{f_s}{W}\right)^3} > $$ **Step 6 — Interpretation in dB:** $$ \left(\frac{S}{N_g}\right)_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\left[\frac{3}{16\pi^2}\right] + 30 \log_{10}\left(\frac{f_s}{W}\right) \approx -12.2 \text{ dB} + 30 \log_{10}\left(\frac{f_s}{W}\right) $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight (often asked in KTU):** Doubling the sampling frequency (oversampling) **improves SNR by 9 dB** — a 30 log rule, because the cubic dependence comes from the cube of $f_s/W$. --- ## 3.4 Adaptive Delta Modulation (ADM) — Algorithm In ADM, the step size is **not fixed** — it adapts to the signal's slope. **Algorithm (one common form, the "CFDM/CVSDM" rule):** Let $b_{k-1}$, $b_{k-2}$ be the last two transmitted bits. $$ \Delta_k = \begin{cases} G \cdot \Delta_{k-1} & \text{if } b_{k-1} = b_{k-2} \quad \text{(slope overload detected)}\\[4pt] \Delta_{k-1} / P & \text{if } b_{k-1} \neq b_{k-2} \quad \text{(granular region detected)} \end{cases} $$ Where $G > 1$ (growth factor, e.g., 1.5) and $P > 1$ (decay factor, e.g., 1.5). - When the same bit repeats, signal is "running away" → **grow** $\Delta$ rapidly. - When bits alternate, signal is flat → **shrink** $\Delta$ to reduce granular noise. This breaks the slope-overload vs granular-noise trade-off by making $\Delta$ **time-varying**. --- ## 3.5 Numerical Problem Bank (KTU-style) **Problem 1:** A DM system uses $\Delta = 100$ mV, $f_s = 64$ kHz to encode a $4$ kHz sine wave. Find (a) the maximum amplitude that avoids slope overload, (b) the granular noise power, and (c) the maximum SNR. **Solution (a):** $$ A_{\max} = \frac{\Delta f_s}{2\pi f_m} = \frac{0.1 \times 64\,000}{2\pi \times 4\,000} = \frac{6400}{25\,133} \approx 0.2546 \text{ V} = 254.6 \text{ mV} $$ **Solution (b):** $$ N_g = \frac{\Delta^2}{3} = \frac{(0.1)^2}{3} = 3.33 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W} = 3.33 \text{ mW} $$ **Solution (c):** $$ \left(\frac{S}{N_g}\right)_{\max} = \frac{3}{16\pi^2}\left(\frac{f_s}{W}\right)^3 = \frac{3}{16\pi^2}(16)^3 = 0.019 \times 4096 \approx 77.82 $$ In dB: $10\log_{10}(77.82) \approx 18.9$ dB. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Delta Modulation Transmitter (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Input m(t)"] --> B["Sampler at fs"] B --> C["Comparator: e k = m k minus mq k minus 1"] C --> D["1-bit Quantizer: sign of e"] D --> E["Output bit stream bk"] D --> F["Accumulator: add or subtract Delta"] F --> G["Zero Order Hold: ZOH"] G --> H["Staircase mq(t)"] H -.-> C E --> I["Channel to Receiver"] ``` ## 4.2 Delta Modulation Receiver (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Received bit stream bk"] --> B["Accumulator: add or subtract Delta"] B --> C["Zero Order Hold: ZOH"] C --> D["Low Pass Filter: LPF"] D --> E["Reconstructed mhat(t)"] B -.-> F["Staircase estimate"] F -.-> C ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture of DM System (Tx + Rx) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TX["Transmitter Side"] direction TB IN["Analog Input m(t)"] --> CMP{"Comparator"} LDC["Local Decoder
Accumulator plus ZOH
produces mq(t)"] --> CMP CMP --> Q["1-bit Quantizer
Output: 0 or 1"] Q --> CH["Binary Channel
Rb = fs bits per sec"] end CH --> RX["Receiver Side"] subgraph RX["Receiver Side"] direction TB RXIN["Received Bits bk"] --> ACC["Accumulator
add or subtract Delta"] ACC --> ZH["Zero Order Hold"] ZH --> LPF["Low Pass Filter
Bandwidth W"] LPF --> OUT["Analog Output mhat(t)"] end ``` ## 4.4 Adaptive Delta Modulation (ADM) Step-Size Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Compute Error e k"] --> B{"Is error positive?"} B -- Yes --> C["bk = 1"] B -- No --> D["bk = 0"] C --> E{"Did previous bit bk-1 equal bk?"} D --> E E -- Yes, repeated --> F["Slope Overload Region
Multiply Delta by G greater than 1"] E -- No, alternating --> G["Granular Noise Region
Divide Delta by P greater than 1"] F --> H["New Delta k ready for next sample"] G --> H ``` ## 4.5 Slope Overload vs Granular Noise — Visual Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph S1["Slope Overload Case"] direction LR SA["Signal m(t)
Steep ramp"] --> SB["Staircase mq(t)
Cannot keep up
Persistent gap below signal"] end subgraph S2["Granular Noise Case"] direction LR GA["Signal m(t)
Nearly flat"] --> GB["Staircase mq(t)
Jitters around signal
Error bounded by Delta"] end ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!NOTE] > All questions below follow the KTU 2024 ESE pattern: Part A (3 marks, short answer) and Part B (14 marks, with internal choice and sub-parts of 7 + 7 marks). --- ## PART A — 3-Mark Questions ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023] **What is Delta Modulation? Why is it called a 1-bit DPCM system?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Delta Modulation (DM) is a differential pulse code modulation scheme in which the difference between successive samples of the input signal is encoded using **only one bit** per sample. The single bit indicates whether the staircase approximation should be incremented or decremented by a fixed step size $\Delta$. It is called a **1-bit DPCM** because: 1. It is a special case of DPCM (which transmits the quantized *difference* of samples), AND 2. The quantizer has only **two levels** $\{-1, +1\}$, so the output is always a single binary digit per sample, giving a bit rate equal to the sampling rate $R_b = f_s$ bits/s. **(1 mark each point, 1 mark for the bit-rate statement.)** --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] **Distinguish between slope overload distortion and granular noise in Delta Modulation.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Slope Overload | Granular Noise | |---|---|---| | Cause | Signal slope exceeds $\Delta/T_s$ | Signal is too flat; staircase jitters | | Remedy | Increase $\Delta$ | Decrease $\Delta$ | | Visible as | Staircase lagging behind signal | Staircase oscillating around flat signal | | Error magnitude | Can be very large | Bounded within $\pm\Delta$ | (1½ marks for each distinction with a clear 2-bullet comparison; 1 mark for the remedy difference.) --- ## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > Common mistakes that cost marks in DM questions: > 1. **Forgetting to convert kHz to Hz** in $\Delta f_s$ products — a single dimensional slip loses 1 full mark. > 2. **Confusing the formula** $A_{\max} = \Delta f_s / (2\pi f_m)$ with $A_{\max} = \Delta / T_s$. The latter is *not* an amplitude — it is the maximum *slope*, not amplitude. Mixing them is a classic 2-mark deduction. > 3. **Stating granular noise as $\Delta^2$** instead of $\Delta^2/3$ — losing 1 mark for the missing division. > 4. **Not specifying "no slope overload"** when quoting the SNR formula — the cubic law is only valid *below* the slope overload threshold. Examiners deduct 1 mark if the assumption is not stated. > 5. **In ADM problems, students often forget the rule** that *bit repetition* indicates slope overload. Wrong direction → entire 7-mark sub-part is marked wrong. --- ### QUESTION A — 14 Marks #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q. Module 2]** > Explain the generation and detection of Delta Modulated signals with neat block diagrams. Derive the expression for the maximum signal amplitude that can be tracked without slope overload distortion. **Model Solution:** **Block Diagram Description (3 marks):** The **DM transmitter** consists of a sampler, a comparator (subtractor), a 1-bit quantizer (signum function), and a local decoder. The local decoder is an accumulator followed by a zero-order hold (ZOH) that produces the staircase approximation $m_q(t)$. This $m_q(t)$ is fed back to the comparator's negative input. The comparator computes $e(kT_s) = m(kT_s) - m_q((k-1)T_s)$. The 1-bit quantizer outputs $b_k = +1$ if $e \geq 0$ and $b_k = -1$ otherwise. The bit stream $b_k$ is sent over the channel. The **DM receiver** consists of an accumulator (identical to the local decoder), a ZOH, and a low-pass filter of bandwidth $W$. The accumulator adds $\pm \Delta$ for each received bit, and the LPF smooths the staircase to recover $\hat{m}(t)$. (Draw the block diagrams as in Section 4.1 and 4.2 above for full credit.) **Derivation (4 marks):** For a sinusoidal input $m(t) = A\sin(2\pi f_m t)$: $$ \frac{dm(t)}{dt} = 2\pi f_m A \cos(2\pi f_m t) $$ $$ \left|\frac{dm(t)}{dt}\right|_{\max} = 2\pi f_m A_{\max} $$ The maximum slope the staircase can produce is $\Delta / T_s = \Delta f_s$. To avoid slope overload: $$ 2\pi f_m A_{\max} \leq \Delta f_s $$ $$ \boxed{A_{\max} = \frac{\Delta f_s}{2\pi f_m}} $$ This is the **maximum amplitude that can be encoded without slope overload distortion**. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks > A 4 kHz sine wave of peak amplitude 1 V is transmitted using a Delta Modulation system with $f_s = 32$ kHz. > (i) Determine the minimum step size $\Delta$ required to avoid slope overload. > (ii) Compute the granular noise power. > (iii) Find the output SNR in dB (assume receiver LPF bandwidth = 4 kHz). **Model Solution:** **(i) Minimum step size (3 marks):** $$ A_{\max} = \frac{\Delta f_s}{2\pi f_m} \implies \Delta_{\min} = \frac{2\pi f_m A_{\max}}{f_s} $$ Substituting $f_m = 4000$ Hz, $A_{\max} = 1$ V, $f_s = 32\,000$ Hz: $$ \Delta_{\min} = \frac{2\pi \times 4000 \times 1}{32\,000} = \frac{25\,133}{32\,000} \approx 0.7854 \text{ V} = 785.4 \text{ mV} $$ **(ii) Granular noise power (2 marks):** $$ N_g = \frac{\Delta^2}{3} = \frac{(0.7854)^2}{3} = \frac{0.6168}{3} \approx 0.2056 \text{ W} $$ **(iii) Output SNR (2 marks):** $$ P_s = \frac{A^2}{2} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5 \text{ W} $$ $$ \left(\frac{S}{N_g}\right) = \frac{0.5}{0.2056} \approx 2.432 $$ $$ \text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10\log_{10}(2.432) \approx 3.86 \text{ dB} $$ --- ### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) #### Part (a) — 7 Marks > With a neat block diagram, explain **Adaptive Delta Modulation (ADM)**. How does it overcome the slope overload vs granular noise trade-off of conventional DM? **Model Solution:** **Block Diagram (3 marks):** ADM is identical to DM except that the step size $\Delta$ in the local decoder (and receiver accumulator) is **variable**, controlled by an adaptive logic block that examines the recent bit history. **Algorithm (3 marks):** - Maintain step size $\Delta_k$ that adapts sample-by-sample. - If the last two bits are equal ($b_{k-1} = b_{k-2}$), the signal is steep → **slope overload region** → multiply step size by growth factor $G > 1$. - If the bits alternate ($b_{k-1} \neq b_{k-2}$), the signal is flat → **granular region** → divide step size by decay factor $P > 1$. $$ \Delta_k = \begin{cases} G \cdot \Delta_{k-1} & b_{k-1} = b_{k-2} \\ \Delta_{k-1} / P & b_{k-1} \neq b_{k-2} \end{cases} $$ **Overcoming the trade-off (1 mark):** In conventional DM, a single fixed $\Delta$ must simultaneously prevent slope overload (need large $\Delta$) and limit granular noise (need small $\Delta$). ADM uses a **large $\Delta$ on signal slopes** and a **small $\Delta$ on flat regions**, dynamically optimizing both. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks > Compare Delta Modulation with PCM and DPCM in terms of bit rate, complexity, and SNR. A DM system has $f_s = 64$ kHz, $\Delta = 0.5$ V, and signal bandwidth $W = 3.5$ kHz. Compute the maximum achievable SNR in dB at the output. **Model Solution:** **Comparison Table (3 marks):** | Parameter | PCM | DPCM | DM | |---|---|---|---| | Bits/sample | $n$ (e.g., 8) | 4–6 | 1 | | Bit rate | $n f_s$ | $(4 \text{ to } 6) f_s$ | $f_s$ | | Complexity | High | Medium | Lowest | | SNR | Highest | High | Moderate | | Distortions | Quantization noise | Quantization of diff | Slope overload + granular | **Numerical SNR (4 marks):** Using the cubic SNR formula valid when the signal amplitude is at the slope-overload limit: $$ \left(\frac{S}{N_g}\right)_{\max} = \frac{3}{16\pi^2}\left(\frac{f_s}{W}\right)^3 $$ Compute $f_s / W = 64\,000 / 3500 \approx 18.286$: $$ \left(\frac{f_s}{W}\right)^3 = (18.286)^3 \approx 6113 $$ $$ \left(\frac{S}{N_g}\right)_{\max} = \frac{3 \times 6113}{16\pi^2} = \frac{18\,339}{157.91} \approx 116.13 $$ $$ \text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10\log_{10}(116.13) \approx 20.65 \text{ dB} $$ > **Note:** $\Delta$ cancels out of the *maximum* SNR expression — the maximum achievable SNR is governed only by the oversampling ratio $f_s / W$. This is a key KTU insight. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Delta Modulation** - **DM = 1-bit DPCM.** Bit rate $R_b = f_s$ bits/sec. - **Transmitter** = Sampler + Comparator + 1-bit Quantizer + Local Decoder (accumulator + ZOH). - **Receiver** = Accumulator + ZOH + Low-Pass Filter. - **Two distortions:** - **Slope Overload** → staircase too slow → increase $\Delta$. - **Granular Noise** → staircase too jittery → decrease $\Delta$. - **Slope overload condition:** $A_{\max} = \dfrac{\Delta f_s}{2\pi f_m}$ (for sine input). - **Granular noise power:** $N_g = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{3}$ (unfiltered), $\dfrac{\Delta^2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{2W}{f_s}$ (after LPF). - **Maximum SNR (sine, no slope overload):** $\left(\dfrac{S}{N_g}\right)_{\max} = \dfrac{3}{16\pi^2}\left(\dfrac{f_s}{W}\right)^3 \approx 0.019\left(\dfrac{f_s}{W}\right)^3$. - **Doubling $f_s$ → +9 dB SNR** (cubic / 30-log rule). - **ADM algorithm:** $\Delta_k$ grows ($G$) on bit repetition, decays ($1/P$) on bit alternation — resolves the slope vs granular trade-off. - **PCM vs DPCM vs DM:** Bits/sample = $n$ vs 4–6 vs 1; complexity = High vs Medium vs Low. - **Standard KTU examples to master:** (1) $A_{\max}$ from given $\Delta, f_s, f_m$. (2) Granular noise $N_g$. (3) Output SNR in dB. (4) ADM adaptation logic.

topics.title: Slope Overload
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT502 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Topic:** Slope Overload

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Slope Overload** is a fundamental form of **distortion (error)** that occurs in a **Delta Modulation (DM)** system when the **rate of change of the input analog message signal $m(t)$ exceeds the maximum tracking slope capability of the staircase approximation waveform generated by the DM encoder.

In formal mathematical terms, slope overload occurs whenever:

$$\left| \frac{dm(t)}{dt} \right| > \frac{\Delta}{T_s} = \Delta f_s = \sigma$$

where:
- $\Delta$ = **Step size** of the staircase waveform (volts)
- $T_s$ = **Sampling period** (seconds)
- $f_s$ = **Sampling frequency** ($1/T_s$) in Hz
- $\sigma$ = **Maximum slope tracking capability** of the DM system (V/s)

When this condition is violated, the reconstructed waveform **fails to keep up** with the rapid variations of the input, producing a rounded, lagging approximation that introduces **unrecoverable signal-dependent distortion**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 2):** Slope overload is studied under *Delta Modulation and Adaptive Delta Modulation* as a primary limitation of constant-step-size DM, alongside *granular noise* and *slope overload distortion*.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are an artist trying to **trace a moving rabbit** using a pen that can only move in **fixed vertical jumps of 1 cm (step size $\Delta$)** every second (sampling period $T_s$). 

- If the rabbit walks **slowly** on flat ground, you can keep up easily.
- If the rabbit **suddenly sprints uphill** (rapidly changing signal), your pen can only step up 1 cm per second, but the rabbit is climbing 5 cm per second. Your drawing will **lag behind**, flattening out the rabbit's actual path.

That **flattened, lagging trail** is exactly what **slope overload** looks like on a DM output. The stair-step waveform simply cannot rise or fall fast enough to follow the steep slope of the message signal.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** Slope Overload = "Stairs too small / too slow to climb a steep hill." The hill is the rapidly changing $m(t)$; the stairs are the DM staircase of step $\Delta$ and step-time $T_s$.

---

## 1.3 Why Slope Overload Matters (Engineering Significance)

- **Severe signal-dependent distortion** that is **not reversible** at the receiver.
- Sets the **upper limit on input signal amplitude** for a given $\Delta$ and $f_s$.
- Directly determines the **dynamic range** and **SNR performance** of any DM system.
- Motivates the use of **Adaptive Delta Modulation (ADM)**, where $\Delta$ is dynamically enlarged to track steep slopes.

> [!NOTE]
> Standard metrics to remember: **Step size $\Delta$ (V)**, **Sampling frequency $f_s$ (Hz)**, **Slope capability $\sigma = \Delta f_s$ (V/s)**, **Signal frequency $f_m$ (Hz)**, **Message amplitude $A_m$ (V)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic

The Delta Modulation encoder works as a **1-bit quantizer with feedback**:

1. The input message $m(t)$ is compared with a locally generated staircase approximation $m_q(t)$.
2. The comparator outputs a **single bit**: `+1` if $m(t) > m_q(t)$, else `-1`.
3. The accumulator adds or subtracts a **fixed step $\Delta$** every $T_s$ seconds, producing $m_q(t+1) = m_q(t) \pm \Delta$.
4. The maximum slope this staircase can physically achieve is **one step per sample period**:

$$\text{Max slope} = \frac{\Delta}{T_s} = \Delta f_s$$

5. If the **derivative of the message signal exceeds this maximum slope**, the staircase waveform cannot catch up — this is the onset of **slope overload**.

---

## 2.2 Critical Conditions for Slope Overload

### 2.2.1 General Condition (Time Domain)

$$\boxed{\left| \frac{dm(t)}{dt} \right|_{\max} > \Delta f_s}$$

### 2.2.2 Condition for Sinusoidal Test Signal

For the standard KTU benchmark signal $m(t) = A_m \sin(2\pi f_m t)$:

$$\frac{dm(t)}{dt} = 2\pi f_m A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$$

The **maximum slope** of the sinusoid occurs at the zero crossings:

$$\left| \frac{dm(t)}{dt} \right|_{\max} = 2\pi f_m A_m$$

Therefore, **slope overload occurs when**:

$$2\pi f_m A_m > \Delta f_s$$

### 2.2.3 Maximum Permissible Input Amplitude (Critical KTU Formula!)

Rearranging the inequality to find the **maximum amplitude $A_m$** that the DM system can transmit without slope overload:

$$\boxed{A_m \leq \frac{\Delta f_s}{2\pi f_m}}$$

This is the **most-tested formula in KTU exams** — it links signal amplitude, step size, sampling rate, and signal frequency in one equation.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | $\sigma = \Delta f_s = \dfrac{\Delta}{T_s}$ | Maximum tracking slope of DM | V/s |
| 2 | $\left\lvert \dfrac{dm(t)}{dt}\right\rvert_{\max} > \sigma$ | Slope overload condition (general) | V/s |
| 3 | $A_m \leq \dfrac{\Delta f_s}{2\pi f_m}$ | Max amplitude before slope overload (sinusoid) | V |
| 4 | $A_m \leq \dfrac{\sigma}{2\pi f_m}$ | Alternative form of (3) | V |
| 5 | $f_s \geq \dfrac{2\pi f_m A_m}{\Delta}$ | Min sampling rate to avoid slope overload | Hz |
| 6 | $\Delta_{\min} = \dfrac{2\pi f_m A_m}{f_s}$ | Min step size to track signal | V |
| 7 | Slope overload error $e_{so} = m(t) - m_q(t)$ | Residual error due to overload | V |
| 8 | Two conditions for acceptable DM | (i) $\Delta f_s \geq \left\lvert \dot{m}\right\rvert_{\max}$ (no overload), (ii) $\Delta \leq 2\sigma_m$ (low granular noise) | Mixed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on table notation:** All absolute values are written using `\lvert` and `\rvert` (rendered as `|`) to preserve markdown table integrity. Do NOT write bare `|x|` inside a table cell.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Speech and audio codecs:** Determines the choice of $\Delta$ and $f_s$ in low-bit-rate digital voice systems.
- **Radar and telemetry:** Prevents loss of fast transients in signals with sudden amplitude jumps.
- **Foundation for ADM design:** Slope overload is the *primary motivator* for Adaptive Delta Modulation, where $\Delta$ is increased during steep slopes and reduced during low-activity periods to simultaneously combat slope overload and granular noise.
- **Bandwidth-budget planning:** Provides the link between $f_s$ and allowable input frequency/amplitude — crucial in KTU problems on DM system design.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Permissible Amplitude for a Sinusoidal Signal

**Given:**
- Test signal: $m(t) = A_m \sin(2\pi f_m t)$
- Step size: $\Delta$
- Sampling frequency: $f_s$
- Sampling period: $T_s = 1/f_s$

**To Find:** Maximum amplitude $A_m$ that avoids slope overload.

### Step 1 — Write the message signal derivative

$$m(t) = A_m \sin(2\pi f_m t)$$

Differentiating with respect to $t$:

$$\frac{dm(t)}{dt} = A_m \cdot 2\pi f_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$$

### Step 2 — Identify the maximum slope of the message

Since $\cos(\theta)$ has a maximum absolute value of **1** (attained at $\theta = 0, \pi, 2\pi, \dots$):

$$\left| \frac{dm(t)}{dt} \right|_{\max} = A_m \cdot 2\pi f_m \cdot 1 = 2\pi f_m A_m$$

**Explanation:** The steepest part of a sine wave is at its **zero crossings** (where it crosses the horizontal axis most rapidly). This is intuitively correct — at the peaks, the slope is momentarily zero.

### Step 3 — State the slope overload condition

The DM staircase can only produce a maximum slope of:

$$\sigma = \frac{\Delta}{T_s} = \Delta f_s$$

To avoid slope overload:

$$\left| \frac{dm(t)}{dt} \right|_{\max} \leq \sigma$$

$$2\pi f_m A_m \leq \Delta f_s$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the maximum amplitude

Rearranging for $A_m$:

$$A_m \leq \frac{\Delta f_s}{2\pi f_m}$$

$$\boxed{A_{m,\max} = \frac{\Delta f_s}{2\pi f_m}}$$

### Step 5 — Units verification

$$\frac{[\text{V}] \cdot [\text{Hz}]}{[\text{Hz}] \cdot [\text{radians}]} \rightarrow \text{Volts} \checkmark$$

(Recall that $2\pi f_m$ has units of rad/s, and the radians are dimensionless, so the final result is in Volts.)

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Sampling Frequency to Avoid Slope Overload

**Given:** Same sinusoidal signal with amplitude $A_m$ and frequency $f_m$.

**To Find:** Minimum $f_s$ such that no slope overload occurs.

Starting from the condition:

$$2\pi f_m A_m \leq \Delta f_s$$

Solving for $f_s$:

$$f_s \geq \frac{2\pi f_m A_m}{\Delta}$$

$$\boxed{f_{s,\min} = \frac{2\pi f_m A_m}{\Delta}}$$

### Step 3 — Units check

$$\frac{[\text{rad/s}] \cdot [\text{V}]}{[\text{V}]} = [\text{Hz}] \checkmark$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Slope Overload Distortion Power (for Sinusoid)

**Given:** Message signal $m(t) = A_m \sin(2\pi f_m t)$, and assume slope overload is occurring.

**To Find:** Average slope overload distortion power $\overline{e_{so}^2}$.

### Step 1 — Define the overload error

$$e_{so}(t) = m(t) - m_q(t)$$

During overload, the staircase lags by an amount that grows linearly with time. Modeling the worst-case error as a triangular wave with peak $\Delta$ (one full step) over the overload region, the mean-square error over a cycle of period $T_m = 1/f_m$ is:

$$\overline{e_{so}^2} = \frac{1}{T_m}\int_0^{T_m} e_{so}^2(t)\, dt$$

For a triangular overload waveform of peak $\Delta$ over an overload fraction $\rho$ of the cycle:

$$\overline{e_{so}^2} \approx \frac{\rho \Delta^2}{3}$$

A commonly used KTU form (assuming overload during the steep portions of the sinusoid):

$$\boxed{\overline{e_{so}^2} \approx \frac{\Delta^2}{3} \left(1 - \frac{2\pi f_m A_m}{\Delta f_s}\right) \quad \text{for } 2\pi f_m A_m < \Delta f_s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The form shown is the **standard textbook expression** for the slope-overload component of quantization noise in DM. When the inequality is satisfied with margin, this term is small; as the limit is approached, it grows rapidly.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Slope Overload vs Granular Noise

| Property | Slope Overload | Granular Noise |
|----------|----------------|----------------|
| **Cause** | $\vert \dot{m}(t)\vert_{\max} > \Delta f_s$ | $\Delta$ too large when $\vert \dot{m}(t)\vert$ is small |
| **Nature of error** | Staircase **cannot keep up** (under-tracking) | Staircase **jitters around** flat portions (over-tracking) |
| **Signal dependency** | Occurs in **rapidly changing** regions | Occurs in **slow / constant** regions |
| **Remedy** | Increase $\Delta$ or $f_s$ | Decrease $\Delta$ (use ADM) |
| **Error type** | **Slope-dependent** distortion | **Step-size dependent** noise |
| **Effect of increasing $\Delta$** | Reduces slope overload | Increases granular noise |
| **Trade-off** | $\Delta$ must be **large enough** to avoid overload | $\Delta$ must be **small enough** to avoid granular noise |
| **Solution** | **Adaptive Delta Modulation** (variable $\Delta$) | **Adaptive Delta Modulation** (variable $\Delta$) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Delta Modulation Encoder — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Message m of t] --> B[Comparator +]
    C[Staircase Approximation mq of t] --> B
    B --> D[1 bit Quantizer Output bk]
    D --> E[Encoder Output to Channel]
    D --> F[Accumulator Integrator with Step Delta]
    F --> G[Step Generator +-Delta]
    G --> C
    H[Slope Overload Region Detected when dm by dt exceeds Delta fs] -.-> A
    H -.-> G
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The dashed feedback line `H` represents the **slope overload condition** being violated. The accumulator (integrator) cannot produce a slope greater than $\Delta f_s$, so when the input's derivative exceeds this, the comparator output saturates and the staircase falls behind.

---

## 4.2 Slope Overload Occurrence Logic — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Input m of t enters DM encoder] --> S1[Compute dm by dt at sample k]
    S1 --> S2{Check absolute slope}
    S2 -->|dm by dt less than Delta fs| S3[Normal Tracking: mq follows m of t]
    S2 -->|dm by dt equals Delta fs| S4[Critical Edge: marginal tracking]
    S2 -->|dm by dt greater than Delta fs| S5[Slope Overload: mq lags behind m of t]
    S3 --> S6[Receiver reconstructs faithfully]
    S4 --> S7[Borderline: small residual error]
    S5 --> S8[Slope overload distortion e so added to output]
    S8 --> S9[Remedy Options: Increase Delta, Increase fs, or switch to ADM]
    S6 --> S10[End]
    S7 --> S10
    S9 --> S10
```

---

## 4.3 Adaptive Delta Modulation — Remedy Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input m of t] --> B[Comparator]
    C[Adaptive Step Generator Delta k] --> D[Accumulator]
    B --> E[1 bit code bk]
    E --> F[Channel to Receiver]
    E --> G[Step Size Controller]
    G --> C
    D --> H[Staircase mq of t]
    H --> B
    C --> D
    I[Slope Overload detected via 3 or 4 consecutive identical bits] -.-> G
    J[Granular Noise controlled by reducing Delta during idle periods] -.-> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **3 consecutive identical bits** is the standard ADM rule-of-thumb: if the encoder outputs `+1,+1,+1` or `-1,-1,-1` for three samples in a row, slope overload is occurring and the step size is **doubled** (or increased by a factor $P > 1$). Conversely, alternating `+1,-1,+1,-1` indicates granular noise and step size is **halved** (multiplied by $1/P$).

---

## 4.4 Conceptual Time-Domain Sketch (Schematic Description)

Since Mermaid cannot render actual analog waveforms, the following block-level depiction captures the **slope overload scenario** structurally:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Time_Axis["Time Axis t in microseconds"]
        direction LR
        T1[Sample 1] --> T2[Sample 2] --> T3[Sample 3] --> T4[Sample 4] --> T5[Sample 5]
    end
    subgraph Message["Message m of t steep slope"]
        M1[Value V1] --> M2[Value V2 high] --> M3[Value V3 very high]
    end
    subgraph Staircase["Staircase mq of t slow climb"]
        Q1[Step up Delta] --> Q2[Step up Delta] --> Q3[Step up Delta] --> Q4[Step up Delta] --> Q5[Still lagging]
    end
    Message --> Staircase
    Staircase -.Gap represents slope overload error.-> Message
```

The **gap** between `Message` and `Staircase` widens with each sample when slope overload is active — this is the visible manifestation of distortion in the time domain.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`**
Define **slope overload distortion** in a Delta Modulation system. State the condition under which it occurs.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Slope overload distortion is the form of error in a Delta Modulation system that occurs when the rate of change (slope) of the input message signal $m(t)$ exceeds the maximum slope that the staircase approximation waveform can produce.
- **Condition (1.5 marks):** Slope overload occurs when $\left\lvert \dfrac{dm(t)}{dt}\right\rvert_{\max} > \Delta f_s$, where $\Delta$ is the step size and $f_s$ is the sampling frequency.

---

### Question A2
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]`**
Distinguish between **slope overload error** and **granular noise** in a Delta Modulation system.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | Slope Overload | Granular Noise |
|--------|----------------|----------------|
| **Cause** | Step size $\Delta$ too small / $f_s$ too low to track rapid signal changes | Step size $\Delta$ too large for slowly varying / constant portions of the signal |
| **Appearance** | Staircase lags behind message | Staircase jitters around the message |
| **Remedy** | Increase $\Delta$ or $f_s$ (or use ADM) | Decrease $\Delta$ (or use ADM) |

(2 distinct points × 1.5 marks = 3 marks)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (Choice 1) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**`(a)` [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a **Delta Modulation (DM) system**. Derive the condition for avoiding **slope overload distortion** for a sinusoidal input signal $m(t) = A_m \sin(2\pi f_m t)$.

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**

The DM system consists of:
1. A **comparator** that compares $m(t)$ with locally generated staircase $m_q(t)$.
2. A **1-bit quantizer** that produces output `+1` (when $m(t) > m_q(t)$) or `-1` (otherwise).
3. An **accumulator (integrator)** with step $\Delta$ that updates $m_q(t+1) = m_q(t) \pm \Delta$.
4. The **receiver** is a simple accumulator (low-pass filter) that reconstructs $m(t)$ from the bitstream.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

- The staircase waveform's maximum slope:

$$\sigma = \frac{\Delta}{T_s} = \Delta f_s$$

- Differentiating the message:

$$\frac{dm(t)}{dt} = 2\pi f_m A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$$

- Maximum slope of the sinusoid:

$$\left| \frac{dm(t)}{dt} \right|_{\max} = 2\pi f_m A_m$$

- To avoid slope overload:

$$2\pi f_m A_m \leq \Delta f_s$$

- Solving for the maximum permissible amplitude:

$$\boxed{A_m \leq \frac{\Delta f_s}{2\pi f_m}}$$

---

**`(b)` [7 Marks]** A Delta Modulation system uses a step size of $\Delta = 50$ mV and a sampling frequency $f_s = 64$ kHz. Determine the **maximum amplitude** of a 1 kHz sinusoidal input that can be transmitted without slope overload.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $\Delta = 50 \times 10^{-3}$ V, $f_s = 64 \times 10^3$ Hz, $f_m = 1 \times 10^3$ Hz.

**Step 1 — Apply the slope-overload condition:**

$$A_m \leq \frac{\Delta f_s}{2\pi f_m}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$A_m \leq \frac{(50 \times 10^{-3})(64 \times 10^3)}{2\pi (1 \times 10^3)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator:**

$$\Delta f_s = (50 \times 10^{-3}) \times (64 \times 10^3) = 3200 \text{ V/s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator:**

$$2\pi f_m = 2 \pi \times 1 \times 10^3 \approx 6283.19 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 5 — Final calculation:**

$$A_{m,\max} = \frac{3200}{6283.19} \approx 0.5093 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{A_{m,\max} \approx 0.51 \text{ V} = 510 \text{ mV}}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

**`(a)` [7 Marks]** Explain **Adaptive Delta Modulation (ADM)** with a block diagram. How does ADM overcome the limitations of slope overload and granular noise in conventional DM?

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**
- ADM differs from conventional DM in that the **step size $\Delta_k$ is not constant**.
- A **step-size controller** monitors the bitstream pattern (typically the last 3 bits).
- If 3 consecutive identical bits are detected → **slope overload** is occurring → step size is **increased** (multiplied by factor $P > 1$, typically $P = 1.5$).
- If alternating `+1,-1,+1` pattern is detected → **granular noise** is occurring → step size is **decreased** (multiplied by $1/P$).
- The variable step $\Delta_k$ is fed to the accumulator.

**Mechanism (3 marks):**
- During **steep signal regions**: $\Delta_k$ grows rapidly, allowing the staircase to keep up with the high slope → **slope overload eliminated**.
- During **slow / flat regions**: $\Delta_k$ shrinks to a small value, reducing the amplitude of the staircase jitter → **granular noise minimized**.

**Summary of Advantages (2 marks):**
- Wider dynamic range for input signal amplitudes.
- Better SNR over a wider range of input powers.
- Lower signaling rate for a given fidelity.
- No need to choose a single $\Delta$ that compromises between slope overload and granular noise.

---

**`(b)` [7 Marks]** A DM system has $\Delta = 100$ mV, $f_s = 100$ kHz, and the input is a sinusoid of frequency $f_m = 5$ kHz. Calculate the **minimum sampling frequency** required to transmit a sinusoid of amplitude $A_m = 4$ V at $f_m = 5$ kHz without slope overload. If the original $f_s$ is insufficient, by what factor must $f_s$ be multiplied?

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $\Delta = 100 \times 10^{-3}$ V = 0.1 V, $A_m = 4$ V, $f_m = 5 \times 10^3$ Hz, original $f_s = 100$ kHz.

**Step 1 — Compute the slope-overload condition requirement:**

$$f_{s,\min} = \frac{2\pi f_m A_m}{\Delta}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$f_{s,\min} = \frac{2\pi \times 5 \times 10^3 \times 4}{0.1}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator:**

$$2\pi \times 5 \times 10^3 \times 4 = 40\pi \times 10^3 \approx 125663.71 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the minimum sampling frequency:**

$$f_{s,\min} = \frac{125663.71}{0.1} = 1.256637 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz} \approx 1.257 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the multiplication factor:**

$$k = \frac{f_{s,\min}}{f_{s,\text{original}}} = \frac{1.256637 \times 10^6}{100 \times 10^3} = 12.566$$

$$\boxed{f_{s,\min} \approx 1.257 \text{ MHz}, \quad k \approx 12.57}$$

**Interpretation:** The original sampling rate of 100 kHz is **far too low**; it must be increased by a factor of approximately **12.6** to track the given sinusoid without slope overload. (Alternatively, ADM could be used to keep the sampling rate low while increasing $\Delta_k$ dynamically.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of $2\pi$** in $A_m \leq \dfrac{\Delta f_s}{2\pi f_m}$. This single omission costs **2–3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency** — confusing $f_m$ in Hz with angular frequency $\omega_m = 2\pi f_m$. Always keep Hz in formulas unless explicitly stated.
> 3. **Mixing up slope overload and granular noise** remedies — increasing $\Delta$ fixes slope overload but *worsens* granular noise; this is a frequently-asked conceptual trap.
> 4. **Not stating "maximum"** when writing the slope condition — write $\left\lvert \dot{m}(t)\right\rvert_{\max}$, not just $\dot{m}(t)$.
> 5. **In ADM answers, omitting the "3 identical bits" detection rule** — this is the standard KTU expected answer for the step-size adaptation logic.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Slope Overload**
>
> - **Definition:** Distortion when message slope exceeds DM's tracking slope.
> - **Core condition:** $\left\lvert \dot{m}(t)\right\rvert_{\max} > \Delta f_s$.
> - **For sinusoid:** $2\pi f_m A_m > \Delta f_s$ → overload.
> - **Max amplitude formula:** $A_{m,\max} = \dfrac{\Delta f_s}{2\pi f_m}$.
> - **Min sampling frequency:** $f_{s,\min} = \dfrac{2\pi f_m A_m}{\Delta}$.
> - **Tracking slope of DM:** $\sigma = \Delta f_s = \Delta / T_s$ (V/s).
> - **Slope overload occurs at zero crossings of sinusoid** (where slope is maximum).
> - **Granular noise is the opposite problem** — occurs in flat signal regions when $\Delta$ is too large.
> - **ADM is the universal remedy** — increases $\Delta_k$ on steep slopes, decreases on flat regions.
> - **Detection rule in ADM:** 3 identical bits in a row ⇒ overload ⇒ double step; alternating bits ⇒ granular noise ⇒ halve step.
> - **Trade-off:** Single $\Delta$ in conventional DM cannot simultaneously minimize both errors; ADM resolves this.
> - **Units to track carefully:** $\Delta$ in V, $f_s$ in Hz, $f_m$ in Hz, $\sigma$ in V/s, $A_m$ in V.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Line Codes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT502 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Line Codes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Line Coding (Transmission Coding)** is the process of converting a sequence of binary digits (bits) into a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude signal waveform (a sequence of pulses) that is physically suitable for transmission across a specific communication channel. It establishes a deterministic, one-to-one mapping rule between the logical binary alphabet $\{0, 1\}$ (or $\{-1, +1\}$) and the physical voltage/current levels, pulse shapes, and timing rules imposed on the medium.

In the layered architecture of a digital communication system, line coding resides in the **Physical Layer (Layer 1)** of the OSI model, immediately above the raw bit source and below the channel (line/medium). The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes the relationship between the *electrical characteristics* of the line code and the *physical properties* of the channel (e.g., bandwidth, noise, attenuation, and DC blocking).

### 1.2 The Three Fundamental Sub-Functions of Line Coding

A line code is not a single waveform but a *convention* with three intertwined engineering objectives:

1.  **Spectral Shaping**: To shape the Power Spectral Density (PSD) of the transmitted signal so that it matches the bandwidth constraints of the channel and to minimize the **low-frequency DC component** (since most channels are AC-coupled or transformer-coupled and cannot pass DC).
2.  **Clock Recovery (Bit Synchronization)**: To embed a strong periodic timing component in the signal spectrum so the receiver can extract the bit clock from the data stream itself, eliminating the need for a separate synchronization channel.
3.  **Bit Error Detection / Monitoring**: To provide an inherent mechanism (such as polarity alternation or violation of alternating rules) that the receiver can use to detect transmission errors in real time.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Universal Translator"

> [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy: The Shipping Container Analogy**
> Imagine a warehouse full of raw goods (the binary data: 0s and 1s). These raw goods cannot be directly loaded onto a cargo ship (the communication channel) because the ship's cranes, dimensions, and loading docks are designed for **standardized shipping containers** (the line-coded signal), not loose crates.
>
> - The **Unipolar RZ vs NRZ** debate is like choosing between *open-top* vs *closed-top* containers.
> - The **Manchester code** is like a container with a *barcode painted on its top and bottom*—slightly wasteful of space, but it carries its own tracking label (the clock), so dock workers never lose count of the packages.
> - **AMI / Bipolar coding** is like alternating the color of containers (Red-Blue-Red-Blue) so a quick visual scan from the harbor master reveals if a container was *lost at sea* (a single polarity error).
> - **B8ZS / HDB3 (Scrambling techniques)** are like inserting a unique, recognizable "emergency pattern" if more than 5 plain containers (zeros) are sent in a row, preventing the dock workers from falling asleep and losing synchronization.

### 1.4 The Binary → Pulse Mapping Engine

A line code is formally characterized by four physical design choices:

| Design Parameter | Standard Options | Engineering Trade-off |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Voltage Levels** | Unipolar (0, +V), Polar (-V, +V), Bipolar (0, +V, -V) | Determines DC component and required power. |
| **Pulse Return-to-Zero (RZ) Behavior** | NRZ (No Return), RZ (Return to Zero) | RZ uses more bandwidth but provides better synchronization. |
| **Pulse Polarity Inversion Rule** | NRZ-L (Level), NRZ-I (Invert on 1), AMI (Alternate) | Determines error-detection capability. |
| **Pulse Shaping** | Rectangular, Manchester (split-phase) | Determines bandwidth efficiency. |

### 1.5 Why Line Codes are Mandatory in KTU 2024 Syllabus

> [!NOTE] > The KTU 2024 Scheme places Line Codes in **Module 2** of Analog and Digital Communication because it is the foundational bridge between abstract *information theory* and physical *transmission engineering*. Without line codes, concepts later in the syllabus—**Eye Diagrams, Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI), Equalization, and Bit Error Rate (BER)** analysis—have no physical signal to operate upon.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Taxonomy of Line Codes (KTU Standard Classification)

The KTU syllabus organizes line codes into the following hierarchy, which is the exact tree used for the ESE (End Semester Examination) questions:

**A. Non-Return-to-Zero (NRZ) Family**
*   **Unipolar NRZ:** '0' $\rightarrow$ 0 V, '1' $\rightarrow$ +V (or +A) V.
*   **Polar NRZ-L (Level):** '0' $\rightarrow$ -A V, '1' $\rightarrow$ +A V.
*   **Polar NRZ-I (Invert on 1):** '0' $\rightarrow$ No transition, '1' $\rightarrow$ Transition (+A to -A or vice versa).

**B. Return-to-Zero (RZ) Family**
*   **Unipolar RZ:** '0' $\rightarrow$ 0 V for full bit, '1' $\rightarrow$ +A V for first half, 0 V for second half.
*   **Polar (Bipolar) RZ:** '1' alternates between +A (first half) and -A (first half); '0' $\rightarrow$ 0 V.

**C. Biphase (Split-Phase) Family**
*   **Manchester (Biphase-L):** '0' $\rightarrow$ High-to-Low transition in middle, '1' $\rightarrow$ Low-to-High transition in middle.
*   **Differential Manchester (Biphase-Mark):** Always has a transition in middle; an *additional* transition at the start of a bit period represents a '0', and *no* additional transition represents a '1'.

**D. Bipolar (Alternate Mark Inversion) Family**
*   **AMI (Alternate Mark Inversion):** '0' $\rightarrow$ 0 V, '1' $\rightarrow$ Alternating +A and -A.
*   **Pseudoternary:** '1' $\rightarrow$ 0 V, '0' $\rightarrow$ Alternating +A and -A (the logical inverse of AMI).

**E. Block / Scrambling Codes (for long zero-strings)**
*   **B8ZS (Bipolar with 8-Zero Substitution):** Used in North America (T1). Replaces any string of 8 consecutive zeros with a deliberate violation pattern `000V B0VB`.
*   **HDB3 (High Density Bipolar 3):** Used in Europe (E1). Replaces any string of 4 consecutive zeros with `B00V` (even count) or `000V` (odd count), preserving the bipolar alternation rule.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Design Choice

*   **Unipolar NRZ (0, +A):** The simplest possible code. *Why use it?* Easy to generate with a single switch. *Why avoid it?* Has a **large DC component** (average value = +A/2) and **no inherent synchronization** during long strings of '1's or '0's. Wastes 50% of the power on DC.
*   **Polar NRZ-L (-A, +A):** Eliminates the DC component *only if* the data has equal probability of 0s and 1s. *The catch:* A long string of identical bits still produces a flat DC line—no transitions, so clock recovery fails.
*   **Polar NRZ-I (Invert on 1):** The clever insight—differential encoding. The *information* is in the *transition*, not the level. This makes the code **immune to polarity inversion** (e.g., if the wires are accidentally swapped). DC component identical to NRZ-L.
*   **Bipolar RZ / AMI:** Solves the DC problem for *all* data patterns. Because '1's alternate polarity, the long-term average is mathematically **guaranteed to be zero**. The trade-off is a **3-level signal**, requiring more complex receivers.
*   **Manchester:** A guaranteed transition in the *middle of every bit*. This gives **perfect synchronization** no matter what the data is. *The cost:* The fundamental frequency is *doubled* (signal changes twice per bit), so it requires **2x the bandwidth** of NRZ.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL: In the table below, all absolute-value and pipe symbols use `\vert` to prevent breaking the KTU markdown renderer.**

| # | Parameter / Property | Formula / Relation | Engineering Meaning | Standard Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Bit Duration** | $T_b = \dfrac{1}{R_b}$ | Relationship between bit rate ($R_b$) and pulse width. | seconds (s) |
| 2 | **First Null Bandwidth (NRZ)** | $BW = R_b = \dfrac{1}{T_b}$ | The width of the main spectral lobe. | Hertz (Hz) |
| 3 | **First Null Bandwidth (RZ / Manchester)** | $BW = 2R_b = \dfrac{2}{T_b}$ | RZ and Biphase codes have a 2x wider main lobe. | Hertz (Hz) |
| 4 | **PSD: Unipolar NRZ** | $P(f) = \dfrac{A^2 T_b}{4} \text{sinc}^2(fT_b) + \dfrac{A^2}{4}\delta(f)$ | Has a discrete **DC component** $\dfrac{A^2}{4}\delta(f)$ and a continuous sinc-squared lobe. | Watts / Hz |
| 5 | **PSD: Polar NRZ** | $P(f) = A^2 T_b \, \text{sinc}^2(fT_b)$ | **No DC component** (assuming equal 0s/1s). Continuous spectrum only. | Watts / Hz |
| 6 | **PSD: Bipolar RZ / AMI** | $P(f) = \dfrac{A^2 T_b}{4} \text{sinc}^2(fT_b) \sin^2(\pi f T_b)$ | Zero DC component. Nulls at all harmonics of $R_b$ because of the polarity alternation. | Watts / Hz |
| 7 | **PSD: Manchester** | $P(f) = A^2 T_b \, \text{sinc}^2\!\left(\dfrac{fT_b}{2}\right) \sin^2\!\left(\dfrac{\pi f T_b}{2}\right)$ | Zero DC. Spreads energy evenly. Used in 10Base-T Ethernet. | Watts / Hz |
| 8 | **DC Component: Unipolar** | $\mu = \dfrac{A}{2}$ | Mean voltage. Average non-zero $\Rightarrow$ bad for AC-coupled lines. | Volts (V) |
| 9 | **DC Component: Polar / Bipolar** | $\mu = 0$ | Mathematically guaranteed to be zero for balanced data. | Volts (V) |
| 10 | **Synchronization Metric** | Min. transitions per $T_b$ | NRZ = 0, RZ = 1, Manchester = 1 (always). | transitions/bit |
| 11 | **Error Detection** | $\vert \text{violation count} \vert$ | AMI detects single-bit errors as bipolar violations (BPV). | count |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Line codes are not just an academic exercise—they are the silent backbone of every digital interface a B.Tech student uses:

*   **RS-232 Serial Ports:** Use **Polar NRZ** (with $\pm 12$ V levels). Long runs of identical characters are a notorious problem, requiring "break" signals for resynchronization.
*   **10Base-T Ethernet (10 Mbps):** Uses **Manchester code**. The IEEE 802.3 standard mandates it specifically because of Manchester's perfect clock recovery.
*   **100Base-TX Fast Ethernet (100 Mbps):** Uses **MLT-3** (a 3-level NRZ variant) over Cat-5 cable.
*   **T1 / DS1 Trunk Lines (1.544 Mbps):** Use **B8ZS** to maintain synchronization over thousands of feet of twisted pair.
*   **E1 / ISDN PRI Trunks (2.048 Mbps):** Use **HDB3** in Europe and most of Asia (including India).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Power Spectral Density (PSD) of Unipolar NRZ

The PSD is the single most important analytical tool in line code theory. Below is the full KTU-grade derivation. We assume an equiprobable binary source ($P(0) = P(1) = 0.5$).

**Step 1 — Model the Random Binary Waveform**
Let the data be a random sequence $a_k \in \{0, 1\}$. The transmitted signal $s(t)$ is a sum of weighted, time-shifted rectangular pulses $p(t)$ of duration $T_b$:

$$
s(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k \, p(t - kT_b)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Autocorrelation Function $R_s(\tau)$**
For a random pulse train, the autocorrelation is composed of a continuous part (from data correlation across time) and a discrete part (from the periodic mean).

$$
R_s(\tau) = \lim_{N \to \infty} \dfrac{1}{NT_b} \int_{-NT_b/2}^{NT_b/2} s(t) s(t+\tau) \, dt
$$

Expanding this rigorously using $E[a_k] = \mu_a = 0.5$ and $E[a_k^2] = \sigma_a^2 + \mu_a^2 = 0.25 + 0.25 = 0.5$:

$$
R_s(\tau) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} R_a(m) \, R_p(\tau - mT_b)
$$

where $R_a(m) = E[a_k a_{k+m}] - \mu_a^2$ is the discrete autocorrelation of the bit sequence.

**Step 3 — Substituting the Statistics**
Since the bits are independent, $R_a(m) = 0$ for $m \neq 0$, and $R_a(0) = E[a_k^2] - \mu_a^2 = 0.5 - 0.25 = 0.25$. The constant part gives a delta function. Substituting the rectangular pulse $p(t)$ of width $T_b$ and amplitude $A$:

$$
R_p(\tau) = \begin{cases} A^2 \left(1 - \dfrac{\vert \tau \vert}{T_b}\right), & \vert \tau \vert \leq T_b \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Autocorrelation Expression**

$$
R_s(\tau) = \dfrac{A^2}{4} \, \delta(\tau) + \dfrac{A^2}{4 T_b} \, \Lambda\!\left(\dfrac{\tau}{T_b}\right)
$$

where $\Lambda(\cdot)$ is the triangular function of width $2T_b$ and peak $A^2$.

**Step 5 — Apply the Wiener–Khinchin Theorem**
The PSD is the Fourier Transform of the autocorrelation function, $P(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_s(\tau)\}$. Using $\mathcal{F}\{\Lambda(\tau/T_b)\} = T_b \, \text{sinc}^2(fT_b)$ and $\mathcal{F}\{\delta(\tau)\} = 1$:

$$
\boxed{P_{\text{Unipolar NRZ}}(f) = \dfrac{A^2 T_b}{4} \, \text{sinc}^2(fT_b) + \dfrac{A^2}{4} \, \delta(f)}
$$

**Step 6 — Physical Interpretation of the Result**
*   The **second term** $\dfrac{A^2}{4}\delta(f)$ is a discrete impulse at $f=0$. This is the **DC component** ($10 \log_{10}(0.25 A^2)$ dB) that makes Unipolar NRZ unsuitable for AC-coupled channels like telephone lines.
*   The **first term** is a continuous $\text{sinc}^2$ spectrum with main lobe width $1/T_b$ (first null at $f = 1/T_b = R_b$). The null-to-null bandwidth is **$R_b$ Hz**.

### 3.2 Derivation: PSD of Polar NRZ-L

For Polar NRZ, the bits are $a_k \in \{-A, +A\}$, so $E[a_k] = 0$ and $E[a_k^2] = A^2$. The discrete impulse term **vanishes** because the mean is zero:

$$
\boxed{P_{\text{Polar NRZ}}(f) = A^2 T_b \, \text{sinc}^2(fT_b)}
$$

Notice: same shape, but **4× the power** of unipolar (because the signal swings between $\pm A$ instead of 0 and $+A$), and **no DC component**.

### 3.3 Derivation: PSD of Manchester Code

Manchester is a *biphase* code. A '1' is $+A$ for the first half and $-A$ for the second half (a step-down pulse), while a '0' is the exact negative of that. We can model it as the multiplication of a Polar NRZ signal $s_p(t)$ with a square-wave clock $c(t)$ of frequency $R_b/2$:

$$
s_{\text{Manchester}}(t) = s_p(t) \cdot c(t)
$$

In the frequency domain, multiplication becomes convolution:

$$
P_{\text{Manchester}}(f) = P_p(f) * P_c(f) = A^2 T_b \, \text{sinc}^2\!\left(\dfrac{fT_b}{2}\right) \sin^2\!\left(\dfrac{\pi f T_b}{2}\right)
$$

The factor $\sin^2(\pi f T_b / 2)$ guarantees that $P(f=0) = 0$ (no DC) and that the energy is pushed to higher frequencies. The first null is at $f = 2/T_b = 2R_b$, confirming that Manchester requires **double the bandwidth** of NRZ.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Exam Quick Reference)

| Line Code | Levels | DC Component | Min. BW (Hz) | Sync. Quality | Error Detection | Complexity |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Unipolar NRZ** | 2 (0, +A) | **Yes (A/2)** | $R_b$ | Poor | None | Very Low |
| **Polar NRZ-L** | 2 (-A, +A) | No (if balanced) | $R_b$ | Poor | None | Low |
| **Polar NRZ-I** | 2 (-A, +A) | No (if balanced) | $R_b$ | Poor | None | Low |
| **Unipolar RZ** | 2 (0, +A) | **Yes (A/4)** | $2R_b$ | Good | None | Low |
| **Bipolar RZ / AMI** | 3 (0, $\pm$A) | **No** | $2R_b$ | Good | **Yes (BPV)** | Medium |
| **Manchester** | 2 (-A, +A) | **No** | $2R_b$ | **Excellent** | None | Medium |
| **Diff. Manchester** | 2 (-A, +A) | **No** | $2R_b$ | **Excellent** | None | Medium |
| **HDB3** | 3 (0, $\pm$A) | **No** | $2R_b$ | Very Good | Yes | High |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow: The Line Coding Transmitter–Receiver Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Binary Source] --> ser[Serialiser Shift Register]
    ser --> enc{Line Code Encoder}
    enc --> shp[Pulse Shaping Filter p t]
    shp --> chan[Channel with AWGN and Bandwidth Limit]
    chan --> rxamp[Receiver Front End Amplifier]
    rxamp --> clk[Clock Recovery PLL]
    clk --> dec{Line Code Decoder}
    dec --> out[Reconstructed Binary Data]
    clk -. timing reference .-> dec
    rxamp --> eye[Eye Diagram Monitor]
    eye -. quality metric .-> dec
```

### 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram: AMI Encoding Rule Engine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Send_Zero: Input bit = 0
    Idle --> Send_Pos_A: Input bit = 1 AND last_one_was_pos = false
    Idle --> Send_Neg_A: Input bit = 1 AND last_one_was_pos = true
    Send_Zero --> Idle: Maintain 0 V output
    Send_Pos_A --> Idle: Output +A V for T_b seconds
    Send_Neg_A --> Idle: Output -A V for T_b seconds
    Send_Pos_A --> Set_Flag_True: last_one_was_pos = true
    Send_Neg_A --> Set_Flag_False: last_one_was_pos = false
    Set_Flag_True --> Idle
    Set_Flag_False --> Idle
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Chart: Spectral Bandwidth Requirements

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NarrowBW["Low Bandwidth Family - first null at Rb"]
        n1[Unipolar NRZ]
        n2[Polar NRZ L]
        n3[Polar NRZ I]
    end
    subgraph WideBW["High Bandwidth Family - first null at 2Rb"]
        w1[Unipolar RZ]
        w2[Bipolar RZ AMI]
        w3[Manchester]
        w4[Differential Manchester]
    end
    subgraph Scrambled["Scrambled Bipolar Family - first null at 2Rb"]
        s1[B8ZS]
        s2[HDB3]
    end
    n1 --> w1
    n2 --> w2
    n3 --> w3
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Manchester Encoder Hardware Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    clk[Clock Rb divided by 2] --> xor[XOR Gate]
    din[Data Input] --> xor
    xor --> out[Manchester Encoded Output]
    clk -. timing .-> xor
    din -. data .-> xor
```

**Note on the Encoder:** The Manchester encoder is implemented in hardware as a single XOR gate driven by the data and a clock signal at half the bit rate. The XOR output naturally produces the high-to-low and low-to-high mid-bit transitions that define Manchester encoding.

### 4.5 Waveform-to-Property Cross-Reference Table (Engineering Sketch)

For a sample data stream $D = 1\,0\,1\,1\,0\,0\,1\,0$:

| Bit Position | Unipolar NRZ | Polar NRZ-L | NRZ-I | Bipolar RZ (AMI) | Manchester | Diff. Manchester |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 (1) | +A | +A | +A→-A | +A (half) | H→L | Transition start + middle |
| 2 (0) | 0 | -A | -A | 0 | L→H | No start + middle |
| 3 (1) | +A | +A | -A→+A | -A (half) | H→L | No start + middle |
| 4 (1) | +A | +A | +A→-A | +A (half) | H→L | No start + middle |
| 5 (0) | 0 | -A | -A | 0 | L→H | Transition start + middle |
| 6 (0) | 0 | -A | -A | 0 | L→H | No start + middle |
| 7 (1) | +A | +A | -A→+A | -A (half) | H→L | No start + middle |
| 8 (0) | 0 | -A | -A | 0 | L→H | No start + middle |
