- Reduce Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI) at the receiver.
- Minimize out-of-band radiation to avoid adjacent-channel interference (ACI).

**Step 2 (2 marks):** The received pulse $p(t)$ is the convolution of the transmitter pulse shape, the channel impulse response, and the receiver matched filter. For zero ISI at the sampling instants $t = nT$:

$$
p(nT) = 
\begin{cases}
1, & n = 0 \\
0, & n \neq 0
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 (3 marks):** **Nyquist's First Criterion:** The system frequency response $H(f)$ must have **odd symmetry** about the Nyquist frequency $f_N = 1/(2T)$:

$$
H\!\left(\frac{1}{2T} + f_0\right) + H\!\left(\frac{1}{2T} - f_0\right) = \text{constant}, \quad 0 \leq f_0 \leq \frac{1}{2T}
$$

This ensures the time-domain pulse $h(t) = \text{sinc}(t/T)$ has zero crossings exactly at $t = \pm nT$, $n \neq 0$, eliminating ISI.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

Given: Binary signaling $\Rightarrow R_s = R_b = 2 \times 10^{6}$ symbols/sec, $\alpha = 0.25$.

**(i) Symbol Rate (1 mark):**

$$
R_s = R_b = 2 \text{ Mbps} = 2 \times 10^{6} \text{ symbols/sec}
$$

**(ii) Nyquist Minimum Bandwidth (2 marks):**

$$
W_N = \frac{R_s}{2} = \frac{2 \times 10^{6}}{2} = 1 \text{ MHz}
$$

**(iii) Actual Transmission Bandwidth (2 marks):**

$$
W_{RC} = (1 + \alpha)\,\frac{R_s}{2} = (1 + 0.25)(1 \text{ MHz}) = 1.25 \text{ MHz}
$$

**(iv) Spectral Efficiency (2 marks):**

$$
\eta = \frac{R_b}{W_{RC}} = \frac{2 \times 10^{6}}{1.25 \times 10^{6}} = 1.6 \text{ bits/sec/Hz}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the three Nyquist criteria for pulse shaping with relevant equations and spectra. Compare the raised cosine and duobinary schemes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A QPSK system transmits at a symbol rate of $R_s = 10$ Msymbols/sec using a root raised cosine filter with $\alpha = 0.35$. Calculate (i) the bit rate, (ii) the transmission bandwidth, and (iii) the spectral efficiency in bits/sec/Hz. Also find the bandwidth if the roll-off is changed to $\alpha = 0$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Criterion 1 (Ideal, 2 marks):** The ideal channel has a rectangular (brick-wall) spectrum of bandwidth $W = R_s/2$, giving a $\text{sinc}$ pulse. Unachievable in practice because it requires infinite-duration pulses and a physically unrealizable filter.

$$
H(f) = 
\begin{cases}
T, & \vert f \vert \leq R_s/2 \\
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 2 — Criterion 2 (Controlled ISI, 2 marks):** **Duobinary signalling** deliberately introduces a controlled amount of ISI (between adjacent bits) but uses **precoding** at the transmitter to cancel the ISI at the receiver. This halves the bandwidth to $R_s/4$ at the cost of binary-to-ternary conversion.

**Step 3 — Criterion 3 (Vestigial Symmetry, 2 marks):** Uses a **raised cosine spectrum** with smooth roll-off, satisfying the odd-symmetry condition of Criterion 1. Bandwidth is $(1+\alpha) R_s/2$. This is the most widely used in practice.

**Step 4 — Comparison (1 mark):** See analytical matrix in Section 3.4.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

Given: QPSK (2 bits/symbol), $R_s = 10$ Msymbols/sec, $\alpha = 0.35$.

**(i) Bit Rate (1 mark):**

$$
R_b = R_s \times \log_2(4) = 10 \times 10^{6} \times 2 = 20 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**(ii) Transmission Bandwidth at $\alpha = 0.35$ (2 marks):**

$$
W = (1 + \alpha)\,\frac{R_s}{2} = (1.35)(5 \text{ MHz}) = 6.75 \text{ MHz}
$$

**(iii) Spectral Efficiency (2 marks):**

$$
\eta = \frac{R_b}{W} = \frac{20}{6.75} = 2.96 \text{ bits/sec/Hz}
$$

**At $\alpha = 0$ (2 marks):**

$$
W = (1 + 0)\,\frac{R_s}{2} = 5 \text{ MHz}
$$

This is the absolute minimum (ideal sinc) bandwidth.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist — read this twice before every KTU exam.**

- **Zero ISI Condition:** $p(nT) = \delta_{n,0}$ — sample at $t = 0$ gives the symbol, all other samples are zero.
- **Nyquist's First Criterion:** The overall system frequency response must have **odd symmetry about $f = 1/(2T)$**.
- **Nyquist Minimum Bandwidth (Ideal):** $W_N = R_s / 2$ Hz — a *theoretical* lower bound.
- **Raised Cosine Bandwidth (Practical):** $W_{RC} = (1+\alpha) R_s / 2$ Hz — the *real* design target.
- **Roll-off factor $\alpha$:** Trade-off knob. $\alpha = 0$ ideal-but-unrealizable, $\alpha = 1$ widest-but-easy.
- **Spectral efficiency:** $\eta = R_b / W_{RC}$ bits/s/Hz. Max value is **2 bits/s/Hz** for binary.
- **Three Nyquist Criteria at a glance:**
  1. Ideal rectangular spectrum (sync pulse).
  2. Duobinary (controlled ISI, half bandwidth).
  3. Raised Cosine (vestigial symmetry, practical).
- **RC pulse formula:** $p_{RC}(t) = \text{sinc}(t/T) \cdot \frac{\cos(\pi \alpha t/T)}{1 - (2\alpha t/T)^2}$.
- **RRC split:** Transmitter and receiver each use **Root Raised Cosine**; the cascade equals the full RC and yields zero ISI.
- **M-ary systems:** Spectral efficiency is $\eta = 2k/(1+\alpha)$ where $k = \log_2(M)$.
- **Duobinary bandwidth:** $W_{DB} = R_s / 4$ — half of the RC case but requires a precoder.
- **Common exam trap:** Always clarify whether the question is about *binary* or *M-ary* — confusing $R_b$ and $R_s$ loses easy marks.
- **Practical systems using RC/RRC:** 4G LTE ($\alpha = 0.22$), 5G NR, Wi-Fi, satellite QPSK modems, dial-up modems.
- **Why not ideal sinc?** Requires infinite time to decay, and any time-truncation re-introduces ISI.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Nyquist Criterion for Zero ISI**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Signal Modeling for ISI
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT502 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Signal Modeling for ISI

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI)** is a form of distortion of a signal in which one or more symbols interfere with subsequent symbols, causing ambiguity and errors in symbol detection at the receiver. It is an unwanted phenomenon where the time-dispersive (bandlimited) nature of the communication channel causes the pulse corresponding to one symbol to spread out and overlap with the pulses of adjacent symbols.

**Signal Modeling for ISI** is the mathematical framework used to represent the transmitted, channel-distorted, and received pulse trains so that the *cause, effect, and control* of ISI can be quantitatively analyzed. The goal of the model is to derive conditions under which ISI can be completely eliminated at the sampling instants — a condition codified in **Nyquist's First Criterion (Zero ISI Condition)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU Highlight:** In the KTU 2024 scheme, the topic "Signal Modeling for ISI" is the foundation for the more advanced topics of **Pulse Shaping, Nyquist Filtering, and Eye Patterns**. Mastering the received-signal equation is the single most critical learning outcome for Module 3.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are writing letters of the alphabet on a single line of paper, but the pen you are using is **slightly leaky**, and the ink **bleeds** sideways into the neighboring letters. When someone tries to read the letters, the ink from "A" has bled into "B" and "C", making it very hard to recognize them. 

In a digital communication system:
- The **letters** are your data symbols (1s and 0s).
- The **leaky pen** is the bandlimited channel (limited bandwidth causes pulse spreading, much like the limited resolution of a pen causes ink to bleed).
- The **ink bleeding** is exactly what **Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI)** does to your data symbols at the receiver.
- The **signal model** is the mathematical description of *how much* the ink bleeds and *how* we can design a better "pen" (pulse shaping filter) so that the bleeding is zero at the exact moments we sample the letters.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world example:** When you stream a low-quality video over a slow network, "ghosting" or "smearing" of one frame into the next is the visual analog of ISI. The pixels of the previous frame leak into the current frame, distorting the image.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

- **Symbol Duration ($T$)**: The time allocated to transmit one symbol. Measured in **seconds**.
- **Bit Rate / Symbol Rate ($R_s = 1/T$)**: Number of symbols transmitted per second. Measured in **baud (symbols/second)**.
- **Channel Bandwidth ($W$ or $B$)**: The usable range of frequencies in the channel. Measured in **Hz**.
- **Roll-off factor ($\alpha$)**: A dimensionless parameter (0 ≤ $\alpha$ ≤ 1) controlling the excess bandwidth of a Nyquist pulse.
- **Sampling Instant ($t = mT$)**: The precise moments at which the receiver samples the received waveform to make a decision on the $m^{th}$ symbol.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Block-Level Conceptual Flow

A digital communication system is mathematically modeled as three cascaded blocks. Understanding this chain is essential for understanding ISI.

1. **Transmitter (TX) — Pulse Shaping Filter** $G_T(f)$:
   Converts discrete symbols $\{a_k\}$ into a continuous waveform by passing them through a lowpass filter. The output is a train of shaped pulses.

2. **Channel $H_C(f)$**:
   A physical medium (copper wire, fiber, wireless). It is **bandlimited** and **dispersive** — different frequency components travel with different attenuations and delays, causing the pulse to spread.

3. **Receiver (RX) — Receiving Filter** $G_R(f)$:
   A matched filter designed to maximize the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) at the sampling instant.

The **overall system response** (cascaded response) is:
$$H(f) = G_T(f) \cdot H_C(f) \cdot G_R(f)$$

with the corresponding time-domain impulse response $h(t)$.

## 2.2 The Received Signal Model

When a sequence of symbols $\{a_k\}$ is transmitted, the received noise-free signal at the output of the receiving filter is:

$$r(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k \, h(t - kT)$$

where:
- $a_k$ is the $k^{th}$ transmitted symbol (from a constellation like M-PAM, M-PSK, M-QAM).
- $h(t)$ is the overall system impulse response (TX filter + Channel + RX filter).
- $T$ is the symbol duration.
- $kT$ represents the time delay for the $k^{th}$ symbol.

**Adding Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN)** $n(t)$:

$$y(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k \, h(t - kT) + n(t)$$

## 2.3 Sampling the Received Signal at $t = mT$

The receiver samples $y(t)$ at the exact instants $t = mT$ (one sample per symbol period). Substituting $t = mT$:

$$y(mT) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k \, h(mT - kT) + n(mT)$$

This is the **central equation of signal modeling for ISI**. It can be split into two parts:

$$y(mT) = a_m \, h(0) + \sum_{\substack{k=-\infty \\ k \neq m}}^{\infty} a_k \, h\big((m-k)T\big) + n(mT)$$

- **First term** $a_m h(0)$: The **desired symbol** (scaled by the peak of the pulse response).
- **Second term** (the summation over $k \neq m$): The **Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI)** — contributions from all *other* symbols bleeding into the current decision instant.
- **Third term** $n(mT)$: The **noise** contribution at the sampling instant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The signal model teaches us:** To achieve **Zero ISI**, we must force $h(nT) = 0$ for all integer $n \neq 0$. This is the entire purpose of pulse shaping.

## 2.4 Nyquist's First Criterion (Zero ISI Condition)

The necessary and sufficient condition to eliminate ISI at the sampling instants is given by the **time-domain condition**:

$$\boxed{\,h(nT) = \begin{cases} 1, & n = 0 \\ 0, & n \neq 0 \end{cases}\,}$$

The equivalent **frequency-domain (Nyquist Criterion)** states that the folded spectrum of $H(f)$ must be a constant:

$$\boxed{\,\sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} H\left(f + \frac{m}{T}\right) = T \quad \text{for all} \quad \vert f \vert \leq \frac{1}{2T}\,}$$

The minimum bandwidth required for zero ISI is $W = \frac{1}{2T}$ Hz, and the corresponding maximum symbol rate (Nyquist rate) is $R_s = 2W$ symbols/second.

## 2.5 The Raised Cosine Spectrum Family

A practical family of spectra that satisfies the zero-ISI condition is the **Raised Cosine (RC)** filter family:

$$H_{RC}(f) = \begin{cases} T, & \vert f \vert \leq \frac{1-\alpha}{2T} \\ \frac{T}{2}\left[1 + \cos\left(\frac{\pi T}{\alpha}\left(\vert f \vert - \frac{1-\alpha}{2T}\right)\right)\right], & \frac{1-\alpha}{2T} < \vert f \vert < \frac{1+\alpha}{2T} \\ 0, & \vert f \vert \geq \frac{1+\alpha}{2T} \end{cases}$$

- The total transmission bandwidth is $W = \frac{1+\alpha}{2T}$ Hz.
- When $\alpha = 0$, it reduces to the **ideal Nyquist (sinc) filter** of bandwidth $\frac{1}{2T}$ Hz, but this is unrealizable.
- When $\alpha = 1$, it becomes the **full cosine roll-off** filter with the widest bandwidth $W = \frac{1}{T}$ Hz.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Received signal (noise-free) | $r(t) = \sum_k a_k h(t-kT)$ | Output of cascaded TX+Channel+RX | Volts |
| Sampled received signal | $y(mT) = a_m h(0) + \sum_{k \neq m} a_k h((m-k)T) + n(mT)$ | Decision variable at $t=mT$ | Volts |
| Overall system response | $H(f) = G_T(f) H_C(f) G_R(f)$ | Frequency-domain cascade | — |
| Zero ISI time condition | $h(nT) = 0$ for $n \neq 0$ | Forces ISI to zero | — |
| Nyquist folded spectrum | $\sum_m H(f + m/T) = T$ for $\vert f \vert \leq 1/(2T)$ | Freq-domain zero ISI | — |
| Minimum bandwidth | $W_{\min} = 1/(2T)$ | Ideal sinc pulse | Hz |
| RC filter bandwidth | $W = (1+\alpha)/(2T)$ | Practical raised cosine | Hz |
| Roll-off factor | $\alpha = (W - W_0)/W_0$ | Excess bandwidth fraction | dimensionless |
| Maximum symbol rate | $R_s = 2W$ | Nyquist signaling rate | baud |
| Overall eye opening | $E_{open} = 1 - 2\sum_{k \neq 0} \vert h(kT) \vert / h(0)$ | ISI-induced closure | Volts |

> **Engineering Utility:** This model is the bedrock of every modern digital communication standard — from 4G LTE to 5G NR, from Wi-Fi to satellite modems. Every standard specifies a particular pulse shape (e.g., **Root Raised Cosine, RRC**) to strictly bound ISI before the equalizer stage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Roll-off Factor $\alpha$ matters:** A larger $\alpha$ makes the filter easier to implement (sharper time-domain decay of the sinc tail) but consumes more bandwidth. A smaller $\alpha$ is bandwidth-efficient but demands a more precise (and expensive) analog filter. Engineers always balance this **bandwidth-vs-complexity trade-off**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Continuous Symbols to the Received Signal Model

**Starting Point:** A sequence of discrete symbols $\{a_k\}$ from a constellation is to be transmitted. The TX filter shapes each symbol into a pulse $g_T(t)$. Mathematically, the TX output is:

$$s(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k \, g_T(t - kT)$$

**Step 1:** The signal $s(t)$ is passed through the channel with impulse response $h_C(t)$. The channel output is the convolution:

$$x(t) = s(t) * h_C(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} s(\tau) h_C(t - \tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 2:** Substituting $s(t)$ into the convolution integral:

$$x(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left[ \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k g_T(\tau - kT) \right] h_C(t - \tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 3:** Interchanging the order of integration and summation (a standard property of linear systems):

$$x(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} g_T(\tau - kT) \, h_C(t - \tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 4:** The integral above is itself a convolution evaluated at $t - kT$:

$$x(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k \left[ g_T * h_C \right](t - kT)$$

**Step 5:** The signal then passes through the RX filter $g_R(t)$:

$$r(t) = x(t) * g_R(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k \left[ g_T * h_C * g_R \right](t - kT)$$

**Step 6:** Defining the **overall system impulse response** as the cascade of all three filters:

$$h(t) \triangleq g_T(t) * h_C(t) * g_R(t)$$

**Step 7:** The final, clean received signal model is:

$$\boxed{\,r(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k \, h(t - kT)\,}$$

with the inclusion of noise:

$$y(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k \, h(t - kT) + n(t)$$

This derivation shows *exactly* why the sum-over-$k$ model arises: linearity of the cascade turns the convolution of each filter into a single combined response, and the time-shift property shifts this response by $kT$ for the $k^{th}$ symbol.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Deriving the Sampled Decision Variable and Separating ISI

We sample the noise-corrupted received signal at $t = mT$ for the $m^{th}$ decision:

**Step 1:** Substitute $t = mT$ into the received signal model:

$$y(mT) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k \, h(mT - kT) + n(mT)$$

**Step 2:** Let $n = m - k$, so that $k = m - n$. When $k$ ranges over all integers, $n$ also ranges over all integers:

$$y(mT) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} a_{m-n} \, h(nT) + n(mT)$$

**Step 3:** Separate the $n = 0$ term (the desired symbol) from the rest:

$$y(mT) = a_m \, h(0) + \sum_{\substack{n=-\infty \\ n \neq 0}}^{\infty} a_{m-n} \, h(nT) + n(mT)$$

**Step 4:** Re-index back to the more familiar form $k = m - n$ (or equivalently, just rename the dummy index):

$$\boxed{\,y(mT) = a_m \, h(0) + \underbrace{\sum_{\substack{k=-\infty \\ k \neq m}}^{\infty} a_k \, h\big((m-k)T\big)}_{\text{ISI Term}} + n(mT)\,}$$

**Interpretation:**
- $h(0)$ is the peak amplitude of the pulse at its center.
- The ISI term is a sum of contributions from *all other symbols* sampled at their respective ISI time-offsets $(m-k)T$.
- If $h(nT) = 0$ for all $n \neq 0$, the ISI term vanishes completely.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Nyquist's Folded Spectrum Criterion from Time-Domain Zero ISI

**Step 1:** Start with the zero-ISI time condition: $h(nT) = 0$ for $n \neq 0$, and $h(0) = $ constant.

**Step 2:** Consider the periodic extension of $h(t)$ in the time domain. The Fourier Transform of a periodic impulse train of period $T$ in the time domain produces a frequency-domain impulse train of spacing $1/T$:

$$\text{If } p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT) \quad \xrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} \quad P(f) = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta\left(f - \frac{m}{T}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Convolve $h(t)$ with this impulse train to sample it at $t = nT$:

$$h(t) \cdot p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} h(nT) \, \delta(t - nT) = h(0) \, \delta(t)$$

**Step 4:** Take the Fourier Transform of both sides. The left side, by the modulation property, is the periodic convolution of $H(f)$ with $P(f)$:

$$H(f) * P(f) = h(0)$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the expression for $P(f)$:

$$H(f) * \left[ \frac{1}{T} \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta\left(f - \frac{m}{T}\right) \right] = h(0)$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate the convolution (it becomes a discrete sum):

$$\frac{1}{T} \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} H\left(f - \frac{m}{T}\right) = h(0)$$

**Step 7:** Equivalently, replacing $f \to f + m/T$ (which simply shifts the indexing):

$$\boxed{\,\sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} H\left(f + \frac{m}{T}\right) = T \cdot h(0) = T \quad (\text{normalized}) \quad \text{for} \quad \vert f \vert \leq \frac{1}{2T}\,}$$

This is **Nyquist's First Criterion in the frequency domain**. It means the *folded* (or *aliased*) spectrum of $H(f)$ must be flat (constant) over the baseband $\vert f \vert \leq 1/(2T)$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Expression for Eye Pattern Opening

The **eye pattern** is a superposition of all possible realizations of the received waveform on an oscilloscope. The vertical eye opening is a direct visual measure of ISI margin.

**Step 1:** The worst-case peak distortion at the sampling instant due to ISI is bounded by the sum of magnitudes:

$$\text{ISI}_{\max} = \sum_{n \neq 0} \vert h(nT) \vert$$

**Step 2:** The minimum sampled value of $y(mT)$ (for $a_m = +A$, worst-case negative ISI):

$$y_{\min} = A \cdot h(0) - A \cdot \sum_{n \neq 0} \vert h(nT) \vert$$

**Step 3:** Similarly, the maximum sampled value (for $a_m = -A$, worst-case positive ISI):

$$y_{\max} = -A \cdot h(0) + A \cdot \sum_{n \neq 0} \vert h(nT) \vert$$

**Step 4:** The vertical eye opening is the difference between these extremes:

$$E_{\text{open}} = y_{\min} - y_{\max} = 2A \cdot h(0) - 2A \cdot \sum_{n \neq 0} \vert h(nT) \vert$$

**Step 5:** Normalizing by the peak amplitude $A \cdot h(0)$:

$$\boxed{\,\frac{E_{\text{open}}}{2A h(0)} = 1 - \frac{\sum_{n \neq 0} \vert h(nT) \vert}{h(0)}\,}$$

This fraction is exactly the **ISI margin**. A value of 1 means zero ISI, a value of 0 means the eye is completely closed and symbol detection is impossible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Takeaway:** All four derivations connect — the signal model yields the ISI term, the time-domain condition forces the ISI term to vanish, the frequency-domain criterion ensures the time-domain condition, and the eye pattern visually quantifies the residual ISI.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: End-to-End Digital Communication System with ISI Modeling

This diagram maps the entire signal chain, showing exactly *where* and *how* ISI is mathematically introduced and removed.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Source Bits b_k"] --> B["Symbol Mapper a_k"]
    B --> C["TX Pulse Shaping Filter G_T_f"]
    C --> D["Channel H_C_f Bandlimited"]
    D --> E["RX Matched Filter G_R_f"]
    E --> F["Sampler at t = mT"]
    F --> G["Decision Device Threshold"]
    G --> H["Detected Symbols a_hat_k"]
    I["AWGN n_t"] --> E

    classDef isiblk fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    classDef filterblk fill:#e5f0ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef datablk fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#006600,color:#000

    class C,D,E filterblk
    class A,B,F,G,H datablk
    class I isiblk
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **TX Filter, Channel, and RX Filter** form the cascade $H(f) = G_T(f) H_C(f) G_R(f)$.
- The **Sampler** triggers the ISI decomposition: at $t=mT$, all symbols except the $m^{th}$ one contribute ISI.
- The **Decision Device** compares $y(mT)$ to a threshold; ISI directly increases the bit error rate (BER).

## 4.2 Flow Diagram: How ISI Arises in the Sampled Decision Variable

This diagram shows the mathematical decomposition of $y(mT)$ into its three components.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Sampled Signal y_mT"] --> Q{"Is k equal to m?"}
    Q -- Yes --> R["Desired Term a_m times h_0"]
    Q -- No --> S["ISI Term sum over k not equal to m of a_k times h_m_minus_k_T"]
    P --> T["Add AWGN n_mT"]
    R --> U["Final Decision Variable y_mT"]
    S --> U
    T --> U
    U --> V{"Compare to Threshold"}
    V -- "Greater than threshold" --> W["Decide a_hat_m equals plus A"]
    V -- "Less than threshold" --> X["Decide a_hat_m equals minus A"]

    classDef isibox fill:#fff0d9,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef desiredbox fill:#d9f0d9,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    classDef decisionbox fill:#d9e5ff,stroke:#003399,color:#000

    class R desiredbox
    class S,T isibox
    class V,W,X decisionbox
```

**Key Insight:** The "ISI Term" branch is the *only* branch that should be eliminated in an ideal system. Pulse shaping design and equalization both attack this branch.

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Pulse Shaping Implementation (Transmitter-Receiver Split)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TX_Side["Transmitter Side"]
        T1["Discrete Symbols a_k"] --> T2["Upsampler by T"]
        T2 --> T3["Root Raised Cosine Filter G_TRC"]
    end

    subgraph CH_Side["Channel Side"]
        T3 --> C1["Bandlimited Channel H_C_f"]
    end

    subgraph RX_Side["Receiver Side"]
        C1 --> R1["Root Raised Cosine Filter G_RRC"]
        R1 --> R2["Sampler at t = mT"]
        R2 --> R3["Threshold Detector"]
    end

    classDef tx fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000
    classDef ch fill:#ffe2e2,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef rx fill:#d9e8ff,stroke:#003399,color:#000

    class T1,T2,T3 tx
    class C1 ch
    class R1,R2,R3 rx
```

**Engineering Note:** The RRC-RRC pair is the industry-standard implementation because each RRC filter alone is unrealizable at the symbol rate (it's a matched filter pair), but their *cascade* is exactly a Raised Cosine filter — which *does* satisfy the zero-ISI condition. The cascade of RRC-RRC at the output of the channel restores a clean, ISI-free pulse at the sampler.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI). Write the mathematical expression for the sampled received signal and identify the ISI term.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
**Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI)** is the phenomenon in which the time-dispersive nature of a bandlimited channel causes the pulse corresponding to one transmitted symbol to spread and overlap with the pulses of adjacent symbols, thereby corrupting the decision variable at the receiver.

**Sampled Signal Expression (1 Mark):**
For a transmitted symbol sequence $\{a_k\}$ and overall system impulse response $h(t)$, the noise-corrupted received signal sampled at $t = mT$ is:

$$y(mT) = a_m h(0) + \sum_{\substack{k=-\infty \\ k \neq m}}^{\infty} a_k h\big((m-k)T\big) + n(mT)$$

**ISI Term Identification (1 Mark):**
The second term $\sum_{k \neq m} a_k h((m-k)T)$ is the **ISI term**, representing the unwanted contributions from all symbols *other than* the $m^{th}$ one.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State and explain Nyquist's first criterion for zero ISI in both time and frequency domains.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Time-Domain Condition (1.5 Marks):**
The sampled values of the overall system impulse response $h(t)$ must be:

$$h(nT) = \begin{cases} 1, & n = 0 \\ 0, & n \neq 0 \end{cases}$$

This means the impulse response must have zero crossings at all integer multiples of the symbol period $T$ (except at the origin).

**Frequency-Domain Condition (1.5 Marks):**
The folded spectrum of the overall response $H(f)$ must be a constant over the baseband:

$$\sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} H\left(f + \frac{m}{T}\right) = T \quad \text{for} \quad \vert f \vert \leq \frac{1}{2T}$$

The minimum required bandwidth is $W_{\min} = \frac{1}{2T}$ Hz, supporting a maximum signaling rate of $R_s = 2W$ symbols/second.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often forget the noise term $n(mT)$ when writing the sampled signal — losing 1–2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** For Nyquist's criterion, students mix up the *minimum* bandwidth formula $W = 1/(2T)$ with the *RC* bandwidth $W = (1+\alpha)/(2T)$ — be precise.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** In the folded spectrum derivation, the limits $\vert f \vert \leq 1/(2T)$ are often dropped, which is a frequently-missed 1-mark point.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** For the raised cosine spectrum, the roll-off factor is sometimes defined inversely; always use the *standard* form $\alpha = (W - W_0)/W_0$.

---

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]`

**Derive the mathematical model for the received signal in a baseband digital communication system in the presence of ISI and noise. Hence, explain Nyquist's first criterion for zero ISI in both time and frequency domains, with relevant diagrams.**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of the Received Signal Model (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Start with the discrete symbol sequence $\{a_k\}$ being applied to the TX pulse shaping filter $G_T(f)$ with impulse response $g_T(t)$. The TX output is:

$$s(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k \, g_T(t - kT)$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** This signal propagates through the channel $H_C(f)$ with impulse response $h_C(t)$. The channel output is the convolution:

$$x(t) = s(t) * h_C(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k \left[ g_T * h_C \right](t - kT)$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** The receiver filter $G_R(f)$ with impulse response $g_R(t)$ processes $x(t)$:

$$r(t) = x(t) * g_R(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k \left[ g_T * h_C * g_R \right](t - kT)$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Define the overall system impulse response as the cascade:

$$h(t) \triangleq g_T(t) * h_C(t) * g_R(t)$$

Substituting:

$$r(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k \, h(t - kT)$$

**Step 5 (2 Marks):** Adding the AWGN $n(t)$ and sampling at $t = mT$:

$$y(mT) = a_m h(0) + \underbrace{\sum_{k \neq m} a_k h((m-k)T)}_{\text{ISI}} + n(mT)$$

The first term is the desired signal, the second is the **ISI term**, and the third is the noise.

#### Part (b) — Nyquist's First Criterion (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Time-Domain Condition (3 Marks):**

For zero ISI, we require that $h(t)$ have **zero crossings at all integer multiples of $T$** except at the origin:

$$h(nT) = 0 \quad \text{for all integers } n \neq 0$$

**Intuition:** If the pulse crosses zero at every $nT$ except $n=0$, then sampling at $t = mT$ picks up only the contribution of the $m^{th}$ symbol; all other symbols contribute zero.

**Step 2 — Frequency-Domain Condition (4 Marks):**

The Fourier transform of a periodically-sampled impulse response (with zero ISI) is a constant. Equivalently, the *folded* spectrum must be flat:

$$\sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} H\left(f + \frac{m}{T}\right) = T \quad \text{for} \quad \vert f \vert \leq \frac{1}{2T}$$

**Proof Outline (2 Marks within the 4):** Convolve $h(t)$ with an impulse train of period $T$, apply the Nyquist sampling theorem in the frequency domain, and observe that the zero-ISI condition forces the result to a constant.

**Minimum Bandwidth Result (1 Mark):** The minimum bandwidth required is $W_{\min} = \frac{1}{2T}$ Hz, supporting a maximum symbol rate of $R_s = 2W_{\min} = \frac{1}{T}$ baud.

**Practical Realization (1 Mark):** The ideal sinc pulse corresponding to a brick-wall $H(f)$ is unrealizable. Practical systems use the **Raised Cosine spectrum** with roll-off $\alpha$, which still satisfies the folded-spectrum condition and trades excess bandwidth for realizability.

---

### Question B (Internal Choice): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper 2024]`

**Explain the Raised Cosine spectrum family for ISI control. Derive the expression for the impulse response $h(t)$ corresponding to a raised cosine spectrum. Discuss the effect of the roll-off factor $\alpha$ on bandwidth and time-domain pulse shape.**

#### Part (a) — The Raised Cosine Spectrum and Its Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** Start with the Nyquist folded spectrum condition. A family of spectra satisfying this is obtained by adding a cosine roll-off segment around the ideal brick-wall cutoff at $W_0 = 1/(2T)$:

$$H_{RC}(f) = \begin{cases} T, & \vert f \vert \leq W_0 - W_x \\ \frac{T}{2}\left[1 + \cos\left(\frac{\pi ( \vert f \vert - W_0 + W_x)}{2W_x}\right)\right], & W_0 - W_x < \vert f \vert < W_0 + W_x \\ 0, & \vert f \vert \geq W_0 + W_x \end{cases}$$

where $W_x = \alpha W_0 = \alpha/(2T)$ is the *excess bandwidth*.

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Define the roll-off factor:

$$\alpha = \frac{W_x}{W_0} = \frac{W - W_0}{W_0} \quad \Rightarrow \quad W = \frac{1+\alpha}{2T}$$

**Step 3 (3 Marks):** Take the inverse Fourier transform of $H_{RC}(f)$ to obtain the impulse response. The result is:

$$h_{RC}(t) = \text{sinc}\left(\frac{t}{T}\right) \cdot \frac{\cos\left(\frac{\pi \alpha t}{T}\right)}{1 - \left(\frac{2\alpha t}{T}\right)^2}$$

This impulse response has the property $h_{RC}(nT) = 0$ for $n \neq 0$ and $h_{RC}(0) = 1$, satisfying the zero-ISI condition.

#### Part (b) — Effect of Roll-off Factor $\alpha$ (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Bandwidth Effect (2 Marks):**
- $W(\alpha) = \frac{1+\alpha}{2T}$
- For $\alpha = 0$: $W = 1/(2T)$ Hz (minimum, ideal sinc).
- For $\alpha = 1$: $W = 1/T$ Hz (maximum, full cosine roll-off).
- **Conclusion:** Larger $\alpha$ → more bandwidth used.

**Step 2 — Time-Domain Pulse Decay (3 Marks):**
- For $\alpha = 0$ (sinc): the pulse decays as $1/t$ — very slow decay, requires precise sampling.
- For $\alpha = 1$ (full RC): the pulse decays as $1/t^3$ — much faster decay, more robust to timing jitter.
- The pulse is real-valued and even-symmetric.

**Step 3 — Practical Engineering Trade-off (2 Marks):**
- A larger $\alpha$ (e.g., 0.5) is preferred when:
  - Sampling-clock jitter is significant.
  - The channel has severe multipath.
  - Hardware implementation must be cheap.
- A smaller $\alpha$ (e.g., 0.1) is preferred when:
  - Bandwidth is the most precious resource (e.g., satellite links).
  - Very precise timing recovery is feasible.

**Numerical Example:** For a symbol rate $R_s = 1$ Msps:
- $\alpha = 0$: $W = 500$ kHz.
- $\alpha = 0.5$: $W = 750$ kHz.
- $\alpha = 1$: $W = 1$ MHz.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Signal Modeling for ISI**

- **ISI Definition:** Time-dispersive spreading of one symbol into adjacent symbol periods, caused by the **bandlimited** nature of the physical channel.
- **The Central Equation:** $y(mT) = a_m h(0) + \sum_{k \neq m} a_k h((m-k)T) + n(mT)$. Memorize the three terms: **Desired + ISI + Noise**.
- **Cascade Rule:** $H(f) = G_T(f) \cdot H_C(f) \cdot G_R(f)$; in time domain this is the convolution $h(t) = g_T(t) * h_C(t) * g_R(t)$.
- **Nyquist's Time-Domain Criterion:** $h(nT) = 0$ for all $n \neq 0$.
- **Nyquist's Frequency-Domain Criterion:** $\sum_m H(f + m/T) = T$ for $\vert f \vert \leq 1/(2T)$.
- **Minimum Bandwidth:** $W_{\min} = 1/(2T)$ Hz for ideal zero-ISI.
- **Raised Cosine Bandwidth:** $W_{RC} = (1+\alpha)/(2T)$ Hz.
- **Roll-off Factor:** $\alpha \in [0, 1]$; $0$ = ideal (unrealizable), $1$ = easy (bandwidth-wasteful).
- **Eye Pattern Opening:** $E_{\text{open}} \propto 1 - \sum_{n \neq 0} \vert h(nT) \vert / h(0)$.
- **RRC-RRC Pair:** Practical implementation splits the RC filter equally between TX and RX so that the *cascade* gives the desired RC response.
- **Key Standard:** Most modern systems (LTE, 5G NR, Wi-Fi) use a Root Raised Cosine (RRC) filter pair with $\alpha \in [0.1, 0.35]$.
- **Common Exam Pitfall:** Always include $n(mT)$; always specify limits on $\vert f \vert$ in Nyquist's criterion; always normalize $h(0) = 1$ when checking zero-ISI.
- **Practical Takeaway:** ISI cannot be *completely* removed in real systems — only minimized. The residual ISI determines the irreducible BER floor before the equalizer stage.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Raised Cosine Spectrum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT502 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Raised Cosine Spectrum

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Raised Cosine Spectrum (RCS)** is a **bandlimited pulse-shaping filter spectrum** used in digital communication systems to confine the transmitted signal bandwidth while achieving **zero Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI)**. It is characterized by a frequency response whose roll-off region follows a **cosine-shaped transition** between the flat passband and the sharp stopband, governed by the **roll-off factor (α)**.

Mathematically, the frequency response of an ideal raised cosine filter is defined piecewise as:

$$H(f) = \begin{cases} T, & \vert f \vert \leq \frac{1-\alpha}{2T} \\ \frac{T}{2}\left[1 + \cos\left(\frac{\pi T}{\alpha}\left(\vert f \vert - \frac{1-\alpha}{2T}\right)\right)\right], & \frac{1-\alpha}{2T} < \vert f \vert < \frac{1+\alpha}{2T} \\ 0, & \vert f \vert \geq \frac{1+\alpha}{2T} \end{cases}$$

Where:
- **T** = Symbol duration (in seconds)
- **α (roll-off factor)** = A dimensionless parameter ranging from **0 ≤ α ≤ 1**
- **R_s = 1/T** = Symbol rate (in symbols/second or baud)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Highway Speed Breaker (Speed Hump):**
>
> Imagine you are driving a car at a constant speed on a highway. At the entry of a small town, you encounter a **raised speed breaker** shaped like a smooth cosine curve. The breaker:
>
> 1. **Does not damage your car** (the rise is smooth, not abrupt) — analogous to **no ISI**.
> 2. **Reduces the spread of your journey** (you slow down only over a short region) — analogous to **controlled bandwidth**.
> 3. Has a **flat top** where you can drive smoothly — analogous to the **flat passband**.
> 4. Has an **excess width parameter** that determines how "wide" the breaker is — analogous to the **roll-off factor α**.
>
> The Raised Cosine Spectrum is exactly this: a **smooth, cosine-shaped frequency transition** that limits bandwidth gracefully without distorting adjacent data symbols.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value/Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Roll-off factor | **α** | **0 ≤ α ≤ 1** | Controls excess bandwidth |
| Nyquist bandwidth | **B_N** | **1/(2T)** Hz | Minimum theoretical bandwidth |
| Actual bandwidth | **B_T** | **(1+α)/(2T)** Hz | Total occupied bandwidth |
| Symbol rate | **R_s** | **1/T** baud | Rate of data transmission |
| Bit rate (binary) | **R_b** | **R_s** bps | For binary signaling |
| Sampling instant | **t = kT** | k = 0, ±1, ±2… | Zero-ISI sampling points |

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Scheme expects you to draw the raised cosine spectrum, derive its impulse response, and compute the bandwidth for given T and α.

---

## 1.4 Why is Raised Cosine Spectrum Important?

- **Eliminates ISI**: The zero crossings of the impulse response occur exactly at integer multiples of T (except at t = 0).
- **Bandwidth Control**: Allows engineers to trade off between bandwidth efficiency (low α) and implementation simplicity (high α).
- **Practical Realizability**: Forms the basis of practical filters like **Root Raised Cosine (RRC)** used in transmitters/receivers.
- **Used in**: 4G LTE, 5G NR, Wi-Fi, satellite communication, and digital modems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Principle — Nyquist's First Criterion

The raised cosine spectrum is a **direct implementation of Nyquist's First Criterion for Zero ISI**, which states:

> **A pulse-shaping filter achieves zero ISI if its frequency response satisfies:**
> $$\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} H\left(f + \frac{k}{T}\right) = \text{constant} \quad \forall f$$
>
> Equivalently, the **impulse response h(t) must have uniformly spaced zero crossings at t = kT for k ≠ 0**.

The raised cosine spectrum **exactly satisfies this criterion** because:
1. It has **odd symmetry about the point f = 1/(2T)** in the roll-off region (cosine is an even function folded at this point).
2. The resulting impulse response **h(t) ∝ sinc(πt/T)** multiplied by a correction factor that ensures zero crossings at t = ±T, ±2T, …

---

## 2.2 Frequency Domain Analysis — Why the "Cosine" Shape?

### Why a cosine and not a linear roll-off?

The choice of cosine in the roll-off region ensures that the resulting **time-domain impulse response has the slowest possible decay** (only as 1/t³), which makes timing jitter errors less catastrophic. A linear roll-off would decay as 1/t², leading to higher sensitivity to sampling errors.

### Three Distinct Frequency Regions:

**Region 1 — Flat Passband:**
$$H(f) = T \quad \text{for} \quad \vert f \vert \leq \frac{1-\alpha}{2T}$$

**Region 2 — Cosine Roll-off (Transition Band):**
$$H(f) = \frac{T}{2}\left[1 + \cos\left(\frac{\pi T}{\alpha}\left(\vert f \vert - \frac{1-\alpha}{2T}\right)\right)\right]$$
This is the **"raised cosine"** part. The cosine argument starts at **π** (when |f| = (1-α)/(2T), giving cos(π) = -1, so H = 0... wait, this needs careful re-examination):

- At |f| = (1-α)/(2T): argument = 0 → cos(0) = 1 → H = (T/2)(1+1) = T ✓ (matches Region 1)
- At |f| = (1+α)/(2T): argument = π → cos(π) = -1 → H = (T/2)(1-1) = 0 ✓ (matches Region 3)

**Region 3 — Stopband (Zero Transmission):**
$$H(f) = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad \vert f \vert \geq \frac{1+\alpha}{2T}$$

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Use of Raised Cosine Spectrum |
|---|---|
| **4G/5G Mobile** | LTE uses RRC filter with α = 0.22 to limit out-of-band emissions |
| **Wi-Fi (802.11)** | Uses pulse shaping with α typically 0.3–0.5 |
| **Satellite Comm** | Bandwidth-limited channels rely on RCS for efficiency |
| **Cable Modems** | QAM systems employ RRC filters at both TX and RX |
| **Digital Audio** | CD players use oversampling with RRC-like filters |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear almost every semester in KTU exams.**

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H(f) = T$ for $\vert f \vert \leq \frac{1-\alpha}{2T}$ | Flat passband region |
| 2 | $H(f) = \frac{T}{2}\left[1 + \cos\left(\frac{\pi T}{\alpha}\left(\vert f \vert - \frac{1-\alpha}{2T}\right)\right)\right]$ | Cosine roll-off region |
| 3 | $H(f) = 0$ for $\vert f \vert \geq \frac{1+\alpha}{2T}$ | Stopband region |
| 4 | $B_T = \frac{1+\alpha}{2T} = \frac{(1+\alpha)R_s}{2}$ | Total (absolute) bandwidth |
| 5 | $B_N = \frac{1}{2T} = \frac{R_s}{2}$ | Nyquist minimum bandwidth |
| 6 | $\text{Excess BW} = \frac{\alpha}{2T} = \frac{\alpha R_s}{2}$ | Bandwidth beyond Nyquist |
| 7 | $h(t) = \text{sinc}\left(\frac{\pi t}{T}\right) \cdot \frac{\cos\left(\frac{\pi \alpha t}{T}\right)}{1 - \left(\frac{2\alpha t}{T}\right)^2}$ | Impulse response |
| 8 | $h(0) = 1$ (normalized) | Peak value of impulse response |
| 9 | $h(kT) = 0$ for $k = \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$ | Zero-ISI condition |
| 10 | $h(t) \sim \frac{1}{t^3}$ for large t | Asymptotic decay rate |
| 11 | $\alpha = 1$ → Brick-wall filter becomes fully cosine | Maximum roll-off (Sinc-shaped impulse) |
| 12 | $\alpha = 0$ → Ideal brick-wall (Sinc impulse) | Minimum bandwidth = Nyquist |

---

## 2.5 Special Cases and Limiting Behavior

> [!NOTE]
> **Case 1: α = 0 (Ideal Nyquist / Brick-wall Filter)**
> - Bandwidth = 1/(2T) = Nyquist minimum
> - Impulse response: h(t) = sinc(πt/T)
> - Impossible to realize exactly (non-causal, infinite duration)

> [!NOTE]
> **Case 2: α = 1 (Full Roll-off / Sinc Impulse)**
> - Bandwidth = 1/T (double the Nyquist)
> - Impulse response: h(t) = sinc²(πt/T)
> - Easiest to approximate, but bandwidth-inefficient

> [!NOTE]
> **Case 3: 0 < α < 1 (Practical Compromise)**
> - Common choice: α = 0.25 to 0.5
> - Balances bandwidth efficiency and filter practicality

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Impulse Response h(t) of the Raised Cosine Filter

We want to find:
$$h(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} H(f) e^{j2\pi f t} \, df$$

Due to the even symmetry of H(f), we can write:
$$h(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} H(f) \cos(2\pi f t) \, df$$

Substituting the piecewise definition of H(f) and exploiting symmetry (H(f) is even):

$$h(t) = 2\left[\int_{0}^{\frac{1-\alpha}{2T}} T \cos(2\pi f t)\, df + \int_{\frac{1-\alpha}{2T}}^{\frac{1+\alpha}{2T}} \frac{T}{2}\left(1 + \cos\left(\frac{\pi T}{\alpha}\left(f - \frac{1-\alpha}{2T}\right)\right)\right)\cos(2\pi f t)\, df\right]$$

### Step 1: Evaluate the First Integral (Passband)

$$I_1 = \int_{0}^{\frac{1-\alpha}{2T}} T \cos(2\pi f t)\, df$$

Applying the standard integral:
$$\int \cos(2\pi f t)\, df = \frac{\sin(2\pi f t)}{2\pi t}$$

So:
$$I_1 = T \cdot \frac{\sin(2\pi f t)}{2\pi t} \Bigg|_{0}^{\frac{1-\alpha}{2T}}$$

$$I_1 = \frac{T}{2\pi t} \sin\left(2\pi t \cdot \frac{1-\alpha}{2T}\right) = \frac{T}{2\pi t} \sin\left(\frac{\pi t(1-\alpha)}{T}\right)$$

### Step 2: Substitution for the Second Integral (Roll-off Region)

Let us define a new variable to simplify the cosine argument. Let:
$$u = f - \frac{1-\alpha}{2T} \quad \Rightarrow \quad du = df$$

When $f = \frac{1-\alpha}{2T}$: $u = 0$
When $f = \frac{1+\alpha}{2T}$: $u = \frac{\alpha}{T}$

The second integral becomes:
$$I_2 = \int_{0}^{\frac{\alpha}{T}} \frac{T}{2}\left(1 + \cos\left(\frac{\pi T}{\alpha} u\right)\right) \cos\left(2\pi t \left(u + \frac{1-\alpha}{2T}\right)\right) du$$

Split this into two parts:
$$I_2 = \frac{T}{2}\int_{0}^{\frac{\alpha}{T}} \cos\left(2\pi t u + \frac{\pi t(1-\alpha)}{T}\right) du + \frac{T}{2}\int_{0}^{\frac{\alpha}{T}} \cos\left(\frac{\pi T u}{\alpha}\right) \cos\left(2\pi t u + \frac{\pi t(1-\alpha)}{T}\right) du$$

### Step 3: Evaluate the First Part of I₂

$$I_{2a} = \frac{T}{2} \cdot \frac{\sin\left(2\pi t u + \frac{\pi t(1-\alpha)}{T}\right)}{2\pi t} \Bigg|_{0}^{\frac{\alpha}{T}}$$

At $u = \frac{\alpha}{T}$:
$$2\pi t \cdot \frac{\alpha}{T} + \frac{\pi t(1-\alpha)}{T} = \frac{\pi t(2\alpha + 1 - \alpha)}{T} = \frac{\pi t(1+\alpha)}{T}$$

At $u = 0$:
$$\frac{\pi t(1-\alpha)}{T}$$

So:
$$I_{2a} = \frac{T}{4\pi t}\left[\sin\left(\frac{\pi t(1+\alpha)}{T}\right) - \sin\left(\frac{\pi t(1-\alpha)}{T}\right)\right]$$

### Step 4: Use the Product-to-Sum Identity

Recall the identity:
$$\sin A - \sin B = 2\cos\left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right)\sin\left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)$$

With $A = \frac{\pi t(1+\alpha)}{T}$ and $B = \frac{\pi t(1-\alpha)}{T}$:

$$\frac{A+B}{2} = \frac{\pi t}{T}, \quad \frac{A-B}{2} = \frac{\pi t \alpha}{T}$$

Therefore:
$$I_{2a} = \frac{T}{4\pi t} \cdot 2 \cos\left(\frac{\pi t}{T}\right) \sin\left(\frac{\pi t \alpha}{T}\right) = \frac{T \cos\left(\frac{\pi t}{T}\right) \sin\left(\frac{\pi \alpha t}{T}\right)}{2\pi t}$$

### Step 5: Combine with I₁

Recall that $I_1 = \frac{T}{2\pi t} \sin\left(\frac{\pi t(1-\alpha)}{T}\right)$. Using the same identity:
$$\sin\left(\frac{\pi t(1-\alpha)}{T}\right) = \sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{T}\right)\cos\left(\frac{\pi \alpha t}{T}\right) - \cos\left(\frac{\pi t}{T}\right)\sin\left(\frac{\pi \alpha t}{T}\right)$$

After careful trigonometric simplification (combining $I_1$ and $I_{2a}$), one arrives at:

$$2(I_1 + I_{2a}) = \frac{T \sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{T}\right) \cos\left(\frac{\pi \alpha t}{T}\right)}{\pi t}$$

### Step 6: Recognize the Standard Form

Using $\text{sinc}(x) = \frac{\sin(\pi x)}{\pi x}$, we can rewrite:

$$\frac{\sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{T}\right)}{\pi t} = \frac{1}{T} \cdot \text{sinc}\left(\frac{t}{T}\right)$$

So the **final closed-form impulse response** is:

$$\boxed{\,h(t) = \text{sinc}\left(\frac{t}{T}\right) \cdot \frac{\cos\left(\frac{\pi \alpha t}{T}\right)}{1 - \left(\frac{2\alpha t}{T}\right)^2}\,}$$

Or equivalently:
$$h(t) = \frac{\sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{T}\right)}{\frac{\pi t}{T}} \cdot \frac{\cos\left(\frac{\pi \alpha t}{T}\right)}{1 - \left(\frac{2\alpha t}{T}\right)^2}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Bandwidth Formula

Starting from the highest non-zero frequency in H(f):
$$f_{\max} = \frac{1+\alpha}{2T}$$

This is the **absolute bandwidth** (one-sided):
$$B_T = \frac{1+\alpha}{2T} \text{ Hz}$$

Substituting $R_s = 1/T$ (symbol rate):
$$B_T = \frac{(1+\alpha) R_s}{2} \text{ Hz}$$

For binary signaling, $R_b = R_s$, so:
$$B_T = \frac{(1+\alpha) R_b}{2} \text{ Hz}$$

The **spectral efficiency** is:
$$\eta = \frac{R_b}{B_T} = \frac{2}{1+\alpha} \text{ bits/sec/Hz}$$

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A binary communication system uses a raised cosine spectrum with symbol duration T = 1 μs and roll-off factor α = 0.5. Calculate: (a) Symbol rate, (b) Nyquist bandwidth, (c) Actual transmission bandwidth, (d) Spectral efficiency.

### Given:
- T = 1 μs = 10⁻⁶ s
- α = 0.5
- Binary signaling

### Solution:

**(a) Symbol rate:**
$$R_s = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{10^{-6}} = 10^6 \text{ symbols/sec} = 1 \text{ Mbaud}$$

**(b) Nyquist bandwidth:**
$$B_N = \frac{1}{2T} = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.5 \text{ MHz} = 500 \text{ kHz}$$

**(c) Actual transmission bandwidth:**
$$B_T = \frac{1+\alpha}{2T} = \frac{1 + 0.5}{2 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.5}{2 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.75 \text{ MHz} = 750 \text{ kHz}$$

**(d) Spectral efficiency (binary, R_b = R_s):**
$$\eta = \frac{R_b}{B_T} = \frac{10^6}{0.75 \times 10^6} = \frac{4}{3} \approx 1.33 \text{ bits/sec/Hz}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Verification:** For α = 0, η → 2 bits/s/Hz (maximum). For α = 1, η → 1 bit/s/Hz. Our α = 0.5 gives 1.33, which lies between these bounds. ✓

---

## 3.4 Verification of Zero-ISI Property

For t = kT where k is a non-zero integer:
$$h(kT) = \text{sinc}(k) \cdot \frac{\cos(\pi \alpha k)}{1 - (2\alpha k)^2}$$

Since sinc(k) = sin(πk)/(πk) = 0 for all non-zero integers k, we have:
$$h(kT) = 0 \quad \text{for all } k = \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$$

This **proves zero ISI** at all sampling instants. ✓

# 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Frequency Response H(f) — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Bit Stream] --> B[Symbol Mapper]
    B --> C[Pulse Shaping Filter H_f]
    C --> D[Modulator Carrier]
    D --> E[Channel]
    E --> F[Matched Filter H_f_conj]
    F --> G[Sampler at t=kT]
    G --> H[Decision Device]
    H --> I[Output Bit Stream]
    
    subgraph FR[Raised Cosine Spectrum H_f]
    FR1[Region 1: Flat Passband f ≤ 1-α / 2T] --> FR2[Region 2: Cosine Roll-off]
    FR2 --> FR3[Region 3: Stopband f ≥ 1+α / 2T]
    end
    
    C -.Uses.-> FR
    F -.Uses.-> FR
```

## 4.2 Three Frequency Regions Visual Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Frequency Domain H_f] --> B[Region 1: Passband]
    A --> C[Region 2: Roll-off]
    A --> D[Region 3: Stopband]
    
    B --> B1[Range: f from minus B_N to plus B_N times 1 minus alpha]
    B --> B2[Value: H_f equals T constant]
    B --> B3[Shape: Flat horizontal line]
    
    C --> C1[Range: B_N to B_T transition]
    C --> C2[Value: H_f equals T over 2 times bracket 1 plus cosine of piT over alpha times f]
    C --> C3[Shape: Smooth cosine arc]
    
    D --> D1[Range: f greater than B_T]
    D --> D2[Value: H_f equals 0]
    D --> D3[Shape: Zero baseline]
```

## 4.3 Trade-off Analysis: Roll-off Factor vs Performance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Choose Roll-off Factor alpha] --> B{Trade-off Decision}
    B -->|alpha = 0| C[Ideal Nyquist Filter]
    B -->|alpha = 0.25| D[Good Compromise LTE]
    B -->|alpha = 0.5| E[Balanced Choice]
    B -->|alpha = 1| F[Maximum Roll-off]
    
    C --> C1[Minimum BW = 0.5 per T]
    C --> C2[Non-realizable]
    
    D --> D1[BW = 0.625 per T]
    D --> D2[Used in 4G LTE]
    
    E --> E1[BW = 0.75 per T]
    E --> E2[Common in textbooks]
    
    F --> F1[Maximum BW = 1.0 per T]
    F --> F2[Sinc squared impulse]
```

## 4.4 Impulse Response Decay Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Time Domain h_t] --> B[Sample at t=0]
    A --> C[Zero Crossings at t=kT]
    A --> D[Decay Envelope]
    
    B --> B1[Peak value h_0 = 1]
    C --> C1[At t = T, 2T, 3T: h_t = 0]
    D --> D1[Decay rate = 1 over t cubed]
    D --> D2[Faster than sinc which is 1 over t]
```

# 📕 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Raised Cosine Spectrum. Mention its significance.
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Raised Cosine Spectrum is a bandlimited pulse-shaping filter spectrum used in digital communication systems to control intersymbol interference (ISI) and limit transmission bandwidth. Its frequency response exhibits a flat passband followed by a cosine-shaped roll-off region, characterized by the roll-off factor α (0 ≤ α ≤ 1).

**Significance:**
1. **Achieves Zero ISI** at sampling instants t = kT (Nyquist's first criterion).
2. **Bandwidth control** through α — total bandwidth = (1+α)/(2T).
3. **Practical realizability** — forms the basis of matched filter pairs (Root Raised Cosine) in real systems.
4. Used in 4G/5G, Wi-Fi, and satellite communications. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2: What is the role of the roll-off factor α in a Raised Cosine Spectrum?
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The roll-off factor **α** is a dimensionless parameter (0 ≤ α ≤ 1) that controls the **shape of the transition band** between passband and stopband in the raised cosine spectrum. Its roles are:

1. **Bandwidth Determination**: Higher α increases total bandwidth: B_T = (1+α)/(2T).
2. **ISI Control**: Ensures zero ISI regardless of α value (all valid α achieve Nyquist's first criterion).
3. **Implementation Trade-off**:
   - α = 0 → Minimum bandwidth, non-realizable.
   - α = 1 → Maximum bandwidth, easiest to implement.
   - 0 < α < 1 → Practical compromise (e.g., α = 0.25 in LTE).
4. **Time-Domain Decay**: Higher α leads to faster decay of impulse response tails. (3 marks)

---

## 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### 🅰️ Question A: Comprehensive Problem on Raised Cosine Spectrum
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> **Question:** *(a)* Derive the frequency response H(f) of a raised cosine spectrum filter, explaining each region clearly. Plot H(f) for α = 0.5 and T = 1 μs. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* A binary baseband transmission system uses raised cosine spectrum with symbol duration T = 4 μs and roll-off factor α = 0.25. Determine: (i) Symbol rate, (ii) Nyquist bandwidth, (iii) Absolute transmission bandwidth, (iv) Spectral efficiency, (v) Verify the zero-ISI condition by evaluating h(T). *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**The Raised Cosine Spectrum H(f) is derived from Nyquist's first criterion.** A pulse-shaping filter achieves zero ISI if its frequency response has odd symmetry about the Nyquist frequency f_N = 1/(2T) in the roll-off region. The raised cosine filter satisfies this by using a cosine transition.

**Three Regions of H(f):**

**Region 1 — Flat Passband:**
$$H(f) = T \quad \text{for} \quad \vert f \vert \leq \frac{1-\alpha}{2T}$$
Here the spectrum is flat at maximum value T. (1 mark)

**Region 2 — Cosine Roll-off (Transition):**
$$H(f) = \frac{T}{2}\left[1 + \cos\left(\frac{\pi T}{\alpha}\left(\vert f \vert - \frac{1-\alpha}{2T}\right)\right)\right]$$
This is the "raised cosine" portion. At |f| = (1-α)/(2T): cos(0) = 1 → H = T. At |f| = (1+α)/(2T): cos(π) = -1 → H = 0. (2 marks)

**Region 3 — Stopband:**
$$H(f) = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad \vert f \vert \geq \frac{1+\alpha}{2T}$$
Beyond this, no signal power is transmitted. (1 mark)

**Plot Description** (for α = 0.5, T = 1 μs):
- Passband edge: (1-0.5)/(2×10⁻⁶) = 0.25 MHz
- Stopband edge: (1+0.5)/(2×10⁻⁶) = 0.75 MHz
- The plot shows a flat plateau at H(f) = T = 10⁻⁶ between ±0.25 MHz, then a smooth cosine roll-down to zero at ±0.75 MHz, and zero beyond. (3 marks)

---

#### ✅ Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** T = 4 μs = 4 × 10⁻⁶ s, α = 0.25, binary signaling.

**(i) Symbol Rate:**
$$R_s = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{4 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.25 \times 10^6 = 250 \text{ kbaud}$$ (1 mark)

**(ii) Nyquist Bandwidth:**
$$B_N = \frac{1}{2T} = \frac{1}{2 \times 4 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.125 \text{ MHz} = 125 \text{ kHz}$$ (1.5 marks)

**(iii) Absolute Transmission Bandwidth:**
$$B_T = \frac{1+\alpha}{2T} = \frac{1+0.25}{2 \times 4 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.25}{8 \times 10^{-6}} = 156.25 \text{ kHz}$$ (1.5 marks)

**(iv) Spectral Efficiency (binary, R_b = R_s):**
$$\eta = \frac{R_b}{B_T} = \frac{250 \text{ kbps}}{156.25 \text{ kHz}} = 1.6 \text{ bits/sec/Hz}$$ (1.5 marks)

**(v) Zero-ISI Verification at t = T:**

Using $h(t) = \text{sinc}(t/T) \cdot \frac{\cos(\pi \alpha t/T)}{1 - (2\alpha t/T)^2}$:

At t = T:
- $\text{sinc}(T/T) = \text{sinc}(1) = \frac{\sin(\pi)}{\pi} = 0$
- Since the sinc factor is zero, $h(T) = 0$ regardless of the second factor.
- Therefore, **zero ISI is confirmed** at t = T. (1.5 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #1:** Students often forget to convert T into seconds before substitution. Always write T = 4 × 10⁻⁶ s explicitly in calculations. (–0.5 marks deducted if missing)
>
> **Pitfall #2:** Confusing Nyquist bandwidth B_N = 1/(2T) with total bandwidth B_T = (1+α)/(2T). These are different! B_N is the minimum (theoretical), B_T is the actual. (–1 mark if swapped)
>
> **Pitfall #3:** For spectral efficiency, students sometimes use R_s instead of R_b. For binary, R_b = R_s; for M-ary, R_b = R_s · log₂(M).

---

### 🅱️ Question B: Alternative Comprehensive Question
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

> **Question:** *(a)* Starting from the frequency response of the raised cosine spectrum, derive its impulse response h(t). Show that h(t) has zero crossings at t = kT, k ≠ 0, proving zero ISI. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* A QPSK system uses raised cosine pulse shaping with symbol rate R_s = 2 Mbps and roll-off factor α = 0.35. Find: (i) Bit rate, (ii) Nyquist bandwidth, (iii) Total transmission bandwidth, (iv) Bandwidth efficiency, (v) Excess bandwidth beyond Nyquist. *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Derivation of h(t):**

We compute the inverse Fourier transform of H(f). Due to the even symmetry of H(f):

$$h(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} H(f) e^{j2\pi f t}\, df = 2\int_{0}^{\infty} H(f) \cos(2\pi f t)\, df$$

Splitting the integral over the three regions:

$$h(t) = 2\left[\int_{0}^{\frac{1-\alpha}{2T}} T \cos(2\pi f t)\, df + \int_{\frac{1-\alpha}{2T}}^{\frac{1+\alpha}{2T}} \frac{T}{2}\left(1 + \cos\left(\frac{\pi T u}{\alpha}\right)\right)\cos\left(2\pi t\left(u + \frac{1-\alpha}{2T}\right)\right) du\right]$$

where the substitution $u = f - \frac{1-\alpha}{2T}$ has been used. (2 marks)

After applying the integral $\int \cos(2\pi f t) df = \frac{\sin(2\pi f t)}{2\pi t}$ and the product-to-sum identity $\sin A - \sin B = 2\cos\left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right)\sin\left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)$:

$$h(t) = \frac{\sin(\pi t/T)}{\pi t/T} \cdot \frac{\cos(\pi \alpha t/T)}{1 - (2\alpha t/T)^2}$$ (3 marks)

**Final result:**
$$\boxed{\,h(t) = \text{sinc}\left(\frac{t}{T}\right) \cdot \frac{\cos\left(\frac{\pi \alpha t}{T}\right)}{1 - \left(\frac{2\alpha t}{T}\right)^2}\,}$$

**Zero-ISI Proof:** At t = kT, k ≠ 0:
$$h(kT) = \text{sinc}(k) \cdot \frac{\cos(\pi \alpha k)}{1 - (2\alpha k)^2} = \frac{\sin(\pi k)}{\pi k} \cdot (\cdots) = 0$$

Since sin(πk) = 0 for all non-zero integers k, the impulse response has **zero crossings exactly at t = kT**, satisfying Nyquist's first criterion. ✓ (2 marks)

---

#### ✅ Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** R_s = 2 Mbps, α = 0.35, QPSK modulation.

**(i) Bit Rate (QPSK carries 2 bits per symbol):**
$$R_b = R_s \cdot \log_2(4) = 2 \times 2 = 4 \text{ Mbps}$$ (1.5 marks)

**(ii) Nyquist Bandwidth:**
$$B_N = \frac{R_s}{2} = \frac{2 \text{ Mbps}}{2} = 1 \text{ MHz}$$ (1 mark)

**(iii) Total Transmission Bandwidth:**
$$B_T = \frac{(1+\alpha) R_s}{2} = \frac{1.35 \times 2}{2} = 1.35 \text{ MHz}$$ (1.5 marks)

**(iv) Bandwidth Efficiency:**
$$\eta = \frac{R_b}{B_T} = \frac{4 \text{ Mbps}}{1.35 \text{ MHz}} \approx 2.96 \text{ bits/sec/Hz}$$ (1.5 marks)

**(v) Excess Bandwidth Beyond Nyquist:**
$$B_{excess} = B_T - B_N = 1.35 - 1.0 = 0.35 \text{ MHz}$$
Equivalently, $B_{excess} = \frac{\alpha R_s}{2} = \frac{0.35 \times 2}{2} = 0.35 \text{ MHz}$ ✓ (1.5 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #1:** For QPSK, students frequently forget that each symbol carries **2 bits** and report R_b = R_s. Always multiply by log₂(M). (–1 mark deducted)
>
> **Pitfall #2:** Excess bandwidth is asked as "beyond Nyquist" — this means B_T - B_N, not B_T itself.
>
> **Pitfall #3:** When α is given as 0.35, write it as 1.35 in the formula — students lose marks by writing 1 + 0.35 incorrectly.

---

## 🔥 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before the exam!**

- [ ] **Raised Cosine Spectrum** is a bandlimited pulse-shaping filter with three regions: flat passband, cosine roll-off, and stopband.
- [ ] **Roll-off factor α** ranges from 0 to 1 and controls the excess bandwidth.
- [ ] **Total bandwidth:** $B_T = \frac{(1+\alpha)}{2T} = \frac{(1+\alpha) R_s}{2}$
- [ ] **Nyquist bandwidth:** $B_N = \frac{1}{2T} = \frac{R_s}{2}$
- [ ] **Excess bandwidth:** $\frac{\alpha}{2T} = \frac{\alpha R_s}{2}$
- [ ] **Impulse response:** $h(t) = \text{sinc}(t/T) \cdot \frac{\cos(\pi \alpha t/T)}{1 - (2\alpha t/T)^2}$
- [ ] **Zero-ISI:** $h(kT) = 0$ for all k = ±1, ±2, ...
- [ ] **α = 0** → Ideal brick-wall (non-realizable); **α = 1** → sinc² pulse (bandwidth = 1/T)
- [ ] **Decay rate of h(t) is 1/t³** — much faster than sinc, hence less sensitive to timing jitter.
- [ ] For QPSK, M-PSK, M-QAM: $R_b = R_s \log_2 M$; for binary, $R_b = R_s$.
- [ ] **Spectral efficiency:** $\eta = \frac{R_b}{B_T} = \frac{2 \log_2 M}{1+\alpha}$ bits/sec/Hz.
- [ ] Raised cosine forms the **matched filter pair** (Root Raised Cosine at TX, RRC at RX) in practical systems.
- [ ] Always check units — T must be in **seconds**, R_s in **symbols/sec**, bandwidth in **Hz**.
- [ ] The frequency response has **odd symmetry** about f = 1/(2T) in the roll-off region — this is the key reason zero ISI is achieved.
- [ ] KTU 2024 expects derivations, plots (use ASCII or describe in words), and numerical problem-solving — be prepared for all three.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Equalization** is a signal-processing technique used at the receiver of a digital communication system to mitigate the distorting effects introduced by the channel, particularly **Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI)**. ISI arises when the impulse response of a bandlimited or multipath channel causes a transmitted symbol to spread (smear) into adjacent symbol intervals, corrupting the decision statistics at the sampler.

An **equalizer** is, formally, a filter whose transfer function is designed (or adaptively updated) to approximate the **inverse of the channel transfer function** $C(f)$ in the frequency domain, or equivalently to "compress" the dispersive channel impulse response $c(t)$ into an ideal impulse $\delta(t)$ (or a Nyquist pulse).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Why equalization is indispensable:**
> - In practice, the channel $H(f)$ is unknown a priori and time-varying (fading, mobility).
> - The matched filter alone cannot remove ISI produced by a non-flat $|H(f)|$ or non-linear phase.
> - Therefore, an **adaptive equalizer** is mandatory in high-speed wireless modems (LTE/5G NR), optical fiber links, and magnetic recording channels.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine a group of ten students shouting their names in a small echoey room. The echoes of the first student's name bleed into the second student's name, the second's into the third's, and so on — making it impossible for a listener to recognize who said what.

The **room is the channel**; the **echoes are ISI**.

An **equalizer** is like a smart "anti-echo" microphone placed at the listener's ear that has learned the shape of the room's echo pattern. By applying a carefully designed **anti-echo filter**, it subtracts the predicted echo from the incoming sound, restoring clean, distinguishable names.

In engineering terms:
- **Channel** = dispersive medium (multipath radio, ISI-limited wireline)
- **Equalizer** = adaptive inverse filter whose tap weights are continuously tuned to track channel variations
- **Goal** = produce an output with **open eye-pattern** and **minimum probability of error**

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metrics used in KTU problems:**
> - **ISI (Inter-Symbol Interference):** Unwanted overlap of adjacent symbols, measured as the eye opening amplitude.
> - **Bit Error Rate (BER):** $\mathrm{BER} = Q\!\left(\dfrac{d}{\sigma}\right)$ where $d$ is the half-eye opening and $\sigma$ is the noise std. deviation.
> - **Maximum Doppler Spread** $f_D$ and **Channel Coherence Time** $T_c \approx \dfrac{0.423}{f_D}$ — sets the **equalizer tracking speed**.
> - **Maximum Delay Spread** $\tau_{\max}$ and **Coherence Bandwidth** $B_c \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi\tau_{\max}}$ — sets the required **equalizer length**.
> - **Convergence factor** $\mu$ in LMS: $0 < \mu < \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{\max}}$ (bounded by largest eigenvalue of $R_{yy}$).

## 1.4 Classification of Equalizers (Overview)

| Type | Symbol | Structural Detail | KTU Relevance |
|------|--------|------------------|---------------|
| **Maximum-Likelihood Sequence Estimator (MLSE)** | MLSE | Implemented with the Viterbi algorithm; optimum in BER sense | High — direct 14-mark question favorite |
| **Linear Transversal Equalizer (LTE)** | LTE | FIR tap-delay line (N taps) | High — adaptive/LMS derivations |
| **Decision Feedback Equalizer (DFE)** | DFE | Feedforward (FFF) + Feedback (FBF) sections | High — wireless channel standard |
| **Passband / Baseband variants** | — | Real vs. complex tap weights | Moderate |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Equalization? — The Operational Logic

A baseband PAM system transmits symbols $d_k$ through a baseband-equivalent channel $c(t)$ with AWGN $n(t)$. The received signal is:

$$r(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} d_k \, h(t - kT) + n(t)$$

where $h(t)$ is the **pulse-shapes-with-channel** response (transmit filter × channel × receive filter).

Sampling at the symbol rate gives the discrete-time model used in every KTU derivation:

$$y_k = \sum_{j=0}^{L-1} c_j \, d_{k-j} + n_k$$

If $c_0 \neq 1$ and $c_1, c_2, \ldots \neq 0$, then **every decision on $d_k$ is corrupted by past symbols** $d_{k-1}, d_{k-2}, \ldots$ — this is precisely the ISI the equalizer must remove.

**The "Why" Behind Each Equalizer Architecture:**
1. **MLSE** — "If the channel is too dispersive, instead of trying to flatten it, *track* the most-likely symbol sequence using a trellis." Optimum but exponentially complex in $L$.
2. **LTE (FIR)** — "If complexity must be low, approximate the inverse of the channel by an FIR transversal filter and force the equalized pulse to Nyquist shape." Suboptimum but linear and simple.
3. **DFE** — "Once we have decided on $d_{k-1}$, use that decision to subtract its predicted ISI from $y_k$ via a feedback filter." Removes post-cursor ISI exactly.
4. **Adaptive** — "Channel is unknown and time-varying, so the tap weights $c_n(k)$ must be updated *online* using an algorithm such as LMS or RLS."

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat-Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master the table below — every KTU question on Module 3 (Equalization) ultimately reduces to one of these equations.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notation & Units |
|---|---------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | Discrete received model (baseband) | $y_k = \sum_{j=0}^{L-1} h_j d_{k-j} + n_k$ | $h_j$: combined pulse samples; $n_k$: AWGN sample (V) |
| 2 | Linear Equalizer output | $\hat{d}_k = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} w_n \, y_{k-n} = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{y}_k$ | $w_n$: complex tap weights; $\mathbf{w}, \mathbf{y}_k \in \mathbb{C}^{N}$ |
| 3 | Estimation error | $e_k = d_k - \hat{d}_k$ | Scalar complex error |
| 4 | MMSE cost function | $J = E[\vert e_k \vert^2]$ | Minimized at $\mathbf{w}_{opt} = R_{yy}^{-1} \mathbf{r}_{yd}$ |
| 5 | **LMS update rule** (core) | $w_n(k+1) = w_n(k) + \mu \, e_k \, y_{k-n}^*$ | $\mu$: step-size, $0 < \mu < 1/\lambda_{\max}$ |
| 6 | LMS in vector form | $\mathbf{w}(k+1) = \mathbf{w}(k) + \mu \, e_k \, \mathbf{y}_k^*$ | — |
| 7 | Zero-Forcing (ZF) solution | $\mathbf{w}_{ZF} = H^{-1} \mathbf{e}_0$ | Forces ISI = 0; ignores noise |
| 8 | MMSE solution | $\mathbf{w}_{MMSE} = (H^H H + \sigma^2 I)^{-1} H^H \mathbf{d}$ | Trades ISI vs. noise enhancement |
| 9 | Excess MSE (LMS) | $J_{ex}(\infty) \approx \dfrac{\mu \sigma_n^2 \, J_{\min}}{2}$ | Misadjustment $\mathcal{M} \approx \mu \, \mathrm{tr}(R_{yy})/2$ |
| 10 | DFE output | $\hat{d}_k = \sum_{n=0}^{N_1-1} w_n y_{k-n} - \sum_{m=1}^{N_2} b_m \tilde{d}_{k-m}$ | First sum: FFF; second: FBF with past decisions |
| 11 | Eye-pattern opening | $A_{open} = 1 - 2\sum_{n \neq 0} \vert h_n \vert$ | Worst-case vertical opening for binary signaling |
| 12 | Training MSE (least squares) | $J_{LS} = \sum_{k} \vert d_k - \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{y}_k \vert^2$ | Used in pre-equalizer block adaptation |

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

- **Mobile cellular systems (LTE, 5G NR)** use a Decision-Feedback Equalizer (or a turbo-equalizer) to handle multipath delay spreads up to $\tau_{\max} \approx 16\,\mu s$.
- **Magnetic recording (HDD)** relies on MLSE to combat severe ISI from intersymbol crowding.
- **DSL broadband** deploys Tomlinson-Harashima precoding (a transmit-side cousin of DFE) to prevent noise enhancement inherent to LTE.
- **Underwater acoustic modems** use adaptive LMS equalizers because the channel varies over seconds, not milliseconds.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why DFE outperforms LTE in severe ISI:** LTE must invert the channel, which can amplify noise strongly when the channel has deep spectral nulls. DFE, by using *decisions* in the feedback path, can perfectly cancel postcursor ISI *without* inverting nulls — the noise penalty is dramatically reduced.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Optimum (Wiener) Linear Equalizer Weights (MMSE)

We seek the FIR filter $\mathbf{w} = [w_0, w_1, \ldots, w_{N-1}]^T$ that minimizes the **mean-squared error** between the transmitted symbol $d_k$ and the equalizer output $\hat{d}_k$.

### Step 1: Write the equalizer output
The FIR equalizer output is a tapped-delay-line combination of the received samples:

$$\hat{d}_k = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} w_n \, y_{k-n} = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{y}_k$$

where $\mathbf{y}_k = [y_k, y_{k-1}, \ldots, y_{k-N+1}]^T$ is the received observation vector at time $k$.

### Step 2: Define the error
The estimation error (a complex scalar) is the difference between the desired symbol and the equalizer's estimate:

$$e_k = d_k - \hat{d}_k = d_k - \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{y}_k$$

### Step 3: Form the mean-square-error cost
Square the magnitude and take expectation with respect to the data and noise random processes:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = E\!\left[\,\vert e_k \vert^2\right] = E\!\left[\,e_k \, e_k^{*}\right]$$

### Step 4: Expand the cost
Substitute $e_k$ and distribute the expectation:

$$\begin{aligned}
J(\mathbf{w}) &= E\!\left[\,(d_k - \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{y}_k)(d_k^{*} - \mathbf{y}_k^H \mathbf{w}^{*})\right] \\
&= E\!\left[\,\vert d_k \vert^2\right] - E\!\left[\,d_k \, \mathbf{y}_k^H\right] \mathbf{w}^{*} - \mathbf{w}^T E\!\left[\,\mathbf{y}_k d_k^{*}\right] + \mathbf{w}^T E\!\left[\,\mathbf{y}_k \mathbf{y}_k^H\right] \mathbf{w}^{*}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the conversion of a single quadratic in $\mathbf{w}$ into a standard Hermitian form using the cross-correlation vector and the input autocorrelation matrix.

### Step 5: Define the correlation entities
Let us introduce the conventional names so the result is compact:

$$\mathbf{r}_{yd} = E\!\left[\,\mathbf{y}_k d_k^{*}\right] \qquad R_{yy} = E\!\left[\,\mathbf{y}_k \mathbf{y}_k^H\right]$$

Substituting these into Step 4 gives the canonical quadratic:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = \sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{r}_{yd}^H \mathbf{w}^{*} - \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{r}_{yd} + \mathbf{w}^T R_{yy} \, \mathbf{w}^{*}$$

### Step 6: Take the gradient with respect to $\mathbf{w}^{*}$
For complex variables, the optimality condition is:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{w}^{*}} = - \mathbf{r}_{yd} + R_{yy} \, \mathbf{w}^{*} = 0$$

This is the well-known **Wiener-Hopf equation** for the discrete FIR filter.

### Step 7: Solve the linear system
Provided $R_{yy}$ is positive-definite (i.e., the received signal is persistently exciting — guaranteed if $N \le L$ and noise is nonzero), invert:

$$\boxed{\mathbf{w}_{opt} = R_{yy}^{-1} \, \mathbf{r}_{yd}}$$

This is the **Wiener MMSE linear equalizer**. It is the theoretical *batch* optimum: it requires knowledge of second-order statistics of $\mathbf{y}_k$ and $d_k$. In practice, this matrix equation is never directly inverted in real time — the adaptive LMS algorithm is used to *iteratively* find this $\mathbf{w}_{opt}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — LMS Adaptive Algorithm (Full Step-by-Step)

The LMS algorithm replaces the explicit inversion of $R_{yy}$ with a stochastic gradient-descent recursion. The gradient is approximated by the *instantaneous* squared error $e_k e_k^{*}$, which is unbiased but noisy.

### Step 1: Start with the steepest-descent update
True gradient descent would update the weight vector as:

$$\mathbf{w}(k+1) = \mathbf{w}(k) - \mu \, \nabla_{\mathbf{w}^{*}} J(\mathbf{w})$$

The true gradient is $\nabla J = -2(\mathbf{r}_{yd} - R_{yy}\mathbf{w})$, but $\mathbf{r}_{yd}$ and $R_{yy}$ are unknown in a live receiver.

### Step 2: Replace the true gradient with the instantaneous estimate
Use the *instantaneous* error $e_k = d_k - \mathbf{w}^T(k)\,\mathbf{y}_k$ and the *instantaneous* correlation $\hat{\mathbf{r}}_{yd} \approx \mathbf{y}_k d_k^{*}$. The instantaneous gradient of $\vert e_k \vert^2$ with respect to $\mathbf{w}^*$ is:

$$\hat{\nabla} = \frac{\partial \vert e_k \vert^2}{\partial \mathbf{w}^{*}} = -\, e_k \, \mathbf{y}_k^{*}$$

This is a noisy but unbiased estimator of the true gradient.

### Step 3: Plug into the steepest-descent update
Replace the true gradient with its instantaneous estimate. This is the LMS rule:

$$\mathbf{w}(k+1) = \mathbf{w}(k) + \mu \, e_k \, \mathbf{y}_k^{*}$$

### Step 4: Write the per-tap scalar form (most often asked in KTU)
For tap index $n = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$:

$$\boxed{w_n(k+1) = w_n(k) + \mu \, e_k \, y_{k-n}^{*}}$$

This is the **canonical LMS weight-update** equation. It is the single most important formula in equalization — and almost always appears in Part A (3-markers) and Part B (14-markers) questions.

### Step 5: Stability and convergence condition
For mean-square convergence, the step-size must satisfy the so-called Widrow-Hopf stability bound:

$$0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\lambda_{\max}}$$

where $\lambda_{\max}$ is the largest eigenvalue of the input autocorrelation matrix $R_{yy}$. In practice, an even tighter engineering bound uses the trace (sum of eigenvalues, equal to the average input power):

$$0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\mathrm{tr}(R_{yy})} = \frac{1}{\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} E[\,\vert y_{k-n} \vert^2\,]}$$

### Step 6: Misadjustment (steady-state penalty)
The LMS algorithm does not reach the exact Wiener solution; it fluctuates around it, giving an *excess* MSE. The misadjustment is:

$$\mathcal{M} \approx \frac{\mu}{2} \, \mathrm{tr}(R_{yy})$$

The **excess MSE** at steady state is:

$$J_{ex}(\infty) \approx \frac{\mu \, \sigma_n^2 \, J_{\min}}{2}$$

A larger $\mu$ → faster convergence but larger misadjustment. A smaller $\mu$ → slower convergence but lower final MSE. This is the fundamental **convergence-vs-accuracy trade-off** of LMS.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Decision Feedback Equalizer (DFE) Output

A DFE cancels **post-cursor ISI** (tails of $h(t)$) using previously detected symbols.

### Step 1: Received signal with ISI
$$y_k = h_0 d_k + \underbrace{\sum_{j=1}^{L-1} h_j d_{k-j}}_{\text{post-cursor ISI}} + n_k$$

### Step 2: Split DFE into FFF + FBF
$$\hat{d}_k = \underbrace{\sum_{n=0}^{N_1-1} w_n y_{k-n}}_{\text{FeedForward Filter (FFF)}} - \underbrace{\sum_{m=1}^{N_2} b_m \, \tilde{d}_{k-m}}_{\text{Feedback Filter (FBF)}}$$

The term $\tilde{d}_{k-m}$ is the *decision* on $d_{k-m}$ (assumed correct with high probability at high SNR).

### Step 3: Substituting detected symbols
When all previous decisions are correct ($\tilde{d}_{k-m} = d_{k-m}$), the FBF perfectly cancels the post-cursor ISI. The remaining task of the FFF is to handle **pre-cursor ISI** ($h_{-j}$ for $j > 0$) and to shape the residual spectrum to a Nyquist pulse.

$$\hat{d}_k \approx h_0 d_k + n_k^{\text{filtered}}$$

This demonstrates why DFE outperforms LTE: no inverse of channel nulls is needed, hence no noise enhancement.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm vs. Complexity vs. Application

| Algorithm | Update Equation | Complexity / tap | Convergence Speed | KTU Use |
|-----------|-----------------|------------------|-------------------|---------|
| **LMS** | $w(k+1) = w(k) + \mu e_k y_k^{*}$ | $O(N)$ | Slow (depends on $\lambda_{\max}/\lambda_{\min}$) | Always tested |
| **Normalized LMS (NLMS)** | $w(k+1) = w(k) + \dfrac{\mu}{\Vert y_k \Vert^2} e_k y_k^{*}$ | $O(N)$ | Faster, $\mu \in (0, 2)$ | Common 7-mark sub-part |
| **RLS** | $w(k) = w(k-1) + R_y^{-1}(k)\, e_k y_k^{*}$ | $O(N^2)$ | Fast (≈ N iters) | Less common, advanced |
| **DFE (LMS-trained)** | Two coupled LMS updates (FFF, FBF) | $O(N_1+N_2)$ | Same as LMS | Frequent 14-mark question |
