# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Linear Transversal Equalizer (LTE)

The figure below shows an FIR equalizer with $N$ complex taps operating on the received sample stream $y_k$. The conjugate $\mathbf{y}_k^{*}$ is used so that the complex multiply matches the LMS rule.

```mermaid
graph LR
    src1["Received y_k"] --> delay1["z^-1"]
    delay1 --> delay2["z^-1"]
    delay2 --> delay3["z^-1"]
    delay3 --> delayN["z^-1 to z^-N+1"]
    src1 --> w0["w_0 conj"]
    delay1 --> w1["w_1 conj"]
    delay2 --> w2["w_2 conj"]
    delay3 --> w3["w_3 conj"]
    delayN --> wN["w_N-1 conj"]
    w0 --> sum["Sum Node"]
    w1 --> sum
    w2 --> sum
    w3 --> sum
    wN --> sum
    sum --> est["Equalized d hat k"]
    sum --> diff["Subtract"]
    train["Training d_k or decision"] --> diff
    diff --> err["Error e_k"]
    err --> lms["LMS Engine mu"]
    lms --> w0
    lms --> w1
    lms --> w2
    lms --> w3
    lms --> wN
    err --> out["Output to Decision"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Decision Feedback Equalizer (DFE)

The DFE uses a Feed-Forward Filter (FFF) on received samples, and a Feed-Back Filter (FBF) on past decisions, with both jointly adapted.

```mermaid
graph TB
    r["Received y_k"] --> fff["Feed-Forward Filter w_n, N_1 taps"]
    fff --> sumD["Adder"]
    past["Past Decisions d k-1 ... d k-N2"] --> fbf["Feedback Filter b_m, N_2 taps"]
    fbf --> sumD
    sumD --> eqOut["Equalized d hat k"]
    eqOut --> dec["Decision Device"]
    dec --> det["Detected d tilde k"]
    det --> past
    train["Training Symbol d_k"] --> diffD["Subtract"]
    eqOut --> diffD
    diffD --> errD["Error e_k"]
    errD --> lmsD["LMS / RLS Engine"]
    lmsD --> fff
    lmsD --> fbf
```

## 4.3 Adaptive Equalizer Training vs. Tracking Modes

A real adaptive equalizer operates in two modes. (1) **Training** — a known pseudo-random training sequence is transmitted; the equalizer uses the known $d_k$ to drive LMS. (2) **Decision-Directed Mode** — after training, the equalizer uses its own decisions as the "training" reference to track slow channel variations.

```mermaid
graph TD
    pow["Receiver Power-On"] --> tr["Training Mode: use known d_k as reference"]
    tr --> conv{"Converged?"}
    conv -- "No, large MSE" --> tr
    conv -- "Yes, MSE below threshold" --> dd["Decision-Directed Mode: use own decisions"]
    dd --> track{"Channel drift detected?"}
    track -- "No" --> dd
    track -- "Yes, MSE rises" --> tr
    tr -.->|"periodic re-training"| pow
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Equalizer in a Complete Receiver

This shows the place of the equalizer inside the broader digital receiver chain, mapping data-flow from antenna to recovered bits.

```mermaid
graph LR
    ant["Antenna"] --> lna["LNA + Downconversion"]
    lna --> mf["Matched Filter"]
    mf --> sr["Symbol-Rate Sampler"]
    sr --> eq["ADAPTIVE EQUALIZER LMS DFE"]
    eq --> dec["Decision / Detector"]
    dec --> demap["De-Mapper"]
    demap --> chdec["Channel Decoder Viterbi LDPC"]
    chdec --> out["Recovered Bits"]
    tr["Training Source"] -.->|"initial known d_k"| eq
    dec -.->|"decision directed ref"| eq
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023] Define the term *Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI)*. Why is equalization necessary in a digital communication receiver?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI)** is the phenomenon in which the energy of a transmitted symbol spreads (due to bandlimited or dispersive channel impulse response) into the time interval of adjacent symbols, so that the sample taken for the $k$-th symbol is corrupted by contributions from $d_{k-1}, d_{k-2}, \ldots$ **(1 mark)**.
Mathematically, with $h(t)$ as the combined pulse response:

$$y_k = \sum_{j \ne 0} h_j d_{k-j} + h_0 d_k + n_k$$

The terms $h_j d_{k-j}$ for $j \ne 0$ constitute the ISI **(1 mark)**.
**Equalization is necessary** because a bandlimited or multipath channel distorts the transmitted pulse shape, the matched filter alone cannot eliminate the resulting ISI, and the channel is typically time-varying — hence an *adaptive* inverse filter (equalizer) is inserted between the sampler and the decision device to restore a clean, ISI-free sample and minimize the bit error rate **(1 mark)**.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] State the LMS weight-update equation used in an adaptive linear equalizer. What is the condition on the step-size $\mu$ for convergence?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The LMS (Least Mean Squares) weight update for the $n$-th tap of the equalizer at the $k$-th iteration is **(2 marks)**:

$$w_n(k+1) = w_n(k) + \mu \, e_k \, y_{k-n}^{*}$$

where $e_k = d_k - \hat{d}_k$ is the error between the reference (training) symbol and the equalizer output, and $y_{k-n}^{*}$ is the complex conjugate of the received sample at tap $n$.
**Convergence condition** **(1 mark)**: For mean-square stability of the LMS algorithm, the step-size $\mu$ must satisfy:

$$0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\lambda_{\max}}$$

where $\lambda_{\max}$ is the largest eigenvalue of the input autocorrelation matrix $R_{yy} = E[\mathbf{y}_k \mathbf{y}_k^H]$. A practical engineering bound uses the trace, $0 < \mu < 1/\mathrm{tr}(R_{yy})$.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module-Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the operation of a **linear transversal equalizer (LTE)** as a discrete-time FIR filter. Derive the expression for the optimum tap-weight vector using the **MMSE criterion**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Starting from the MMSE cost function, derive the **LMS adaptive algorithm** for updating the equalizer tap weights. Discuss the effect of the step-size $\mu$ on convergence speed and steady-state misadjustment. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a) — (7 marks)

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**
The linear transversal equalizer consists of $N$ tapped delay-line sections. The received samples $y_k, y_{k-1}, \ldots, y_{k-N+1}$ are each multiplied by complex tap weights $w_0, w_1, \ldots, w_{N-1}$ and summed to form the equalized output $\hat{d}_k$. The error $e_k$ is obtained by subtracting $\hat{d}_k$ from a reference $d_k$ (training sequence) and is fed back to the LMS engine, which adjusts the tap weights. *(See the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1.)*

**Mathematical Model (1 mark):**

$$\hat{d}_k = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} w_n \, y_{k-n} = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{y}_k, \quad e_k = d_k - \hat{d}_k$$

**MMSE Derivation (4 marks):**
Define the cost $J(\mathbf{w}) = E[\,\vert e_k \vert^2\,]$. Expand:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = E[\vert d_k \vert^2] - E[d_k \mathbf{y}_k^H]\mathbf{w}^{*} - \mathbf{w}^T E[\mathbf{y}_k d_k^{*}] + \mathbf{w}^T E[\mathbf{y}_k \mathbf{y}_k^H] \mathbf{w}^{*}$$

Introduce $\mathbf{r}_{yd} = E[\mathbf{y}_k d_k^{*}]$ and $R_{yy} = E[\mathbf{y}_k \mathbf{y}_k^H]$. Setting the gradient $\partial J / \partial \mathbf{w}^{*} = 0$:

$$- \mathbf{r}_{yd} + R_{yy} \mathbf{w}^{*} = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \boxed{\mathbf{w}_{opt} = R_{yy}^{-1} \mathbf{r}_{yd}}$$

This is the **Wiener-Hopf MMSE solution** (1 mark for final boxed result + 1 mark for gradient step).

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b) — (7 marks)

**Stochastic-gradient motivation (1 mark):**
Direct computation of $\mathbf{w}_{opt} = R_{yy}^{-1} \mathbf{r}_{yd}$ requires knowledge of second-order statistics. Since these are unknown and time-varying, we use the **method of steepest descent** with the *instantaneous* gradient of $\vert e_k \vert^2$ in place of the true gradient.

**Instantaneous gradient (2 marks):**

$$\hat{\nabla} J = \frac{\partial \vert e_k \vert^2}{\partial \mathbf{w}^{*}} = - e_k \, \mathbf{y}_k^{*}$$

**Update rule (2 marks):**

$$\mathbf{w}(k+1) = \mathbf{w}(k) + \mu \, e_k \, \mathbf{y}_k^{*} \quad\text{or}\quad w_n(k+1) = w_n(k) + \mu \, e_k \, y_{k-n}^{*}$$

**Effect of step-size (2 marks):**
- *Small $\mu$*: $\;\;$ slow convergence, low steady-state misadjustment $\mathcal{M} \approx \mu \, \mathrm{tr}(R_{yy})/2$.
- *Large $\mu$*: $\;\;$ fast convergence, large misadjustment, and possible instability if $\mu \geq 1/\lambda_{\max}$.
- *Optimal $\mu$*: $\;\;$ a trade-off value near $1/\lambda_{\max}$ (or $1/\mathrm{tr}(R_{yy})$ in practice).

---

### QUESTION B — (14 Marks, Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the structure and operating principle of a **Decision Feedback Equalizer (DFE)**. How does it overcome the noise-enhancement problem of the linear transversal equalizer? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An adaptive linear equalizer with **2 taps** operates on a real-valued channel with impulse response samples $h_0 = 0.5,\; h_1 = 0.3,\; h_2 = 0.1$ (after matched filtering). A training sequence $d_k$ is sent: $d_0 = +1,\; d_1 = -1,\; d_2 = +1$ with noise-free reception.
**(i)** Form the received vector $R_{yy}$ and the cross-correlation vector $\mathbf{r}_{yd}$ using the standard data model. **(3 marks)**
**(ii)** Compute the **Wiener MMSE weight vector** $\mathbf{w}_{opt} = R_{yy}^{-1} \mathbf{r}_{yd}$ for a 2-tap equalizer. **(4 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a) — (7 marks)

**DFE Structure (2 marks):**
A DFE consists of two filters: a **Feed-Forward Filter (FFF)** of $N_1$ taps processing the received samples $y_k, y_{k-1}, \ldots$, and a **Feed-Back Filter (FBF)** of $N_2$ taps processing **past *detected* symbols** $\tilde{d}_{k-1}, \tilde{d}_{k-2}, \ldots, \tilde{d}_{k-N_2}$. The two filter outputs are summed (with the FBF subtracted) to yield the equalized sample $\hat{d}_k$, which is then sliced to produce the new decision $\tilde{d}_k$.

**Mathematical Model (2 marks):**

$$\hat{d}_k = \sum_{n=0}^{N_1-1} w_n y_{k-n} - \sum_{m=1}^{N_2} b_m \tilde{d}_{k-m}$$

The FBF cancels **post-cursor ISI** exactly (assuming correct past decisions).

**Noise-enhancement comparison (3 marks):**
The LTE must invert the channel $C(f)$; if $C(f)$ has deep spectral nulls, the inverse $1/C(f)$ has large peaks that amplify in-band noise — **noise enhancement**. The DFE, by contrast, *never inverts* the channel: it uses past *decisions* (a clean, noise-free reference, once decisions are correct) to subtract ISI. Hence the FFF is designed only to handle pre-cursor ISI and shape the residual response, avoiding the noise amplification that LTE suffers. The result: at low SNR and severe ISI, DFE achieves a lower BER than LTE.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b) — (7 marks)

**(i) Form $R_{yy}$ and $\mathbf{r}_{yd}$ (3 marks):**

The 2-tap equalizer observes $\mathbf{y}_k = [y_k, y_{k-1}]^T$. The combined channel-impulse samples give $y_k = 0.5 d_k + 0.3 d_{k-1} + 0.1 d_{k-2}$ (no noise).

Assuming independent, zero-mean, unit-variance training data $d_k$ (i.e., $E[d_k d_j] = \delta_{kj}$):

$$\begin{aligned}
r_{yd,0} &= E[y_k d_k] = 0.5 \cdot 1 + 0.3 \cdot 0 + 0.1 \cdot 0 = 0.5 \\
r_{yd,1} &= E[y_{k-1} d_k] = 0.3 \cdot 1 + 0.5 \cdot 0 + \ldots = 0.3
\end{aligned}$$

So $\mathbf{r}_{yd} = [0.5, 0.3]^T$. **(1 mark)**

For $R_{yy} = E[\mathbf{y}_k \mathbf{y}_k^T]$:

$$\begin{aligned}
E[y_k^2] &= 0.5^2 + 0.3^2 + 0.1^2 = 0.25 + 0.09 + 0.01 = 0.35 \\
E[y_k y_{k-1}] &= (0.5)(0.3) + (0.3)(0.5) + (0.1)(0.3) = 0.15 + 0.15 + 0.03 = 0.33 \\
E[y_{k-1}^2] &= 0.35
\end{aligned}$$

$$R_{yy} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.35 & 0.33 \\ 0.33 & 0.35 \end{bmatrix} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**(ii) Compute the Wiener weights (4 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\det(R_{yy}) &= (0.35)(0.35) - (0.33)(0.33) = 0.1225 - 0.1089 = 0.0136 \\
R_{yy}^{-1} &= \dfrac{1}{0.0136}\begin{bmatrix} 0.35 & -0.33 \\ -0.33 & 0.35 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{w}_{opt} = R_{yy}^{-1} \mathbf{r}_{yd} &= \dfrac{1}{0.0136}\begin{bmatrix} 0.35 & -0.33 \\ -0.33 & 0.35 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.3 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.0136}\begin{bmatrix} 0.175 - 0.099 \\ -0.165 + 0.105 \end{bmatrix} = \dfrac{1}{0.0136}\begin{bmatrix} 0.076 \\ -0.060 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \begin{bmatrix} w_0 \\ w_1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} +5.588 \\ -4.412 \end{bmatrix}}$$

**(Verification check — optional, 1 mark):** The inverse channel intuition matches: positive main tap $w_0$ and a negative secondary tap $w_1$ to cancel $h_1 = 0.3$. The product $w_0 \cdot h_0 \approx 5.588 \times 0.5 \approx 2.79$ — overshoots because MMSE *balances* ISI against noise; with zero noise here, $\mathbf{w}_{opt}$ becomes the *Zero-Forcing* inverse instead. To get the strict ZF answer, one would set $R_{yy} = H H^T$ (without the noise floor); KTU accepts either justification if correctly derived.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Sign error in DFE feedback:** The FBF is *subtracted*, not added. Writing $\hat{d}_k = \text{FFF} + \text{FBF}$ will cost 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the conjugate in LMS for complex signals:** The exact update is $w_n(k+1) = w_n(k) + \mu e_k y_{k-n}^{*}$, *not* $y_{k-n}$. Real-valued derivations are simpler but the KTU syllabus explicitly states *complex* baseband.
> 3. **Confusing MMSE with Zero-Forcing:** ZF sets ISI = 0 (enhances noise); MMSE minimizes the *total* error power. Always state which you are deriving.
> 4. **Matrix-inversion arithmetic:** KTU awards 1 mark for the *formulation* and 1 mark for the *arithmetic* of $R_{yy}^{-1}$. Writing only the final answer without showing $\det$ and cofactor expansion is treated as incomplete — expect to lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Stability bound:** Writing only $0 < \mu < 1$ is too loose; the KTU accepted answer is $0 < \mu < 1/\lambda_{\max}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Memorize Before the Exam**

- **ISI** = overlap of adjacent symbols caused by channel dispersion; equalizer = filter that *cancels* ISI.
- **Linear Transversal Equalizer (LTE)** = $N$-tap FIR filter; output $\hat{d}_k = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{y}_k$.
- **MMSE Wiener solution** = $\mathbf{w}_{opt} = R_{yy}^{-1} \mathbf{r}_{yd}$ (batch, requires $2^{nd}$-order statistics).
- **LMS adaptive rule** = $w_n(k+1) = w_n(k) + \mu e_k y_{k-n}^{*}$ (online, no matrix inversion).
- **Convergence bound** = $0 < \mu < 1/\lambda_{\max}$; misadjustment $\mathcal{M} \approx \mu\, \mathrm{tr}(R_{yy})/2$.
- **DFE** = FFF on received samples + FBF on past *decisions*; cancels post-cursor ISI; **no noise enhancement**.
- **MLSE (Viterbi)** = optimum in BER; complexity grows exponentially with channel length $L$.
- **Two operating modes** = *Training* (known $d_k$) → *Decision-Directed* (use own decisions as reference).
- **LTE vs DFE trade-off** = LTE simple, suffers noise enhancement at spectral nulls; DFE superior in severe ISI.
- **Algorithm comparison** = LMS: $O(N)$, slow; NLMS: $O(N)$, faster, $\mu \in (0,2)$; RLS: $O(N^2)$, fastest.
- **Convergence vs accuracy** = small $\mu$ → slow, accurate; large $\mu$ → fast, noisy, may diverge.
- **Real-world use** = LTE/5G NR (DFE), HDD read channels (MLSE), DSL (Tomlinson-Harashima precoding).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Zero Forcing Equalizer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT502 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Zero Forcing Equalizer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Zero Forcing Equalizer — Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PCECT502 – Analog and Digital Communication**, Module 3 addresses the critical problem of **channel-induced distortion** in bandlimited digital communication systems. The **Zero Forcing Equalizer (ZFE)** is a linear transversal filter placed at the receiver whose coefficients are designed to **completely eliminate Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI)** by forcing the combined impulse response of the channel-and-equalizer cascade to satisfy the **Nyquist zero-ISI criterion** at the symbol sampling instants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Board Standard):**
> A **Zero Forcing Equalizer** is a finite-duration impulse response (FIR) discrete-time filter whose tap weights $\{c_n\}$ are chosen such that the convolution of the equalizer coefficients with the sampled channel impulse response $\{h_n\}$ yields a response of the form:
> $$q_n = h_n * c_n = \begin{cases} 1, & n = 0 \\ 0, & n \neq 0 \end{cases}$$
> That is, the equalizer inverts the channel frequency response at the sampling instants, forcing all ISI samples to **zero** while preserving the desired symbol at $n=0$.

The word **"Zero Forcing"** literally means *forcing the intersymbol interference components to zero*, leaving only the intended symbol as the equalizer output at the decision instant.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuitive Picture

Imagine you are shouting instructions to a friend across a **large, echoey canyon**. Your single clear voice reaches your friend as a *smear* — the original shout plus several delayed echoes (reflections from canyon walls). The echoes overlap and garble the message, just like **Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI)** in a multipath communication channel.

A **Zero Forcing Equalizer** is like giving your friend a special **"echo-cancellation headset"** that has been pre-calibrated to play back an *exact anti-echo* — a delayed, scaled, and inverted version of the canyon response. When the headset plays this anti-echo into your friend's ear canal, every echo is **annihilated** precisely at the moment the listener is trying to focus on a particular word. Only the original, undistorted word remains.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Realisations from the Analogy:**
> - The headset must "know" the canyon's echo pattern beforehand (i.e., the channel must be **estimated** or **known**).
> - The anti-echo is applied *after* the signal arrives (i.e., equalization is done at the **receiver**).
> - The cancellation is exact only at the sampling instants (i.e., zero forcing is a **discrete-time** technique applied to **sampled** signals).

## 1.3 The Channel Distortion Problem — Why We Need an Equalizer

In a bandlimited, dispersive communication channel, the transmitted pulse $s(t)$ undergoes temporal spreading. As a result, the received signal $r(t)$ is the convolution:

$$r(t) = s(t) * c(t) + n(t)$$

where $c(t)$ is the channel's continuous-time impulse response and $n(t)$ is **additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN)**.

At the receiver, after matched filtering and symbol-rate sampling (at $t = kT$, where $T$ is the symbol period), the discrete-time received sequence is:

$$y_k = \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} h_n \, x_{k-n} + \eta_k$$

Here, $h_n$ are the **sampled channel coefficients** (channel taps), $L$ is the channel memory length, $x_k$ is the transmitted symbol, and $\eta_k$ is the noise sample. The summation shows that the current received sample $y_k$ is contaminated by **adjacent symbols** $x_{k-1}, x_{k-2}, \ldots, x_{k-L+1}$ — this contamination is **ISI**.

## 1.4 The Channel Model in Z-Domain

For a discrete-time channel with finite impulse response, the z-domain transfer function is:

$$H(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} h_n \, z^{-n}$$

The equalizer $C(z)$ is cascaded such that the **overall response** is:

$$Q(z) = H(z) \cdot C(z)$$

The ZFE criterion demands that $Q(z) = 1$ (a perfect delay-free link), which gives:

$$C(z) = \frac{1}{H(z)} = H^{-1}(z)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Zero Forcing in One Line:** The ZFE is the **direct inverse filter** of the discrete-time channel. It perfectly cancels ISI at the cost of potentially enhancing noise.

## 1.5 Standard KTU Notation (Memorise This)

| Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $h_n$ | $n$-th tap of the channel impulse response |
| $c_n$ | $n$-th tap of the equalizer (FIR filter) |
| $L$ | Channel memory (number of taps) |
| $M$ | Number of equalizer taps |
| $x_k$ | $k$-th transmitted symbol |
| $y_k$ | $k$-th received (sampled) symbol |
| $\eta_k$ | Noise sample at time $kT$ |
| $I_k$ | ISI contribution at sampling instant $kT$ |
| $\mathcal{D}$ | Peak Distortion (ISI metric) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Zero Forcing Equalizer — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Why" Behind ZFE

The ZFE operates on a single, elegant idea: **invert the channel**. If the channel spreads each transmitted symbol into $L$ time-adjacent copies with weights $h_0, h_1, \ldots, h_{L-1}$, then an equalizer that *cancels* this spreading must produce an output whose value is determined **only** by the symbol intended for the current sampling instant.

This is achieved by solving a system of linear equations — one equation per ISI position to be zeroed. The matrix formulation is central to the KTU board examination.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The equalizer is implemented as a **linear tapped-delay-line filter** (transversal structure):

1. **Step 1 — Sample the received signal:** The matched-filter output is sampled once per symbol at instants $t = kT$, producing the sequence $\{y_k\}$.

2. **Step 2 — Store the last $M$ samples:** The equalizer's delay line stores $y_k, y_{k-1}, y_{k-2}, \ldots, y_{k-M+1}$ in its $M$ memory elements.

3. **Step 3 — Multiply by tap weights:** Each stored sample is multiplied by a coefficient $c_0, c_1, \ldots, c_{M-1}$.

4. **Step 4 — Sum to form the decision variable:** The equalizer output is $\hat{x}_k = \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} c_n \, y_{k-n}$.

5. **Step 5 — Apply the zero-forcing criterion:** The taps $\{c_n\}$ are chosen so that $\hat{x}_k = x_k$ (no ISI), assuming $\eta_k = 0$.

## 2.3 The Peak Distortion Criterion

To make the ISI problem mathematically tractable, define the **Peak Distortion** at the sampling instant:

$$\mathcal{D} = \frac{1}{\vert h_0 \vert} \sum_{\substack{n = -N_1 \\ n \neq 0}}^{N_2} \vert q_n \vert$$

where $q_n = \sum_{j} h_j \, c_{n-j}$ is the **combined channel-plus-equalizer response** at sampling instant $n$.

> [!NOTE]
> The peak distortion is essentially the **sum of all ISI components** (everything except the desired sample $q_0$) normalised by the desired sample's magnitude. The ZFE forces $\mathcal{D} = 0$.

## 2.4 The Zero-Forcing Solution — Matrix Form

For a channel with $L$ taps and an equalizer with $M$ taps (let $M = L$ for the basic case), the zero-forcing conditions are:

$$q_n = \sum_{j=0}^{L-1} h_j \, c_{n-j} = \begin{cases} 1, & n = 0 \\ 0, & n = \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots, \pm (L-1) \end{cases}$$

In matrix form, defining the **channel convolution matrix** $\mathbf{H}$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} h_0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ h_1 & h_0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ h_2 & h_1 & h_0 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ h_{L-1} & h_{L-2} & h_{L-3} & \cdots & h_0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} c_0 \\ c_1 \\ c_2 \\ \vdots \\ c_{L-1} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

The solution is $\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{H}^{-1} \mathbf{e}_0$, where $\mathbf{e}_0 = [1, 0, 0, \ldots, 0]^T$.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Units / Notes** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $y_k = \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} h_n x_{k-n} + \eta_k$ | Discrete channel model with ISI | Sample index $k$ is dimensionless |
| $H(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} h_n z^{-n}$ | Z-domain channel response | Valid for $\vert z \vert > $ pole radius |
| $C(z) = 1/H(z)$ | Zero Forcing Equalizer transfer function | Exists only if $H(z)$ has no zeros on unit circle |
| $q_n = \sum_{j} h_j c_{n-j}$ | Combined channel–equalizer response | Convolution of $h$ and $c$ |
| $q_0 = 1, \; q_n = 0$ for $n \neq 0$ | Zero Forcing criterion | Nyquist zero-ISI condition |
| $\mathcal{D} = \frac{1}{\vert h_0 \vert} \sum_{n \neq 0} \vert q_n \vert$ | Peak Distortion | $\mathcal{D} = 0$ for ideal ZFE |
| $\sigma_{\eta,\text{out}}^2 = \sigma_\eta^2 \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} \vert c_n \vert^2$ | Output noise variance after ZFE | $\sigma_\eta^2$ is input noise variance (Watts) |
| $\text{SNR}_{\text{out}} = \frac{\sigma_x^2}{\sigma_\eta^2 \sum \vert c_n \vert^2}$ | Output SNR | Decreases if $\sum \vert c_n \vert^2$ is large |
| $\text{BER} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2 E_b}{N_0 \sum \vert c_n \vert^2}}\right)$ | Bit Error Rate (BPSK, ZFE) | $E_b/N_0$ is the bit energy per noise PSD |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** The ZFE *always* eliminates ISI ($\mathcal{D} = 0$), but it can **enhance noise** when $H(z)$ has a deep null (spectral zero) near the unit circle. The ZFE must place a pole of $C(z)$ at that null, blowing up noise at those frequencies. This is the fundamental **noise-enhancement trade-off** of ZFE.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Zero Forcing Equalizers are widely used in:

- **Wireline channels** (e.g., copper telephone lines, DSL) where SNR is high and ISI is the dominant impairment.
- **Historical magnetic recording read channels** where the channel response is well-modelled and stable.
- **Teaching and benchmarking** — ZFE serves as the baseline against which more sophisticated equalizers (MMSE, DFE, adaptive LMS/RLS) are compared.

> [!NOTE]
> In modern wireless systems (4G/5G LTE, Wi-Fi), **Decision-Feedback Equalizers (DFE)** and **MMSE-based turbo equalizers** are preferred because they balance ISI cancellation with noise enhancement — but ZFE remains a *conceptual cornerstone* in every KTU 2024 Scheme communication syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Zero Forcing Equalizer — Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Equalizer Coefficient Equation from the Convolution Constraint

**Goal:** Derive the linear system of equations that yields the ZFE coefficients $\{c_n\}$ for an $L$-tap channel and an $L$-tap equalizer.

**Starting Point:** The combined channel–equalizer response at the equalizer output is the discrete convolution:

$$q_n = \sum_{j=0}^{L-1} h_j \, c_{n-j}, \quad n = -(L-1), \ldots, 0, \ldots, +(L-1)$$

We define the equalizer taps $c_n$ to be nonzero only for $n = 0, 1, \ldots, L-1$, so the equation above, expanded for each $n$, gives the **triangular system**.

**Step 1 — Expand for $n = 0$ (the desired sample):**

$$q_0 = h_0 c_0 + h_1 c_{-1} + h_2 c_{-2} + \cdots$$

Since $c_n = 0$ for $n < 0$, this simplifies to:

$$q_0 = h_0 c_0$$

**Step 2 — Expand for $n = 1$ (the first post-cursor ISI position):**

$$q_1 = h_0 c_1 + h_1 c_0$$

The ZFE demands $q_1 = 0$, giving:

$$h_0 c_1 + h_1 c_0 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad c_1 = -\frac{h_1}{h_0} c_0$$

**Step 3 — Expand for $n = 2$ (the second post-cursor ISI position):**

$$q_2 = h_0 c_2 + h_1 c_1 + h_2 c_0$$

Setting $q_2 = 0$:

$$h_0 c_2 + h_1 c_1 + h_2 c_0 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad c_2 = -\frac{h_1 c_1 + h_2 c_0}{h_0}$$

**Step 4 — General pattern:** For $n \geq 1$, the recursive relation is:

$$c_n = -\frac{1}{h_0} \sum_{j=1}^{\min(n, L-1)} h_j \, c_{n-j}$$

**Step 5 — Final closed form (ZFE on the $L$-tap channel):** Choosing $c_0 = 1/h_0$ to enforce $q_0 = 1$:

$$c_0 = \frac{1}{h_0}, \quad c_n = -\frac{1}{h_0} \sum_{j=1}^{\min(n, L-1)} h_j \, c_{n-j}, \quad n = 1, 2, \ldots, L-1$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Worked Numerical Example (2-Tap Channel, Classic KTU Problem)

**Given:** A discrete-time channel has two taps: $h_0 = 0.8$ and $h_1 = 0.6$. Design a **2-tap Zero Forcing Equalizer** ($c_0, c_1$) to eliminate ISI.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the ISI elimination equations.** For a 2-tap channel and 2-tap equalizer, the only ISI position to zero (besides $n=0$) is $n=1$:

$$q_0 = h_0 c_0 = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad c_0 = \frac{1}{h_0} = \frac{1}{0.8} = 1.25$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $c_1$:**

$$q_1 = h_0 c_1 + h_1 c_0 = 0$$

$$c_1 = -\frac{h_1 c_0}{h_0} = -\frac{h_1}{h_0^2} = -\frac{0.6}{(0.8)^2} = -\frac{0.6}{0.64} = -0.9375$$

**Step 3 — Verification by convolution:**

$$q_0 = h_0 c_0 = (0.8)(1.25) = 1.0 \quad \checkmark$$

$$q_1 = h_0 c_1 + h_1 c_0 = (0.8)(-0.9375) + (0.6)(1.25) = -0.75 + 0.75 = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Compute the equalized output signal (assume input $x_0 = 1, x_1 = -1$):**

Equalizer output at $k=0$: $\hat{x}_0 = c_0 y_0 + c_1 y_{-1}$. Taking $y_{-1} = 0$ (no prior symbols):

$$\hat{x}_0 = 1.25 \cdot y_0 = 1.25 \cdot (h_0 x_0) = 1.25 \cdot (0.8 \cdot 1) = 1.0$$

**Step 5 — Noise enhancement factor:** The output noise variance gain is:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{1} \vert c_n \vert^2 = (1.25)^2 + (-0.9375)^2 = 1.5625 + 0.8789 = 2.4414$$

The noise is amplified by a factor of **2.4414**, which is the price we pay for zero ISI.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: ZFE Output Noise Variance (The Noise Enhancement Penalty)

**Given:** The input noise $\eta_k$ has variance $\sigma_\eta^2$, and the equalizer has $M$ taps.

**Derivation:**

The equalizer output is:

$$\hat{x}_k = \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} c_n \, y_{k-n} = \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} c_n \left( \sum_{j=0}^{L-1} h_j x_{k-n-j} + \eta_{k-n} \right)$$

The noise component is:

$$\xi_k = \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} c_n \, \eta_{k-n}$$

Since the $\eta_k$ are independent and identically distributed with variance $\sigma_\eta^2$:

$$\sigma_\xi^2 = E[\xi_k^2] = E\!\left[ \left( \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} c_n \eta_{k-n} \right) \left( \sum_{m=0}^{M-1} c_m \eta_{k-m} \right) \right]$$

Because $E[\eta_{k-n}\eta_{k-m}] = \sigma_\eta^2 \delta_{nm}$ (uncorrelated noise):

$$\sigma_\xi^2 = \sigma_\eta^2 \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} c_n^2$$

**This is the canonical ZFE noise-enhancement expression used in KTU derivations.**

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Equivalence Between ZFE and Channel Inversion in Z-Domain

**Given:** Channel $H(z) = h_0 + h_1 z^{-1}$.

**Derivation:**

The ZFE transfer function is $C(z) = 1/H(z)$. Performing long division (or polynomial inversion):

$$C(z) = \frac{1}{h_0 + h_1 z^{-1}} = \frac{1}{h_0} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + (h_1/h_0) z^{-1}}$$

Using the geometric series expansion $\frac{1}{1+az^{-1}} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (-a)^n z^{-n}$ (valid for $\vert z^{-1} \vert < 1/\vert a \vert$):

$$C(z) = \frac{1}{h_0} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left( -\frac{h_1}{h_0} \right)^n z^{-n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} c_n z^{-n}$$

This produces the **infinite-impulse-response (IIR)** ZFE with taps:

$$c_n = \frac{1}{h_0} \left( -\frac{h_1}{h_0} \right)^n$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The ZFE for a 2-tap channel is *strictly* IIR (infinite-length). Practical implementations **truncate** the IIR to a finite-length FIR equalizer, which causes a small residual ISI — this is a frequent KTU exam question.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Table

| **Scenario** | **Equalizer Type** | **ISI After EQ** | **Noise After EQ** | **Stability** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 2-tap channel, infinite-tap ZFE | IIR | **Zero (exact)** | $\sigma_\eta^2 \sum \vert c_n \vert^2$ | Stable iff $\vert h_1 \vert < \vert h_0 \vert$ |
| 2-tap channel, 2-tap FIR ZFE | FIR (truncated) | **Zero (exact)** | $\sigma_\eta^2 (\vert c_0 \vert^2 + \vert c_1 \vert^2)$ | Always stable |
| 2-tap channel, 3-tap FIR ZFE | FIR (over-sized) | Zero at more positions | Higher noise gain | Always stable |
| Channel with zero on unit circle | ZFE impossible | Undefined | Infinite | **Unstable** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Zero Forcing Equalizer — Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Zero Forcing Equalizer in a Communication Receiver

The diagram below shows the complete ZFE receiver chain, from antenna matched filter through decision device, with the ZFE highlighted as a transversal filter.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TX["Transmitted Symbol xk"] --> CH["Channel H of z"]
    NOISE["AWGN Noise nk"] --> SUM1(("+"))
    CH --> SUM1
    SUM1 --> RX["Received Sequence yk"]
    RX --> TAP1["Delay z^-1"]
    TAP1 --> TAP2["Delay z^-1"]
    TAP2 --> TAP3["Delay z^-1"]
    TAP3 --> TAPDASH["More Delays"]

    RX --> MUL0["x c0"]
    TAP1 --> MUL1["x c1"]
    TAP2 --> MUL2["x c2"]
    TAP3 --> MUL3["x c3"]
    TAPDASH --> MULN["x cM-1"]

    MUL0 --> ADDSUM(("Sigma"))
    MUL1 --> ADDSUM
    MUL2 --> ADDSUM
    MUL3 --> ADDSUM
    MULN --> ADDSUM

    ADDSUM --> DEC["Decision Device"]
    DEC --> OUT["Estimated Symbol xhat k"]

    style CH fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style ADDSUM fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style DEC fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style OUT fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The received sequence $y_k$ enters the equalizer's **tapped delay line**.
- Each tap output is multiplied by a ZFE coefficient $c_0, c_1, \ldots, c_{M-1}$.
- The summed result $\hat{x}_k$ is fed to the **decision device** (slicer), which estimates the transmitted symbol.

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: ZFE Inside a Digital Communication Receiver

The following flowchart illustrates the **functional architecture** showing the ZFE's position within a complete receiver, including the channel estimation feedback path (essential in practice, since the ZFE needs to know $H(z)$).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TRANSMITTER["Transmitter Section"]
        A1["Source Bits"] --> A2["Mapper BPSK or QAM"]
        A2 --> A3["Pulse Shaping Filter"]
    end

    subgraph CHANNEL["Channel Section"]
        B1["Multipath Channel H of z"]
        B2["AWGN Noise Source"]
        B1 --> ADDNODE(("+"))
        B2 --> ADDNODE
    end

    subgraph RECEIVER["Receiver Section with ZFE"]
        C1["Matched Filter"]
        C2["Symbol Rate Sampler"]
        C3["Channel Estimator LS or LMS"]
        C4["Zero Forcing Equalizer Tap Calculator"]
        C5["FIR Equalizer Filter"]
        C6["Decision Device Slicer"]
        C7["Output Symbol Estimate"]

        C1 --> C2 --> C5
        C3 --> C4 --> C5
        C5 --> C6 --> C7
    end

    A3 --> B1
    ADDNODE --> C1

    style B1 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C4 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style C5 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C6 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C7 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Architecture Insights for KTU Board Exam:**

- The **Channel Estimator** block is critical: it estimates $H(z)$ (e.g., using a known training sequence with the **Least Squares (LS)** or **LMS** adaptive algorithm).
- The estimator feeds the **Tap Calculator** which solves $\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{H}^{-1} \mathbf{e}_0$ to obtain the ZFE coefficients.
- The resulting FIR filter $\mathbf{c} = (c_0, c_1, \ldots, c_{M-1})$ equalises the incoming stream in real time.

## 4.3 Functional Comparison: ZFE vs. Channel Response

The schematic below compares the **channel impulse response** $h_n$ and the **equaliser impulse response** $c_n$, highlighting how they combine to form a single clean sample.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CHANNEL_RESP["Channel Impulse Response hn"]
        H0["h0 = 0.8"]
        H1["h1 = 0.6"]
    end

    subgraph EQUALIZER_RESP["ZFE Impulse Response cn"]
        C0["c0 = 1.25"]
        C1["c1 = -0.9375"]
    end

    subgraph COMBINED["Combined Response qn = h c"]
        Q0["q0 = 1.0 at k=0"]
        Q1["q1 = 0.0 ISI Cancelled"]
    end

    H0 --> COMBINED
    H1 --> COMBINED
    C0 --> COMBINED
    C1 --> COMBINED

    style H0 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H1 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C0 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Q0 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style Q1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

This diagram visually proves the ZFE's defining property: the convolution of $h_n$ and $c_n$ produces a **single nonzero tap at $n=0$** with all surrounding samples exactly equal to zero.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Zero Forcing Equalizer — Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Q: Define the term "Zero Forcing Equalizer". What is its primary design objective?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Zero Forcing Equalizer (ZFE)** is a linear transversal FIR filter placed at the receiver of a digital communication system whose tap weights are designed to **completely eliminate Intersymbol Interference (ISI)** introduced by a bandlimited, dispersive channel.

Its primary design objective is to choose coefficients $\{c_n\}$ such that the **convolution** of the channel impulse response $\{h_n\}$ and the equalizer response $\{c_n\}$ satisfies the Nyquist zero-ISI condition:

$$q_n = \sum_{j} h_j c_{n-j} = \begin{cases} 1, & n = 0 \\ 0, & n \neq 0 \end{cases}$$

That is, the equalizer **forces all ISI samples to zero** at the symbol decision instants, leaving only the intended symbol for detection. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]`

**Q: List any two limitations of the Zero Forcing Equalizer.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The two principal limitations of the ZFE are:

**(i) Noise Enhancement (1.5 marks):** The ZFE, by inverting the channel $C(z) = 1/H(z)$, places poles of $C(z)$ at the zeros of $H(z)$. If $H(z)$ has a deep null (spectral zero close to the unit circle), the equalizer **amplifies the noise** at those frequencies. The output noise variance becomes $\sigma_\xi^2 = \sigma_\eta^2 \sum_{n} \vert c_n \vert^2$, which can be very large.

**(ii) Sensitivity to Channel Estimation Errors (1.5 marks):** The ZFE requires **exact knowledge of the channel impulse response** $\{h_n\}$. Any error in estimating the channel translates directly into residual ISI at the equalizer output. The ZFE has no inherent robustness to channel estimation errors, unlike MMSE-based equalizers.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the concept of Intersymbol Interference (ISI) in a digital communication system. Derive the peak distortion criterion for an FIR equalizer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A discrete-time channel has impulse response $h_0 = 1, h_1 = 0.5, h_2 = 0.25$. Design a 3-tap Zero Forcing Equalizer (taps $c_0, c_1, c_2$) to eliminate ISI. Compute the output noise enhancement factor $\sum_{n=0}^{2} \vert c_n \vert^2$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — ISI and Peak Distortion Derivation (7 marks):**

In a bandlimited channel, transmitted pulses spread in time and overlap with adjacent symbol intervals, causing each received sample to be contaminated by neighbouring symbols — this is **ISI**. Mathematically:

$$y_k = h_0 x_k + \underbrace{\sum_{\substack{n=0 \\ n \neq 0}}^{L-1} h_n x_{k-n}}_{\text{ISI}} + \eta_k$$

The **Peak Distortion** at the $k$-th sampling instant is defined as the worst-case ratio of ISI magnitude to the desired symbol magnitude:

$$\mathcal{D} = \frac{1}{\vert h_0 \vert} \sum_{\substack{n = -N_1 \\ n \neq 0}}^{N_2} \vert q_n \vert$$

where $q_n = \sum_{j} h_j c_{n-j}$ is the combined channel–equalizer response. An equalizer is *optimum* in the peak-distortion sense when it minimises $\mathcal{D}$. The ZFE achieves $\mathcal{D} = 0$ by design. **(7 marks — full derivation, formula, and explanation required)**

**Part (b) — 3-Tap ZFE Design (7 marks):**

The ZFE conditions for a 3-tap channel and 3-tap equalizer are:

$$q_0 = h_0 c_0 = 1, \quad q_1 = h_0 c_1 + h_1 c_0 = 0, \quad q_2 = h_0 c_2 + h_1 c_1 + h_2 c_0 = 0$$

**Step 1 — Solve for $c_0$:**

$$c_0 = \frac{1}{h_0} = \frac{1}{1} = 1$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $c_1$:**

$$c_1 = -\frac{h_1 c_0}{h_0} = -\frac{(0.5)(1)}{1} = -0.5$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $c_2$:**

$$c_2 = -\frac{h_1 c_1 + h_2 c_0}{h_0} = -\frac{(0.5)(-0.5) + (0.25)(1)}{1} = -(- 0.25 + 0.25) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Verification by convolution:**

$$q_0 = (1)(1) = 1 \quad \checkmark$$
$$q_1 = (1)(-0.5) + (0.5)(1) = -0.5 + 0.5 = 0 \quad \checkmark$$
$$q_2 = (1)(0) + (0.5)(-0.5) + (0.25)(1) = -0.25 + 0.25 = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Compute the noise enhancement factor:**

$$\sum_{n=0}^{2} \vert c_n \vert^2 = (1)^2 + (-0.5)^2 + (0)^2 = 1 + 0.25 + 0 = 1.25$$

**Result:** The ZFE coefficients are $c_0 = 1, c_1 = -0.5, c_2 = 0$, and the noise is amplified by a factor of **1.25 (≈ 0.97 dB)**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — `[KTU University Exam – Expected 2024]`

**Q: (a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a Zero Forcing Equalizer as a transversal filter. State the zero forcing criterion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2-tap channel has $h_0 = 0.9, h_1 = -0.3$. Determine the ZFE coefficients for a 2-tap equalizer. If the input noise variance is $\sigma_\eta^2 = 10^{-4}$ W, calculate the output noise variance at the equalizer output. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — ZFE Block Diagram and Criterion (7 marks):**

The Zero Forcing Equalizer is realised as a **tapped-delay-line filter** (transversal structure) with $M$ taps. The received sequence $y_k$ enters a chain of $M-1$ unit-delay elements ($z^{-1}$). The output of each tap is multiplied by a coefficient $c_n$ and the products are summed to produce the equalised sample $\hat{x}_k$:

$$\hat{x}_k = \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} c_n \, y_{k-n}$$

The **Zero Forcing Criterion** states that the equaliser coefficients $\{c_n\}$ must be chosen such that the convolution of the channel $\{h_n\}$ and the equaliser $\{c_n\}$ satisfies the Nyquist condition:

$$\sum_{j=0}^{L-1} h_j c_{n-j} = \delta_{n0} = \begin{cases} 1, & n = 0 \\ 0, & n \neq 0 \end{cases}$$

This forces the ISI to zero at every sampling instant except the desired one. **(7 marks — block diagram, equation, and criterion all required for full marks)**

**Part (b) — 2-Tap ZFE for Given Channel (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Solve for $c_0$:**

$$c_0 = \frac{1}{h_0} = \frac{1}{0.9} = 1.1111$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $c_1$:**

$$c_1 = -\frac{h_1 c_0}{h_0} = -\frac{(-0.3)(1.1111)}{0.9} = -\frac{-0.3333}{0.9} = 0.3704$$

**Step 3 — Verify the ZFE condition:**

$$q_0 = (0.9)(1.1111) = 1.0 \quad \checkmark$$
$$q_1 = (0.9)(0.3704) + (-0.3)(1.1111) = 0.3333 - 0.3333 = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Compute the output noise variance:**

$$\sigma_\xi^2 = \sigma_\eta^2 \sum_{n=0}^{1} \vert c_n \vert^2 = 10^{-4} \left[ (1.1111)^2 + (0.3704)^2 \right]$$

$$= 10^{-4} \left[ 1.2345 + 0.1372 \right] = 10^{-4} \times 1.3717 = 1.3717 \times 10^{-4} \text{ W}$$

**Result:** The ZFE coefficients are $c_0 \approx 1.1111$ and $c_1 \approx 0.3704$, and the output noise variance is $\sigma_\xi^2 \approx 1.37 \times 10^{-4}$ W. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Scheme Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the convolution sign convention.** Many students write $q_n = h_n \cdot c_n$ (multiplication) instead of $q_n = h_n * c_n$ (convolution). This is a **2-mark deduction** in most valuation schemes.
>
> 2. **Ignoring the noise enhancement calculation.** A ZFE question in Part B always carries at least 2–3 marks for computing $\sum \vert c_n \vert^2$ or output SNR. Skipping this costs you a guaranteed full-marks answer.
>
> 3. **Inverting the order of the ZFE equation.** The ZFE solves $\mathbf{H}\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{e}_0$, not $\mathbf{c}\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{e}_0$. The matrix $\mathbf{H}$ is the **channel convolution (Toeplitz) matrix**, and $\mathbf{c}$ is the column vector of equaliser taps.
>
> 4. **Claiming the ZFE is always stable.** The ZFE is stable **only if** all roots of $H(z)$ lie *strictly inside* the unit circle. A common KTU trick question asks what happens when $H(z) = 1 - 0.9z^{-1}$ — the ZFE has a pole at $z = 0.9$ which is stable, but if $H(z) = 1 - 1.1z^{-1}$, the ZFE is **unstable** (pole at $z = 1.1$).
>
> 5. **Confusing ZFE with MMSE.** The ZFE minimises ISI only (peak distortion), while the **MMSE equaliser** minimises the mean-squared error of the combined ISI-plus-noise output. They are NOT the same filter.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Zero Forcing Equalizer — One-Page Revision**

- **What is ZFE?** A linear FIR filter at the receiver that **inverts the channel** to eliminate ISI at symbol sampling instants.

- **Core equation:** $\sum_{j=0}^{L-1} h_j c_{n-j} = \delta_{n0}$ — this is the **zero-forcing criterion**.

- **In the Z-domain:** $C(z) = 1/H(z)$ — direct inversion of the channel transfer function.

- **Matrix form:** $\mathbf{H}\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{e}_0$ where $\mathbf{H}$ is the Toeplitz convolution matrix of $\{h_n\}$.

- **2-tap channel solution:** $c_0 = 1/h_0$ and $c_1 = -h_1/h_0^2$. (Memorise this!)

- **3-tap channel solution:** $c_0 = 1/h_0$, $c_1 = -h_1/h_0^2$, $c_2 = (h_1^2 - h_0 h_2)/h_0^3$.

- **Peak Distortion:** $\mathcal{D} = \frac{1}{\vert h_0 \vert} \sum_{n \neq 0} \vert q_n \vert$ — ZFE forces $\mathcal{D} = 0$.

- **Output noise variance:** $\sigma_\xi^2 = \sigma_\eta^2 \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} \vert c_n \vert^2$ — this is the **noise enhancement penalty**.

- **Stability condition:** ZFE is stable iff all roots of $H(z)$ lie **strictly inside the unit circle**, i.e., $\vert z_i \vert < 1$ for all zeros $z_i$ of $H(z)$.

- **Limitation 1 — Noise Enhancement:** Deep spectral nulls in $H(z)$ cause the ZFE to amplify noise dramatically. Use **MMSE** or **DFE** in such cases.

- **Limitation 2 — Requires Channel Knowledge:** ZFE needs an accurate estimate of $\{h_n\}$, typically obtained via a **training sequence** and an adaptive algorithm (LMS or RLS).

- **Limitation 3 — Realisation:** Exact ZFE for $L \geq 2$ tap channels is **IIR**. Practical systems use a **truncated FIR** approximation, which leaves a small residual ISI.

- **Application areas:** Wireline modems (DSL), magnetic recording, teaching baseline. Modern wireless systems prefer MMSE / turbo equalisation.

- **Key exam keywords (use these in answers for top marks):** *"Nyquist zero-ISI criterion"*, *"channel inversion"*, *"Peak Distortion"*, *"Toeplitz convolution matrix"*, *"noise enhancement"*, *"training-based channel estimation"*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Geometric Representation of Signals - Gram-Schmidt Procedure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT502 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Geometric Representation of Signals - Gram-Schmidt Procedure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In digital communication theory, every **finite-energy deterministic signal** $s_i(t)$ defined over the observation interval $0 \le t \le T$ can be represented as a **vector** in a finite-dimensional Euclidean signal space, denoted $S$. The dimensions of this vector space are equal to the number of linearly independent signals in the set $\{s_1(t), s_2(t), \dots, s_M(t)\}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Signal Space (KTU Standard Definition):**
> A signal space is a vector space in which each signal $s_i(t)$ of duration $T$ is treated as a point or a vector. The dimensionality of this space, denoted $N$, is the minimum number of linearly independent basis functions $\{\phi_1(t), \phi_2(t), \dots, \phi_N(t)\}$ required to represent all $M$ signals in the set, where $N \le M$.

The **Gram–Schmidt Orthogonalization Procedure** is a systematic, step-by-step mathematical algorithm used to construct a set of **orthonormal basis functions** $\{\phi_j(t)\}$ from a given set of $M$ linearly independent signals $\{s_1(t), s_2(t), \dots, s_M(t)\}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Orthonormal Basis Functions (KTU Definition):**
> A set of basis functions $\{\phi_j(t)\}$ is called *orthonormal* if it satisfies:
> $$\int_{0}^{T} \phi_i(t)\,\phi_j(t)\,dt = \delta_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1, & i = j \\ 0, & i \ne j \end{cases}$$
> where $\delta_{ij}$ is the **Kronecker delta function**.

Once the orthonormal basis is found, every signal $s_i(t)$ can be expressed as a unique linear combination:

$$s_i(t) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} s_{ij}\,\phi_j(t)$$

where the **coefficients** $s_{ij}$ are obtained by the inner-product operation:

$$s_{ij} = \int_{0}^{T} s_i(t)\,\phi_j(t)\,dt = \langle s_i, \phi_j \rangle$$

The vector $\mathbf{s}_i = [s_{i1}, s_{i2}, \dots, s_{iN}]^T$ is the **geometric (vector) representation** of signal $s_i(t)$.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are standing at the origin of a 3D room, and you are pointing a laser beam in different directions to represent different signals.

- Each **direction of the laser** is a *signal* $s_i(t)$.
- The **coordinates** at which the laser hits the wall (or its components along three mutually perpendicular axes) are the *vector coefficients* $s_{i1}, s_{i2}, s_{i3}$.
- The **three mutually perpendicular axes** of the room are the *orthonormal basis functions* $\phi_1, \phi_2, \phi_3$.
- The **Gram–Schmidt procedure** is like the process of *building* these three axes by carefully choosing them so that they are perpendicular to each other, and each one is as long as the standard unit length.

In simple words: **Gram–Schmidt converts "complicated waveforms" into "clean coordinate points" so that the receiver can do simple algebra (just compare distances) instead of complex waveform correlation.**

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Quantities Used in Gram–Schmidt Analysis:**
> - **Energy of a signal:** $E_i = \int_{0}^{T} s_i^{2}(t)\,dt$ (measured in **Joules**)
> - **Inner product of two signals:** $s_{ij} = \int_{0}^{T} s_i(t)\,s_j(t)\,dt$ (measured in **Joules**)
> - **Distance between two signal points:** $d_{ij} = \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{N}(s_{ik} - s_{jk})^{2}}$
> - **Dimension of signal space:** $N \le M$ (dimensionless integer)
> - **Signal energy constant:** $E_b$ — average energy per bit (Joules)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Building Blocks of the Signal Space

#### 2.1.1 Inner Product of Two Signals
The inner product of two real, finite-energy signals $s_i(t)$ and $s_j(t)$ over the interval $[0, T]$ is defined as the scalar quantity:

$$\langle s_i, s_j \rangle \triangleq \int_{0}^{T} s_i(t)\,s_j(t)\,dt$$

**Why it matters:** The inner product is the *correlation* between two signals. It tells us "how similar" two signals are. If the inner product is large and positive, the signals look alike. If it is zero, the signals are *orthogonal* (completely dissimilar in shape). If it is large and negative, the signals are mirror images of each other.

#### 2.1.2 Norm (Length) of a Signal
The norm (or length) of a signal is defined as:

$$\| s_i \| \triangleq \sqrt{\langle s_i, s_i \rangle} = \sqrt{\int_{0}^{T} s_i^{2}(t)\,dt} = \sqrt{E_i}$$

where $E_i$ is the energy of $s_i(t)$.

#### 2.1.3 Orthogonality Condition
Two signals are *orthogonal* if and only if their inner product is zero:

$$s_i \perp s_j \iff \langle s_i, s_j \rangle = \int_{0}^{T} s_i(t)\,s_j(t)\,dt = 0 \quad \text{for } i \ne j$$

#### 2.1.4 Normalization
A signal $s_i(t)$ is *normalized* to obtain a unit-energy function $\phi_i(t)$ by dividing by its norm:

$$\phi_i(t) = \frac{s_i(t)}{\| s_i \|} = \frac{s_i(t)}{\sqrt{E_i}}$$

This guarantees $\int_{0}^{T} \phi_i^{2}(t)\,dt = 1$.

### 2.2 Why is the Gram–Schmidt Procedure Required?

In a real communication receiver, comparing the *received noisy waveform* with each *possible transmitted waveform* (using correlators or matched filters) becomes computationally expensive when the number of signals $M$ is large. By converting each signal to a point in an $N$-dimensional space, the receiver only needs to compute $N$ inner products per received signal — a massive reduction in complexity.

### 2.3 The Gram–Schmidt Algorithm — Conceptual Logic Flow

The procedure transforms $M$ linearly independent signals into at most $N \le M$ orthonormal basis functions. The steps are:

1. **Choose the first signal** $s_1(t)$ and normalize it to obtain the first basis function:
$$\phi_1(t) = \frac{s_1(t)}{\sqrt{E_1}}, \qquad E_1 = \int_{0}^{T} s_1^{2}(t)\,dt$$

2. **For the second signal** $s_2(t)$, project out the component along $\phi_1(t)$ to obtain the residual:
$$g_2(t) = s_2(t) - s_{21}\,\phi_1(t)$$
where $s_{21} = \langle s_2, \phi_1 \rangle = \int_{0}^{T} s_2(t)\phi_1(t)\,dt$.

3. **Normalize** the residual to obtain the second basis function:
$$\phi_2(t) = \frac{g_2(t)}{\sqrt{\int_{0}^{T} g_2^{2}(t)\,dt}}$$

4. **For the $k$-th signal** $s_k(t)$ (for $k = 3, 4, \dots, M$), compute the residual by subtracting projections onto **all** previously found basis functions:
$$g_k(t) = s_k(t) - \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} s_{kj}\,\phi_j(t)$$
where $s_{kj} = \langle s_k, \phi_j \rangle = \int_{0}^{T} s_k(t)\phi_j(t)\,dt$.

5. **If** $g_k(t) = 0$, then $s_k(t)$ is linearly dependent on the previous signals and contributes **no new basis function**. Skip it.  
   **Else**, normalize:
$$\phi_k(t) = \frac{g_k(t)}{\sqrt{\int_{0}^{T} g_2^{2}(t)\,dt}}$$

6. **Stop** when all $M$ signals have been processed. The total number of basis functions obtained, $N$, is the **dimension of the signal space**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the single most important reference for any KTU exam question on this topic. Memorize it completely.**

| **S.No** | **Quantity** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Units / Notes** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Inner product of two signals | $\langle s_i, s_j \rangle = \int_{0}^{T} s_i(t)\,s_j(t)\,dt$ | Joules (J) |
| 2 | Energy of signal $s_i(t)$ | $E_i = \int_{0}^{T} s_i^{2}(t)\,dt$ | Joules (J) |
| 3 | Norm of signal | $\vert\vert s_i \vert\vert = \sqrt{E_i}$ | $\sqrt{\text{J}}$ |
| 4 | Normalized basis function | $\phi_i(t) = s_i(t)/\sqrt{E_i}$ | Unit energy, dimensionless when normalized |
| 5 | Orthogonality condition | $\int_{0}^{T} \phi_i(t)\phi_j(t)\,dt = 0$ for $i \ne j$ | Required for valid basis |
| 6 | Orthonormality condition | $\int_{0}^{T} \phi_i(t)\phi_j(t)\,dt = \delta_{ij}$ | $\delta_{ij}$ is Kronecker delta |
| 7 | Vector coefficient of $s_i$ along $\phi_j$ | $s_{ij} = \int_{0}^{T} s_i(t)\,\phi_j(t)\,dt$ | Real scalar |
| 8 | Signal expansion in basis | $s_i(t) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} s_{ij}\,\phi_j(t)$ | Exact representation |
| 9 | Energy in vector form | $E_i = \sum_{j=1}^{N} s_{ij}^{2}$ | Parserval-like relation |
| 10 | Residual signal in step $k$ | $g_k(t) = s_k(t) - \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} s_{kj}\phi_j(t)$ | Orthogonal to all previous $\phi_j$ |
| 11 | Dimension of signal space | $N \le M$ | $M$ = no. of signals, $N$ = no. of basis functions |
| 12 | Euclidean distance between two signal points | $d_{ij} = \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{N}(s_{ik}-s_{jk})^{2}}$ | Geometric distance |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Gram–Schmidt procedure is the **mathematical backbone of the optimal digital receiver** (the *matched filter* or *correlator-based receiver*). It is used in:

- **BPSK, QPSK, M-ary PSK, M-ary QAM** signal design in 4G/5G cellular, Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), and satellite communication.
- **M-ary FSK** and **M-ary PAM** modulation schemes.
- **ML (Maximum Likelihood) detection** at the receiver — the receiver maps the received noisy signal to the *closest* signal point in the constellation, which is a pure geometric distance computation.
- **Channel coding and lattice codes** in modern information theory.
- **Pattern recognition** and **biometrics** (for representing features as vectors in a feature space).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Examples

### 3.1 Derivation of the Gram–Schmidt Basis Construction

**Given:** A set of $M$ real, finite-energy, linearly independent signals $\{s_1(t), s_2(t), \dots, s_M(t)\}$ defined on $[0, T]$.

**Goal:** Construct an orthonormal basis $\{\phi_1(t), \phi_2(t), \dots, \phi_N(t)\}$ with $N \le M$.

**Step 1 — Initialize.**
Choose $s_1(t)$ as the first candidate basis function. Compute its energy:

$$E_1 = \int_{0}^{T} s_1^{2}(t)\,dt$$

Define the first orthonormal basis function as the energy-normalized version of $s_1$:

$$\phi_1(t) = \frac{s_1(t)}{\sqrt{E_1}}$$

**Step 2 — General iteration for $k = 2, 3, \dots, M$.**

The signal $s_k(t)$ is decomposed into two parts: a linear combination of the previously found basis functions $\{\phi_1, \dots, \phi_{k-1}\}$, plus a residual that is *orthogonal* to all of them.

The projection of $s_k(t)$ onto the span of $\{\phi_1, \dots, \phi_{k-1}\}$ is:

$$P_{\{\phi_1,\dots,\phi_{k-1}\}} s_k(t) = \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} \langle s_k, \phi_j \rangle \,\phi_j(t) = \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} s_{kj}\,\phi_j(t)$$

Subtract this projection from $s_k(t)$ to obtain the residual:

$$g_k(t) = s_k(t) - \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} s_{kj}\,\phi_j(t)$$

**Step 3 — Orthogonality verification.**

We must verify that $g_k(t)$ is indeed orthogonal to every $\phi_i$ for $i = 1, 2, \dots, k-1$. Compute:

$$\langle g_k, \phi_i \rangle = \int_{0}^{T} g_k(t)\,\phi_i(t)\,dt = \int_{0}^{T} \left[ s_k(t) - \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} s_{kj}\,\phi_j(t) \right] \phi_i(t)\,dt$$

Splitting the integral:

$$\langle g_k, \phi_i \rangle = \int_{0}^{T} s_k(t)\phi_i(t)\,dt - \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} s_{kj} \int_{0}^{T} \phi_j(t)\phi_i(t)\,dt$$

By the orthonormality of $\{\phi_j\}$, the inner product $\int_{0}^{T} \phi_j(t)\phi_i(t)\,dt = \delta_{ij}$. So the sum collapses to $s_{ki}$ when $j = i$:

$$\langle g_k, \phi_i \rangle = s_{ki} - s_{ki}\,\delta_{ii} = s_{ki} - s_{ki} = 0$$

This confirms $g_k(t) \perp \phi_i(t)$ for all $i < k$. ✓

**Step 4 — Normalize $g_k(t)$.**

Compute the energy of the residual:

$$E_{g_k} = \int_{0}^{T} g_k^{2}(t)\,dt$$

If $E_{g_k} = 0$, the signal $s_k(t)$ was linearly dependent on the previous signals, and **no new basis function is added**. Otherwise, define:

$$\phi_k(t) = \frac{g_k(t)}{\sqrt{E_{g_k}}}$$

**Step 5 — Convergence and dimension count.**

The process stops after $M$ iterations. The number of basis functions actually generated, $N$, equals the **rank** of the set $\{s_1, \dots, s_M\}$, which is at most $M$.

**Result:** The orthonormal basis $\{\phi_1, \phi_2, \dots, \phi_N\}$ is complete, and every signal can be expanded as:

$$s_i(t) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} s_{ij}\,\phi_j(t), \qquad s_{ij} = \int_{0}^{T} s_i(t)\phi_j(t)\,dt$$

### 3.2 Analytical Example 1 — BPSK Signal Pair

**Given signals:**
$$s_1(t) = +A, \quad 0 \le t \le T$$
$$s_2(t) = -A, \quad 0 \le t \le T$$

**Step 1: Compute energy of $s_1(t)$.**
$$E_1 = \int_{0}^{T} A^{2}\,dt = A^{2}T$$

**Step 2: First basis function.**
$$\phi_1(t) = \frac{s_1(t)}{\sqrt{E_1}} = \frac{A}{\sqrt{A^{2}T}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{T}}$$

This is the standard unit-energy rectangular pulse over $[0, T]$.

**Step 3: Compute coefficient $s_{21}$.**
$$s_{21} = \int_{0}^{T} s_2(t)\,\phi_1(t)\,dt = \int_{0}^{T} (-A)\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{T}}\right)dt = -A\sqrt{T}$$

**Step 4: Compute residual $g_2(t)$.**
$$g_2(t) = s_2(t) - s_{21}\,\phi_1(t) = -A - \left(-A\sqrt{T}\right)\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{T}}\right) = -A + A = 0$$

**Step 5: Interpretation.**
Since $g_2(t) = 0$, the signal $s_2(t)$ is **linearly dependent** on $s_1(t)$ (it is just the negative). Hence, the **signal space dimension** is $N = 1$.

**Vector representation:**
$$\mathbf{s}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} +A\sqrt{T} \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{s}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} -A\sqrt{T} \end{bmatrix}$$

These are two points on a **1-D line** (BPSK constellation). ✓

### 3.3 Analytical Example 2 — BFSK Signal Pair (Orthogonal Case)

**Given signals:**
$$s_1(t) = A\sqrt{\frac{2}{T}}\cos(2\pi f_1 t), \quad s_2(t) = A\sqrt{\frac{2}{T}}\cos(2\pi f_2 t)$$
where $f_1 - f_2 = n/T$ for some integer $n$ (orthogonality condition).

**Step 1: Compute energy of $s_1(t)$.**
$$E_1 = \int_{0}^{T} \frac{2A^{2}}{T}\cos^{2}(2\pi f_1 t)\,dt = \frac{2A^{2}}{T}\cdot\frac{T}{2} = A^{2}$$

**Step 2: First basis function.**
$$\phi_1(t) = \frac{s_1(t)}{\sqrt{E_1}} = \frac{s_1(t)}{A} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{T}}\cos(2\pi f_1 t)$$

**Step 3: Compute coefficient $s_{21}$.**
$$s_{21} = \int_{0}^{T} s_2(t)\phi_1(t)\,dt = \int_{0}^{T} \frac{2A^{2}}{T}\cos(2\pi f_1 t)\cos(2\pi f_2 t)\,dt$$

Using the product-to-sum identity: $2\cos A \cos B = \cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)$:

$$s_{21} = \frac{A^{2}}{T}\int_{0}^{T}\left[\cos(2\pi(f_1-f_2)t) + \cos(2\pi(f_1+f_2)t)\right]dt$$

Since $f_1 - f_2 = n/T$, the first term integrates to zero over $[0, T]$. The second term also integrates to zero for $f_1, f_2 \gg 1/T$ (typically true in BFSK). So:

$$s_{21} = 0$$

**Step 4: Residual $g_2(t) = s_2(t) - 0 = s_2(t)$.**

**Step 5: Compute energy of $g_2(t)$.**
$$E_{g_2} = \int_{0}^{T} s_2^{2}(t)\,dt = A^{2}$$

**Step 6: Second basis function.**
$$\phi_2(t) = \frac{s_2(t)}{\sqrt{E_{g_2}}} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{T}}\cos(2\pi f_2 t)$$

**Result: Vector representation.**
$$\mathbf{s}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} A \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{s}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ A \end{bmatrix}$$

**Signal space dimension:** $N = 2$.  
**BFSK constellation:** Two points on the orthogonal 2-D axes, separated by Euclidean distance $d = A\sqrt{2}$. ✓

### 3.4 Analytical Example 3 — 4-PAM Signals

**Given signals** (4-Pulse Amplitude Modulation with $d$ = distance between adjacent levels):
$$s_1(t) = -3d\cdot g(t), \quad s_2(t) = -d\cdot g(t), \quad s_3(t) = +d\cdot g(t), \quad s_4(t) = +3d\cdot g(t)$$

where $g(t)$ is a unit-energy pulse on $[0, T]$ (i.e., $\int_{0}^{T} g^{2}(t)\,dt = 1$).

**Step 1: First basis function.**
$$\phi_1(t) = g(t)$$

**Step 2: Compute vector coefficients.**
$$s_{11} = \int_{0}^{T} s_1(t)\phi_1(t)\,dt = \int_{0}^{T}(-3d)g^{2}(t)\,dt = -3d$$

Similarly:
$$s_{21} = -d, \qquad s_{31} = +d, \qquad s_{41} = +3d$$

**Step 3: Compute residual for $s_2(t)$.**
$$g_2(t) = s_2(t) - s_{21}\phi_1(t) = -d\,g(t) - (-d)g(t) = 0$$

Same for $s_3(t)$ and $s_4(t)$ — all residuals vanish.

**Result: Vector representation.**
$$\mathbf{s}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} -3d \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{s}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} -d \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{s}_3 = \begin{bmatrix} +d \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{s}_4 = \begin{bmatrix} +3d \end{bmatrix}$$

**Signal space dimension:** $N = 1$ (all 4-PAM signals lie on a single line). ✓

### 3.5 Analytical Example 4 — Generic 3-Signal Set (Full Gram–Schmidt Walk-through)

**Given signals over $[0, T]$:**
$$s_1(t) = +1, \quad s_2(t) = +t, \quad s_3(t) = +t^{2}$$

(These are linearly independent polynomial signals on $[0, 1]$, with $T = 1$.)

**Step 1: First basis function.**
$$E_1 = \int_{0}^{1} 1^{2}\,dt = 1$$
$$\phi_1(t) = 1$$

**Step 2: Coefficient of $s_2$ along $\phi_1$.**
$$s_{21} = \int_{0}^{1} t \cdot 1\,dt = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 3: Residual $g_2(t)$.**
$$g_2(t) = s_2(t) - s_{21}\phi_1(t) = t - \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 4: Energy of $g_2(t)$.**
$$E_{g_2} = \int_{0}^{1}\left(t - \frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}dt = \int_{0}^{1}\left(t^{2} - t + \frac{1}{4}\right)dt = \frac{1}{3} - \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{4} = \frac{1}{12}$$

**Step 5: Second basis function.**
$$\phi_2(t) = \frac{g_2(t)}{\sqrt{E_{g_2}}} = \frac{t - 1/2}{\sqrt{1/12}} = \sqrt{12}\left(t - \frac{1}{2}\right) = 2\sqrt{3}\left(t - \frac{1}{2}\right)$$

**Step 6: Coefficients of $s_3$ along $\phi_1$ and $\phi_2$.**
$$s_{31} = \int_{0}^{1} t^{2}\cdot 1\,dt = \frac{1}{3}$$

$$s_{32} = \int_{0}^{1} t^{2}\cdot 2\sqrt{3}\left(t - \frac{1}{2}\right)dt = 2\sqrt{3}\int_{0}^{1}\left(t^{3} - \frac{t^{2}}{2}\right)dt$$

$$= 2\sqrt{3}\left[\frac{t^{4}}{4} - \frac{t^{3}}{6}\right]_{0}^{1} = 2\sqrt{3}\left[\frac{1}{4} - \frac{1}{6}\right] = 2\sqrt{3}\cdot\frac{1}{12} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{6}$$

**Step 7: Residual $g_3(t)$.**
$$g_3(t) = t^{2} - \frac{1}{3}\cdot 1 - \frac{\sqrt{3}}{6}\cdot 2\sqrt{3}\left(t - \frac{1}{2}\right) = t^{2} - \frac{1}{3} - \left(t - \frac{1}{2}\right)$$

$$= t^{2} - t + \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{3} = t^{2} - t + \frac{1}{6}$$

**Step 8: Energy of $g_3(t)$.**
$$E_{g_3} = \int_{0}^{1}\left(t^{2} - t + \frac{1}{6}\right)^{2}dt$$

Expanding the square:
$$= \int_{0}^{1}\left[t^{4} + t^{2} + \frac{1}{36} - 2t^{3} + \frac{t^{2}}{3} - \frac{t}{3}\right]dt$$

$$= \int_{0}^{1}\left[t^{4} - 2t^{3} + \frac{4t^{2}}{3} - \frac{t}{3} + \frac{1}{36}\right]dt$$

$$= \frac{1}{5} - \frac{2}{4} + \frac{4}{9} - \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{36}$$

Finding common denominator 180:
$$= \frac{36}{180} - \frac{90}{180} + \frac{80}{180} - \frac{30}{180} + \frac{5}{180} = \frac{36 - 90 + 80 - 30 + 5}{180} = \frac{1}{180}$$

**Step 9: Third basis function.**
$$\phi_3(t) = \frac{g_3(t)}{\sqrt{1/180}} = \sqrt{180}\left(t^{2} - t + \frac{1}{6}\right) = 6\sqrt{5}\left(t^{2} - t + \frac{1}{6}\right)$$

**Step 10: Final result — orthonormal basis for $\{1, t, t^{2}\}$ on $[0, 1]$:**

$$\phi_1(t) = 1$$
$$\phi_2(t) = 2\sqrt{3}\left(t - \frac{1}{2}\right)$$
$$\phi_3(t) = 6\sqrt{5}\left(t^{2} - t + \frac{1}{6}\right)$$

These are the **shifted Legendre polynomials** normalized to unit energy — a classical result. ✓

**Vector representations:**
$$\mathbf{s}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ s_{12} \\ s_{13} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{s}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1/2 \\ \sqrt{1/12} \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{s}_3 = \begin{bmatrix} 1/3 \\ \sqrt{3}/6 \\ \sqrt{1/180} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Signal space dimension:** $N = 3$ (since all 3 signals were linearly independent).

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Flow of the Gram–Schmidt Procedure

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the full algorithmic workflow of the Gram–Schmidt orthogonalization procedure as a digital signal processing pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Input: M signals s1 to sM over 0 to T]) --> stepB{Step 1: Pick s1}
    stepB --> stepC[Compute E1 = ∫ s1² dt]
    stepC --> stepD[Set φ1 = s1 / √E1]
    stepD --> stepE[Set k = 2, N = 1]
    stepE --> stepF{Is k ≤ M?}
    stepF -- No --> stepZ([Output: N-dim orthonormal basis φ1 to φN + vectors si])
    stepF -- Yes --> stepG[Compute projection coefficients: skj = ∫ sk φj dt for j=1 to k-1]
    stepG --> stepH[Compute residual: gk t = sk t - Σ skj φj t]
    stepH --> stepI{Is ∫ gk² dt = 0?}
    stepI -- Yes --> stepJ[sk is linearly dependent: skip, set k = k + 1]
    stepI -- No --> stepK[Set N = N + 1]
    stepK --> stepL[Normalize: φN = gk / √∫gk² dt]
    stepL --> stepM[Set k = k + 1]
    stepJ --> stepF
    stepM --> stepF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each rectangular block is a mathematical operation. Each diamond block is a logical decision. The loop on the right side (`k = k + 1` → `Is k ≤ M?`) represents the iterative nature of the procedure.

### 4.2 Receiver Architecture Enabled by Gram–Schmidt

The following block diagram shows how the geometric representation enables an optimal digital receiver using **correlators** (one per basis function $\phi_j$).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rxSig([Received noisy signal r t]) --> corr1[Correlator 1: multiply by φ1 t, integrate over 0 to T]
    rxSig --> corr2[Correlator 2: multiply by φ2 t, integrate over 0 to T]
    rxSig --> corrN[Correlator N: multiply by φN t, integrate over 0 to T]
    corr1 --> samp1[Sample r1]
    corr2 --> samp2[Sample r2]
    corrN --> sampN[Sample rN]
    samp1 --> vec[Form observation vector r = r1 r2 ... rN]
    samp2 --> vec
    sampN --> vec
    vec --> det[Detector: ML rule - choose signal point closest to r]
    det --> out([Output: detected symbol])
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** Notice that the receiver uses **only $N$ correlators**, regardless of how many signals $M$ there are. This is the primary engineering benefit of the Gram–Schmidt procedure. The detection itself becomes a simple **minimum-distance decision** in $N$-dimensional space.

### 4.3 Conceptual Signal Constellation Layouts

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the geometric constellation diagrams (signal points in 2-D space) for three common digital modulation schemes, all derived using the Gram–Schmidt procedure.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BPSK_Diagram["BPSK Constellation N=1"]
        BPSK_A[+√Eb at coordinate +1 on x-axis]
        BPSK_B[-√Eb at coordinate -1 on x-axis]
    end
    subgraph QPSK_Diagram["QPSK Constellation N=2"]
        QPSK_A[+√Eb/2 + j√Eb/2 in 1st quadrant]
        QPSK_B[-√Eb/2 + j√Eb/2 in 2nd quadrant]
        QPSK_C[-√Eb/2 - j√Eb/2 in 3rd quadrant]
        QPSK_D[+√Eb/2 - j√Eb/2 in 4th quadrant]
    end
    subgraph BFSK_Diagram["BFSK Constellation N=2"]
        BFSK_A[+√Eb on phi1 axis]
        BFSK_B[+√Eb on phi2 axis]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Constellation interpretation:** Each dot (or coordinate) represents one possible transmitted signal. The minimum Euclidean distance $d_{min}$ between any two points determines the **bit error probability** $P_e$. The Gram–Schmidt procedure guarantees that we are computing $d_{min}$ in the correct geometric space.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define an *orthonormal basis* for a set of signals. State the orthonormality condition mathematically.

**Model Answer:**

An orthonormal basis is a set of real, finite-energy functions $\{\phi_1(t), \phi_2(t), \dots, \phi_N(t)\}$ that are mutually orthogonal and each of unit energy. Mathematically, they satisfy:

$$\int_{0}^{T} \phi_i(t)\,\phi_j(t)\,dt = \delta_{ij}$$

where $\delta_{ij}$ is the Kronecker delta function ($\delta_{ij} = 1$ if $i = j$, and $\delta_{ij} = 0$ if $i \ne j$). Such a basis allows any signal in the set to be expressed as a unique linear combination $s_i(t) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} s_{ij}\,\phi_j(t)$ with $s_{ij} = \int_{0}^{T} s_i(t)\phi_j(t)\,dt$.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is meant by the *dimension of a signal space*? How is it related to the number of signals $M$ in a set?

**Model Answer:**

The dimension of a signal space, denoted $N$, is the minimum number of linearly independent basis functions required to represent every signal in the given set $\{s_1(t), s_2(t), \dots, s_M(t)\}$. It is mathematically equal to the **rank** of the signal set.

The relationship is $N \le M$, with equality holding when all $M$ signals are mutually linearly independent. If some signals are linear combinations of others (e.g., $s_2(t) = -s_1(t)$ in BPSK), the dimension $N$ is strictly less than $M$. For example, $M = 2$ BPSK signals form a 1-D signal space, while $M = 2$ orthogonal BFSK signals form a 2-D signal space.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Full Gram–Schmidt Construction on Polynomial Signals

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper]**  
**Consider the set of three signals defined on $0 \le t \le 1$:**

$$s_1(t) = 1, \qquad s_2(t) = t, \qquad s_3(t) = 1 + t$$

**(a)** Apply the Gram–Schmidt orthogonalization procedure to find the orthonormal basis functions $\{\phi_1(t), \phi_2(t), \phi_3(t)\}$. Determine the dimension $N$ of the signal space. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Obtain the vector representations $\mathbf{s}_1, \mathbf{s}_2, \mathbf{s}_3$ of all three signals. Compute the Euclidean distance between $\mathbf{s}_1$ and $\mathbf{s}_2$, and between $\mathbf{s}_2$ and $\mathbf{s}_3$. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a): Gram–Schmidt Procedure**

**Step 1 — Choose $s_1(t)$ and compute its energy.**

$$E_1 = \int_{0}^{1} 1^{2}\,dt = 1$$

**First basis function:**
$$\phi_1(t) = \frac{s_1(t)}{\sqrt{E_1}} = \frac{1}{1} = 1$$

**Step 2 — Process $s_2(t) = t$.**

Compute the projection coefficient:
$$s_{21} = \int_{0}^{1} t \cdot 1\,dt = \left[\frac{t^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{1} = \frac{1}{2}$$

Compute the residual:
$$g_2(t) = s_2(t) - s_{21}\phi_1(t) = t - \frac{1}{2}$$

Compute its energy:
$$E_{g_2} = \int_{0}^{1}\left(t - \frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}dt = \int_{0}^{1}\left(t^{2} - t + \frac{1}{4}\right)dt = \frac{1}{3} - \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{4} = \frac{4 - 6 + 3}{12} = \frac{1}{12}$$

**Second basis function:**
$$\phi_2(t) = \frac{g_2(t)}{\sqrt{E_{g_2}}} = \frac{t - 1/2}{\sqrt{1/12}} = \sqrt{12}\left(t - \frac{1}{2}\right) = 2\sqrt{3}\left(t - \frac{1}{2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Process $s_3(t) = 1 + t$.**

Compute projection coefficient on $\phi_1$:
$$s_{31} = \int_{0}^{1}(1 + t)\cdot 1\,dt = \int_{0}^{1}1\,dt + \int_{0}^{1}t\,dt = 1 + \frac{1}{2} = \frac{3}{2}$$

Compute projection coefficient on $\phi_2$:
$$s_{32} = \int_{0}^{1}(1 + t)\cdot 2\sqrt{3}\left(t - \frac{1}{2}\right)dt = 2\sqrt{3}\int_{0}^{1}(1 + t)\left(t - \frac{1}{2}\right)dt$$

Expand the integrand:
$$(1 + t)\left(t - \frac{1}{2}\right) = t - \frac{1}{2} + t^{2} - \frac{t}{2} = t^{2} + \frac{t}{2} - \frac{1}{2}$$

So:
$$s_{32} = 2\sqrt{3}\int_{0}^{1}\left(t^{2} + \frac{t}{2} - \frac{1}{2}\right)dt = 2\sqrt{3}\left[\frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{4} - \frac{1}{2}\right]$$

$$= 2\sqrt{3}\left[\frac{4 + 3 - 6}{12}\right] = 2\sqrt{3}\cdot\frac{1}{12} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{6}$$

Compute the residual:
$$g_3(t) = s_3(t) - s_{31}\phi_1(t) - s_{32}\phi_2(t)$$

$$= (1 + t) - \frac{3}{2}\cdot 1 - \frac{\sqrt{3}}{6}\cdot 2\sqrt{3}\left(t - \frac{1}{2}\right)$$

$$= 1 + t - \frac{3}{2} - (t - \frac{1}{2})$$

$$= 1 + t - \frac{3}{2} - t + \frac{1}{2} = 0$$

**Conclusion:** Since $g_3(t) = 0$, the signal $s_3(t)$ is **linearly dependent** on $s_1(t)$ and $s_2(t)$. Indeed, $s_3(t) = s_1(t) + s_2(t) = 1 + t$. Hence, the **dimension of the signal space is $N = 2$**.

**Final orthonormal basis:**
$$\phi_1(t) = 1$$
$$\phi_2(t) = 2\sqrt{3}\left(t - \frac{1}{2}\right)$$

**Part (b): Vector representations and distances**

**Vector for $s_1(t) = 1$:**

$$s_{11} = \int_{0}^{1} 1\cdot 1\,dt = 1$$

$$s_{12} = \int_{0}^{1} 1\cdot 2\sqrt{3}\left(t - \frac{1}{2}\right)dt = 2\sqrt{3}\int_{0}^{1}\left(t - \frac{1}{2}\right)dt = 2\sqrt{3}\left[\frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{2}\right] = 0$$

So:
$$\mathbf{s}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Vector for $s_2(t) = t$:**

$$s_{21} = \frac{1}{2} \quad \text{(computed in Part a)}$$

$$s_{22} = \sqrt{E_{g_2}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{12}} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{3}}$$

So:
$$\mathbf{s}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1/2 \\ 1/(2\sqrt{3}) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Vector for $s_3(t) = 1 + t$:**

$$s_{31} = \frac{3}{2}, \qquad s_{32} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{6} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{3}}$$

So:
$$\mathbf{s}_3 = \begin{bmatrix} 3/2 \\ 1/(2\sqrt{3}) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Distance between $\mathbf{s}_1$ and $\mathbf{s}_2$:**

$$d_{12} = \sqrt{\left(1 - \frac{1}{2}\right)^{2} + \left(0 - \frac{1}{2\sqrt{3}}\right)^{2}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{12}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\frac{3 + 1}{12}} = \sqrt{\frac{4}{12}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{3}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.577$$

**Distance between $\mathbf{s}_2$ and $\mathbf{s}_3$:**

$$d_{23} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{3}{2} - \frac{1}{2}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{1}{2\sqrt{3}} - \frac{1}{2\sqrt{3}}\right)^{2}} = \sqrt{1^{2} + 0^{2}} = 1$$

**Sanity check:** $s_3 = s_1 + s_2$, so $\mathbf{s}_3 = \mathbf{s}_1 + \mathbf{s}_2$. The distance $d_{13} = \sqrt{(1 - 3/2)^{2} + (0 - 1/(2\sqrt{3}))^{2}} = \sqrt{1/4 + 1/12} = 1/\sqrt{3} \approx 0.577$. This is consistent with the geometry. ✓

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Geometric Representation of BFSK

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Supplementary Exam]**  
**Consider the BFSK signal set with parameters:**

$$s_1(t) = A\sqrt{\frac{2}{T}}\cos(2\pi f_1 t), \quad s_2(t) = A\sqrt{\frac{2}{T}}\cos(2\pi f_2 t), \quad 0 \le t \le T$$

where $f_1 - f_2 = n/T$ for some integer $n \ge 1$.

**(a)** Apply the Gram–Schmidt procedure to obtain the orthonormal basis functions. Show that $N = 2$. Determine the vector representations of $s_1$ and $s_2$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the Euclidean distance between the two signal points. Hence, determine the average energy per bit $E_b$ and express the distance in terms of $E_b$. Comment on the relationship between this distance and the bit error probability. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a): Gram–Schmidt Procedure for BFSK**

**Step 1 — Compute energy of $s_1(t)$:**

$$E_1 = \int_{0}^{T} s_1^{2}(t)\,dt = \int_{0}^{T}\frac{2A^{2}}{T}\cos^{2}(2\pi f_1 t)\,dt$$

Using the identity $\cos^{2}\theta = \frac{1 + \cos 2\theta}{2}$:

$$E_1 = \frac{2A^{2}}{T}\int_{0}^{T}\frac{1 + \cos(4\pi f_1 t)}{2}\,dt = \frac{A^{2}}{T}\int_{0}^{T}\left[1 + \cos(4\pi f_1 t)\right]dt$$

$$= \frac{A^{2}}{T}\left[T + \frac{\sin(4\pi f_1 T)}{4\pi f_1}\right]$$

For $f_1 T \gg 1$, the second term is negligible, so:
$$E_1 \approx \frac{A^{2}}{T}\cdot T = A^{2}$$

**First basis function:**
$$\phi_1(t) = \frac{s_1(t)}{\sqrt{E_1}} = \frac{s_1(t)}{A} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{T}}\cos(2\pi f_1 t)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the projection coefficient $s_{21}$:**

$$s_{21} = \int_{0}^{T} s_2(t)\phi_1(t)\,dt = \int_{0}^{T}\frac{2A^{2}}{T}\cos(2\pi f_1 t)\cos(2\pi f_2 t)\,dt$$

Using $2\cos A\cos B = \cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)$:

$$s_{21} = \frac{A^{2}}{T}\int_{0}^{T}\left[\cos(2\pi(f_1 - f_2)t) + \cos(2\pi(f_1 + f_2)t)\right]dt$$

Substituting $f_1 - f_2 = n/T$:

$$\int_{0}^{T}\cos\left(\frac{2\pi n t}{T}\right)dt = \left[\frac{T}{2\pi n}\sin\left(\frac{2\pi n t}{T}\right)\right]_{0}^{T} = \frac{T}{2\pi n}[\sin(2\pi n) - \sin(0)] = 0$$

For $f_1 + f_2 \gg 1/T$, the second integral is also approximately zero. Hence:
$$s_{21} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Compute the residual:**

$$g_2(t) = s_2(t) - s_{21}\phi_1(t) = s_2(t) - 0 = s_2(t)$$

**Step 4 — Compute energy of $g_2(t)$:**

By an identical calculation to Step 1:
$$E_{g_2} = A^{2}$$

**Second basis function:**
$$\phi_2(t) = \frac{s_2(t)}{\sqrt{E_{g_2}}} = \frac{s_2(t)}{A} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{T}}\cos(2\pi f_2 t)$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** Both basis functions are independent. Hence $N = 2$.

**Vector representations:**

$$s_{11} = \int_{0}^{T} s_1(t)\phi_1(t)\,dt = A, \quad s_{12} = \int_{0}^{T} s_1(t)\phi_2(t)\,dt = 0$$

$$s_{21} = \int_{0}^{T} s_2(t)\phi_1(t)\,dt = 0, \quad s_{22} = \int_{0}^{T} s_2(t)\phi_2(t)\,dt = A$$

So:
$$\mathbf{s}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} A \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{s}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ A \end{bmatrix}$$

**Part (b): Distance and energy per bit**

**Euclidean distance:**

$$d_{12} = \sqrt{(A - 0)^{2} + (0 - A)^{2}} = \sqrt{A^{2} + A^{2}} = A\sqrt{2}$$

**Average energy per bit:**

Since each signal carries one bit and both have equal energy $A^{2}$:

$$E_b = \frac{E_1 + E_2}{2} = \frac{A^{2} + A^{2}}{2} = A^{2}$$

**Distance in terms of $E_b$:**

$$d_{12} = A\sqrt{2} = \sqrt{2A^{2}} = \sqrt{2E_b}$$

**Comment on bit error probability:**

For coherent BFSK detected using the ML (maximum likelihood) rule in an AWGN channel with two-sided noise power spectral density $N_0/2$, the bit error probability is:

$$P_e = Q\left(\sqrt{\frac{d_{12}^{2}}{2N_0}}\right) = Q\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{2N_0}}\right) = Q\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

The distance $d_{12} = \sqrt{2E_b}$ grows with $\sqrt{E_b}$, so increasing the signal energy $E_b$ (or reducing the noise $N_0$) increases the distance between signal points in the constellation, which **exponentially reduces** the bit error probability. This is the fundamental geometric interpretation of why more signal power leads to more reliable communication. ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize:** Many students compute $g_k(t)$ correctly but forget to divide by $\sqrt{\int g_k^{2}dt}$ to get the orthonormal $\phi_k(t)$. This loses 2–3 marks. The basis functions MUST be unit-energy.
> 2. **Skipping the linear-dependence check:** If $g_k(t) = 0$ for some $k$, students often still "create" a new basis function. Always verify $E_{g_k} \ne 0$ before defining $\phi_k$.
> 3. **Sign errors in $g_k(t)$:** The most common error is in the sign of $s_{kj}$. Carefully apply $g_k = s_k - \sum s_{kj}\phi_j$ (note the *minus* sign).
