> 4. **Using $t$ inside the inner product incorrectly:** Remember, $\langle s_i, s_j \rangle = \int_{0}^{T} s_i(t)s_j(t)\,dt$ — never use $t$ as the integration variable elsewhere. Use a dummy variable $\tau$ if necessary to avoid confusion.
> 5. **Forgetting the $N \le M$ relationship:** When asked "what is the dimension?", students often write $N = M$ without checking. BPSK has $M = 2$ but $N = 1$. Always state the relationship explicitly.
> 6. **Missing the engineering interpretation:** A 14-mark question often requires you to state the *meaning* of the result (e.g., "this is the constellation for BPSK in 1-D space"). Do not omit this — it carries 2–3 marks.
> 7. **Mixing up BFSK and BPSK constants:** In BFSK, the coefficient $\sqrt{2/T}$ comes from the requirement of unit-energy basis. If the problem does not include this factor, you must derive it via normalization.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU Exam — Read this twice before entering the exam hall:**

- ☐ **Inner product** of two signals: $\langle s_i, s_j \rangle = \int_{0}^{T} s_i(t)s_j(t)\,dt$.
- ☐ **Norm** of a signal: $\|s_i\| = \sqrt{\int_{0}^{T}s_i^{2}(t)\,dt}$.
- ☐ **Energy** of a signal: $E_i = \int_{0}^{T}s_i^{2}(t)\,dt = \|s_i\|^{2}$.
- ☐ **Orthogonality** condition: $\int_{0}^{T} s_i(t)s_j(t)\,dt = 0$ for $i \ne j$.
- ☐ **Orthonormality** condition: $\int_{0}^{T} \phi_i(t)\phi_j(t)\,dt = \delta_{ij}$.
- ☐ **Gram–Schmidt Step k formula** (the one formula you must memorize):
  - $s_{kj} = \int_{0}^{T} s_k(t)\phi_j(t)\,dt$ for $j = 1, 2, \dots, k-1$
  - $g_k(t) = s_k(t) - \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} s_{kj}\phi_j(t)$
  - If $\int_{0}^{T} g_k^{2}(t)\,dt = 0$, skip; else, $\phi_k(t) = g_k(t)/\sqrt{\int_{0}^{T}g_k^{2}(t)\,dt}$.
- ☐ **Dimension relationship:** $N \le M$, with $N = M$ only when all signals are linearly independent.
- ☐ **BPSK has** $N = 1$, **QPSK has** $N = 2$, **orthogonal BFSK has** $N = 2$, **M-PAM has** $N = 1$.
- ☐ **Vector representation:** $\mathbf{s}_i = [s_{i1}, s_{i2}, \dots, s_{iN}]^T$ where $s_{ij} = \int_{0}^{T} s_i(t)\phi_j(t)\,dt$.
- ☐ **Euclidean distance between two signal points:** $d_{ij} = \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{N}(s_{ik} - s_{jk})^{2}}$.
- ☐ **Energy from vector form:** $E_i = \sum_{j=1}^{N} s_{ij}^{2}$ (Parseval-like).
- ☐ **Engineering significance:** The receiver uses only $N$ correlators (one per $\phi_j$) regardless of how many signals $M$ are present, and the detection is a simple minimum-distance (ML) decision.
- ☐ **Common exam trick:** When two signals differ only by a sign (e.g., BPSK), the dimension collapses to 1 — recognize this instantly.
- ☐ **Always end** your answer with the **vector representations** and the **dimension $N$** of the signal space.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Signal Space
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT502 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Signal Space

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Signal Space Representation** is a geometric framework that maps time-domain signals (energy signals of finite duration) onto an *N*-dimensional vector space, where each signal $s_i(t)$ is uniquely represented as a point (or vector) in terms of *N* linearly independent **orthogonal basis functions** $\{\phi_1(t), \phi_2(t), \dots, \phi_N(t)\}$.

Formally, a real-valued energy signal $s(t)$ defined on the interval $0 \leq t \leq T$ is represented as a linear combination of orthonormal basis functions:

$$s(t) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} s_j \, \phi_j(t)$$

where the coefficients (coordinates) are obtained via the inner product:

$$s_j = \int_{0}^{T} s(t)\,\phi_j(t)\,dt$$

The collection of all possible signal vectors $\{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_M\}$ representing the *M* symbols of the communication system forms a **Signal Constellation** in the *N*-dimensional **Euclidean Signal Space**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The GPS Coordinate System:**
> Imagine the Earth is your *signal space*. Every city on Earth is a "signal" you might want to represent. Instead of describing a city by its messy historical address (the time-domain waveform), you describe it by **(latitude, longitude)** — two clean numbers. The axes (latitude, longitude) are your **basis functions** $\phi_1, \phi_2$. Every city, no matter how complex its road network, is reduced to a single point in 2D space.
>
> The Euclidean distance between two such points (cities) tells you how different the two signals are. In communication systems, this geometric distance is the *direct determinant* of how reliably a receiver can distinguish between two transmitted symbols, especially in the presence of noise.

**Key Conceptual Translations:**

| Abstract Math Concept | Communication Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|
| Vector | Symbol waveform $s_i(t)$ |
| Basis function $\phi_j(t)$ | Building block used to reconstruct signals |
| Inner product $\langle s_i, s_j \rangle$ | Measure of similarity between signals |
| Vector norm $\|s_i\|$ | Energy of the signal, $E_i$ |
| Euclidean distance $d_{ij}$ | Distinguishability between symbols |
| Dimensionality $N$ | Minimum number of basis signals required |

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Signal Energy** $E_i = \int_0^T s_i^2(t)\,dt$
- **Inner Product**: $\langle s_i(t), s_j(t)\rangle = \int_0^T s_i(t)\,s_j(t)\,dt$
- **Orthonormality Condition**: $\int_0^T \phi_i(t)\phi_j(t)\,dt = \delta_{ij}$ (Kronecker delta)
- **Number of Dimensions $N$**: bounded by the number of linearly independent signals $N \leq M$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Orthonormal Basis Functions — The Building Blocks

A set of functions $\{\phi_1(t), \phi_2(t), \dots, \phi_N(t)\}$ is said to be **orthonormal** over the interval $[0, T]$ if and only if:

$$\int_{0}^{T} \phi_i(t)\,\phi_j(t)\,dt = \begin{cases} 1, & i = j \\ 0, & i \neq j \end{cases} \;=\; \delta_{ij}$$

**Properties of a Valid Basis:**
1. **Orthogonality** — distinct basis functions have zero inner product (they carry no mutual information).
2. **Normalization** — each basis function has unit energy.
3. **Completeness** — every signal in the set can be *exactly* reconstructed from its projections.

## 2.2 The Gram–Schmidt Orthogonalization Procedure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Gram–Schmidt (GS) procedure** is the algorithmic heart of signal space analysis. It takes a set of $M$ arbitrary energy signals $\{s_1(t), s_2(t), \dots, s_M(t)\}$ and produces an *orthonormal* basis set of size $N \leq M$. This is the algorithm KTU expects you to write in Part B (14-mark) questions.

### Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1 — Pick the first basis:** Take the first signal and normalize it:

$$g_1(t) = s_1(t) \quad\Rightarrow\quad \phi_1(t) = \frac{g_1(t)}{\sqrt{E_1}}, \quad E_1 = \int_0^T s_1^2(t)\,dt$$

- **Step 2 — Subtract the projection:** For the second signal, remove its component along $\phi_1$:

$$g_2(t) = s_2(t) - s_{21}\,\phi_1(t), \quad \text{where } s_{21} = \int_0^T s_2(t)\,\phi_1(t)\,dt$$

- **Step 3 — Normalize:** $\phi_2(t) = g_2(t)/\sqrt{\int_0^T g_2^2(t)\,dt}$

- **Step 4 — Iterate:** For the $k$-th signal, remove projections onto *all* previous bases:

$$g_k(t) = s_k(t) - \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} s_{kj}\,\phi_j(t), \quad s_{kj} = \int_0^T s_k(t)\,\phi_j(t)\,dt$$

- **Step 5 — Check for zero:** If $g_k(t) \equiv 0$ at any stage, then $s_k(t)$ is linearly dependent and is *not* a new basis function. Skip it.

The signal coordinates are then the vector of inner products:

$$\mathbf{s}_i = \big[s_{i1},\, s_{i2},\, \dots,\, s_{iN}\big]^T$$

## 2.3 Distance, Energy, and Correlation

For two signal vectors $\mathbf{s}_i$ and $\mathbf{s}_j$:

- **Euclidean Distance**: $d_{ij} = \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{N}(s_{ik} - s_{jk})^2} = \|\mathbf{s}_i - \mathbf{s}_j\|$
- **Correlation Coefficient**: $\rho_{ij} = \dfrac{\langle s_i(t), s_j(t)\rangle}{\sqrt{E_i E_j}}$
- **Signal Energy from Coordinates**: $E_i = \|\mathbf{s}_i\|^2 = \sum_{k=1}^{N} s_{ik}^2$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Reference Table** — all KTU-expected formulas for this topic. Use $\vert$ or `\mid` instead of `|` to preserve table formatting.

| Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Inner product of two signals | $\langle s_i, s_j \rangle = \int_0^T s_i(t)\,s_j(t)\,dt$ | Joules (V²·s) |
| Energy of signal $s_i(t)$ | $E_i = \int_0^T s_i^2(t)\,dt = \sum_{k=1}^{N} s_{ik}^2$ | Joules |
| Orthonormality | $\int_0^T \phi_i(t)\phi_j(t)\,dt = \delta_{ij}$ | Dimensionless |
| Projection coefficient | $s_{ij} = \int_0^T s_i(t)\,\phi_j(t)\,dt$ | $\sqrt{\text{Joules}}$ |
| Correlation coefficient | $\rho_{ij} = \dfrac{\int_0^T s_i(t)s_j(t)\,dt}{\sqrt{E_i E_j}}$ | $-1 \le \rho \le 1$ |
| Euclidean distance | $d_{ij} = \sqrt{E_i + E_j - 2\sqrt{E_i E_j}\,\rho_{ij}}$ | $\sqrt{\text{Joules}}$ |
| Dimensionality bound | $N \le M$ | $M$ = number of signals |
| Basis functions (Fourier) | $\phi_k(t) = \sqrt{\tfrac{2}{T}}\cos\!\left(\tfrac{2\pi k t}{T}\right)$ | Used for periodic signals |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Modulation Design:** BPSK, QPSK, QAM, and PSK constellations are designed as points in 2-D signal space; this enables the **minimum-distance criterion** for symbol detection.
- **BER Estimation:** Probability of error in AWGN is dominated by $Q\!\left(\frac{d_{min}}{2\sqrt{N_0}}\right)$ — entirely a signal-space quantity.
- **Source Coding (Karhunen–Loève Transform):** extends the orthonormal basis idea to stochastic processes for optimal compression.
- **Channel Capacity (SVD):** MIMO channel decomposition uses signal-space ideas to compute the maximum data rate.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Time-Domain Signal to Vector Representation

**Goal:** Show that $s(t) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} s_j\,\phi_j(t)$ is unique for a given orthonormal basis.

Starting from the expansion and projecting onto $\phi_k(t)$:

$$s(t) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} s_j\,\phi_j(t)$$

Multiply both sides by $\phi_k(t)$ and integrate over $[0, T]$:

$$\int_0^T s(t)\,\phi_k(t)\,dt = \int_0^T \sum_{j=1}^{N} s_j\,\phi_j(t)\,\phi_k(t)\,dt$$

Exchanging the sum and integral on the right-hand side:

$$\int_0^T s(t)\,\phi_k(t)\,dt = \sum_{j=1}^{N} s_j \int_0^T \phi_j(t)\,\phi_k(t)\,dt$$

Apply the **orthonormality condition** $\int_0^T \phi_j(t)\phi_k(t)\,dt = \delta_{jk}$:

$$\int_0^T s(t)\,\phi_k(t)\,dt = \sum_{j=1}^{N} s_j\,\delta_{jk} = s_k$$

Therefore each coefficient is uniquely determined:

$$\boxed{\,s_k = \int_0^T s(t)\,\phi_k(t)\,dt\,}$$

This proves the **one-to-one correspondence** between a time-domain signal and its signal-space vector.

## 3.2 Derivation: The Euclidean Distance in Terms of Energies and Correlation

**Goal:** Derive the alternate form $d_{ij}^2 = E_i + E_j - 2\sqrt{E_i E_j}\,\rho_{ij}$.

Starting from the squared distance:

$$d_{ij}^2 = \int_0^T \big[s_i(t) - s_j(t)\big]^2\,dt$$

Expand the square:

$$d_{ij}^2 = \int_0^T s_i^2(t)\,dt - 2\int_0^T s_i(t)s_j(t)\,dt + \int_0^T s_j^2(t)\,dt$$

Recognize each integral as energy or inner product:

$$d_{ij}^2 = E_i - 2\langle s_i, s_j\rangle + E_j$$

By the definition of the correlation coefficient:

$$\langle s_i, s_j\rangle = \rho_{ij}\sqrt{E_i E_j}$$

Substituting back:

$$\boxed{\,d_{ij}^2 = E_i + E_j - 2\rho_{ij}\sqrt{E_i E_j}\,}$$

For **equal-energy signals** ($E_i = E_j = E$):

$$d_{ij}^2 = 2E\,(1 - \rho_{ij})$$

This is the central formula for distinguishing orthogonal ($\rho = 0 \Rightarrow d^2 = 2E$) and antipodal ($\rho = -1 \Rightarrow d^2 = 4E$) signals.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Gram–Schmidt on BPSK

**Problem:** Two BPSK signals over $[0, T]$ are:
- $s_1(t) = +A$ for $0 \le t \le T$
- $s_2(t) = -A$ for $0 \le t \le T$

Find the orthonormal basis, vector representations, and the minimum distance.

**Step 1 — Energy of the first signal:**

$$E_1 = \int_0^T A^2\,dt = A^2 T$$

**Step 2 — First basis function:**

$$\phi_1(t) = \frac{s_1(t)}{\sqrt{E_1}} = \frac{A}{\sqrt{A^2 T}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{T}}$$

**Step 3 — Projection of $s_2(t)$ onto $\phi_1(t)$:**

$$s_{21} = \int_0^T s_2(t)\,\phi_1(t)\,dt = \int_0^T (-A)\,\frac{1}{\sqrt{T}}\,dt = -A\sqrt{T}$$

**Step 4 — Residual signal $g_2(t)$:**

$$g_2(t) = s_2(t) - s_{21}\,\phi_1(t) = -A - \big(-A\sqrt{T}\big)\frac{1}{\sqrt{T}} = -A + A = 0$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** $g_2(t) = 0$ means $s_2(t)$ lies entirely in the span of $\phi_1(t)$. Hence $N = 1$.

**Step 6 — Vector representations:**

$$\mathbf{s}_1 = \big[+\sqrt{A^2 T}\big] = \big[+A\sqrt{T}\big], \quad \mathbf{s}_2 = \big[-A\sqrt{T}\big]$$

**Step 7 — Minimum distance:**

$$d_{12} = \big\vert +A\sqrt{T} - (-A\sqrt{T})\big\vert = 2A\sqrt{T}$$

$$\boxed{\,d_{12} = 2A\sqrt{T}\,}$$

**Step 8 — Verification via correlation:** $\rho_{12} = -1$ (antipodal), $E_1 = E_2 = A^2 T$:

$$d_{12}^2 = 2E(1-\rho) = 2A^2 T(1-(-1)) = 4A^2 T \;\Rightarrow\; d_{12} = 2A\sqrt{T} \;\checkmark$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Modulation Schemes in Signal Space

| Modulation | $M$ | $N$ | Basis | Signal Vectors / Constellation | $d_{min}$ | $\rho_{min}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| BPSK | 2 | 1 | $\phi_1(t) = \sqrt{1/T}$ | $\{\pm\sqrt{E}\}$ | $2\sqrt{E}$ | $-1$ |
| Orthogonal (e.g., 2-FSK) | 2 | 2 | $\{\phi_1, \phi_2\}$ | $\{\sqrt{E}(1,0), \sqrt{E}(0,1)\}$ | $\sqrt{2E}$ | $0$ |
| QPSK | 4 | 2 | $\{\phi_1, \phi_2\}$ | $\sqrt{E/2}\{\pm 1, \pm 1\}$ | $\sqrt{2E}$ | $0$ |
| 16-QAM | 16 | 2 | $\{\phi_1, \phi_2\}$ | $a\sqrt{E/10}\,\{2i-1,\,2j-1\}$ | $\sqrt{\tfrac{4E}{10}}$ | varies |
| Biorthogonal (4-PAM-like) | 4 | 2 | $\{\phi_1, \phi_2\}$ | $\{\pm\sqrt{E},\, \pm\sqrt{E}\}$ | $\sqrt{2E}$ | $\pm 1,\,0$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Signal-to-Vector Mapping Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Signal Set s1 of t, s2 of t, ..., sM of t] --> B[Step 1 - Pick s1 t as seed]
    B --> C[Step 2 - Compute Energy E1]
    C --> D[Step 3 - Normalize to get phi1 t]
    D --> E[Step 4 - For k = 2 to M, subtract projections]
    E --> F[Step 5 - Test if residual gk t is nonzero]
    F -->|Yes| G[Normalize residual to get phik t]
    G --> E
    F -->|No, gk t equals 0| H[Skip - s k t is dependent]
    H --> E
    E --> I[Collect orthonormal basis phi1, phi2, ..., phiN]
    I --> J[Compute vector coordinates sij equals inner product]
    J --> K[Output Signal Vectors s1, s2, ..., sM in RN]
    K --> L[Draw Signal Constellation in N-D Space]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Concept Map: Signal Space Conceptual Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Signal Space Theory] --> B1[Orthonormal Basis Functions]
    A0 --> B2[Signal Vector Representation]
    A0 --> B3[Distance and Energy Metrics]
    A0 --> B4[Gram Schmidt Procedure]

    B1 --> C1[Orthogonality Condition]
    B1 --> C2[Normalization Condition]
    B1 --> C3[Completeness Condition]

    B2 --> D1[Inner Product as Projection]
    B2 --> D2[Coordinates as inner products]
    B2 --> D3[One-to-one mapping]

    B3 --> E1[Euclidean Distance d ij]
    B3 --> E2[Signal Energy E i]
    B3 --> E3[Correlation Coefficient rho ij]

    B4 --> F1[Forward Projection Step]
    B4 --> F2[Subtraction of Components]
    B4 --> F3[Normalization Step]
    B4 --> F4[Dimensionality Reduction]
```

## 4.3 Signal Space Constellation (Conceptual Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Constellation[Two-Dimensional Signal Constellation Example QPSK]
        direction LR
        P1[Vector s1 at plus sqrt E, plus sqrt E] --- OriginA[(Origin 0,0)]
        P2[Vector s2 at minus sqrt E, plus sqrt E] --- OriginA
        P3[Vector s3 at minus sqrt E, minus sqrt E] --- OriginA
        P4[Vector s4 at plus sqrt E, minus sqrt E] --- OriginA
        P1 -. Minimum Euclidean Distance .-> P2
        P2 -. Minimum Euclidean Distance .-> P3
    end

    Note["d_min equals sqrt 2E for QPSK - Critical for BER calculation in AWGN"]
    Constellation --> Note
```

## 4.4 Receiver Decision Regions in Signal Space

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Received Noisy Vector r] --> B[Compute Distances to all M Symbols]
    B --> C[Choose Symbol with Minimum Distance]
    C --> D[Decision Region D i - Voronoi Cell around s i]

    style D fill:#FFE4B5
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> Define the **signal space representation** of signals. What do you mean by an **orthonormal basis**?

**Model Answer:**
Signal space representation is the geometric mapping of energy signals $\{s_i(t)\}$ onto a finite-dimensional Euclidean vector space, where each signal is represented as a linear combination of *N* orthonormal basis functions $\{\phi_j(t)\}_{j=1}^{N}$. A set of functions is **orthonormal** over $[0, T]$ if every pair is mutually orthogonal *and* each function has unit energy:

$$\int_0^T \phi_i(t)\,\phi_j(t)\,dt = \delta_{ij}$$

where $\delta_{ij}$ is the Kronecker delta.

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> What is the **Gram–Schmidt orthogonalization procedure**? Why is it important in digital communication?

**Model Answer:**
The Gram–Schmidt procedure is a systematic algorithm that converts a set of $M$ linearly independent (or dependent) energy signals into an equivalent **orthonormal basis** of size $N \le M$. It works by sequentially subtracting the projections of each new signal onto all previously found basis functions, then normalizing the residual. Its importance in digital communication is threefold:
1. It provides a *canonical* way to obtain signal-space coordinates.
2. It determines the **minimum dimensionality** $N$ required to represent the constellation.
3. It enables **distance-based receiver design** — the Euclidean distance between constellation points becomes the metric for symbol detection and probability-of-error estimation in AWGN.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Internal Choice Pattern (KTU Standard):** Two sub-parts, each 7 marks. We provide **Choice A** and **Choice B** as independent alternates.

---

### ⭐ CHOICE A

#### **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**Question A(a): (7 Marks)**
For the three signals given below defined over $0 \le t \le T$, find an orthonormal basis set using the Gram–Schmidt procedure:
- $s_1(t) = 1$ for $0 \le t \le T$ and $0$ otherwise
- $s_2(t) = t/T$ for $0 \le t \le T$ and $0$ otherwise
- $s_3(t) = (t/T)^2$ for $0 \le t \le T$ and $0$ otherwise

Express each signal as a vector in the derived signal space and compute the minimum Euclidean distance between any pair.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Energy of $s_1(t)$:**

$$E_1 = \int_0^T 1^2\,dt = T$$

**Step 2 — First basis function:**

$$\phi_1(t) = \frac{s_1(t)}{\sqrt{E_1}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{T}}$$

**Step 3 — Projection of $s_2(t)$ onto $\phi_1(t)$:**

$$s_{21} = \int_0^T \frac{t}{T}\,\frac{1}{\sqrt{T}}\,dt = \frac{1}{T\sqrt{T}} \int_0^T t\,dt = \frac{1}{T\sqrt{T}} \cdot \frac{T^2}{2} = \frac{\sqrt{T}}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Residual $g_2(t)$:**

$$g_2(t) = s_2(t) - s_{21}\,\phi_1(t) = \frac{t}{T} - \frac{\sqrt{T}}{2}\cdot\frac{1}{\sqrt{T}} = \frac{t}{T} - \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Energy of $g_2(t)$:**

$$\int_0^T \left(\frac{t}{T} - \frac{1}{2}\right)^2 dt = \int_0^T \left(\frac{t^2}{T^2} - \frac{t}{T} + \frac{1}{4}\right)dt$$

$$= \frac{1}{T^2}\cdot\frac{T^3}{3} - \frac{1}{T}\cdot\frac{T^2}{2} + \frac{T}{4} = \frac{T}{3} - \frac{T}{2} + \frac{T}{4} = T\left(\frac{4-6+3}{12}\right) = \frac{T}{12}$$

**Step 6 — Second basis function:**

$$\phi_2(t) = \frac{g_2(t)}{\sqrt{T/12}} = \sqrt{\frac{12}{T}}\left(\frac{t}{T} - \frac{1}{2}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Projections for $s_3(t)$:**

$$s_{31} = \int_0^T \left(\frac{t}{T}\right)^2 \frac{1}{\sqrt{T}}\,dt = \frac{1}{T^2\sqrt{T}}\cdot\frac{T^3}{3} = \frac{\sqrt{T}}{3}$$

$$s_{32} = \int_0^T \left(\frac{t}{T}\right)^2 \sqrt{\frac{12}{T}}\left(\frac{t}{T} - \frac{1}{2}\right)dt$$

$$= \sqrt{\frac{12}{T}}\left[\frac{1}{T^3}\int_0^T t^3 dt - \frac{1}{2T^2}\int_0^T t^2 dt\right] = \sqrt{\frac{12}{T}}\left[\frac{T}{4} - \frac{T}{6}\right] = \sqrt{\frac{12}{T}}\cdot\frac{T}{12} = \frac{\sqrt{12T}}{12} = \frac{\sqrt{3T}}{6}$$

**Step 8 — Residual $g_3(t)$ and its energy:** Computation yields $\int g_3^2 dt = T/180$, hence a non-zero $\phi_3(t)$ exists, so $N = 3$.

**Step 9 — Vector representations:**

$$\mathbf{s}_1 = \big[\sqrt{T},\; 0,\; 0\big]$$
$$\mathbf{s}_2 = \big[\tfrac{\sqrt{T}}{2},\; \sqrt{T/12},\; 0\big]$$
$$\mathbf{s}_3 = \big[\tfrac{\sqrt{T}}{3},\; \tfrac{\sqrt{3T}}{6},\; \sqrt{T/180}\,\big]$$

**Step 10 — Minimum distance (between $\mathbf{s}_1$ and $\mathbf{s}_2$):**

$$d_{12}^2 = \left(\sqrt{T} - \tfrac{\sqrt{T}}{2}\right)^2 + \left(0 - \sqrt{T/12}\right)^2 = \frac{T}{4} + \frac{T}{12} = \frac{4T}{12} = \frac{T}{3}$$

$$\boxed{\,d_{min} = \sqrt{\tfrac{T}{3}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{T}}{\sqrt{3}}\,}$$

#### **Question A(b): (7 Marks)**
Explain how the **minimum Euclidean distance** between signal points affects the **probability of error** for a digital communication system operating in an **AWGN channel**. State the relevant expression and discuss why an *orthogonal* signaling scheme is generally inferior to an *antipodal* scheme of the same energy.

**Model Solution:**

**Probability of error expression** for equiprobable binary signaling with minimum distance $d_{min}$ in AWGN of two-sided PSD $N_0/2$:

$$P_e = Q\!\left(\frac{d_{min}}{2\sqrt{N_0}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{d_{min}^2}{4N_0}}\right)$$

**Effect of $d_{min}$:** The argument of the $Q$-function is **monotonically increasing** with $d_{min}$. Hence, **larger $d_{min} \Rightarrow$ smaller $P_e$** for the same noise level. Maximizing minimum distance is therefore the central goal of constellation design.

**Comparison of signaling schemes** at equal energy $E$:

| Scheme | $\rho$ | $d_{min}^2$ | $P_e$ Expression |
|---|---|---|---|
| Antipodal (e.g., BPSK) | $-1$ | $4E$ | $Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E/N_0}\right)$ |
| Orthogonal (e.g., 2-FSK) | $0$ | $2E$ | $Q\!\left(\sqrt{E/N_0}\right)$ |

For the same energy $E$ and noise PSD $N_0$, antipodal signaling has $d_{min}^2$ that is **twice as large** as the orthogonal case. The argument of its $Q$-function is therefore $\sqrt{2}$ times larger, giving a strictly lower $P_e$ (by approximately $3$ dB in SNR at low error rates). Consequently, **antipodal schemes are universally preferred** when both options are available at equal symbol energy.

---

### ⭐ CHOICE B

#### **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Paper]**

**Question B(a): (7 Marks)**
For the four signal waveforms defined over $[0, T]$ as
- $s_1(t) = +A$ on $[0, T/2]$, $0$ otherwise
- $s_2(t) = 0$ on $[0, T/2]$, $+A$ on $[T/2, T]$
- $s_3(t) = -A$ on $[0, T/2]$, $0$ otherwise
- $s_4(t) = 0$ on $[0, T/2]$, $-A$ on $[T/2, T]$

(i) Find the orthonormal basis. (ii) Represent each signal as a vector. (iii) Find all pairwise distances.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Energy of each signal:** $E_i = A^2 \cdot (T/2) = A^2 T/2$ for all $i$.

**Step 2 — First basis function:**

$$\phi_1(t) = \frac{s_1(t)}{\sqrt{E_1}} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{T}}\;\text{on } [0, T/2], \quad 0 \text{ otherwise}$$

**Step 3 — Projection of $s_2(t)$ onto $\phi_1(t)$:**

$$s_{21} = \int_0^{T/2} 0 \cdot \phi_1(t)\,dt + \int_{T/2}^T A\cdot\sqrt{\frac{2}{T}}\,dt = A\sqrt{\frac{2}{T}}\cdot\frac{T}{2} = A\sqrt{\frac{T}{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Residual $g_2(t)$:**

$$g_2(t) = s_2(t) - s_{21}\phi_1(t) = 0 - A\sqrt{T/2}\cdot\sqrt{2/T} = -A \;\;\text{on } [0, T/2]$$

On $[T/2, T]$: $g_2(t) = A - A\sqrt{T/2}\cdot 0 = A$.

So $g_2(t) = -A$ on $[0, T/2]$ and $+A$ on $[T/2, T]$. Its energy is $2 \cdot A^2 \cdot T/2 = A^2 T$.

**Step 5 — Second basis function:**

$$\phi_2(t) = \frac{g_2(t)}{A\sqrt{T}} = \begin{cases} -1/\sqrt{T}, & 0 \le t < T/2 \\ +1/\sqrt{T}, & T/2 \le t \le T \end{cases}$$

**Step 6 — Check $s_3$ and $s_4$ are dependent:** Projecting $s_3$ onto both $\phi_1$ and $\phi_2$ yields non-zero components for each, but $s_3$ and $s_4$ are linear combinations of $s_1$ and $s_2$. Hence $N = 2$.

**Step 7 — All projection coefficients:**

$$s_{11} = A\sqrt{T/2},\quad s_{12} = -A\sqrt{T}$$

$$s_{21} = A\sqrt{T/2},\quad s_{22} = +A\sqrt{T}$$

$$s_{31} = -A\sqrt{T/2},\quad s_{32} = -A\sqrt{T}$$

$$s_{41} = -A\sqrt{T/2},\quad s_{42} = +A\sqrt{T}$$

**Step 8 — Vector representations:**

$$\mathbf{s}_1 = A\sqrt{T/2}\,[1,\; -\sqrt{2}]^T$$
$$\mathbf{s}_2 = A\sqrt{T/2}\,[1,\; +\sqrt{2}]^T$$
$$\mathbf{s}_3 = A\sqrt{T/2}\,[-1,\; -\sqrt{2}]^T$$
$$\mathbf{s}_4 = A\sqrt{T/2}\,[-1,\; +\sqrt{2}]^T$$

**Step 9 — Pairwise distances:**

$$d_{12} = d_{34} = A\sqrt{T/2}\cdot 2\sqrt{2} = 2A\sqrt{T}$$
$$d_{13} = d_{24} = A\sqrt{T/2}\cdot 2 = A\sqrt{2T}$$
$$d_{14} = d_{23} = \sqrt{(A\sqrt{T/2}\cdot 2)^2 + (A\sqrt{T/2}\cdot 2\sqrt{2})^2} = A\sqrt{T/2}\cdot 2\sqrt{3} = A\sqrt{6T}$$

$$\boxed{\,d_{min} = d_{13} = d_{24} = A\sqrt{2T}\,}$$

This is the **orthogonal 4-point constellation** (a 2-D variant of 4-FSK).

#### **Question B(b): (7 Marks)**
Define the **correlation coefficient** $\rho_{ij}$ between two signals. For three signals with energies $E_1 = E_2 = E_3 = E$ and $\rho_{12} = 0.5$, $\rho_{13} = -0.5$, $\rho_{23} = 0$, find the minimum distance and identify the geometric configuration (orthogonal / biorthogonal / equilateral).

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of correlation coefficient:**

$$\rho_{ij} = \frac{\int_0^T s_i(t)\,s_j(t)\,dt}{\sqrt{E_i E_j}}$$

with $-1 \le \rho_{ij} \le 1$. It is bounded by the **Cauchy–Schwarz inequality** and represents the cosine of the angle between signal vectors in the signal space.

**Compute pairwise squared distances** using $d_{ij}^2 = E_i + E_j - 2\rho_{ij}\sqrt{E_i E_j} = 2E(1 - \rho_{ij})$:

$$d_{12}^2 = 2E(1 - 0.5) = E$$
$$d_{13}^2 = 2E(1 - (-0.5)) = 3E$$
$$d_{23}^2 = 2E(1 - 0) = 2E$$

**Minimum distance:** $d_{min} = \sqrt{E}$.

**Geometric classification:** The three pairwise distances are $\sqrt{E}$, $\sqrt{2E}$, $\sqrt{3E}$, which scale as $1 : \sqrt{2} : \sqrt{3}$. This matches the **equilateral triangle** configuration (when drawn in a 2-D plane), and is **neither fully orthogonal nor biorthogonal**. Equilateral constellations achieve the largest minimum distance per unit energy for $M = 3$ signals — a useful design insight for low-$M$ alphabets.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks on Signal Space problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize.** Gram–Schmidt does *not* stop at subtraction; you **must** divide by $\sqrt{E}$ of the residual. Losing normalization = losing 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Skipping the linearity check.** If $g_k(t) = 0$, the signal is dependent — *do not* create a new basis function. Examiners award marks for explicitly identifying the dimensionality $N \le M$.
> 3. **Wrong interval in integrals.** For Gram–Schmidt and inner products, integrate **only over the support of the signal**, not blindly over $[0, T]$. Mis-bounded integrals are a silent mark-killer.
> 4. **Mixing up $\rho$ and $d$.** Correlation $\rho$ and distance $d$ are *not* the same. $d^2 = 2E(1-\rho)$ is the only correct relation; many students write $d^2 = 2E(1-\rho^2)$ or similar.
> 5. **Not drawing the constellation.** KTU examiners give **2 marks** for the constellation diagram. Even a hand-drawn 2-D plot of vector endpoints earns full credit.
> 6. **Confusing energy units.** $\int_0^T s^2(t)\,dt$ is in Joules only if $s$ is in Volts. State the unit assumption explicitly.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Signal Space**
>
> - **Signal-to-vector mapping:** $s_i(t) \;\leftrightarrow\; \mathbf{s}_i = [s_{i1}, s_{i2}, \dots, s_{iN}]^T$ with $s_{ij} = \int_0^T s_i(t)\phi_j(t)\,dt$.
> - **Orthonormality condition** is the cornerstone: $\int_0^T \phi_i(t)\phi_j(t)\,dt = \delta_{ij}$.
> - **Gram–Schmidt algorithm** has 5 key steps: (1) pick seed, (2) normalize, (3) project, (4) subtract, (5) test residual $\ne 0$.
> - **Dimensionality bound:** $N \le M$ always; check for linear dependence at every iteration.
> - **Distance formula** (equal energy): $d_{ij}^2 = 2E(1 - \rho_{ij})$.
> - **Antipodal signals:** $\rho = -1$, $d^2 = 4E$ — gives the *best* $P_e$ in AWGN at equal energy.
> - **Orthogonal signals:** $\rho = 0$, $d^2 = 2E$ — a 3 dB SNR loss vs. antipodal.
> - **Probability of error in AWGN:** $P_e = Q(d_{min}/(2\sqrt{N_0}))$ — directly controlled by signal-space geometry.
> - **Constellation design goal:** maximize $d_{min}$ subject to a fixed average energy constraint.
> - **Common exam trap:** $N = M$ is *not* guaranteed — many constellations (BPSK, 4-FSK-like) have $N < M$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vector Model of AWGN Channel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT502 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vector Model of AWGN Channel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — VECTOR MODEL OF THE AWGN CHANNEL

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Vector Model of the Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN) Channel** is a mathematical abstraction in which the received continuous-time signal $r(t)$ is decomposed into a finite set of projections (inner products) over an orthonormal basis of the signal space. The transmitted signal $s_i(t)$, the noise $n(t)$, and the received signal $r(t)$ are each represented as N-dimensional vectors $\mathbf{s}_i$, $\mathbf{n}$, and $\mathbf{r}$ respectively, with the canonical relationship:

$$\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{s}_i + \mathbf{n}$$

where the noise vector $\mathbf{n} = [n_1, n_2, \ldots, n_N]^T$ consists of **independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) zero-mean Gaussian random variables**, each with variance $\sigma_n^2 = \dfrac{N_0}{2}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The vector model is the foundational bridge between the **continuous-time waveform channel model** and the **discrete-time statistical detection framework** used to derive optimum receivers and the bit-error probability $P_e$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are inside a completely dark room holding a 3-D object (say, a twisted wire sculpture). You cannot see the *whole* object at once. However, you can shine a light from three perpendicular directions and record the length of the shadow cast on the wall for each direction. Those three shadow lengths form a **coordinate vector** that uniquely identifies the object.

In the same way, the AWGN channel delivers a continuous waveform $r(t)$ that lives in an infinite-dimensional function space. Since information is encoded in a finite-dimensional signal set $\{s_i(t)\}_{i=1}^{M}$, the receiver only needs to **project** $r(t)$ onto the $N$ orthogonal directions that span the signal space. These projections give a finite **vector** $\mathbf{r}$, which fully captures all the information needed for detection. The noise adds a random "jitter" to each shadow length — and that jitter is Gaussian because of the Central Limit Theorem applied to thermal noise.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in This Module

- **$N_0/2$** — two-sided Power Spectral Density of AWGN in **watts/Hz**.
- **$E_b$** — energy per bit in **joules**.
- **$E_s$** — energy per symbol in **joules**.
- **$N$** — dimensionality of the signal space.
- **$\mathbf{s}_i \cdot \mathbf{s}_j$** — inner product between signal vectors.
- **$\Vert \mathbf{s}_i \Vert^2$** — squared Euclidean norm = $E_i$ (signal energy).

> [!NOTE]
> The vector model assumes **coherent detection** (the receiver knows the carrier phase and timing), so signal vectors are deterministic and reside at fixed points in the signal space.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Construction

The construction of the vector model proceeds through the following rigorous chain of logic:

- **Step 1 — Continuous-time channel model.** The received signal at the matched-filter/correlator input is written as the sum of the transmitted waveform and white Gaussian noise:
$$r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t), \quad 0 \le t \le T$$

- **Step 2 — Choose an orthonormal basis.** Using the **Gram–Schmidt orthogonalization procedure**, we generate $N$ real orthonormal basis functions $\{\phi_1(t), \phi_2(t), \ldots, \phi_N(t)\}$ such that:
$$\int_0^T \phi_j(t)\,\phi_k(t)\,dt = \delta_{jk} = \begin{cases} 1, & j = k \\ 0, & j \neq k \end{cases}$$

- **Step 3 — Project $s_i(t)$ onto the basis.** Each transmitted signal is uniquely expressed as a linear combination of the basis functions:
$$s_i(t) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} s_{ij}\,\phi_j(t), \quad \text{where} \quad s_{ij} = \int_0^T s_i(t)\,\phi_j(t)\,dt$$

- **Step 4 — Project $n(t)$ onto the basis.** Since white Gaussian noise is *infinite-dimensional*, it has projections on all basis functions, but only the $N$ in-signal-space components affect detection:
$$n_j = \int_0^T n(t)\,\phi_j(t)\,dt \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \mathbf{n} = [n_1, n_2, \ldots, n_N]^T$$

- **Step 5 — Form the received vector.** By linearity of the inner product:
$$r_j = \int_0^T r(t)\,\phi_j(t)\,dt = s_{ij} + n_j \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \mathbf{r} = \mathbf{s}_i + \mathbf{n}$$

- **Step 6 — Statistical characterization.** Each $n_j$ is Gaussian with mean $0$ and variance $N_0/2$. Since the projections are integrals of the *same* white process over disjoint (orthogonal) supports, the $n_j$ are statistically **independent**, making $\mathbf{n}$ a zero-mean Gaussian random vector with covariance matrix $\mathbf{C_n} = \dfrac{N_0}{2}\mathbf{I}_N$.

- **Step 7 — Conditional PDF of $\mathbf{r}$ given $\mathbf{s}_i$.** Because $\mathbf{r}$ is a translation of a Gaussian vector by a constant $\mathbf{s}_i$, it remains Gaussian with mean $\mathbf{s}_i$ and the same covariance:
$$f_{\mathbf{r}}(\mathbf{r} \mid \mathbf{s}_i) = \frac{1}{(\pi N_0)^{N/2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{N_0}\,\Vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i \Vert^2\right)$$

- **Step 8 — Optimum decision rule (Maximum Likelihood).** For equiprobable signals, minimizing $\Vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i \Vert^2$ over $i$ is equivalent to maximizing $f_{\mathbf{r}}(\mathbf{r} \mid \mathbf{s}_i)$. The receiver therefore selects the signal **closest (in Euclidean distance) to the received vector**.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

| Step | Why it matters |
|---|---|
| Orthonormal basis | Ensures projections are uncorrelated — directly giving independent noise components. |
| Truncation to $N$ dimensions | All information about $s_i(t)$ is captured; noise in the orthogonal complement cannot help detection. |
| Gaussian PDF form | The likelihood ratio test reduces to a clean Euclidean-distance decision rule. |
| $\mathbf{C_n} = \dfrac{N_0}{2}\mathbf{I}_N$ | Spherical noise isotropy means **all directions are equally noisy**, so geometry alone drives error probability. |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning / Condition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{s}_i + \mathbf{n}$ | Canonical vector channel model |
| 2 | $s_{ij} = \displaystyle\int_0^T s_i(t)\,\phi_j(t)\,dt$ | Signal coordinate on $\phi_j(t)$ |
| 3 | $n_j = \displaystyle\int_0^T n(t)\,\phi_j(t)\,dt$ | Noise coordinate, $\mathcal{N}\!\left(0, \tfrac{N_0}{2}\right)$ |
| 4 | $E_i = \Vert \mathbf{s}_i \Vert^2 = \displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{N} s_{ij}^2$ | Energy of $i$-th signal |
| 5 | $E[n_j n_k] = \tfrac{N_0}{2}\,\delta_{jk}$ | Independence of noise components |
| 6 | $\mathbf{C_n} = \tfrac{N_0}{2}\mathbf{I}_N$ | Noise covariance matrix |
| 7 | $f_{\mathbf{r}}(\mathbf{r} \mid \mathbf{s}_i) = \dfrac{1}{(\pi N_0)^{N/2}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i \Vert^2}{N_0}\right)$ | Conditional Gaussian PDF |
| 8 | $\hat{i} = \arg\min_i \Vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i \Vert^2$ | Minimum-distance (ML) decision rule |
| 9 | $d_{ij}^2 = \Vert \mathbf{s}_i - \mathbf{s}_j \Vert^2$ | Squared Euclidean distance between signals |
| 10 | $P_e = Q\!\left(\dfrac{d_{ij}}{2\sqrt{N_0/2}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{d_{ij}^2}{2N_0}}\right)$ | Pairwise error probability (binary) |

> [!NOTE]
> The notation $Q(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\displaystyle\int_x^{\infty} e^{-u^2/2}\,du$ is the Gaussian tail-integral function and is the standard KTU-board notation for complementary error functions: $Q(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}\operatorname{erfc}\!\left(\tfrac{x}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The vector model is the **mathematical engine** behind:
- **Matched-filter and correlator receivers** in 4G/5G baseband processing.
- **M-ary PSK and QAM constellation design** in Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11ax), 5G NR, and satellite modems.
- **MLSE / Viterbi equalizers** in ISI channels (treating ISI taps as basis functions).
- **MIMO spatial multiplexing**, where the channel matrix $\mathbf{H}$ acts on a vector of symbols.
- **Software-Defined Radio (SDR)** implementations that compute inner products in DSP blocks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Continuous Channel to Vector Model

We begin with the received waveform and reduce it to an $N$-dimensional vector.

**Starting equation:**
$$r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t), \qquad 0 \le t \le T$$

**Step 1.** Define the correlator outputs for each basis function. Multiply both sides by $\phi_k(t)$ and integrate over one symbol duration:
$$\int_0^T r(t)\,\phi_k(t)\,dt = \int_0^T s_i(t)\,\phi_k(t)\,dt + \int_0^T n(t)\,\phi_k(t)\,dt$$

**Step 2.** By the linearity of integration, this simplifies to:
$$r_k = s_{ik} + n_k$$

**Step 3.** Write the collection of all $N$ correlator outputs as a single vector equation:
$$\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{s}_i + \mathbf{n}$$

This is the **vector model of the AWGN channel**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mean and Covariance of the Noise Vector

We must show that the components $n_k$ are i.i.d. Gaussian with mean $0$ and variance $N_0/2$.

**Step 1 — Mean.** Since $n(t)$ is a zero-mean Gaussian random process:
$$E[n_k] = E\!\left[\int_0^T n(t)\,\phi_k(t)\,dt\right] = \int_0^T E[n(t)]\,\phi_k(t)\,dt = 0$$

**Step 2 — Variance of a single component.**
$$E[n_k^2] = E\!\left[\int_0^T\!\!\int_0^T n(t)\,n(\tau)\,\phi_k(t)\,\phi_k(\tau)\,dt\,d\tau\right]$$

Pulling the expectation inside and using the autocorrelation of white noise $E[n(t)n(\tau)] = \dfrac{N_0}{2}\,\delta(t-\tau)$:
$$E[n_k^2] = \int_0^T\!\!\int_0^T \frac{N_0}{2}\,\delta(t-\tau)\,\phi_k(t)\,\phi_k(\tau)\,dt\,d\tau$$

**Step 3.** Use the sifting property of the delta function to perform the inner integral over $\tau$:
$$E[n_k^2] = \frac{N_0}{2}\int_0^T \phi_k^2(t)\,dt$$

**Step 4.** Apply the normalization $\displaystyle\int_0^T \phi_k^2(t)\,dt = 1$:
$$E[n_k^2] = \frac{N_0}{2}$$

Hence $\operatorname{Var}(n_k) = \sigma_n^2 = \dfrac{N_0}{2}$ as claimed.

**Step 5 — Cross-correlation between two distinct components** ($k \ne m$):
$$E[n_k n_m] = \int_0^T\!\!\int_0^T \frac{N_0}{2}\,\delta(t-\tau)\,\phi_k(t)\,\phi_m(\tau)\,dt\,d\tau$$

**Step 6.** Evaluate the inner integral over $\tau$ first, leaving $t$ fixed:
$$E[n_k n_m] = \frac{N_0}{2}\int_0^T \phi_k(t)\,\phi_m(t)\,dt$$

**Step 7.** Apply the orthogonality condition $\displaystyle\int_0^T \phi_k(t)\phi_m(t)\,dt = 0$ for $k \ne m$:
$$E[n_k n_m] = 0$$

**Step 8.** Combining the diagonal and off-diagonal results into matrix form:
$$E[\mathbf{n}\,\mathbf{n}^T] = \frac{N_0}{2}\mathbf{I}_N$$

This confirms the noise vector is zero-mean with spherical covariance — i.e., its components are uncorrelated (and since they are jointly Gaussian, **statistically independent**).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Conditional PDF of the Received Vector

The receiver computes $f_{\mathbf{r}}(\mathbf{r} \mid \mathbf{s}_i)$ to perform ML detection.

**Step 1.** Since $n_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2)$ and the components are independent, the joint PDF of $\mathbf{n}$ is the product of the marginals:
$$f_{\mathbf{n}}(\mathbf{n}) = \prod_{k=1}^{N} \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi N_0}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{n_k^2}{N_0}\right)$$

**Step 2.** Combine the product by summing the exponents:
$$f_{\mathbf{n}}(\mathbf{n}) = \frac{1}{(\pi N_0)^{N/2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{N_0}\sum_{k=1}^{N} n_k^2\right)$$

**Step 3.** Recognize the squared sum as the squared Euclidean norm:
$$\sum_{k=1}^{N} n_k^2 = \Vert \mathbf{n} \Vert^2$$

**Step 4.** Substitute the vector relationship $\mathbf{n} = \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i$ into the joint PDF. The Jacobian of the translation is unity, so:
$$f_{\mathbf{r}}(\mathbf{r} \mid \mathbf{s}_i) = \frac{1}{(\pi N_0)^{N/2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{N_0}\Vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i \Vert^2\right)$$

**Step 5.** The ML rule for equiprobable signals is:
$$\hat{i} = \arg\max_i \, f_{\mathbf{r}}(\mathbf{r} \mid \mathbf{s}_i)$$

**Step 6.** Since the natural-logarithm is monotonic, the arg-max is preserved:
$$\hat{i} = \arg\max_i \left[-\frac{1}{N_0}\Vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i \Vert^2\right]$$

**Step 7.** The negative sign flips the max into a min:
$$\hat{i} = \arg\min_i \, \Vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i \Vert^2$$

This is the celebrated **minimum-distance receiver**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Pairwise Probability of Error (Binary Case)

For two equiprobable signals $\mathbf{s}_1$ and $\mathbf{s}_2$ with $E_1 = E_2 = E_b$ and Euclidean separation $d = \Vert \mathbf{s}_1 - \mathbf{s}_2 \Vert$:

**Step 1.** Define the midpoint $\mathbf{m} = \dfrac{\mathbf{s}_1 + \mathbf{s}_2}{2}$. A decision error occurs when the noise vector $\mathbf{n}$ pushes $\mathbf{r}$ across this midpoint.

**Step 2.** The error probability is the probability that the noise projection along the vector $\mathbf{u} = \dfrac{\mathbf{s}_1 - \mathbf{s}_2}{\Vert \mathbf{s}_1 - \mathbf{s}_2 \Vert}$ exceeds $d/2$:
$$P_e = P\!\left(n_u > \frac{d}{2}\right)$$

**Step 3.** Because $n_u$ is a linear combination of jointly Gaussian random variables, it is itself Gaussian with zero mean and variance $N_0/2$.

**Step 4.** Standardize by dividing by the standard deviation $\sqrt{N_0/2}$:
$$P_e = P\!\left(\frac{n_u}{\sqrt{N_0/2}} > \frac{d/2}{\sqrt{N_0/2}}\right) = Q\!\left(\frac{d}{2\sqrt{N_0/2}}\right)$$

**Step 5.** Simplify the inner expression:
$$\frac{d}{2\sqrt{N_0/2}} = \frac{d}{2}\cdot\sqrt{\frac{2}{N_0}} = \sqrt{\frac{d^2}{2N_0}}$$

**Step 6.** Final closed-form expression:
$$P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{d^2}{2N_0}}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This compact expression is the **cornerstone formula** for nearly every KTU Board problem on binary signaling — substituting specific constellation distances yields $P_e$ for BPSK ($d^2 = 4E_b$), BFSK orthogonal ($d^2 = 2E_b$), and BFSK antipodal ($d^2 = 4E_b$).

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem.** Two equiprobable signals $\mathbf{s}_1 = [+\sqrt{E_b},\, 0]^T$ and $\mathbf{s}_2 = [-\sqrt{E_b},\, 0]^T$ are transmitted over an AWGN channel with $N_0/2 = 10^{-20}$ W/Hz. Compute the probability of error if $E_b = 4 \times 10^{-19}$ J.

**Step 1.** Compute the squared Euclidean distance:
$$d^2 = \Vert \mathbf{s}_1 - \mathbf{s}_2 \Vert^2 = (2\sqrt{E_b})^2 = 4E_b = 4(4\times 10^{-19}) = 1.6 \times 10^{-18}\,\text{J}$$

**Step 2.** Compute the argument of the $Q$-function:
$$\sqrt{\frac{d^2}{2N_0}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.6\times 10^{-18}}{2 \times 2 \times 10^{-20}}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.6\times 10^{-18}}{4\times 10^{-20}}} = \sqrt{40} \approx 6.32$$

**Step 3.** Evaluate $Q(6.32)$ using the Chernoff bound $Q(x) \le \tfrac{1}{2}e^{-x^2/2}$:
$$P_e \le \frac{1}{2}e^{-6.32^2/2} = \frac{1}{2}e^{-19.97} \approx 9.6 \times 10^{-10}$$

This is an extremely low error probability, consistent with deep-space communication links.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Vector Channel Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Continuous received waveform r of t] --> A1[Choose orthonormal basis phi 1 to phi N]
    A1 --> A2[Compute N correlator outputs]
    A2 --> A3[Form received vector r as N by 1 column]
    A3 --> A4[Compute squared distance to each candidate s i]
    A4 --> A5[Pick the index i that minimises norm of r minus s i]
    A5 --> A6[Decision output s i hat]
    B1[Transmitted signal s i of t] --> B2[Expand s i of t on the basis]
    B2 --> B3[Signal vector s i in N dim]
    C1[AWGN n of t] --> C2[Project n of t on each phi j]
    C2 --> C3[Noise vector n in N dim]
    B3 --> A3
    C3 --> A3
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Functional Receiver Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Received waveform r of t] --> R2[Bank of N correlators]
    R2 --> R3[Vector r]
    R3 --> R4[Subtract each s i]
    R4 --> R5[Square norm]
    R5 --> R6[Compare to find minimum]
    R6 --> R7[Detector output]
    P1[Local reference set s 1 to s M] --> R4
    N1[AWGN n of t] --> R1
    S1[Transmitter s i of t] --> N1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Geometry — 2-D Constellation Example (BPSK / BFSK)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Signal space of dimension N equals 2] --> Q2[S 1 at plus sqrt E b comma 0]
    Q1 --> Q3[S 2 at minus sqrt E b comma 0]
    Q4[Decision boundary perpendicular bisector at x equals 0] --> Q5[Receiver picks nearest point]
    Q2 --> Q5
    Q3 --> Q5
    Q6[Received vector r equals s i plus n Gaussian cloud] --> Q5
```

## 4.4 ASCII Constellation Diagram (BPSK, 2-D)

```
                       N-dim Signal Space
                             |
            s_1 = (+√E_b, 0) |  *
                             |
       ----------------------+----------------------  Decision Boundary
                             |  *  s_2 = (-√E_b, 0)
                             |
                  Received r = s_i + n (Gaussian blob)
                  Probability cloud centred on s_i
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the vector model of the AWGN channel and write the expression for the conditional probability density function of the received vector.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 Mark)** Definition: The vector model of the AWGN channel represents the received signal $r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t)$ in terms of $N$ orthonormal basis functions $\phi_j(t)$, yielding the vector relationship $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{s}_i + \mathbf{n}$, where $\mathbf{r}, \mathbf{s}_i, \mathbf{n} \in \mathbb{R}^N$.
- **(1 Mark)** The noise vector $\mathbf{n}$ is zero-mean Gaussian with covariance $\mathbf{C_n} = \dfrac{N_0}{2}\mathbf{I}_N$.
- **(1 Mark)** Conditional PDF:
$$f_{\mathbf{r}}(\mathbf{r} \mid \mathbf{s}_i) = \frac{1}{(\pi N_0)^{N/2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i \Vert^2}{N_0}\right)$$

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State the minimum-distance decision rule for detecting M-ary signals in an AWGN channel. Why is this rule optimal for equiprobable signals?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 Mark)** The rule: choose $\hat{i} = \arg\min_i \Vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i \Vert^2$.
- **(1 Mark)** Justification: For equiprobable signals, ML detection maximizes $f_{\mathbf{r}}(\mathbf{r} \mid \mathbf{s}_i)$. Since $f_{\mathbf{r}}(\mathbf{r} \mid \mathbf{s}_i) \propto \exp\!\left(-\Vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i \Vert^2/N_0\right)$, maximizing the likelihood is equivalent to minimizing the squared distance.
- **(1 Mark)** The rule is also the **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)** rule when priors are equal, making it **minimum-probability-of-error** optimal.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**

**(a)** Starting from the continuous-time received signal $r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t)$, derive the vector model of the AWGN channel. Show that the components of the noise vector are i.i.d. Gaussian with zero mean and variance $N_0/2$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the conditional PDF of the received vector and obtain the minimum-distance decision rule. Hence derive the probability of error expression for a binary equiprobable signal set in terms of the Euclidean distance $d$ and the noise PSD $N_0$. **(7 Marks)**

#### ⭐ Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Define the basis and projections (2 Marks).** Using Gram–Schmidt, construct $N$ orthonormal basis functions $\phi_1(t), \ldots, \phi_N(t)$ over $[0,T]$. The correlator output for the $k$-th basis is:
$$r_k = \int_0^T r(t)\,\phi_k(t)\,dt = s_{ik} + n_k, \quad s_{ik} = \int_0^T s_i(t)\,\phi_k(t)\,dt, \quad n_k = \int_0^T n(t)\,\phi_k(t)\,dt$$

**Step 2 — Form the vector equation (1 Mark):**
$$\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{s}_i + \mathbf{n}, \qquad \mathbf{r}, \mathbf{s}_i, \mathbf{n} \in \mathbb{R}^N$$

**Step 3 — Mean of $n_k$ (1 Mark).**
$$E[n_k] = \int_0^T E[n(t)]\,\phi_k(t)\,dt = 0$$

**Step 4 — Variance of $n_k$ (2 Marks).**
$$E[n_k^2] = \int_0^T\!\!\int_0^T \frac{N_0}{2}\,\delta(t-\tau)\,\phi_k(t)\,\phi_k(\tau)\,dt\,d\tau = \frac{N_0}{2}\int_0^T \phi_k^2(t)\,dt = \frac{N_0}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Independence / uncorrelatedness (1 Mark).** For $k \ne m$:
$$E[n_k n_m] = \frac{N_0}{2}\int_0^T \phi_k(t)\phi_m(t)\,dt = 0$$

Joint Gaussianity + zero cross-covariance implies **statistical independence**. Hence $\mathbf{n} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \tfrac{N_0}{2}\mathbf{I}_N)$.

#### ⭐ Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Joint PDF of $\mathbf{n}$ (2 Marks).**
$$f_{\mathbf{n}}(\mathbf{n}) = \prod_{k=1}^{N} \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi N_0}}\,e^{-n_k^2/N_0} = \frac{1}{(\pi N_0)^{N/2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Vert \mathbf{n} \Vert^2}{N_0}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Translation $\mathbf{n} = \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i$ (1 Mark):**
$$f_{\mathbf{r}}(\mathbf{r} \mid \mathbf{s}_i) = \frac{1}{(\pi N_0)^{N/2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i \Vert^2}{N_0}\right)$$

**Step 3 — ML detection (1 Mark).** For equiprobable signals:
$$\hat{i} = \arg\max_i f_{\mathbf{r}}(\mathbf{r} \mid \mathbf{s}_i) = \arg\min_i \Vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i \Vert^2$$

**Step 4 — Pairwise error (2 Marks).** For binary signals with separation $d = \Vert \mathbf{s}_1 - \mathbf{s}_2 \Vert$, an error occurs if the noise projection $n_u$ on the direction $\mathbf{u} = (\mathbf{s}_1 - \mathbf{s}_2)/d$ exceeds $d/2$:
$$P_e = P\!\left(n_u > \frac{d}{2}\right), \qquad n_u \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0, \frac{N_0}{2}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Final closed form (1 Mark):**
$$P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{d^2}{2N_0}}\right)$$

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the construction of an orthonormal basis for an M-ary signal set using the Gram–Schmidt orthogonalization procedure. Illustrate with a numerical example for a 3-signal set. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a binary signal set $\mathbf{s}_1 = (+\sqrt{E_b}, 0)$ and $\mathbf{s}_2 = (-\sqrt{E_b}, 0)$ over an AWGN channel with $N_0 = 4 \times 10^{-20}$ W/Hz, compute the probability of error when $E_b = 10^{-19}$ J. Also, find the minimum SNR per bit required to achieve $P_e \le 10^{-6}$. **(7 Marks)**

#### ⭐ Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Motivation (1 Mark).** Any set of $M$ energy signals $\{s_i(t)\}$ spans a signal space of dimension $N \le M$. The Gram–Schmidt process produces an explicit orthonormal basis.

**Step 2 — Algorithm (3 Marks).** Start with $s_1(t)$. Set:
$$\phi_1(t) = \frac{s_1(t)}{\sqrt{E_1}}, \qquad E_1 = \int_0^T s_1^2(t)\,dt$$

For $i = 2, \ldots, M$, compute the residual of $s_i(t)$ after subtracting projections on $\{\phi_1, \ldots, \phi_{i-1}\}$:
$$g_i(t) = s_i(t) - \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} s_{ij}\,\phi_j(t), \quad s_{ij} = \int_0^T s_i(t)\,\phi_j(t)\,dt$$

If $g_i(t) \ne 0$, define the next basis function:
$$\phi_i(t) = \frac{g_i(t)}{\sqrt{\int_0^T g_i^2(t)\,dt}}$$

If $g_i(t) = 0$, $s_i(t)$ is a linear combination of previous basis functions and the dimension is unchanged.

**Step 3 — Numerical example (3 Marks).** Take three signals:
$$s_1(t) = \sqrt{E}\,\psi(t), \quad s_2(t) = \sqrt{E/2}\,\psi(t) + \sqrt{E/2}\,\psi(t-T/2), \quad s_3(t) = \sqrt{E/2}\,\psi(t) - \sqrt{E/2}\,\psi(t-T/2)$$

Apply Gram–Schmidt. First, $E_1 = E$, so $\phi_1(t) = \psi(t)$. Then $s_{21} = \sqrt{E/2}$ and the residual $g_2(t) = \sqrt{E/2}\,\psi(t-T/2)$ has energy $E/2$, hence $\phi_2(t) = \psi(t-T/2)$. For $s_3$, both projections exist, but the residual $g_3(t) = -\sqrt{E/2}\,\psi(t-T/2)$ is proportional to $\phi_2(t)$, so $s_3$ lies in the 2-D space spanned by $\{\phi_1, \phi_2\}$ — and the signal-space dimension is $N = 2$.

#### ⭐ Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Distance computation (1 Mark).**
$$d^2 = \Vert \mathbf{s}_1 - \mathbf{s}_2 \Vert^2 = (2\sqrt{E_b})^2 = 4E_b = 4 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{J}$$

**Step 2 — Error probability (2 Marks).**
$$\sqrt{\frac{d^2}{2N_0}} = \sqrt{\frac{4\times 10^{-19}}{2 \times 4 \times 10^{-20}}} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236$$
$$P_e = Q(\sqrt{5}) \approx 1.25 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 3 — SNR per bit (1 Mark).** Define $\gamma_b = E_b/N_0$. From $P_e = Q(\sqrt{2\gamma_b})$, the condition $P_e \le 10^{-6}$ requires:
$$\sqrt{2\gamma_b} \ge Q^{-1}(10^{-6}) \approx 4.753$$
$$\gamma_b \ge \frac{(4.753)^2}{2} \approx 11.29 \quad (\text{or } 10.53\,\text{dB})$$

**Step 4 — Numerical SNR check (3 Marks).** With given $E_b = 10^{-19}$ J and $N_0 = 4\times 10^{-20}$ W/Hz:
$$\gamma_b = \frac{10^{-19}}{4\times 10^{-20}} = 2.5 \quad (\approx 3.98\,\text{dB})$$

This is **insufficient** to meet $P_e \le 10^{-6}$ — the SNR must be increased by a factor of $11.29/2.5 \approx 4.52$ (i.e., $E_b$ must be raised to $\approx 4.52 \times 10^{-19}$ J) to achieve the target.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of $1/2$** in the variance — students often write $\sigma_n^2 = N_0$ instead of $N_0/2$, costing 1 full mark in derivations.
> 2. **Mixing up $E_b$ and $E_s$** — for M-ary signaling, $E_s = (\log_2 M)E_b$; examiners explicitly deduct 1 mark if these are conflated.
> 3. **Skipping the "sifting property"** of the delta function in the variance derivation — this is a 2-mark line item; write it explicitly.
> 4. **Not stating "jointly Gaussian implies independent"** — without this phrase, full credit for the i.i.d. claim is not awarded.
> 5. **Forgetting the Jacobian argument** when changing variables from $f_{\mathbf{n}}$ to $f_{\mathbf{r}}$ — a translation has unit Jacobian, but you must mention it for full marks.
> 6. **In $Q$-function arguments** — write the full simplification $\dfrac{d/2}{\sqrt{N_0/2}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{d^2}{2N_0}}$ step by step; jump-cutting loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Vector Model of AWGN Channel):**
> - The vector model is $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{s}_i + \mathbf{n}$ with $\mathbf{n} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \tfrac{N_0}{2}\mathbf{I}_N)$.
> - **Gram–Schmidt** generates an $N$-dimensional orthonormal basis, where $N \le M$.
> - Each signal $s_i(t)$ maps to a deterministic vector $\mathbf{s}_i \in \mathbb{R}^N$.
> - Each noise component $n_k$ is $\mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2)$ and **independent** across $k$.
> - Conditional PDF: $f_{\mathbf{r}}(\mathbf{r} \mid \mathbf{s}_i) = \dfrac{1}{(\pi N_0)^{N/2}}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i \Vert^2}{N_0}\right)$.
> - **Minimum-distance receiver** $\hat{i} = \arg\min_i \Vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i \Vert^2$ is ML-optimal for equiprobable signals.
> - **Binary error probability:** $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{d^2 / 2N_0}\right)$.
> - **BPSK:** $d^2 = 4E_b \Rightarrow P_e = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$.
> - **Orthogonal BFSK:** $d^2 = 2E_b \Rightarrow P_e = Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$.
> - **Antipodal BFSK:** $d^2 = 4E_b \Rightarrow P_e = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ (same as BPSK).
> - Always specify whether you are using $E_b$ (energy/bit) or $E_s$ (energy/symbol).
> - Remember $Q(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}\operatorname{erfc}(x/\sqrt{2})$; conversions must show the factor $\sqrt{2}$.
> - For M-ary unions, use the **union bound**: $P_e \le \sum_{i \ne j} P_i\,P(\mathbf{s}_i \to \mathbf{s}_j)$.
> - The covariance isotropy $\mathbf{C_n} = \tfrac{N_0}{2}\mathbf{I}_N$ implies **all directions in signal space are equally noisy** — geometry alone determines performance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Matched Filter and Correlation Receivers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT502 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Matched Filter and Correlation Receivers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Matched Filter — Formal Definition

In the presence of Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN), a **Matched Filter** is a linear, time-invariant (LTI) filter whose impulse response $h(t)$ is specifically engineered (matched) to a known input signal $s(t)$ such that, at the sampling instant $t = T_b$, it **maximizes the output Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**. The filter is "matched" to the signal shape, not to the noise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition:** A matched filter is the optimal linear filter for detecting a known signal in AWGN, designed to maximize the output SNR at the decision instant. It forms the cornerstone of optimal detection theory under the Neyman–Pearson and Bayesian (Minimum Error Probability) criteria.

The formal condition is:

$$h(t) = K \cdot s(T_b - t)$$

where $K$ is an arbitrary gain constant, $T_b$ is the bit duration, and $s(t)$ is the known transmitted signal pulse shape.

### 1.2 The Correlation Receiver — Formal Definition

A **Correlation Receiver** is a mathematically equivalent alternative realization of the matched filter. Instead of passing the received signal through a filter and sampling, it multiplies the received signal $r(t)$ by a locally generated, synchronized replica of the known signal $s(t)$ over one bit duration, and then integrates the product. The decision is made based on the integrator output at $t = T_b$.

Mathematically, the decision variable is:

$$y(T_b) = \int_{0}^{T_b} r(t) \cdot s(t) \, dt$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Equivalence Theorem:** For all practical purposes in digital communication, the matched filter and the correlation receiver are **mathematically equivalent** and yield identical decision statistics at $t = T_b$. The choice between them is purely an **implementation perspective**.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **The "Tuning Fork" Analogy** — Imagine you are trying to hear a faint, pure musical note (say, 440 Hz "A") in a noisy concert hall. The smartest way to detect it is NOT to use your ears as a generic amplifier. Instead, you bring a **tuning fork precisely tuned to 440 Hz**. When held near the source, the tuning fork vibrates *strongly* in sympathy with the right note, but barely responds to the rumble of other instruments. The tuning fork is "matched" to the desired signal.
>
> In communication, the **matched filter is the tuning fork of electronics**: its internal memory (impulse response) is sculpted to be the *time-reversed mirror image* of the incoming pulse. When the right pulse arrives, the filter output builds up constructively to a sharp maximum; when noise (random) arrives, it averages to near zero.

### 1.4 Key Engineering Metrics

- **Optimality Criterion:** Maximization of instantaneous output SNR at decision instant $t = T_b$.
- **Noise Assumption:** AWGN with two-sided Power Spectral Density $N_0/2$ (in W/Hz).
- **Decision Rule:** Threshold comparison against a fixed level (zero for equiprobable binary antipodal signaling).
- **Bit Error Rate (BER) for Binary Signaling:** $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ for antipodal, and $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ for orthogonal.
- **Standard Symbols:** $E_b$ = energy per bit, $N_0$ = noise PSD, $Q(\cdot)$ = Q-function (Gaussian tail integral).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Optimization Problem — "Why" Matched Filter?

A corrupted received signal in AWGN is modeled as:

$$r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t), \quad 0 \le t \le T_b$$

where $s_i(t)$ is one of $M$ known deterministic signals (e.g., $s_1(t)$ for bit "1" and $s_2(t)$ for bit "0"), and $n(t)$ is zero-mean AWGN with PSD $S_n(f) = N_0/2$.

The receiver passes $r(t)$ through an LTI filter with impulse response $h(t)$ and frequency response $H(f)$. At the sampling instant $t_0$, the output is:

$$y(t_0) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} H(f) \, S(f) \, e^{j 2\pi f t_0} \, df + \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} H(f) \, N(f) \, e^{j 2\pi f t_0} \, df$$

The first term is the signal component $s_o(t_0)$; the second is the noise component $n_o(t_0)$.

### 2.2 The "How" — Step-by-Step Logic of the Matched Filter Derivation

1. **Identify the signal component** of the filter output at $t_0 = T_b$:
$$s_o(T_b) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} H(f) \, S(f) \, e^{j 2\pi f T_b} \, df$$

2. **Compute the output noise power** (mean-square value) using Parseval's theorem:
$$\overline{n_o^2(t)} = \frac{N_0}{2} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert H(f) \vert^2 df$$

3. **Formulate the output SNR** at the decision instant:
$$\text{SNR}_o = \frac{s_o^2(T_b)}{\overline{n_o^2(t)}} = \frac{\left\vert \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} H(f) S(f) e^{j 2\pi f T_b} df \right\vert^2}{\frac{N_0}{2} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert H(f) \vert^2 df}$$

4. **Apply the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality**, $\left\vert \int A(f) B(f) df \right\vert^2 \le \int \vert A(f) \vert^2 df \cdot \int \vert B(f) \vert^2 df$, with the substitution $A(f) = H(f)$ and $B(f) = S(f) e^{j 2 \pi f T_b}$.

5. **The SNR is maximized** when equality in Cauchy–Schwarz holds, i.e., when $H(f)$ is proportional to $S^*(f) e^{-j 2 \pi f T_b}$:
$$H_{\text{opt}}(f) = K \, S^*(f) \, e^{-j 2 \pi f T_b}$$

6. **Take the inverse Fourier transform** to obtain the impulse response:
$$h(t) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{H_{\text{opt}}(f)\} = K \, s(T_b - t)$$

7. **The maximum achievable SNR** at the output is:
$$\text{SNR}_{\max} = \frac{2 E_s}{N_0}$$
where $E_s = \int_0^{T_b} s^2(t) \, dt$ is the signal energy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The matched filter's transfer function is the **complex conjugate of the signal spectrum**, multiplied by a linear phase delay $e^{-j 2 \pi f T_b}$. This is the famous "conjugate filter" property in radar/sonar literature.

### 2.3 The Correlation Receiver Logic

Starting from the matched filter output at $t = T_b$:

$$y(T_b) = \int_{0}^{T_b} r(\tau) \, h(T_b - \tau) \, d\tau = \int_{0}^{T_b} r(\tau) \, K \cdot s(\tau) \, d\tau$$

Since $K$ is a constant, the decision can be based on:

$$z = \int_{0}^{T_b} r(\tau) \, s(\tau) \, d\tau$$

This is a **single integration** (multiply-by-$s(t)$ and integrate), which is **easier to implement digitally** in modern DSP-based receivers. For causal implementation, the matched filter is non-causal (it has support on $[0, T_b]$ but the filter is defined over $[-T_b, 0]$ before time reversal), while the correlator is naturally causal.

### 2.4 Properties of the Matched Filter

- **Property 1 — Shape Match:** $h(t)$ is a time-reversed and shifted copy of $s(t)$.
- **Property 2 — Causal Realization:** A practical delay $\Delta \ge T_b$ is introduced: $h(t) = s(\Delta - t)$.
- **Property 3 — Waveform Preservation:** The output $y(t)$ is proportional to the **autocorrelation** $R_s(\tau)$ of the input signal.
- **Property 4 — Noise Whitening:** The matched filter is equivalent to a **whitened matched filter** when followed by a pre-whitening filter that flattens the colored noise spectrum.
- **Property 5 — Maximum SNR:** The output SNR depends only on signal energy $E_s$ and $N_0$, *not* on the signal shape.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Variables / Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $h(t) = K \, s(T_b - t)$ | Matched filter impulse response | $K$ = gain (unitless), $t$ in seconds |
| 2 | $H(f) = K \, S^*(f) \, e^{-j 2 \pi f T_b}$ | Frequency response (conjugate filter) | $S^*(f)$ = complex conjugate spectrum |
| 3 | $\text{SNR}_{\max} = \dfrac{2 E_s}{N_0}$ | Maximum output SNR (real signal) | $E_s$ in Joules, $N_0$ in W/Hz |
| 4 | $y(T_b) = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{T_b} r(t) \, s(t) \, dt$ | Correlator decision variable | Output in Volt-seconds |
| 5 | $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_d}{2 N_0}}\right)$ | Probability of error (general) | $E_d = \int (s_1 - s_2)^2 dt$ |
| 6 | $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2 E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | BER — antipodal binary (BPSK) | Antipodal signaling |
| 7 | $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | BER — orthogonal binary (BFSK) | Orthogonal signaling |
| 8 | $Q(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \displaystyle\int_x^{\infty} e^{-u^2/2} du$ | Q-function definition | Probability tail |
| 9 | $R_s(\tau) = \displaystyle\int s(t) \, s(t+\tau) \, dt$ | Autocorrelation function | $\tau$ in seconds |
| 10 | $\overline{n_o^2} = \dfrac{N_0}{2} \displaystyle\int \vert H(f) \vert^2 df$ | Output noise variance | Power in Watts |
| 11 | $E_s = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{T_b} s^2(t) \, dt$ | Signal energy | Joules |
| 12 | $d_{\min}^2 = \displaystyle\int (s_1 - s_2)^2 dt$ | Squared Euclidean distance | Energy units |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Matched filters are the heart of nearly every digital communication receiver — from BPSK in Wi-Fi to QPSK in 4G/5G to pulse compression in radar. The "matched filter concept" extends to *spread spectrum* (RAKE receivers), *OFDM channel estimation*, and *optical communication*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Optimality of the Matched Filter

We begin with the received signal:

$$r(t) = s(t) + n(t), \quad 0 \le t \le T_b$$

where $s(t)$ is a known deterministic signal and $n(t)$ is zero-mean AWGN with two-sided PSD $N_0/2$.

**Step 1 — Output of the Linear Filter:**

The received signal $r(t)$ is passed through a linear filter with impulse response $h(t)$ and frequency response $H(f) = \mathcal{F}\{h(t)\}$.

The output is given by convolution:

$$y(t) = r(t) * h(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} r(\tau) \, h(t - \tau) \, d\tau$$

In the frequency domain:

$$Y(f) = R(f) \cdot H(f) = S(f) H(f) + N(f) H(f)$$

**Step 2 — Signal Component at $t = T_b$:**

We sample the filter output at the decision instant $t = T_b$. Using the inverse Fourier transform:

$$s_o(T_b) = y_s(T_b) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S(f) H(f) \, e^{j 2\pi f T_b} \, df$$

This can be written in inner-product form as:

$$s_o(T_b) = \langle S(f) e^{j 2 \pi f T_b}, H(f) \rangle$$

**Step 3 — Output Noise Power:**

The output noise is $n_o(t) = n(t) * h(t)$. Its PSD is the input PSD multiplied by $\vert H(f) \vert^2$:

$$S_{n_o}(f) = S_n(f) \cdot \vert H(f) \vert^2 = \frac{N_0}{2} \vert H(f) \vert^2$$

The total average noise power at the filter output is the integral of the PSD:

$$\overline{n_o^2(t)} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S_{n_o}(f) \, df = \frac{N_0}{2} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert H(f) \vert^2 \, df$$

**Step 4 — Output SNR:**

Define the output SNR at the decision instant:

$$\text{SNR}_o = \frac{s_o^2(T_b)}{\overline{n_o^2(t)}} = \frac{\left\vert \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} H(f) S(f) e^{j 2 \pi f T_b} df \right\vert^2}{\frac{N_0}{2} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert H(f) \vert^2 df}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Cauchy–Schwarz Inequality:**

The Cauchy–Schwarz inequality for two complex functions $A(f)$ and $B(f)$ states:

$$\left\vert \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} A(f) B^*(f) \, df \right\vert^2 \le \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert A(f) \vert^2 df \cdot \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert B(f) \vert^2 df$$

with equality if and only if $A(f) = c \, B(f)$ for some constant $c$.

We identify:

$$A(f) = H(f), \quad B(f) = S(f) e^{j 2\pi f T_b}$$

Then the numerator of the SNR becomes:

$$\left\vert \int H(f) \left[ S(f) e^{j 2 \pi f T_b} \right]^* df \right\vert^2 \le \int \vert H(f) \vert^2 df \cdot \int \vert S(f) \vert^2 df$$

Note that $\left[ S(f) e^{j 2 \pi f T_b} \right]^* = S^*(f) e^{-j 2 \pi f T_b}$.

**Step 6 — Substitute the Upper Bound:**

$$\text{SNR}_o \le \frac{\int \vert H(f) \vert^2 df \cdot \int \vert S(f) \vert^2 df}{\frac{N_0}{2} \int \vert H(f) \vert^2 df} = \frac{2}{N_0} \int \vert S(f) \vert^2 df$$

**Step 7 — Apply Parseval's Theorem:**

By Parseval's theorem, the energy of the signal is:

$$E_s = \int_{0}^{T_b} s^2(t) \, dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert S(f) \vert^2 \, df$$

**Step 8 — Maximum SNR Expression:**

$$\boxed{\text{SNR}_{\max} = \frac{2 E_s}{N_0}}$$

This is achieved when equality holds in Cauchy–Schwarz, which requires:

$$H(f) = K \, S^*(f) \, e^{-j 2 \pi f T_b}$$

**Step 9 — Derive the Impulse Response:**

Take the inverse Fourier transform:

$$h(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} H(f) \, e^{j 2 \pi f t} df = K \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S^*(f) \, e^{-j 2 \pi f T_b} \, e^{j 2 \pi f t} df$$

$$= K \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S^*(f) \, e^{j 2 \pi f (t - T_b)} df = K \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S^*(f) \, e^{-j 2 \pi f (T_b - t)} df$$

Using the property that $\mathcal{F}^{-1}\{S^*(f)\} = s(-t)$:

$$h(t) = K \, s(-(T_b - t)) = K \, s(t - T_b)$$

Wait — let us carefully redo this step. We have:

$$H(f) = K \, S^*(f) \, e^{-j 2 \pi f T_b}$$

The inverse Fourier transform of $S^*(f)$ is $s(-t)$ (time-reversed signal). Multiplication by $e^{-j 2 \pi f T_b}$ in frequency corresponds to a delay of $T_b$ in time. Therefore:

$$h(t) = K \, s(-(t - T_b)) = K \, s(T_b - t)$$

$$\boxed{h(t) = K \, s(T_b - t)}$$

**Step 10 — Final Decision Variable via Correlator:**

The matched filter output at $t = T_b$ is:

$$y(T_b) = \int_{0}^{T_b} r(\tau) \, h(T_b - \tau) \, d\tau = K \int_{0}^{T_b} r(\tau) \, s(\tau) \, d\tau$$

Setting $K = 1$ (since it cancels in threshold comparison):

$$\boxed{y(T_b) = \int_{0}^{T_b} r(\tau) \, s(\tau) \, d\tau}$$

This is the **correlation receiver** realization. ∎

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Matched Filter Output Equals Autocorrelation

**Step 1 — Substitute** $r(t) = s(t) + n(t)$ into the matched filter output:

$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} r(\tau) \, h(t - \tau) \, d\tau = \int_{0}^{T_b} [s(\tau) + n(\tau)] \cdot s(T_b - (t - \tau)) \, d\tau$$

Wait, using $h(t) = s(T_b - t)$ (assuming $K = 1$):

$$y(t) = \int_{0}^{T_b} r(\tau) \, s(T_b - t + \tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 2 — Let** $\lambda = T_b - t$. Then $s(T_b - t + \tau) = s(\tau + \lambda)$, and:

$$y(t) = \int_{0}^{T_b} r(\tau) \, s(\tau + \lambda) \, d\tau = R_{rs}(\lambda) = R_{rs}(T_b - t)$$

**Step 3 — At the decision instant** $t = T_b$, $\lambda = 0$:

$$y(T_b) = R_{rs}(0) = \int_{0}^{T_b} r(\tau) \, s(\tau) \, d\tau$$

The matched filter output is thus proportional to the **cross-correlation** of the received signal and the known pulse.

> [!TIP]
> **Crucial Property:** When $r(t) = s(t)$ (no noise), the output $y(T_b) = R_s(0) = E_s$ (the signal energy), which is the **maximum possible** autocorrelation value at zero lag. This explains the name "matched" — the filter's output peaks exactly at $E_s$ when the input matches the design signal.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Probability of Error for Binary Signaling

**Step 1 — Decision Variable:**

For equiprobable binary signals $s_1(t)$ and $s_2(t)$, the optimal receiver computes:

$$y = \int_{0}^{T_b} r(t) [s_1(t) - s_2(t)] \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Compute Mean Values:**

Under hypothesis $H_1$ (signal $s_1$ sent): $E[y \mid H_1] = E_s^{(1)} - \int s_1 s_2 dt$

Under hypothesis $H_0$ (signal $s_2$ sent): $E[y \mid H_0] = \int s_2 s_1 dt - E_s^{(2)}$

**Step 3 — Compute the Mean-Square Distance:**

$$E_d = \int_{0}^{T_b} [s_1(t) - s_2(t)]^2 dt = E_s^{(1)} + E_s^{(2)} - 2 \int s_1 s_2 dt$$

**Step 4 — Noise Variance at the Output:**

$$\sigma_n^2 = \frac{N_0}{2} \int [s_1(t) - s_2(t)]^2 dt = \frac{N_0}{2} E_d$$

**Step 5 — Probability of Error:**

With threshold at the midpoint and Gaussian noise:

$$P_e = Q\!\left( \frac{E_d / 2}{\sigma_n} \right) = Q\!\left( \frac{E_d / 2}{\sqrt{N_0 E_d / 2}} \right) = Q\!\left( \sqrt{\frac{E_d}{2 N_0}} \right)$$

$$\boxed{P_e = Q\!\left( \sqrt{\frac{E_d}{2 N_0}} \right)}$$

**Special Cases:**

- **Antipodal Signaling** (e.g., BPSK: $s_1 = -s_2$, $E_s$ per bit): $E_d = 4 E_b \Rightarrow P_e = Q\!\left( \sqrt{\dfrac{2 E_b}{N_0}} \right)$

- **Orthogonal Signaling** (e.g., BFSK: $\int s_1 s_2 dt = 0$): $E_d = 2 E_b \Rightarrow P_e = Q\!\left( \sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}} \right)$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Matched Filter Receiver

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the architectural flow of a matched filter receiver in a binary communication system. Each block is a functional unit, and the arrows indicate the direction of signal flow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inA["Received Signal r(t)"] --> inB["Matched Filter h(t)=K*s(Tb-t)"]
    inB --> inC["Sampler at t=Tb"]
    inC --> inD["Decision Device"]
    inD --> inE{"Threshold Compare"}
    inE --> inF["Output Bit 0"]
    inE --> inG["Output Bit 1"]
    inH["AWGN Channel n(t)"] --> inA
    inI["Local Reference s(t)"] --> inB
```

**Functional Description:**

1. The **Received Signal** $r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t)$ arrives from the channel.
2. The **Matched Filter** has its impulse response matched to the known pulse shape $s(t)$.
3. A **Sampler** captures the filter output at the optimal decision instant $t = T_b$.
4. The **Decision Device** compares the sampled value against a threshold.
5. The threshold is zero for equiprobable antipodal signaling; positive threshold for unequal energies.
6. The final output is the detected bit (0 or 1).

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Correlation Receiver

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Received r(t)"] --> nodeB["Multiplier"]
    nodeC["Local Replica s(t)"] --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeD["Integrator 0 to Tb"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Sample and Hold at Tb"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Threshold Comparator"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Output Decision Bit"]
```

**Functional Description:**

1. The **Received Signal** is multiplied element-wise by a locally generated, synchronized replica of $s(t)$.
2. The **Integrator** accumulates the product over one symbol period $[0, T_b]$.
3. A **Sample-and-Hold** circuit captures the integrator output at $t = T_b$.
4. The **Threshold Comparator** makes the binary decision.
5. This is mathematically equivalent to the matched filter but uses synchronous multiplication instead of convolution.

---

### 4.3 Equivalence Flow — Matched Filter ↔ Correlation Receiver

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    eqA["Matched Filter y(t) = r(t) * h(t)"] -- "at t = Tb" --> eqB["y(Tb) = Integral r(tau) h(Tb-tau) dtau"]
    eqB -- "Substitute h=Ks" --> eqC["y(Tb) = K * Integral r(tau) s(tau) dtau"]
    eqC -- "Define z=y/K" --> eqD["Correlator Output z = Integral r(tau) s(tau) dtau"]
    eqD -- "Mathematically Identical" --> eqE["Same Decision Statistic"]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- The matched filter (time-domain convolution) and the correlator (multiply-and-integrate) are **mathematically equivalent** at the decision instant.
- The constant $K$ cancels out in the threshold comparison.
- The choice between them depends on **implementation**: matched filters are preferred for analog/RF front-ends; correlators are preferred for digital/DSP implementations.

---

### 4.4 Whitened Matched Filter Architecture (for Colored Noise)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    wmA["Received r(t) in Colored Noise"] --> wmB["Pre-Whitening Filter 1 over Hc f"]
    wmB --> wmC["Whitened Signal v(t) in AWGN"]
    wmC --> wmD["Matched Filter for v(t)"]
    wmD --> wmE["Decision Device"]
    wmF["Colored Noise PSD Hc f squared"] --> wmB
```

**Description:** When the noise is *colored* (non-flat PSD), the optimal receiver is the **whitened matched filter**: a pre-whitening filter $1/H_c(f)$ is cascaded before the standard matched filter, transforming the problem back into AWGN. This is a critical extension covered in advanced detection theory.
