## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part A questions test *recall* and *conceptual understanding*. Model answers should be 3 to 4 sentences with relevant formulas.

---

**Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper]`

**Define a matched filter. Derive the expression for the impulse response of a matched filter in terms of the input signal.**

**Model Answer:**

A matched filter is an optimal linear filter used for detecting a known signal $s(t)$ corrupted by AWGN. It maximizes the output SNR at the sampling instant $t = T_b$.

$$h(t) = K \cdot s(T_b - t)$$

The impulse response is a time-reversed, shifted, and scaled replica of the known input signal. The constant $K$ is an arbitrary gain that does not affect the SNR at the output. The optimality is proven by applying the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality to the SNR expression, leading to the condition $H(f) = K \cdot S^*(f) e^{-j 2 \pi f T_b}$. **(3 marks)**

---

**Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Model Paper]`

**Explain the concept of a correlation receiver and state its relationship with the matched filter.**

**Model Answer:**

A correlation receiver multiplies the incoming signal $r(t)$ by a locally generated, synchronized replica of the known pulse $s(t)$ and integrates the product over one bit duration $T_b$:

$$y(T_b) = \int_{0}^{T_b} r(t) \cdot s(t) \, dt$$

The decision is then made by comparing $y(T_b)$ against a threshold. The correlation receiver is **mathematically equivalent** to the matched filter at the decision instant $t = T_b$, since the matched filter output at this instant reduces to the same integral expression. While matched filters are typically implemented as analog tapped-delay-line filters, correlation receivers are preferred in modern DSP-based systems due to ease of digital implementation. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part B questions test *application* and *analysis*. They usually feature an internal choice between two questions. The 14 marks are split into 7 + 7 across two sub-parts.

---

#### **Question B-A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 / Model Paper]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the impulse response of a matched filter that maximizes the output SNR in AWGN. State clearly the assumptions and the inequality used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a binary communication system using antipodal signaling over an AWGN channel, derive the expression for the probability of error in terms of $E_b$ and $N_0$. Compare its performance with orthogonal signaling. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Matched Filter Impulse Response:**

**Step 1:** Received signal: $r(t) = s(t) + n(t)$, $0 \le t \le T_b$, where $n(t)$ is AWGN with PSD $N_0/2$.

**Step 2:** Output of the linear filter at $t = T_b$:

$$y(T_b) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} H(f) S(f) e^{j 2\pi f T_b} df + \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} H(f) N(f) e^{j 2\pi f T_b} df$$

**Step 3:** Signal component: $s_o(T_b) = \int H(f) S(f) e^{j 2\pi f T_b} df$.

**Step 4:** Output noise power: $\overline{n_o^2} = \dfrac{N_0}{2} \int \vert H(f) \vert^2 df$.

**Step 5:** Output SNR:

$$\text{SNR}_o = \frac{\left\vert \int H(f) S(f) e^{j 2\pi f T_b} df \right\vert^2}{\frac{N_0}{2} \int \vert H(f) \vert^2 df}$$

**Step 6:** Apply the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality $\left\vert \int A(f) B^*(f) df \right\vert^2 \le \int \vert A(f) \vert^2 df \cdot \int \vert B(f) \vert^2 df$, with $A = H(f)$ and $B^* = S(f) e^{j 2\pi f T_b}$:

$$\text{SNR}_o \le \frac{\int \vert H(f) \vert^2 df \cdot \int \vert S(f) \vert^2 df}{\frac{N_0}{2} \int \vert H(f) \vert^2 df} = \frac{2 E_s}{N_0}$$

**Step 7:** Equality holds when $H(f) = K S^*(f) e^{-j 2\pi f T_b}$. Taking the inverse Fourier transform:

$$h(t) = K s(T_b - t)$$

This is the **optimal matched filter impulse response**. The maximum SNR is $\text{SNR}_{\max} = 2E_s / N_0$, independent of signal shape. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Probability of Error for Antipodal Signaling:**

**Step 1:** For antipodal signaling, $s_1(t) = s(t)$ and $s_2(t) = -s(t)$ with energy $E_b$ per bit.

**Step 2:** Decision variable: $y = \int_0^{T_b} r(t) s(t) dt$.

**Step 3:** Under $H_1$ (bit 1 sent): $E[y \mid H_1] = E_b$, under $H_0$: $E[y \mid H_0] = -E_b$.

**Step 4:** Noise variance: $\sigma_n^2 = \dfrac{N_0}{2} \int s^2(t) dt = \dfrac{N_0 E_b}{2}$.

**Step 5:** With threshold at zero:

$$P_e = Q\!\left( \frac{E_b}{\sigma_n} \right) = Q\!\left( \frac{E_b}{\sqrt{N_0 E_b / 2}} \right) = Q\!\left( \sqrt{\frac{2 E_b}{N_0}} \right)$$

**Step 6:** For orthogonal signaling, $E_d = 2 E_b$, so:

$$P_e^{\text{ortho}} = Q\!\left( \sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}} \right)$$

**Comparison:** Antipodal signaling is **3 dB better** than orthogonal signaling because the argument of $Q(\cdot)$ is $\sqrt{2}$ times larger. Equivalently, antipodal requires half the power to achieve the same BER. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B-B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2023 / Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the working of a correlation receiver with a neat block diagram. Show mathematically that it is equivalent to a matched filter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A binary communication system transmits bit "1" using $s_1(t) = A$ for $0 \le t \le T_b$ and bit "0" using $s_2(t) = -A$ for $0 \le t \le T_b$. The received signal is corrupted by AWGN with PSD $N_0/2$. Determine: (i) the matched filter impulse response, (ii) the maximum output SNR, and (iii) the probability of error. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Correlation Receiver — Working and Equivalence:**

A correlation receiver consists of a multiplier, an integrator, a sampler, and a threshold comparator. The received signal $r(t)$ is multiplied by a locally generated, time-synchronized replica $s(t)$ of the known pulse. The product is integrated over one bit duration $[0, T_b]$. The integrator output is sampled at $t = T_b$ and compared against a threshold.

**Equivalence Proof:**

The matched filter output at $t = T_b$ is:

$$y_{MF}(T_b) = \int_{0}^{T_b} r(\tau) h(T_b - \tau) d\tau = K \int_{0}^{T_b} r(\tau) s(\tau) d\tau$$

The correlator output is:

$$y_{\text{corr}}(T_b) = \int_{0}^{T_b} r(\tau) s(\tau) d\tau$$

Thus, $y_{MF}(T_b) = K \cdot y_{\text{corr}}(T_b)$. Since $K$ is a constant scaling factor that cancels in the threshold comparison, both receivers make **identical decisions**. Hence they are mathematically equivalent at the sampling instant. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Numerical Problem:**

**Given:** $s_1(t) = A$, $s_2(t) = -A$ for $0 \le t \le T_b$.

**(i) Matched Filter Impulse Response:**

Using $h(t) = s(T_b - t)$ with reference $s_1(t) = A$:

$$h(t) = A, \quad 0 \le t \le T_b$$

(i.e., a rectangular pulse of amplitude $A$ and width $T_b$)

**(ii) Maximum Output SNR:**

Signal energy: $E_b = \int_0^{T_b} A^2 \, dt = A^2 T_b$

$$\text{SNR}_{\max} = \frac{2 E_b}{N_0} = \frac{2 A^2 T_b}{N_0}$$

**(iii) Probability of Error:**

For antipodal signaling with equiprobable bits:

$$P_e = Q\!\left( \sqrt{\frac{2 E_b}{N_0}} \right) = Q\!\left( \sqrt{\frac{2 A^2 T_b}{N_0}} \right) = Q\!\left( A \sqrt{\frac{2 T_b}{N_0}} \right)$$

**Numerical Example:** If $A = 1$ V, $T_b = 1 \mu s$, $N_0 = 10^{-20}$ W/Hz:

$$E_b = 10^{-6} \text{ J}, \quad \text{SNR}_{\max} = 2 \times 10^{14} \text{ (linear)}, \quad P_e \approx 0$$

**(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the time-reversal** in $h(t) = s(T_b - t)$ — Students often write $h(t) = s(t)$, losing 2 marks. The time-reversal $T_b - t$ is essential.
>
> 2. **Missing the linear phase term** $e^{-j 2\pi f T_b}$ in $H(f) = K S^*(f) e^{-j 2\pi f T_b}$ — This delay factor ensures causality in the practical realization. Omitting it is a 1-mark deduction.
>
> 3. **Confusing $E_s$ and $E_b$** — $E_s$ is energy per *symbol*; $E_b$ is energy per *bit*. For binary signaling they are equal, but in M-ary systems $E_s = \log_2 M \cdot E_b$.
>
> 4. **Writing $P_e$ without specifying the signaling format** — $P_e = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ is for *antipodal* only. For orthogonal, it is $P_e = Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$. Mismatch loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 5. **Not stating the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality explicitly** — KTU examiners specifically check whether you *named the mathematical tool*. Writing "by applying the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality..." is mandatory for full marks in derivation questions.
>
> 6. **Ignoring the noise PSD assumption** — A common omission: forgetting to mention that the result is valid for AWGN with PSD $N_0/2$. Always state the assumption.
>
> 7. **Confusing matched filter with correlator implementation details** — In *analog hardware*, the matched filter is a tapped delay line; in *DSP*, the correlator is preferred. Examiners may ask which is more practical in modern systems — answer: **correlator (DSP)**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Matched Filter & Correlation Receiver**

- **Definition (1-liner):** Matched filter is the LTI filter that maximizes output SNR for a known signal in AWGN.
- **Impulse Response:** $h(t) = K \, s(T_b - t)$ — *time-reversed, shifted, scaled* replica of the known signal.
- **Frequency Response:** $H(f) = K \, S^*(f) \, e^{-j 2 \pi f T_b}$ — *conjugate filter* with linear phase delay.
- **Optimality:** Proven via **Cauchy–Schwarz inequality** applied to the SNR expression.
- **Max SNR:** $\text{SNR}_{\max} = 2 E_s / N_0$ — depends only on signal energy, not on shape.
- **Correlation Receiver Output:** $y(T_b) = \displaystyle\int_0^{T_b} r(t) \, s(t) \, dt$ — multiply, integrate, sample, decide.
- **Equivalence:** Matched filter ≡ Correlation receiver at $t = T_b$ (constant scaling difference).
- **Correlator Output = Autocorrelation:** At $t = T_b$, $y(T_b) = R_{rs}(0) = E_s$ when noise-free.
- **General BER Formula:** $P_e = Q\!\left( \sqrt{E_d / (2 N_0)} \right)$, where $E_d = \int (s_1 - s_2)^2 dt$.
- **Antipodal BER (BPSK):** $P_e = Q\!\left( \sqrt{2 E_b / N_0} \right)$ — *3 dB better than orthogonal.*
- **Orthogonal BER (BFSK):** $P_e = Q\!\left( \sqrt{E_b / N_0} \right)$.
- **Whitened Matched Filter:** Used when noise is *colored*; pre-whitening filter $1/H_c(f)$ first.
- **Implementation Preference:** Analog → matched filter; Digital/DSP → correlation receiver.
- **Q-function Property:** $Q(-x) = 1 - Q(x)$; $Q(0) = 0.5$; decays super-exponentially.
- **Key Assumptions:** Signal is *deterministic* and *known*; noise is *AWGN* with PSD $N_0/2$; bits are *equiprobable*.
- **Engineering Applications:** BPSK/QPSK demodulators, radar pulse compression, RAKE receivers in CDMA, OFDM channel estimation, optical coherent detection.

> **Final Note:** The matched filter concept is *the* foundational building block of optimal digital communication receivers. Mastering its derivation via Cauchy–Schwarz is a high-yield KTU exam skill — expect it as a compulsory 7-mark derivation question in the End Semester Examination.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MAP Receiver
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT502 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** MAP Receiver

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

The **MAP (Maximum A Posteriori) Receiver** is an optimal digital communication receiver that minimizes the **average probability of symbol error** by selecting, for each received signal vector **r**, the transmitted symbol **sᵢ** that has the **largest posterior probability** $P(s_i \mid \mathbf{r})$ among all possible transmitted symbols.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Decision Rule of the MAP Receiver**
> 
> $$\text{Decide } \hat{s} = s_i \quad \text{if} \quad P(s_i \mid \mathbf{r}) \geq P(s_j \mid \mathbf{r}) \quad \forall \, j \neq i$$
> 
> This is mathematically equivalent to minimizing the **Bayes Risk** when all error costs are equal.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are a doctor (the receiver) trying to diagnose a disease (the transmitted symbol) based on a set of symptoms (the received signal $\mathbf{r}$).

- A **maximum likelihood (ML)** doctor only looks at how well the symptoms match a disease, ignoring how common the disease is.
- A **MAP** doctor is smarter — they consider both **how well the symptoms fit the disease** AND **how prevalent that disease is in the population**.

> [!NOTE]
> In digital communication, the "prevalence" of a symbol is its **a priori probability** $P(s_i)$. When symbols are **equiprobable** (each equally likely), the MAP receiver **reduces to the ML receiver**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Assumptions

- **Channel Model**: Additive White Gaussian Noise (**AWGN**) channel with noise variance $\sigma^2$ and zero mean.
- **Signal Vector**: The received vector $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{s} + \mathbf{n}$, where $\mathbf{n} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \sigma^2 \mathbf{I})$.
- **Cost of Error**: Uniform cost assignment $C_{ij} = 1$ for $i \neq j$ and $C_{ii} = 0$.
- **A Priori Probabilities**: $P(s_i) \in [0, 1]$ with $\sum_{i} P(s_i) = 1$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Logic: Why MAP is Optimal

The MAP criterion is the formal solution to the **Bayesian decision problem** under equal-error costs. It can be broken into the following logical chain:

1. **Goal**: Find the symbol $s_i$ that minimizes the average probability of error $P_e$.
2. **Bayes' Theorem**: Express the unknown $P(s_i \mid \mathbf{r})$ in terms of known quantities $P(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i)$ and $P(s_i)$.
3. **Denominator is Constant**: Since $P(\mathbf{r})$ is independent of the symbol index, it does not influence the maximization.
4. **Decision Rule**: The optimal decision is the index that maximizes the product $P(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i) \cdot P(s_i)$.

### 2.2 Bayesian Derivation Pathway

- **Likelihood Function**: $f(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i)$ describes the probability density of receiving $\mathbf{r}$ given that $s_i$ was sent.
- **Prior Probability**: $P(s_i)$ encodes the transmitter's symbol statistics.
- **Posterior Probability**: $P(s_i \mid \mathbf{r})$ is what the receiver actually wants — the chance that $s_i$ was sent, given the observation.
- **Evidence**: $f(\mathbf{r}) = \sum_j f(\mathbf{r} \mid s_j) P(s_j)$ acts only as a normalizer.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Interpretation** | **Units** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Bayes' Theorem | $P(s_i \mid \mathbf{r}) = \dfrac{P(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i)\, P(s_i)}{P(\mathbf{r})}$ | Relates posterior to likelihood $\times$ prior | Dimensionless |
| MAP Decision Rule | $\hat{s} = \arg\max_i \, P(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i) P(s_i)$ | Choose the most probable a posteriori symbol | — |
| Likelihood Ratio Test (Binary) | $\Lambda(\mathbf{r}) = \dfrac{f(\mathbf{r} \mid s_1)}{f(\mathbf{r} \mid s_2)} \gtrless \eta = \dfrac{P(s_2)}{P(s_1)}$ | Compares likelihoods to a threshold | Dimensionless |
| AWGN Likelihood (Binary) | $f(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i) = \dfrac{1}{(\pi N_0)^N} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\lVert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i \rVert^2}{N_0}\right)$ | Probability of $\mathbf{r}$ given $s_i$ | $V^{-N}$ |
| Log-Likelihood Form | $\Lambda(\mathbf{r})_{\text{dB}} = \dfrac{1}{N_0}\left(\lVert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_2 \rVert^2 - \lVert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_1 \rVert^2\right) + \ln\dfrac{P(s_2)}{P(s_1)}$ | Monotonic transform of LRT | dB or nats |
| ML Special Case | $\hat{s} = \arg\max_i \, f(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i)$ | MAP $\to$ ML when $P(s_i)$ are equal | — |
| Average Error Probability | $P_e = \sum_{i} \sum_{j \neq i} P(s_i)\, P(\text{decide } s_j \mid s_i)\, C_{ij}$ | Performance metric | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU 2024 Examinations**: Always write the MAP rule as a *monotonic* transformation of the log-likelihood when signals are corrupted by AWGN. Examiners consistently reward the **log-domain manipulation** of the Gaussian density.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Wireless Cellular Systems (5G NR)**: Used in initial synchronization where pilot symbols have known but non-uniform priors.
- **Optical Fiber Receivers**: Adaptive thresholding uses MAP logic for non-uniform QAM symbol priors introduced by constellation shaping.
- **Radar and Sonar Detection**: Track-before-detect pipelines use MAP to incorporate target motion priors.
- **Machine-Type Communications (mMTC)**: Massive IoT devices transmit sporadically — non-uniform priors are natural and MAP is essential.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the MAP Decision Rule from First Principles

We start with the **average probability of error** for a $M$-ary communication system.

**Step 1 — Define the total probability of error.**

For a decision rule that maps the observation space $\mathbb{R}^N$ into $M$ regions $\{R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_M\}$, the average probability of error is

$$
P_e = \sum_{i=1}^{M} P(s_i) \int_{\mathbf{r} \in R_j, \, j \neq i} f(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i) \, d\mathbf{r}
$$

**Step 2 — Rewrite as 1 minus probability of correct decision.**

Since the probability of being correct is $P_c = \sum_i P(s_i) \int_{R_i} f(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i) d\mathbf{r}$, we have $P_e = 1 - P_c$. Maximizing $P_c$ is equivalent to minimizing $P_e$.

**Step 3 — Maximize the integrand pointwise.**

For each observation point $\mathbf{r}$, we can maximize the integrand of $P_c$ by choosing the region assignment that makes the largest contribution. This is justified by the **non-negativity** of both $P(s_i)$ and $f(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i)$.

**Step 4 — State the pointwise MAP rule.**

For every $\mathbf{r}$, assign it to the region $R_k$ where

$$
P(s_k) f(\mathbf{r} \mid s_k) \geq P(s_i) f(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i) \quad \forall \, i
$$

**Step 5 — Eliminate the constant denominator.**

Using Bayes' theorem,

$$
P(s_i \mid \mathbf{r}) = \frac{P(s_i) f(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i)}{f(\mathbf{r})}
$$

Because $f(\mathbf{r}) > 0$ is a constant for a given observation, maximizing $P(s_i \mid \mathbf{r})$ is identical to maximizing the numerator $P(s_i) f(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i)$. This yields the canonical MAP rule.

### 3.2 Derivation for AWGN with Binary Signaling

Consider $M = 2$ with transmitted signals $s_1 = +A$ and $s_2 = -A$ over a single observation $r = s + n$ where $n \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$.

**Step 1 — Write the conditional PDFs.**

$$
f(r \mid s_1) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(r - A)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

$$
f(r \mid s_2) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(r + A)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Form the likelihood ratio.**

$$
\Lambda(r) = \frac{f(r \mid s_1)}{f(r \mid s_2)} = \exp\!\left(\frac{(r + A)^2 - (r - A)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the exponent.**

$$
(r + A)^2 - (r - A)^2 = (r^2 + 2rA + A^2) - (r^2 - 2rA + A^2) = 4rA
$$

So,

$$
\Lambda(r) = \exp\!\left(\frac{4rA}{2\sigma^2}\right) = \exp\!\left(\frac{2rA}{\sigma^2}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the MAP threshold.**

The MAP rule becomes

$$
\Lambda(r) \gtrless \frac{P(s_2)}{P(s_1)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \exp\!\left(\frac{2rA}{\sigma^2}\right) \gtrless \frac{P(s_2)}{P(s_1)}
$$

**Step 5 — Take the natural logarithm (monotonic transform).**

$$
\frac{2rA}{\sigma^2} \gtrless \ln\!\left(\frac{P(s_2)}{P(s_1)}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the decision threshold $r_T$.**

$$
r_T = \frac{\sigma^2}{2A} \ln\!\left(\frac{P(s_2)}{P(s_1)}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** When $P(s_1) = P(s_2) = 0.5$, the threshold collapses to $r_T = 0$, recovering the classical **ML (Maximum Likelihood)** detector. When $P(s_1) > P(s_2)$, the threshold shifts **toward the mean of $s_1$**, biasing decisions in favor of the more likely symbol.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Probability of Error for Binary MAP

The probability of error is

$$
P_e = P(s_1) P(\text{decide } s_2 \mid s_1) + P(s_2) P(\text{decide } s_1 \mid s_2)
$$

**Step 1 — Express the conditional error probabilities.**

$$
P(\text{decide } s_2 \mid s_1) = \int_{-\infty}^{r_T} f(r \mid s_1) \, dr = Q\!\left(\frac{A - r_T}{\sigma}\right)
$$

$$
P(\text{decide } s_1 \mid s_2) = \int_{r_T}^{\infty} f(r \mid s_2) \, dr = Q\!\left(\frac{A + r_T}{\sigma}\right)
$$

Here, $Q(\cdot)$ is the Gaussian Q-function: $Q(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_x^{\infty} e^{-t^2/2} dt$.

**Step 2 — Substitute the threshold expression.**

Using $r_T = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{2A} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P(s_2)}{P(s_1)}\right)$,

$$
P_e = P(s_1) \, Q\!\left(\frac{A}{\sigma} - \frac{\sigma}{2A} \ln\!\frac{P(s_2)}{P(s_1)}\right) + P(s_2) \, Q\!\left(\frac{A}{\sigma} + \frac{\sigma}{2A} \ln\!\frac{P(s_2)}{P(s_1)}\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Minimum $P_e$ Property:** This expression is the *absolute minimum* achievable average error probability under the given priors and AWGN. No other deterministic receiver can do better — this is the **Cramér-Rao lower bound**-like optimality of MAP.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a MAP Receiver

The diagram below captures the canonical signal-flow architecture of a digital MAP receiver. Each block represents a functional transformation rather than a specific circuit implementation, which is appropriate for the system-level perspective required by KTU 2024.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Received Vector r] --> B[Correlator Bank or Matched Filter Bank]
    B --> C[Compute Likelihoods f of r given s sub i]
    C --> D[Multiply by Priors P sub s sub i]
    D --> E[Take Log Likelihood ln of f times P]
    E --> F[Comparator Stage Maximum Selector]
    F --> G[Decision Output hat s]
    P[Channel Statistics Source P sub s sub i and noise variance sigma squared] --> C
    P --> D
    H[Threshold Reference Generator r sub T] --> F
```

### 4.2 Detailed Component-Level MAP Receiver Pipeline

The following flowchart expands the comparator logic and decision regions to make the **likelihood-ratio thresholding** explicit — a key conceptual step frequently tested in KTU examinations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Channel Observation r] --> B[Compute LRT Numerator L1 equals f r given s1]
    A --> C[Compute LRT Denominator L2 equals f r given s2]
    P1[P of s1] --> D[Form Product L1 times P of s1]
    P2[P of s2] --> E[Form Product L2 times P of s2]
    B --> D
    C --> E
    D --> F{Compare L1 P1 with L2 P2}
    E --> F
    F -->|Larger| G[Decide s1]
    F -->|Larger| H[Decide s2]
    G --> I[Symbol Output hat s]
    H --> I
    J[Noise Variance sigma squared from Channel Estimator] --> B
    J --> C
```

### 4.3 Decision Region Geometry in 2-D Signal Space

For a binary constellation $\mathbf{s}_1 = (+A, 0)$ and $\mathbf{s}_2 = (-A, 0)$, the MAP boundary is a **perpendicular bisector shifted by the log-prior ratio**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DS[Binary Signal Space]
        s1[Node A equals plus A and zero] --- s2[Node B equals minus A and zero]
        rT[Decision Boundary r sub T shifted by log prior ratio] -.-> s1
        rT -.-> s2
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The shifted boundary visually explains why the **MAP** rule outperforms the **ML** rule under non-uniform priors. The boundary tilts toward the less-likely symbol, expanding the decision region of the more-likely symbol and lowering the overall $P_e$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State and explain the MAP decision rule for a digital communication receiver.**

**Model Answer:**

The MAP (Maximum A Posteriori) decision rule selects the transmitted symbol $s_i$ that maximizes the posterior probability $P(s_i \mid \mathbf{r})$ for a given received vector $\mathbf{r}$. The formal rule is

$$
\hat{s} = \arg\max_{i} \, P(s_i \mid \mathbf{r})
$$

Using Bayes' theorem and discarding the constant denominator $f(\mathbf{r})$, this simplifies to

$$
\hat{s} = \arg\max_{i} \, P(s_i) \, f(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i)
$$

When the symbol priors are equiprobable, the rule degenerates into the **Maximum Likelihood (ML)** rule. The MAP receiver achieves the **minimum average probability of error** under uniform error costs.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between MAP and ML receivers. When do they become identical?**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **MAP Receiver** | **ML Receiver** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Selection Criterion | Maximizes $P(s_i \mid \mathbf{r})$ | Maximizes $f(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i)$ |
| Uses Priors | Yes — $P(s_i)$ | No |
| Performance | Minimum $P_e$ (true optimum) | Optimal only for equiprobable symbols |
| Decision Rule | $P(s_i) f(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i)$ | $f(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i)$ |

The two receivers become **mathematically identical** when $P(s_i) = 1/M$ for all $i$ (uniform priors), since the constant prior factor can be dropped from the maximization.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the MAP decision rule for a binary communication system transmitting symbols $s_1$ and $s_2$ with prior probabilities $P(s_1)$ and $P(s_2)$ over an AWGN channel. Show the final decision threshold explicitly.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a binary system with $s_1 = +1$ V, $s_2 = -1$ V, noise variance $\sigma^2 = 0.5$ V$^2$, and $P(s_1) = 0.7$, $P(s_2) = 0.3$, find the decision threshold and the minimum probability of error.

##### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Step 1 — State the rule.**

The MAP rule is

$$
P(s_1) f(r \mid s_1) \gtrless P(s_2) f(r \mid s_2)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Gaussian densities.**

$$
P(s_1) \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(r - s_1)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) \gtrless P(s_2) \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(r - s_2)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Cancel the common normalizer.**

$$
P(s_1) \exp\!\left(-\frac{(r - s_1)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) \gtrless P(s_2) \exp\!\left(-\frac{(r - s_2)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Take the natural logarithm.**

$$
\ln P(s_1) - \frac{(r - s_1)^2}{2\sigma^2} \gtrless \ln P(s_2) - \frac{(r - s_2)^2}{2\sigma^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to isolate $r$.**

$$
\frac{(r - s_2)^2 - (r - s_1)^2}{2\sigma^2} \gtrless \ln\!\frac{P(s_2)}{P(s_1)}
$$

**Step 6 — Expand the squares.**

$$
\frac{r^2 - 2rs_2 + s_2^2 - r^2 + 2rs_1 - s_1^2}{2\sigma^2} \gtrless \ln\!\frac{P(s_2)}{P(s_1)}
$$

$$
\frac{2r(s_1 - s_2) + (s_2^2 - s_1^2)}{2\sigma^2} \gtrless \ln\!\frac{P(s_2)}{P(s_1)}
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for the threshold.**

$$
r_T = \frac{s_1 + s_2}{2} + \frac{\sigma^2}{s_1 - s_2} \ln\!\frac{P(s_2)}{P(s_1)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric Meaning of $r_T$:** The first term is the **midpoint** between $s_1$ and $s_2$ (which is the ML threshold), and the second term is the **prior-bias shift** that moves the boundary toward the less-likely symbol.

##### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.**

$s_1 = +1$, $s_2 = -1$, $\sigma^2 = 0.5$, $P(s_1) = 0.7$, $P(s_2) = 0.3$.

**Step 2 — Compute the midpoint term.**

$$
\frac{s_1 + s_2}{2} = \frac{1 + (-1)}{2} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the prior-bias term.**

$$
\frac{\sigma^2}{s_1 - s_2} \ln\!\frac{P(s_2)}{P(s_1)} = \frac{0.5}{1 - (-1)} \ln\!\frac{0.3}{0.7} = \frac{0.5}{2} \times (-0.8473) = 0.25 \times (-0.8473) = -0.2118
$$

**Step 4 — Threshold.**

$$
r_T = 0 + (-0.2118) = -0.2118 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the error probability.**

The effective signal amplitude relative to noise is

$$
\frac{A}{\sigma} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.5}} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.4142
$$

Using the expression

$$
P_e = P(s_1) Q\!\left(\frac{A - r_T}{\sigma}\right) + P(s_2) Q\!\left(\frac{A + r_T}{\sigma}\right)
$$

**Compute argument 1:**

$$
\frac{A - r_T}{\sigma} = \frac{1 - (-0.2118)}{\sqrt{0.5}} = \frac{1.2118}{0.7071} = 1.714
$$

**Compute argument 2:**

$$
\frac{A + r_T}{\sigma} = \frac{1 + (-0.2118)}{\sqrt{0.5}} = \frac{0.7882}{0.7071} = 1.115
$$

**Step 6 — Look up Q-values.**

$$
Q(1.714) \approx 0.0432, \quad Q(1.115) \approx 0.1325
$$

**Step 7 — Final calculation.**

$$
P_e = 0.7 \times 0.0432 + 0.3 \times 0.1325 = 0.03024 + 0.03975 = 0.0700
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The threshold shifted **toward the less-likely symbol** ($s_2 = -1$ V), expanding the decision region for $s_1$. The resulting $P_e \approx 0.07$ is lower than the ML receiver (which would give $P_e = Q(\sqrt{2}) \approx 0.0786$).

---

#### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of likelihood function in MAP detection. Derive the likelihood ratio test (LRT) for a binary hypothesis testing problem.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For an $M$-ary MAP receiver with equal priors operating over AWGN with noise PSD $N_0/2$, derive an expression for the minimum average probability of error in terms of the Euclidean distances between the received vector and the candidate signal vectors.

##### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Step 1 — Define the likelihood function.**

The likelihood function $f(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i)$ is the conditional probability density of the received vector $\mathbf{r}$ given that symbol $s_i$ was transmitted. For binary hypothesis testing, we have two competing hypotheses $H_1: s = s_1$ and $H_2: s = s_2$ with likelihoods $f(\mathbf{r} \mid H_1)$ and $f(\mathbf{r} \mid H_2)$.

**Step 2 — Define the likelihood ratio.**

$$
\Lambda(\mathbf{r}) = \frac{f(\mathbf{r} \mid H_1)}{f(\mathbf{r} \mid H_2)}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Bayes' rule to the MAP rule.**

$$
P(H_1) f(\mathbf{r} \mid H_1) \gtrless P(H_2) f(\mathbf{r} \mid H_2)
$$

Dividing both sides by $f(\mathbf{r} \mid H_2)$ and $P(H_2)$,

$$
\Lambda(\mathbf{r}) \gtrless \eta = \frac{P(H_2)}{P(H_1)}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision logic.**

- If $\Lambda(\mathbf{r}) > \eta$: decide $H_1$.
- If $\Lambda(\mathbf{r}) < \eta$: decide $H_2$.

**Step 5 — Monotonic transformation to log-domain.**

Taking natural logarithm (which is monotonic increasing),

$$
\ln \Lambda(\mathbf{r}) \gtrless \ln \eta
$$

This is the **log-likelihood ratio test (LLRT)** and is the practical form used in digital hardware implementations.

##### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1 — Equal priors assumption.**

When $P(s_i) = 1/M$ for all $i$, the MAP rule reduces to the **ML rule**:

$$
\hat{s} = \arg\max_{i} f(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i)
$$

**Step 2 — AWGN likelihood for vector observations.**

For $N$-dimensional observations with noise covariance $N_0/2 \cdot \mathbf{I}$,

$$
f(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i) = \frac{1}{(\pi N_0)^N} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\lVert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i \rVert^2}{N_0}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Maximize the likelihood.**

Maximizing the exponential is equivalent to minimizing the squared Euclidean distance $\lVert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i \rVert^2$. Hence,

$$
\hat{s} = \arg\min_{i} \lVert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i \rVert^2
$$

**Step 4 — Probability of error via pairwise distance.**

The total error probability is dominated by the **minimum-distance neighbors**. For equiprobable, equipowered signals, the union bound gives

$$
P_e \leq \sum_{i} \sum_{j \neq i} P(s_i) Q\!\left(\frac{d_{ij}}{\sqrt{2N_0}}\right)
$$

where $d_{ij} = \lVert \mathbf{s}_i - \mathbf{s}_j \rVert$ is the Euclidean distance between signal points $i$ and $j$.

**Step 5 — Asymptotic form.**

For high SNR, the term with the **smallest** $d_{ij}$ dominates:

$$
P_e \approx c \cdot Q\!\left(\frac{d_{\min}}{\sqrt{2N_0}}\right)
$$

where $c$ is a constellation-dependent constant counting the number of nearest neighbors and $d_{\min}$ is the **minimum Euclidean distance** of the constellation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes**
> 1. **Forgetting to drop $P(\mathbf{r})$**: Many students incorrectly keep the denominator of Bayes' theorem in the maximization. Always state explicitly that $P(\mathbf{r})$ is independent of the symbol index.
> 2. **Mixing up MAP and ML decision rules**: A frequent error is writing $f(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i) P(s_i)$ as MAP and $f(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i)$ as ML, but then evaluating the threshold as if both are the same. Clearly state when MAP $\to$ ML (uniform priors).
> 3. **Sign errors in the threshold derivation**: Watch the algebra in the expansion of $(r - s_1)^2 - (r - s_2)^2$. Examiners frequently deduct **1–2 marks** for sign slips.
> 4. **Confusing $N_0$ with $\sigma^2$**: In AWGN, $\sigma^2 = N_0/2$ per dimension. State the convention explicitly at the start of the solution.
> 5. **Forgetting to specify "minimum"**: The phrase *"MAP minimizes the average probability of error"* must accompany the rule; otherwise the optimality claim is incomplete.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **MAP receiver minimizes the average probability of error** $P_e$ under equal-cost Bayesian decision theory.
- Decision rule: $\hat{s} = \arg\max_{i} P(s_i) f(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i)$ — choose the symbol maximizing **prior $\times$ likelihood**.
- For **equiprobable** symbols, MAP $\equiv$ ML: $\hat{s} = \arg\max_{i} f(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i)$.
- For **AWGN** with vector observations, the rule becomes $\hat{s} = \arg\min_{i} \lVert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i \rVert^2$ (the **minimum-distance detector**).
- For **binary** signaling, the **decision threshold** is

$$
r_T = \frac{s_1 + s_2}{2} + \frac{\sigma^2}{s_1 - s_2} \ln\!\frac{P(s_2)}{P(s_1)}
$$

- The threshold **shifts toward the less-likely symbol**, enlarging the decision region of the more-likely symbol and lowering $P_e$.
- The **Likelihood Ratio Test (LRT)** $\Lambda(\mathbf{r}) \gtrless P(s_2)/P(s_1)$ is the binary MAP rule in ratio form; the **Log-LRT** is the implementation-friendly variant.
- The MAP receiver is **coherent** — it requires knowledge of the channel's noise variance $\sigma^2$ and the symbol priors.
- The probability of error for binary MAP over AWGN is

$$
P_e = P(s_1) Q\!\left(\frac{A - r_T}{\sigma}\right) + P(s_2) Q\!\left(\frac{A + r_T}{\sigma}\right)
$$

- **High-SNR asymptotic** error performance: $P_e \sim c \cdot Q(d_{\min}/\sqrt{2N_0})$ — the minimum Euclidean distance $d_{\min}$ governs the error floor.
- The MAP framework generalizes to **vector signals, M-ary constellations, fading channels, and MIMO systems** — always re-derive the likelihood under the new channel model.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maximum Likelihood Receiver
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT502 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Maximum Likelihood Receiver # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) The **Maximum Likelihood (ML) Receiver** is an optimal detection strategy employed in digital communication systems corrupted by **Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN)**. It decides which of the $M$ possible transmitted symbols $s_1, s_2, \dots, s_M$ was sent by selecting the hypothesis $H_i$ that **maximizes the likelihood function** $p(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i)$, i.e., the conditional probability density of receiving observation vector $\mathbf{r}$ given that symbol $s_i$ was transmitted. Mathematically, the ML decision rule is expressed as: $$\hat{s} = \arg\max_{s_i \in \{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_M\}} \; p(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition:** The ML receiver is the optimal detector in the sense of **minimizing the probability of error** when all transmitted symbols are *equally likely a priori*. Under equiprobable signaling, the ML rule becomes mathematically identical to the **Minimum Distance Receiver** in signal space geometry. > [!NOTE] > **Why "Likelihood"?** The function $p(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i)$ is called the *likelihood* of hypothesis $H_i$ — it tells us "how plausible is it that the received waveform $\mathbf{r}$ was generated by symbol $s_i$?" The ML rule picks the *most plausible* cause. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine a **forensic doctor** at a crime scene who finds a muddy boot print at the doorway. The doctor has 3 suspects, $A$, $B$, and $C$, each known to wear a distinct shoe size. The boot print is of size **42**. Now, the doctor calculates: - "How likely is it that suspect $A$ (size 41) made this print?" → low likelihood - "How likely is it that suspect $B$ (size 42) made this print?" → high likelihood - "How likely is it that suspect $C$ (size 43) made this print?" → low likelihood The doctor concludes: **"It was most likely $B$."** This is *Maximum Likelihood estimation* — picking the cause that makes the observation *most probable*. In communication: - The **boot print** ↔ the **received noisy signal** $\mathbf{r}$ - The **suspects** ↔ the **transmitted symbols** $s_1, s_2, \dots, s_M$ - The **noise** ↔ the uncertainty in measurement (fog, distortion, etc.) The ML receiver is the digital communication system's "forensic doctor," picking the symbol whose presence best explains the noisy observation. --- ## 1.3 Physical & Statistical Constants - **Noise Power Spectral Density:** $N_0 / 2$ (in W/Hz, two-sided) - **Standard Gaussian Q-function:** $Q(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{x}^{\infty} e^{-u^2/2} \, du$ - **Signal energy per bit:** $E_b$ (in Joules) - **Symbol energy:** $E_s = E_b \log_2 M$ for $M$-ary signaling > [!TIP] > For the KTU 2024 exam, remember the canonical constants: $\sigma^2 = N_0/2$ (AWGN variance per dimension) and the identity $Q(-x) = 1 - Q(x)$. These appear in **every** probability-of-error question. --- ## 1.4 Relation to MAP and Bayes' Framework | Detector | Decision Rule | When Optimal | |---------|---------------|--------------| | **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)** | $\max_i \; P(s_i \mid \mathbf{r})$ | Always (minimizes $P_e$) | | **Maximum Likelihood (ML)** | $\max_i \; p(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i)$ | When $P(s_i)$ are equal | | **Minimum Distance (MD)** | $\min_i \; \Vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i \Vert^2$ | When $P(s_i)$ are equal + AWGN | Using Bayes' theorem: $$P(s_i \mid \mathbf{r}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i) \, P(s_i)}{p(\mathbf{r})}$$ Since $p(\mathbf{r})$ is a constant independent of $i$, **for equal priors** $P(s_i) = 1/M$, the rule that maximizes $P(s_i \mid \mathbf{r})$ also maximizes $p(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i)$. Thus **ML $\equiv$ MAP** under equiprobable signaling. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Principle — Structured Logic The ML receiver operates in five logical stages: 1. **Observation Formation:** The received waveform $r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t)$ is sampled and projected onto an $N$-dimensional orthonormal basis $\{\phi_1(t), \dots, \phi_N(t)\}$ to form the observation vector $\mathbf{r} = [r_1, r_2, \dots, r_N]^T$. 2. **Signal Vector Construction:** Each candidate symbol $s_i(t)$ is similarly projected to give the *reference* signal vector $\mathbf{s}_i = [s_{i1}, s_{i2}, \dots, s_{iN}]^T$. 3. **Likelihood Evaluation:** For each $i \in \{1, \dots, M\}$, compute the AWGN likelihood: $$p(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i) = \frac{1}{(\pi N_0)^{N/2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\|\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i\|^2}{N_0}\right)$$ 4. **Comparison:** The receiver picks the index $i^*$ such that $p(\mathbf{r} \mid s_{i^*}) \geq p(\mathbf{r} \mid s_j)$ for all $j$. 5. **Decision Output:** The chosen symbol is declared as the transmitted one. --- ## 2.2 Why It Works — The Core "Why" and "How" - **Why Gaussian?** AWGN is modeled as a zero-mean Gaussian process with PSD $N_0/2$. Joint pdf of $N$ independent samples is the product of univariate Gaussians — yielding the exponential form above. - **Why maximize the exponent?** The monotonically decreasing nature of the negative exponential means *maximizing the likelihood = minimizing the Euclidean distance* $\|\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i\|$. - **How is it implemented?** Via a bank of **correlators** (or equivalently, **matched filters**) followed by a comparator — the classical *correlation receiver* architecture. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---------|---------|-------| | 1 | $\hat{s} = \arg\max_i \; p(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i)$ | ML decision rule | — | | 2 | $p(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i) = \dfrac{1}{(\pi N_0)^{N/2}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\|\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i\|^2}{N_0}\right)$ | AWGN likelihood | — | | 3 | $D = \mathbf{r} \cdot (\mathbf{s}_1 - \mathbf{s}_2)$ | Binary decision variable (correlation) | V·s | | 4 | Decide $s_1$ if $D > \gamma$, else $s_2$ | Threshold test | — | | 5 | $\gamma = \dfrac{\|\mathbf{s}_2\|^2 - \|\mathbf{s}_1\|^2}{2}$ | ML threshold (equal priors) | V·s | | 6 | $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{d_{12}^2}{2N_0}}\right)$ | Binary error probability | dimensionless | | 7 | $d_{12}^2 = \|\mathbf{s}_1 - \mathbf{s}_2\|^2 = E_1 + E_2 - 2\langle \mathbf{s}_1, \mathbf{s}_2 \rangle$ | Squared Euclidean distance | $\text{J}\cdot\Omega$ | | 8 | Antipodal: $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | BPSK / antipodal error | dimensionless | | 9 | Orthogonal: $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | BFSK / orthogonal error | dimensionless | | 10 | $Q(x) = \dfrac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$ | Q-to-erfc conversion | — | | 11 | $\langle \mathbf{s}_i, \mathbf{s}_j \rangle = \int_0^T s_i(t) s_j(t) \, dt$ | Inner product in function space | $\text{J}\cdot\Omega$ | > [!NOTE] > **Table Note on Pipes:** All absolute-value bars and norms in the table above use LaTeX `\Vert` and `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax — KTU valuation scripts strictly check rendering. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **5G NR & LTE baseband chipsets** use ML detection variants (e.g., **MIMO ML, sphere decoding**) for spatial multiplexing. - **Optical fiber receivers** at 100 Gbps employ ML-based equalization to combat intersymbol interference. - **Deep-space communication (NASA DSN):** ML decoding of convolutional/LDPC codes is at the heart of error correction in Voyager, Perseverance rover links. - **GNSS (GPS/Galileo):** Spread-spectrum ML receivers track satellite signals buried under thermal noise. - **Biomedical telemetry:** ML detection recovers low-amplitude neural/muscle signals from EMG/EEG recordings. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From ML Rule to Minimum Distance Receiver **Starting Premise:** Received vector under AWGN, $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{s}_i + \mathbf{n}$, where $\mathbf{n} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \tfrac{N_0}{2} \mathbf{I}_N)$. **Step 1 — Likelihood Function (multivariate Gaussian).** The joint pdf of $\mathbf{r}$ conditioned on $s_i$ is the product of $N$ i.i.d. Gaussians: $$p(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i) = \prod_{k=1}^{N} \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi N_0}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(r_k - s_{ik})^2}{N_0}\right)$$ **Step 2 — Combine the product into a single exponential.** $$p(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i) = \frac{1}{(\pi N_0)^{N/2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{N_0} \sum_{k=1}^{N}(r_k - s_{ik})^2\right)$$ **Step 3 — Recognize the squared Euclidean distance.** The summation is exactly $\|\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i\|^2$. So: $$p(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i) = \frac{1}{(\pi N_0)^{N/2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\|\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i\|^2}{N_0}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Apply the monotonicity of $\exp(\cdot)$.** The prefactor $1/(\pi N_0)^{N/2}$ does not depend on $i$. Since the negative exponential is *strictly monotonically decreasing* in its argument, maximizing $p(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i)$ is equivalent to **minimizing** $\|\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i\|^2$. $$\hat{s} = \arg\max_i \; p(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i) = \arg\min_i \; \|\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i\|^2$$ This is the celebrated **Minimum Distance Receiver** (also called the *Minimum Energy Detector*). --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Binary ML Decision Rule with Threshold For binary signaling ($M=2$, symbols $s_1, s_2$ with equal probability), the ML decision becomes: $$\text{Decide } s_1 \iff \|\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_1\|^2 \leq \|\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_2\|^2$$ **Step 1 — Expand both squared distances.** $$\|\mathbf{r}\|^2 - 2\mathbf{r}\cdot\mathbf{s}_1 + \|\mathbf{s}_1\|^2 \leq \|\mathbf{r}\|^2 - 2\mathbf{r}\cdot\mathbf{s}_2 + \|\mathbf{s}_2\|^2$$ **Step 2 — Cancel $\|\mathbf{r}\|^2$ on both sides.** $$- 2\mathbf{r}\cdot\mathbf{s}_1 + \|\mathbf{s}_1\|^2 \leq - 2\mathbf{r}\cdot\mathbf{s}_2 + \|\mathbf{s}_2\|^2$$ **Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate the decision variable.** Define the decision variable $D$ and threshold $\gamma$: $$D = \mathbf{r}\cdot(\mathbf{s}_1 - \mathbf{s}_2), \qquad \gamma = \frac{\|\mathbf{s}_2\|^2 - \|\mathbf{s}_1\|^2}{2}$$ **Step 4 — Final test.** $$\text{Decide } s_1 \iff D > \gamma$$ For **equi-energy** signals ($\|\mathbf{s}_1\| = \|\mathbf{s}_2\|$), the threshold $\gamma = 0$ — the receiver simply correlates with the difference of the two reference signals. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Probability of Error for Binary Antipodal Signaling **Setup:** $s_1(t) = +g(t)$ and $s_2(t) = -g(t)$ with $E_b = \int_0^T g^2(t)\,dt$, equal priors. **Step 1 — Decision variable under hypothesis $H_1$.** Correlate $r(t) = g(t) + n(t)$ with $g(t)$: $$D \mid H_1 = \int_0^T [g(t) + n(t)] \, g(t)\, dt = E_b + \int_0^T n(t) g(t)\, dt$$ **Step 2 — Identify the noise component.** The integral $\int_0^T n(t) g(t)\,dt$ is Gaussian with mean 0 and variance: $$\text{Var}\!\left[\int_0^T n(t) g(t)\,dt\right] = \frac{N_0}{2} \int_0^T g^2(t)\,dt = \frac{N_0 E_b}{2}$$ **Step 3 — Decision variable distribution.** $$D \mid H_1 \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(E_b, \tfrac{N_0 E_b}{2}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Probability of error.** An error occurs when $D < 0$ under $H_1$: $$P_e = P(D < 0 \mid H_1) = P\!\left(\mathcal{N}\!\left(E_b, \tfrac{N_0 E_b}{2}\right) < 0\right)$$ **Step 5 — Standardize the Gaussian variable.** Let $Z = \mathcal{N}(0,1)$: $$P_e = P\!\left(Z < \frac{0 - E_b}{\sqrt{N_0 E_b/2}}\right) = P\!\left(Z < -\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$ **Step 6 — Apply the Q-function identity $Q(-x) = 1 - Q(x)$ and symmetry $P(Z<-x) = Q(x)$.** $$\boxed{\,P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)\,}$$ This is the **BPSK error probability** — the benchmark against which all other binary schemes are compared. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Probability of Error for Binary Orthogonal Signaling **Setup:** $s_1(t) = \sqrt{E_b}\,\phi_1(t)$ and $s_2(t) = \sqrt{E_b}\,\phi_2(t)$ where $\int_0^T \phi_i(t)\phi_j(t)\,dt = \delta_{ij}$. **Step 1 — Under $H_1$, correlate with both basis functions:** $$D_1 = \int_0^T r(t) \phi_1(t) dt = \sqrt{E_b} + n_1, \qquad D_2 = \int_0^T r(t) \phi_2(t) dt = n_2$$ with $n_1, n_2 \sim \mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2)$, independent. **Step 2 — Decision:** $H_1$ if $D_1 - D_2 > 0$. **Step 3 — Distribution of $D_1 - D_2 \mid H_1$:** mean $= \sqrt{E_b}$, variance $= N_0$. **Step 4 — Standardize and apply Q-function.** $$P_e = Q\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{E_b}}{\sqrt{N_0}}\right) = \boxed{\,Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)\,}$$ **3 dB Penalty:** Orthogonal signaling (BFSK) requires **twice the energy** of antipodal (BPSK) for the same error rate — a fundamental KTU result. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Signaling | $d_{12}^2$ | $P_e$ | $E_b/N_0$ for $P_e=10^{-5}$ | |-----------|-----------|-------|-----------------------------| | **BPSK (antipodal)** | $4E_b$ | $Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)$ | **9.6 dB** | | **BFSK (orthogonal)** | $2E_b$ | $Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_b/N_0}\right)$ | **12.6 dB** | | **BFSK (coherent)** | $2E_b$ | $Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_b/N_0}\right)$ | 12.6 dB | | **DPSK (differentially coherent)** | $2E_b(1-\rho)$ | $\tfrac{1}{2}e^{-E_b/N_0}$ | 11.2 dB (asymptotic) | > [!TIP] > For KTU derivations, always remember: **doubling $d_{12}^2$ halves the required $E_b/N_0$** (3 dB gain). This is why BPSK outperforms BFSK by 3 dB at any given $P_e$. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — ML Receiver Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Received Signal r of t"] --> B["Matched Filter Bank
or Correlator Bank"] B --> C1["Correlator 1 output r1"] B --> C2["Correlator 2 output r2"] B --> C3["Correlator N output rN"] C1 --> D["Vector Formulation
r = s sub i plus n"] C2 --> D C3 --> D D --> E["Likelihood Engine
p of r given s sub i"] E --> F["Comparator / Maximum Selector"] F --> G["Decision Logic
arg max p of r given s sub i"] G --> H["Detected Symbol s hat"] H --> I["Output to Symbol Decoder"] I --> J["Bit Stream Output"] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Decision Flow — Binary ML Detection ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Start: Receive r of t over 0 T"] --> B["Project r on phi 1 and phi 2
Compute r1 and r2"] B --> C["Form Decision Variable
D equals r1 minus r2"] C --> D{"D greater than
threshold gamma?"} D -- "Yes" --> E["Declare s1 transmitted"] D -- "No" --> F["Declare s2 transmitted"] E --> G["Output Symbol s hat"] F --> G ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Signal Space Geometry ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SignalSpace ["2D Signal Space with Noise Cloud"] P1["s1
Reference 1"] P2["s2
Reference 2"] P3["r
Received Vector"] P1 -.- P2 P3 -.-|"d1 = distance to s1"| P1 P3 -.-|"d2 = distance to s2"| P2 end MID["Perpendicular Bisector
Decision Boundary"] -.-> P1 MID -.-> P2 P3 --> DEC{"d1 less than d2?"} DEC -- "Yes" --> OUT1["Choose s1"] DEC -- "No" --> OUT2["Choose s2"] ``` ## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Correlation Receiver (textual schematic) | Block | Function | KTU Notation | |-------|----------|--------------| | **Input Matched Filter** | Maximizes SNR at sampling instant | $h(t) = s(T - t)$ | | **Sampler** | Samples at $t = T$ | $r_k = \int_0^T r(t)\phi_k(t) dt$ | | **Reference Bank** | Stores all $M$ symbol templates | $\{\mathbf{s}_1, \dots, \mathbf{s}_M\}$ | | **Distance Computer** | Computes $\|\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i\|^2$ | Per-symbol metric | | **ML Decision** | Arg-min over $i$ | $\hat{s} = \arg\min_i \|\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i\|^2$ | | **Output** | Recovered symbol → bits | $\hat{s} \to$ bitstream | # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Define the Maximum Likelihood (ML) receiver. State the conditions under which the ML receiver is optimal.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1 mark)** *Definition:* The ML receiver decides in favor of the symbol $s_i$ that maximizes the likelihood function $p(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i)$, i.e., the conditional probability density of the received vector $\mathbf{r}$ given $s_i$ was transmitted. - **(1 mark)** *Decision rule:* $\hat{s} = \arg\max_i \; p(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i)$. - **(1 mark)** *Optimality condition:* The ML receiver is optimal (minimizes the probability of error) when **all transmitted symbols are equally likely a priori**, i.e., $P(s_i) = 1/M$ for all $i$. Under this condition, the ML rule becomes equivalent to the MAP rule. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **Show that for AWGN channels with equiprobable signals, the ML receiver reduces to a minimum distance receiver.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1 mark)** Likelihood under AWGN: $$p(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i) = \frac{1}{(\pi N_0)^{N/2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\|\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i\|^2}{N_0}\right)$$ - **(1 mark)** Since the prefactor is independent of $i$ and $\exp(\cdot)$ is monotonically decreasing, maximizing $p(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i) \iff$ minimizing $\|\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i\|^2$. - **(1 mark)** Therefore $\hat{s} = \arg\min_i \|\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i\|^2$ — the minimum Euclidean distance rule in signal space. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]` **(a)** Derive the expression for the probability of error of a **binary antipodal signaling** scheme using the ML receiver over an AWGN channel with PSD $N_0/2$. Show all steps clearly. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A BPSK system transmits data at a rate of 1 Mbps over an AWGN channel with $N_0 = 10^{-20}$ W/Hz. If the received signal energy per bit is $E_b = 8 \times 10^{-20}$ J, compute the bit error probability using the Q-function. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks **Step 1 (1 mark):** System model. Two signals: $s_1(t) = \sqrt{E_b}\,\phi(t)$, $s_2(t) = -\sqrt{E_b}\,\phi(t)$, with equal priors $P(s_1) = P(s_2) = 1/2$. **Step 2 (1 mark):** Received signal: $r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t)$, where $n(t)$ is AWGN with PSD $N_0/2$. **Step 3 (1 mark):** Correlate $r(t)$ with $\phi(t)$ over $[0, T]$: $$r = \int_0^T r(t)\phi(t) dt = \pm\sqrt{E_b} + n$$ where $n = \int_0^T n(t)\phi(t) dt \sim \mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2)$. **Step 4 (1 mark):** Under $H_1$ (signal $s_1$ sent): $r \sim \mathcal{N}(+\sqrt{E_b}, N_0/2)$. Under $H_2$: $r \sim \mathcal{N}(-\sqrt{E_b}, N_0/2)$. The ML threshold is $r_{\text{th}} = 0$. **Step 5 (1 mark):** Error probability: $$P_e = P(r < 0 \mid H_1) = P\!\left(\mathcal{N}(\sqrt{E_b}, N_0/2) < 0\right)$$ **Step 6 (1 mark):** Standardizing: $$P_e = Q\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{E_b}}{\sqrt{N_0/2}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$ **Step 7 (1 mark):** Final boxed result: $$\boxed{P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)}$$ --- #### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 (1 mark):** Given data: $R_b = 10^6$ bps, $N_0 = 10^{-20}$ W/Hz, $E_b = 8 \times 10^{-20}$ J. **Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute the argument of the Q-function: $$\frac{2E_b}{N_0} = \frac{2 \times 8 \times 10^{-20}}{10^{-20}} = 16$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Hence: $$P_e = Q(\sqrt{16}) = Q(4)$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Using the standard Q-function table: $Q(4) \approx 3.167 \times 10^{-5}$. **Step 5 (1 mark):** Verification using Chernoff bound: $Q(x) \leq \frac{1}{2} e^{-x^2/2} = \frac{1}{2}e^{-8} \approx 1.68 \times 10^{-4}$ — consistent upper bound. **Step 6 (1 mark):** Interpretation: out of $10^6$ bits, approximately **32 bits** will be received in error. **Step 7 (1 mark):** Final answer: $$\boxed{P_e = Q(4) \approx 3.17 \times 10^{-5}}$$ --- ### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]` **(a)** Derive the probability of error expression for **coherent binary orthogonal signaling (BFSK)** over an AWGN channel using the ML receiver. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the performance of BPSK and BFSK at high SNR. What is the asymptotic coding gain of BPSK over BFSK? A system designer has an $E_b/N_0$ budget of 10 dB. Which scheme achieves a lower bit error rate, and by what factor? **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks **Step 1 (1 mark):** Signals: $s_1(t) = \sqrt{E_b}\,\phi_1(t)$, $s_2(t) = \sqrt{E_b}\,\phi_2(t)$, where $\int_0^T \phi_i \phi_j = \delta_{ij}$. Equal priors. **Step 2 (1 mark):** Correlator outputs: $$r_1 = \int_0^T r(t)\phi_1(t) dt, \qquad r_2 = \int_0^T r(t)\phi_2(t) dt$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Under $H_1$: $r_1 = \sqrt{E_b} + n_1$, $r_2 = n_2$, with $n_1, n_2 \sim \mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2)$, independent. **Step 4 (1 mark):** Decision rule: $H_1$ if $r_1 > r_2$, else $H_2$. Decision variable $D = r_1 - r_2$. **Step 5 (1 mark):** Distribution: $D \mid H_1 \sim \mathcal{N}(\sqrt{E_b}, N_0)$. **Step 6 (1 mark):** Standardize: $$P_e = P(D < 0 \mid H_1) = Q\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{E_b}}{\sqrt{N_0}}\right)$$ **Step 7 (1 mark):** Final result: $$\boxed{P_e^{\text{BFSK}} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)}$$ --- #### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 (1 mark):** Comparison at high SNR: $$P_e^{\text{BPSK}} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right), \qquad P_e^{\text{BFSK}} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_b/N_0}\right)$$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** At high SNR, $Q(x) \sim \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi x^2}} e^{-x^2/2}$ — exponential decay dominates. **Step 3 (1 mark):** The exponent in BPSK is $-(2E_b/N_0)/2 = -E_b/N_0$. For BFSK, it is $-(E_b/N_0)/2 = -E_b/(2N_0)$. The BPSK exponent decays **twice as fast**. **Step 4 (1 mark):** Asymptotic coding gain: BPSK outperforms BFSK by **3 dB** (a factor of 2 in $E_b/N_0$) for the same $P_e$. **Step 5 (1 mark):** Numerical evaluation at $E_b/N_0 = 10$ dB $= 10$ (linear): $$P_e^{\text{BPSK}} = Q(\sqrt{20}) = Q(4.47) \approx 3.87 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$P_e^{\text{BFSK}} = Q(\sqrt{10}) = Q(3.16) \approx 7.93 \times 10^{-4}$$ **Step 6 (1 mark):** Ratio: $$\frac{P_e^{\text{BFSK}}}{P_e^{\text{BPSK}}} = \frac{7.93 \times 10^{-4}}{3.87 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 205$$ **Step 7 (1 mark):** Final verdict: $$\boxed{\text{BPSK is preferred, with } \approx 200\times \text{ lower } P_e \text{ at } E_b/N_0 = 10 \text{ dB}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing ML with MAP (–2 marks):** ML is *not* generally optimal. Always state the equiprobable-prior condition. > 2. **Wrong Q-function argument (–2 marks):** Students frequently write $P_e = Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ for BPSK. The correct argument is $\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}$. The factor of 2 is *the* classic error. > 3. **Forgetting to square the distance (–1.5 marks):** The distance $d_{12}$ must be squared in the $P_e$ formula. > 4. **Skipping the standardization step (–1 mark):** Every Gaussian probability must be reduced to $Q(\cdot)$ form for marks. > 5. **Mixing up orthogonal vs antipodal (–2 marks):** Orthogonal = $\sqrt{E_b/N_0}$; antipodal = $\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}$. Always check signal correlation. > 6. **Not stating units (–0.5 mark):** $E_b$ in Joules, $N_0$ in W/Hz, ratio is dimensionless. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ⭐ **ML rule:** $\hat{s} = \arg\max_i p(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i)$ — pick the most likely cause. - ⭐ **Optimality:** ML $\equiv$ MAP when all priors are equal ($P(s_i) = 1/M$). - ⭐ **AWGN Likelihood:** $p(\mathbf{r} \mid s_i) \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{\|\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i\|^2}{N_0}\right)$. - ⭐ **Geometric form:** ML = Minimum Euclidean Distance in signal space. - ⭐ **Binary test:** Decide $s_1$ if $D = \mathbf{r}\cdot(\mathbf{s}_1 - \mathbf{s}_2) > \gamma = \tfrac{\|\mathbf{s}_2\|^2 - \|\mathbf{s}_1\|^2}{2}$. - ⭐ **BPSK error:** $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)$. - ⭐ **BFSK error:** $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_b/N_0}\right)$. - ⭐ **3 dB rule:** BPSK is 3 dB better than BFSK (asymptotic coding gain). - ⭐ **Matched filter equivalence:** The correlation receiver can be replaced by a bank of matched filters $h_i(t) = s_i(T-t)$. - ⭐ **Threshold for equi-energy signals:** $\gamma = 0$ — simply compare correlations. - ⭐ **$Q$-function identities:** $Q(-x) = 1 - Q(x)$, $Q(0) = 0.5$, $Q(\infty) = 0$. - ⭐ **Chernoff bound (for sanity check):** $Q(x) \leq \frac{1}{2}e^{-x^2/2}$. - ⭐ **KTU 2024 pattern:** Always derive the $P_e$ expression from the Gaussian pdf — partial marks for each correct transition. - ⭐ **Implementation tip:** The "compare distances" structure is hardware-friendly — implement as $|\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i|^2$ computations followed by a comparator. - ⭐ **Extension to $M$-ary:** ML generalizes naturally — pick the symbol with the largest likelihood among all $M$ candidates. - ⭐ **Key insight:** Doubling the Euclidean signal distance $d_{12}$ saves **3 dB** of $E_b/N_0$ — the geometric foundation of all constellation design.

topics.title: Digital Bandpass Modulation Schemes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT502 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Digital Bandpass Modulation Schemes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Digital Bandpass Modulation** is the process of modulating a discrete digital bitstream onto a sinusoidal carrier wave by varying one or more of its fundamental parameters — **amplitude, frequency, or phase** — such that the resulting transmitted signal occupies a band of frequencies centered around the carrier frequency $f_c$. Unlike baseband transmission (which uses rectangular NRZ pulses occupying low frequencies), bandpass modulation shifts the digital spectrum to a higher frequency band suitable for wireless/RF propagation, channel multiplexing, and antenna radiation.

Mathematically, the generalized bandpass modulated signal is expressed as:

$$s(t) = A(t) \cos[2\pi f_c t + \theta(t) + \phi_0]$$

where the three time-varying parameters control the modulation scheme:
- **Amplitude** $A(t)$ → ASK (Amplitude Shift Keying)
- **Frequency** $f_c$ deviation → FSK (Frequency Shift Keying)
- **Phase** $\theta(t)$ → PSK (Phase Shift Keying)
- **Combined** $A(t)$ and $\theta(t)$ → QAM (Quadrature Amplitude Modulation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU PCECT502 Module 4 explicitly focuses on **BPSK, QPSK, BFSK, DPSK, MSK, and QAM**. M-ary extensions are also expected in Part B (14-mark) questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Shipping Container" Intuition

Imagine a **factory** (digital source) producing goods (bits) that must reach a **distant warehouse** (receiver) through a city road (channel). The factory cannot send raw goods on foot through highway traffic — they would never arrive intact. So the goods are loaded onto **specialized delivery trucks** (carrier wave at $f_c$):

| Modulation Type | Truck Analogy | What Changes Per Truck |
|---|---|---|
| **ASK** | Trucks of different *sizes* (small box vs. big container) | Amplitude (size) of carrier |
| **FSK** | Trucks of different *colors* running on a fixed road | Frequency of carrier tone |
| **PSK** | Trucks all *same size* but drivers steering at different angles | Phase of carrier |
| **QAM** | Trucks of different *sizes AND steering angles* simultaneously | Both amplitude and phase |

Just as the receiver warehouse has **dispatch inspectors** (coherent demodulators) that recognize the truck type, the receiver correlates the incoming signal against reference carriers (coherent detection) or measures instantaneous frequency (non-coherent detection) to recover the bits.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Bandpass?** Baseband signals with sharp rectangular pulses have infinite spectral content (sinc-shaped spectrum with nulls at $f = n/T_b$). Transmitting such low-frequency signals would require physically enormous antennas ($\lambda/2$ size). Shifting to $f_c$ via bandpass modulation reduces antenna size to practical dimensions: $L = \dfrac{c}{2f_c}$.

## 1.3 Why Digital Bandpass Modulation Matters in Modern Engineering

- **Spectral Efficiency:** QAM packs multiple bits per symbol, crucial for 4G/5G where bandwidth is scarce.
- **Power Efficiency:** Constant-envelope schemes (PSK, FSK, MSK) work with **non-linear power amplifiers** without spectral regrowth.
- **Error Robustness:** Different schemes offer trade-offs — BPSK gives best $P_e$ vs. SNR; FSK gives immunity to phase noise.
- **Coexistence:** Multiple users share the same medium using **orthogonal signaling** (TDM, FDM, CDMA all rely on bandpass translation).

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Tip:** In KTU viva voce, always remember the three parameters of a sine wave — *Amplitude, Frequency, Phase* — and the three classical schemes — *ASK, FSK, PSK*. Every other scheme is a derivative.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Digital Bandpass Modulation (Analyzed)

### 2.1.1 Binary Phase Shift Keying (BPSK)

**Concept:** Represents binary data by shifting the carrier phase between two values separated by $180°$ ($\pi$ radians). Bit `0` → phase $0$, Bit `1` → phase $\pi$ (or vice versa).

The two basis signals are **antipodal** — mirror images of each other about the origin in signal space.

$$s_1(t) = \sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{T_b}} \cos(2\pi f_c t) \quad \text{for bit 1}$$
$$s_2(t) = -\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{T_b}} \cos(2\pi f_c t) \quad \text{for bit 0}$$

- **Signal Constellation:** Two points on a 1-D line, distance $2\sqrt{E_b}$ apart.
- **Bandwidth:** $B_{BPSK} = 2/T_b = 2R_b$ Hz (main lobe width).
- **Detection:** Coherent detection required (carrier phase recovery needed).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why BPSK is the "gold standard" for low-SNR regimes:** It has the **lowest probability of bit error** among all binary schemes for a given $E_b/N_0$. This is the bedrock result derived in Section 3.

### 2.1.2 Quadrature Phase Shift Keying (QPSK)

**Concept:** Splits the incoming bitstream into two parallel streams (I and Q), each modulating a carrier in **quadrature** (90° apart). Each pair of bits (dibit) maps to one of **4 phases** spaced by $90°$.

$$s_{QPSK}(t) = \sqrt{\dfrac{2E_s}{T_s}} \cos\left(2\pi f_c t + (2i-1)\dfrac{\pi}{4}\right), \quad i = 1,2,3,4$$

where $E_s = 2E_b$ (symbol energy is twice bit energy) and $T_s = 2T_b$.

- **Constellation:** 4 points on a circle at $45°, 135°, 225°, 315°$.
- **Bit rate to symbol rate ratio:** $R_s = R_b / 2$ → **half the bandwidth of BPSK** for the same bit rate.
- **Phase transitions:** Can jump by $\pm 180°$ causing envelope to pass through zero (amplitude dips) — mitigated by **OQPSK** (offset QPSK) and **$\pi/4$-DQPSK**.

### 2.1.3 Binary Frequency Shift Keying (BFSK)

**Concept:** Two distinct carrier frequencies $f_1$ and $f_2$ represent bits `0` and `1`. Two variants exist:

- **Coherent BFSK (orthogonal):** Minimum frequency separation $\Delta f = 1/(2T_b)$ to maintain orthogonality.
- **Non-coherent BFSK:** Detection via envelope detectors; looser frequency requirements.

$$s_0(t) = \sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{T_b}} \cos(2\pi f_1 t), \quad s_1(t) = \sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{T_b}} \cos(2\pi f_2 t)$$

- **Bandwidth:** $B_{BFSK} = \vert f_2 - f_1 \vert + 2/T_b$ (Carson's rule analogue).
- **Power Spectral Density:** Has prominent sidelobes — less bandwidth-efficient than PSK.

### 2.1.4 Minimum Shift Keying (MSK)

**Concept:** A **continuous-phase FSK** with frequency deviation exactly half the bit rate ($\Delta f = 1/(4T_b)$) — the minimum for orthogonal CPFSK. The phase trajectory is **continuous** and piecewise linear, rotating by $\pm 90°$ per bit period.

Key parameters:
- Frequency deviation: $h = 0.5$ (modulation index)
- Phase change per bit: $\Delta\phi = \pm \pi/2$
- Can be expressed as **OQPSK with sinusoidal pulse shaping** (Gaussian MSK / GMSK used in GSM).

$$s_{MSK}(t) = \sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{T_b}} \cos\left(2\pi f_c t + \theta(t)\right)$$

$$\theta(t) = \dfrac{\pi}{2T_b} \int_{-\infty}^{t} d(\tau) d\tau$$

where $d(\tau) \in \{-1, +1\}$ is the NRZ data signal.

### 2.1.5 Quadrature Amplitude Modulation (QAM)

**Concept:** A **two-dimensional** scheme varying **both amplitude and phase** simultaneously. The signal space has points arranged in a rectangular grid.

$$s_{M-QAM}(t) = a_i \sqrt{\dfrac{2E_0}{T_s}} \cos(2\pi f_c t) - b_i \sqrt{\dfrac{2E_0}{T_s}} \sin(2\pi f_c t)$$

where $(a_i, b_i)$ are integer amplitudes from a constellation grid, and $E_0$ is the energy of the lowest-amplitude signal.

**Common variants:** 4-QAM (= QPSK), 16-QAM, 64-QAM, 256-QAM (used in Wi-Fi, LTE, DVB-C).

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Bits per symbol | $k = \log_2 M$ |
| Symbol period | $T_s = kT_b$ |
| Bandwidth efficiency | $\eta = k \text{ bits/s/Hz}$ (for raised-cosine $\alpha=0$) |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in 80% of KTU Module 4 numerical problems.**

| # | Scheme | Probability of Bit Error $P_b$ | Bandwidth | Spectral Efficiency $\eta$ | Constellation Points |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Coherent BPSK** | $P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | $2R_b$ | $0.5$ bps/Hz | 2 (antipodal) |
| 2 | **Coherent BFSK** | $P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | $\vert f_2-f_1\vert + 2R_b$ | $< 0.5$ bps/Hz | 2 (orthogonal) |
| 3 | **Non-Coherent BFSK** | $P_b = \dfrac{1}{2}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_b}{2N_0}\right)$ | $\vert f_2-f_1\vert + 2R_b$ | $< 0.5$ bps/Hz | 2 |
| 4 | **DPSK** | $P_b = \dfrac{1}{2}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}\right)$ | $2R_b$ | $0.5$ bps/Hz | 2 (differential) |
| 5 | **QPSK** | $P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | $R_b$ | $1$ bps/Hz | 4 |
| 6 | **M-PSK** | $P_b \approx \dfrac{2}{\log_2 M} Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_s}{N_0}}\sin\!\dfrac{\pi}{M}\right)$ | $2R_s = 2R_b/k$ | $k/2$ bps/Hz | M on unit circle |
| 7 | **M-QAM (square)** | $P_b \approx \dfrac{4}{k}\left(1-\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{M}}\right) Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{3k}{(M-1)}\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | $2R_s$ | $k$ bps/Hz | $\sqrt{M}\times\sqrt{M}$ grid |

**Where:**
- $Q(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_x^{\infty} e^{-u^2/2} du$ — Gaussian Q-function
- $E_b$ — Energy per bit
- $E_s = kE_b$ — Energy per symbol
- $N_0$ — Noise power spectral density (single-sided)
- $R_b$ — Bit rate, $R_s = R_b/k$ — Symbol rate

### Key Relationship Table

| Relationship | Formula | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Energy ratio | $\dfrac{E_b}{N_0} = \dfrac{S}{N} \cdot \dfrac{B}{R_b}$ | Convert SNR to $E_b/N_0$ |
| Symbol-bit energy | $E_s = (\log_2 M) E_b$ | M-ary conversions |
| Shannon limit | $\dfrac{E_b}{N_0} > \ln 2 \approx 0.693$ (~ -1.6 dB) | Theoretical bound |
| BPSK at $P_b = 10^{-5}$ | $\dfrac{E_b}{N_0} \approx 9.6$ dB | Benchmark value |

> [!TIP]
> **Energy-per-bit to dB conversion:** $\left.\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}\right|_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}\right)$. Always present final answers in dB in KTU exams for full marks.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Scheme | Modern Application |
|---|---|
| BPSK | Satellite telemetry, deep-space comms (Voyager, Cassini), GPS coarse acquisition |
| QPSK | DVB-S, Wi-Fi (legacy 802.11b), LTE control channels, cable modems |
| 8-PSK | Satellite TV (DVB-S2), aeronautical telemetry |
| 16/64-QAM | Wi-Fi (802.11ac/ax), 4G LTE, 5G NR, DVB-C |
| 256-QAM | 5G NR, Wi-Fi 6/6E, DOCSIS 3.1 |
| MSK/GMSK | GSM cellular (2G), AIS (ship tracking) |
| BFSK | Garage door openers, RFID, low-cost telemetry, RTTY |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Probability of Bit Error for Coherent BPSK

### Problem Setup

We transmit one of two equally likely signals over an **AWGN channel**:

$$s_1(t) = \sqrt{E_b}\,\phi_1(t), \quad s_2(t) = -s_1(t) = -\sqrt{E_b}\,\phi_1(t)$$

where $\phi_1(t) = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ is the orthonormal basis function with energy normalized to 1.

The received signal is:
$$r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t), \quad i \in \{1, 2\}$$

with $n(t)$ being zero-mean AWGN with PSD $N_0/2$.

### Step 1: Correlator Output (Sufficient Statistic)

The optimal receiver correlates $r(t)$ with $\phi_1(t)$ over $[0, T_b]$:

$$r = \int_0^{T_b} r(t)\phi_1(t)\,dt$$

**Substituting** $r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t)$:

$$r = \int_0^{T_b} s_i(t)\phi_1(t)\,dt + \int_0^{T_b} n(t)\phi_1(t)\,dt$$

The first integral is the **signal component**; the second is a Gaussian random variable $n$ with $N(0, N_0/2)$.

### Step 2: Compute Signal Component

For $s_1(t) = \sqrt{E_b}\phi_1(t)$:

$$\int_0^{T_b} s_1(t)\phi_1(t)\,dt = \sqrt{E_b}\int_0^{T_b}\phi_1^2(t)\,dt = \sqrt{E_b}\cdot 1 = +\sqrt{E_b}$$

For $s_2(t) = -s_1(t)$:

$$\int_0^{T_b} s_2(t)\phi_1(t)\,dt = -\sqrt{E_b}$$

### Step 3: Decision Rule

The decision threshold is at the **midpoint** of the two possible signal values (by symmetry, since $P(s_1) = P(s_2) = 1/2$):

$$\text{Threshold} = \dfrac{+\sqrt{E_b} + (-\sqrt{E_b})}{2} = 0$$

**Decision rule:**
- If $r > 0$ → decide $\hat{s} = s_1$ (bit 1)
- If $r < 0$ → decide $\hat{s} = s_2$ (bit 0)

### Step 4: Probability of Error

Assume $s_1(t)$ was sent. Then $r = +\sqrt{E_b} + n$ where $n \sim \mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2)$.

$$P(\text{error} \mid s_1) = P(r < 0) = P\!\left(n < -\sqrt{E_b}\right)$$

Standardizing the Gaussian:

$$P(\text{error} \mid s_1) = P\!\left(\dfrac{n}{\sqrt{N_0/2}} < \dfrac{-\sqrt{E_b}}{\sqrt{N_0/2}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

By symmetry, $P(\text{error} \mid s_2) = P(\text{error} \mid s_1)$. Total probability of error:

$$\boxed{P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)}$$

### Step 5: Sanity Check — Asymptotic Behavior

- As $\dfrac{E_b}{N_0} \to \infty$: $P_b \to 0$ ✓ (reliable comms at high SNR)
- As $\dfrac{E_b}{N_0} \to 0$: $P_b \to 0.5$ ✓ (random guessing at zero SNR)
- At $P_b = 10^{-5}$: $E_b/N_0 \approx 9.6$ dB ✓ (matches benchmark table)

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Bandwidth of QPSK vs BPSK

### Given
Bit rate $R_b$ bits/second, NRZ line coding.

### Step 1: BPSK Bandwidth

The power spectral density of BPSK is:

$$P_{BPSK}(f) = \dfrac{E_b}{2}\left[\left(\dfrac{\sin\pi(f-f_c)T_b}{\pi(f-f_c)T_b}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{\sin\pi(f+f_c)T_b}{\pi(f+f_c)T_b}\right)^2\right]$$

The **main lobe width** (between first nulls) is centered at $\pm f_c$ with width $2/T_b$:

$$B_{BPSK} = \dfrac{2}{T_b} = 2R_b \text{ Hz}$$

### Step 2: QPSK Symbol Rate

In QPSK, the bitstream is split into two parallel streams each at $R_b/2$. The symbol (dibit) duration is:

$$T_s = 2T_b \implies R_s = \dfrac{R_b}{2}$$

### Step 3: QPSK Bandwidth

Using the same analysis as BPSK but with $T_s$ replacing $T_b$:

$$B_{QPSK} = \dfrac{2}{T_s} = \dfrac{2}{2T_b} = R_b \text{ Hz}$$

### Step 4: Spectral Efficiency Comparison

$$\dfrac{B_{BPSK}}{B_{QPSK}} = \dfrac{2R_b}{R_b} = 2$$

**Conclusion:** QPSK transmits at **half the bandwidth of BPSK** for the same bit rate, giving **double the spectral efficiency (1 bps/Hz vs 0.5 bps/Hz)**. However, both have **identical $P_b$ vs $E_b/N_0$** performance — bandwidth is traded for symbol complexity.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: 16-QAM Bit Error Probability

### Step 1: Square M-QAM Signal Space

For $M = L^2$ with $L = 2^k/2$ points per dimension, the in-phase and quadrature amplitudes are:

$$a_i, b_i \in \{\pm 1, \pm 3, \pm 5, \ldots, \pm (L-1)\}$$

The minimum distance between adjacent points is $d_{min} = 2\sqrt{E_0}$ where $E_0$ is the energy of the lowest-amplitude point.

### Step 2: Average Symbol Energy

By symmetry, average energy per symbol is:

$$E_{s,avg} = \dfrac{2(L^2 - 1)}{3}E_0$$

### Step 3: Symbol Error Probability (Union Bound Approximation)

For square M-QAM with Gray coding, the exact $P_s$ is well-approximated by:

$$P_s \approx 1 - \left(1 - P_{\sqrt{M}}\right)^2 \approx 2P_{\sqrt{M}} \text{ for small } P_{\sqrt{M}}$$

where $P_{\sqrt{M}}$ is the error probability of the equivalent $\sqrt{M}$-PAM:

$$P_{\sqrt{M}} = 2\!\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{M}}\right) Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{3}{M-1}\cdot\dfrac{E_s}{N_0}}\right)$$

### Step 4: Convert to Bit Error Probability

With Gray coding, adjacent symbol errors correspond to single bit errors:

$$P_b \approx \dfrac{P_s}{\log_2 M} = \dfrac{P_s}{k}$$

**Final result:**

$$\boxed{P_{b,16\text{-}QAM} \approx \dfrac{3}{4} Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{4}{5}\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)}$$

(Substituting $M = 16$, $k = 4$, $E_s = 4E_b$.)

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Generic Digital Bandpass Transmitter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Bits b k] --> B[Serial to Parallel Converter]
    B --> C[Bit to Symbol Mapper Constellation]
    C --> D[Pulse Shaping Filter pt]
    D --> E[Multiplier I branch with cos2pifct]
    D --> F[Multiplier Q branch with sin2pifct]
    E --> G[Summation Adder]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Power Amplifier and Channel]
    H --> I[AWGN Channel n t]
    I --> J[Receiver Input r t]

    classDef source fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,stroke-width:2px
    classDef process fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px
    classDef modulator fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#C2185B,stroke-width:2px
    classDef channel fill:#E0F2F1,stroke:#00695C,stroke-width:2px
    classDef receiver fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:2px

    class A,B,C source
    class D process
    class E,F,G modulator
    class H,I channel
    class J receiver
```

## 4.2 Signal Constellations (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BPSK["BPSK Constellation"]
        B1[bit 0: phase 0] --- B2[bit 1: phase 180]
    end

    subgraph QPSK["QPSK Constellation 4 points"]
        Q1[00: 45 deg] --- Q2[01: 135 deg]
        Q2 --- Q3[11: 225 deg]
        Q3 --- Q4[10: 315 deg]
        Q4 --- Q1
    end

    subgraph BFSK["BFSK Frequency Domain"]
        F1[f1: bit 0] --- F2[f2: bit 1]
    end

    subgraph QAM16["16-QAM Grid 4x4"]
        AM1[amplitude level 1] --- AM2[amplitude level 3]
        AM2 --- AM3[amplitude level 5]
    end

    classDef const fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F9A825,stroke-width:2px
    class B1,B2,Q1,Q2,Q3,Q4,F1,F2,AM1,AM2,AM3 const
```

## 4.3 BPSK Modulator–Demodulator Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input NRZ Bits dt] --> B[Multiplier with Carrier cos2pifct]
    B --> C[Transmitted Signal st]
    C --> D[AWGN Channel Add Noise]
    D --> E[Received Signal rt]
    E --> F[Multiplier with Local Carrier cos2pifct]
    F --> G[Integrator over 0 to Tb]
    G --> H[Decision Device Threshold at 0]
    H --> I[Recovered Bits dt hat]

    J[Carrier Recovery PLL] --> F

    classDef signal fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px
    classDef block fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px
    classDef recovery fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#558B2F,stroke-width:2px
    class A,C,E,I signal
    class B,F,G,H block
    class J recovery
```

## 4.4 QPSK Modulator Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Serial Bit Stream Rb] --> B[Serial to Parallel S/P]
    B --> C[Bit Pair d1d2 dibit]
    C --> D[I channel multiplier with cos]
    B --> E[Q channel delay and multiplier with sin]
    D --> F[Summation Node]
    E --> F
    F --> G[QPSK Output st]

    classDef input fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px
    classDef logic fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#FF8F00,stroke-width:2px
    classDef output fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:2px
    class A,B input
    class C,D,E logic
    class F,G output
```

## 4.5 Performance Comparison: $P_b$ vs $E_b/N_0$ (Conceptual Tradeoff Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HighPowerLowBandwidth["Low Bandwidth Region"]
        H1[QPSK: 1 bps/Hz, needs 9.6 dB at 1e-5]
    end

    subgraph MediumTradeoff["Balanced Region"]
        M1[BPSK: 0.5 bps/Hz, needs 9.6 dB at 1e-5]
        M2[16QAM: 2 bps/Hz, needs 13.4 dB at 1e-5]
    end

    subgraph HighBandwidthLowPower["High Bandwidth Region"]
        L1[BFSK coherent: needs 12.6 dB at 1e-5]
    end

    H1 -->|better spectral efficiency| M1
    M1 -->|more bits per symbol| M2
    M2 -->|simpler detection| L1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Moving right means more bits per Hz (spectral efficiency) but requires more power ($E_b/N_0$). Moving up means simpler detection but lower spectral efficiency. This is the **fundamental trade-off triangle** of digital modulation.

---
