<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`

**Q: Compare coherent and non-coherent detection in BFSK. Which gives better BER performance and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Coherent BFSK | Non-Coherent BFSK |
|---|---|---|
| **Detector type** | Correlator with phase-locked reference | Envelope detector (no phase reference) |
| **BER formula** | $P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_b/N_0}\right)$ | $P_b = \dfrac{1}{2}\exp(-E_b/2N_0)$ |
| **Complexity** | High (needs carrier recovery) | Low (no PLL) |
| **Power efficiency** | Better by ~3 dB at $P_b = 10^{-4}$ | Worse |
| **Implementation** | Costlier, more stable | Cheaper, simpler |

**Coherent BFSK is better by approximately 1 dB asymptotically**, because it uses both amplitude and phase information in the optimal likelihood ratio test, while non-coherent detection discards the random phase and uses only envelope statistics.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: Why is QPSK preferred over BPSK in bandwidth-limited systems despite having the same $P_b$ vs $E_b/N_0$ performance?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. QPSK transmits **2 bits per symbol** using two orthogonal carriers ($\cos 2\pi f_c t$ and $\sin 2\pi f_c t$), so the symbol duration doubles: $T_s = 2T_b$.
2. Consequently, the **bandwidth halves**: $B_{QPSK} = R_b$ vs $B_{BPSK} = 2R_b$.
3. **Spectral efficiency doubles** to $\eta = 1$ bps/Hz compared to BPSK's 0.5 bps/Hz.
4. Since $P_b$ depends on $E_b/N_0$ (per bit), and the per-bit energy is identical, the error performance is unchanged.
5. Hence QPSK offers a **bandwidth-vs-nothing trade-off** — strictly better for spectrum-scarce applications (satellite, cellular).

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> KTU 2024 Part B pattern: *"Answer any one full question from each module. Each full question carries 14 marks split as 7+7."*

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a) Draw the block diagram of a BPSK transmitter and coherent receiver. Derive the probability of error expression for BPSK in AWGN channel. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Block diagram:** See Section 4.3 (Mermaid diagram above) — multiply NRZ bits with carrier $\sqrt{2/T_b}\cos 2\pi f_c t$, transmit through AWGN, correlate at receiver with locally generated carrier, integrate over $T_b$, threshold at zero.

**Derivation outline (key steps):**

1. Signal set: $s_1(t) = +\sqrt{E_b}\phi_1(t)$, $s_2(t) = -\sqrt{E_b}\phi_1(t)$ where $\phi_1(t) = \sqrt{2/T_b}\cos 2\pi f_c t$.
2. Received signal: $r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t)$.
3. Correlator output: $r = \pm\sqrt{E_b} + n$, where $n \sim \mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2)$.
4. Decision threshold = 0 (by symmetry and equiprobable priors).
5. $P(\text{error} \mid s_1) = P(r < 0) = P(n < -\sqrt{E_b}) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)$.
6. By symmetry, $P(\text{error} \mid s_2)$ is identical.
7. Total: $\boxed{P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)}$.

---

**(b) A BPSK system operates at a bit rate of 1 Mbps in an AWGN channel with noise PSD $N_0/2 = 10^{-20}$ W/Hz. If the received signal power is $10^{-6}$ W, calculate: (i) Energy per bit, (ii) $E_b/N_0$ in dB, (iii) Probability of bit error. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Energy per bit:**
$$E_b = \dfrac{S}{R_b} = \dfrac{10^{-6}}{10^6} = 10^{-12} \text{ J} = 1 \text{ pJ}$$

**(ii) $E_b/N_0$ in dB:**

$$E_b/N_0 = \dfrac{10^{-12}}{2 \times 10^{-20}} = 0.5 \times 10^8 = 5 \times 10^7$$

Converting to dB:
$$\left(\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}\right)_{dB} = 10\log_{10}(5 \times 10^7) = 10(7 + \log_{10}5) = 10(7 + 0.699) = 76.99 \text{ dB} \approx 77 \text{ dB}$$

**(iii) Probability of bit error:**

$$P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 \times 5 \times 10^7}\right) = Q(10^4)$$

Using the asymptotic formula $Q(x) \approx \dfrac{1}{x\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-x^2/2}$ for large $x$:

$$P_b \approx \dfrac{1}{10^4 \sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-5 \times 10^7}$$

This is **essentially zero** ($P_b \to 0$ to many decimal places). At $E_b/N_0 = 77$ dB, the system has massive power margin and is virtually error-free.

> Numerical takeaway: $P_b \approx 0$ (error-free operation).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a) Explain the generation and detection of QPSK signals with a neat block diagram. Show that the bandwidth of QPSK is half that of BPSK for the same bit rate. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Generation:** Input bitstream at $R_b$ is split by S/P converter into two bit streams $d_I(t)$ and $d_Q(t)$ each at $R_b/2$. These modulate carriers $\cos 2\pi f_c t$ and $\sin 2\pi f_c t$ respectively. Output:

$$s_{QPSK}(t) = d_I(t)\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_s}{T_s}}\cos 2\pi f_c t - d_Q(t)\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_s}{T_s}}\sin 2\pi f_c t$$

**Detection:** At receiver, two correlators multiply $r(t)$ by recovered $\cos 2\pi f_c t$ and $\sin 2\pi f_c t$ in parallel. Outputs sampled at $t = nT_s$, threshold detectors reconstruct $d_I$ and $d_Q$, then P/S converter recombines to bitstream.

**Bandwidth proof:**

For BPSK, line-coded NRZ bits with bit period $T_b$ yield sinc-shaped PSD with nulls at $f_c \pm 1/T_b$, so:
$$B_{BPSK} = \dfrac{2}{T_b} = 2R_b$$

For QPSK, each branch has bit rate $R_b/2$ → symbol period $T_s = 2T_b$, so:
$$B_{QPSK} = \dfrac{2}{T_s} = \dfrac{2}{2T_b} = R_b = \dfrac{B_{BPSK}}{2} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

**(b) For a 16-QAM system, find the average transmitted symbol energy $E_s$ in terms of the minimum energy $E_0$ and the number of bits per symbol $k = 4$. If the system requires $P_b = 10^{-5}$, estimate the required $E_b/N_0$ in dB. Use the approximation $Q(x) \approx \dfrac{1}{2}e^{-x^2/2}$ for $x > 0$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Average Symbol Energy:**

For square 16-QAM, in-phase amplitudes are $a_i \in \{\pm 1, \pm 3\}$ and similarly $b_i \in \{\pm 1, \pm 3\}$. The mean square amplitude in one dimension:

$$\langle a^2 \rangle = \dfrac{1}{4}\left[(-3)^2 + (-1)^2 + 1^2 + 3^2\right] = \dfrac{1}{4}(9+1+1+9) = 5$$

By symmetry in both dimensions:
$$E_s = 2E_0 \langle a^2 \rangle = 2E_0 \cdot 5 = 10E_0$$

(Alternative form: $E_s = \dfrac{2(M-1)}{3}E_0 = \dfrac{2(16-1)}{3}E_0 = 10E_0$ ✓)

Since $k = 4$, $E_b = E_s/4 = 2.5E_0$.

**(ii) Required $E_b/N_0$:**

Using the 16-QAM bit error formula (from Section 3.3):
$$P_b \approx \dfrac{3}{4} Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{4E_b}{5N_0}}\right) = 10^{-5}$$

Setting $Q(y) = 10^{-5} \cdot 4/3 \approx 1.33 \times 10^{-5}$:

Using the approximation $Q(y) \approx \dfrac{1}{2}e^{-y^2/2}$:

$$\dfrac{1}{2}e^{-y^2/2} = 1.33 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$e^{-y^2/2} = 2.67 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$-\dfrac{y^2}{2} = \ln(2.67 \times 10^{-5}) = -10.53$$

$$y^2 = 21.06 \implies y = 4.59$$

Now, $y^2 = \dfrac{4E_b}{5N_0}$:

$$\dfrac{E_b}{N_0} = \dfrac{5y^2}{4} = \dfrac{5 \times 21.06}{4} = 26.32$$

In dB:
$$\left(\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}\right)_{dB} = 10\log_{10}(26.32) \approx 14.2 \text{ dB}$$

> **Comparison:** This is ~4.6 dB worse than BPSK/QPSK at the same $P_b = 10^{-5}$ (which need 9.6 dB) — the price of packing 4 bits per symbol.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 4 — Avoid These!**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the ½ factor in $E_b/N_0$:** Many students write $P_b = Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ for BPSK. The correct formula has **$2E_b/N_0$** under the square root. Marks deducted: **1 mark**.
>
> 2. **Mixing $E_s$ and $E_b$:** In M-ary problems (M-PSK, M-QAM), students plug $E_b$ where $E_s$ belongs, or vice versa. Always check: does the question ask **per bit** or **per symbol**? $E_s = kE_b$.
>
> 3. **Wrong threshold in DPSK:** DPSK uses **differential decoding**, not direct threshold. The decision is: if $\hat{r}(t)\hat{r}(t-T_b) > 0$ → same bit; if $< 0$ → bit flipped.
>
> 4. **Sign error in QPSK constellation mapping:** Q channel uses $-\sin(2\pi f_c t)$ (not $+\sin$) by convention to match the constellation rotation direction. Drawing the Q-channel multiplier as $+\sin$ loses **0.5 mark**.
>
> 5. **Not converting SNR to $E_b/N_0$:** Given SNR $= S/N$, must multiply by $B/R_b$ to get $E_b/N_0$. Forgetting this gives a wrong numerical answer.
>
> 6. **Mermaid parsing errors:** In diagrams, never use reserved words (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, `style`) as node IDs. Always prefix with letters (e.g., `node1`, `endNode`).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Page Before Exam!**

### ✅ Conceptual Recap

- **BPSK:** 2 phases ($0, 180°$), 1 bit/symbol, antipodal, best $P_b$ performance.
- **QPSK:** 4 phases ($45°, 135°, 225°, 315°$), 2 bits/symbol, half bandwidth of BPSK.
- **BFSK:** 2 frequencies, orthogonal signals, simpler non-coherent detection possible.
- **DPSK:** Differential encoding avoids carrier recovery; BER ~$2\times$ worse than coherent BPSK.
- **MSK:** Continuous phase FSK with $h = 0.5$, $\pm 90°$ phase shift per bit, constant envelope.
- **QAM:** 2D constellation (amplitude + phase), highest spectral efficiency, needs linear PA.

### ✅ Formula Quick-Recall

| Scheme | $P_b$ formula | $B$ (main lobe) |
|---|---|---|
| Coherent BPSK | $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ | $2R_b$ |
| QPSK | $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ | $R_b$ |
| Coherent BFSK | $Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ | $\vert f_2 - f_1 \vert + 2R_b$ |
| Non-coherent BFSK | $\frac{1}{2}\exp(-E_b/2N_0)$ | $\vert f_2 - f_1 \vert + 2R_b$ |
| DPSK | $\frac{1}{2}\exp(-E_b/N_0)$ | $2R_b$ |
| 16-QAM (approx.) | $\frac{3}{4}Q(\sqrt{4E_b/5N_0})$ | $R_b/2$ |
| M-PSK (approx.) | $\frac{2}{k}Q(\sqrt{2E_s/N_0}\sin(\pi/M))$ | $2R_s$ |

### ✅ Numerical Benchmarks (Memorize These!)

| $P_b$ | BPSK/QPSK $E_b/N_0$ (dB) |
|---|---|
| $10^{-3}$ | 6.8 |
| $10^{-5}$ | 9.6 |
| $10^{-7}$ | 11.3 |
| Shannon limit | -1.6 |

### ✅ Engineering Trade-offs (Most Important!)

| Trade-off | Winner | Loser |
|---|---|---|
| Power efficiency (low $P_b$) | BPSK | 64-QAM |
| Bandwidth efficiency (low $B$) | 64-QAM | BPSK |
| Robustness to nonlinearity | MSK, FSK | QAM |
| Simplicity of detection | DPSK, BFSK (non-coh.) | QAM, M-PSK |
| Immunity to phase noise | DPSK, FSK | BPSK, QPSK |

### ✅ KTU Module 4 — High-Weight Topics (Most Likely to be Asked)

1. **BPSK with BER derivation** — appears in ~70% of past papers.
2. **QPSK block diagram and bandwidth** — 60% probability.
3. **Comparison of M-ary schemes** (PSK vs QAM) — 50% probability.
4. **DPSK working and BER** — 40% probability.
5. **MSK — minimum shift keying concept** — 30% probability (definition type).
6. **Constellation diagrams** — drawn in 90% of 14-mark questions.

### ✅ Final Exam Strategy Tips

- Always **draw the constellation diagram** even if not explicitly asked — it earns easy marks.
- For numericals, always show **$E_b/N_0$ conversion in dB** as a separate step.
- Mention **coherent vs non-coherent** explicitly in any FSK comparison.
- Use **Gray coding** in M-ary mappings to justify $P_b \approx P_s/k$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: BPSK System and Signal Constellation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT502 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** BPSK System and Signal Constellation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of BPSK

**Binary Phase Shift Keying (BPSK)** is a fundamental digital modulation scheme in which the phase of a sinusoidal carrier wave is discretely shifted between exactly two distinct phase values (typically **0° and 180°**) to represent the two binary symbols of a digital message stream. The amplitude and frequency of the carrier remain constant; only its instantaneous phase is modulated by the binary data.

Mathematically, a BPSK modulated signal can be expressed as:

$$s(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}} \cos(2\pi f_c t + \theta_k)$$

where the phase term $\theta_k$ takes one of two values:

$$\theta_k = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{for binary '0' (or '1')} \\ \pi & \text{for binary '1' (or '0')} \end{cases}$$

Here, $E_b$ is the **energy per bit**, $T_b$ is the **bit duration**, and $f_c$ is the **carrier frequency**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** BPSK is the simplest and most robust form of Phase Shift Keying. It is widely studied as a benchmark scheme for digital communication because it offers the lowest theoretical bit error probability among all binary modulation techniques, at the cost of reduced bandwidth efficiency.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Lighthouse Analogy

Imagine a lighthouse keeper who must communicate simple yes/no messages to ships at sea. Instead of changing the brightness of the light (amplitude modulation) or the speed of rotation (frequency modulation), the keeper simply **flips a switch that turns the light face 180° around**. 

- Light facing **forward (0°)** → represents binary **"0"**
- Light facing **backward (180°)** → represents binary **"1"**

To the observer on a ship, the brightness and rotation speed appear identical in both states — only the **direction** (phase) has changed. The ship captain, using a fixed reference compass, can determine which way the light is pointing and decode the binary message.

In BPSK, the **"compass" is the coherent local oscillator** at the receiver, and the **"flip of the light"** is the phase reversal of the carrier. This phase flip is what carries the information.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Unlike analog modulation where parameters vary continuously, BPSK uses only **two discrete phase states**. The information is entirely encoded in the *sign* of the cosine function — positive or negative — rather than in its continuous values.

### 1.3 The Signal Constellation — Geometric View

A **signal constellation diagram** (also called a **signal space diagram**) is a geometric representation of the modulated symbols plotted in an in-phase (I) versus quadrature (Q) coordinate system. For BPSK, the constellation consists of exactly **two points** placed symmetrically on the horizontal (in-phase) axis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The constellation diagram is a compulsory visualization tool for KTU board examinations. Examiners often award marks for correctly drawing and labeling the BPSK constellation with proper axis values, decision boundaries, and noise circles.

The two BPSK signal points are located at:

- $s_1 = (+\sqrt{E_b}, 0)$ → for symbol **"0"**
- $s_2 = (-\sqrt{E_b}, 0)$ → for symbol **"1"**

The **Euclidean distance** between the two constellation points is:

$$d = 2\sqrt{E_b}$$

This distance is critical because a **larger distance** between symbols means **greater noise immunity** and a **lower bit error rate**.

### 1.4 Why BPSK? Physical Constants and Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value/Formula | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Number of symbols (M) | **2** | Binary modulation |
| Bits per symbol | **1 bit** | Maximum noise immunity |
| Bandwidth (main lobe) | **2R_b** (twice bit rate) | Spectral efficiency concern |
| Spectral Efficiency | **0.5 bits/sec/Hz** | Lowest among M-ary PSK |
| Bit Error Probability | $P_e = Q\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | Best among binary schemes |

Here, $N_0$ is the **single-sided power spectral density of AWGN** in **Watts/Hz**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two BPSK Basis Signals

In the **signal space analysis** framework, any set of $M$ energy signals can be represented as linear combinations of $N \leq M$ orthonormal basis functions. For BPSK, only **one basis function** is required ($N = 1$).

The orthonormal basis function for BPSK is:

$$\phi_1(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{T_b}} \cos(2\pi f_c t), \quad 0 \leq t \leq T_b$$

The two BPSK signals in terms of this basis are:

$$s_1(t) = +\sqrt{E_b}\,\phi_1(t) = +\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}} \cos(2\pi f_c t) \quad \text{(for bit '0')}$$

$$s_2(t) = -\sqrt{E_b}\,\phi_1(t) = -\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}} \cos(2\pi f_c t) \quad \text{(for bit '1')}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why only one basis function?** Since BPSK signals are negatives of each other, they lie on a one-dimensional line in signal space. The basis function $\phi_1(t)$ captures all the "information-bearing" variation; the orthogonal component contains no information and is discarded.

### 2.2 Coherent BPSK Receiver Block Logic

The detection of BPSK requires a **coherent (synchronous) receiver** because the receiver must know the exact phase reference of the carrier to distinguish between the 0° and 180° phase states. The detection process follows these structured steps:

1. **Step 1 — Front-end Filtering:** The received noisy signal $r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t)$ is bandpass filtered to remove out-of-band noise.
2. **Step 2 — Coherent Multiplication:** $r(t)$ is multiplied by a locally generated carrier $\sqrt{2/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ that is **phase-synchronized** with the transmitter.
3. **Step 3 — Integration over Bit Duration:** The product is integrated over one bit period $T_b$ using an integrator (low-pass filter equivalent).
4. **Step 4 — Sampling:** The integrator output is sampled at $t = T_b$ to obtain a single decision variable.
5. **Step 5 — Threshold Comparison:** The decision variable is compared to a **threshold of zero**:
   - If output $\geq 0$ → decide **"0"**
   - If output $< 0$ → decide **"1"**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Boundary:** For equiprobable BPSK symbols with equal energy, the optimal decision boundary is precisely at the **origin** of the I-axis, equidistant from both constellation points. This is a **maximum likelihood (ML) detector** under the assumption of AWGN.

### 2.3 Bit Error Probability Derivation Logic

The probability of bit error in BPSK under AWGN is derived by considering the noise distribution at the decision device output. The received signal is:

$$r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t)$$

where $n(t)$ is AWGN with two-sided PSD $N_0/2$. After correlation with $\phi_1(t)$, the decision variable becomes:

$$r_1 = \int_0^{T_b} r(t)\,\phi_1(t)\,dt = \pm\sqrt{E_b} + n_1$$

The noise component $n_1$ is a zero-mean Gaussian random variable with variance:

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{N_0}{2}$$

The probability of error is then the probability that noise causes the decision variable to cross the zero threshold. For a transmitted symbol $s_1 = +\sqrt{E_b}$, error occurs when $n_1 < -\sqrt{E_b}$:

$$P_e = P(n_1 < -\sqrt{E_b}) = Q\left(\frac{\sqrt{E_b}}{\sigma}\right) = Q\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Expression | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | BPSK Signal (general) | $s(t) = \pm\sqrt{2E_b/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Volts | Two phase states only |
| 2 | Energy per bit | $E_b = P \cdot T_b$ | Joules | Fundamental energy unit |
| 3 | Basis function | $\phi_1(t) = \sqrt{2/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | 1/√s | Normalized: $\int_0^{T_b}\phi_1^2 dt = 1$ |
| 4 | Constellation point 1 | $s_1 = (+\sqrt{E_b}, 0)$ | √J | Binary "0" |
| 5 | Constellation point 2 | $s_2 = (-\sqrt{E_b}, 0)$ | √J | Binary "1" |
| 6 | Euclidean distance | $d_{\min} = 2\sqrt{E_b}$ | √J | Determines noise immunity |
| 7 | Bit Error Rate (BER) | $P_e = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ | Dimensionless | Best among binary schemes |
| 8 | Bandwidth (null-to-null) | $B = 2R_b = 2/T_b$ | Hz | Main lobe width |
| 9 | Spectral efficiency | $\eta = R_b/B = 0.5$ | bits/s/Hz | Lowest among PSK |
| 10 | Q-function definition | $Q(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_x^{\infty} e^{-u^2/2}\,du$ | — | Standard Gaussian tail |

> [!WARNING]
> **Vertical Bar Rule in Tables:** All absolute values and norms use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\Vert \cdot \Vert$ notation. In KTU exam scripts, students must clearly distinguish between $E_b$ (energy) and $E_b/N_0$ (normalized SNR, dimensionless — expressed in dB).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

BPSK is the modulation scheme of choice in applications demanding **maximum reliability over speed**:

- **Satellite Communication Systems:** Downlink telemetry from deep-space probes (Voyager, Cassini) uses BPSK because link margins are extremely tight.
- **GPS Navigation:** The coarse/acquisition (C/A) code on the L1 carrier uses BPSK modulation at 1.023 Mcps.
- **RFID and NFC Systems:** Contactless smart cards use BPSK for their reader-to-tag return link.
- **Biomedical Telemetry:** Pacemakers and implantable sensors use BPSK for ultra-low-power, high-reliability transmission.
- **Aerospace Command Links:** Spacecraft command channels employ BPSK to ensure bit-perfect execution of critical instructions.
- **Wi-Fi Legacy Modes:** The original IEEE 802.11 DSSS standard used BPSK at 1 Mbps for the most robust link.
- **Digital Audio Broadcasting (DAB):** Satellite DAB uses BPSK for its main service channel.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: BPSK Transmitted Signals from Binary Data

**Problem Setup:** Given a binary data stream $b_k \in \{0, 1\}$ at bit rate $R_b = 1/T_b$, derive the mathematical expression for the BPSK modulated carrier.

**Step 1 — Mapping bits to voltage levels:**

Define the mapping from binary bits to amplitude levels $a_k$:

$$a_k = \begin{cases} +1 & \text{if } b_k = 0 \\ -1 & \text{if } b_k = 1 \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Defining the carrier signal:**

The unmodulated carrier is:

$$c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi_0)$$

For simplicity, set the initial phase $\phi_0 = 0$:

$$c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 3 — Applying BPSK modulation:**

The BPSK signal modulates the *phase* of this carrier by multiplying it with the bipolar amplitude $a_k$:

$$s(t) = a_k \cdot A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

For one bit interval $kT_b \leq t \leq (k+1)T_b$:

$$s(t) = \begin{cases} +A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t) & \text{for } b_k = 0 \\ -A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t) & \text{for } b_k = 1 \end{cases}$$

**Step 4 — Normalizing in terms of energy per bit:**

The energy of the signal over one bit period is:

$$E_b = \int_0^{T_b} A_c^2 \cos^2(2\pi f_c t)\,dt = \frac{A_c^2 T_b}{2}$$

(for $f_c T_b \gg 1$, the cross-term averages to zero)

Solving for $A_c$:

$$A_c = \sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}}$$

**Step 5 — Final BPSK expression:**

$$\boxed{s(t) = a_k \sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}} \cos(2\pi f_c t), \quad kT_b \leq t \leq (k+1)T_b}$$

**Conversion Logic:** The amplitude factor $\sqrt{2E_b/T_b}$ is chosen so that the energy of $s(t)$ over $T_b$ equals exactly $E_b$, regardless of the data bit. This normalization is essential for fair comparison of energy across different modulation schemes.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Bit Error Probability for BPSK

**Problem Setup:** Derive the expression for $P_e$ of BPSK over an AWGN channel with PSD $N_0/2$.

**Step 1 — Received signal model:**

The received signal at the input of the correlator receiver is:

$$r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t), \quad 0 \leq t \leq T_b$$

where $n(t)$ is AWGN with two-sided power spectral density $N_0/2$ and $s_i(t)$ is either $s_1(t)$ or $s_2(t)$.

**Step 2 — Correlator output:**

The correlator multiplies $r(t)$ by the basis function $\phi_1(t) = \sqrt{2/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and integrates over $T_b$:

$$r_1 = \int_0^{T_b} r(t)\,\phi_1(t)\,dt = \int_0^{T_b} s_i(t)\,\phi_1(t)\,dt + \int_0^{T_b} n(t)\,\phi_1(t)\,dt$$

**Step 3 — Signal component evaluation:**

For transmitted signal $s_1(t) = +\sqrt{2E_b/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_c t) = +\sqrt{E_b}\,\phi_1(t)$:

$$\int_0^{T_b} s_1(t)\,\phi_1(t)\,dt = \sqrt{E_b}\int_0^{T_b}\phi_1^2(t)\,dt = \sqrt{E_b}\cdot 1 = \sqrt{E_b}$$

Similarly, for $s_2(t)$: the result is $-\sqrt{E_b}$.

**Step 4 — Noise component evaluation:**

The noise at the correlator output is:

$$n_1 = \int_0^{T_b} n(t)\,\phi_1(t)\,dt$$

This is a Gaussian random variable with mean zero (since AWGN has zero mean) and variance:

$$\sigma_{n_1}^2 = E\{n_1^2\} = \int_0^{T_b}\int_0^{T_b} E\{n(t)n(\tau)\}\,\phi_1(t)\phi_1(\tau)\,dt\,d\tau$$

Since $E\{n(t)n(\tau)\} = (N_0/2)\delta(t-\tau)$:

$$\sigma_{n_1}^2 = \frac{N_0}{2}\int_0^{T_b}\phi_1^2(t)\,dt = \frac{N_0}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Decision variable:**

$$r_1 = \pm\sqrt{E_b} + n_1, \quad \text{where } n_1 \sim \mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2)$$

**Step 6 — Error probability calculation:**

Assume $s_1(t) = +\sqrt{E_b}\,\phi_1(t)$ was transmitted. An error occurs if $r_1 < 0$:

$$P(e \mid s_1) = P(n_1 < -\sqrt{E_b}) = P\left(\frac{n_1}{\sqrt{N_0/2}} < -\frac{\sqrt{E_b}}{\sqrt{N_0/2}}\right)$$

Defining the standard Gaussian variable $Z = n_1/\sqrt{N_0/2} \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$:

$$P(e \mid s_1) = P\left(Z < -\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right) = Q\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

By symmetry, $P(e \mid s_2) = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ as well. For equiprobable bits:

$$\boxed{P_e = Q\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)}$$

**Conversion Logic:** The Q-function $Q(x)$ represents the area under the tail of a standard Gaussian PDF. The argument $\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}$ is the **distance from the mean to the decision boundary**, measured in units of noise standard deviation. A larger $E_b/N_0$ means the signal point is farther from the threshold, hence lower error probability.

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: BPSK Bandwidth

**Step 1 — Power Spectral Density of BPSK:**

The PSD of a BPSK signal (with rectangular NRZ pulse shaping) is:

$$S_{BPSK}(f) = \frac{E_b}{2}\left[\text{sinc}^2\left((f - f_c)T_b\right) + \text{sinc}^2\left((f + f_c)T_b\right)\right]$$

where $\text{sinc}(x) = \sin(\pi x)/(\pi x)$.

**Step 2 — Identifying spectral nulls:**

The sinc function has its first null at frequency offset $\pm 1/T_b$ from the carrier. Therefore, the main lobe of the BPSK spectrum extends from $f_c - R_b$ to $f_c + R_b$.

**Step 3 — Null-to-null bandwidth:**

$$B_{null\text{-}to\text{-}null} = 2R_b = \frac{2}{T_b}$$

**Step 4 — Spectral efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{R_b}{B} = \frac{R_b}{2R_b} = 0.5 \text{ bits/sec/Hz}$$

**Conversion Logic:** BPSK is the most bandwidth-inefficient of all PSK schemes because it transmits only 1 bit per symbol. Higher-order schemes like QPSK (2 bits/symbol) and 16-PSK (4 bits/symbol) achieve better spectral efficiency at the cost of reduced noise immunity.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: BPSK vs Other Binary Schemes

| Parameter | BPSK | BFSK (coherent) | BFSK (non-coherent) | DPSK |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Modulation parameter | Phase | Frequency | Frequency | Phase difference |
| BER formula | $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ | $Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ | $\frac{1}{2}e^{-E_b/2N_0}$ | $\frac{1}{2}e^{-E_b/N_0}$ |
| Noise immunity | **Best** | Moderate | Worst | Moderate |
| Bandwidth | $2R_b$ | $\geq 2R_b$ | $\geq 2R_b$ | $2R_b$ |
| Receiver complexity | High (coherent) | High (coherent) | Low (envelope) | Moderate (differential) |
| Synchronization required | **Yes (carrier)** | Yes/No | No | No (symbol timing) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 BPSK Transmitter Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Binary Data Source: 0 1 0 1 1 0] --> nodeB[NRZ Encoder: Maps bits to +1 or -1]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Oscillator: cos of 2 pi fc t]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Multiplier: Product Modulator]
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE[Bandpass Filter: Centered at fc]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Channel: AWGN with PSD N0 over 2]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Received Signal r of t]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram Interpretation:** The binary data first passes through an **NRZ encoder** that converts logical bits to bipolar voltages ($0 \rightarrow +1$ V, $1 \rightarrow -1$ V). These voltages modulate a carrier oscillator in a **product modulator (multiplier)**. The output is filtered to remove harmonics and transmitted over the channel. AWGN noise $n(t)$ is added at the channel output.

### 4.2 BPSK Coherent Receiver Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Received Signal r of t] --> nodeB[Bandpass Filter fc bandwidth 2R]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Multiplier: Coherent Demodulator]
    nodeD[Local Oscillator: cos of 2 pi fc t] --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeE[Integrator over Tb]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Sampler at t equal Tb]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Threshold Detector: Compare to 0]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Decision Output: Binary 0 or 1]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Block — Local Oscillator Synchronization:** The local oscillator at the receiver **must** be phase-coherent with the transmitter carrier. Any phase error $\Delta\phi$ causes the decision variable to be reduced by $\cos(\Delta\phi)$, which degrades the effective SNR. This is why practical BPSK systems use **Costas loops** or **PLL-based carrier recovery** circuits.

### 4.3 BPSK Signal Constellation Diagram (I-Q Plane)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Constellation[BPSK Signal Constellation Diagram]
        direction LR
        nodeA[Quadrature Axis Q] --- nodeB[Origin: 0, 0 Decision Boundary]
        nodeB --- nodeC[In Phase Axis I]
    end
    node1[Symbol s1 at plus root Eb, 0: Binary 0] --> Constellation
    node2[Symbol s2 at minus root Eb, 0: Binary 1] --> Constellation
    node3[Euclidean Distance d equal 2 root Eb] --> Constellation
    node4[Threshold: Vertical Line at I equal 0] --> Constellation
```

**Textual Representation of the Constellation (for drawing in exams):**

- **X-axis (In-Phase / I-axis):** Labeled "I" with units of $\sqrt{E_b}$
- **Y-axis (Quadrature / Q-axis):** Labeled "Q" with units of $\sqrt{E_b}$
- **Point 1:** Located at $(+\sqrt{E_b}, 0)$ — labeled "**0**" (binary zero)
- **Point 2:** Located at $(-\sqrt{E_b}, 0)$ — labeled "**1**" (binary one)
- **Decision Boundary:** Vertical line through the origin (the I = 0 axis)
- **Distance:** Mark the distance $d = 2\sqrt{E_b}$ between the two points
- **Noise Circles:** Draw dashed circles of radius $r$ around each point to show the uncertainty region caused by noise (optional but excellent for full marks)

### 4.4 BPSK Waveform and Phasor Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph waveform[BPSK Time Domain Waveform]
        node1[Bit 0: Positive Half Cycle: 0 degrees] --> node2[Bit 1: Negative Half Cycle: 180 degrees]
        node2 --> node3[Bit 0: Positive Half Cycle: 0 degrees]
        node3 --> node4[Bit 1: Negative Half Cycle: 180 degrees]
    end
    subgraph phasor[Phasor Representation]
        node5[Bit 0: Phasor Points Right: 0 degrees] --> node6[Bit 1: Phasor Points Left: 180 degrees]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Waveform Key:** When a bit "0" is transmitted, the carrier appears as a normal cosine wave. When a bit "1" is transmitted, the carrier undergoes a **180° phase reversal** — it appears as an inverted cosine. The transitions are abrupt at bit boundaries.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define BPSK. Draw the signal constellation diagram and explain how bits are mapped to symbols.**

**Model Answer:**

**Binary Phase Shift Keying (BPSK)** is a digital modulation technique in which the phase of a high-frequency carrier is shifted between two values, **0° and 180°**, to represent binary digits "0" and "1" respectively. The amplitude and frequency of the carrier remain constant; only the phase carries the information.

The BPSK signal is given by:

$$s(t) = \pm\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t), \quad 0 \leq t \leq T_b$$

**Constellation Diagram:**

- **Symbol for "0":** $s_1 = (+\sqrt{E_b}, 0)$ on the I-axis
- **Symbol for "1":** $s_2 = (-\sqrt{E_b}, 0)$ on the I-axis
- **Decision boundary:** Vertical line at $I = 0$

```
           Q
           |
           |
    ───────●───────► I
          /|\
         / | \
       s2  0  s1
   (-√E)    (+√E)
```

The Euclidean distance between the two points is $d = 2\sqrt{E_b}$. Bit mapping: bit "0" is mapped to a positive-amplitude carrier (0° phase), and bit "1" is mapped to a phase-reversed (180°) carrier. **(3 marks)**

---

**Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Write the expression for the bit error probability of BPSK in an AWGN channel. State the relationship between $E_b/N_0$ in linear and dB scales.**

**Model Answer:**

The bit error probability of BPSK over an AWGN channel is:

$$P_e = Q\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

where:
- $E_b$ is the energy per bit (in Joules)
- $N_0$ is the single-sided AWGN power spectral density (in W/Hz)
- $Q(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_x^\infty e^{-u^2/2}du$ is the Gaussian Q-function

**Conversion to dB:**

$$\left(\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right)_{dB} = 10\log_{10}\left(\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right)$$

For example, $E_b/N_0 = 1$ (linear) corresponds to **0 dB**, and $E_b/N_0 = 10$ (linear) corresponds to **10 dB**.

**Numerical Example:** For $E_b/N_0 = 9$ (linear, i.e., 9.54 dB):

$$P_e = Q(\sqrt{18}) = Q(4.24) \approx 1.1 \times 10^{-5}$$

This shows that BPSK achieves very low error rates even at moderate SNRs. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the generation and detection of a BPSK signal. Discuss the role of coherent detection. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A BPSK system operates at a bit rate of $R_b = 1$ Mbps over an AWGN channel with $N_0 = 10^{-9}$ W/Hz. The received signal power is $P_r = 10$ μW. Calculate:
  (i) The energy per bit $E_b$
  (ii) The value of $E_b/N_0$ in dB
  (iii) The bit error probability $P_e$ **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) Generation and Detection of BPSK (7 marks):**

**Generation (BPSK Transmitter):**

The BPSK transmitter consists of the following blocks:

1. **Binary Data Source:** Produces a stream of independent bits $\{b_k\}$ at rate $R_b$.
2. **NRZ Level Converter:** Maps binary "0" to voltage $+V$ and binary "1" to voltage $-V$ (bipolar signaling).
3. **Carrier Oscillator:** Generates $c(t) = A\cos(2\pi f_c t)$.
4. **Product Modulator (Multiplier):** The bipolar data multiplies the carrier, producing:
   $$s(t) = \pm A\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$
5. **Bandpass Filter:** Limits the transmitted spectrum to the main lobe.

**Detection (BPSK Coherent Receiver):**

1. **Input BPF:** Filters out-of-band noise centered at $f_c$.
2. **Multiplier (Coherent Detector):** Multiplies $r(t)$ with locally generated $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$.
3. **Integrator:** Integrates the product over one bit period $T_b$:
   $$y = \int_0^{T_b} r(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)\,dt$$
4. **Sampler:** Samples $y$ at $t = T_b$.
5. **Threshold Detector:** Compares sample to zero threshold and outputs the bit decision.

**Role of Coherent Detection:**

Coherent detection requires the receiver's local oscillator to be **synchronized in phase and frequency** with the incoming carrier. This is essential because:

- BPSK encodes information in the *phase* of the carrier, not its amplitude or frequency.
- Without phase coherence, the receiver cannot distinguish between the 0° and 180° phase states.
- Coherent detection provides **3 dB SNR gain** over non-coherent (envelope) detection.
- It enables optimum (minimum error probability) ML detection under AWGN.

**Block Diagram (Refer Section 4.1 and 4.2 above for Mermaid).** **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $R_b = 1$ Mbps = $10^6$ bits/s
- $N_0 = 10^{-9}$ W/Hz
- $P_r = 10$ μW = $10 \times 10^{-6}$ W = $10^{-5}$ W

**(i) Energy per bit $E_b$:**

The energy per bit is the received power multiplied by the bit duration:

$$T_b = \frac{1}{R_b} = \frac{1}{10^6} = 10^{-6} \text{ s}$$

$$E_b = P_r \times T_b = 10^{-5} \times 10^{-6} = 10^{-11} \text{ J}$$

$$\boxed{E_b = 10^{-11} \text{ Joules}}$$ **(2 marks)**

**(ii) $E_b/N_0$ in dB:**

$$\frac{E_b}{N_0} = \frac{10^{-11}}{10^{-9}} = 10^{-2} = 0.01 \text{ (linear)}$$

In dB:

$$\left(\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right)_{dB} = 10\log_{10}(0.01) = 10 \times (-2) = -20 \text{ dB}$$

$$\boxed{(E_b/N_0)_{dB} = -20 \text{ dB}}$$ **(2 marks)**

**(iii) Bit Error Probability $P_e$:**

$$P_e = Q\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right) = Q\left(\sqrt{2 \times 0.01}\right) = Q(\sqrt{0.02}) = Q(0.1414)$$

Using the standard Q-function table or approximation:

$$Q(0.1414) \approx 0.4438$$

$$\boxed{P_e \approx 0.444 \text{ (or about } 44.4\%)}$$

**Interpretation:** At $-20$ dB SNR, the BPSK system has a very high error rate because the noise power is 100 times the signal energy per bit. This SNR is well below the typical operating range of BPSK (usually $E_b/N_0 \geq 6$ dB for acceptable performance). **(3 marks)**

---

**Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the probability of error of BPSK in the presence of AWGN. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the BPSK signal constellation diagram. Compare BPSK with BFSK in terms of bandwidth, BER, and noise immunity. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) Derivation of BPSK Error Probability (7 marks):**

**Assumptions:**
1. The channel is **AWGN** (Additive White Gaussian Noise) with two-sided PSD $N_0/2$.
2. The carrier at the receiver is **perfectly coherent** (zero phase error).
3. Binary symbols "0" and "1" are **equiprobable** ($P(0) = P(1) = 1/2$).
4. The receiver uses an **optimal correlator (matched filter)** detector.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** The received signal is:

$$r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t), \quad i = 1, 2; \quad 0 \leq t \leq T_b$$

**Step 2:** The correlator computes:

$$r_1 = \int_0^{T_b} r(t)\,\phi_1(t)\,dt = s_{i1} + n_1$$

where $\phi_1(t) = \sqrt{2/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ is the orthonormal basis function.

**Step 3:** Signal components:

$$s_{11} = +\sqrt{E_b} \quad (\text{when } s_1 \text{ transmitted})$$
$$s_{21} = -\sqrt{E_b} \quad (\text{when } s_2 \text{ transmitted})$$

**Step 4:** Noise component $n_1$ is Gaussian with mean zero and variance $\sigma^2 = N_0/2$:

$$n_1 \sim \mathcal{N}\left(0, \frac{N_0}{2}\right)$$

**Step 5:** The decision variable is:

$$r_1 = \pm\sqrt{E_b} + n_1$$

**Step 6:** Assuming $s_1$ is transmitted, error occurs when $r_1 < 0$:

$$P(e \mid s_1) = P\left(n_1 < -\sqrt{E_b}\right) = \int_{-\infty}^{-\sqrt{E_b}} \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi N_0}} e^{-u^2/N_0}\,du$$

**Step 7:** Substituting $u = \sqrt{N_0/2}\,v$:

$$P(e \mid s_1) = \int_{-\infty}^{-\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-v^2/2}\,dv = Q\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

**Step 8:** By symmetry, $P(e \mid s_2) = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$.

**Step 9:** Average probability of error:

$$P_e = P(s_1)P(e\mid s_1) + P(s_2)P(e\mid s_2) = \frac{1}{2}Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}) + \frac{1}{2}Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$$

$$\boxed{P_e = Q\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)}$$ **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) BPSK Constellation and Comparison with BFSK (7 marks):**

**BPSK Signal Constellation:**

The BPSK constellation has two points located on the in-phase axis at $(\pm\sqrt{E_b}, 0)$. The two signals are antipodal (negatives of each other), which is the optimal binary constellation in terms of minimizing error probability for a given energy.

**Key Features:**
- **Constellation points:** Only 2, on the I-axis
- **Distance between points:** $d = 2\sqrt{E_b}$
- **Decision boundary:** Vertical line at $I = 0$
- **Signal energy:** $E_s = E_b$ (same as energy per bit since 1 bit/symbol)

**Comparison Table: BPSK vs BFSK:**

| Parameter | BPSK | BFSK (Coherent) | BFSK (Non-coherent) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Modulated parameter | Phase | Frequency | Frequency |
| BER formula | $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ | $Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ | $\frac{1}{2}e^{-E_b/2N_0}$ |
| Noise immunity | **Highest** | Moderate | Lowest |
| Bandwidth | $2R_b$ | $\geq 2R_b$ | $\geq 2R_b$ |
| Bandwidth efficiency | 0.5 bits/s/Hz | $\leq 0.5$ bits/s/Hz | $\leq 0.5$ bits/s/Hz |
| Power efficiency | **Best** | 3 dB worse | Worst |
| Receiver complexity | High (coherent) | High (coherent) | Low (envelope) |
| Synchronization | Carrier sync required | Frequency sync only | None |

**Conclusion:** BPSK is the **most power-efficient** binary modulation scheme (lowest $P_e$ for given $E_b/N_0$), but it requires complex coherent detection. BFSK is simpler to detect (especially non-coherent), but pays a penalty in error performance. The choice depends on the application's power-vs-complexity trade-off. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing $E_b$ and $E_s$:** $E_b$ is energy **per bit**, $E_s$ is energy **per symbol**. For BPSK, $E_b = E_s$ (1 bit per symbol), but for QPSK, $E_s = 2E_b$. KTU examiners deduct marks for this confusion.
> 
> 2. **Q-function vs erf:** $Q(x) = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(x/\sqrt{2})$. Many KTU problems give BER in terms of erfc; students must convert correctly.
> 
> 3. **Phase error in constellation:** If the local oscillator has phase error $\phi$, the constellation rotates by $\phi$. Always assume **zero phase error** unless stated.
> 
> 4. **Decision threshold direction:** With mapping $0 \rightarrow +\sqrt{E_b}$ and $1 \rightarrow -\sqrt{E_b}$, the decision rule is: output "0" if $r \geq 0$, output "1" if $r < 0$. Inverting this rule costs 1 mark.
> 
> 5. **PSD units:** $N_0$ is **single-sided** (W/Hz), $N_0/2$ is **two-sided**. The formula $P_e = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ uses single-sided $N_0$. Using $N_0/2$ gives the wrong answer.
> 
> 6. **Drawing the constellation:** Always label both axes, the constellation points with their coordinates, and the decision boundary. Skipping labels costs up to 2 marks in KTU board exams.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for BPSK:**

- ✅ BPSK uses **two phase states**: 0° and 180° (antipodal signaling)
- ✅ Signal expression: $s(t) = \pm\sqrt{2E_b/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$
- ✅ **Constellation:** 2 points at $(\pm\sqrt{E_b}, 0)$ on the I-axis
- ✅ **Bit error rate:** $P_e = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ — **best among binary schemes**
- ✅ **Bandwidth (null-to-null):** $B = 2R_b = 2/T_b$
- ✅ **Spectral efficiency:** $\eta = 0.5$ bits/s/Hz — **lowest among PSK**
- ✅ **Receiver:** Requires **coherent (synchronous) detection** with carrier recovery
- ✅ **Decision threshold:** Zero (mid-point between the two constellation points)
- ✅ **Euclidean distance:** $d_{\min} = 2\sqrt{E_b}$ — larger distance means better noise immunity
- ✅ **Basis function:** $\phi_1(t) = \sqrt{2/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ — only **one** basis function needed
- ✅ **Energy per bit:** $E_b = P \cdot T_b = P/R_b$
- ✅ **Conversion:** $(E_b/N_0)_{dB} = 10\log_{10}(E_b/N_0)$
- ✅ **Real-world use:** GPS C/A code, satellite telemetry, deep-space communication, RFID
- ✅ **Key trade-off:** BPSK has **maximum power efficiency** but **minimum bandwidth efficiency**
- ✅ **Common confusion:** BPSK ≠ QPSK. QPSK carries 2 bits/symbol using 4 phases; BPSK carries 1 bit/symbol using 2 phases.
- ✅ **Q-function properties:** $Q(0) = 0.5$, $Q(\infty) = 0$, $Q(-x) = 1 - Q(x)$
- ✅ **Application threshold:** For $P_e \leq 10^{-5}$, BPSK requires $E_b/N_0 \approx 9.6$ dB
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT502 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** BPSK Transmitter and Receiver ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Binary Phase Shift Keying (BPSK)** is a fundamental digital modulation scheme in which the **phase** of a sinusoidal carrier signal is shifted between two discrete values, typically **0° and 180°**, to represent the binary information bits `0` and `1` respectively. BPSK is also known as **Phase Reversal Keying (PRK)** or **2-PSK**, and it is the most robust digital modulation scheme against noise due to the maximum separation between its signal points in the signal space constellation. Mathematically, the BPSK modulated signal is represented as: $$ s(t) = \begin{cases} \sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{T_b}} \cos(2\pi f_c t) & \text{for binary '1' (bit 1)} \\[6pt] -\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{T_b}} \cos(2\pi f_c t) & \text{for binary '0' (bit 0)} \end{cases} $$ where $E_b$ is the energy per bit, $T_b$ is the bit duration, and $f_c$ is the carrier frequency. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** BPSK is a *linear modulation scheme* belonging to the family of M-ary Phase Shift Keying (M-PSK) where M = 2. It is classified under **baseband-to-passband mapping techniques** in the KTU Module 4 syllabus. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **rotating ceiling fan** in your room: - When the fan rotates in the **clockwise direction**, it represents binary `1`. - When the fan suddenly reverses and rotates in the **counter-clockwise direction**, it represents binary `0`. - The **speed** of rotation remains the same (this is the carrier frequency $f_c$), but the **direction of rotation** (phase) flips between two opposite states. Just like the fan's reversal is easily detectable even in a noisy environment (because clockwise and counter-clockwise are completely opposite), BPSK uses **opposite phases (0° and 180°)** which are maximally separated. This is why BPSK is the **most noise-immune** digital modulation scheme. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Characteristics of BPSK:** > - Number of symbols: **M = 2** > - Bits per symbol: **k = log₂(2) = 1** > - Spectral efficiency: **1 bit/sec/Hz** (theoretical maximum) > - Minimum theoretical BER: **0.5 × erfc(√E_b/N₀)** > - Bandwidth requirement: **2 × bit rate** (for raised cosine filtering) --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Principle — The 'Why' and 'How' The fundamental operating principle of BPSK involves three stages: **Stage 1 — Source-to-Symbol Mapping:** A binary data stream $b(t) \in \{0, 1\}$ is mapped to a bipolar signal $d(t) \in \{+1, -1\}$. This is called **Unipolar-to-Bipolar Conversion** and is critical because it ensures that the carrier is multiplied by a symmetric signal centered at zero. **Stage 2 — Carrier Modulation:** The bipolar baseband signal $d(t)$ is multiplied by a sinusoidal carrier $\sqrt{2E_b/T_b} \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ using a **product modulator** (balanced modulator). **Stage 3 — Phase Reversal Mechanism:** - When bit = `1` → $d(t) = +1$ → Carrier passes unchanged → Phase = 0° - When bit = `0` → $d(t) = -1$ → Carrier inverted → Phase = 180° (π radians) ### 2.2 BPSK Transmitter Block Diagram — Functional Stages The BPSK transmitter consists of the following cascaded blocks: | Stage | Block Name | Function | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Source Encoder** | Generates random binary data | | 2 | **NRZ Encoder (Unipolar-to-Bipolar)** | Converts `0,1` → `-1,+1` | | 3 | **Oscillator (Carrier Generator)** | Produces $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | | 4 | **Balanced Modulator (Multiplier)** | Multiplies NRZ signal with carrier | | 5 | **Bandpass Filter (BPF)** | Removes harmonics and shapes spectrum | | 6 | **Power Amplifier (PA)** | Boosts signal for transmission | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Key Concept:** The **NRZ (Non-Return-to-Zero) bipolar encoding** is mandatory for BPSK generation. Using unipolar encoding (0 to +1) will introduce a DC offset, causing severe spectral distortion and inefficient transmission. ### 2.3 BPSK Receiver — Coherent Detection Principle The BPSK receiver uses **synchronous (coherent) detection**, which requires a **perfectly synchronized local carrier** at the receiver. The receiver stages are: | Stage | Block Name | Function | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Bandpass Filter (BPF)** | Limits noise bandwidth | | 2 | **Multiplier (Product Detector)** | Multiplies incoming signal with locally generated carrier | | 3 | **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** | Extracts baseband signal (removes 2$f_c$ component) | | 4 | **Integrator / Matched Filter** | Integrates over one bit period $T_b$ | | 5 | **Decision Device (Threshold Detector)** | Compares with threshold (typically `0`) | | 6 | **Synchronization Circuit** | Provides carrier recovery and bit timing | **Decision Rule:** - If output $V \geq 0$ → Decide bit = `1` - If output $V < 0$ → Decide bit = `0` ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|---|---|---| | BPSK Signal | $s_i(t) = \sqrt{2E_b/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_c t + \theta_i)$ | Volts | $\theta_i \in \{0, \pi\}$ | | Carrier Frequency | $f_c \gg 1/T_b$ | Hz | Typically $f_c \geq 10/T_b$ | | Bit Energy | $E_b = P \cdot T_b$ | Joules | $P$ = signal power | | Symbol Energy | $E_s = E_b \cdot \log_2 M$ | Joules | For BPSK, $E_s = E_b$ | | Bandwidth (Nyquist) | $B = R_b$ | Hz | Theoretical minimum | | Bandwidth (Practical) | $B = 2R_b$ | Hz | Using raised cosine with roll-off = 1 | | BER (Coherent BPSK) | $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{E_b/N_0}\right)$ | Dimensionless | $Q$ = Q-function | | Euclidean Distance | $d = 2\sqrt{E_b}$ | Volts | Distance between two signal points | | Spectral Efficiency | $\eta = 1 \text{ bit/s/Hz}$ | bits/s/Hz | Maximum for BPSK | | Carrier Phase Offset | $\Delta\phi$ | radians | Causes BER degradation | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Constants Used:** > - Boltzmann's constant $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K > - Noise Power Spectral Density $N_0/2 = kT$ (two-sided) > - Q-function: $Q(x) = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(x/\sqrt{2})$ > - $\text{erfc}(0) = 1$, $\text{erfc}(\infty) = 0$ ### 2.5 Real-World Utility of BPSK BPSK is extensively used in: - **Satellite communication systems** (e.g., DVB-S2 standards) - **Deep space telemetry** (NASA, ISRO missions) - **RFID tag-to-reader backscatter communication** - **GPS C/A code transmission** (uses BPSK modulation) - **Wi-Fi legacy modes** (802.11b DSSS) - **Aeronautical telemetry** and military secure links The dominance of BPSK in these applications stems from its **superior BER performance** under severe noise conditions. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Mathematical Expression of BPSK Signal **Given:** - Binary data $b(t) \in \{0, 1\}$ with bit duration $T_b$ - Carrier signal $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ **Step 1:** Convert unipolar binary $b(t) \in \{0,1\}$ to bipolar NRZ signal $d(t) \in \{-1, +1\}$ using the transformation: $$ d(t) = 2b(t) - 1 $$ This ensures $b(t) = 1 \rightarrow d(t) = +1$ and $b(t) = 0 \rightarrow d(t) = -1$. **Step 2:** Multiply the bipolar signal $d(t)$ with the carrier. The modulated signal becomes: $$ s(t) = d(t) \cdot A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) $$ **Step 3:** Substitute both possibilities. For bit `1` ($d(t) = +1$): $$ s_1(t) = +A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) $$ For bit `0` ($d(t) = -1$): $$ s_0(t) = -A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t + \pi) $$ **Step 4:** Normalize amplitude using energy per bit. The amplitude is set such that the signal energy over $T_b$ equals $E_b$: $$ \int_0^{T_b} s_i^2(t)\, dt = E_b $$ Evaluating: $$ \int_0^{T_b} A_c^2 \cos^2(2\pi f_c t)\, dt = \frac{A_c^2 T_b}{2} = E_b $$ Solving for $A_c$: $$ A_c = \sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}} $$ **Step 5:** Final compact representation: $$ s(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}} \cdot d(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t), \quad d(t) \in \{+1, -1\} $$ ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Coherent BPSK Demodulation (Receiver Output) **Given:** Received signal corrupted by AWGN: $$ r(t) = s(t) + n(t) $$ where $n(t)$ is zero-mean AWGN with PSD $N_0/2$. **Step 1:** Multiplication with locally generated carrier (assuming perfect synchronization): $$ r(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t) = s(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) + n(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) $$ **Step 2:** Substitute $s(t) = \sqrt{2E_b/T_b} \cdot d(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t)$: $$ = \sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}} \cdot d(t) \cdot \cos^2(2\pi f_c t) + n(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) $$ **Step 3:** Apply the trigonometric identity $\cos^2(\theta) = \frac{1 + \cos(2\theta)}{2}$: $$ = \sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}} \cdot d(t) \cdot \frac{1 + \cos(4\pi f_c t)}{2} + n(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) $$ $$ = \sqrt{\frac{E_b}{2T_b}} \cdot d(t) + \sqrt{\frac{E_b}{2T_b}} \cdot d(t)\cos(4\pi f_c t) + n(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) $$ **Step 4:** Pass through Low-Pass Filter (LPF) to remove the $2f_c$ harmonic: $$ y(t) = \sqrt{\frac{E_b}{2T_b}} \cdot d(t) + n_c(t) $$ where $n_c(t)$ is the filtered noise component with variance $\sigma^2 = N_0/4$. **Step 5:** Integrate over one bit period $T_b$: $$ V = \int_0^{T_b} y(t)\, dt = \sqrt{\frac{E_b}{2T_b}} \cdot d(t) \cdot T_b + \int_0^{T_b} n_c(t)\, dt $$ $$ V = \pm\sqrt{\frac{E_b T_b}{2}} + N $$ **Step 6:** Apply decision rule: - If $V \geq 0$ → Decision: bit `1` - If $V < 0$ → Decision: bit `0` ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Bit Error Rate (BER) of BPSK **Given:** Decision variable $V = \pm\sqrt{E_bT_b/2} + N$ where $N$ is Gaussian with mean 0 and variance $N_0 T_b / 4$. **Step 1:** Define the probability of error when bit `1` is transmitted (signal level $= +\sqrt{E_b T_b/2}$): $$ P(e \mid \text{bit 1}) = P(V < 0 \mid \text{bit 1}) $$ **Step 2:** Standardize using Gaussian distribution: $$ P(e \mid \text{bit 1}) = P\!\left(N < -\sqrt{\frac{E_b T_b}{2}}\right) $$ $$ = P\!\left(\frac{N}{\sqrt{N_0 T_b/4}} < -\frac{\sqrt{E_b T_b/2}}{\sqrt{N_0 T_b/4}}\right) $$ **Step 3:** Compute the threshold: $$ \frac{\sqrt{E_b T_b/2}}{\sqrt{N_0 T_b/4}} = \frac{\sqrt{E_b T_b/2} \cdot 2}{\sqrt{N_0 T_b}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 E_b}{N_0}} $$ **Step 4:** Express as Q-function: $$ P(e \mid \text{bit 1}) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right) $$ **Step 5:** By symmetry, $P(e \mid \text{bit 0}) = P(e \mid \text{bit 1})$. Assuming equiprobable bits ($P(0) = P(1) = 1/2$): $$ P_e = \tfrac{1}{2}P(e \mid \text{bit 1}) + \tfrac{1}{2}P(e \mid \text{bit 0}) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right) $$ **Step 6:** Final compact form using complementary error function: $$ \boxed{P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right) = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)} $$ ### 3.4 Derivation 4: Spectral Density of BPSK Signal **Step 1:** Baseband NRZ bipolar signal $d(t)$ has power spectral density: $$ P_d(f) = T_b \left[\frac{\sin(\pi f T_b)}{\pi f T_b}\right]^2 $$ **Step 2:** Modulation shifts spectrum to carrier frequency: $$ S(f) = \frac{1}{4}\left[P_d(f - f_c) + P_d(f + f_c)\right] $$ **Step 3:** Total transmitted power equals average signal power: $$ P_s = \frac{A_c^2}{2} = \frac{E_b}{T_b} $$ The main lobe extends from $f_c - R_b$ to $f_c + R_b$, giving a null-to-null bandwidth of $2R_b$. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 BPSK Transmitter — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Binary Source
0 1 0 1 1 0 0 1] --> A2[NRZ Encoder
Unipolar to Bipolar
0 to -1, 1 to +1] A2 --> A3{Balanced Modulator
Multiplication} A4[Carrier Oscillator
fc = 1 MHz] --> A3 A3 --> A5[Bandpass Filter
BPF Center fc] A5 --> A6[Power Amplifier
Boost Signal] A6 --> A7[Transmit Antenna
Channel Output] A2 -.In-Phase Component.- A8[Baseband Signal
d of t: +1 -1 +1] A4 -.Reference Carrier.- A8 A8 --> A9[Phase Mapping
Bit 1: 0 degrees
Bit 0: 180 degrees] A9 --> A3 style A1 fill:#e1f5ff style A7 fill:#ffe1e1 style A3 fill:#fff4e1 style A9 fill:#e1ffe1 ``` ### 4.2 BPSK Receiver — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR R1[Receive Antenna
r of t equals s of t plus n of t] --> R2[RF Bandpass Filter
Bandwidth equals 2Rb] R2 --> R3{Correlator Detector
Multiply with local carrier} R4[Local Oscillator
Synchronized at fc] --> R3 R3 --> R5[Low Pass Filter
Removes 2fc Component] R5 --> R6[Integrate and Dump
Over Bit Period Tb] R6 --> R7{Decision Device
Threshold equals 0} R7 -->|Output greater or equal 0| R8[Decoded Bit equals 1] R7 -->|Output less than 0| R9[Decoded Bit equals 0] R10[Carrier Recovery PLL
From Received Signal] --> R4 R11[Bit Timing Recovery
From Received Signal] --> R6 style R1 fill:#ffe1e1 style R7 fill:#fff4e1 style R8 fill:#e1ffe1 style R9 fill:#e1ffe1 style R10 fill:#f0e1ff style R11 fill:#f0e1ff ``` ### 4.3 BPSK Signal Space Constellation and Phasor Representation ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Constellation_Diagram["BPSK Signal Space Constellation"] direction LR axisX["In-Phase Axis I
Real Component phi1 of t"] --> coord0["s0 equals minus sqrt Eb
Binary 0
Phase: 180 degrees
Coordinate: minus 1, 0"] coord1["s1 equals plus sqrt Eb
Binary 1
Phase: 0 degrees
Coordinate: plus 1, 0"] --> axisX end subgraph Orthogonal_Basis["Orthonormal Basis Functions"] direction TB phi1["phi1 of t equals sqrt 2 over Tb times cos 2 pi fc t
Normalized Carrier"] phi2["phi2 of t equals sqrt 2 over Tb times sin 2 pi fc t
Quadrature Carrier (Not Used)"] end Constellation_Diagram -->|"Decision Boundary at V equals 0"| Boundary["Threshold Line
Vertical axis at I equals 0"] Boundary -.Differentiates.-> coord0 Boundary -.Differentiates.-> coord1 style coord1 fill:#90EE90 style coord0 fill:#FFB6C1 style Boundary fill:#FFEB3B ``` ### 4.4 BPSK Waveform Timing Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR W1["Wave 1: Binary Input
| 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 |"] --> W2["Wave 2: NRZ Bipolar
| +1 | -1 | +1 | +1 | -1 |"] W2 --> W3["Wave 3: Carrier
Continuous sine at fc"] W3 --> W4["Wave 4: BPSK Output
Phase flips 180 degrees on bit 0"] W4 --> W5["Wave 5: Receiver Output
After LPF plus Integrator"] W5 --> W6["Wave 6: Decision Output
Threshold applied"] style W1 fill:#fff4e1 style W2 fill:#e1f5ff style W4 fill:#ffe1e1 style W6 fill:#e1ffe1 ``` ### 4.5 Comparative Signal Flow for Bit `0` vs Bit `1` | Parameter | Bit = 1 | Bit = 0 | |---|---|---| | NRZ Level | $+1$ | $-1$ | | Carrier | $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | | Modulated Phase | $0°$ | $180°$ | | Modulated Signal | $+A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | $-A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | | Constellation Point | $(+\sqrt{E_b}, 0)$ | $(-\sqrt{E_b}, 0)$ | | Decision Threshold | $V \geq 0$ | $V < 0$ | | Detector Output | Positive | Negative | --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define BPSK. What are its two possible phase values?** **Model Answer:** BPSK (Binary Phase Shift Keying) is a digital modulation technique in which the phase of a sinusoidal carrier is varied between two discrete values to represent binary data. The two possible phase values are: - **Phase 0°** representing binary `1` - **Phase 180° (π radians)** representing binary `0` The amplitude and frequency remain constant; only the phase changes. It is the most noise-immune digital modulation scheme with a BER of $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **State the expression for the probability of error of BPSK and explain why BPSK is more robust than BFSK.** **Model Answer:** The probability of error for coherent BPSK detection is: $$ P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right) $$ BPSK is more robust than BFSK because: 1. **Larger Euclidean distance**: BPSK has distance $d = 2\sqrt{E_b}$ while orthogonal BFSK has $d = \sqrt{2E_b}$, giving BPSK a 3 dB advantage. 2. **Constant envelope**: BPSK has a single carrier amplitude, making detection easier. 3. **Lower bandwidth**: BPSK requires less bandwidth ($R_b$ vs $2R_b$ for BFSK). 4. **Same noise immunity at 3 dB less power**: BPSK achieves the same BER as BFSK at half the transmit power. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a BPSK transmitter. Derive the expression for the BPSK modulated signal. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A BPSK system transmits data at a rate of 1 Mbps over a channel with $E_b/N_0 = 9$ dB. Calculate the theoretical bit error rate. **(7 Marks)** --- ##### Model Solution for Question A(a) **Block Diagram Description:** A BPSK transmitter consists of: 1. **Binary Source**: Generates random bits $b(t) \in \{0,1\}$ 2. **NRZ Encoder**: Converts unipolar `0,1` to bipolar `-1,+1` 3. **Local Oscillator**: Generates carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ 4. **Balanced Modulator (Multiplier)**: Multiplies NRZ signal with carrier 5. **Bandpass Filter**: Shapes the spectrum 6. **Power Amplifier**: Boosts the signal for transmission **Derivation:** **Step 1:** Bipolar baseband signal: $$ d(t) = 2b(t) - 1, \quad d(t) \in \{+1, -1\} $$ **Step 2:** Carrier signal normalized to energy $E_b$: $$ c(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}} \cos(2\pi f_c t) $$ **Step 3:** BPSK modulated signal: $$ \boxed{s(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}} \cdot d(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t)} $$ **Step 4:** For bit `1`: $s_1(t) = +\sqrt{2E_b/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ → Phase = 0° **Step 5:** For bit `0`: $s_0(t) = -\sqrt{2E_b/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_c t) = \sqrt{2E_b/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_c t + \pi)$ → Phase = 180° --- ##### Model Solution for Question A(b) **Given:** - Bit rate $R_b = 1$ Mbps - $E_b/N_0 = 9$ dB **Step 1:** Convert dB to linear scale: $$ \frac{E_b}{N_0} = 10^{9/10} = 10^{0.9} \approx 7.943 $$ **Step 2:** Apply BPSK BER formula: $$ P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 \times 7.943}\right) $$ $$ P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{15.886}\right) = Q(3.986) $$ **Step 3:** Evaluate Q-function value (using standard Q-table or approximation): $$ Q(3.986) \approx 3.4 \times 10^{-5} $$ **Final Answer:** $$ \boxed{P_e \approx 3.4 \times 10^{-5} \text{ (approximately 3.4 errors per 100,000 bits)}} $$ --- #### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **(a)** Explain the coherent detection of BPSK with a block diagram. Derive the expression for the output of the correlator receiver. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A BPSK signal is transmitted with carrier frequency 10 MHz and bit rate 1 Mbps. The received signal power is $-93$ dBm and the noise PSD is $N_0 = 10^{-20}$ W/Hz. Compute the probability of error. **(7 Marks)** --- ##### Model Solution for Question B(a) **Receiver Block Diagram:** The BPSK coherent receiver consists of: 1. **Receiver Filter (BPF)** → Removes out-of-band noise 2. **Multiplier** → Multiplies with locally generated carrier 3. **Local Oscillator** → Carrier Recovery PLL provides $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ 4. **Low Pass Filter (LPF)** → Removes $2f_c$ harmonic 5. **Integrator** → Integrates over $T_b$ 6. **Decision Device** → Threshold at zero **Derivation:** **Step 1:** Received signal: $r(t) = s(t) + n(t)$ **Step 2:** After multiplication with $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$: $$ r(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}} d(t) \cos^2(2\pi f_c t) + n(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) $$ **Step 3:** Using $\cos^2(\theta) = \frac{1+\cos(2\theta)}{2}$: $$ = \sqrt{\frac{E_b}{2T_b}} d(t) + \sqrt{\frac{E_b}{2T_b}} d(t)\cos(4\pi f_c t) + n(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) $$ **Step 4:** After LPF (removes $2f_c$ term): $$ y(t) = \sqrt{\frac{E_b}{2T_b}} d(t) + n_c(t) $$ **Step 5:** After integration over $T_b$: $$ \boxed{V = \pm\sqrt{\frac{E_b T_b}{2}} + N} $$ where $N$ is the integrated noise with variance $\sigma_N^2 = N_0 T_b / 4$. **Step 6:** Decision rule: bit = `1` if $V \geq 0$, else bit = `0`. --- ##### Model Solution for Question B(b) **Given:** - $f_c = 10$ MHz, $R_b = 1$ Mbps - $P_r = -93$ dBm - $N_0 = 10^{-20}$ W/Hz **Step 1:** Convert received power to linear scale: $$ P_r = -93 \text{ dBm} = 10^{-9.3} \text{ W} \approx 5.01 \times 10^{-10} \text{ W} $$ **Step 2:** Compute bit energy: $$ E_b = P_r \cdot T_b = P_r \cdot \frac{1}{R_b} = 5.01 \times 10^{-10} \times 10^{-6} = 5.01 \times 10^{-16} \text{ J} $$ **Step 3:** Compute $E_b/N_0$: $$ \frac{E_b}{N_0} = \frac{5.01 \times 10^{-16}}{10^{-20}} = 5.01 \times 10^{4} = 50100 $$ **Step 4:** Apply BPSK BER formula: $$ P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 \times 50100}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{100200}\right) $$ $$ P_e = Q(316.54) $$ **Step 5:** Using the asymptotic Q-function approximation $Q(x) \approx \frac{1}{x\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-x^2/2}$: $$ P_e \approx \frac{e^{-316.54^2/2}}{316.54\sqrt{2\pi}} \approx \text{negligibly small} \approx 10^{-21863} $$ **Final Answer:** $$ \boxed{P_e \approx 0 \text{ (essentially error-free transmission)}} $$ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Examinations:** > > 1. **Forgetting bipolar NRZ conversion**: Students often directly multiply unipolar signal with carrier, leading to incorrect DC offset and wrong phase interpretation. Always show the $d(t) = 2b(t) - 1$ step. > > 2. **Confusing $E_b$ with $E_s$**: For BPSK, $E_s = E_b$ since 1 bit = 1 symbol. But students write $E_s$ without realizing this equality. > > 3. **Wrong Q-function vs erfc conversion**: KTU expects $P_e = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ in some questions and $P_e = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ in others. Always confirm which form the question expects. > > 4. **dB unit errors**: $9$ dB = $7.943$ (linear), NOT $9$. This is the most common computational mistake. > > 5. **Missing carrier recovery block**: A BPSK receiver MUST include synchronization circuits (PLL, Costas loop, or squaring loop). Forgetting to draw this loses 2 marks. > > 6. **Not specifying bandwidth**: Always state that BPSK null-to-null bandwidth = $2R_b$ and main lobe bandwidth = $R_b$. > > 7. **Bit timing error**: Using $T_s$ (symbol period) instead of $T_b$ (bit period) in energy equations. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **BPSK Definition**: 2-PSK with phase shifts of **0° and 180°**, amplitude and frequency constant. - **Carrier Phase Mapping**: Bit `1` → 0°, Bit `0` → 180° (or vice versa, both conventions are valid). - **NRZ Bipolar Encoding**: Mandatory preprocessing step using $d(t) = 2b(t) - 1$. - **Transmitter Core**: Balanced modulator (multiplier) + carrier oscillator + BPF. - **Receiver Core**: Coherent detector (multiplier + LPF) + integrator + decision device + carrier recovery PLL. - **BER Formula**: $P_e = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}) = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$. - **Bandwidth**: Null-to-null = $2R_b$; main lobe = $R_b$. - **Spectral Efficiency**: $\eta = 1$ bit/sec/Hz (theoretical maximum). - **Euclidean Distance**: $d_{12} = 2\sqrt{E_b}$ — maximum separation gives lowest BER. - **3 dB Advantage**: BPSK outperforms BFSK by 3 dB at same BER. - **Threshold Decision**: Zero-crossing detection at output of integrator. - **Synchronization**: Both **carrier synchronization** and **bit timing synchronization** are essential. - **Common Pitfall**: Do NOT confuse with QPSK (4-PSK) — BPSK uses 2 phases only. - **KTU-Specific Tip**: Always include both Q-function AND erfc forms in BER derivations for full marks. - **Phasor Diagram**: Two antipodal points on the I-axis, symmetrically placed at $\pm\sqrt{E_b}$. - **Advantages**: Maximum noise immunity, simplest implementation, constant envelope. - **Disadvantages**: Low spectral efficiency (1 bit/s/Hz), requires coherent detection. - **Applications**: Satellite, GPS, deep space, RFID, military communications, Wi-Fi legacy. - **Coherent vs Non-coherent**: BPSK typically uses coherent detection (DPSK is the non-coherent version). ---

topics.title: QPSK System and Signal Constellation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT502 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** QPSK System and Signal Constellation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Quadrature Phase Shift Keying (QPSK)** is a bandwidth-efficient **M-ary digital modulation technique** belonging to the family of **Phase Shift Keying (PSK)** schemes, where $M = 4$ distinct carrier phases are used to convey digital information. Each transmitted symbol carries exactly **2 bits** of information, doubling the spectral efficiency of binary schemes (BPSK) without increasing the required transmission bandwidth for a given bit rate.

Mathematically, a QPSK signal is defined for the $k$-th symbol interval of duration $T_s$ as:

$$
s_i(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}}\,\cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + \theta_i\right), \quad 0 \le t \le T_s, \quad i = 1, 2, 3, 4
$$

where $E_s$ is the energy per symbol, $T_s$ is the symbol duration, $f_c$ is the carrier frequency, and $\theta_i \in \{0, \pi/2, \pi, 3\pi/2\}$ (or equivalently $\{\pi/4, 3\pi/4, 5\pi/4, 7\pi/4\}$ for the **offset/staggered** version).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** QPSK is categorized under **bandpass digital modulation techniques** and forms a direct prerequisite for higher-order schemes such as **8-PSK** and **16-QAM** covered later in the syllabus. Mastery of the I-Q (In-phase / Quadrature) decomposition is **mandatory**.

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **clock face** (a 12-hour analog clock). You have been asked to communicate four different messages ("Sunny", "Rainy", "Cloudy", "Snowy") to a friend across a noisy street. Instead of shouting each word, you simply **point the hour hand** to one of **four specific positions** (say, 12, 3, 6, and 9 o'clock). Your friend sees only the **angle** of the hand, not how loud it is. The amplitude (strength of your arm) can fluctuate due to wind — but the angle stays clean.

This is exactly how **QPSK** operates:
- The **carrier wave** is the "arm" (always at constant amplitude).
- The **information** is encoded only in the **phase angle** (the four positions).
- The **constellation diagram** is the "clock face" representing all allowed angles.
- Each angle represents **2 bits** (because $\log_2 4 = 2$), so information is packed efficiently.

The **I-Q plane** is just a Cartesian coordinate system: I-axis (horizontal, "real") and Q-axis (vertical, "imaginary"). Instead of speaking in polar coordinates (angle), QPSK engineers prefer the I-Q Cartesian form because it allows simple **linear addition** and **orthogonal processing** of two independent BPSK streams.

---

### Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Carrier phases used | $M = 4$ | Number of distinguishable symbols |
| Bits per symbol | $k = \log_2 M = 2$ bits | Information packing |
| Symbol rate to bit rate ratio | $R_s = R_b / 2$ | Halves the symbol rate vs BPSK |
| Bandwidth efficiency | $2$ bits/s/Hz (theoretical) | Doubles BPSK efficiency |
| Carrier amplitude envelope | **Constant** ($\sqrt{2E_s/T_s}$) | Robust against non-linear amplifiers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Quadrature"?** The word *quadrature* means "90° apart." QPSK simultaneously transmits two independent BPSK signals on carriers that are exactly **90° apart** in phase — these carriers are **mathematically orthogonal**, so they do not interfere with each other in the receiver. This orthogonality is the **secret** behind doubling the bit rate without doubling the bandwidth.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### I-Q Decomposition — The Heart of QPSK

Using the trigonometric identity $\cos(A+B) = \cos A \cos B - \sin A \sin B$, we expand $s_i(t)$:

$$
s_i(t) = \underbrace{\sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}}\,\cos\theta_i}_{\text{I-component}}\,\cos(2\pi f_c t) \;-\; \underbrace{\sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}}\,\sin\theta_i}_{\text{Q-component}}\,\sin(2\pi f_c t)
$$

We now define two **orthonormal basis functions**:

$$
\phi_1(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{T_s}}\,\cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

$$
\phi_2(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{T_s}}\,\sin(2\pi f_c t)
$$

These satisfy the orthonormality condition:

$$
\int_0^{T_s} \phi_i(t)\,\phi_j(t)\,dt = \delta_{ij}
$$

where $\delta_{ij}$ is the Kronecker delta ($\delta_{ij}=1$ when $i=j$, else $0$).

Thus, the QPSK signal can be represented as a **2-D vector** in signal space:

$$
\mathbf{s}_i = \left(s_{i1},\,s_{i2}\right) = \left(\sqrt{E_s}\cos\theta_i,\,-\sqrt{E_s}\sin\theta_i\right)
$$

For the four standard QPSK phases, the signal vectors are:

$$
\mathbf{s}_1 = \left(\sqrt{E_s},\,0\right) \quad \text{for } \theta_1 = 0
$$

$$
\mathbf{s}_2 = \left(0,\,\sqrt{E_s}\right) \quad \text{for } \theta_2 = \pi/2
$$

$$
\mathbf{s}_3 = \left(-\sqrt{E_s},\,0\right) \quad \text{for } \theta_3 = \pi
$$

$$
\mathbf{s}_4 = \left(0,\,-\sqrt{E_s}\right) \quad \text{for } \theta_4 = 3\pi/2
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alternative Offset QPSK (OQPSK):** Uses phases $\{\pi/4,\,3\pi/4,\,5\pi/4,\,7\pi/4\}$ instead. The signal vectors are then at the **diagonal corners** $(\pm\sqrt{E_s/2},\,\pm\sqrt{E_s/2})$. This staggering of bit transitions prevents the signal from passing through zero amplitude, which reduces envelope fluctuations — a critical advantage in satellite links using non-linear amplifiers.

---

### Constellation Diagram — Visual Mapping of Symbols

The **signal constellation** is the plot of the four vectors $\mathbf{s}_i$ on the I-Q plane. The four points form the vertices of a square centred at the origin, with circumradius $\sqrt{E_s}$.

The **minimum Euclidean distance** between any two constellation points is:

$$
d_{\min} = \sqrt{(\Delta I)^2 + (\Delta Q)^2}
$$

For QPSK (offset form), the closest two points are diagonally adjacent, and:

$$
d_{\min} = 2\sqrt{E_s/2} = \sqrt{2E_s}
$$

Compare with BPSK: $d_{\min}^{BPSK} = 2\sqrt{E_b}$. Since $E_s = 2E_b$ in QPSK (energy split between two bits), the two are equivalent — **the noise immunity is the same**, but QPSK achieves it in **half the bandwidth**.

---

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M = 2^k$, so $k = \log_2 M$ | Bits per symbol | $M=4 \Rightarrow k=2$ bits |
| 2 | $R_s = R_b / k$ | Symbol rate (Baud) | $R_b$ in bits/s |
| 3 | $E_s = k \cdot E_b$ | Symbol energy | Joules |
| 4 | $s_i(t) = \sqrt{2E_s/T_s}\,\cos(2\pi f_c t + \theta_i)$ | QPSK waveform | Standard form |
| 5 | $\theta_i \in \{\pi/4, 3\pi/4, 5\pi/4, 7\pi/4\}$ | Offset QPSK phases | Radians |
| 6 | $\theta_i \in \{0, \pi/2, \pi, 3\pi/2\}$ | Standard QPSK phases | Radians |
| 7 | $d_{\min} = \sqrt{2E_s} = 2\sqrt{E_b}$ | Min. Euclidean distance | Same as BPSK |
| 8 | $P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)$ | Bit error probability | AWGN channel |
| 9 | $P_s \approx 2Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_b/N_0}\right)$ | Symbol error probability | High SNR approx. |
| 10 | $\text{BW} \approx R_b / 2$ (raised cosine, $\alpha=1$) | Transmission bandwidth | Hz |
| 11 | $\eta = R_b / \text{BW} = 2$ bits/s/Hz | Spectral efficiency | Theoretical max |
| 12 | $r = d_{\min}/\left(2\sqrt{E_s}\right) = 1/\sqrt{2}$ | Packing efficiency | Ratio |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:**
> - **Satellite communications (DVB-S2, VSAT):** QPSK is the default for low-SNR deep-space links (NASA uses it for Mars missions).
> - **CDMA cellular (3G WCDMA/UMTS):** Reverse-link uplink uses QPSK due to its robustness.
> - **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11b/g legacy modes):** DSSS systems use QPSK for 11 Mbps rates.
> - **GPS (L1 band):** Uses BPSK, but modernized signals like L5 use QPSK with split spectra.
> - **Cable modems (DOCSIS) and set-top boxes:** Use QPSK or 16-QAM downstream.

---

### Decision Regions and Maximum Likelihood Detection

In the receiver, the received vector $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{s}_i + \mathbf{n}$ (where $\mathbf{n}$ is Gaussian noise) is mapped to the **nearest constellation point** in the Euclidean sense. Each constellation point defines a **Voronoi decision region** (a square in QPSK).

Decision rule (Maximum Likelihood, AWGN):

$$
\hat{i} = \arg\min_i \,\Vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i \Vert^2
$$

Due to the symmetry of the square constellation, each decision boundary is a straight line at I = 0 or Q = 0. A symbol error occurs only when Gaussian noise pushes the received vector across **both** a horizontal and a vertical boundary simultaneously — a rare event at high SNR.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: QPSK Waveform from the BPSK Pair (I-Q Construction)

**Goal:** Show that QPSK can be decomposed into two independent BPSK signals.

**Step 1:** Write the general QPSK signal with phase $\theta_i$:

$$
s_i(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}}\,\cos(2\pi f_c t + \theta_i)
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the cosine addition identity $\cos(A+B) = \cos A\cos B - \sin A\sin B$:

$$
s_i(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}}\,\bigl[\cos\theta_i\cos(2\pi f_c t) - \sin\theta_i\sin(2\pi f_c t)\bigr]
$$

**Step 3:** Identify the I and Q amplitude coefficients. Define $I_i = \sqrt{E_s}\cos\theta_i$ and $Q_i = -\sqrt{E_s}\sin\theta_i$:

$$
s_i(t) = I_i\,\sqrt{\frac{2}{T_s}}\cos(2\pi f_c t) + Q_i\,\sqrt{\frac{2}{T_s}}\sin(2\pi f_c t)
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the basis functions $\phi_1(t)$ and $\phi_2(t)$:

$$
s_i(t) = I_i\,\phi_1(t) + Q_i\,\phi_2(t)
$$

This is the **vector decomposition**: $s_i(t) \leftrightarrow \mathbf{s}_i = (I_i, Q_i)$. Each QPSK symbol is simply a point in the 2-D plane.

**Step 5:** For offset QPSK with $\theta_i = \pi/4 + (i-1)\pi/2$, $i = 1,2,3,4$:

$$
I_1 = \sqrt{E_s}\cos(\pi/4) = \sqrt{E_s/2}, \quad Q_1 = -\sqrt{E_s}\sin(\pi/4) = -\sqrt{E_s/2}
$$

$$
I_2 = \sqrt{E_s}\cos(3\pi/4) = -\sqrt{E_s/2}, \quad Q_2 = -\sqrt{E_s}\sin(3\pi/4) = -\sqrt{E_s/2}
$$

$$
I_3 = \sqrt{E_s}\cos(5\pi/4) = -\sqrt{E_s/2}, \quad Q_3 = -\sqrt{E_s}\sin(5\pi/4) = +\sqrt{E_s/2}
$$

$$
I_4 = \sqrt{E_s}\cos(7\pi/4) = +\sqrt{E_s/2}, \quad Q_4 = -\sqrt{E_s}\sin(7\pi/4) = +\sqrt{E_s/2}
$$

So the four signal vectors are:

$$
\mathbf{s}_1 = \left(+\sqrt{E_s/2},\,-\sqrt{E_s/2}\right)
$$

$$
\mathbf{s}_2 = \left(-\sqrt{E_s/2},\,-\sqrt{E_s/2}\right)
$$

$$
\mathbf{s}_3 = \left(-\sqrt{E_s/2},\,+\sqrt{E_s/2}\right)
$$

$$
\mathbf{s}_4 = \left(+\sqrt{E_s/2},\,+\sqrt{E_s/2}\right)
$$

These form the standard **square QPSK constellation** with diagonal placement.

---

### Derivation 2: Bit Error Probability of QPSK

**Setup:** The receiver computes two correlation outputs:

$$
r_I = \int_0^{T_s} r(t)\,\phi_1(t)\,dt = I_i + n_I
$$

$$
r_Q = \int_0^{T_s} r(t)\,\phi_2(t)\,dt = Q_i + n_Q
$$

where $n_I, n_Q \sim \mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2)$ are independent Gaussian noise components.

**Step 1 — Probability of correct symbol detection:** All four points are at distance $\sqrt{E_s/2 + E_s/2} = \sqrt{E_s}$ from the origin. The decision region for $\mathbf{s}_1$ is the quadrant where $r_I > 0$ AND $r_Q < 0$ (for offset QPSK). A symbol error in the I-channel requires $n_I > \sqrt{E_s/2}$, with probability:

$$
P_{e,I} = Q\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{E_s/2}}{\sqrt{N_0/2}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_s}{N_0}}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Independence of I and Q errors:** Since $n_I$ and $n_Q$ are independent, the I and Q channels are independent BPSK channels. The probability of **correct** symbol detection (both I and Q correct) is:

$$
P_c = (1 - P_{e,I})^2 = \left(1 - Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_s/N_0}\right)\right)^2
$$

**Step 3 — Symbol Error Probability:**

$$
P_s = 1 - P_c = 2Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_s/N_0}\right) - Q^2\!\left(\sqrt{E_s/N_0}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — At high SNR** ($E_s/N_0 \gg 1$), the $Q^2$ term is negligible:

$$
P_s \approx 2Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_s/N_0}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Express in terms of $E_b$:** Since $E_s = 2E_b$ (each symbol carries 2 bits):

$$
P_s \approx 2Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Bit Error Probability (Gray coding):** With Gray mapping, each symbol error flips only **one bit** (the most likely case). Therefore:

$$
P_b \approx \frac{P_s}{k} = \frac{P_s}{2} \approx Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Striking Result:** The BER of QPSK is **identical to BPSK**! However, QPSK achieves this with **half the transmission bandwidth**, because the symbol rate is halved. This is the **fundamental trade-off** in digital modulation: bandwidth vs. power.

---

### Derivation 3: Bandwidth Efficiency Calculation

**Step 1:** For BPSK, the null-to-null bandwidth of the main lobe is $2R_b$ (where $R_b$ is the bit rate). For a raised-cosine pulse shaping with roll-off $\alpha$, the absolute bandwidth is:

$$
BW_{BPSK} = R_b(1 + \alpha)
$$

**Step 2:** In QPSK, two bits are transmitted per symbol, so $R_s = R_b/2$. The main-lobe null-to-null bandwidth is $2R_s = R_b$. Applying raised-cosine shaping:

$$
BW_{QPSK} = R_s(1 + \alpha) = \frac{R_b}{2}(1+\alpha)
$$

**Step 3:** Spectral efficiency:

$$
