\eta_{QPSK} = \frac{R_b}{BW_{QPSK}} = \frac{2}{1+\alpha} \quad \text{(bits/s/Hz)}
$$

For $\alpha = 0$ (ideal Nyquist pulse): $\eta = 2$ bits/s/Hz. For $\alpha = 1$: $\eta = 1$ bit/s/Hz.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 QPSK Transmitter — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Bit Stream b1,b2,b3,b4,...] --> B[Serial-to-Parallel Converter]
    B --> C[Even Bits: I-channel b1,b3,b5...]
    B --> D[Odd Bits: Q-channel b2,b4,b6...]
    C --> E[BPSK Modulator 1]
    D --> F[90 degree Phase Shifter on Q-bit carrier]
    F --> F1[Multiplier for Q-channel]
    E --> E1[Multiplier for I-channel]
    G[Local Oscillator fc] --> E1
    G --> H[90 degree Phase Shifter]
    H --> F1
    E1 --> I[SUMMER combiner]
    F1 --> I
    I --> J[QPSK Transmitted Signal sQPSK of t]
    K[Carrier: cos2πfct] -.feeds.-> E1
    L[Quadrature Carrier: sin2πfct] -.feeds.-> F1
```

### 4.2 QPSK Receiver — Coherent Detection Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Received Signal r of t with AWGN] --> B[Multiplier with cos2πfct]
    A --> C[Multiplier with sin2πfct]
    B --> D[Integrator over 0 to Ts]
    C --> E[Integrator over 0 to Ts]
    D --> F[Sampler at t = Ts]
    E --> G[Sampler at t = Ts]
    F --> H[Decision Device I-channel]
    G --> I[Decision Device Q-channel]
    H --> J[Parallel-to-Serial Converter]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Recovered Bit Stream b1,b2,b3,b4...]
    L[Local Oscillator cos2πfct] -.feeds.-> B
    M[Local Oscillator sin2πfct phase shifted.-> feeds.-> C]
```

### 4.3 QPSK Constellation — Visual Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Constellation
    P1["S1: +sqrtE_s/2, -sqrtE_s/2  Bit 00"]
    P2["S2: -sqrtE_s/2, -sqrtE_s/2  Bit 01"]
    P3["S3: -sqrtE_s/2, +sqrtE_s/2  Bit 11"]
    P4["S4: +sqrtE_s/2, +sqrtE_s/2  Bit 10"]
    end
    P1 --- P2
    P2 --- P3
    P3 --- P4
    P4 --- P1
    O[Origin 0,0] -.centroid.- P1
    O -.centroid.- P2
    O -.centroid.- P3
    O -.centroid.- P4
```

### 4.4 Decision Regions (Voronoi Cells) on the I-Q Plane

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Quadrant_NE["Decision Region for S4: I greater than 0 AND Q greater than 0"]
    R1[Top-Right Square]
    end
    subgraph Quadrant_NW["Decision Region for S3: I less than 0 AND Q greater than 0"]
    R2[Top-Left Square]
    end
    subgraph Quadrant_SW["Decision Region for S2: I less than 0 AND Q less than 0"]
    R3[Bottom-Left Square]
    end
    subgraph Quadrant_SE["Decision Region for S1: I greater than 0 AND Q less than 0"]
    R4[Bottom-Right Square]
    end
    XAxis[I-axis horizontal] --- YAxis[Q-axis vertical]
```

### 4.5 Functional Flow — From Bits to Constellation Points

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bit pair bI, bQ arrives] --> B{Are bits mapped via Gray code?}
    B -- YES --> C[Gray mapper: only 1 bit changes per adjacent symbol]
    B -- NO --> D[Natural binary mapper]
    C --> E[Compute phase theta_i = pi/4 + 2k minus 1 pi/2]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Compute I-component: sqrtE_s cos theta_i]
    E --> G[Compute Q-component: sqrtE_s sin theta_i]
    F --> H[Modulate cos2pi fc t with I-component]
    G --> I[Modulate sin2pi fc t with Q-component]
    H --> J[SUM the I and Q carriers]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Transmit sQPSK of t over channel]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** Define QPSK. Why is it called a bandwidth-efficient modulation scheme compared to BPSK?

**Model Answer:**
QPSK (Quadrature Phase Shift Keying) is a digital modulation technique in which the carrier phase takes one of **four equally spaced values** (0°, 90°, 180°, 270° or offset by 45°), with each phase representing **2 bits** of information. It is called **bandwidth-efficient** because it transmits the same bit rate using only **half the symbol rate** of BPSK, and hence occupies only **half the transmission bandwidth** for the same information throughput. While BPSK sends 1 bit per Hz, QPSK sends 2 bits per Hz — effectively doubling spectral efficiency without sacrificing BER performance (since both have identical $P_b = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$).

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]** Draw the QPSK signal constellation for the bit sequence "00 01 11 10" using Gray coding and label the four signal vectors.

**Model Answer:**
The QPSK constellation for offset QPSK using Gray coding maps the bit pairs as follows:

$$
\mathbf{s}_1 = \left(\sqrt{E_s/2},\,-\sqrt{E_s/2}\right) \to 00
$$

$$
\mathbf{s}_2 = \left(-\sqrt{E_s/2},\,-\sqrt{E_s/2}\right) \to 01
$$

$$
\mathbf{s}_3 = \left(-\sqrt{E_s/2},\,+\sqrt{E_s/2}\right) \to 11
$$

$$
\mathbf{s}_4 = \left(+\sqrt{E_s/2},\,+\sqrt{E_s/2}\right) \to 10
$$

The four points lie on a circle of radius $\sqrt{E_s}$, forming the vertices of a square. The Gray code ensures that any single bit-flip error corresponds to a transition between adjacent corners, flipping only **one bit** (minimizing BER).

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the QPSK transmitted signal. Show that it can be represented as the sum of two orthogonal BPSK signals. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a QPSK system operating over an AWGN channel with $E_b/N_0 = 10$ dB, calculate the **bit error probability** and the **symbol error probability**. Use the relation $P_b = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ and the approximation $Q(x) \approx \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,x}e^{-x^2/2}$ for large $x$, or read from standard $Q$-function tables. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(a):**

**Step 1:** A QPSK signal in the $k$-th symbol interval of duration $T_s = 2T_b$ is:

$$
s_i(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}}\,\cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + (2i-1)\frac{\pi}{4}\right), \quad i = 1,2,3,4
$$

where $E_s = 2E_b$ is the symbol energy.

**Step 2:** Apply the cosine addition identity:

$$
s_i(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}}\,\bigl[\cos\theta_i\cos(2\pi f_c t) - \sin\theta_i\sin(2\pi f_c t)\bigr]
$$

**Step 3:** Define the in-phase and quadrature carriers (orthonormal basis):

$$
\phi_1(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{T_s}}\cos(2\pi f_c t), \quad \phi_2(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{T_s}}\sin(2\pi f_c t)
$$

**Step 4:** Rewrite as a vector sum:

$$
s_i(t) = \underbrace{\sqrt{E_s}\cos\theta_i}_{I_i}\,\phi_1(t) + \underbrace{\left(-\sqrt{E_s}\sin\theta_i\right)}_{Q_i}\,\phi_2(t)
$$

**Step 5:** Each component is an independent BPSK modulation. The I-channel transmits one bit stream on $\phi_1(t)$, while the Q-channel transmits the other bit stream on $\phi_2(t)$. Because $\phi_1(t)$ and $\phi_2(t)$ are **orthogonal over $[0, T_s]$**, the two BPSK signals do not interfere.

**Step 6:** Signal vectors (for $\theta_i = \pi/4, 3\pi/4, 5\pi/4, 7\pi/4$):

$$
\mathbf{s}_1 = \left(+\sqrt{E_b},\,-\sqrt{E_b}\right), \quad \mathbf{s}_2 = \left(-\sqrt{E_b},\,-\sqrt{E_b}\right)
$$

$$
\mathbf{s}_3 = \left(-\sqrt{E_b},\,+\sqrt{E_b}\right), \quad \mathbf{s}_4 = \left(+\sqrt{E_b},\,+\sqrt{E_b}\right)
$$

This completes the proof that QPSK is a **sum of two orthogonal BPSK modulations**.

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Step 1:** Convert $E_b/N_0 = 10$ dB to linear scale:

$$
\frac{E_b}{N_0} = 10^{10/10} = 10
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the argument of the Q-function:

$$
x = \sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}} = \sqrt{2 \times 10} = \sqrt{20} \approx 4.4721
$$

**Step 3:** Read $Q(4.4721)$ from the standard $Q$-function table (or use approximation):

$$
Q(4.4721) \approx 3.87 \times 10^{-6}
$$

(Using the Chernoff bound gives $\approx 6.30 \times 10^{-6}$ as an upper bound.)

**Step 4:** Bit error probability:

$$
P_b = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}) = Q(4.4721) \approx 3.87 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 5:** Symbol error probability (exact formula):

$$
P_s = 2Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}) - Q^2(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})
$$

$$
P_s \approx 2(3.87 \times 10^{-6}) - (3.87 \times 10^{-6})^2 \approx 7.74 \times 10^{-6}
$$

(The $Q^2$ term is negligible and is usually dropped in practical calculations.)

**Step 6:** Verify the Gray-coding approximation $P_b \approx P_s/2$:

$$
\frac{P_s}{2} \approx 3.87 \times 10^{-6} = P_b \quad \checkmark
```

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Module 4 Pattern]**

**(a)** Draw the block diagram of a QPSK transmitter and explain the function of each block. Show that the transmitted QPSK waveform can be expressed in I-Q form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A QPSK signal is transmitted over an AWGN channel with two-sided noise PSD $N_0/2 = 10^{-20}$ W/Hz. If the bit duration $T_b = 10^{-6}$ s and $E_b = 2 \times 10^{-14}$ J, find: **(i)** the symbol energy $E_s$, **(ii)** the minimum Euclidean distance $d_{\min}$, and **(iii)** the bit error probability $P_b$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(a):**

**Block Diagram Description:** Refer to Section 4.1 above for the transmitter block diagram.

**Function of each block:**
1. **Serial-to-Parallel Converter:** Separates the incoming serial bit stream into two parallel streams — the **odd-indexed bits** (I-channel) and the **even-indexed bits** (Q-channel).
2. **Local Oscillator (cosine carrier):** Generates $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ for the I-channel.
3. **90° Phase Shifter:** Converts $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ into $\sin(2\pi f_c t)$ for the Q-channel.
4. **Multipliers (Mixers):** Multiply the binary bit values (converted to $\pm 1$ levels) with their respective carriers.
5. **Summer:** Adds the two BPSK-modulated carriers to produce the QPSK waveform.

**I-Q Form Derivation:**

Let $m_I(t) = \pm \sqrt{E_b}$ (I-channel bit amplitude) and $m_Q(t) = \pm \sqrt{E_b}$ (Q-channel bit amplitude). The transmitted signal is:

$$
s(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{T_s}}\,\bigl[m_I(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) - m_Q(t)\sin(2\pi f_c t)\bigr]
$$

Substituting $T_s = 2T_b$:

$$
s(t) = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T_b}}\,\bigl[m_I(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) - m_Q(t)\sin(2\pi f_c t)\bigr]
$$

This is the canonical I-Q representation, showing the two orthogonal carriers carrying independent data streams.

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(b):**

**Given:** $N_0/2 = 10^{-20}$ W/Hz $\Rightarrow N_0 = 2 \times 10^{-20}$ W/Hz. $T_b = 10^{-6}$ s. $E_b = 2 \times 10^{-14}$ J.

**(i) Symbol Energy:**
Since QPSK carries 2 bits per symbol, $T_s = 2T_b$ and:

$$
E_s = k \cdot E_b = 2 \times 2 \times 10^{-14} = 4 \times 10^{-14} \text{ J}
$$

**(ii) Minimum Euclidean Distance:**
For offset QPSK with vectors $(\pm\sqrt{E_b}, \pm\sqrt{E_b})$, the minimum distance is between diagonally adjacent points:

$$
d_{\min} = 2\sqrt{E_b} = 2\sqrt{2 \times 10^{-14}} = 2\sqrt{2} \times 10^{-7} \approx 2.828 \times 10^{-7}
$$

**(iii) Bit Error Probability:**
First compute $E_b/N_0$:

$$
\frac{E_b}{N_0} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-14}}{2 \times 10^{-20}} = 10^6
$$

This is a very high SNR. Compute:

$$
\sqrt{2E_b/N_0} = \sqrt{2 \times 10^6} \approx 1414.21
$$

From the $Q$-function table or using the approximation:

$$
P_b = Q(1414.21) \approx \text{essentially zero} \;(\text{less than } 10^{-870000})
$$

In practice, this means the link is **virtually error-free** at this SNR, and the system is dominated by other impairments (e.g., phase noise, quantization, synchronization).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $E_b$ and $E_s$:** Many students write $P_b = Q(\sqrt{E_s/N_0})$ instead of $P_b = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$. **Always** convert to $E_b$ before substituting. **[-2 marks]**
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in dB conversion:** $E_b/N_0$ in dB $= 10\log_{10}(E_b/N_0)$, but $E_s/N_0$ in dB $= 10\log_{10}(2E_b/N_0) = E_b/N_0 (\text{dB}) + 3 \text{ dB}$. **[-1 mark]**
> 3. **Using non-Gray mapping:** Without Gray coding, a symbol error may flip 2 bits, doubling the apparent BER. Always state "**using Gray coding**" or explicitly use $P_b \approx P_s/\log_2 M$. **[-2 marks]**
> 4. **Ignoring the 90° phase shifter in the block diagram:** A common mistake is drawing the Q-channel with the same cosine carrier. This loses 2 marks immediately in (a)-part questions.
> 5. **Wrong symbol period:** $T_s = T_b$ is a frequent slip. The correct relation is $T_s = kT_b = 2T_b$ for QPSK.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **QPSK** = **Quaternary PSK**, $M = 4$, **2 bits per symbol**, four carrier phases ($\pi/4, 3\pi/4, 5\pi/4, 7\pi/4$ for offset variant).
- **Bandwidth efficiency** is **doubled** vs. BPSK: $\eta_{QPSK} = 2$ bits/s/Hz (Nyquist, $\alpha = 0$).
- **BER performance** is **identical** to BPSK: $P_b = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ — the famous "**no gain in power efficiency, gain in bandwidth efficiency**" result.
- **Constellation:** 4 points at $(\pm\sqrt{E_s/2}, \pm\sqrt{E_s/2})$ — vertices of a square on the I-Q plane.
- **Decomposition:** QPSK = orthogonal sum of **two BPSK signals** on $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and $\sin(2\pi f_c t)$.
- **Generation:** 1 local oscillator + 90° phase shifter + 2 multipliers + 1 summer + serial-to-parallel converter.
- **Detection:** Coherent (synchronous) detection with two correlators + 2 decision devices + parallel-to-serial.
- **Gray coding** maps bit pairs so that adjacent constellation points differ in only 1 bit, minimizing BER.
- **$E_s = kE_b = 2E_b$** — always remember the factor of 2.
- **Symbol rate** $R_s = R_b / 2$ — QPSK transmits symbols at half the bit rate.
- **Constant envelope:** $|s_i(t)| = \sqrt{2E_s/T_s}$ for all $i$ — QPSK is robust against non-linear amplifiers.
- **Offset QPSK (OQPSK):** Staggers I and Q bit transitions by $T_b$, preventing 180° phase jumps through zero — reduces envelope fluctuations.
- **Differential QPSK (DQPSK):** Encodes information in **phase differences** between successive symbols — avoids need for carrier synchronization, at the cost of ~2–3 dB higher required $E_b/N_0$.
- **Applications:** 3G WCDMA reverse link, DVB-S2, satellite telemetry, NASA deep-space missions, cable modems, legacy Wi-Fi.
- **Minimum distance formula:** $d_{\min} = \sqrt{2E_s} = 2\sqrt{E_b}$.
- **Decision regions** in QPSK are **square quadrants** on the I-Q plane (Voronoi cells).
- **Power Spectral Density (PSD):** Same shape as BPSK main lobe, but with **half the null-to-null bandwidth** (i.e., $\text{sinc}^2$ centred at $f_c$ with first null at $f_c \pm R_s = f_c \pm R_b/2$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Quick-Fire Mnemonics for KTU Viva:**
> - "**QPSK = 2 BPSKs in parallel**" — the one-line description.
> - "**Same power, half the bandwidth**" — the trade-off vs BPSK.
> - "**45, 135, 225, 315**" — the four offset-QPSK phases in degrees.
> - "**I do, Q do, summer sends**" — the transmitter motto.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: BER Analysis of BPSK and QPSK in erfc
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT502 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** BER Analysis of BPSK and QPSK in erfc

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Binary Phase Shift Keying (BPSK)

**BPSK (Binary Phase Shift Keying)** is the most fundamental digital modulation scheme where the phase of a constant-amplitude carrier is shifted between **two discrete values (0° and 180°)** depending on the input binary data bit. It is also called **PRK (Phase Reversal Keying)** or **2-PSK**.

Mathematically, the two transmitted signals over a bit duration $T_b$ are:

$$s_1(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t) \quad \text{for bit } \mathbf{'1'}$$

$$s_2(t) = -\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t) \quad \text{for bit } \mathbf{'0'}$$

where $E_b$ = energy per bit, $T_b$ = bit duration, and $f_c$ = carrier frequency.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** BPSK is the most bandwidth-efficient binary scheme in terms of **minimum Euclidean distance** for a given bit energy. It serves as the **gold-standard benchmark** against which all other M-ary schemes are compared.

## 1.2 Quadrature Phase Shift Keying (QPSK)

**QPSK (Quadrature Phase Shift Keying)** is an **M-ary modulation scheme (M = 4)** where two bits are transmitted simultaneously by shifting the carrier phase to one of **four equally-spaced values (45°, 135°, 225°, 315°)**. The symbol duration $T_s = 2T_b$, meaning QPSK transmits **twice the data rate of BPSK in the same bandwidth**.

The four transmitted signals are:

$$s_i(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}}\cos\left(2\pi f_c t + (2i-1)\frac{\pi}{4}\right), \quad i = 1, 2, 3, 4$$

where $E_s = 2E_b$ is the symbol energy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Bit Error Rate (BER):** The Bit Error Rate is the **ratio of the number of bits received in error to the total number of bits transmitted**. In AWGN (Additive White Gaussian Noise) channels, BER is expressed analytically as a function of $E_b/N_0$ (energy per bit to noise spectral density ratio), and is the **single most important performance metric** in digital communication.

## 1.3 The Complementary Error Function — `erfc(·)`

The **complementary error function** is a transcendental function defined as:

$$\text{erfc}(x) = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}}\int_{x}^{\infty} e^{-u^2}\,du$$

It is mathematically equivalent to the more common **Q-function** (Gaussian Q-function) by the relation:

$$Q(x) = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\left(\frac{x}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \text{erfc}(x) = 2Q(x\sqrt{2})$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition):**
> Imagine a **lifeguard at a beach** with a line drawn in the sand. The lifeguard watches swimmers (signals) approach the shore. The water is choppy (representing **AWGN noise**). The lifeguard must decide: "Is this swimmer on the *left* of the line (bit 0) or the *right* of the line (bit 1)?" The `erfc` function is essentially a **mathematical ruler** that tells us — for a given swimmer's energy ($E_b$) and the strength of the waves (noise $N_0$) — **how often the lifeguard will make a wrong decision** (i.e., commit a bit error). Stronger swimmers (high $E_b/N_0$) and calmer water (low noise) = **fewer errors** = **smaller erfc argument tail**.

## 1.4 Why is BER Analysis in `erfc` Important for KTU?

KTU examiners and engineers worldwide prefer the `erfc` form because:
- The error function is **standardized in the IEEE mathematical library**.
- It appears natively in integral tables and **MATLAB/Python** as `erfc()`.
- It allows **closed-form comparison** between modulation schemes (BPSK, QPSK, MPSK, MQAM).
- It cleanly demonstrates the **exponential decay** of error with $E_b/N_0$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Transmitter–Receiver Block Logic

### BPSK Coherent Receiver
1. **Input binary data** $b(t) \in \{0, 1\}$ is NRZ encoded to $\pm 1$.
2. The carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ is **phase-modulated** by $\pm 1$.
3. At the receiver, the signal is **correlated** with the locally generated coherent reference $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ over one bit period.
4. The correlator output is **sampled at $t = T_b$** and compared to a **threshold of zero**.
5. Decision rule: Positive sample → bit `1`; Negative sample → bit `0`.
6. Errors occur only when noise **pushes the sample across the zero threshold**.

### QPSK Coherent Receiver
1. Input bit stream is **serial-to-parallel converted** into two streams: **I (in-phase)** and **Q (quadrature)**.
2. Two BPSK modulators run in parallel: one on the in-phase carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$, the other on the quadrature carrier $\sin(2\pi f_c t)$.
3. The outputs are **summed** to produce the QPSK waveform.
4. At the receiver, the signal is **correlated separately** with both $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and $\sin(2\pi f_c t)$.
5. Two parallel decisions are made and recombined (parallel-to-serial) to recover the original bit stream.

## 2.2 Why and How the Error Occurs

The **noise** at the receiver input is **AWGN** $n(t)$ with two-sided PSD $N_0/2$. After correlation, the noise becomes a **zero-mean Gaussian random variable** with variance $\sigma^2 = N_0/2$.

The **decision variable** for BPSK is:

$$r = \pm\sqrt{E_b} + n$$

where $n \sim \mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2)$.

An error occurs if $r$ has the **opposite sign** to the transmitted bit. The probability of this is the area under the Gaussian tail beyond the zero threshold — which is **exactly the `erfc` integral**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains the **mandatory, examiner-favorite formulas** for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE questions on this topic.

| **#** | **Parameter / Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units / Notes** |
|:-----:|:-------------------------|:------------|:------------------|
| 1 | BPSK Transmitted Signals | $s_i(t) = \pm\sqrt{2E_b/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Volts |
| 2 | QPSK Transmitted Signals | $s_i(t) = \sqrt{2E_s/T_s}\cos(2\pi f_c t + (2i-1)\pi/4)$ | $i = 1,2,3,4$ |
| 3 | Symbol Energy for QPSK | $E_s = 2E_b$ | Joules |
| 4 | Symbol Duration for QPSK | $T_s = 2T_b$ | Seconds |
| 5 | **BPSK BER (Exact)** | $P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | **BPSK BER in `erfc`** | $P_b = \dfrac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{E_b/N_0}\right)$ | Dimensionless |
| 7 | **QPSK BER (Exact)** | $P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | **QPSK BER in `erfc`** | $P_b = \dfrac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{E_b/N_0}\right)$ | Dimensionless |
| 9 | QPSK Symbol Error Rate | $P_s = \text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{E_b/N_0}\right) - \dfrac{1}{4}\text{erfc}^2\!\left(\sqrt{E_b/N_0}\right)$ | For $M = 4$ |
| 10 | QPSK SER (Approximation) | $P_s \approx \text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{E_b/N_0}\right)$ for high $E_b/N_0$ | Valid for $E_b/N_0 > 10$ dB |
| 11 | Q-function ↔ erfc Relation | $Q(x) = \dfrac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$ | Universal identity |
| 12 | Noise Variance (after correlation) | $\sigma^2 = N_0/2$ | Watts/Hz × Hz |
| 13 | Euclidean Distance BPSK | $d_{12} = 2\sqrt{E_b}$ | Volts (normalized) |
| 14 | Euclidean Distance QPSK | $d_{\min} = \sqrt{2E_b}$ | Volts (normalized) |
| 15 | Bandwidth Efficiency QPSK vs BPSK | $\eta_{QPSK} = 2\times\eta_{BPSK}$ | bits/s/Hz |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **BPSK** is the modulation of choice in **deep-space communications** (Voyager, Cassini), **satellite TT&C links**, and **GPS** because of its **maximum noise immunity** (~9.6 dB required for $10^{-5}$ BER).
- **QPSK** is the workhorse of **3G/4G cellular (WCDMA, LTE)**, **DVB-S satellite TV**, **Wi-Fi (802.11)**, and **cable modems (DOCSIS)** due to its **2× bandwidth efficiency** over BPSK at the **same $E_b/N_0$ performance**.
- The `erfc` form is the **direct output of standard digital communication textbooks** (Proakis, Lathi's "Modern Digital and Analog Communication Systems", B.P. Lathi) — making it the **default expected form in KTU answer scripts**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of BPSK Bit Error Rate in `erfc` Form

### Setup
Consider two equiprobable BPSK signals transmitted over an AWGN channel:

$$s_1(t) = +\sqrt{E_b}\,\phi_1(t) \quad ; \quad s_2(t) = -\sqrt{E_b}\,\phi_1(t)$$

where the orthonormal basis function is:

$$\phi_1(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t), \quad 0 \le t \le T_b$$

The **received signal** is:

$$r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t), \quad i = 1, 2$$

with $n(t)$ being AWGN of two-sided PSD $N_0/2$.

### Step 1: Correlator Output

The receiver computes the projection of $r(t)$ onto $\phi_1(t)$:

$$r = \int_0^{T_b} r(t)\,\phi_1(t)\,dt = \pm\sqrt{E_b} + n$$

where the noise component is:

$$n = \int_0^{T_b} n(t)\,\phi_1(t)\,dt$$

### Step 2: Statistics of the Noise Variable

Since $n(t)$ is Gaussian and the integration is linear, $n$ is **Gaussian** with:

- **Mean:** $E[n] = 0$
- **Variance:** $\sigma_n^2 = E[n^2] = \dfrac{N_0}{2}$

### Step 3: Conditional PDFs at the Decision Point

Given $s_1(t)$ was sent, the decision variable is:

$$r \mid s_1 \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(+\sqrt{E_b},\; \frac{N_0}{2}\right)$$

Given $s_2(t)$ was sent:

$$r \mid s_2 \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(-\sqrt{E_b},\; \frac{N_0}{2}\right)$$

### Step 4: Probability of Error — Conditional

Assume $s_1$ is sent. An error occurs if $r < 0$ (decision crosses threshold at zero). Therefore:

$$P(e \mid s_1) = P(r < 0 \mid s_1) = \int_{-\infty}^{0} \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi N_0}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(r - \sqrt{E_b})^2}{N_0}\right)dr$$

### Step 5: Substitution of Variable

Let $u = \dfrac{r - \sqrt{E_b}}{\sqrt{N_0/2}}$, so $du = \dfrac{dr}{\sqrt{N_0/2}}$. The lower limit becomes:

$$u_{\text{low}} = \frac{0 - \sqrt{E_b}}{\sqrt{N_0/2}} = -\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}$$

Substituting:

$$P(e \mid s_1) = \int_{-\infty}^{-\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{u^2}{2}\right)du$$

### Step 6: Using the Q-Function

By the standard definition $Q(x) = \int_{x}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-u^2/2}\,du$, and the symmetry $Q(-x) = 1 - Q(x)$:

$$P(e \mid s_1) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

By symmetry, $P(e \mid s_2) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)$ as well.

### Step 7: Total Average BER

Since the bits are equiprobable ($P(s_1) = P(s_2) = 1/2$):

$$P_b = P(s_1)P(e \mid s_1) + P(s_2)P(e \mid s_2)$$

$$P_b = \frac{1}{2}Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right) + \frac{1}{2}Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

$$\boxed{P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)}$$

### Step 8: Converting to `erfc` Form

Using the identity $Q(x) = \dfrac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$ with $x = \sqrt{2E_b/N_0}$:

$$Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

$$\boxed{P_{b,\text{BPSK}} = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **golden KTU answer** for BPSK BER. Always present it in **both Q-function and erfc forms** to score full marks in ESE.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of QPSK Bit Error Rate in `erfc` Form

### Setup

QPSK uses **two independent BPSK channels** (I and Q) operating on orthogonal carriers. The four signals in terms of the 2D basis $\{\phi_1(t), \phi_2(t)\}$ are:

$$\phi_1(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{T_s}}\cos(2\pi f_c t), \quad \phi_2(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{T_s}}\sin(2\pi f_c t)$$

The signal vectors are:

$$\vec{s}_1 = (+\sqrt{E_s},\, +\sqrt{E_s}) \quad ; \quad \vec{s}_2 = (-\sqrt{E_s},\, +\sqrt{E_s})$$

$$\vec{s}_3 = (-\sqrt{E_s},\, -\sqrt{E_s}) \quad ; \quad \vec{s}_4 = (+\sqrt{E_s},\, -\sqrt{E_s})$$

### Step 1: Receiver Decomposition

The received signal after projection onto the 2D basis:

$$r_1 = s_{i1} + n_1, \quad r_2 = s_{i2} + n_2$$

where $n_1, n_2 \sim \mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2)$ are **independent** Gaussian noise variables.

### Step 2: Key Insight — Two Independent BPSK Decisions

The QPSK decision rule is:

- If $r_1 > 0$ → first bit (I-channel) is `1`; else `0`.
- If $r_2 > 0$ → second bit (Q-channel) is `1`; else `0`.

This is **exactly equivalent to two parallel BPSK decisions** on the same symbol energy $E_s = 2E_b$.

### Step 3: Bit Error Probability per Channel

The probability of error on the I-channel alone (treating it as BPSK with energy $E_s$ per symbol) is:

$$P_{b,\text{channel}} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{N_0}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2(2E_b)}{N_0}}\right) = Q\!\left(2\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often write $P_b = Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ for QPSK. This is wrong! The energy per **bit** in QPSK is $E_b = E_s/2$, so the Q-function argument involves $\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}$, not $\sqrt{E_b/N_0}$.

### Step 4: Gray Coding Optimization

In QPSK, **adjacent constellation points differ by only one bit** (Gray coding). The probability of a **bit error** is therefore:

$$P_b = \frac{P_s}{\log_2 M} = \frac{P_s}{2}$$

where $P_s$ is the **symbol error rate**.

### Step 5: Symbol Error Rate Derivation

The **minimum Euclidean distance** in QPSK is:

$$d_{\min} = \sqrt{(\sqrt{E_s} - 0)^2 + (0 - (-\sqrt{E_s}))^2} = \sqrt{2E_s} = 2\sqrt{E_b}$$

For a 2D constellation, the union bound gives:

$$P_s \le 2Q\!\left(\frac{d_{\min}}{\sqrt{2N_0}}\right) = 2Q\!\left(\frac{2\sqrt{E_b}}{\sqrt{2N_0}}\right) = 2Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

A more **exact** symbol error probability (accounting for only the two nearest neighbors in 2D) is:

$$P_s = 1 - \left[1 - Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)\right]^2 \approx 2Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right) \quad \text{for high } E_b/N_0$$

### Step 6: QPSK Bit Error Rate (Gray Coded)

$$P_b = \frac{P_s}{2} \approx Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

$$\boxed{P_{b,\text{QPSK}} \approx Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)}$$

### Step 7: Converting QPSK BER to `erfc`

$$P_{b,\text{QPSK}} = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Striking Conclusion:** BPSK and QPSK have the **IDENTICAL** BER versus $E_b/N_0$ performance! The advantage of QPSK is **bandwidth efficiency (2 bits/Hz)**, not power efficiency.

---

## 3.3 Comparative Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Calculate the BER of BPSK and QPSK at $E_b/N_0 = 9.6$ dB. Express in `erfc` form.

**Solution:**

Convert dB to linear:

$$\frac{E_b}{N_0} = 10^{9.6/10} = 10^{0.96} \approx 9.12$$

Compute the argument:

$$\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}} = \sqrt{9.12} \approx 3.02$$

Apply the formula:

$$P_b = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(3.02) = \frac{1}{2}(2.0 \times 10^{-5}) \approx 10^{-5}$$

**Verification using Q-function:**

$$P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 \times 9.12}\right) = Q(4.27) \approx 10^{-5} \;\checkmark$$

**Interpretation:** At $E_b/N_0 = 9.6$ dB, both BPSK and QPSK deliver **1 bit error in 100,000 bits** — the classic KTU reference point for $10^{-5}$ BER.

---

## 3.4 Summary Table — Side-by-Side Comparison

| **Metric** | **BPSK** | **QPSK** |
|:-----------|:---------|:---------|
| Number of symbols (M) | 2 | 4 |
| Bits per symbol | 1 | 2 |
| Bandwidth efficiency | 0.5 bits/s/Hz | 1 bit/s/Hz |
| BER vs $E_b/N_0$ | $\frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ | $\frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ |
| Symbol error vs $E_b/N_0$ | $\text{erfc}(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ | $\text{erfc}(\sqrt{E_b/N_0}) - \frac{1}{4}\text{erfc}^2(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ |
| Minimum bandwidth | $R_b$ Hz | $R_b/2$ Hz |
| Spectral efficiency gain | Baseline | **2×** over BPSK |
| Application | Deep-space, GPS | LTE, Wi-Fi, DVB-S |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 BPSK System — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Binary Data b of t] --> B[NRZ Encoder to ±1]
    B --> C[Multiplier MOD]
    D[Local Oscillator cos 2 pi fc t] --> C
    C --> E[Bandpass Filter]
    E --> F[AWGN Channel n of t]
    F --> G[Multiplier DEMOD]
    H[Coherent Local Osc cos 2 pi fc t] --> G
    G --> I[Integrate over Tb]
    I --> J[Sampler at t equal Tb]
    J --> K{Threshold Decision r greater than 0}
    K -- Yes --> L[Output bit = 1]
    K -- No --> M[Output bit = 0]
```

## 4.2 QPSK Transmitter — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Input Serial Bit Stream] --> B[Serial to Parallel Converter]
    B --> C[I channel bits]
    B --> D[Q channel bits]
    C --> E[I channel BPSK Modulator]
    D --> F[Q channel BPSK Modulator]
    G[Local Osc cos 2 pi fc t] --> E
    H[Local Osc sin 2 pi fc t] --> F
    E --> I[Summing Junction]
    F --> I
    I --> J[QPSK Waveform Output s of t]
```

## 4.3 QPSK Receiver — Coherent Demodulation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Received r of t with noise] --> B[Multiply by cos carrier]
    A --> C[Multiply by sin carrier]
    D[Local Osc cos 2 pi fc t] --> B
    E[Local Osc sin 2 pi fc t] --> C
    B --> F[Integrate over Ts]
    C --> G[Integrate over Ts]
    F --> H[Sampler I decision]
    G --> I[Sampler Q decision]
    H --> J{Threshold zero}
    I --> K{Threshold zero}
    J -- Greater --> L[I bit = 1]
    J -- Lesser --> M[I bit = 0]
    K -- Greater --> N[Q bit = 1]
    K -- Lesser --> O[Q bit = 0]
    L --> P[Parallel to Serial]
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Recovered Output Bit Stream]
```

## 4.4 BPSK Constellation (Signal Space Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[I Channel Horizontal Axis] --- B(( s1 at + sqrt Eb ))
    A --- C(( s2 at - sqrt Eb ))
    D[Decision Boundary at I = 0] --- B
    D --- C
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The two BPSK points lie on the in-phase axis. Noise moves a received point in a 2D Gaussian "cloud" around the transmitted point. If the cloud crosses the vertical "I=0" boundary, a **bit error** occurs.

## 4.5 QPSK Constellation (Signal Space Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Origin 0,0] --- B(( s1 at + sqrt Es , + sqrt Es ))
    A --- C(( s2 at - sqrt Es , + sqrt Es ))
    A --- D(( s3 at - sqrt Es , - sqrt Es ))
    A --- E(( s4 at + sqrt Es , - sqrt Es ))
    F[I Channel Vertical Decision Boundary at 0] --- B
    F --- D
    G[Q Channel Horizontal Decision Boundary at 0] --- C
    G --- E
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The four QPSK points form a square (45°, 135°, 225°, 315° phases). Two orthogonal decision boundaries (I=0 and Q=0) split the plane into **4 regions**, each containing one constellation point. Crossing either boundary causes a **single-bit error** (Gray coding).

## 4.6 BER Performance Curves — Concept Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Eb over N0 in dB increases] --> B[Argument sqrt Eb over N0 increases]
    B --> C[Tail of erfc becomes smaller]
    C --> D[BER decreases exponentially]
    D --> E[System becomes more reliable]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

### Q1. Define Bit Error Rate (BER) and Complementary Error Function (`erfc`). State the relation between Q-function and `erfc`.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Bit Error Rate (BER):** The Bit Error Rate is defined as the **expected number of bit errors per unit time** or equivalently the **probability that a transmitted bit is received in error** at the demodulator output.

$$P_b = \lim_{N \to \infty}\frac{\text{Number of bit errors}}{N}$$

**Complementary Error Function (`erfc`):** It is a transcendental function representing the area under the tail of a normalized Gaussian PDF:

$$\text{erfc}(x) = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}}\int_{x}^{\infty} e^{-u^2}\,du$$

**Relation:**

$$Q(x) = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{x}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \quad ; \quad \text{erfc}(x) = 2Q(x\sqrt{2})$$

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

### Q2. Compare BPSK and QPSK in terms of bandwidth efficiency and BER performance. Justify why QPSK is preferred in 4G LTE.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Parameter** | **BPSK** | **QPSK** |
|:--------------|:---------|:---------|
| Bits per symbol | 1 | 2 |
| Bandwidth | $R_b$ | $R_b/2$ |
| Bandwidth efficiency | 0.5 bits/s/Hz | **1 bit/s/Hz** |
| BER in `erfc` | $\frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ | $\frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ |

Both schemes have **identical** BER vs $E_b/N_0$, but QPSK uses **half the bandwidth** for the same data rate. Hence, QPSK is preferred in 4G LTE where spectral efficiency is critical.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | ESE Module 4]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**a.** Derive the Bit Error Rate expression for **BPSK** in an AWGN channel using coherent detection. Express the final result in terms of the **complementary error function (`erfc`)**. **(7 Marks)**

**b.** For a BPSK system operating at $E_b/N_0 = 8$ dB in AWGN, calculate the **theoretical BER** using the `erfc` form. Comment on the system's reliability. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Signal Representation (1 Mark):**
The two BPSK signals over a bit duration $T_b$ are:

$$s_1(t) = +\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t) \quad ; \quad s_2(t) = -\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

with $E_b$ = bit energy, $f_c$ = carrier frequency, and bits equiprobable.

**Step 2 — Received Signal and Noise Statistics (1 Mark):**
The received signal is $r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t)$ where $n(t)$ is AWGN with PSD $N_0/2$. After correlation with $\phi_1(t) = \sqrt{2/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$:

$$r = \pm\sqrt{E_b} + n, \quad n \sim \mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2)$$

**Step 3 — Conditional PDFs (1 Mark):**

$$f(r \mid s_1) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi N_0}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(r-\sqrt{E_b})^2}{N_0}\right)$$

$$f(r \mid s_2) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi N_0}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(r+\sqrt{E_b})^2}{N_0}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Probability of Error Given $s_1$ (2 Marks):**

$$P(e \mid s_1) = \int_{-\infty}^{0} f(r \mid s_1)\,dr = \int_{-\infty}^{0}\frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi N_0}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(r-\sqrt{E_b})^2}{N_0}\right)dr$$

Substituting $u = \frac{r - \sqrt{E_b}}{\sqrt{N_0/2}}$:

$$P(e \mid s_1) = \int_{-\infty}^{-\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-u^2/2}\,du = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

By symmetry, $P(e \mid s_2) = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$.

**Step 5 — Average BER and `erfc` Conversion (2 Marks):**

$$P_b = \frac{1}{2}Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right) + \frac{1}{2}Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

Using $Q(x) = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(x/\sqrt{2})$:

$$\boxed{P_{b,\text{BPSK}} = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)}$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert dB to Linear (2 Marks):**

$$E_b/N_0 \text{ (dB)} = 10\log_{10}(E_b/N_0) = 8 \text{ dB}$$

$$E_b/N_0 = 10^{8/10} = 10^{0.8} \approx 6.31$$

**Step 2 — Compute the `erfc` Argument (2 Marks):**

$$\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}} = \sqrt{6.31} \approx 2.512$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate `erfc` (2 Marks):**
Using a standard `erfc` table or calculator:

$$\text{erfc}(2.512) \approx 2.00 \times 10^{-3}$$

Therefore:

$$P_b = \frac{1}{2}(2.00 \times 10^{-3}) = 1.00 \times 10^{-3} = 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4 — Reliability Comment (1 Mark):**
A BER of $10^{-3}$ means **1 error per 1000 bits**, which is acceptable for voice communication but **not sufficient for data** (typically $10^{-9}$ required). The system would need to increase $E_b/N_0$ to ~9.6 dB to achieve $10^{-5}$ BER.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**a.** With a neat **signal space (constellation) diagram**, derive the Bit Error Rate of **QPSK** in an AWGN channel. Express the final answer in `erfc` form. Justify why Gray coding is used. **(8 Marks)**

**b.** A QPSK link transmits at $E_b/N_0 = 6$ dB. Calculate the **BER** in `erfc` form and compare it with BPSK at the same $E_b/N_0$. **(6 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — QPSK BER Derivation (8 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Constellation Diagram (2 Marks):**
The QPSK constellation has 4 points on a circle of radius $\sqrt{E_s}$, located at phases $\pi/4, 3\pi/4, 5\pi/4, 7\pi/4$:

$$\vec{s}_1 = (+\sqrt{E_s}, +\sqrt{E_s}),\; \vec{s}_2 = (-\sqrt{E_s}, +\sqrt{E_s})$$

$$\vec{s}_3 = (-\sqrt{E_s}, -\sqrt{E_s}),\; \vec{s}_4 = (+\sqrt{E_s}, -\sqrt{E_s})$$

**Step 2 — Receiver Decomposition (2 Marks):**
The received vector has two components:

$$r_1 = s_{i1} + n_1, \quad r_2 = s_{i2} + n_2, \quad n_1, n_2 \sim \mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2) \text{ i.i.d.}$$

**Step 3 — Symbol Error Probability (2 Marks):**
A symbol error occurs if the received vector crosses either the I=0 or Q=0 decision boundary. Using $d_{\min} = \sqrt{2E_s} = 2\sqrt{E_b}$:

$$P_s \approx 2Q\!\left(\frac{d_{\min}}{\sqrt{2N_0}}\right) = 2Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Bit Error via Gray Coding (1 Mark):**
Adjacent constellation points differ in only one bit, so:

$$P_b = \frac{P_s}{\log_2 4} = \frac{P_s}{2} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

**Step 5 — `erfc` Form (1 Mark):**

$$\boxed{P_{b,\text{QPSK}} = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)}$$

**Gray Coding Justification:** Gray coding ensures that the **most likely error event** (crossing one boundary) results in only **one bit error** instead of two, reducing BER by half.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Comparison (6 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert dB (1 Mark):**

$$E_b/N_0 = 10^{6/10} = 10^{0.6} \approx 3.98$$

**Step 2 — QPSK BER (2 Marks):**

$$\sqrt{E_b/N_0} = \sqrt{3.98} \approx 1.995$$

$$P_{b,\text{QPSK}} = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(1.995) \approx \frac{1}{2}(0.0469) \approx 2.35 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 3 — BPSK BER at Same $E_b/N_0$ (2 Marks):**

$$P_{b,\text{BPSK}} = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(\sqrt{3.98}) = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(1.995) \approx 2.35 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison (1 Mark):**
**The BER is identical for BPSK and QPSK at the same $E_b/N_0$**, but QPSK achieves this at **half the bandwidth** of BPSK. This is the **key advantage of QPSK** for spectrum-limited applications like LTE.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
>
> 1. **Confusing $E_b$ and $E_s$ in QPSK:** Many students plug $E_s$ instead of $E_b$ into the final `erfc` formula. **Remember:** In QPSK, $E_b = E_s/2$. The `erfc` argument is $\sqrt{E_b/N_0}$, NOT $\sqrt{E_s/N_0}$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the factor 1/2:** The exact `erfc` form for BPSK/QPSK has a leading **½**. Writing $P_b = \text{erfc}(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ without the half is **wrong by a factor of 2** and will lose 2 marks.
>
> 3. **Using $E_b/N_0$ in dB directly inside `erfc`:** You MUST convert dB to linear ratio FIRST, then take the square root, then call `erfc`. The dB value cannot be the argument.
>
> 4. **Missing the Gray Coding Justification:** When asked for QPSK BER, simply writing the formula without explaining **why** $P_b = P_s/2$ is treated as incomplete (lose 1–2 marks).
>
> 5. **No Constellation Diagram:** A QPSK BER derivation **without a constellation diagram** typically loses 1–2 marks in Part B. Always draw the 4-point square.
>
> 6. **Confusing BER with SER:** Symbol Error Rate and Bit Error Rate are **different** for M-ary schemes. State clearly whether you are deriving SER or BER.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must Memorize Before ESE)**

- **BPSK signals:** $s_i(t) = \pm\sqrt{2E_b/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$, $M=2$.
- **QPSK signals:** 4-phase constellation, $M=4$, $E_s = 2E_b$, $T_s = 2T_b$.
- **Key identity:** $Q(x) = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(x/\sqrt{2})$ and $\text{erfc}(x) = 2Q(x\sqrt{2})$.
- **BPSK BER:** $P_b = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}) = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$.
- **QPSK BER:** $P_b = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}) = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ (same as BPSK!).
- **QPSK SER:** $P_s = 2Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}) = \text{erfc}(\sqrt{E_b/N_0}) - \frac{1}{4}\text{erfc}^2(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$.
- **Gray coding rule:** $P_b = P_s / \log_2 M$ for M-ary schemes.
- **QPSK advantage:** 2× bandwidth efficiency over BPSK, identical power efficiency.
- **Minimum Euclidean distances:** $d_{\min,\text{BPSK}} = 2\sqrt{E_b}$, $d_{\min,\text{QPSK}} = \sqrt{2E_b} = 2\sqrt{E_s/2}$.
- **Reference BER points (must memorize):** $E_b/N_0 = 9.6$ dB → $P_b = 10^{-5}$ for BPSK/QPSK; $E_b/N_0 = 7$ dB → $P_b \approx 10^{-3}$.
- **Decision rule:** Positive sample → bit `1`; Negative sample → bit `0` (BPSK). QPSK: same rule independently on I and Q channels.
- **Noise after correlation:** Variance $= N_0/2$ (always, for both BPSK and QPSK).
- **KTU formula must-show:** Always end with the boxed $\frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ expression.
- **Applications to remember for viva:** GPS uses BPSK; LTE/4G uses QPSK; DVB-S uses QPSK; deep-space uses BPSK.
- **Watch the dB trap:** $E_b/N_0 \text{ (dB)} = 10\log_{10}(E_b/N_0)$. Always convert to linear before applying `erfc`.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plots of BER vs SNR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT502 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Plots of BER vs SNR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of BER

The **Bit Error Rate (BER)**, often expressed as the **Bit Error Probability ($P_b$)**, is defined as the expected ratio of the number of bits received in error to the total number of bits transmitted over a digital communication channel within a given observation window. Mathematically,

$$P_b = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{\text{Number of bit errors}}{N}$$

where $N$ is the total number of transmitted bits. The complementary metric, the **Symbol Error Rate (SER)** or **Symbol Error Probability ($P_s$)**, represents the probability that a transmitted multi-bit symbol is decoded incorrectly at the receiver.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 4, the focus is on coherent detection of binary and M-ary digital modulation schemes. The BER is computed at the **decision device output**, after matched filtering and threshold detection, under the assumption of **AWGN (Additive White Gaussian Noise)** channel conditions.

### 1.2 Formal Definition of SNR

The **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** is the ratio of the received signal power to the noise power corrupting it. In digital communication theory, the most meaningful SNR measure is the **Energy-per-bit to Noise Spectral Density Ratio ($E_b/N_0$)**, expressed in decibels as

$$\left(\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right)_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right)$$

The $E_b/N_0$ ratio is a **normalized SNR** that makes performance comparisons across different modulation schemes and data rates possible.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: Shouting Across a Noisy Stadium

Imagine you are trying to convey a coded message — a series of binary digits (`0` or `1`) — by shouting `HIGH` (binary 1) or `LOW` (binary 0) to a friend sitting across a packed cricket stadium. Three scenarios unfold:

1. **Low SNR (Noisy Stadium):** The crowd roars, drowning out your whispers. Your friend often misinterprets a `HIGH` as a `LOW`, resulting in a high number of bit errors. This is a **high BER** scenario.
2. **High SNR (Silent Stadium):** The stadium is empty, and your friend hears every syllable perfectly. The probability of miscommunication is virtually zero, giving you a **low BER** (often approaching $10^{-9}$ or lower).
3. **Plotting the Curve:** If you gradually reduce the crowd noise (i.e., increase $E_b/N_0$) and plot the number of misunderstandings against noise level, you obtain a steep, monotonically decreasing curve. This curve, when plotted on a **semi-logarithmic scale**, is the famous **BER vs SNR waterfall curve** — the central performance signature of every digital modulation scheme.

### 1.4 Why $E_b/N_0$ and Not Plain SNR?

The plain SNR (signal power to noise power) depends on the bit rate and bandwidth, making cross-scheme comparisons misleading. $E_b/N_0$ normalizes out these factors, isolating the **energy efficiency** of the modulation scheme itself.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Constant:** The relationship between the conventional SNR (with units of W/W) and $E_b/N_0$ in linear scale is governed by the **bit duration $T_b$** and the **noise bandwidth $B$**:
>
> $$\frac{E_b}{N_0} = \frac{S}{N_0 B} = \frac{S}{N} \cdot \frac{B}{R_b}$$
>
> where $R_b = 1/T_b$ is the bit rate.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Noise Assumption: AWGN

All BER vs SNR plots in KTU Module 4 are derived under the **Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN)** assumption. The received signal model is:

$$r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t), \quad i = 0, 1, \dots, M-1$$

where $n(t)$ is a zero-mean Gaussian random process with **two-sided power spectral density $N_0/2$** (in W/Hz). The decision variable at the matched filter output follows a Gaussian distribution, which is the foundation for the **Q-function based BER expressions**.

### 2.2 The Q-Function: Mathematical Engine of BER Curves

The **Gaussian Q-function** quantifies the tail probability of a standard normal distribution and is the universal language of digital communication performance:

$$Q(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{x}^{\infty} e^{-u^2/2}\, du, \quad x \geq 0$$

The bit error probability for antipodal binary schemes (like BPSK) is directly the Q-function of the **distance between signal points normalized by the noise standard deviation**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet: BER Expressions

> [!NOTE]
> The table below contains the **exact BER expressions** that students must memorize for the KTU ESE. All arguments inside parentheses use `\vert` notation (not `|`) to preserve markdown table integrity.

| Modulation Scheme | Coherent BER $P_b$ (Exact) | Coherent BER $P_b$ (High SNR Approximation) | Bandwidth Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **BPSK** | $Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 E_b / N_0}\right)$ | $Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 E_b / N_0}\right)$ | 1 bps/Hz |
| **BFSK (Coherent)** | $Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_b / N_0}\right)$ | $Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_b / N_0}\right)$ | 0.5 bps/Hz |
| **BFSK (Non-Coherent)** | $\tfrac{1}{2}\exp\!\left(-E_b / 2 N_0\right)$ | $\tfrac{1}{2}\exp\!\left(-E_b / 2 N_0\right)$ | 0.5 bps/Hz |
| **DPSK** | $\tfrac{1}{2}\exp\!\left(-E_b / N_0\right)$ | $\tfrac{1}{2}\exp\!\left(-E_b / N_0\right)$ | 1 bps/Hz |
| **QPSK / 4-QAM** | $Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 E_b / N_0}\right)$ | $Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 E_b / N_0}\right)$ | 2 bps/Hz |
| **M-PSK (Coherent)** | $\approx 2 Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 E_b / N_0}\,\sin(\pi/M)\right)/\log_2 M$ | Same as exact for large $E_b/N_0$ | $\log_2 M$ bps/Hz |
| **M-QAM (Square)** | $\approx 4\!\left(1 - 1/\sqrt{M}\right) Q\!\left(\sqrt{3 E_b \log_2 M \,/\, N_0 (M-1)}\right)/\log_2 M$ | Same | $\log_2 M$ bps/Hz |
| **Differential QPSK (DQPSK)** | $\approx 2 Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 E_b / N_0}\,\sin(\pi/4)\right)$ | Same | 2 bps/Hz |

**Common Chernoff Upper Bound** (useful for sketching curves without computing Q-function tables):

$$P_b \leq \exp\!\left(-E_b \gamma_b / N_0\right)$$

where $\gamma_b$ is the **coding/modulation gain factor**. For BPSK, $\gamma_b = 1$.

### 2.4 The "Waterfall" Behavior of BER Curves

When $P_b$ is plotted on a **logarithmic y-axis** (dB or linear log scale) versus $E_b/N_0$ in dB on the x-axis, all digital modulation schemes exhibit a characteristic **monotonically decreasing S-shaped curve** that drops steeply once $E_b/N_0$ crosses a threshold.

The **slope** of this curve at high SNR is **steeper for BPSK/QPSK** than for higher-order M-ary schemes. This is the *waterfall* — once sufficient SNR is supplied, the system transitions rapidly from an unreliable to a near-error-free regime.

### 2.5 The Concept of Coding Gain

Two schemes operating at the same $P_b$ require different $E_b/N_0$ values. The horizontal separation (in dB) between two BER curves, measured at a target $P_b$, is the **coding/modulation gain**:

$$G_c = \left(\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right)_{\text{un-coded}} \bigg/ \left(\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right)_{\text{coded}}$$

For example, BPSK outperforms BFSK by exactly **3 dB** at any given $P_b < 10^{-3}$, because BPSK uses the larger Euclidean distance of antipodal signaling.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Link Budget Design:** Satellite and deep-space missions (e.g., NASA's Voyager, ISRO's Mangalyaan) use BER vs SNR curves to compute the required transmit power and antenna gain to achieve a target $P_b$ (typically $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-9}$).
- **Adaptive Modulation:** 4G LTE and 5G NR continuously switch between QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM, and 256-QAM based on instantaneous $E_b/N_0$ estimated at the receiver.
- **Forward Error Correction (FEC):** Coded modulation schemes (Turbo, LDPC, Polar codes) shift the BER curve **leftward**, reducing the required $E_b/N_0$ by 5–10 dB at $P_b = 10^{-6}$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of BPSK BER over AWGN

The BPSK signal set is

$$s_1(t) = +\sqrt{E_b}\,\phi(t), \qquad s_0(t) = -\sqrt{E_b}\,\phi(t)$$

where $\phi(t)$ is the unit-energy basis waveform with $\int_0^{T_b} \phi^2(t)\, dt = 1$. Under AWGN, the received signal is

$$r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t), \quad i \in \{0, 1\}$$

**Step 1: Matched Filter Output.** The receiver correlates $r(t)$ with $\phi(t)$ to produce the decision variable:

$$r = \int_0^{T_b} r(t)\,\phi(t)\, dt = \pm \sqrt{E_b} + n$$

where the noise component $n$ is a zero-mean Gaussian RV with variance $\sigma_n^2 = N_0/2$.

**Step 2: Optimal Threshold.** By symmetry of the antipodal constellation, the optimal Bayes threshold is at $r = 0$. Decision rule:

$$\hat{b} = \begin{cases} 1 & r \geq 0 \\ 0 & r < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 3: Conditional Error Probability.** Assume bit `1` is transmitted, i.e., $r = +\sqrt{E_b} + n$. An error occurs if $r < 0$, i.e., if $n < -\sqrt{E_b}$:

$$P(\text{error} \mid b = 1) = P\!\left(n < -\sqrt{E_b}\right)$$

**Step 4: Substitute Gaussian Distribution.** With $n \sim \mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2)$,

$$P(\text{error} \mid b = 1) = \int_{-\infty}^{-\sqrt{E_b}} \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi N_0}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{n^2}{N_0}\right) dn$$

**Step 5: Standardize the Variable.** Let $u = n\,\sqrt{2/N_0}$, so $du = \sqrt{2/N_0}\, dn$. The integral becomes:

$$\begin{aligned} P(\text{error} \mid b = 1) &= \int_{-\infty}^{-\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{u^2}{2}\right) du \\ &= Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right) \end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Symmetry Gives Total BER.** By symmetry, $P(\text{error} \mid b = 0) = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$. With equiprobable bits, the total bit error probability is:

$$P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right) \quad \text{(BPSK, Coherent)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Result:** BPSK requires the **minimum energy per bit** among all binary schemes because it is the only **antipodal** signaling. This is why BPSK is the **benchmark** against which all other schemes are compared in BER plots.

### 3.2 Derivation of BFSK (Coherent) BER

For coherent BFSK, the two orthogonal basis functions are $\phi_1(t) = \sqrt{2/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_1 t)$ and $\phi_2(t) = \sqrt{2/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_2 t)$, with frequency separation $|f_1 - f_2| = n/T_b$, $n$ integer.

The decision variables at the two matched filter outputs are:

$$r_1 = \pm\sqrt{E_b} + n_1, \qquad r_2 = n_2$$

For bit `1` transmitted ($r_1 = +\sqrt{E_b} + n_1$, $r_2 = n_2$), an error occurs if $r_2 > r_1$, i.e., if $n_2 - n_1 > \sqrt{E_b}$. The difference $z = n_2 - n_1$ is Gaussian with mean $0$ and variance $N_0$ (sum of two independent variances each $N_0/2$).

$$\begin{aligned} P_b &= P(z > \sqrt{E_b}) = \int_{\sqrt{E_b}}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi N_0}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{z^2}{2N_0}\right) dz \\ &= Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}}\right) \quad \text{(BFSK Coherent)} \end{aligned}$$

**Comparison with BPSK:** The argument inside $Q(\cdot)$ for BFSK is $\sqrt{E_b/N_0}$ versus $\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}$ for BPSK. At high SNR, the $E_b/N_0$ required by BFSK to achieve a given $P_b$ is **exactly 3 dB higher** than BPSK.

### 3.3 High SNR Approximation Using Chernoff Bound

For fast analytical work, the Chernoff bound gives an upper bound on $P_b$:

$$P_b \leq \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_b \gamma_b}{N_0}\right)$$

This explains why **BER curves are straight lines on a log scale at high SNR** (because $\log P_b$ is linear in $E_b/N_0$ in dB). The slope of each line in dB/dB is the **diversity order** of the modulation.

### 3.4 Numerical Example: BPSK at $E_b/N_0 = 9.6$ dB

Convert $E_b/N_0$ to linear: $10 \log_{10}(x) = 9.6 \Rightarrow x = 10^{0.96} \approx 9.12$.

$$P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 \times 9.12}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{18.24}\right) = Q(4.27) \approx 9.7 \times 10^{-6}$$

This is the famous $P_b = 10^{-5}$ operating point that students should remember for the **$E_b/N_0 = 9.6$ dB benchmark**.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: How to Read a BER vs SNR Plot

| Region of Curve | Physical Meaning | Mathematical Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| Low SNR (left tail) | Noise dominates; bits are nearly random | $P_b \to 0.5$ as $E_b/N_0 \to 0$ |
| Kneepoint (transition) | Scheme starts to "lock on" to symbols | $P_b \approx 10^{-1}$ to $10^{-3}$ |
| Waterfall (mid) | Rapid BER drop with added SNR | Slope $\approx -E_b/N_0$ (in dB/dB) |
| Error floor (right) | Residual errors dominated by ISI/nonlinearity | Floor set by implementation losses |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When a question asks to "compare the BER performance of BPSK and BFSK," examiners expect you to state: (1) the exact formulas, (2) the 3 dB penalty of BFSK at high SNR, and (3) a sketch showing BPSK to the left of BFSK on the BER axis.

### 3.6 Comprehensive Plot Behavior Across Schemes

The standard textbook ordering of curves on a BER vs $E_b/N_0$ plot (left = best, right = worst) is:

1. **BPSK = QPSK** (identical $E_b/N_0$ requirement; QPSK is two BPSK channels in quadrature).
2. **DPSK** (≈ 1 dB worse than BPSK at $P_b = 10^{-4}$).
3. **Coherent BFSK** (3 dB worse than BPSK).
4. **Non-coherent BFSK** (≈ 0.5 dB worse than coherent BFSK at $P_b = 10^{-4}$).
5. **M-PSK** (M = 8, 16, 32, ...): progressively worse as M increases.
6. **M-QAM**: more power-efficient than M-PSK for $M \geq 16$ at the cost of higher peak-to-average power ratio (PAPR).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow of BER vs SNR Generation

The following block diagram illustrates the functional pipeline that produces a single point on a BER vs SNR curve in a typical communication laboratory simulation or analytical derivation:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bit Source Random Bernoulli Trials] --> B[Modulator BPSK QPSK M ary]
    B --> C[AWGN Channel Variable N0]
    C --> D[Matched Filter Correlator Bank]
    D --> E[Threshold Decision Device]
    E --> F[Error Counter XOR with Original Bits]
    F --> G[BER Estimator Pb = Errors / Total Bits]
    H[SNR Controller Eb N0 Sweep] --> C
    H --> G
    G --> I[Plot Generator Semi log Scale]
    I --> J[BER vs EbN0 Waterfall Curve]
```

### 4.2 Decision-Theoretic Interpretation

The following diagram visualizes the geometric origin of BER for BPSK — the overlap of two Gaussian distributions centered at the antipodal signal points:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Signal s0 at minus sqrt Eb] --> B[Gaussian PDF centered at minus sqrt Eb variance N0/2]
    C[Signal s1 at plus sqrt Eb] --> D[Gaussian PDF centered at plus sqrt Eb variance N0/2]
    B --> E[Threshold at zero Optimal Bayes]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Overlap Area equals BER Pb]
    B --> F
    D --> F
    F --> G[Reduce N0 Right shift of curves Lower Pb]
    F --> H[Increase Eb Right shift of signal points Lower Pb]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Performance Curve Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[BER vs EbN0 Master Plot] --> B[BPSK QPSK Antipodal Best Performance]
    A --> C[DPSK Differential Detection]
    A --> D[Coherent BFSK Orthogonal 3 dB Worse]
    A --> E[Non Coherent BFSK]
    A --> F[M PSK Curves for M equals 8 16 32 64]
    A --> G[M QAM Curves Power Efficient at High M]
    H[Target Pb equals 10 to the power minus 5] --> I[Read EbN0 for Each Scheme]
    H --> J[Compute Power Penalty in dB]
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture: From Theory to Measured Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Analytical Q function Expression] --> B[Numerical Evaluation in MATLAB or Python]
    A2[Monte Carlo Simulation 10 to the power 6 bits] --> B
    C[Hardware BER Tester BERT] --> B
    B --> D[Tabulate Pb for Swept EbN0]
    D --> E[Plot semilogy Pb vs EbN0dB]
    E --> F[Annotate Operating Point at 9.6 dB for 10 to the power minus 5]
    E --> G[Annotate 3 dB Gap BPSK vs BFSK]
    E --> H[Annotate Coding Gain from FEC]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Notes:** All node identifiers are alphanumeric (e.g., `A`, `B2`, `H`), avoiding reserved keywords like `end` or `graph`. Labels contain no markdown bold, italics, or HTML formatting. This guarantees clean rendering in any Mermaid v8+ engine.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Define Bit Error Rate (BER). Why is $E_b/N_0$ preferred over plain SNR in plotting digital modulation performance curves?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Bit Error Rate (BER)** is defined as the ratio of the number of received bits in error to the total number of bits transmitted over a digital communication link, expressed as a probability $P_b$ in the limit of large $N$:

$$P_b = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{\text{Number of erroneous bits}}{N}$$

The metric $E_b/N_0$ (energy per bit to noise spectral density ratio) is preferred over plain SNR because it is a **normalized** measure that isolates the **energy efficiency** of the modulation scheme itself. Plain SNR ($S/N$) depends on the bit rate $R_b$ and the noise bandwidth $B$, which makes cross-scheme comparisons unfair. By using $E_b/N_0$, two schemes with different data rates, bandwidths, or constellations can be compared on an **equal footing**, since $E_b/N_0 = (S/N)(B/R_b)$ removes these dependencies. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] State the exact BER expression for coherent BPSK and coherent BFSK. What is the performance gap between them in dB at high SNR?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Coherent BPSK:** $P_b = Q(\sqrt{2 E_b/N_0})$
- **Coherent BFSK:** $P_b = Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$

At high SNR, the argument of the Q-function for BPSK is $\sqrt{2}$ times larger than that of BFSK. Equivalently, for a given target $P_b$, BFSK requires **twice the energy per bit**, which corresponds to a **3 dB penalty** for BFSK relative to BPSK. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (ESE Pattern, 14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Derive the bit error probability of **coherent BPSK** over an AWGN channel. Start from the received signal model and arrive at the closed-form $P_b$ expression. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A BPSK system operates at $E_b/N_0 = 8$ dB. Compute the numerical value of $P_b$. Then, determine how much additional $E_b/N_0$ (in dB) is required to reduce $P_b$ to $10^{-9}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of BPSK BER (7 marks):**

- **Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the received signal under AWGN: $r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t)$, where $s_1(t) = +\sqrt{E_b}\,\phi(t)$ and $s_0(t) = -\sqrt{E_b}\,\phi(t)$ for antipodal signaling.

- **Step 2 (1 mark):** Pass $r(t)$ through the matched filter (correlator with $\phi(t)$). The decision variable becomes:

$$r = \int_0^{T_b} r(t)\phi(t)\, dt = \pm\sqrt{E_b} + n$$

where $n$ is Gaussian with mean 0 and variance $N_0/2$.

- **Step 3 (1 mark):** The optimum threshold (Bayes, equiprobable) is at $r = 0$. An error occurs when bit `1` is sent but $r < 0$, i.e., $n < -\sqrt{E_b}$:

$$P(\text{error} \mid b = 1) = P(n < -\sqrt{E_b})$$

- **Step 4 (2 marks):** Substituting the Gaussian PDF of $n$ and standardizing $u = n\sqrt{2/N_0}$:

$$P(\text{error} \mid b = 1) = \int_{-\infty}^{-\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-u^2/2}\, du = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

- **Step 5 (1 mark):** By symmetry, $P(\text{error} \mid b = 0)$ equals the same expression. With equiprobable bits, the average BER is:

$$\boxed{P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)}$$

- **Step 6 (1 mark):** Conclusion: BPSK is the **optimal binary scheme** because it is antipodal, maximizing the minimum Euclidean distance $d_{\min} = 2\sqrt{E_b}$.

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

- **Step 1 (1 mark):** Convert $E_b/N_0 = 8$ dB to linear: $10 \log_{10}(x) = 8 \Rightarrow x = 10^{0.8} \approx 6.31$.

- **Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute the argument of Q:

$$\sqrt{2 E_b/N_0} = \sqrt{2 \times 6.31} = \sqrt{12.62} \approx 3.553$$

- **Step 3 (2 marks):** Look up $Q(3.55)$ from standard Q-tables:

$$P_b = Q(3.55) \approx 1.9 \times 10^{-4}$$

- **Step 4 (2 marks):** For $P_b = 10^{-9}$, find the required argument $x$ such that $Q(x) = 10^{-9}$. From Q-tables, $Q(5.99) \approx 10^{-9}$, so $x \approx 6.0$. Thus:

$$\sqrt{2 E_b/N_0} = 6.0 \Rightarrow \frac{E_b}{N_0} = \frac{36}{2} = 18 \text{ (linear)} = 10 \log_{10}(18) \approx 12.55 \text{ dB}$$

- **Step 5 (Final, included in 2 marks):** Additional $E_b/N_0$ required = $12.55 - 8.0 = \mathbf{4.55 \text{ dB}}$.

This illustrates the **waterfall** property: reducing $P_b$ by 5 orders of magnitude requires only ~4.5 dB more $E_b/N_0$.

---

#### **Question B [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With the help of clear mathematical expressions, compare the **BER performance of BPSK, BFSK (coherent), and DPSK** over an AWGN channel. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Sketch the **BER vs $E_b/N_0$ curves** for these three schemes on a semi-log scale, and identify the **dB gaps** at $P_b = 10^{-4}$. Discuss the **bandwidth efficiency** trade-off. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Mathematical Comparison (7 marks):**

| Scheme | BER Expression $P_b$ | Bandwidth |
|---|---|---|
| BPSK (Coherent) | $Q(\sqrt{2 E_b/N_0})$ | $2/T_b$ Hz (main lobe) |
| BFSK (Coherent) | $Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ | $4/T_b$ Hz (orthogonal tones) |
| DPSK | $\frac{1}{2}\exp(-E_b/N_0)$ | $2/T_b$ Hz |

- **Step 1 (2 marks):** All three are derived under the AWGN assumption with matched filter reception (BPSK, BFSK) or differential detection (DPSK). BPSK is antipodal (maximum distance), BFSK is orthogonal, and DPSK is non-coherent.

- **Step 2 (2 marks):** At high SNR, BPSK outperforms BFSK by exactly **3 dB** because the Q-argument is $\sqrt{2}$ times larger. DPSK lies between BPSK and coherent BFSK — typically **1 dB worse than BPSK** at $P_b = 10^{-4}$ (since the exponential decays slower than $Q$ at moderate SNR but approaches the same asymptotic slope).

- **Step 3 (2 marks):** At $P_b = 10^{-4}$:
  - BPSK requires $\approx 8.4$ dB
  - DPSK requires $\approx 9.2$ dB
  - Coherent BFSK requires $\approx 11.4$ dB

- **Step 4 (1 mark):** Conclude: BPSK is the **most power-efficient**, but it requires **coherent carrier recovery**, which adds receiver complexity. DPSK trades ~1 dB power for **simpler receiver** (no phase tracking needed). BFSK pays a 3 dB power penalty for being robust to channel fading.

---

**(b) Sketch and Trade-off (7 marks):**

- **Step 1 (3 marks):** Sketch (textual description, since the student is expected to draw):

```
   BER
   10^0  |*
         |  * (BFSK coherent)
   10^-2 |    *
         |      **
   10^-4 |        ***  (DPSK)
   10^-6 |           **** (BPSK)
   10^-8 |              *****
         |________________________
         0    5   10  15  20  25   Eb/N0 (dB)
```

- **Step 2 (1 mark):** At $P_b = 10^{-4}$, the dB gaps are: **DPSK – BPSK ≈ 1 dB**, **BFSK – BPSK ≈ 3 dB**, **BFSK – DPSK ≈ 2 dB**.

- **Step 3 (2 marks):** **Bandwidth trade-off:** BPSK and DPSK both occupy $\approx 2/T_b$ Hz (theoretically minimum for binary signaling), while coherent BFSK needs $4/T_b$ Hz for orthogonal tone separation. Thus **BFSK is the least bandwidth-efficient**, costing twice the spectrum of BPSK/DPSK.

- **Step 4 (1 mark):** **Engineering trade-off summary:** Choose **BPSK** when power is precious (deep-space, satellite). Choose **DPSK** for low-complexity mobile/IoT receivers. Choose **BFSK** only when channel distortion is severe and orthogonal tones are preferred for robustness.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing BER and SER** — For QPSK and M-QAM, $P_b$ and $P_s$ differ by a factor of $\log_2 M$. Writing $P_b = P_s$ for QPSK (true by symmetry) and then carrying this over to 16-QAM (false) is a frequent error.
> 2. **Forgetting the 3 dB gap factor** — When asked to compare BPSK and BFSK at $P_b = 10^{-5}$, students often compute both numerically and forget to *state explicitly* that the gap is 3 dB. The examiner expects the *qualitative* observation alongside the math.
> 3. **Using linear $E_b/N_0$ inside `log10` or `Q()`** — Always convert from dB to linear *before* substituting into formulas. A common slip is writing $Q(\sqrt{2 \times 8})$ instead of $Q(\sqrt{2 \times 10^{0.8}})$. This causes the answer to be off by a factor of 6.
> 4. **Missing the bandwidth dimension in the table** — When asked to compare schemes, a complete answer must include both **power efficiency** (BER) and **bandwidth efficiency** (bps/Hz). Skipping the latter costs 2 marks.
> 5. **Mixing up $E_b/N_0$ with SNR (S/N)** — In some KTU questions, the value is given as SNR. Convert using $E_b/N_0 = (S/N)(B/R_b)$ before applying the BER formula.
> 6. **Not drawing the waterfall region on sketches** — A BER vs SNR plot must show the characteristic S-curve with a marked waterfall, not a straight line.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **BER Definition:** $P_b = Q(\text{normalized distance})$, evaluated at the matched filter output.
- **BPSK is the gold standard** for binary digital modulation over AWGN, with $P_b = Q(\sqrt{2 E_b/N_0})$.
- **BFSK coherent** lags BPSK by **3 dB**; BFSK non-coherent is worse by an additional ~0.5 dB.
- **DPSK** is ~1 dB worse than BPSK but avoids carrier synchronization.
- **QPSK** has the same $E_b/N_0$ requirement as BPSK but **double the bandwidth efficiency** (2 bps/Hz).
- **M-QAM** is more power-efficient than M-PSK for $M \geq 16$, but its **PAPR is higher**.
- **$E_b/N_0$ in dB** = $10 \log_{10}(E_b/N_0 \text{ in linear})$, where linear value is dimensionless.
- **Conversion shortcut:** $E_b/N_0 = (S/N)(B/R_b)$ when bit rate $R_b = 1/T_b$.
- **Benchmark point:** BPSK at $E_b/N_0 = 9.6$ dB gives $P_b = 10^{-5}$; at 12.55 dB gives $P_b = 10^{-9}$.
- **Chernoff bound** $P_b \leq \exp(-\gamma_b E_b/N_0)$ explains the **straight-line** behavior of BER curves on semi-log plots at high SNR.
- **Coding gain** = horizontal shift (in dB) between an un-coded and a coded BER curve at a target $P_b$.
- **Waterfall region** is where $P_b$ drops from $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-6}$ with each additional 2–3 dB of $E_b/N_0$.
- **Error floor** at low BER is caused by **ISI, phase noise, fading, or quantization** — not by AWGN alone.
- **Plot axis convention:** y-axis is **logarithmic** (BER), x-axis is **linear in dB** ($E_b/N_0$).
- **Anti-pipe safeguard:** In all KTU submissions, write $Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ with `\vert` notation if used in tables to avoid markdown rendering issues.
- **KTU exam tip:** Always annotate the **3 dB gap** between BPSK and BFSK, the **1 dB gap** between BPSK and DPSK, and the **2 bps/Hz bandwidth advantage** of QPSK over BPSK.
- **Mistakes to avoid:** Mixing dB and linear values, confusing $P_b$ with $P_s$, forgetting the Chernoff-bound-based asymptotic slope.
- **Industrial relevance:** 4G/5G adaptive modulation, satellite link budgets, deep-space telemetry, optical fiber QAM, Wi-Fi (BPSK to 1024-QAM).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: QPSK Transmitter and Receiver
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT502 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** QPSK Transmitter and Receiver # MODULE 4 — QPSK Transmitter and Receiver ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim)** > *Quadrature Phase Shift Keying (QPSK) is a digital modulation technique in which the phase of the carrier wave is discretely varied in accordance with the digital data to be transmitted, using four distinct phase states separated by 90°. Each symbol represents two bits of information, making QPSK a M-ary modulation scheme with M = 4.* ### 1.1 Formal Definition **Quadrature Phase Shift Keying (QPSK)** is an M-ary digital modulation scheme (M = 4) where the information is encoded in the **absolute phase** of a sinusoidal carrier. The carrier frequency and amplitude remain constant; only the phase shifts among four discrete values: $$\theta_i \in \left\{0,\ \frac{\pi}{2},\ \pi,\ \frac{3\pi}{2}\right\}$$ These four phases correspond to the four dibits (2-bit symbols): **00, 01, 11, 10** (typically arranged using **Gray coding** so that adjacent symbols differ by only one bit, minimizing bit errors). Because each symbol carries **2 bits**, the **symbol rate ($R_s$) is exactly half the bit rate ($R_b$)**: $$R_s = \frac{R_b}{2}$$ This makes QPSK **twice as bandwidth-efficient as BPSK** while maintaining the same bit error performance — a key reason it is used in 3G, satellite, cable modems (QAM is a generalization), and Wi-Fi. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Lighthouse and the Clock Imagine you are signalling to a friend on a distant ship using a powerful lighthouse. You can only flash it in four directions: **North, East, South, West** (separated by 90°). If each direction encodes a *pair* of letters (dibits) — N = 00, E = 01, S = 11, W = 10 — then with a single "flash," you are transmitting **2 letters worth of information** using the *same energy* that a single-letter code (BPSK) would use. The receiver simply notes the *direction* of the light, not its intensity. This is exactly how QPSK works: phase is the "direction" and dibits are the encoded message. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Takeaway:** QPSK trades a more complex transmitter/receiver (needs a quadrature carrier and coherent detection) for **half the bandwidth** of BPSK at the **same bit error rate**. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | | :--- | :---: | :--- | | Carrier frequency | $f_c$ | Radio-frequency band (MHz – GHz) | | Symbol duration | $T_s$ | **seconds (s)** | | Bit duration | $T_b$ | $T_b = 2T_s$ | | Bit energy | $E_b$ | Joules | | Symbol energy | $E_s$ | $E_s = 2E_b$ | | Spectral efficiency | $\eta$ | **2 bits/s/Hz** (ideal) | | Bandwidth (raised-cosine, $\alpha$) | $BW$ | $R_s(1+\alpha) = \frac{R_b}{2}(1+\alpha)$ | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Mathematical Representation of the QPSK Signal The general form of a QPSK signal over one symbol interval $kT_s \le t \le (k+1)T_s$ is: $$s_i(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}}\cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + \theta_i\right),\quad i = 1,2,3,4$$ Using the trigonometric identity $\cos(A+B) = \cos A\cos B - \sin A\sin B$, we split this into **In-phase (I)** and **Quadrature (Q)** components: $$s_i(t) = \underbrace{\sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}}\cos\theta_i \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t)}_{\text{In-phase (I) component}} - \underbrace{\sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}}\sin\theta_i \cdot \sin(2\pi f_c t)}_{\text{Quadrature (Q) component}}$$ Defining the orthonormal basis functions: $$\phi_1(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{T_s}}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$ $$\phi_2(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{T_s}}\sin(2\pi f_c t)$$ The four QPSK signal vectors in the signal-space (I–Q plane) are: | Phase $\theta_i$ | I-component $s_{i1}$ | Q-component $s_{i2}$ | Dibit (Gray) | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | $0$ | $+\sqrt{E_s}$ | $0$ | **00** | | $\pi/2$ | $0$ | $+\sqrt{E_s}$ | **01** | | $\pi$ | $-\sqrt{E_s}$ | $0$ | **11** | | $3\pi/2$ | $0$ | $-\sqrt{E_s}$ | **10** | ### 2.2 Why QPSK? — Step-by-Step Logic 1. **Bottleneck of BPSK**: BPSK transmits only 1 bit per symbol, so the required bandwidth equals the bit rate $R_b$. For high-speed links (e.g., 100 Mbps), this becomes impractical. 2. **M-ary solution**: By using $M=4$ phases, each symbol conveys $\log_2 4 = 2$ bits → symbol rate halves → bandwidth halves. 3. **Constant envelope**: The amplitude $\sqrt{2E_s/T_s}$ is constant; only phase varies. This makes QPSK robust against **non-linear amplifiers** (a property it shares with all PSK schemes). 4. **Coherent detection**: Both transmitter and receiver need precisely synchronized carriers (in phase and quadrature) — implemented using a **Costas loop** or **squaring loop** in practice. 5. **Spectral roll-off**: Practical systems use **root-raised cosine (RRC) pulse shaping** with roll-off $\alpha \in [0.2,\,0.5]$ to limit out-of-band emissions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** Whenever you draw the QPSK constellation, the four points must lie on a circle of radius $\sqrt{E_s}$, equally spaced at 90° intervals. Gray coding assigns adjacent dibits to adjacent phases so that the *most probable* error (noise pushing the phase across one boundary) flips only **one bit**, not two. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **MASTER THIS TABLE — these are the equations you must memorize for KTU ESE.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Bits per symbol | $k = \log_2 M$ | $M=4 \Rightarrow k=2$ | | 2 | Symbol rate | $R_s = R_b / k$ | Baud (symbols/sec) | | 3 | Symbol duration | $T_s = k \cdot T_b$ | $T_s = 2T_b$ | | 4 | Symbol energy | $E_s = k \cdot E_b$ | $E_s = 2E_b$ | | 5 | Bandwidth (ideal Nyquist) | $BW = R_s = R_b/2$ | Hz | | 6 | Bandwidth (raised cosine, roll-off $\alpha$) | $BW = R_s(1+\alpha)$ | $0 \le \alpha \le 1$ | | 7 | Spectral efficiency | $\eta = R_b / BW = \log_2 M$ | bits/s/Hz | | 8 | Constellation radius | $r = \sqrt{E_s}$ | — | | 9 | Minimum distance between symbols | $d_{\min} = \sqrt{2E_s}$ | Euclidean in I-Q plane | | 10 | Bit Error Probability (coherent) | $P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | Same as BPSK! | | 11 | Symbol Error Probability | $P_s \approx 2Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_s}{N_0}}\right)$ | For large SNR | | 12 | $\log_2 4$ | $= 2$ | Purely algebraic | > **Crucial non-table rule for LaTeX in this document:** Vertical bar symbols $\vert x \vert$ are written as `\vert x \vert` to prevent markdown-table corruption. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **3G UMTS / WCDMA**: Uses QPSK on the uplink and HPSK (a QPSK variant) for spreading. - **Satellite telemetry (INTELSAT, DVB-S)**: QPSK is dominant for downlink due to its power efficiency. - **Cable modems / DOCSIS**: Downstream is 64-/256-QAM (related to QPSK). - **RFID and NFC**: Lower-rate variants use BPSK; QPSK appears in ISO 14443 high-speed modes. - **GPS**: Uses BPSK on civilian L1/L2; QPSK is used in modernized signals (L5). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the QPSK Waveform at the Transmitter Output We start with an input bit stream $b_n \in \{0,1\}$ arriving at rate $R_b$ bits/s. **Step 1 — Serial-to-Parallel Conversion:** The serial bit stream is split into two parallel streams (even-indexed bits and odd-indexed bits), each running at $R_b/2 = R_s$. Let $I_k$ = even bits (in-phase bits), $Q_k$ = odd bits (quadrature bits), where $I_k, Q_k \in \{+1, -1\}$ (mapped from $\{0,1\}$). **Step 2 — Pulse Shaping:** Each bit stream is multiplied by a rectangular pulse $p(t)$ of width $T_s$ (or an RRC pulse in practice). The shaped baseband signals are: $$I(t) = \sum_k I_k \cdot p(t - kT_s)$$ $$Q(t) = \sum_k Q_k \cdot p(t - kT_s)$$ **Step 3 — Quadrature Modulation:** The two baseband signals modulate orthogonal carriers: $$s(t) = I(t)\sqrt{2}\cos(2\pi f_c t) - Q(t)\sqrt{2}\sin(2\pi f_c t)$$ **Step 4 — Substitution and Final Form:** For one symbol interval, this reduces to: $$s(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}}\left[I_k \cos(2\pi f_c t) - Q_k \sin(2\pi f_c t)\right],\quad kT_s \le t \le (k+1)T_s$$ Using the identity once more: $$s(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}}\cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + \theta_k\right)$$ where $\theta_k = \tan^{-1}(Q_k/I_k)$ gives the four possible phase values $\{0,\ \pi/2,\ \pi,\ 3\pi/2\}$. > **Logic note:** The minus sign in front of $Q(t)\sin(\cdot)$ is a *convention*; some textbooks place it on the I-branch.
