Both are correct as long as transmitter and receiver are consistent. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of the Bit Error Probability of QPSK We consider coherent detection in AWGN with PSD $N_0/2$. **Step 1 — Received signal:** $$r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t),\quad 0 \le t \le T_s$$ where $n(t)$ is zero-mean Gaussian noise with PSD $N_0/2$. **Step 2 — Correlator outputs (matched filter outputs):** The receiver projects $r(t)$ onto $\phi_1(t)$ and $\phi_2(t)$: $$r_1 = \int_0^{T_s} r(t)\phi_1(t)\,dt = s_{i1} + n_1$$ $$r_2 = \int_0^{T_s} r(t)\phi_2(t)\,dt = s_{i2} + n_2$$ **Step 3 — Noise statistics:** Each noise component $n_1, n_2$ is Gaussian with mean 0 and variance: $$\sigma^2 = \mathrm{Var}(n_1) = \mathrm{Var}(n_2) = \frac{N_0}{2}$$ **Step 4 — Decision regions:** For QPSK with the four symbols at $(\pm\sqrt{E_s}, 0)$ and $(0, \pm\sqrt{E_s})$, the optimal (ML) decision boundaries are the **axes** $r_1 = 0$ and $r_2 = 0$. **Step 5 — Probability of correct decision (per symbol, say $\theta_1 = 0$):** $$P_c = P(r_1 > 0 \text{ and } \vert r_2 \vert < \sqrt{E_s})$$ **Step 6 — Compute each factor (independence of $r_1$ and $r_2$):** $$P(r_1 > 0) = P\!\left(n_1 > -\sqrt{E_s}\right) = Q\!\left(-\frac{\sqrt{E_s}}{\sigma}\right) = 1 - Q\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{E_s}}{\sigma}\right)$$ Substituting $\sigma = \sqrt{N_0/2}$: $$P(r_1 > 0) = 1 - Q\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{E_s}}{\sqrt{N_0/2}}\right) = 1 - Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{N_0}}\right)$$ **Step 7 — The $\vert r_2 \vert$ term:** Since the transmitted $s_{i2} = 0$ for $\theta_1$, the variable $r_2$ is pure noise, so $\vert r_2 \vert < \sqrt{E_s}$ has probability: $$P(\vert n_2 \vert < \sqrt{E_s}) = 1 - 2Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{N_0}}\right)$$ **Step 8 — Symbol error probability:** $$P_s = 1 - P_c = 1 - \left[1 - Q\!\left(\sqrt{\tfrac{2E_s}{N_0}}\right)\right]\left[1 - 2Q\!\left(\sqrt{\tfrac{2E_s}{N_0}}\right)\right]$$ **Step 9 — Simplification (assuming $Q(\cdot) \ll 1$, i.e., moderate-to-high SNR):** The dominant term is the linear one: $$P_s \approx 2Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{N_0}}\right)$$ **Step 10 — Convert to bit error probability (Gray coding assumption):** Since adjacent symbol errors flip only 1 bit, the symbol error translates almost directly to bit error, with $E_s = 2E_b$: $$P_b \approx \frac{P_s}{2} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{N_0}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2(2E_b)}{N_0}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$ **Final boxed result:** $$\boxed{\,P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)\,}$$ This is **identical to the BPSK bit-error probability** — meaning QPSK gives BPSK's robustness at **half the bandwidth**. This is the single most important KTU result in this module. --- ### 3.3 Bandwidth Efficiency Comparison | Modulation | $k = \log_2 M$ | $BW$ (ideal) | $P_b$ (AWGN) | Spectral Efficiency $\eta$ | | :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: | | BPSK | 1 | $R_b$ | $Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)$ | 1 bit/s/Hz | | **QPSK** | **2** | $\mathbf{R_b/2}$ | $Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)$ | **2 bits/s/Hz** | | 16-PSK | 4 | $R_b/4$ | $Q\!\left(\sqrt{4E_b/N_0}\sin(\pi/16)\right)$ | 4 bits/s/Hz | | 16-QAM | 4 | $R_b/4$ | $\approx \frac{3}{4}Q\!\left(\sqrt{4E_b/(5N_0)}\right)$ | 4 bits/s/Hz | > **Logic note:** QPSK is the *sweet spot* — beyond it, pure PSK (16-PSK, 64-PSK) suffers because the constellation points crowd together on a circle, hurting error performance. QAM (mixed amplitude and phase) is then preferred. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 QPSK Transmitter — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Input Binary
Data Stream
Rb bits/s] --> B[Serial to
Parallel
Converter] B --> C1[Bit 1
Even Bits] B --> C2[Bit 2
Odd Bits] C1 --> D1[NRZ
Encoder
0 to -1, 1 to +1] C2 --> D2[NRZ
Encoder
0 to -1, 1 to +1] D1 --> E1[Multiplier
I Branch] D2 --> E2[Multiplier
Q Branch] O1[Local
Oscillator
cos 2pi fc t] --> E1 O1 --> F1[90 Degree
Phase
Shifter] F1 --> E2 E1 --> G[Summing
Amplifier
Combiner] E2 --> G G --> H[QPSK
Transmitted
Signal s of t] ``` **Block-Level Functional Walk-Through:** 1. **Input bits** arrive serially at rate $R_b$. 2. **Serial-to-Parallel (S/P)** splits them into two streams at rate $R_b/2$. 3. **NRZ encoders** map $\{0,1\} \to \{-1,+1\}$ for bipolar signaling. 4. **Multiplier (I-branch)** modulates the cosine carrier; the **Q-branch** modulates the sine carrier (90° shifted). 5. **Summing amplifier** linearly adds the two modulated branches to produce the QPSK waveform with constant envelope. --- ### 4.2 QPSK Coherent Receiver — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Received
Signal r of t
plus AWGN] --> S1[Multiplier 1
I Branch] A --> S2[Multiplier 2
Q Branch] L[Local
Oscillator
cos 2pi fc t] --> S1 L --> P1[90 Degree
Phase
Shifter] P1 --> S2 S1 --> I1[Integrator 1
0 to Ts] S2 --> I2[Integrator 2
0 to Ts] I1 --> D1[Sampler
at t equals kTs] I2 --> D2[Sampler
at t equals kTs] D1 --> T1[Threshold
Detector
I decision] D2 --> T2[Threshold
Detector
Q decision] T1 --> M[Parallel to
Serial
Converter] T2 --> M M --> OUT[Recovered
Binary
Output] CAR[Carrier
Recovery
Costas Loop] -.-> L SYM[Symbol
Timing
Recovery] -.-> D1 SYM -.-> D2 ``` **Block-Level Functional Walk-Through:** 1. **Received signal** $r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t)$ enters the receiver. 2. **Carrier recovery** (a Costas loop or squaring loop) regenerates a coherent local oscillator locked to the incoming carrier. 3. **Two multipliers** correlate the received signal with the I and Q basis functions $\phi_1(t), \phi_2(t)$. 4. **Integrators (matched filters)** integrate over a symbol period $T_s$ to maximize SNR. 5. **Samplers** capture the integrator output at $t = kT_s$ (timing supplied by symbol clock recovery). 6. **Threshold detectors** compare each sample to zero and decide whether the bit was $+1$ or $-1$. 7. **Parallel-to-Serial (P/S)** recombines the two parallel bit streams into the original serial bit stream. --- ### 4.3 QPSK Constellation Diagram (Mermaid Signal-Space) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CONST[QPSK Constellation on the I-Q Plane] direction TB Q1["Symbol 01 — phase pi by 2 — top — I equals 0, Q equals plus sqrt Es"] Q2["Symbol 11 — phase pi — left — I equals minus sqrt Es, Q equals 0"] Q3["Symbol 00 — phase 0 — right — I equals plus sqrt Es, Q equals 0"] Q4["Symbol 10 — phase 3pi by 2 — bottom — I equals 0, Q equals minus sqrt Es"] end Q3 --- Q1 Q1 --- Q2 Q2 --- Q4 Q4 --- Q3 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Visualization Rule:** Always draw the QPSK constellation as **four points on a circle of radius $\sqrt{E_s}$**, with the **I-axis horizontal** and **Q-axis vertical**, axes labelled, and Gray-coded dibits adjacent. This is a guaranteed 3-mark question type. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) --- **Q1.** *[KTU University Exam — May 2024]* **Define QPSK. What is the bandwidth efficiency of QPSK compared to BPSK?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **QPSK (Quadrature Phase Shift Keying)** is a digital modulation technique in which the phase of the carrier takes one of four equally spaced values, namely $0,\ \pi/2,\ \pi,$ and $3\pi/2$, with each phase corresponding to a unique 2-bit symbol (dibit). Since each symbol carries 2 bits, the symbol rate $R_s = R_b/2$, and the minimum Nyquist bandwidth is also halved compared to BPSK. > > **Bandwidth efficiency of QPSK = 2 bits/s/Hz**, which is **double** that of BPSK (1 bit/s/Hz), while delivering the **same bit error probability** under coherent detection in AWGN. *(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for bandwidth, 1 for efficiency comparison.)* --- **Q2.** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]* **Why is Gray coding preferred for dibit assignment in QPSK?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > In QPSK, the most probable error at moderate-to-high SNR is the noise pushing the received phase across the *nearest* decision boundary — a 90° phase error. Under Gray coding, **adjacent constellation points differ in only one bit**. Therefore, a single phase error causes only **one bit error** rather than two, halving the bit error rate relative to the symbol error rate. The assignment typically used is: > - Phase $0° \to$ **00** > - Phase $90° \to$ **01** > - Phase $180° \to$ **11** > - Phase $270° \to$ **10** > > This ensures that neighbouring phases always have Hamming distance 1, minimizing $P_b$. *(3 marks — 1 for nearest-boundary argument, 1 for Hamming distance, 1 for the mapping example.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Questions (Internal Choice, 14 Marks) --- #### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *Design & Derivation Focus* **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4, Set A]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a **QPSK transmitter**. Show the mathematical expression of the transmitted QPSK signal and describe how the four phase states are generated. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **bit error probability** of coherent QPSK in AWGN, and show that it is the same as that of coherent BPSK. Compare the **bandwidth requirements** of QPSK and BPSK. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution to Q.A(a):** 1. **Block diagram** (draw the transmitter from §4.1 — 3 marks: input, S/P, two multipliers, LO with 90° shifter, summer). 2. **Bit stream splitting**: incoming bits at $R_b$ are grouped into pairs (dibits) and split into two parallel streams at $R_b/2$. *(1 mark)* 3. **NRZ mapping** converts $\{0,1\}$ to $\{-1,+1\}$ producing $I_k$ and $Q_k$. *(1 mark)* 4. **Mathematical expression** for one symbol interval $kT_s \le t \le (k+1)T_s$: $$s(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}}\left[I_k\cos(2\pi f_c t) - Q_k\sin(2\pi f_c t)\right] = \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}}\cos(2\pi f_c t + \theta_k)$$ where $\theta_k = \tan^{-1}(Q_k/I_k) \in \{0,\pi/2,\pi,3\pi/2\}$. *(1 mark)* 5. **Generation of four phases**: The I and Q bits control the polarity of the cosine and sine carriers; the linear combination (summer output) yields the four phase states. *(1 mark)* --- **Model Solution to Q.A(b):** 1. **Received signal**: $r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t)$ with $n(t)$ having PSD $N_0/2$. *(1 mark)* 2. **Correlator outputs** (using $\phi_1, \phi_2$ from §2.1): $$r_1 = s_{i1} + n_1, \quad r_2 = s_{i2} + n_2, \quad \mathrm{Var}(n_1) = \mathrm{Var}(n_2) = N_0/2$$ *(1 mark)* 3. **Decision regions** are the I- and Q-axes. For symbol at $(\sqrt{E_s}, 0)$, correct decision requires $r_1 > 0$ and $\vert r_2 \vert < \sqrt{E_s}$. *(1 mark)* 4. **Symbol error probability** (dominant term): $$P_s \approx 2Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{N_0}}\right)$$ *(1 mark)* 5. **Conversion to bit error** (Gray coding): $P_b \approx P_s/2 = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_s/N_0}\right)$. Substituting $E_s = 2E_b$: $$\boxed{P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)}$$ *(1 mark)* 6. **Comparison with BPSK**: BPSK's $P_b = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ — **identical**. *(1 mark)* 7. **Bandwidth comparison**: BPSK needs $BW = R_b$; QPSK needs $BW = R_b/2$ (ideal Nyquist). With raised-cosine roll-off $\alpha$: $$BW_{BPSK} = R_b(1+\alpha), \quad BW_{QPSK} = \frac{R_b}{2}(1+\alpha)$$ Hence QPSK uses **half the bandwidth of BPSK** for the same bit rate and same error performance. *(1 mark)* --- #### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Receiver & Analysis Focus* **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4, Set B]** **(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the operation of a **coherent QPSK receiver**. What is the role of the **Costas loop** in this context? **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a QPSK system operating at a bit rate of $R_b = 2$ Mbps over an AWGN channel with $E_b/N_0 = 10$ (linear), compute: (i) the **symbol rate**, (ii) the **minimum Nyquist bandwidth**, and (iii) the **theoretical bit error probability** using $Q\!\left(\sqrt{20}\right) \approx 2.87 \times 10^{-10}$. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution to Q.B(a):** 1. **Block diagram** of coherent QPSK receiver (see §4.2 — 3 marks: two multipliers, two integrators, samplers, threshold detectors, P/S converter, with carrier and timing recovery shown). 2. **Operation**: The received signal is correlated with locally generated $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and $\sin(2\pi f_c t)$, then integrated over $T_s$, sampled, and threshold-detected. The two parallel decisions are recombined by P/S. *(2 marks)* 3. **Role of Costas loop**: It is a **carrier-recovery circuit** that locks the local oscillator to the suppressed-carrier QPSK signal. Because QPSK has no DC component (the signal is BPSK on each arm), ordinary PLL cannot lock directly. The Costas loop uses two multipliers with the in-phase and quadrature references and a feedback loop to drive the VCO to the correct frequency and phase. Without it, coherent detection is impossible. *(2 marks)* --- **Model Solution to Q.B(b):** **(i) Symbol rate** $R_s$: $$R_s = \frac{R_b}{2} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{2} = 1 \times 10^6 \text{ symbols/s} = 1 \text{ Mbaud}$$ *(2 marks)* **(ii) Minimum Nyquist bandwidth** (ideal, $\alpha = 0$): $$BW_{\min} = R_s = 1 \text{ MHz}$$ *(2 marks)* **(iii) Bit error probability**: $$P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 \times 10}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{20}\right) \approx 2.87 \times 10^{-10}$$ *(3 marks — 1 for substituting $E_b/N_0$, 1 for $\sqrt{20}$, 1 for the numerical value)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — read this before you write the exam:** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $E_s = 2E_b$** — the single most common error in $P_b$ derivations. (Lose 2 marks.) > 2. **Confusing bandwidth with bit rate** — in QPSK, $BW = R_s = R_b/2$, **not** $R_b$. (Lose 1 mark.) > 3. **Writing $P_b = Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$** instead of $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ — drops the BPSK-equivalence proof. (Lose 2 marks.) > 4. **Not mentioning Gray coding** when computing $P_b$ from $P_s$ — without it, the $P_b = P_s/2$ shortcut is unjustified. (Lose 1 mark.) > 5. **Skipping the $90°$ phase shifter** in the block diagram — KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks for an incomplete diagram. > 6. **No mention of carrier recovery** in the receiver — coherent QPSK *requires* a Costas/squaring loop; stating "we multiply by a local oscillator" without explaining *how it is synchronized* loses 1–2 marks. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print this checklist and revise 30 minutes before the exam.** - [ ] QPSK = **M = 4** PSK; phases are $\theta_i \in \{0,\ \pi/2,\ \pi,\ 3\pi/2\}$. - [ ] Each QPSK symbol carries **$k = \log_2 4 = 2$ bits** (one dibit). - [ ] Symbol rate $R_s = R_b / 2$ (one-half the bit rate). - [ ] Symbol energy $E_s = 2 E_b$ (twice the per-bit energy). - [ ] Constellation: **four points on a circle of radius $\sqrt{E_s}$**, 90° apart. - [ ] **Gray coding** maps adjacent dibits to adjacent phases → minimizes $P_b$. - [ ] Transmitter uses a **serial-to-parallel** splitter, two multipliers, a local oscillator, a **90° phase shifter**, and a summer. - [ ] Receiver uses **two correlators** (or matched filters), samplers at $kT_s$, threshold detectors, and a **parallel-to-serial** converter. - [ ] **Carrier recovery** (Costas loop or squaring loop) is mandatory for coherent detection. - [ ] **Bit error probability** of coherent QPSK in AWGN: $$P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$ This is **identical to coherent BPSK**. - [ ] **Symbol error probability** (approximate, high SNR): $$P_s \approx 2Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_s}{N_0}}\right) = 2Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$ - [ ] **Minimum Nyquist bandwidth** of QPSK = $R_b/2$ (half of BPSK). - [ ] **Spectral efficiency** of QPSK = **2 bits/s/Hz** (twice BPSK). - [ ] Raised-cosine bandwidth: $BW = (R_b/2)(1+\alpha)$ where $\alpha$ is the roll-off factor, $0 \le \alpha \le 1$. - [ ] Envelope of QPSK is **constant** → robust to non-linear amplifiers. - [ ] QPSK is used in **3G UMTS, DVB-S, satellite telemetry, cable modems, RFID high-speed modes**. - [ ] Draw the block diagram of transmitter and receiver with **all labels** — KTU awards 3 marks purely for a correct, fully-labelled block diagram. - [ ] Always end derivations with a **boxed final formula** — examiners scan for it.

topics.title: Quadrature Amplitude Modulation and Signal Constellation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT502 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Quadrature Amplitude Modulation and Signal Constellation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Quadrature Amplitude Modulation (QAM)** is a two-dimensional digital passband modulation technique in which two independent baseband data streams (the *In-phase* $\mathbf{I}(t)$ and *Quadrature-phase* $\mathbf{Q}(t)$ signals) modulate a pair of orthogonal (mutually quadrature) carrier waves of identical frequency $f_c$. Because the carriers are $90^{\circ}$ apart, they occupy identical spectral real-estate without mutual interference, effectively **doubling the data rate of binary PSK within the same transmission bandwidth**.

A **Signal Constellation** is the geometric scatter-plot of all permissible complex baseband symbols $s_i = I_i + jQ_i$ on the In-phase–Quadrature ($\mathbf{IQ}$) plane. The Euclidean distance between any two constellation points directly determines the noise immunity (Bit Error Probability) of the modulation scheme.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):**
> Quadrature Amplitude Modulation is universally tested as a high-weightage topic because it consolidates **AM, BPSK, and orthogonal signaling** into a single elegant framework. Every M-ary constellation (4-QAM, 16-QAM, 64-QAM, 256-QAM) is a *Kerala University Examiner favourite*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Two-Person Wooden Boat

Imagine a long narrow canal where a single boat (the carrier) sails. One single rower can only encode **one piece of information** (left-stroke or right-stroke). Now place **two rowers** in the same boat, sitting at right angles to each other:

* **Rower 1** (front-facing) controls the *length* of the forward push → modulates the **In-phase (I)** amplitude.
* **Rower 2** (sideways-facing) controls the *length* of the lateral push → modulates the **Quadrature (Q)** amplitude.

The boat still travels along **one** canal (one carrier frequency, one bandwidth), but the combined vector of pushes now reaches many more unique landing points on a 2-D grid — exactly how QAM symbols populate the **constellation diagram**. The receiver simply observes *where the boat landed* and decodes the (I, Q) tuple.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

* Carrier orthogonality angle: **$90^{\circ}$** or **$\pi/2$ radians**.
* Standard M-ary QAM levels: **$M = 2^{k}$** where $k$ = bits/symbol.
* Standard digital cellular constants referenced in KTU: $M = 4, 16, 64, 256$.
* Reference symbol-energy normalization: minimum Euclidean distance **$d_{min} = 2$** units (KTU convention).

> [!NOTE]
> **4-QAM is mathematically identical to QPSK.** The constellation rotates by $45^{\circ}$ but the symbol-error probability expression is the same. This is a recurring trick question in KTU papers.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Decomposition — The "Why" and the "How"

**Step 1 — Why do we need two carriers?**
Binary modulation schemes (BPSK, ASK) carry only **1 bit/symbol**. To increase spectral efficiency ($\eta$ bits/sec/Hz) without expanding bandwidth, we must exploit a *second orthogonal dimension*. The simplest orthogonal basis in passband signaling is the cosine-sine pair:

$$\phi_1(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{T_s}}\cos(2\pi f_c t), \quad \phi_2(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{T_s}}\sin(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 2 — How is orthogonality achieved?**
Over one symbol interval $T_s$, the inner product vanishes:

$$\int_{0}^{T_s} \phi_1(t)\phi_2(t)\, dt = 0$$

This guarantees the receiver can **uniquely recover** $I_k$ and $Q_k$ from the noisy received waveform $r(t)$ by correlation.

**Step 3 — How are symbols mapped?**
Each $k$-bit block is split into two $(k/2)$-bit sub-blocks. The first sub-block selects the in-phase amplitude $I_i \in \{\pm a, \pm 3a, \dots\}$ and the second selects the quadrature amplitude $Q_j \in \{\pm a, \pm 3a, \dots\}$.

**Step 4 — How is the constellation read?**
The IQ plane is divided into $L \times L = M$ cells where $L = \sqrt{M}$. A noisy received symbol is *hard-decision decoded* to the nearest constellation vertex (minimum Euclidean distance rule).

**Step 5 — Why does higher $M$ reduce power efficiency?**
As $M$ grows, the constellation becomes denser. For fixed average energy $E_{av}$, the Euclidean spacing $d_{min}$ shrinks, shrinking the noise margin and inflating $P_s$.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | M-ary QAM transmitted symbol | $s(t)=I_k\sqrt{\tfrac{2}{T_s}}\cos(2\pi f_c t)-Q_k\sqrt{\tfrac{2}{T_s}}\sin(2\pi f_c t)$ | $0 \le t \le T_s$ |
| 2 | Bits per symbol | $k = \log_2 M$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Number of signal levels per axis | $L = \sqrt{M}$ | required $M$ to be a perfect square |
| 4 | Symbol energy (rectangular $L\times L$ QAM) | $E_s = (I_k^2+Q_k^2)\tfrac{T_s}{2}$ | Joules |
| 5 | Average symbol energy (uniform L-levels) | $E_{av} = \dfrac{2d_{min}^{2}(M-1)}{3}$ | Joules |
| 6 | Average bit energy | $E_{b,av} = E_{av}/k = E_{av}/\log_2 M$ | Joules/bit |
| 7 | Symbol Error Probability (exact, square QAM) | $P_s = 1-\bigl(1-P_{\sqrt{M}}\bigr)^{2}$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Inner PAM symbol-error for $\sqrt{M}$-PAM | $P_{\sqrt{M}} = \dfrac{2(L-1)}{L}\,Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{3\,E_{av}}{(L^{2}-1)N_0}}\right)$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Approximate $P_s$ for large $E_b/N_0$ | $P_s \approx \dfrac{4(\sqrt{M}-1)}{\sqrt{M}}\,Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{3\,k\,E_{b,av}}{(M-1)N_0}}\right)$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Bandwidth Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{\log_2 M}{2}$ | bits/sec/Hz (Nyquist roll-off $\alpha = 0$) |
| 11 | Bandwidth (raised-cosine, roll-off $\alpha$) | $B_T = \dfrac{(1+\alpha)R_b}{\log_2 M}$ | Hz |
| 12 | Minimum Euclidean distance (KTU standard) | $d_{min} = 2a$ (half the spacing) | reference |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In every KTU derivation, the **Marcum-Q function** $Q(x)$ is written explicitly as $Q(x) = \frac{1}{2}\operatorname{erfc}(x/\sqrt{2})$. Memorize the substitution; it appears in *almost* every solution script.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11 a/g/n/ac/ax):** Adapts from **BPSK → 1024-QAM** based on channel SNR.
* **4G LTE & 5G NR:** Downlink PDSCH uses **64-QAM, 256-QAM**, with **1024-QAM** in 5G-Advanced.
* **DVB-C (digital cable TV):** Uses **64-QAM / 256-QAM** for high-bitrate broadcast.
* **ADSL/VDSL:** Uses **DMT-QAM** constellations (up to 32768-QAM equivalent per tone).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the M-ary QAM Transmitted Waveform

We begin with two independent $L$-level PAM data streams, $I_k$ and $Q_k$, each selected from the alphabet:

$$\mathcal{A}_{L} = \{\pm a, \pm 3a, \pm 5a, \dots, \pm (L-1)a\}$$

so that the inner-product normalization constant $a$ sets the constellation scale. The two-dimensional symbol is constructed as the complex baseband vector:

$$\tilde{s}_k = I_k + j\,Q_k$$

Modulating the orthonormal passband basis $\{\phi_1(t),\phi_2(t)\}$, the transmitted passband waveform during symbol interval $kT_s \le t < (k+1)T_s$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_k(t) &= I_k\,\phi_1(t) - Q_k\,\phi_2(t) \\
&= I_k\sqrt{\frac{2}{T_s}}\cos(2\pi f_c t) - Q_k\sqrt{\frac{2}{T_s}}\sin(2\pi f_c t)
\end{aligned}
$$

The **minus sign** convention is adopted because the receiver's in-phase branch correlates with $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and the quadrature branch with $-\sin(2\pi f_c t)$, ensuring identical output gain on both arms.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Average Symbol Energy $E_{av}$

The instantaneous symbol energy is:

$$
E_s = \int_{0}^{T_s} s_k^{2}(t)\, dt = \frac{T_s}{2}\bigl(I_k^{2}+Q_k^{2}\bigr)
$$

Substituting $I_k = (2m-1-L)a$ for $m \in \{1,2,\dots,L\}$ and similarly for $Q_k$, the ensemble average over the uniform $L\times L$ alphabet yields:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{av} &= \frac{T_s}{2}\,\mathbb{E}\bigl[I_k^{2}+Q_k^{2}\bigr] \\
&= \frac{T_s}{2}\,\Bigl(\mathbb{E}[I_k^{2}]+\mathbb{E}[Q_k^{2}]\Bigr) \\
&= \frac{T_s}{2}\,2\,\mathbb{E}[I_k^{2}] \\
&= T_s\,\mathbb{E}[I_k^{2}]
\end{aligned}
$$

The inner expectation is evaluated using the standard $\sum_{m=1}^{L}(2m-1-L)^2$ identity:

$$
\mathbb{E}[I_k^{2}] = \frac{2}{3}\,a^{2}(L^{2}-1)
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,E_{av} = \frac{2}{3}\,T_s\,a^{2}(L^{2}-1) = \frac{2\,d_{min}^{2}(M-1)}{3}\,}
$$

where we have used the substitution $d_{min} = 2a$ (adjacent points along either axis differ by $2a$).

### 3.3 Derivation of Symbol Error Probability $P_s$

Consider AWGN with two-sided PSD $N_0/2$. The receiver forms two decision variables $r_I = I_k + n_I$ and $r_Q = Q_k + n_Q$, each Gaussian with variance $\sigma^{2}=N_0/2$.

**Step 1 — Per-axis correct-decision probability.**
For an $L$-level PAM on the I-axis, the correct-decision probability is:

$$P_{c,I} = 1 - P_{\sqrt{M}} = \left(1 - \frac{2(L-1)}{L}Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{3E_{av}}{(L^{2}-1)N_0}}\right)\right)$$

**Step 2 — Independence of the two axes.**
Because $n_I$ and $n_Q$ are independent Gaussian components, the joint correct-decision probability is the product:

$$P_c = P_{c,I}\cdot P_{c,Q} = (1-P_{\sqrt{M}})^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Symbol error probability.**

$$
P_s = 1 - P_c = 1 - (1-P_{\sqrt{M}})^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — High-SNR approximation.**
For small $P_{\sqrt{M}}$, expand $(1-x)^{2} \approx 1-2x$:

$$
\boxed{\,P_s \approx \frac{4(\sqrt{M}-1)}{\sqrt{M}}\,Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{3kE_{b,av}}{(M-1)N_0}}\right)\,}
$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula** — it is the one written verbatim in the valuation key.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A 16-QAM system transmits $90\text{ Mbps}$ over a channel of bandwidth $B = 15\text{ MHz}$ with raised-cosine roll-off $\alpha = 0.5$. Compute (i) bandwidth efficiency, (ii) average symbol energy if $d_{min}=2\,\mu\text{V·s}$, and (iii) $P_s$ for $E_b/N_0 = 12\text{ dB}$.

**Solution:**

* (i) For $M=16$, $k=4$. Symbol rate $R_s = R_b/k = 90/4 = 22.5\text{ Msps}$. Minimum Nyquist bandwidth is $R_s/2 = 11.25\text{ MHz}$. With raised cosine, $B_T = (1+\alpha)R_s/2 = 1.5 \times 11.25 = 16.875\text{ MHz}$ (≈ 15 MHz after rounding). Efficiency:

$$\eta = \frac{R_b}{B_T} = \frac{90}{15} = 6\;\text{bits/sec/Hz}$$

* (ii) $M=16 \Rightarrow M-1 = 15$:

$$E_{av} = \frac{2 d_{min}^{2}(M-1)}{3} = \frac{2(2\times 10^{-6})^{2}(15)}{3} = 8\times 10^{-11}\;\text{J}$$

* (iii) $E_b/N_0 = 12$ dB $\Rightarrow$ linear $= 15.849$. $k=4$, $M=16$:

$$\sqrt{\frac{3kE_{b,av}}{(M-1)N_0}} = \sqrt{\frac{3 \times 4 \times 15.849}{15}} = \sqrt{12.679} \approx 3.561$$

$$P_s \approx \frac{4(\sqrt{16}-1)}{\sqrt{16}}\,Q(3.561) = \frac{4\times 3}{4}\times 1.85\times 10^{-4} \approx 5.55\times 10^{-4}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The $Q(3.56)$ value is obtained from standard Q-function tables. KTU evaluators accept $\pm 5\%$ tabular interpolation.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 QAM Transmitter — Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Serial Bit Stream] --> B[Serial to Parallel Converter]
    B --> C1[Upper k/2 Bits]
    B --> C2[Lower k/2 Bits]
    C1 --> D1[2 to L Level Mapper I channel]
    C2 --> D2[2 to L Level Mapper Q channel]
    D1 --> E1[Multiplier with cos 2 pi fc t]
    D2 --> E2[Multiplier with sin 2 pi fc t]
    E1 --> F[Summing Junction]
    E2 --> F
    F --> G[Channel AWGN h t]
    G --> H[Receive Filter]
    H --> I[Matched Filter Bank]
    I --> J[Decision Device]
    J --> K[Parallel to Serial]
    K --> L[Recovered Bit Stream]

    style A fill:#e8f4ff,stroke:#1f77b4
    style L fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#2ca02c
```

### 4.2 QAM Receiver — Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R[Received r of t] --> S1[Multiply by cos 2 pi fc t]
    R --> S2[Multiply by sin 2 pi fc t]
    S1 --> T1[Integrate over Ts]
    S2 --> T2[Integrate over Ts]
    T1 --> U1[Sample rI]
    T2 --> U2[Sample rQ]
    U1 --> V1[I Slice Detector]
    U2 --> V2[Q Slice Detector]
    V1 --> W1[L to 2 Decoder I]
    V2 --> W2[L to 2 Decoder Q]
    W1 --> X[Parallel to Serial]
    W2 --> X
    X --> Y[Recovered Bits]

    style R fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#ff7f0e
    style Y fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#2ca02c
```

### 4.3 Signal Constellation — Geometric Topology

The constellation for $M=16$ (rectangular 16-QAM) consists of a $4 \times 4$ grid of equi-spaced points. The Euclidean neighborhood of every interior point is geometrically identical (4 nearest neighbours at distance $2a$ along I or Q axis). This uniformity is the *defining feature* that makes square QAM the preferred choice for AWGN channels.

```
       Q-axis
         |
    3a   |   .  .  .  .
         |
    a    |   .  .  .  .
         |
   ---a--+---.---.---.---.----> I-axis
         |   .   .   .   .
   -a    |   .  .  .  .
         |
  -3a    |   .  .  .  .
         |
       -3a  -a   a   3a
```

The decision regions are the **Voronoi tessellation** — square cells of side $2a$ centred on each constellation point. Any received sample $(r_I, r_Q)$ falls in exactly one cell and is decoded to the corresponding vertex.

> [!NOTE]
> For *cross-shaped* 16-QAM (KTU sometimes calls it "star QAM"), the corner points are removed to give 12 or 16 points in a non-rectangular layout — but rectangular 16-QAM is the *standard* KTU reference.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]**

**Q1. Define signal constellation. With a neat diagram, explain the constellation diagram of 16-QAM. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **signal constellation** is the two-dimensional scatter-plot of the complex baseband symbols $s_i = I_i + jQ_i$ of a digital passband modulation scheme on the In-phase–Quadrature (IQ) plane. Each point represents one unique transmitted symbol during one symbol period $T_s$. The Euclidean distance $d_{ij} = \sqrt{(I_i-I_j)^{2}+(Q_i-Q_j)^{2}}$ between any two points determines the noise immunity; larger $d_{min}$ implies lower $P_s$.

For **16-QAM**, the constellation is a $4 \times 4$ rectangular grid. The points have coordinates $\{(\pm a, \pm a), (\pm a, \pm 3a), (\pm 3a, \pm a), (\pm 3a, \pm 3a)\}$, each corresponding to a unique 4-bit pattern. The minimum Euclidean distance is $d_{min} = 2a$, achieved between horizontally/vertically adjacent points. *(Marks: 1 + 1 + 1)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Compare 4-QAM and BPSK in terms of bandwidth efficiency and bit error probability. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | 4-QAM (QPSK) | BPSK |
|---|---|---|
| Bits/symbol $k$ | **2** | **1** |
| Bandwidth efficiency $\eta$ | **2 bits/s/Hz** | **1 bit/s/Hz** |
| Symbol-error $P_s$ | $2Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ | $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ |
| Bit-error $P_b$ | $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ | $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ |

**Key conclusion:** 4-QAM (QPSK) and BPSK have *identical* bit-error probability but 4-QAM uses **half the bandwidth** for the same bit rate. *(Marks: 1.5 + 1.5)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample QP, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the expression for the transmitted signal of an M-ary QAM system. Show that it can be represented as two independent PAM signals modulating two quadrature carriers. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a 16-QAM system with average symbol energy $E_{av} = 1.6 \times 10^{-10}\;\text{J}$ and noise PSD $N_0/2 = 10^{-20}\;\text{W/Hz}$, compute the probability of symbol error. Use the standard approximation. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

Step 1 — Represent two independent $L$-level PAM streams: $I_k \in \{\pm a, \pm 3a, \dots, \pm (L-1)a\}$ and $Q_k \in \{\pm a, \pm 3a, \dots, \pm (L-1)a\}$ where $L = \sqrt{M}$ and $k = \log_2 M$ bits/symbol.

Step 2 — Define the orthonormal basis functions:

$$\phi_1(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{T_s}}\cos(2\pi f_c t),\quad \phi_2(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{T_s}}\sin(2\pi f_c t)$$

Step 3 — Orthogonality: $\int_0^{T_s}\phi_1(t)\phi_2(t)dt = 0$. ✓

Step 4 — QAM symbol:

$$
s_k(t) = I_k\phi_1(t) - Q_k\phi_2(t)
$$

Step 5 — Substitute:

$$
\boxed{\,s_k(t) = I_k\sqrt{\frac{2}{T_s}}\cos(2\pi f_c t) - Q_k\sqrt{\frac{2}{T_s}}\sin(2\pi f_c t)\,}
$$

Step 6 — Note $I_k$ and $Q_k$ are *statistically independent* because they arise from independent bit-streams. Hence QAM is decomposable into two independent $\sqrt{M}$-PAM signals on orthogonal carriers. *(Marks: 1+1+1+2+1+1)*

**(b) Computation (7 Marks):**

Step 1 — For 16-QAM: $M = 16 \Rightarrow k = 4$, $L = 4$.

Step 2 — Compute $E_{av}/N_0$:

$$E_{av}/N_0 = \frac{1.6\times 10^{-10}}{2\times 10^{-20}} = 8\times 10^{9}$$

Step 3 — Compute $E_{b,av} = E_{av}/k = 0.4\times 10^{-10}\;\text{J}$.

Step 4 — Argument of $Q$:

$$
x = \sqrt{\frac{3kE_{b,av}}{(M-1)N_0}} = \sqrt{\frac{3\times 4 \times 0.4\times 10^{-10}}{15 \times 2\times 10^{-20}}} = \sqrt{\frac{4.8\times 10^{-10}}{3\times 10^{-19}}} = \sqrt{1.6\times 10^{9}}
$$

Step 5 — $x \approx 40000$, hence $Q(x) \approx 0$ (saturation regime). Symbol error:

$$
P_s \approx \frac{4(\sqrt{16}-1)}{\sqrt{16}}\,Q(40000) = 3 \times Q(40000) \approx 0
$$

Step 6 — Engineering conclusion: at this $E_b/N_0$, the 16-QAM link is essentially *error-free*. *(Marks: 1+1+1+1+2+1)*

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the geometric structure of a 16-QAM signal constellation. Discuss why rectangular QAM is preferred over circular (PSK) constellations for $M \geq 16$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A digital link uses 64-QAM with raised-cosine filtering (roll-off $\alpha = 0.25$). If the bit rate is $144\;\text{Mbps}$, determine (i) symbol rate, (ii) transmission bandwidth, (iii) bandwidth efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Geometric Structure (7 Marks):**

The 16-QAM constellation is a $4 \times 4$ rectangular grid with coordinates $\{(\pm a, \pm a), (\pm a, \pm 3a), (\pm 3a, \pm a), (\pm 3a, \pm 3a)\}$. Each point represents one of $2^4 = 16$ possible 4-bit patterns.

**Why rectangular QAM beats PSK for $M \ge 16$:**

* **Distance gain:** For fixed average energy $E_{av}$, the minimum Euclidean distance of square M-QAM grows as $\sqrt{M}$ rather than remaining constant (PSK). Concretely:
$$d_{min}^{QAM} \approx \sqrt{\frac{6E_{av}}{M-1}}, \quad d_{min}^{PSK} \approx 2\sqrt{E_{av}}\sin(\pi/M)$$

* **Power efficiency:** For $M=16$, QAM requires roughly **4 dB less** $E_b/N_0$ than 16-PSK for the same $P_s = 10^{-5}$.

* **Detection simplicity:** Decision regions are axis-aligned rectangles — implementable as two independent 1-D slicers, instead of phase-bucket detectors for PSK.

* **Disadvantage:** QAM has *non-constant envelope*, so it is more sensitive to non-linear amplification (requires linear PA or predistortion). *(Marks: 2+2+2+1)*

**(b) Computation (7 Marks):**

(i) Symbol rate:

$$R_s = \frac{R_b}{\log_2 M} = \frac{144 \times 10^{6}}{6} = 24\;\text{Msps}$$

(ii) Transmission bandwidth with raised cosine:

$$B_T = \frac{(1+\alpha)R_s}{2} = \frac{1.25 \times 24\times 10^{6}}{2} = 15\;\text{MHz}$$

(iii) Bandwidth efficiency:

$$\eta = \frac{R_b}{B_T} = \frac{144}{15} = 9.6\;\text{bits/sec/Hz}$$

Equivalently $\eta = k/(1+\alpha) = 6/1.25 = 4.8$ — note these formulas give **Nyquist-equivalent** values; using actual $B_T$ gives the practical ratio. *(Marks: 2+3+2)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Sign error in $s_k(t)$:** Writing $+Q_k\sin$ instead of $-Q_k\sin$. Although both are valid (it merely rotates the constellation), the *examiner expects the minus-sign convention* used in Carlson/Proakis textbooks. Losing **1 mark** per occurrence.
>
> 2. **Confusing $E_s$ with $E_{av}$:** When the question says *"average symbol energy"*, you **must** include the factor $2(L^{2}-1)/3$. A bare $T_s a^{2}$ loses **2 marks**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the factor 4 in $P_s$ approximation:** The exact formula has *no factor 4*, but the KTU high-SNR approximation does. Writing $2Q(\cdot)$ instead of $4Q(\cdot)$ costs **1 mark**.
>
> 4. **Mixing up $L$ and $M$:** $L = \sqrt{M}$ is non-negotiable. Sloppy notation like $M=16$ when $L=16$ would be marked *immediately wrong*.
>
> 5. **Not labelling axes of the constellation:** "Diagram without axis labels" attracts a flat **1-mark deduction** in KTU 2024 valuation scheme.

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

* **QAM = 2-D passband signaling** using orthogonal carriers $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and $\sin(2\pi f_c t)$.
* **$M$-ary QAM = 2 independent $L$-PAM streams** with $L=\sqrt{M}$, $k=\log_2 M$.
* **Transmitted signal:** $s_k(t) = I_k\sqrt{2/T_s}\cos(2\pi f_c t) - Q_k\sqrt{2/T_s}\sin(2\pi f_c t)$.
* **Signal constellation** = scatter plot of $\{I_k+jQ_k\}$ on IQ plane; decision regions are Voronoi cells.
* **Average energy:** $E_{av} = \dfrac{2d_{min}^{2}(M-1)}{3}$.
* **Symbol error (approx):** $P_s \approx \dfrac{4(\sqrt{M}-1)}{\sqrt{M}}Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{3kE_{b,av}}{(M-1)N_0}}\right)$.
* **4-QAM ≡ QPSK** in $P_b$ but 4-QAM uses half the bandwidth.
* **Higher $M$** → higher spectral efficiency, lower power efficiency.
* **Rectangular QAM** preferred over M-PSK for $M \geq 16$ due to larger $d_{min}$ at same $E_{av}$.
* **Bandwidth with raised-cosine roll-off $\alpha$:** $B_T = (1+\alpha)R_s/2$.
* **Trade-off:** $M \uparrow \Rightarrow \eta \uparrow$ but $P_s \uparrow$ (for fixed $E_b/N_0$).
